> I don't know about that, Mr Dashwood... I've seen far too many places

That is the challenge of being a consultant, Doc <G>. I have no problem with
it and I'm sure you don't either...<G>

The good bit is when their initial hostility gets turned around as they
realise you might actually be able to add value to the place...or even
improve their lot...

When I left the last place they had a party for me (not usual for
contractors) and most of my team gave me their personal emails and contacts
and requested that I stay in touch. Several of them expressed privately that
they felt they had learned a lot by working with me (which is good because I
certainly learned a lot by working with them <G>). I had mail from some of
them today.

My point is that it isn't always hostile and it certainly doesn't HAVE to
be.

Pete.

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3EAFFE5B.3E198F2A@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> So knowing of that reality - I'm not optimistic like you are. Human nature
being what
> it is, I very much doubt the corporate world has changed. 'Seniors' still
find an
> appeal in choosing 'slick' talkers.
>

I'm sick of hearing bad behaviour being excused as "Human Nature"...

I'm Human, and I don't behave like that.

Why is it always the BAD side that is referred to as "Human Nature"?

Human Nature has given us scientific achievement, great works of Art and
Music, the capacity to feel and relate, and the spirit to continue when
things get tough.

I'm also sick of the excuse "Well, I'm only Human...". Being Human is not so
bad. It is definitely what you make it and the choice is YOURS!

As for "slick talkers" doing well, the ability to communicate (listen as
well as talk) is certainly an essential part of the survival kit here on
Earth in the 21st Century...

My experience is that the soured ones are the ones who envy the "slick
talkers". The ones who, instead of improving their own communication skills
(and it can be learned just like anything else), sat back and were beaten by
the "slick talkers".

If by "slick talking" you mean someone who communicates well, can think on
their feet, and is persuasive, well, these are all very valuable skills for
a manager to have. If you mean someone who talks down your case so that it
is never heard, who presents better than you do, well, whose fault is that?

Besides, no matter how well presented, a shit case is still a shit case and
it can be easily demolished without any special "slick talking" skills.

I still prefer to see the bottle as half full rather than half empty.

If you are not persuaded, then I guess I'm not much of a slick talker, or
maybe, I have a shit case and just can't see it...<G>

Whatever...optimism works for me and I enjoy my life.

Pete.


> Jimmy
>
>

```


"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
> news:3EAFFE5B.3E198F2A@shaw.ca...
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Very true and I love the arts just as much as you do. But en route to our
achievements, don't forget the small number, who had a 'telling effect' on
civilization, Genghis Khan, Attila the Hun, Adolf Hitler, Josef Stalin......... Their
milestones have been in no way insignificant.

>
> I'm also sick of the excuse "Well, I'm only Human...". Being Human is not so
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Whatever...optimism works for me and I enjoy my life.

It would appear I've touched a raw nerve with a 'wordsmith'.

First to Humanity - by far and away the MAJORITY are decent human beings. It doesn't
matter whether it's me sitting this side of the pond or you down there counting sheep,
or the humble fellaheen tilling a small patch along the Sweet Water Canal between
Cairo and Ismailia. The latter's dreams are essentially the same as yours and mine -
family, children, (not in your case - unless there's one you don't know about <G>),
prepared to toil and give his family his best. Other than our personal security, we
don't look to poke someone in the eye to achieve our career goals.

What we want as individuals is not what happens as the norm in the commercial world.
It 'kind of' was in the early 20th century, when merchants were concerned with making
money and at the same time some learned to take a paternal attitude to their staff -
which led to the idea you could work with one employer for a lifetime.
A slightly revamped version of this came back into play after WW2 - for my generation
the world appeared to be an oyster - but then puzzlement, concern, cynicism settled
in, Iron Curtain, Korea, JFK's assassination, Watergate.... you get the drift. To a
great extent the commercial world still tended to work in a genteel fashion, until
what, the late 1970s - and then an abrasiveness set in. Cocksure, can do, like Jobs
and Gates, eventually leading to the Enrons and Worldcomms, and the latter are by no
means finished.

As to being a slick talker, (or writer), that I am not. I just 'play' ME - WYSIWYG.
To illustrate using the 'old' Debenhams. Prior to being swallowed up by Burtons the
tailors, and then being sold off, to become the successful 'new' Debenhams - dammit
look at TV shots of affluent Kuwaitis - you see them ascending/descending an escalator
with DEBENHAMS emblazoned over the archway !

In '74 they got into a real schmozzle having acquired the family supermarket group
Caters. The accountants had screwed up royally in their management decisions - so
badly the auditors were prepared to qualify Debenhams accounts and most certainly were
going to qualify the accounts for Caters for the current year. Big meeting in London
between the bean counters and auditors. On command from the Financial Director I had
to be in attendance and took along one of my henchmen.

Once I got the lie of the land, as detailed above, it was obvious my presence was to
'soften the blow', offer some positives from a computer perspective where, hopefully,
the situation could be turned around, with a touch of this and a touch of that  - i.e.
stop the auditors taking the dreadful step of qualifying the accounts, which would
have sent the stock price down the tubes. No detailed prior knowledge of the topic nor
a pre-arranged script about my role - but the tactic worked - I obviously said the
right things..

I left the meeting with my henchman, Jim who commented, "Well, you handled that very
well". My 'Reply, I didn't handle it at all. I said exactly what I thought and what I
meant". Don't ask me what my personality is like - I've never seen myself on a video.
But sometimes common sense and sheer honesty can pay off - a 'slick talker' can become
very transparent in such a situation. BTW the 'slick talker' I'm referring to below,
who was two above me in the hierarchy, was at that meeting and said sweet FA !

Obviously you don't like 'slick talker' used as a definition - so let's flesh out this
particular 'slick talker' for you.

Some five years older than myself, Jesuit educated and had been trained to think like
a friggin Jesuit ! I don't know his EDP background, but had moved around UK at one
stage working  at Craven A (cigs/tobacco), along with above said Financial Director -
I'm extremely doubtful that he ever analysed and most certainly never programmed.

Tall, thinnish, bloodless, humourless, not particularly friendly, and offering only a
sickly smile. Imagine a pair of spidery hands the finger tips touching as though in
prayer and resting on his chin/mouth. - to my mind definitely an eminence grise. Jim
my sidekick summed it up best when he gave him a nickname which stuck - Kipper -
two-faced, yellow-bellied and gutless ! Remember me in private e-mail talking about
some twit who managed to write a book of 200 pages about Decision Tables - well twit =
Kipper !

I already said in the previous message, God knows how or why, the Financial Director
chose him to head up EDP and O & M, very much against the recommendations of the
consultants we had in at the time - said consultants had the ear of the chairman, (
one of their former directors), but that had no effect. For whatever reason, his
oiliness or smoothness just had some sort of appeal to the financial guy. On a rating
from 1 to 10, I would suggest his *practical* knowledge rated a minus 10.

Well I left Debenhams in '75 to come to the Great White North. Back in '74 we had a
new CEO who also brought along an admin sidekick called Jack. I had a farewell
booze-up (Hey ! Joe Muggins did all the buying. I like it better over here, 'they' not
the departee do the buying <G>). With reference to Kipper, I left Jack with a parting
message , "Get rid of the F..... SOB !".  With satisfaction, some six months or more
later, I heard he was gonzo a month or two after I left.

Whatever made you think I have a hang-up about 'slick talkers' <G>

I'd nearly forgotten. I swear to God, on this side of the pond, I've met two of
Kipper's cousins !!!

As regards being positive - well we have to be, otherwise we would go nuts. Even with
optimism, reflect on what is currently going on in the Middle East - one false step,
however well intentioned - and we are probably screwed for the next 50 years. Also I
would like to think the Israeli and Palestinian 'road map' will work - but when I look
at N. Ireland ??????

As a parting shot aimed at 'slick writers' <G> - you said 'there's a subtle difference
between OO Classes and components'  - I *don't* think so, but like many debates here
it isn't worth pursuing. However I do think the following quote from the text 'Core
Java' says it best :-

    "There are as many definitions of a component as there are programmers".

That being said - where's the starting point for designing a component.

Jimmy
```

or

> It would appear I've touched a raw nerve with a 'wordsmith'.

Not at all. Like I said,  I enjoy my life. It just gets tiresome to see the
continual negativity posted here.
>
> First to Humanity - by far and away the MAJORITY are decent human beings.
It doesn't
> matter whether it's me sitting this side of the pond or you down there
counting sheep,
> or the humble fellaheen tilling a small patch along the Sweet Water Canal
between
> Cairo and Ismailia. The latter's dreams are essentially the same as yours
and mine -
> family, children, (not in your case - unless there's one you don't know
about <G>),

You have NO PERCEPTION of my dreams and aspirations so don't make it
personal.

> prepared to toil and give his family his best. Other than our personal
security, we
> don't look to poke someone in the eye to achieve our career goals.
>
> What we want as individuals is not what happens as the norm in the
commercial world.

Then instead of just "wanting" why aren't you "changing" or "doing"?

Yes, bad things in happen in commerce. Yes, there are bad people in good
jobs and there are good people without a job. Bleating about it doesn't
change it. Deal with it.

> It 'kind of' was in the early 20th century, when merchants were concerned
with making
> money and at the same time some learned to take a paternal attitude to
their staff -
> which led to the idea you could work with one employer for a lifetime.

I believe a large number of people in the UK in 1927 would not agree with
this cosy view. In the dust bowl of 1930s America they weren't too enamoured
of it either...

Japan is probably the ONLY place (due to the post war boom in their economy
(in no small measure funded by US aid...) that has ever been able to
guarantee "jobs for life", and even they can't do it any more...

> A slightly revamped version of this came back into play after WW2 - for my
generation
> the world appeared to be an oyster - but then puzzlement, concern,
cynicism settled
> in, Iron Curtain, Korea, JFK's assassination, Watergate.... you get the
drift. To a
> great extent the commercial world still tended to work in a genteel
fashion, until
> what, the late 1970s - and then an abrasiveness set in. Cocksure, can do,
like Jobs
> and Gates, eventually leading to the Enrons and Worldcomms, and the latter
are by no
> means finished.
>

Jobs and Gates have created more jobs and made more millionaires than you've
had jobs in your lifetime. Yet you see "cocksure" and "can do" as negative
traits. I see them as "confidence" and "positivism". It is slanderous to
connect Jobs and Gates with Enron and Worldcomm. Whether we like their
companies and products or not there is no evidence to suggest illegal
dealing.

It is all a question of attitude.

<snip>
>
> As a parting shot aimed at 'slick writers' <G> - you said 'there's a
subtle difference
> between OO Classes and components'  - I *don't* think so, but like many
debates here
> it isn't worth pursuing.

So you haven't grasped it. It is not difficult. An unwrapped OO Class is not
a component. The action of wrapping OO code to comply with a defined
interface (COM or CORBA) enables it to behave as a component. They are not
the same thing but the difference is subtle. If you actually wrote and or
used some components you would realise the difference in an instant. Like
you said, it isn't worth pursuing....


> However I do think the following quote from the text 'Core
> Java' says it best :-
>
>     "There are as many definitions of a component as there are
programmers".
>

That book is out of date. At the time it was written that was a fair
statement. It was written to make people aware that this was a shortcoming
that needed addressing. It has been.  It is also true that from the Java
side (CORBA) there is more debate about what exactly constitutes a
component. On the MS side (ActiveX and COM) it is pretty clear. There are
quite specific definitions now as to what constitutes a "component".
(Although they are by no means unanimous.) The generally accepted one is: "
a .DLL or Java Class that conforms to the COM or CORBA interface
requirements." I use this one when conversing with other people who produce
components and we have no trouble understanding each other...

> That being said - where's the starting point for designing a component.
>

(I'm not sure whether that was a rhetorical question, but, in case it
wasn't...)

As it is intended to provide "pluggable functionality" I usually start with
a description of the functionality I want.

Next, I look at how that specific functionality can be generalised, and what
possible extra behaviours could be valuable. I then define the Properties
(and more and more I am using Properties, rather than the interface, to
determine the behaviours and simplify the Methods. If the properties are
preset before the method is invoked, the code for the method is simpler.
There is also less impact if the component is replaced by a later version,
as the interface to it doesn't change).

Then, the Methods are written and tested.

Finally, the whole thing is wrapped and registered. I let software do this
for me as it a tedious job if you do it manually. Then it is dropped into a
"harness" (container) using a graphic tool (drag and drop with PowerCOBOL)
and it is tested again. I usually also place it into a web page to check
that it works on the Web.

At this point I prepare the Help file for it and the icons.

When it passes all these tests and I am confident with it, it goes on the
component toolbar I use when building applications.

Currently I have 23 icons on this toolbar and the number will grow by the
time I finish the Web Portal I am currently writing.

The next step is to get into SOAP and Web Services and I expect to be doing
that during the Winter here.

The STARTING POINT is Object Oriented programming.

Pete.

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:

> > However I do think the following quote from the text 'Core
> > Java' says it best :-
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Possibly arguable that a book published in 1999, (4th Edition) and
sanctioned/published by Sun,, might lag behind current thinking, but I don't think the
following quote is in disagreement with what you have written :-

"Java will allow the component-based model of computing to take off.

No two people mean the same thing when they talk about components. (** This is the
second time they make this statement ***). Regarding visual controls, like ActiveX
components that can be dropped into a GUI program, Java 1.1. includes the Java Beans
initiative (see Volume 2). Java Beans can potentially do the same sorts of things as
Activex components except that they are automatically cross-platform and
automatically  come with a security manager. All this shows the success of the Java
Beans initiative will go far to making Java successful".

IF and WHEN I should get involved in components then it is very likely I would use
this book as a basis for modelling - there just isn't literature in COBOL.

You do have the advantage with Fujitsu, easily supplying the support runtime. Sure you
could sit down and write components in Net Express - but the real drawback for
distribution - those damned runtime fees !

Now even your approach using String2Num can be paralleled in a desktop GUI approach. I
commented once, "It was a nuisance allowing the user to input integers and then go
back to the to the Business class to reformat - i.e. re-display 9(05) as z9.999 BLANK
WHEN ZERO.". You made the observation this could be achieved.

I thought about it. As I've indicated I use one generic Dialog class, passing it the
particular parameters, (which you correctly refer to as Properties). I am setting up
the Properties for one instance. Taking the above pic formats, this allows me to
separately indicate lengths for an input field of 5 and a display field of 6.

The following is achieved within the generic Dialog, no matter how many 'screens' I
want to generate. 'On event....'.. take the input, check the format property, divide
by 1,000 and redisplay as z9.999. (My parameter has already set that this entryfield
as Numeric as opposed to text).

Naturally it is limited to the formats I currently need. If I was to use that generic
Dialog for accounting, (very, very highly unlikely), then I just add new Level 88s to
the format Property parameter to indicate the dollar/sterling/Euro formats . It
requires very little code to achieve this - and those money formats are merely added
as additional Level 88s in the copyfile.

Now that is in no way disputing your Component approach, and I am well aware that you
can do the above reformatting very easily, plus do your components right and you can
make a buck selling component units to other than the COBOL community. However, if I
don't get into either the Internet or Intranet - then any merit in my designing
Components is dubious. I think <G>. Better watch I don't say, "Never, never..." - the
penny might drop at a later date.

Ticking away in the back of my mind are possibilities such as follows; given a 10 x 24
table of values, perhaps highlight the lowest value, per column,  by re-displaying red
on white. Further for Items being inspected where I indicate 'Top' , Bottom', 'North'
'South' and have 'Exception lines' such as 'At Weld Join', highlight the description
as black on yellow. In the case of the Lowest Value that implies the ability to search
a column for the lowest. Other than checking the table data first, (after the user has
keyed in a value), I don't think either a Dialog or a Component could respond easily
giving the appropriate colour combination.

>
> (I'm not sure whether that was a rhetorical question, but, in case it
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yep. I *really* like that idea of icons on your toolbar.

>
> The next step is to get into SOAP and Web Services and I expect to be doing
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Jimmy
```

>

Of course it can. The routine started as a module CALLable from COBOL.

However, as a component, it can be dropped and used anywhere, not just
on the desktop. I covered this in the COMPONENTS 101 article, don't
know if you read it.


> I
> commented once, "It was a nuisance allowing the user to input integers and then go
> back to the to the Business class to reformat - i.e. re-display 9(05) as z9.999 BLANK
> WHEN ZERO.". You made the observation this could be achieved.
> 

I don't recall... There is certainly a case for having the allegedly
numeric input validated and then, once it is valid, providing it in a
number of formats, some of which are edited (like currency,
f'r'instance). This is achieved through properties of String2Num which
(once the component is instantiated) remain available...


> I thought about it. As I've indicated I use one generic Dialog class, passing it the
> particular parameters, (which you correctly refer to as Properties). I am setting up
> the Properties for one instance. 

Not sure about this, Jimmy. If you are passing it something at the
time you invoke it, that is an interface parameter. (It might well be
included in the object as a property also...) What I found through
some hard experience was that the LESS you provide through the
interface like this, the more stable the component is, the maintenance
when you issue a new version of it (maybe with some new methods), is
less impacted, and the Methods themselves are simpler to write, bcause
the properties are available internally. What I do now is instantiate
the component, make calls to get and set the properties, then invoke
the Method I want. Works for me...<G>

BTW, "BLANK WHEN ZERO" is best avoided...(it calls a subroutine for
many compilers). You can achieve the same effect by zz.zzz

<>Taking the above pic formats, this allows me to
> separately indicate lengths for an input field of 5 and a display field of 6.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Sounds OK.

> Naturally it is limited to the formats I currently need. If I was to use that generic
> Dialog for accounting, (very, very highly unlikely), then I just add new Level 88s to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> make a buck selling component units to other than the COBOL community. 

Well, we are just starting to explore that and that is why the group
is being started. At the moment I have too much on my desk to really
get cracking on it, but I'm hoping that will ease in the next few
months.

>However, if I
> don't get into either the Internet or Intranet - then any merit in my designing
> Components is dubious. I think <G>. Better watch I don't say, "Never, never..." - the
> penny might drop at a later date.
> 

Wise words... I believe the component approach gives the most options,
but then I WOULD say that <G>


> Ticking away in the back of my mind are possibilities such as follows; given a 10 x 24
> table of values, perhaps highlight the lowest value, per column,  by re-displaying red
> on white.

Easy if you do it with SQL. And there is no need then to limit it to
10x24. Just load a table with your input, use the SQL Aggregate
functions to get the cells you are interested in and flick the
"mirrored" display attributes accordingly...

> Further for Items being inspected where I indicate 'Top' , Bottom', 'North'
> 'South' and have 'Exception lines' such as 'At Weld Join', highlight the description
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> giving the appropriate colour combination.

Well you would have colour properties indicating foreground and
background required. These could be preset (using SET) before the
actual method was invoked. I'm sure it could be done, and fairly
easily.

>

…[40 more quoted lines elided]…

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

>
> I sympathise. Perhaps an approach for you could be to move towards
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

V 4.0 is officially out in May, (that's this month now). I'm not sure but I believe it will
give the .Net link. Unfortunately - probably at a considerable price. I don't currently pay
maintenance. Chances are they will say, "You are now buying a NEW product - full price
please".

> I don't recall... There is certainly a case for having the allegedly
> numeric input validated and then, once it is valid, providing it in a
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Good point about BWZ.

Yes I've read your articles which as usual were enjoyable and informative. I'll have to
backtrack through the setup because it gave me an error, (can't find in Registry).
Simplest solution is probably Remove and then re-Add.

Let's see if we can tie this one down - by making a rough parallel comparison. String2Num as
I understand from your narrative is set with methods to accept and display a string, each
"method" saying "I want Text Uppercase", "This text as a numeric 99.999",  etc.

You design a dialog/window/webpage which needs three numeric fields :-

Entryfield A = 9,999,999.99
Entryfield B =        9,999.99
Entryfield C =            99.99

Can we accept for discussion that we both want only numeric ? So we now have three controls
with differing sizes - can be input as decimal point as above or could be integers as above
minus one character, (the decimal point) and the result is displayed as the full picture..

How do you achieve this - (a) three copies of String2Numeric or (b) StringAtoNumeric,
StringBTo..., AStringCTo..... etc. because of the differing sizes. Certainly option (b) is
the more bullet proof  as it is 'cast in stone'.

Now to myDialog - two entry point methods for Entryfields, (c) Pre-coded entry fields from
the designer (Dialog Editor) or (d) dynamically create the entry field -
(c) above contains the 'C' type #define for the ID, whereas (d) contains the x,y, w and h
co-ordinates. After that distinction, from the parameters it is determined whether they are
'text' or 'numerics', and for the latter are set up according to the display format.
Remember this is one instance of MyDialog and if I had, say, 30 different numeric display
formats within the one Dialog -  the 'events'  are recorded against a minimal number of
events - Method-ids "setup" (the (a ) and (b) above,  "display" and "accept and redisplay".
Regardless of the visual content, this reduces the code DRAMATICALLY.

Yes you can certainly screwup on the parameters in the first place - but that really is
covered by thorough testing of the parameter(property ) class. And it is the parameter class
I am testing - NOT MyDialog, which has already been proven. In the case of a 10 x 24 table -
no danger there, as each Entryfield is a repeat format done with a PERFORM VARYING and each
iteration of the PERFORM accessing method (d) above.

Hope that's clear enough. Again not disputing Components, but how does your design stack up,
( or rather ) compare to the above. Should I ever move to components then it makes sense, I
don't want to be out in left field, and hopefully, have the ability to re-use my existing
methods.

>
> Easy if you do it with SQL. And there is no need then to limit it to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yep I get that, displaying an existing record or sets of DB rows. The idea of colouring was
just a thought, a bit gimmicky and might not be introduced. Certainly indicating the
'lowest' for a column on input, would tend to signal to a user that perhaps they had made a
typo.

> Well you would have colour properties indicating foreground and
> background required. These could be preset (using SET) before the
> actual method was invoked. I'm sure it could be done, and fairly
> easily.
>

Disagree if necessary - but in my scenario above I see the colour properties being passed to
the Dialog Entryfield object based on an evaluation of the DB entries in Working Storage. (I
have a "setTheColour" method already in MyDialog - default of course is black on white).

Jimmy
```

```
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
>
> >
> > I sympathise. Perhaps an approach for you could be to move towards etc...

Now I am TOTALLY confused. Which is it in UK-speak 'sympathise' or 'sympathize" ? Like you I
invariably type with an "s". But Netscape spell checker always picks up on it and suggests "z" -
and that's with Netscape set to 'English(UK)". Excluding pure typos, you'd be amazed how much
Netscape mangles my text.

The OED is no use; it's a cop out giving both "ise" and "ize". One thing I vaguely recall is
that "ise" was referred to as 'lazy' English. You're a wordsmith - answer please <G>

BTW - when I *remember*, the messages I want you to specifically see rather than have to go
googling, I address to both CLC and PECD.

PS: 1 : There's a guy in Toronto set up a site picking up on newly coined phrases. He listed 'to
google'. Whammo ! Comes a message from Google's female legal counsel. "Cease and desist". Rather
than the hassle, he uppercased it "to Google". He ain't complainin' - it has increased the
traffic to his site tenfold.

DANGER ALERT FOR BRITS : I know it's been in usage since the late 'twenties - but tell your good
lady or spouse to stop using, "I am going to hoover the carpets". - we really shouldn't impinge
on trademarks <G>

PS: 2 : Only found out as result of an article in the local paper in March. James Gosling,
(Java) was born in Calgary, educated at UofC then headed for parts south for bigger bucks. From
the photo forty/fiveish, sandy hair, beard and pony tail. As a VP for Sun his idea of official
corporate dress is sweats, jeans and sneakers Gave his talk at UofC - liberally dosed with
expletives. If he mentions Microsoft at all it's always with an adjectival prefix.

Talking of putting three people in a room - Gosling, JerryMouse and Richard. Do you think you
could fight off the other two Jerry <G>

Jimmy
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0305041135.cac4e13@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eadaa65_2@127.0.0.1> <b8m0sp$iol$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eaf2140_2@127.0.0.1> <b8napr$31v$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eaf95fa_1@127.0.0.1> <3EAFFE5B.3E198F2A@shaw.ca> <3eb07c6b_1@127.0.0.1> <3EB0A935.C96B1314@shaw.ca> <3eb0fe07_2@127.0.0.1> <3EB160A3.8E6EC967@shaw.ca> <b3638c46.0305011546.a422a6f@posting.google.com> <3EB1C76E.ABDB8CA5@shaw.ca> <3EB1EF7F.37803976@shaw.ca>`

```
James Gavan wrote

> DANGER ALERT FOR BRITS : I know it's been in usage since the late 'twenties - but tell your good
> lady or spouse to stop using, "I am going to hoover the carpets". - we really shouldn't impinge
> on trademarks <G>
> 

Apparently, according to an article I read recently, but can't
presently find, it is not improper or illegal to verbalize (use a
noun, etc as a verb with or without an added suffix) a trademark,
though obviously trademark owners try to discourage it.  For example,
it is not valid to describe any vacuum cleaner as a hoover, unless it
is made by Hoover, but it is valid to hoover the carpet even if not
done with a Hoover product.  I think that Aspirin is beyond recall as
it has passed into the language, but I have never heard of anyone
being aspirin, aspirined or aspirinized when given or prescriped
aspirin.

Robert
```

>> >> and
>> >> xerox.

>> Trademark holders have successfully defended those two, and they are not used

>> as
>> verbs. Nobody says 'I need to kleenex my nose'. 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Ditto for steril cotton swabs.

You won't find kleenex nor q-tip listed in a dictionary as a noun. If dictionary
publishers did that, the Trademark holders would sue their pants off. You will
find aspirin as a noun because Bayer lost their trademark. 

But you will find xerox listed as a verb. Evidentally, verbifying a trademarked
word is ok, so long as you don't use it as a noun.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT : No Tea but "Sympathy"

_(thread depth: 36)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T03:01:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030506230144.21533.00000939@mb-m17.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<joe_zitzelberger-994A35.10074905052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
>From: Joe Zitzelberger joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com 
>Date: 5/5/03 10:07 AM Eastern Daylight Time

>In article <3eb5ccba.8667184@news.optonline.net>,
> robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

>> Trademark holders have successfully defended those two, and they are not
>used 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm not sure either has lost the trademark.  It's Kleenex brand facial tissues
and Q-Tip brand cotton swabs.  Had they lost the trademark then it would
Wal-Mart kleenex not facial tissues and K-Mart Q-tips not cotton swabs. You'll
see XYZ asprin which definately indicates the loss of trademark.

Coca Cola almost lost the trademark on Coke and started the campaign that when
one ordered a 'coke' in a restaurant and the establisment didn't serve Coca
Cola they were not to serve you the beverage without telling you that they
don't serve Coke.

And BTW - not all cotton swabs are sterile.
```

- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eadaa65_2@127.0.0.1> <b8m0sp$iol$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eaf2140_2@127.0.0.1> <b8napr$31v$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eaf95fa_1@127.0.0.1> <3EAFFE5B.3E198F2A@shaw.ca> <3eb07c6b_1@127.0.0.1> <3EB0A935.C96B1314@shaw.ca> <3eb0fe07_2@127.0.0.1> <3EB160A3.8E6EC967@shaw.ca> <b3638c46.0305011546.a422a6f@posting.google.com> <3EB1C76E.ABDB8CA5@shaw.ca> <3EB1EF7F.37803976@shaw.ca> <fcd09c56.0305041135.cac4e13@posting.google.com> <3eb5a10e.92130855@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eb5a10e.92130855@news.optonline.net...
> robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones) wrote:
>
> >James Gavan wrote
> >
> >> DANGER ALERT FOR BRITS : I know it's been in usage since the late
'twenties -
> but tell your good
> >> lady or spouse to stop using, "I am going to hoover the carpets". - we
really
> shouldn't impinge
> >> on trademarks <G>
> >>
> >
> As for nouns, opium was originally a trademark of Beyer, the aspirin
company.

As a sometime shareholder of Bayer (note spelling please) I refute this
calumny vehemently. <G>

Bayer AG is a good and socially aware reputable company.

My etymological sources show the derivation of the word opium as:

opium - 1392, from L. opium, from Gk. opion "poppy juice, poppy," dim. of
opos "vegetable juice."

The first use of  the word "opiates" was around 1540 and it was derived from
Middle Latin going back to the original Latin source noted above.

As Bayer AG was not even a gleam in anyone's eye in the late 14th century,
they cannot be held responsible for the global use of the word.

As for registering it as a trademark... it is generally NOT possible to
register a word in general use as a trademark. (Although trademarks
sometimes come into general use, as we have seen in this thread).

You might as well try and register the use of "sugar"  or "water" or
"bullshit" as a trademark...

Quote your sources or retract please, Robert.

Pete.

> Pete.

I think he intended to say Heroin, which was marketed by Bayer as a 
general cough remedy.  It was also marketed with great success at 
treating Morphine addiction (as the addicts liked smack much better).

In defense of Bayer, Heroin, Morphine and other opiods have been great 
boons to humanity.  While the only recognition they ever receive is 
scorne and paranoia from the various drug warriors because of the abuse 
by a very few.  There are few people alive today (the ones that have 
never had surgery or severe pain) that can argue against their use.

IIRC, my father the CRNA once stated that Heroin is a perfectly good 
narcotic and should be availble for surgical use.  

Whereas my wife, the ex-pharmicist (UK translation, ex-chemist), would 
claim such a statement was the moral equal of peddling smack to 
kindergarteners on a playground.

It takes all kinds....anyway, I'm glad Bayer did all that research so 
that the few surgeries I've had have been mostly painless and my 
recoveries have been rather nice when viewed against history.

And while we are at it, I think the government of the state of Texas 
owns the rights to "bullshit", you will need to send them their license 
fee for its use...
```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:<3EB1C76E.ABDB8CA5@shaw.ca>...
<snip>> 
> Yes I've read your articles which as usual were enjoyable and informative.
 
Thanks <G>, encouragement is always welcome...

>I'll have to
> backtrack through the setup because it gave me an error, (can't find in Registry).
> Simplest solution is probably Remove and then re-Add.
>

Yes, try that. If it still gives trouble, mail me. Two people have had
a problem with installing and we found very quick workarounds in both
cases.

It always amazes me that stuff that works in one environment,
sometimes doesn't work in another environment that is SUPPOSED to be
the same... Still, I guess that's Windows...there is such a huge
diversity of settings and update levels on different machines I guess
it is a miracle anything works on more than one machine...<G>


> Let's see if we can tie this one down - by making a rough parallel comparison. String2Num as
> I understand from your narrative is set with methods to accept and display a string, each
> "method" saying "I want Text Uppercase", "This text as a numeric 99.999",  etc.
>

No, not at all.

It accepts a single string as a parameter when it is invoked. It
doesn't "want" anything or "expect" anything, not
even numbers. You pass it a string and ask it to see if that string
represents a valid number. (The number
[if it IS a number] could be edited, not edited, any length (up to 18
digits, although the passed string can be
longer to allow for sign characters and editing characters). It
decides whether it does represent a number or not,
and says "yes" or "no".

If the answer is "yes", it provides the number in a bunch of different
formats, along with some information about the
number.

Some examples might help...

Let's say the following are typical input strings (all of them would
set "itsANumber" to TRUE):

a. 1234.567890
b. -123,456,789.012345
c. 123
d. 123-
e. -123
f. .12345678901234567
g. -.123456789012345
h. $1,234.78
i. +12345.
j. 12345.+


You instantiate the component in the usual way...(Probably in an
INITIALISE-PARA)

	invoke COM "CREATE-OBJECT" 
               using "String2Num.String2Num.1" 
               returning String2Num
        end-invoke

(At this point we have an Object Reference to the component...So we
can invoke its methods and access its properties.)


	invoke String2Num "getNumber"
	       using inString
        end-invoke

(Let's say that "inString" contained example "a" above... Then the
state of the component's properties after this
invocation would be: [please note the word "integer" here is taken to
mean "the digits to the LEFT of the decimal point"; the word
"decimal" is taken to mean "the digits to the RIGHT of the decimal
point" - no correspondence will be entered into <G>])

dblDecimal    - decimal digits as 64 bit binary
.567890

dblInteger      - integer  digits as  64 bit binary
1234

intDecimal     - number of digits to right of the point
6

intInteger       - number of digits to left of the point
4

ItsANumber   - Boolean TRUE or FALSE
TRUE

realCurrency  - 64 bit VB type currency [14.4]
00000000001234.5678

realNum        - 64 bit real number floating point
(FP representation of +1234.567890)

strDecimal     - decimal digits as a string
.567890

strInteger       - integer digits as a string
+1234

Now I'll show how these properties can be useful by applying it to
your example...
 
> 
> You design a dialog/window/webpage which needs three numeric fields :-
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> How do you achieve this - 

Pretty easily by the following... I am assuming that you want data
entered by a user in the entry
fields, processed, and returned in edited format if it is valid, and
the user notified and given
a chance to correct it if it isn't (?)

Start by defining all the entry fields as string. It doesn't matter
what size you make them as
long as they are big enough to accommodate the largest entry you will
accept. (I would make them all
20 bytes so they will align nicely on the GUI screen, but it really
doesn't matter.

First, a couple of working-storage fields...

01      inString   pic x(20).

01      edit-mask.
	12 edit-mask-num  pic 999,999,999,999.99. (or zzz,zzz,zzz,zzz.zz or
whatever...)

Now, to business...

	move "Text" of EntryfieldA to instring
	perform check-numeric
	if "itsANumber" of String2Num = TRUE
        *>
	*> It is a valid numeric string (You would do any other validations
here
        *> before processing with it...)
	*>
           move edit-mask to "Text" of EntryfieldA
           (process using "realNum" or "realCurrency" which are
suitable for arithmetic
	    or simply store one of these values into a variable for later
processing after
	    validation is complete. There are a number of ways to do this but
you get the idea...)
	else
	   *>
	   *> It is not numeric. The User has been notified. We need to
insert the
           *> cursor on the offending field and exit to let him try
again...
	   *>
	   invoke EntryfieldA "insert-cursor"
           end-invoke
           (there are a number of ways to code this as
well...whichever way you choose
	    it needs to exit this process and update the screen, then wait
for input...)
	end-if

	move "Text" of EntryfieldB to instring
	perform check-numeric
	if "itsANumber" of String2Num = TRUE
        *>
	*> It is a valid numeric string (You would do any other validations
here
        *> before processing with it...)
	*>
           move edit-mask to "Text" of EntryfieldB
           (process using "realnum" or "realCurrency" which are
suitable for arithmetic
	    or simply store one of these values into a variable for later
processing after
	    validation is complete. There are a number of ways to do this but
you get the idea...)
	else
	   *>
	   *> It is not numeric. The User has been notified. We need to
insert the
           *> cursor on the offending field and exit to let him try
again...
	   *>
	   invoke EntryfieldB "insert-cursor"
           end-invoke
           (there are a number of ways to code this as
well...whichever way you choose
	    it needs to exit this process and update the screen, then wait
for input...)
	end-if
        
	move "Text" of EntryfieldC to instring
	perform check-numeric
	if "itsANumber" of String2Num = TRUE
        *>
	*> It is a valid numeric string (You would do any other validations
here
        *> before processing with it...)
	*>
           move edit-mask to "Text" of EntryfieldC
           (process using "realnum" or "realCurrency" which are
suitable for arithmetic
	    or simply store one of these values into a variable for later
processing after
	    validation is complete. There are a number of ways to do this but
you get the idea...)
	else
	   *>
	   *> It is not numeric. The User has been notified. We need to
insert the
           *> cursor on the offending field and exit to let him try
again...
	   *>
	   invoke EntryfieldC "insert-cursor"
           end-invoke
           (there are a number of ways to code this as
well...whichever way you choose
	    it needs to exit this process and update the screen, then wait
for input...)
	end-if
	...

 check-numeric section.
 cn000.
*
*  This subroutine checks the input field for valid numeric and edits
it if it is.
*
*  If it isn't, the user is notified by an intrinsic method in the
component, which
*  gives examples of correct input.
*
*  It is the caller's responsibility to place the edited data in the
appropriate screen
*  field if it is valid, or to insert the cursor on the screen field
if it isn't...
*

	invoke String2Num "getNumber"
               using inString
 	end-invoke
        if "itsANumber" of String2Num = TRUE
           move "realCurrency" of String2Num to edit-mask-num
        else
	   invoke String2Num "ShowError"
           end-invoke
        end-if
	.
 cn999.
	exit.

...


The code shown is somewhat contrived but it is intended to 
show how you can use encapsulated functionality in a COBOL
environment.
(This is NOT the way I personally use components, but it is _A_
way...)

I would not pretend that this is particularly elegant code but it
certainly would do
the job and do so under a number of possible scenarios.

(It could be improved by generalising it to be one set of code for ANY
entry field. If it
was my application, I'd do this, but this code would be necessarily
more complex and I am
trying to show an example. It therefore needs to be simple...)

As I have mentioned elsewhere, the important thing is to focus on the
functionality.

Here, the functionality is simply to establish whether something is
numeric or not and
to present it in a series of useful formats if it is. A single
polymorphic input field is
used and the component recognises what is required.

Using string for numeric input provides a number of advantages over
using numeric attributes on
a screen. The user can key the actual values, edited, or unedited, and
edited data can be
returned to the field, which it often can't be when numeric screen
attributes are used.

Furthermore, most hardware platforms support string input but NOT all
support numeric attributes
on screen fields.

As you know Jimmy, I HATE posting code in CLC because many people see
only style and not
what is intended to be achieved. (And I am really tired of these
endless discussions that persuade
nobody. I don't make my living from writing COBOL any more and I
really don't care.)

I have done it on this occasion because I know you prefer the 
tangibility of source code to an abstract discussion, but I won't be
entering into discussions
on this code unless they deal with the functionality it is seeking to
achieve.


>(a) three copies of String2Numeric or (b) StringAtoNumeric,
> StringBTo..., AStringCTo..... etc. because of the differing sizes. Certainly option (b) is
> the more bullet proof  as it is 'cast in stone'.
> 
 
No, as you have seen, none of those... The differing size is handled
by polymorphism, one string; one edit mask.


> Now to myDialog - two entry point methods for Entryfields, (c) Pre-coded entry fields from
> the designer (Dialog Editor) or (d) dynamically create the entry field -
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> iteration of the PERFORM accessing method (d) above.

You have lost me here...I really don't understand this. It looks a lot
more low level and complex than
I remember DIALOG being. I see no need to deal with GUIs at the X,Y,Z
level. Each field should be
an object with its own methods (like "insert-cursor"). in fact, I
treat each of these GUI items as
components...

> 
> Hope that's clear enough. Again not disputing Components, but how does your design stack up,
> ( or rather ) compare to the above. Should I ever move to components then it makes sense, I
> don't want to be out in left field, and hopefully, have the ability to re-use my existing
> methods.

That is legitimate concern. You SHOULD be able to use existing methods
just by re-packaging the Class.
> 
> >
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> have a "setTheColour" method already in MyDialog - default of course is black on white).

I'd make the colours properties of the superset and inherit them down
the line. But there are many
ways to do this.

Hope this helps. It didn't take three hours this time, but it isn't as
comprehensive as the version that was lost <G>.

Cheers,

Pete.
```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:<3EB5E540.742AC1BC@shaw.ca>...
> So no direct messages - I guess you are SOL. Like Skippy seems somehow your ISP is ignoring
> messages transmitted from shaw.ca.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

That's fairly close...

It isn't that our ISPs won't pick them up. It is far more likely they
simply weren't relayed to a backbone here.

Sometimes if an ISP has a history of misbehaving (relaying spam and
refusing to stop, operating an open relay that can be used by
spammers, are two examples...) other ISPs will refuse to relay his
messages to any hubs. For private mail it isn't a big deal, but for
posts to public Newsgroups like Usenet, it simply means that that
channel is not available to him.

I wouldn't mind betting that shaw.ca is on a "blacklist" somewhere in
this part of the World. If so, it will be because of past
misdemeanours. But they should have been notified of this. Maybe they
were and just chose to ignore it...

Never mind, I can see your posts in Google. (The disadvantage there is
that they appear much later than they would directly in Usenet, and
bloody Google has now lost two responses on me so I'm loth to use
it...)

Anyway, glad you got the stuff and hope it gets the wheels turning.

Pete.
```

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> OK here we go again...<G>
>
> I'm trying it one more time in Google and will attempt to save it
> occasionally...
>

OK printed and had a good read and I think I understand what you are driving at.
Thank you for taking the time out to write the detail - yes I *do* prefer tangible examples.

If our friend 'Grinder' is reading this - it's an update on what I gave your previously.

However, it all really starts at that quote "There are as many definitions of components as there
are programmers". Perhaps more accurately there are as many ways of designing programs as there
are programmers. We each arrive at an individual style. Given say a dozen of us wrote a program to
Edit Customerfile, using a DB which was either COBOL files or (R)DBMS, non-OO or OO we would get
12 different solutions all achieving the same result, perhaps some with slicker techniques than
others.

Some clarification on what I'm doing and particularly the word 'Dialog' which I think has misled
you.

- VISOC - came solely with Dialog Editor and GUIs, Win APIs and Screen Section, but not Dialog
System or Bill's favourite 'toy' Report Writer. Although many went after the beta at about $800 -
many existing Dialog System users held off.

- Net Express V 3.0 - re-introduced the Dialog System you know - but with enhancements. Talk about
on again, off again --   Report Writer was re-introduced. V 3.1  introduced many enhancements, but
the ones I'm specifically aware of - Type Library Assistant (using OLEs), Java Support classes and
the module Forms for web pages - haven't used this but somewhat like your F/J it generates
supporting source to create Web pages.

- Dialog System - that's the 'animal' you know from DOS days, but with enhancements and ability to
invoke OO classes. I haven't used it, already committed to GUI classes, but have referred to
examples and took the basis for my menu creation from a D/S example.

- Dialog Editor and GUIs - that's what I use. Difficult to describe, without going to great
lengths, but anybody interested can bring up the MicroSOFT version - \msTools\bin\dlgedit.exe

It gives you an an empty palette with a Toolbox with control icons which you can drag and drop
into the palette, resize etc. As you create a control you give it a unique ID, e.g. ID_ENTRYFIELD1
and allocate a unique number. The finished dialog is saved to the multi dialog files xxxx.h
(containing #define statements for linking) and xxxxx.res which is an 'image' of your screen with
control co-ordinates and a set or properties per individual control.

I use the Micro FOCUS version of dlgedit.exe which is based on above. The toolbox contains 23
control icons. There are a set of default properties per control, but let's concentrate on
ENTRYFIELD, (which is what String2Num is about) - but the same general concepts apply to other
controls. The properties are reasonably comprehensive but I'm guessing (from the screenshots I've
seen) not quite to the degree of F/J, although that web Forms module probably is. You can change
the default font to Roman or Courier - Whoops ! It changes the font for the whole dialog - that's
not what we want, so invoke a GUI method to change the font for a particular Entryfield. (But see
my parameter method below - I can tell it which font to use for a particular entryfield)
Similarly, colour is not listed as a property, again invoke Colour class.

For an entryfield the default is Text pic x(...); you select 'Number', pic 9(...), if you want an
integer - note, no decimal point allowed. At this stage the only significance to length is how
you've designed, reference your comment about lining them up to make the dialog aesthetically
pleasing. (No problem of course to allow a user to enter a decimal number if you specify the
default as 'Text').

The tool above and how to use it. Recall some years back I sent you that source code ? You thought
it was 'ORRIBLE - so did I ! But to be fair, the M/F demo was doing it in 'longhand' to illustrate
umpteen techniques. Back to design again, where to start. I had a book 'APIs How To", and thumbed
through it and found what appealed to me. Say Customer Edit :-

Left-hand side of screen a set of entryfields, perhaps radio buttons etc and on the right a
Listbox to display Existing records. That turned my crank - show the user what already exists in a
listbox, from which (s)he can select or alternatively enter a fresh record on the left-side. At
the bottom of course the traditional pushbuttons.. This 'split screen' approach doesn't work with
everything but it's not a bad basis on which to design.

Back to using the Dialog in the resource file. You now go through a laborious routine of
specifying each field to create in your dialog, plus add methods for other than default property
settings. Fairly early on I got the message, my own dictum, "If you find yourself writing this
more than once, take a second look - can it be turned into a generic method". It has developed
over stages, becoming more refined, plus as we all tend to have our coding style it is possible to
come up with an outline class template for Edit File X.

This is how it works currently :-

[Edit Class]-------[Property/Parameter Class]------[Instances of MyDialog]

Initially checking the resource file I set the Propertties up manually, but a bit of a pain in the
butt for accuracy and time wise. Then a comment from Richard about how he generates parameters
triggered something lurking in the back of my mind. I backtracked. (Incidentally 'Backtracking' is
something Will Price refers to in his book, and it's also an admission by Donald. With a complete
lack of OO COBOL code to refer to, at this stage it is inevitable. Hopefully as time goes on and
more code becomes available, we will get a progresion at Mr. B enhances code from Mr. A, Mr. C
enhances code from Mr. B etc....) : -

- 1 - Templates for classes (a) Edit X,  and (b) Parameters X
- 2. Read the Resource file (line sequential) and extract the info I need.
   (Not picture perfect getting this info, but it works 90% plus).
- 3 Having done #2, read in the 'master' Edit X to output 'Edit X + 1' to include the skeleton for
methods for entryfields,
   identified as methods "EF-fieldName", similarly LB = Listbox, RB = RadioButton and PB =
PushButton
- 4 Using #2  again, read in the 'master' ParametersX to output 'ParametersX + 1',  generating the
WS table per method for
   each type of control.

MyDialog contains methods to create all controls and return events for those controls I currently
use. Let's assume the Edit Class needs 6 dialogs - then we have Property/Parameter Classes 1
through 6 and we create instances of MyDialog 1 through 6. Doesn't matter if I'm creating a
zillion controls they all are controlled through MyDialog, repetitively using methods particular
to a specific control or 'generic' methods like "show", "hide", "enable", "disable" etc.

- Edit Class -

            invoke ParameterClass using self(Edit Class) returning MyDialogInstance

- ParameterClass -

        invoke MyDialog using EditClass(the self above) returning MyDialogInstance
                - this instance is passed back to Edit Class so that it can subsequently invoke
MyDialogInstance directly

- MyDialogInstance -

        stores the reference to 'self' (Edit Class) so that it can return events directly to Edit
Class

Parameter Class - contains something like :-

*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "begin".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-id                            pic x(4) comp-5.
etc...
Procedure Division using     lnk-Caller
                             lnk-Parent
                             lnk-Resource
                   returning lnk-Dialog.

set os-Parent   to lnk-Parent
move 0770 to ls-id ( this is the ID allocated to the particular Dialog in Dialog Editor)

invoke TabbedMyDialog "start"
       using     lnk-Caller, os-Parent, lnk-Resource, ls-id
       returning os-Dialog

 set lnk-Dialog to os-Dialog

 *> Note sequencing of methods below which activates controls
 *> in the same sequence as their field numbers, so that field
 *> number = index position in the Dialog Control Collections

 invoke self       "ControlCollections"         *> Control Type
 invoke self       "EntryFields"                *> ++02
 invoke self       "RadioButtons"               *> ++03
 invoke self       "Pushbuttons"                *> ++04
 *>invoke self     "CheckBoxes"                 *> ++05
 *>invoke self     "Droplists"                  *> ++06
 invoke self       "Listboxes"                  *> ++07
 invoke self       "Labels"                     *> ++08
 invoke self       "ListboxColors"              *>
 invoke self       "LastField"
 invoke os-Dialog  "zCloseEvent"

End Method "begin".
*>-----------------------------------------------------------

Concentrating on the entryfield parameters :-

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "EntryFields".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------

*> This method only handles entryfields created using the Dialog
*> Editor which are allocated IDs in the files mfres.h and
*> mfres.cpy. Dynamically created entryfields are handled
*> separately.

*> See file "dilogprm.cpy" for list of parameters

Working-storage section.
78 TL                                value 28.
78 ML                                value 30.
01 ws-A.

*> Resource Sequence Field
*> ID       Number   Length    A D  R,C F P V    MethodName
*> IIII     SSS      LLL       9,99,9,9,9,9,9,x-------30--------
*> ----     ---      ---       - -- - - - - - ------------------

*> ws-Alphanumeric :
*> A : 0   = Numerick      1 = Lowercase    2 = Uppercase

*> ws-DisplayFormat :
*> I : 00 = Integer       13 = Decimal 1.3  etc.....

*> ws-ReturnType :
*> E : 0  = No return      1 = Return value to Invoker

*> ws-ClearType :
*> C : 0  = EmptyField     1 = InsertField

*> ws-FontType :
*> F : 0  = DefaultFont    1 = MsFont       2 = SystemFont
*>                                          3 = MonospacedFont
*>

*> ws-Position :
*> P : 0 = LeftJustify    1 = Centered      2 = RightJustify

*> ws-ViewType
*> V : 0 = NotReadOnly    1 = ReadOnly

  05.
    10 pic x(TL) value '0778,001,004,2,00,1,0,0,0,0,'.
    10 pic x(ML) value 'EF-PROBE-ID'.
  05.
    10 pic x(TL) value '0771,002,015,1,00,0,0,0,0,0,'.
    10 pic x(ML) value 'EF-PROBE-MAKE'.
  05.
    10 pic x(TL) value '0772,003,003,0,12,0,0,0,2,0,'.
    10 pic x(ML) value 'EF-PROBE-FREQUENCY'.   *> 9.99
  05.
    10 pic x(TL) value '0773,004,004,0,13,0,0,0,2,0,'.
    10 pic x(ML) value 'EF-PROBE-DIAMETER'.    *> 9.999
  05.
    10 pic x(TL) value '0775,005,010,1,00,0,0,0,0,0,'.
    10 pic x(ML) value 'EF-PROBE-TESTBLOCK'.
  05.
    10 pic x(TL) value '0776,006,010,1,00,0,0,0,0,0,'.
    10 pic x(ML) value 'EF-PROBE-CALIBRATION'.
  05.
    10 pic x(TL) value '0751,007,004,0,00,0,0,0,0,0,'.
    10 pic x(ML) value 'EF-DATE'.

01 ws-B redefines ws-A.
   05 ws-C occurs MaxEntryFields.
      10 ws-ID                       pic 9(04).
      10                             pic x.
      10 ws-field                    pic 9(03).
      10                             pic x.
      10 ws-Length                   pic 9(03).
      10                             pic x.
      10 ws-Alphanumeric             pic 9.
      10                             pic x.
      10 ws-DisplayFormat            pic 99.
      10                             pic x.
      10 ws-ReturnType               pic 9.
      10                             pic x.
      10 ws-ClearType                pic 9.
      10                             pic x.
      10 ws-FontType                 pic 9.
      10                             pic x.
      10 ws-Position                 pic 9.
      10                             pic x.
      10 ws-ViewType                 pic 9.
      10                             pic x.
      10 ws-MethodName               pic x(ML).

01 n                                 pic x(4) comp-5.

Procedure Division.

  initialize Dialog-Params
  perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > MaxEntryFields

      move ws-ID             (n) to Dialog-ID
      move ws-field          (n) to Dialog-field
      move ws-Length         (n) to Dialog-Length
      move the rest etc......

      invoke os-Dialog "zCreateEntryfield" using Dialog-Params
  End-perform

End Method "EntryFields".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------

Note that the Level 05s in the above table were generated from the Resource file as I described
above, with a set of defaults. I manually change to show lengths, decimalization (DisplayFormat)
etc...

Next Stage - the Instance of MyDialog and how Entryfields are handled :-

*** Step A - creation - invoke "zDynamic...." or "zCreate....."

*>------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "zDynamicEntryfield".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
copy "dilogprm.new" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.
Procedure Division using lnk-Params.
 invoke TextEntry "new" using self returning lnk-object
 invoke lnk-object "SetRectangle" using x y w h
 invoke lnk-object "SingleLineEntry"
 invoke self "zCreateEntryField2" using lnk-Params
End Method "zDynamicEntryfield".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "zCreateEntryfield".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
copy "dilogprm.new" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.
Procedure Division using lnk-Params.
 invoke self "getObjectFromId"
        using lnk-ID returning lnk-object
 invoke self "zCreateEntryfield2" using lnk-Params
End Method "zCreateEntryfield".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "zCreateEntryfield2".        *> PRIVATE
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-event                                 pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-Format.
   05 ls-Format1                            pic 9.
   05 ls-Format2                            pic 9.
01 ls-length                                pic x(4) comp-5.
Linkage section.
copy "dilogprm.new" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.
Procedure Division using lnk-Params.
 invoke lnk-object "accessSystemEvents"
*> Dialog-Alphanumeric
 Evaluate true
     when NumericK   invoke lnk-object "numeric"
     when UpperCase  invoke lnk-object "uppercase"
 End-evaluate
*> Dialog-FontType
 if lnk-FontType <> zeroes
    invoke lnk-object "setfont" using os-Font(lnk-FontType)
 End-if
*> Dialog-Position
 Evaluate true
     when Centered       invoke lnk-object "centered"
     when RightJustify   invoke lnk-object "rightjustified"
 End-evaluate
*> Dialog-ViewType
 Evaluate true
   when ReadOnly
       invoke lnk-object "readonly"
       move zeroes to Ls-Length
   when lnk-DisplayFormat <> zeroes
        move lnk-DisplayFormat to ls-Format
        compute ls-length = Ls-Format1 + Ls-Format2
   when other
        move lnk-Length to ls-length
 End-Evaluate

 if ls-length <> zeroes
    invoke lnk-object "setLength" using ls-length
 End-if

 initialize                ws-MethodRec
 move lnk-MethodName    to ws-MethodName
 move lnk-ClearType     to ws-ClearType
 move lnk-DisplayFormat to ws-DisplayFormat
 move lnk-ReturnType    to ws-ReturnType

 if lnk-Line is numeric
    move lnk-Line          to ws-Line
    move lnk-Column        to ws-Column
 End-if

 invoke os-Collection(1) "add" using lnk-object
 invoke os-Collection(2) "add" using ws-MethodRec

*> Dialog-ClearType

 move gained-focus to ls-event
 invoke lnk-object "setEventto" using
   ls-event, self, "setGainedFocus ", lnk-Field

*> Dialog-DisplayFormat and Dialog-ReturnType

move lost-focus to ls-event

Evaluate true
   when lnk-DisplayFormat <> zeroes
        invoke lnk-object "setEventto" using
            ls-event, self, "displayDecimals ", lnk-Field
            *> See method below "displayDecimals"
   when MakeReturn
        invoke lnk-object "setEventto" using
          ls-event, self, "returnEntryNow ", lnk-Field
   when other
        invoke lnk-object "setEventto" using
          ls-event, self, "ElementChanged ", lnk-Field
End-evaluate

*> Note : For specific records, when the user is displaying or
*> entering data, the format by Default = IMPERIAL. If the user
*> has selected 'METRIC' from the Application Menu, then as dialogs
*> are created from the appropriate Dialog parameter class - Metric
*> indicators are substituted for Imperial formats - e.g., the
*> Readings Dialog shows Imperial values of 13 (9.999 inches) in the
*> parameters. Based on the Metric flag - 23 (99.999 cms) is
*> substituted in the Entryfield table to replace the '13'

End Method "zCreateEntryfield2".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

Strictly speaking "zCreateEntryField2" is far too big, negating one of my own self-impposed rules,
"A method should not normally be more than half a page". Arguably I could split it up, but it is
to do with the functionality of creating one
control.

*** Step B - Event triggers user has entered something; if it is numeric formatted,
invokes self for this method :-

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "displayDecimals".   *> PRIVATE
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
*> Used for re-displaying decimal numbers entered as integers

Local-storage section.
copy "dilogprm.new" replacing ==(tag)== by ==Dialog==.
copy "displayF.cpy".
*>------------------ diusplayF.cpy ---------------------------
01 ls-display12                  pic   z9.99  blank when zero.
01 ls-display13                  pic  z9.999  blank when zero.
01 ls-display23                  pic zz9.999  blank when zero.
01 ls-display33                  pic zz9.999  blank when zero.

Note : The copyfile contains just what I currently need for inches and
centimetres. Get involved in accounting - just add formats required.
*>-----------------------------------------------------------
01 n                             pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-length1                    pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-length2                    pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-object                     object reference.
01 ls-string                     object reference.
01 ls-Text1.
   10 occurs 1 to 10
      depending on ls-length1    pic x(01).
01 ls-Text2.
   10 occurs 1 to 10
      depending on ls-length2    pic x(01).

Linkage section.
01 lnk-Field                     pic x(4) comp-5.

Procedure Division using lnk-Field.

 invoke os-Collection(2) "at"
        using Lnk-Field returning ws-MethodRec
 invoke os-Collection(1) "at"
        using lnk-Field returning ls-object
 invoke ls-object "getText" returning ls-string
 invoke ls-string "trimblanks" returning ls-string
 invoke ls-object "Getlength" returning ls-length1
 invoke ls-string "getValueWithSize"
        using ls-Length1 returning ls-text1
 invoke ls-string "finalize" returning ls-string

Evaluate true

 when ws-DisplayFormat = 12
        compute ls-Length2 = function length(ls-display12)
        compute ls-display12 = (function numval(ls-Text1) / 100)
        move ls-display12 to ls-Text2

 when ws-DisplayFormat = 13
        compute ls-Length2 = function length(ls-display13)
        compute ls-display13 = (function numval(ls-Text1) / 1000)
        move ls-display13 to ls-Text2

 when ws-DisplayFormat = 23
        compute ls-Length2 = function length(ls-display23)
        compute ls-display23 = (function numval(ls-Text1) / 1000)
        move ls-display23 to ls-Text2

 when ws-DisplayFormat = 33
        compute ls-Length2 = function length(ls-display33)
        compute ls-display33 = (function numval(ls-Text1) / 1000)
        move ls-display33 to ls-Text2
End-evaluate

initialize                        Dialog-params
move lnk-Field                 to Dialog-Field
move ls-Length2                to Dialog-length
move ls-Text2(1:Dialog-Length) to Dialog-text

invoke self "setEntryField" using Dialog-Params
invoke self "ElementChanged" using Dialog-Field

End Method "displayDecimals".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

Of course, for existing records, I'm passsing - from Edit class data I have for COBOL files/DBMS -

which displays :-

*>-----------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "setEntryField".
*>-----------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-object                         object reference.
01 ls-string                         object reference.
Linkage section.
copy "dilogprm.new" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.

Procedure Division using lnk-Params.

  invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue"
         using lnk-length, lnk-text returning ls-string
  invoke os-Collection(1) "at"
         using lnk-field returning ls-object
  invoke ls-object "setText" using ls-string
  invoke ls-string "finalize" returning ls-string

End Method "setEntryField".
*>-----------------------------------------------------------

To summarize - for a decimalized entry field, four methods :-

    (1) Create - registering the event (callback) to a class/method
    (2) Display for existing records
    (3) Lost focus - this is the event/callback setup by # 1; redisplay using # 2
    (4) PushButton OK - return entryfield values to Edit class - addressed to specific 'receiving
methods'

While MyDialog contains a lot, without actually poring over the code, it's difficult to get a
handle on what it is doing in total.
Still, with ENCAPSULATION there's no need to know is there <G> However a summary, and indicating
some of the 'why(s) I'm doing it this way'. Now the following list is not in any particular order,
and many features are inter-related, coupled of course with my *own* design concepts.

- Firstly N/E does not as yet have auto garbage collection but contains following methods to
destroy objects - (1) finalize a single, (2) deepfinalize a collection and (3)
deepfinalizeWithDepth for multi level collections.

- Recall Simon's source for Print Preview ? Umpteen objects each identified singularly. Ugghhh !
He's latched on since <G>.
Prior to creating controls I set up two OrderedCollection (added to, next in sequence), (a) one
collection holding the displayable objects and (b) the second an associated text reference holding
the method-name to return to, plus some other info - check back on the methods above to see
reference to these two collections. I'm constantly referencing these collections to store/retrieve
objects by field number - Level 88's from the Parameter Class Now if I really had it figured, I
would clone one class to hold both the object reference and the text in one element - rather than
accessing parallel collections..

- I want to destroy the dialog - invoke the two collections above "deepFinalize" - gone !

- Entryfield lengths - input and displayable. While I might show a (text) entryfield as 60
characters long, for appearance, if it was say,
a Corporate Customer Name (stored as 40 characters) - I don't want the user happily keying in 56
characters. When he re-displays that field next time he will see I've truncated it to 40
characters. So specifying input length - the keyboard comes to a dead halt after the 40th
character. Similarly with display I want to COMPUTE NUMVAL etc... based on the pre-designed
length.

- This particular application is well over 80% just keying in numbers 99.999, but most are  0.123
with the odd 1.234. Like
the old DOS system, for quickness user keys in without decimal point and I do the dividing,
re-displaying.

- Using x, y, w and h (in Microsoft-speak = x, y, cx, cy). Recall up above about designing Dialogs
and allocating unique names and IDs. In trepidation that's how I started out with a screen
containing 325 controls - doesn't work; not unreasonably there's a maximum of 250 or 255 controls
per dialog. Apart from which doing the following READING1, READING2 .... etc., all the way up to
READING240 - is definitely a non-starter ! So when you get into repetitive table fields it makes
sense to DYNAMICALLY create them - back to the methods above no difference between 'static' and
'dynamic' once you've identified which you are creating - they both then invoke "zEntryField2".

- I asked somebody what they did when they changed data in an existing record - Ripley's Believe
it or not - 'Well given the speed of modern computers, just update the whole record.". To use your
phrase, "Not on my watch !". Particularly that 10 x 24 table which is no longer a COBOL occurs
record. So initially MyDialogInstance has a MasterChangeFlag set to false. Anything changed the
flag is set to true and when user clicks Pushbutton-OK the flag is returned to the Edit Class,
which in turn invokes  MyDialog to return field values. However as new fields are being entered or
changed in MyDialog, I also set an ElementChangeFlag within the collections referred to above.
Given say 280 elements (dialog controls) in the collection, and ONLY one field has been changed,
then that is the only one signalled back to the Edit Class.

- Validation of records occurs after PB-OK, being done in Business Logic (Edit Class)

- Colouring - at the moment I'm just using the standard set of colour names, black, cyan,
lightcyan etc., and use colours on an exception basis. (At some future stage I may dabble with the
Colour Common Dialog to get different hues). I stick with the conventional defaults of grey and
white, at the moment highlighting Listboxes in different colours. At this stage I see no necessity
to have a colour code as an initial parameter - just send the backgroun/foreground colours to a
particular object as appropriate.

Now I could go on an on with insights as to why I'm doing it this particular way - you can bet as
soon as I've sent this message, I'll remember some more ! So any observations and I'll try and
explain them.

As you observed it is LOW LEVEL - whereas you've gone with what you have, quote : "For most of the
components I build, I use Fujitsu PowerCOBOL because it takes care of the 'housekeeping'
(Component Wrapping) so beautifully, and spares me a lot of mind-numbing detail, but that is not
the only way".

Bearing in mind the tool I had didn't generate code, I had to follow from that basic route. Took
one hell of a while to get there, but so far given a set of Properties/Parameters I have,
regardless of number of dialogs, controls  or styles  :-

- 80% generated Business Logic code
- Properties/Parameters generated
- MyDialog - one class that handles it all the Windowing

Stretching it a bit, but one could view my methods above as being a "Multi-Purpose Entryfield"
component with four methods. Certainly I would like to see a fair number of the 'Whys' above built
into any specific component I designed. And logically at this time, can't see I'll be dashing
around the world trying to flog components anyway <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Components - Was Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T01:55:37+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb91047_1@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eadaa65_2@127.0.0.1> <b8m0sp$iol$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eaf2140_2@127.0.0.1> <b8napr$31v$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eaf95fa_1@127.0.0.1> <3EAFFE5B.3E198F2A@shaw.ca> <3eb07c6b_1@127.0.0.1> <3EB0A935.C96B1314@shaw.ca> <3eb0fe07_2@127.0.0.1> <3EB160A3.8E6EC967@shaw.ca> <b3638c46.0305011546.a422a6f@posting.google.com> <3EB1C76E.ABDB8CA5@shaw.ca> <b3638c46.0305040731.1bb9dc8f@posting.google.com> <3EB86E8D.72B66843@shaw.ca>`

```
I went through this, Jimmy, but I simply haven't the time at the moment to
sit down and apply myself to it blow by blow.

It seems to me you have built something that is very workable for you and
that can only be commended.

What I saw looks fine.

There is a lot more detail here than I would want to know, and it still
looks like maintaining source code to me, but it doesn't matter if you find
it useful.

I like simple, encapsulated functionality. That's why I use components.

If I had to solve your problem of huge amounts of input I would look at the
functions I want... say, check and validate numbers, set colours, display
back to the user (edit, if you like)... and so on, and then I would build a
little component "production line" that would take raw input from a text
field and end up with something usable internally, and an updated screen.
Once  each little "machine" was installed in the "production line", then it
is a fairly trivial exercise to ensure that each input field gets processed
by it. It's a bit like pushing scrap metal in one end and getting knives and
forks out the other. Each "machine" does a bit more towards the end product.
The "machines" themselves are very simple (like String2Num) and that usually
means they are fast...

As far as I am concerned, it isn't about selling components (although I do
intend to have a crack at that sometime this year), it is about simplifying
system development and eliminating source code maintenance.

I say it again...it is the Business Functionality we should be concentrating
on, not the intricacies of computer programming. It is because that HASN'T
happened that more and more users are doing it for themselves (without
"expert" computer programmers) and more and more Companies are outsourcing
their IT and focusing on their primary business instead.

Modern software and development techniques are empowering non technical
users to be able to build their own systems. This is the death knell for
us...(although it will be a few years yet before we are COMPLETELY redundant
<G>) and we can see the first effects of this sea change in lowered contract
rates and fewer jobs.

Ironically, I wrote around 6 years ago that "History will show 'computer
programming' was a phenomenon of the latter half of the 20th century." We
are certainly heading that way...

If nothing else, this exercise will have given you an idea of how
encapsulated packets can be made to work.

If you get any glimmers from it, then I consider my time well spent. <G>

Pete.

<snip of very comprehensive post>

In article <b8rv1r$p9a$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>,
Richard Plinston  <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>cease to be posted to, being considered ended forever.

Not *quite*, Mr Plinston... at least according to 
http://info.astrian.net/jargon/terms/g/godwin_s_law.html :

--begin quoted text:

Godwin's Law prov.  [Usenet] "As a Usenet discussion grows longer, the
probability of a comparison involving Nazis or Hitler approaches one."
There is a tradition in many groups that, once this occurs, that thread is
over, and whoever mentioned the Nazis has automatically lost whatever
argument was in progress. Godwin's Law thus practically guarantees the
existence of an upper bound on thread length in those groups. However
there is also a widely- recognized codicil that any intentional triggering
of Godwin's Law in order to invoke its thread-ending effects will be
unsuccessful.

--end quoted text

DD
```
