(...when I was young we had to walk 15 miles in a blizzard to go to 
work...uphill...both ways...still managed to be decent, good 
people...young whippersnappers...Gates...Jobs...no respect...no respect 
at all...)

Everything from shady accounting to outright swindling and theft have 
been part of the commercial landscape since commerce existed.  Much of 
the commercial world always has and continues to work in 'genteel' 
fashion -- are there not 17,000-odd publically traded companies in the 
US that DIDN'T do what E&W did?

The only thing that might have changed in the late 1970s is more news 
sources might have increased the volume about a few spectacular rip-offs 
that would have only been seen in the back pages of WSJ previously.

You have an interesting view of humanity, but what we do as individuals 
makes up the commercial world.  As the commercial world is nothing more 
than lots of individuals acting commercially.  It has no life of its own 
outside individual choices.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T21:59:03+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb0efe6_1@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea88130.21326254@news.optonline.net> <b8ab71$1dv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <b8eseu$2fv$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eab4d82.204732010@news.optonline.net> <3eab9ddb_1@127.0.0.1> <joe_zitzelberger-7C25B7.09273428042003@corp.supernews.com> <3eadb1d5_2@127.0.0.1> <3EADE6CB.8ECFF964@shaw.ca> <b3638c46.0304291812.6d7da321@posting.google.com> <b8onf0$ihq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:b8onf0$ihq$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 29-Apr-2003, dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood) wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Your "source code" is how you combine these components.  You do
maintenance on
> this "source code" when you add and replace components.

Only in YOUR mind, Howard.

I oversimplified the process in attempting to describe it to you and I
should not have done so. It is a different world.

It is a process unlike anything you are familiar with and that is why you
are desperately trying to make it into something you know and understand.

There is absolutely NO similarity between assembling components for a
solution and maintaining COBOL Source code.

The environment is different, the tools are different, the people doing it
are different, the process is infinitely different, the requirements to
carry out the process successfully are different, consequently, the skill
sets required are different. And the overall Methodology is different (RAD
vs the Waterfall).

The ONLY similarity is that at the end of it you have an application
processing computer system.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T14:30:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8rb2o$8lb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea88130.21326254@news.optonline.net> <b8ab71$1dv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <b8eseu$2fv$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eab4d82.204732010@news.optonline.net> <3eab9ddb_1@127.0.0.1> <joe_zitzelberger-7C25B7.09273428042003@corp.supernews.com> <3eadb1d5_2@127.0.0.1> <3EADE6CB.8ECFF964@shaw.ca> <b3638c46.0304291812.6d7da321@posting.google.com> <b8onf0$ihq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eb0efe6_1@127.0.0.1>`

```

On  1-May-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> > Your "source code" is how you combine these components.  You do
> maintenance on
> > this "source code" when you add and replace components.
>
> Only in YOUR mind, Howard.

It could be.

> I oversimplified the process in attempting to describe it to you and I
> should not have done so. It is a different world.

As is any non-programming build.   I was generalizing from other construction
outside of programming.   Everything that gets built either is a throwaway or
needs maintenance.   I am not limiting myself to computers, data processing, or
even business.  If it needs maintenance, it should be designed with this need in
mind.

> It is a process unlike anything you are familiar with and that is why you
> are desperately trying to make it into something you know and understand.

It could be.

> There is absolutely NO similarity between assembling components for a
> solution and maintaining COBOL Source code.

Interesting.   Building and maintaining a house has a great deal of similarity
to building and maintaining COBOL Source code.

> The environment is different, the tools are different, the people doing it
> are different, the process is infinitely different, the requirements to
> carry out the process successfully are different, consequently, the skill
> sets required are different. And the overall Methodology is different (RAD
> vs the Waterfall).

In building a house, the environment is different, the tools are different, the
people doing it sometimes are different, the process is not infinitely
different...    The need to consider maintenance is the same as making COBOL
programs.

> The ONLY similarity is that at the end of it you have an application
> processing computer system.

There are a lot more similarities between building a house and writing a CoBOL
program than this though.

Since I am not as familiar with building applications the way you are, I will
limit my statement:

Projects that will need to be maintained that I am familiar with, such as CoBOL
programming, Java programming, house building, family building, gardening,
country building, airplane building, and team building should be designed with
maintenance in mind.    OO programming at your level may be the singular
exception to this observation, or maybe there is some other exception I am
unaware of.
```

A good response, Howard. You may be right, that, in a broader sense "ease of
maintenance" should be a consideration. I was looking in the context of
maintaining procedural code, specifically, COBOL.

I have no problem with your last paragraph (quoted above), except that I am

here can't see the difference <G>).

Still, I believe the above is a good compromise we can both agree on.

Pete.

- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea88130.21326254@news.optonline.net> <b8ab71$1dv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <b8eseu$2fv$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eab4d82.204732010@news.optonline.net> <3eab9ddb_1@127.0.0.1> <joe_zitzelberger-7C25B7.09273428042003@corp.supernews.com> <3eadb1d5_2@127.0.0.1> <3EADE6CB.8ECFF964@shaw.ca> <b3638c46.0304291812.6d7da321@posting.google.com> <b8onf0$ihq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eb0efe6_1@127.0.0.1> <b8rb2o$8lb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eb1a57d_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
When the first code generators came out, they all seemed most lacking in the
ability to do maintenance.  Gradually they improved in this area, and as they
improved, they became more acceptable.

I have worked with generated code that almost did what I wanted.  I tweaked the
code and depending upon the generator, I could continue using the tool, or I was
done using the tool.

The former is useful, the latter was more trouble than it was worth.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T06:32:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb199d7.5232774@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ea88130.21326254@news.optonline.net> <b8ab71$1dv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <b8eseu$2fv$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eab4d82.204732010@news.optonline.net> <3eab9ddb_1@127.0.0.1> <joe_zitzelberger-7C25B7.09273428042003@corp.supernews.com> <3eadb1d5_2@127.0.0.1> <3EADE6CB.8ECFF964@shaw.ca> <b3638c46.0304291812.6d7da321@posting.google.com> <b8onf0$ihq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eb0efe6_1@127.0.0.1> <b8rb2o$8lb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:


>Projects that will need to be maintained that I am familiar with, such as CoBOL
>programming, Java programming, house building, family building, gardening,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>unaware of.

Airplane building is analogous to what Peter is talking about. When you build an
airplane, you don't design, build and maintain avionics at the board level. You
buy tested components and plug them in. Same with engines, radios, fuel tanks
and seats. If the component doesn't work right or needs to be changed, you
replace the whole component; you don't tinker with its internals.

Real aircraft engines are expensive, so they often are disassembled and rebuilt.
If they cost less than $100, like their software analogs, we wouldn't bother,
we'd discard and replace them. 

Designing a brand new airplane could be as simple as drawing an outline of the
fuselage (airframe) and wings, plugging in components, and test flying it on a
flight simulator. In fact, that's how airplanes ARE designed today. By the time
they get to real test flights, we already know how they are going to behave. If
there is a discrepency between actual and model, it is most likely an error in
the simulator. 

For instance, the SST acts differently depending on its center of gravity.
Expert pilots learned long ago, from expensive experience, how to balance the
load by moving fuel between tanks when transitioning from subsonic to
supersonic. Now that the dynamic is understood and incorporated into software,
we no longer need experts at the helm. The fuel transfer is automatic. The
upgrade was done by replacing software, which costs little, rather than
retraining pilots, which is expensive. 

In this analogy, you and I are the 'craftperson' pilot who's been superannuated.
We can embrace the change by learning OO, we can ignore it until we get
replaced, or we can find employment at second and third-tier providers, where
our role is maintaining 'the old stuff'. Rarely we find a fourth alternative:
new development. That's what I'm doing now and I love it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "We all agree that" (was: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T17:42:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb3de1f.78864759@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3EA55E79.6090702@newsguy.com> <3ea5ed65.19257366@news.optonline.net> <3EA69C04.7090208@newsguy.com> <3ea88130.21326254@news.optonline.net> <b8ab71$1dv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304261638.35e83817@posting.google.com> <3eabdf6c.242091596@news.optonline.net> <b8h9d7$v28$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <UvWqa.38198$q02.1338663@twister.austin.rr.com> <3eac6d8e.278483072@news.optonline.net> <b8jjiu$ehn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eaef386.443876034@news.optonline.net> <b8oo6v$ips$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 29-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
>> >It sounds like you are talking for performing what I think of as
subroutines.
>> >
>> >Or do you also include the basic
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >program logic?   If so, why should this be encapsulated?   It IS the
program.
>>
>> Does the system design say 'the function of program x is to read a file?' No,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Your code and mine would have the same number of lines, would do the same
thing,
>but paragraphs are named differently and ordered differently.   For a report, I
>kind of like yours, but for walking a database, I prefer the other direction.
>
>Kind of liking yours doesn't mean much though - I will probably use it where
>applicable, but won't evangelize it.

One difference is my second report program can REUSE the io module. I don't have
to rewrite SELECT, FD, OPEN, START, etc. When the file name changes, I make the
change in one place and both report programs 'inherit' it ... including a third
program I didn't know about because it's in Joe's private library.

The aesthetic difference is that your structure is upside-down .. to my way of
thinking. If the primary function of the program is to produce a report, I
prefer to see that as the outer structure or 'driving' logic. 

>> Note that the report 'driver' is at the top and reading a record at the
>> bottom.
>
>I don't see the significance of this.   You call it "one-report".  I call it
>"read-loop".   But the code's the same.

Programs should be designed top-down and programmed bottom-up. Your structure
has the technician, Mr. bottom-up, doing design work. 

I once wrote:

"If you're
not familiar with the bicameral brain
concept it says that you have dual
processors inside your head.  The left
brain deals with things;  the right
brain with people.  The left brain
solves problems from the bottom up;  the
right brain from the top down.  The left
brain is a technician;  the right brain
a designer ("design engineer" is an
oxymoron).  The left brain wants
evolution; the right brain revolution.
In most people, one or the other is
dominant.  In a minority, they're
equal.

  My hypothesis is that really good
programmers are "balanced" while the
hordes of average programmers are
left-brain dominant (a right-brain person
can't make it in the computer world, (s)he
usually becomes a manager).  The left
brain is a bad designer.  For examples
look at most system software.
The tendancy is to take a simple problem
and make it complicated.  The right brain
is a bad technician.  It wants simple
answers to complex problems.  For evidence,
just talk with any salesman.  I believe the key to
success is ambivalence.  The word commonly
means indecisive but to me it means fast alternation
between left and right. Doing so requires
internal harmony. The left and right brains must
cooperate rather than compete for control."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "We all agree that" (was: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T11:54:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb104ec.579422921@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3EA69C04.7090208@newsguy.com> <3ea88130.21326254@news.optonline.net> <b8ab71$1dv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304261638.35e83817@posting.google.com> <3eabdf6c.242091596@news.optonline.net> <b8h9d7$v28$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <UvWqa.38198$q02.1338663@twister.austin.rr.com> <3eac6d8e.278483072@news.optonline.net> <b8jjp0$eiq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eaf0012.447088304@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304301207.1201c0c9@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>In another thread you criticised [all] programmers for being vauge in
>using the term subroutine.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>replace the complete expression with a reference to the function.

Your argument about the word function is nearly identical to mine about integer.
You want to use the math definition. After you rejected my argument, why do you
now expect me to accept yours? 

You will point to the COBOL standard's definition of function. Ok, we'll use
that when discussing COBOL. But my usage is consistant with common usage in
other languages. A C function returning void is form over substance. It is not a
statement about the relationship between variables. Further, the receiving field
need not even be coded. Permitting a programming function to modify its
arguments is inconsistent with the math definition as well. 

>Previously you had called 'nested programs' as 'user functions' when
>they are not functions.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>dictionaries and text books and change the programming language.

It is common usage in other programming languages. 

>> Sideways means disguising (deferring) the explicit call by temporarily
storing
>> it as data. It's really not data, it's a logical state. 
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>a series of blocks of code.

Yes, we're trying to package a three-dimensional problem into two-dimensional
code (written in a one-dimensional language). One solution is to make the blocks
of code out of line functions:

IF  FUNCTION a() AND FUNCTION b() AND FUNCTION c()
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "We all agree that" (was: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T14:48:14-05:00

news:3eb104ec.579422921@news.optonline.net...
> riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

<snip>

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Mr. Wagner,
  If you started using COBOL terminology (for "function" "Integer" etc) for
*ALL* of your posts to this COBOL newsgroup *except* when referring to
environments where you are NOT talking about COBOL, then I think this would
help you significantly in THIS newsgroup.  Then if you think about the
"relevance" of your post (that doesn't have to do with COBOL) before sending
the post that uses the terminology in its non-COBOL sense, then this would
be even more useful.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "We all agree that" (was: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T14:21:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305011321.42f5d297@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304261638.35e83817@posting.google.com> <3eabdf6c.242091596@news.optonline.net> <b8h9d7$v28$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <UvWqa.38198$q02.1338663@twister.austin.rr.com> <3eac6d8e.278483072@news.optonline.net> <b8jjp0$eiq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eaf0012.447088304@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304301207.1201c0c9@posting.google.com> <3eb104ec.579422921@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >As an example of a function we can take sin().  It would be possible
> >to express this as an expression whenever we wanted to calculate this
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Your argument about the word function is nearly identical to mine about integer.

No. That is not true. Your 'definition' of integer is _not_ based on
maths, you have mistakenly supported some nonsense about having to add
1 by using a definition of something completely different.

The _actual_ definition of integer in maths, common usage, and as used
in programming is entirely consistent.  It is only your own unique
'definition' that is discordant.

I know that you have an emotional attachment to your creation because
it is the foundation for a very silly argument about the Cobol
standard.

> You want to use the math definition. After you rejected my argument, why do you
> now expect me to accept yours? 

Yes, I know it seems rather ambitious to expect you to accept anything
you haven't made up yourself.

But you may note that I said the programming takes its meaning from
maths, not that it used the maths definition.

> You will point to the COBOL standard's definition of function. Ok, we'll use
> that when discussing COBOL. But my usage is consistant with common usage in
> other languages. 

No,. It is only 'consistent' with your understanding, which is a
different thing.

> A C function returning void is form over substance. 

A void function _doesn't_ return anything, you misunderstand void in
that context.

> It is not a
> statement about the relationship between variables. 

No, and nor is it a 'function' in any meaningful way.  It is simply
that C makes no formal distinction at all between 'function' and
'procedure'.  This does not mean that there is no distinction.

> Further, the receiving field need not even be coded. 

That is correct the C compiler does not care if you discard values. 
This is the result of C not having an assignment statement.

> Permitting a programming function to modify its
> arguments is inconsistent with the math definition as well. 

The const qualifier can be used to ensure that a parameter is not
modified.  Programming correctly relies on the programmer.

> It is common usage in other programming languages. 

Which 'other programming languages' were you imaginging here ?

Pascal makes a firm distinction between FUNCTION and PROCEDURE, in
exactly the way I describe.

BASIC has its DEF FN and GOSUB.

FORTRAN has FUNCTION and SUBROUTINE 

COBOL has FUNCTION (as in Intrinsic function) and PROGRAM (as in
PROGRAM-ID .. END-PROGRAM).

You used the term FUNCTION to be 'any out of line code that can be
performed called or invoked .. or even in-line'.  This is _not_ how
the term is used in _any_ programming language, especially Cobol.

The function may be 'invoked' in an expression. 

In C a statement of the form 'function_name(..);' _is_ an expression.

You criticised 'programmers' for vague use of subroutine but it is
_this_ term that you wrongly substitute 'function' for.

> Yes, we're trying to package a three-dimensional problem into two-dimensional
> code (written in a one-dimensional language). One solution is to make the blocks
> of code out of line functions:
> 
> IF  FUNCTION a() AND FUNCTION b() AND FUNCTION c()

Which can't be done in any current implementation of Cobol that I know
of, except with intrinsic functions of course.

At least you have used the term function correctly here and invoked
them.

The objection was that your claim included:

   "performed"   - which makes the code a procedure, not a function
   "called"      - which makes the code a subprogram, not a function
   "invoked"     - which may be a method
   "inline"      - which makes it, well, code, and not a function

The generic term is 'subroutine', the one you didn't like.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "We all agree that" (was: Sets and subsets

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Your argument about the word function is nearly identical to mine about
integer.
>
>No. That is not true. Your 'definition' of integer is _not_ based on
>maths, you have mistakenly supported some nonsense about having to add
>1 by using a definition of something completely different.

You're full of it. It isn't _my_ definition, it is based on set theory, and it
isn't nonsense. 

>The _actual_ definition of integer in maths, common usage, and as used
>in programming is entirely consistent.  It is only your own unique
>'definition' that is discordant.

Trying to personalize it to make me seem 'discordant' is an rhetorical trick.
What I believe is unimportant. Look at the mathematical definition, which is
quite rigorous. 

>I know that you have an emotional attachment to your creation because
>it is the foundation for a very silly argument about the Cobol
>standard.

There you go again with assination by adjective .. emotional and silly. If you
want to make these points, you have to substantiate them, not just assert them
as adjectives. 

>> You want to use the math definition. After you rejected my argument, why do
you
>> now expect me to accept yours? 
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>This is the result of C not having an assignment statement.

Huh? The vast majority of C statements are assignment. 

>> Permitting a programming function to modify its
>> arguments is inconsistent with the math definition as well. 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Which 'other programming languages' were you imagining here ?

COBOL has no such protection except BY VALUE. I cannot imagine pushing a 1M
table onto the stack. 

>Pascal makes a firm distinction between FUNCTION and PROCEDURE, in
>exactly the way I describe.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>_this_ term that you wrongly substitute 'function' for.

'Subroutine' is a term from the '60s. 'Function' is from the '80s. What's the
modern equivalent?
```

- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304261638.35e83817@posting.google.com> <3eabdf6c.242091596@news.optonline.net> <b8h9d7$v28$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <UvWqa.38198$q02.1338663@twister.austin.rr.com> <3eac6d8e.278483072@news.optonline.net> <b8jjp0$eiq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eaf0012.447088304@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304301207.1201c0c9@posting.google.com> <3eb104ec.579422921@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305011321.42f5d297@posting.google.com> <3eb30c24.25102579@news.optonline.net>`

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eb30c24.25102579@news.optonline.net...
> riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
> >robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
> >
<snip>
> >
> >No. That is not true. Your 'definition' of integer is _not_ based on
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> You're full of it. It isn't _my_ definition, it is based on set theory,
and it
> isn't nonsense.
>

One more time (with feeling), NO ONE disagrees with about the definition of
what the

   "set of all integers"

means.  However, you keep missing the fact that every member of that set is
an inidividual integer.  Any set (which is finite - and no matter how
described) which is made up ONLY of members of the set of all integers is
ALSO a (sub)set composed of integer members.

It's a simple fact.
  It is true in COBOL, in set theory (which explicitly defines "proper
subsets") and it is true in "normal" usage.

Admit your error and you will find others may actually start accepting those
of your ideas that DO have value.
```

> Any set (which is finite - and no matter how described)

This is not true.  Sets can easily be infinite, countably or
not.
http://mathworld.wolfram.com/CountablyInfinite.html

> Admit your error and you will find others may actually start
accepting those
> of your ideas that DO have value.

It's entirely possible that I'm missing some context here, so
if my remark is not relevant, I apologize.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "We all agree that" (was: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T23:03:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b923e1$rf5$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304261638.35e83817@posting.google.com> <3eabdf6c.242091596@news.optonline.net> <b8h9d7$v28$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <UvWqa.38198$q02.1338663@twister.austin.rr.com> <3eac6d8e.278483072@news.optonline.net> <b8jjp0$eiq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eaf0012.447088304@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304301207.1201c0c9@posting.google.com> <3eb104ec.579422921@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305011321.42f5d297@posting.google.com> <3eb30c24.25102579@news.optonline.net> <b8vg62$8es$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <b918j005oh@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
No fuel added, you just missed my point which was that although the set of
ALL integers is infinite, it is also true that (here comes the quoted text)

    Any set (which is finite - and no matter how described)

may include only members that are ALSO members of the infinite set of all
integers, will include members that are integers and remain so - even though
this proper subset of all integers does not, itself, include all integers.

Does that clarify the context?
```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b923e1$rf5$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net...
> No fuel added, you just missed my point which was that
although the set of
> ALL integers is infinite, it is also true that (here comes
the quoted text)
>
>     Any set (which is finite - and no matter how described)
>
> may include only members that are ALSO members of the
infinite set of all
> integers, will include members that are integers and remain
so - even though
> this proper subset of all integers does not, itself, include
all integers.
>
> Does that clarify the context?

I read the parenthetical part as qualifying the generic
definition of set -- not a particular condition used to qualify
what type of set you're talking about.  Ghosts of an earlier
day remind me of hours spent trying to massage countably
infinite sets into finite ones -- that must be what clouded my
visor.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "We all agree that" (was: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T10:20:54-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b90j86$8of$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eb104ec.579422921@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305011321.42f5d297@posting.google.com> <3eb30c24.25102579@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3eb30c24.25102579@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>quite rigorous. 

The mathematical definition, Mr Wagner, such as 'The positive integers are 
the numbers 1, 2, 3, ... (Sloane's A000027), sometimes called the counting 
numbers or natural numbers'?  Or 'The integers, or whole numbers, consist 
of the natural numbers (0, 1, 2, ...) and the negative whole numbers (-1, 
-2, -3, ...)'?  Or 'Usually, integers are numbers from the sequence ..., 
-2, -1, 0, 1, 2, ..., positive members of which are often called counting 
or natural numbers'?

These are all definitions from the sources you gave, Mr Wagner, and they
apply perfectly to numbers like twenty thousand, three hundred sixty-five
(20365) or twenty million, thirty thousand, three hundred twenty-nine
(20030329).  Looks like those dates *are* integers... didn't someone here
say just recently that to deny something in the face of facts is just
'stupid'?

DD
```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eb30c24.25102579@news.optonline.net...
> riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> You're full of it. It isn't _my_ definition, it is based on set theory,
and it
> isn't nonsense.

The word integer came into usage in 1571.
< http://www.m-w.com/cgi-bin/dictionary >

Set theory was founded about 300 years later by Cantor.

Cantor, Georg (Ferdinand Ludwig Philipp)
born March 3, 1845 , St. Petersburg, Russia
died Jan. 6, 1918 , Halle, Ger.

German mathematician who founded set theory and introduced
the mathematically meaningful concept of transfinite numbers,
indefinitely large but distinct from one another.

"Cantor, Georg" Encyclop�dia Britannica  from Encyclop�dia
Britannica Premium Service.
<http://www.britannica.com/eb/article?eu=20386>
[Accessed May 3, 2003].

Set theory relies on the definition of integer that existed prior
to its founding.

To claim that set theory defines integer to mean something
other than that upon which set theory relies is absurd.


[snip]
> >> You will point to the COBOL standard's definition of function. Ok,
we'll use
> >> that when discussing COBOL. But my usage is consistant with common
usage in
> >> other languages.
> >
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Huh? The vast majority of C statements are assignment.

C has an 'assignment expression' and an 'expression statement'.

An assignment expression may appear where an expression
may appear. An expression statement may consist of an
expression that is not an assignment expression.

Where an assignment expression appears within an expression
statement, this may appear to be an assignment statement as
it is understood in other languages; but C has no assignment
statement.

> >> Permitting a programming function to modify its
> >> arguments is inconsistent with the math definition as well.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL has no such protection except BY VALUE. I cannot imagine pushing a
1M
> table onto the stack.

COBOL also has BY CONTENT.

> >Pascal makes a firm distinction between FUNCTION and PROCEDURE, in
> >exactly the way I describe.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'Subroutine' is a term from the '60s. 'Function' is from the '80s. What's
the
> modern equivalent?

You may be interested in the definition of procedure from:
< http://www.ncits.org/tc_home/k5htm/p3.htm#procedure >.

"A subprogram that does not return a data value, except as
part of the parameter mechanism. In COBOL, a procedure
is a paragraph, or a group of logically successive paragraphs,
or a section (consisting of zero or more paragraphs) within
the procedure division. In some programming languages
(i.e., C and C++), the procedure language construct is not
differentiated from the function language construct except that
returned data values may be void or not used. Synonymous
with subroutine."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "We all agree that" (was: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T11:12:25+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b91ief$a99$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0305011321.42f5d297@posting.google.com> <3eb30c24.25102579@news.optonline.net> <vb88uh5f7dja38@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message

> >This is the result of C not having an assignment statement.
>
> Huh? The vast majority of C statements are assignment.

In C there is _no_ assignment statement.  Assignment is an operator which 
may, or may not, be used in an expression, or may be used several times 
just like any other operator.

> >FORTRAN has FUNCTION and SUBROUTINE

> 'Subroutine' is a term from the '60s. 'Function' is from the '80s. What's
> the modern equivalent?

FORTRAN, from the 50s has both FUNCTION and SUBROUTINE.  Your assertions 
about dates is quite wrong.  This also confirms that they are different 
enough to use distinct keywords for, and disproves your 'as found in other 
languages'.

In fact the usage in FORTRAN follows common usage that existed when the 
language was developed.

Now it may well be that you never heard the term 'function' prior to the 
80s and then made up the idea that it was a 'new' term for subroutine. You 
probably feel 'modern' in using it, but you use it wrongly.
```

> You didn't cite the definition of integer before set theory. I don't know
what
> it was nor, apparently, do you.
>
> I tend to think set theory didn't change the definition, rather it
formalized
> the definition.

The following quotes are from:
Morris Kline, "MATHEMATICS, HISTORY OF"
Copyright � 1996 P. F. Collier, L. P.  All rights reserved.

The first "systematic, clear, and comprehensive treatment
of the integer and ratios of integers" was "'Introductio Arithmetica'
of Nichomachus (c. A.D. 100)."

"The earliest known use of negative numbers was by
Brahmagupta around A.D. 630."

"Mahavira (A.D. 850) stated rules for operating with zero ...."

"The legitimacy of negative numbers was still disputed in the
16th century."

Aristotle (c. 384 - 322 B.C.) contributed to the concept of infinity
and Euclid (about 300 B.C.) used infinity.

Thus, by the time the word 'integer' came into usage in 1571,
all that was required to understand "the set of all integers"
was understood.

Number theory is the branch of mathematics concerned with
the integers, or whole numbers, and generalizations of the
integers. [Encyclop�dia Britannica]

Number theory appears to have preceeded set theory by
about 100 years.

> >You may be interested in the definition of procedure from:
> >< http://www.ncits.org/tc_home/k5htm/p3.htm#procedure >.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mainframers will be delighted to hear someone supports them. Who is the
author
> and what are his bona fides?

The information is supplied at
< http://www.ncits.org/tc_home/k5htm/Foreword.htm >
which states, in part,

"Under the direction of the American National Standards
Institute (ANSI) � National Committee for Information
Technology Standards (NCITS), the Technical Committee
on Vocabulary, K5, prepared this American National
Standard Dictionary of Information Technology (ANSDIT).
The dictionary was developed by studying the use of terms
throughout the field of information technology, including
computers, data communications, data processing, text
processing, artificial intelligence, virtual reality and related
fields. The ANSDIT has been harmonized with ISO/IEC-2382,
Information Technology � Vocabulary (ITV), for which
Technical Committee NCITS/K5 served as the technical
advisory group for the American National Standards Institute."

This is followed by a list of the members of K5.
```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eb5864e.85282383@news.optonline.net...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> According to this document, a subroutine in COBOL is a paragraph, not a
called
> program.

A procedure (aka, subroutine) may be a single paragraph;
but not always. The COBOL standard definition obviously
'influenced' the ANSDIT definition of 'procedure'; though
the ANSDIT description is not accurate.

ISO/IEC 1989:2002, FDIS, page 381, 14.3 Procedures,
"A procedure is composed of a paragraph, or a group of
successive paragraphs, or a section, or a group of
successive sections within the procedure division."

Maybe J4 should send a note to K5! <g>
```

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> you that is NOT the case.
This was true on COBOL D for DOS 360 Release 26 where the ALTER modified
a branch instruction (B PARA or hex 47F0xxxx) but later versions would
have the ALTER replacing an ADCON for a load and branch (L R15,ADDRPARA
followed by BR R15  hex 58F0xxxx07FF).  Thus for re-entrant programs it
would be modifying something in a working area that had been initialized
with the original branching address constants.  I say this as someone
who has read generated code for all of the IBM COBOL compilers he worked
with.  It was quite disconcerting to work with Microfocus COBOL on HP-UX
and not have the foggiest notion of either the generated code or the
instruction set.  
> 
> Although my Intel Assembler is somewhat rusty, I wouldn't mind betting that
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> ---= 19 East/West-Coast Specialized Servers - Total Privacy via Encryption =---
```

Thanks Clark.

My experience on mainframes is rapidly getting outdated (I haven't been near
one for around 12 years now).

I appreciate the explanation and hadn't thought about it being possibloe by
the use of ADCONs.

I guess I'll have to modify future posts with "In the old days..." <G>


Pete.
"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
news:3EC23D3F.95FF8B66@istar.ca...

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

that

> >
> > A protection exception would only apply to storage OUTSIDE the loaded

of
> > computing. I once wrote a program that used ONLY "AND" "OR" "XOR" and
> > "NEGATE" instructions. It read paper tape, processed the input, wrote it
to

did

protection
> > was violated. I did it for a bet and to show that at the machine
language
> > level the only thing that matters is binary. (The program was written in
> > PLAN for an ICL 1900).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T10:35:51+00:00

>

dates

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8rbaj$8ls$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eade498.374507313@news.optonline.net> <b8m4jb$b5s$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eafeef5.508253065@news.optonline.net> <b8p63e$o6s$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb0f1c3.574516924@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  1-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Suppose ADD worked most of the time, but every so often .. say one time in a
> million .. produced an invalid result. Would you say that it 'works'? Would
> you use it in your programs?

It depends on how it fails.   I have had code fail with an ADD or COMPUTE
statement.   If I know about the failure and can handle it, or even if the job
aborts, that's acceptable.   If it creates a wrong answer that I don't know
about it, I wouldn't use it.
```

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3eb0f1c3.574516924@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>>>Yeah, right. As in ADD 1 TO date-integer? You can do that in SQL (which
uses
>>>>>>Julian dates), but not in COBOL. 
>>>>>
…[66 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>I cannot speak for you but in my logic there is an unstated proposition
(axiom
>>>>in math) that an operation such as adding 1 must work in ALL cases to be
>>'valid'
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>>million .. produced an invalid result. Would you say that it 'works'? Would
you
>>use it in your programs?
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>source for the definition of the term that you are applying.

Given your propensity for doing 'research' in the dictionary, I went to
Merriam-Webster:

work (verb)

2 a : to fashion or create a useful or desired product by expending labor or
exertion on 

5 : to solve (a problem) by reasoning or calculation 

intransitive

4 : to produce a desired effect or result : SUCCEED
-------------------------------------------------
The date 20021232 is not a "useful or desired product", does not "effectively
solve a problem", nor does it "succeed."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[131 more quoted lines elided]…
>solve a problem", nor does it "succeed." 

Mr Wagner, it was never stated that the integer 2001232 was any of 
those... what *was* stated above, and re-quoted, was:

--begin quoted text:

Adding 1 to 20030430 gives 20030431; this is not an integer representation 
of a valid date.  As in the example above, one instance of addition 
generates an integer representation of a valid date, one does not.  
Again, sometimes one can generate an integer representation of a valid 
cannot.

If 'sometimes one can' is true then to say without qualification (as has
been your assertion) that 'one cannot' is false.

--end quoted text

Are you trying to change the assertion that you made that one cannot 
perform arithmetic operations on integer dates, now that it has been 
shown, clearly and unambiguously, that under certain conditions one can 
obtain a valid integer representation of a date by doing so?


DD
```

      01  My-Number       PIC S9(6).

      MOVE ZERO       TO My-Number
      PERFORM 1000000 TIMES
          ADD 1       TO My-Number
      END-PERFORM

Well, what do you know, one time in a million I get a wrong answer.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Nothing to do with Sets and subsets (was: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T17:44:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8s80g$ha9$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eade498.374507313@news.optonline.net> <b8m4jb$b5s$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eafeef5.508253065@news.optonline.net> <b8p63e$o6s$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb0f1c3.574516924@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305011401.6d0f1056@posting.google.com>`

```
Well, ....

       01  My-Number       PIC S9(6).

   MOVE ZERO       TO My-Number
       PERFORM 1000000 TIMES
           ADD 1       TO My-Number
                On Size Error
                      Perform Too-Big
           End-Add
       END-PERFORM

 Well, what do you know, ZERO times in a million I get a wrong answer.

    ***

Similarly - in the 2002 Standard

     01  Integer-YYYYMMDD       PIC 9(8).

   MOVE NumVal  (Function Current-Date (1:8)) TO Integer-YYYYMMDD
   ADD 1       TO Integer-YYYYMMDD
   If Function TEST-DATE-YYYYMMDD (Integer-YYYYMMDD) =  0
             Perform Add-1-valid-date
   Else
            Perform ADD-1-Invalid-Date
   End-IF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nothing to do with Sets and subsets (was: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T10:30:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8u9v0$jl0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eade498.374507313@news.optonline.net> <b8m4jb$b5s$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eafeef5.508253065@news.optonline.net> <b8p63e$o6s$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb0f1c3.574516924@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305011401.6d0f1056@posting.google.com> <b8s80g$ha9$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3eb1b698_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
I agree.

But Bill may have added an unnecessary complication in his second example.
The results of CURRENT-DATE are well defined, as are the results of a MOVE
from an eight-character alphanumeric elementary item to an eight-character
numeric elementary item ("when the sending operand is described as being
alphanumeric, data is moved as if the sending operand were described as an
unsigned numeric integer.")  Since we know CURRENT-DATE(1:8} contains
nothing but digits in USAGE DISPLAY form, the NUMVAL is not needed.

Thus, I think the original extraction of the current date via
    MOVE NUMVAL (Function CURRENT-DATE (1:8)) TO Integer-YYYYMMDD
could reasonably be simplified to
    MOVE CURRENT-DATE (1:8) TO Integer-YYYYMMDD
without any loss of reliability or accuracy.

I believe the FUNCTION keyword is optional for intrinsic functions and for
any user-defined functions declared in the REPOSITORY paragraph in COBOL
2002.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

news:b8u9v0$jl0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
> I believe the FUNCTION keyword is optional for intrinsic functions and for
> any user-defined functions declared in the REPOSITORY paragraph in COBOL

OmiGod! NOOOOoooooooooooooo!!!!

As many (most?) of us have only been using COBOL functions for a few years
and are still geting used to the idea, I propose that the installation
standards REQUIRE that the word FUNCTION be included when they are used.
(This will be carved in stone and observed without exception....No more
Mister Nice Guy...)

Anyone violating this will have red hot needles shoved under their
fingernails, then be escorted from the premises with their name being added
to every blacklist in town. "You'll never work again in this town...your
career is over."

Pete.

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 30-Apr-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>But at least one of those statements is something you don't agree with.
Either
>you don't think it should be part of a standard (my argument), or you think it
>can actually be harmful.

>So what do you talk about on this newsgroup?   Everything that you agree with?
>Or whatever you disagree with?

Good point. 

I might propose a compromise -- I'll use periods in exchange for adding a spell
checker to the precompiler. If my boss demurred, he'd look like a hypocrite.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Go To" and "Structured programming"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T10:35:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb0d452.566979012@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b8if76$gdc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3eadb031.361090998@news.optonline.net> <b8lvl7$b5c$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eaf1a9b_2@127.0.0.1> <b8na20$roe$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:


>The exploration is a simple one.  A spec is issued.  The programmer speaks 
>of concerns and these concerns receive a fair, impartial and dispassionate 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>for a certain set of conditions.

Your description is discrepant with reality in the world of formal methodology.
You are describing military and small company environments where methodology is
absent, so order comes from social/management strata. 

One might ask why even write a programming spec? Some organizations don't. There
are two reasons: 

1. Training for junior programmers.
 
2. The client has old-timers who might say, 'How can you expect them to get it
right without programming specs? Harumph. In my day we always had clear marching
orders and rules of engagement.' So we produce something that looks like a
programming spec to appease them. Programming specs are unimportant within the
methedology model,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Go To" and "Structured programming"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T14:07:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8r9nt$7u2$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b8if76$gdc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3eadb031.361090998@news.optonline.net> <b8lvl7$b5c$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eaf1a9b_2@127.0.0.1> <b8na20$roe$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb0d452.566979012@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  1-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >The exploration is a simple one.  A spec is issued.  The programmer speaks
> >of concerns and these concerns receive a fair, impartial and dispassionate
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> is absent, so order comes from social/management strata.

Every shop I have worked in with a formal methodology has had strict rules for
coding.   Most every one has at least one rule that I disagreed with.  My
current shop has at least two rules that you disagree with.   A formal
methodology doesn't mean that you won't have explicit instructions to code
something that you don't believe is proper.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Go To" and "Structured programming"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T10:27:44+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb19f64_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b8if76$gdc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3eadb031.361090998@news.optonline.net> <b8lvl7$b5c$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eaf1a9b_2@127.0.0.1> <b8na20$roe$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb0d452.566979012@news.optonline.net> <b8r9nt$7u2$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:b8r9nt$7u2$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  1-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
>
> Every shop I have worked in with a formal methodology has had strict rules
for
> coding.

Interesting.

How strict?

Like, if I code a GO TO do I get fired?

Who polices it?

At what point? While I am still developing it? Or, by review after it has
gone live? If it is successfully working in production, will they pull it
back and demand the offending GO TO be removed, in order to comply with the
standard? (see "How strict?", above)

I ask these questions because, over the years, I have been responsible for
the implementation of style based standards in a number of shops. My
experience has been that the adoption of a strict authoritarian approach is
counter productive and simply lowers morale and stifles enthusiasm.

The best way to implement coding standards is by discussion and consensus.
They should never be "carved in stone". If the team find that a particular
standard hampers their work it should be reviewed. I have used examples, and
encouragement towards a sensible set of standards (rather than "You WILL do
this. It is mandatory on this site"), always recognising that style is a
matter of preference, but we need to standardise the approach to facilitate
maintaining each other's work.

I also believe that an Annexe to the standards called "Useful code examples"
is an essential part of any shop standard. Anyone on the team who has
developed a method for doing a fairly common task should be encouraged to
review their approach with a senior programmer and then the code can be
posted to this annexe (and placed on the system as a COPY book, if
appropriate...).


>  Most every one has at least one rule that I disagreed with.  My
> current shop has at least two rules that you disagree with.   A formal
> methodology doesn't mean that you won't have explicit instructions to code
> something that you don't believe is proper.

Yep, there is usually something that someone is not comfortable with.
However, if the pros and cons are openly discussed and everyone gets a
chance to make their objections known, then at least they work with it on
the understanding that the majority of the team think it is a good thing.

As is a matter of record here <G>, I actually encourage dissent (for a
limited period, and under specific circumstances) as it is a good way to
cause all concerned to re-evaluate their agreements or objections. However,
once we have decided that something is "in", then there is no place for
smouldering resentment or mutterings under the breath...<G> You win some;
you lose some. I have worked, and coded in compliance with, standards which
I personally thought were clumsy and unwieldy, but I had my chance to make a
case and failed to persuade my colleagues.

The overall morale of the shop is MUCH more important than the autocratic
insistence on "Holy Writ".

Standards are better implemented as guidelines, with the site priorities
spelled out on page one. (For example, is it more important to comply with
the standard than for the code to be efficient? Does the fact that code
works, outweigh the fact that it may have minor standard violations in it?
Etc.)

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Go To" and "Structured programming"

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T14:16:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8tuka$i10$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b8if76$gdc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3eadb031.361090998@news.optonline.net> <b8lvl7$b5c$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eaf1a9b_2@127.0.0.1> <b8na20$roe$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb0d452.566979012@news.optonline.net> <b8r9nt$7u2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eb19f64_2@127.0.0.1>`

```

On  1-May-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> Interesting.
>
> How strict?

It varies.

> Like, if I code a GO TO do I get fired?

Not immediately.   But oddly enough I don't recall that being an enforced
standard.

> Who polices it?

The most strict standards checking is done by whomever migrates the job to
production.   These checks are generally according to guidelines that don't
really matter much to the way the code works.   But they're easy to check.

"Easy to check" seems to be the most important criterion.

I forgot how EDS enforced their requirement about exit paragraphs.   But it was
a requirement, and we followed it.


> I ask these questions because, over the years, I have been responsible for
> the implementation of style based standards in a number of shops. My
> experience has been that the adoption of a strict authoritarian approach is
> counter productive and simply lowers morale and stifles enthusiasm.

I mostly agree.  I have seen shops that required that sorts be either internal
or external (depending on the shop).   If there was a real good reason for going
against the standard, you could appeal your design.

But when a shop said "Your JCL should use these sort work files", I told them
that this is all done dynamically now - is anybody authorized to change this
standard.   Nobody was, so I followed their standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Go To" and "Structured programming"

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T03:03:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb2fc7e.21095380@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b8if76$gdc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3eadb031.361090998@news.optonline.net> <b8lvl7$b5c$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eaf1a9b_2@127.0.0.1> <b8na20$roe$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb0d452.566979012@news.optonline.net> <b8r9nt$7u2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eb19f64_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

I too have established style standards at a number of shops ..about 7. My
technique is based primarily on personal interaction rather than written rules. 

I start with a one page document which talks about high level principles and
objectives.  

1.  I deliberately rotate assignments so the programmer assigned to work on a
program today is never the same one who worked on it last time. I make it clear
that if you are writing new code, when it needs maintenance three months hence,
someone else will be assigned to do it. No program 'belongs' to a programmer;
the whole program library is team property. 

Within a few months, a consensus style emerges. You cannot tell from looking at
a program who wrote it. They all look the same, stylistically. Not because
someone shoved a rule book down their throats; because they developed the style
themselves. 

A standing rule says everyone has permission to rewrite a section of code purely
to improve its style. Of course, he must retest that section if the change is
more than trivial. This is contrary to the policy in most shops, which prohibits
changes that are not relevant to the assignment. It is supported by my high
level objective saying we want the highest quality source code that we're
capable of producing. 

None of the de-facto standards were written down. I didn't want them to be for
fear they'd become immutable. Standards should be permitted to evolve. 

2. I put in place a precompiler that was  mandatory for both test and
production. It did two things. It beautified code by lining up pics, doing
indentation, etc. Those things are trivial. Its second function and real reason
for being was to grade each program for quality. It inserted the grade as a
comment in identification division. I don't say a word about it until they ask. 

Programmers soon asked what the criteria were. I'd tell them it's a measure of
expressiveness and lack of redundancy. Expressiveness is a function of average
data name size (on some kind of log scale, I don't remember the details);
redundancy is measured by assigning points to verbs. Those which create new
information, either data or logical state, get the highest points -- if,
compute, etc. Those which merely shuffle data or control around -- move, perform
-- get medium-low points. Verbs I don't like -- go to, more than one goback --
get zero points. The average verb score goes into the program grade. 

Within weeks, all programs are scoring 100. Again, not because I shoved precepts
down their throats but because I appealed to their sense of pride. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Go To" and "Structured programming"

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eb2fc7e.21095380@news.optonline.net...
<snip>

This sounded to me like a very effective approach, Robert.

You are right that it could only work with the right people and, sometimes,
we just don't have the luxury of getting the people we would like.

Nevertheless, a less than Authoritarian approach will bring out the best in
most people.

I was kind of amused that you would go so far as to rate code by the use of
verbs, but it is an interesting idea.

I wouldn't do it myself because it smacks of covert appraisal and I believe
that appraisals should be overt, but, as you found it to be useful, I won't
make judgements one way or the other.

Pete.

On  2-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Programmers soon asked what the criteria were. I'd tell them it's a measure of
> expressiveness and lack of redundancy. Expressiveness is a function of average
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> down their throats but because I appealed to their sense of pride.

I liked everything you said until this quote.   Programs such as this don't
measure quality of the program, except as how the program should fit a
particular style.   Once people know what the objective is, they can easily
change their style to what they think their boss wants.   That doesn't mean they
have pride in having a better program, it means they want to look good when
evaluations come up.   Do they really believe that changing the data name size
to the pre-determined optimum makes the program better?
```

of

average
>> data name size (on some kind of log scale, I don't remember the details);
>> redundancy is measured by assigning points to verbs. Those which create new
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> -- get medium-low points. Verbs I don't like -- go to, more than one goback

On  6-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> I would have preferred to use a spelling cheeker, but didn't have one at the
> time. The objective was to get rid of abbreviations such as DP, DPT and DEPT.
> They don't save time, they slow you down because they make you look at the
> data description to see how it is spelled *this* time

I've never seen this problem eliminated entirely.  The trouble is, once you buy
some software, we are stuck with multiple standards.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Go To" and "Structured programming"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T06:32:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb1f3a3.28224158@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b8if76$gdc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3eadb031.361090998@news.optonline.net> <b8lvl7$b5c$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eaf1a9b_2@127.0.0.1> <b8na20$roe$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb0d452.566979012@news.optonline.net> <b8r9nt$7u2$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On  1-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>something that you don't believe is proper. 

Those 'shop coding standards' aren't born of modern methodology. They're a
holdover from the past , when they, along with code walkthrus, were all the
quality control we had. They are enforced by Team Leaders who usually have 3-5
years of programming experience. 

Four out of five places where I worked recently did not enforce them. They were
enforced in one shop where COBOL was the primary language; they were not
enforced when the Team Leader didn't know COBOL well. In the one COBOL shop,
Team Leaders supplemented (or contervened) the published standard with their
personal preference.
  
Modern methodologies are oriented toward examining external results. Old
attempts at methodology, designed by programmers, looked inward, thinking
quality could be achieved by self-discipline.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Go To" and "Structured programming"

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T14:23:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8tv0e$i8c$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b8if76$gdc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3eadb031.361090998@news.optonline.net> <b8lvl7$b5c$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eaf1a9b_2@127.0.0.1> <b8na20$roe$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb0d452.566979012@news.optonline.net> <b8r9nt$7u2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eb1f3a3.28224158@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  2-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Those 'shop coding standards' aren't born of modern methodology. They're a
> holdover from the past , when they, along with code walkthrus, were all the
> quality control we had. They are enforced by Team Leaders who usually have 3-5
> years of programming experience.

In my experience, team leaders aren't the enforcers, nor do they have that
limited programming experience.  But my experience quite often disagrees with
the whole world as described by you.


> Modern methodologies are oriented toward examining external results. Old
> attempts at methodology, designed by programmers, looked inward, thinking
> quality could be achieved by self-discipline.

There are some advantages in following a common style in a shop.   Certainly
your "best practices" document supports that people should follow your style.  
If you were king, I suspect you would strongly recommend that your programmers
follow your standards.

And certainly there are practices that we have learned are more prone to
troubles than other practices.   The difficulty for managers is in finding the
fine line between sharing this experience and encouraging innovation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Go To" and "Structured programming"

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T22:05:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb415dd.93137469@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b8if76$gdc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3eadb031.361090998@news.optonline.net> <b8lvl7$b5c$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eaf1a9b_2@127.0.0.1> <b8na20$roe$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb0d452.566979012@news.optonline.net> <b8r9nt$7u2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eb1f3a3.28224158@news.optonline.net> <b8tv0e$i8c$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On  2-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> quality control we had. They are enforced by Team Leaders who usually have
3-5
>> years of programming experience.
>
>In my experience, team leaders aren't the enforcers, nor do they have that
>limited programming experience.  But my experience quite often disagrees with
>the whole world as described by you.

Was any of your experience at shops with formal methodology? 

When they get methodology, they push obsolete qa controls to the bottom of the
political hierarchy. 

>> Modern methodologies are oriented toward examining external results. Old
>> attempts at methodology, designed by programmers, looked inward, thinking
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>follow your standards.

I described how I did it here. I agree that a common style is an important goal.
We disagree on how to achieve it. I believe in voluntary compliance motivated by
teamwork; you seem to support rules imposed from above. 

>And certainly there are practices that we have learned are more prone to
>troubles than other practices.   The difficulty for managers is in finding the
>fine line between sharing this experience and encouraging innovation.

Authoritarian shops are not known for encouraging innovation. But then, routine
programming projects don't NEED innovation; they just follow the established
model.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Go To" and "Structured programming"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T12:03:30-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8rggi$ou2$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b8if76$gdc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3eaf1a9b_2@127.0.0.1> <b8na20$roe$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb0d452.566979012@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3eb0d452.566979012@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Your description is discrepant with reality in the world of formal methodology.

No, Mr Wagner, your 'world of formal methodology' seems to be discrepant 
with 'reality'.

I try to use words like 'reality' as little as possible; Greater Minds 
than Mine have argued about this term for millennia.  I use phrases such 
as 'I have seen' and the like... is there *anyone* reading who has not 
seen a situation like this or something similar?

>You are describing military and small company environments where methodology is
>absent, so order comes from social/management strata. 

I am describing what I have seen, Mr Wagner, and what I believe - rightly 
or wrongly - that others have seen.  No matter how it comes about or 
where it is found the question appears to be valid... and you are dodging 
a direct addressing of it a second time.

>
>One might ask why even write a programming spec? Some organizations don't. There
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>methedology model,  

One might ask for change of a buck, as well... but I have asked this
particular question twice and twice you have not addressed it.  Third and
final time, then:  A spec is issued.  The programmer speaks of concerns
and these concerns receive a fair, impartial and dispassionate review.  
After said review a duly-authorised corporate superior states that the
programmer, as a corporate resource, is to be directed towards the clear,
unambiguous and legal action of coding an ABEND in the program for a
certain set of conditions.  What is the programmer to do?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Go To" and "Structured programming"

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T03:03:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb30685.23662985@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b8if76$gdc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3eaf1a9b_2@127.0.0.1> <b8na20$roe$1@panix1.panix.com> <3eb0d452.566979012@news.optonline.net> <b8rggi$ou2$1@panix5.panix.com> <b8rhqg$bca$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On  1-May-2003, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>companies will have standards that are 100% congruent with any given
programmer,
>I expect he has done this many, many times in his long career.

I've never resigned for reasons remotely close to shop standards. The reason has
usually been because the company was close to bankruptcy. As an insider with
access to financial statements I can see it coming. Every company I've worked
for (as a perm)  has gone belly up. In most cases, I resigned a year before the
end. 

For 25 years, I was the manager who wrote the standards (see my reply to PEC
Dashwood for more on this). For another ten years I was independent, developing
tools, an operating system and OEM stuff, using my own standards. Only in the
last five years, as a contractor,  have I worked in shops using someone else's
standards. They all had formal methodologies such as CMM and ISO9000, which are
oriented toward results rather than navel gazing. In these shops, the old
standards were seen as anachonisms .. something held over from the past. They
were not enforced. 

Is it possible you've worked only in medium sized shops using methodology from
the '80s?
```

><G>...Surprised you didn't pick that up...

Silly me... when you stated a specific number and repeated it I thought 
you were maintaining the finitude you established.

>
>> I am certain there are other alternatives which minds better than mine
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I doubt it...<G>

Well, then... what's Life without a bit of Uncertainty?

DD
```

news:8a2d426e.0304302141.47b7b970@posting.google.com...
> I don't get the tap dancing on this issue.

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote in message
news:<b8p6it$r8a$1@panix1.panix.com>...
> > In article <3eafd941.502695520@news.optonline.net>,
> > Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >>>>When I come across a catastrophic error (e.g. major FILE STATUS
issue)
> > >>>>I have no probs with displaying related info and doing an immediate
> > >>>>STOP RUN or GO TO a shutdown routine.  I mean, there ain't any
further
> > >>>>processing to be done, so why bother (for example) setting a switch
to
> > >>>>indicate that the end of the world has actually arived (as far as
the
> > >>>>program is concerned) and then have to test for it in higher-level
> > >>>>logic.
> > >>>
> > >>>That's like giving a file clerk authority to declare the company
bankrupt
> > >>>because she finds the file drawer jammed shut. Within her narrow
perspective,
> > >>>it's an 'unrecoverable error'.
> > >>>
> > >>>Perhaps someone higher up can recover from the condition by
dispatching a
> > >>>repairperson (VERIFY). Perhaps the file was 'optional' and the
program can
> > >>>operate without it.
> > >>
> > >>... or perhaps the problem is beyond the scope of a VERIFY, perhaps
the
> > >>file is 'mandatory' and maybe the Ops crew needs to wake up on-call
> > >>support at 3:am just to check the readability of the source-code.
> > >>
> > >>>
> > >>>Programs should be organized like corporations, with decisions made
at high
> >  and
> > >>>intermediate levels of management, grunt work done at low levels.
Grunts
> > >>>shouldn't make major decisions like aborting the job.
> > >>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >>>manager will cave today, that might change tomorrow when a new
manager takes
> >  his
> > >>>place.
> > >>
> > >>... or it might not.  Meanwhile the program has to be written and the
> > >>Business Analyst has demanded that if the file containing the dates
for
> > >>business holidays is not found when it is opened then the program's
> > >>calculations of intervening business days will be invalid so the
program
> > >>should be ABENDed and re-run after further research.
> > >>
> > >>In this case - and not a few others like it, Mr Wagner - clear,
> > >>unambiguous and direct instructions are given for the programmer to
code
> > >>an ABEND.  What do you think a programmer should do when given a
clear,
> > >>unambiguous, direct and legal instruction by a duly-authorised
corporate
> > >>superior?
> > >
> > >In environments with a formal methodology, there isn't a single
'program spec'.
> > >There are at least three levels of documentation, usually more:
> > >
> > >1.  Business requirements. Written by a manager.
> > >2.  High level design. Written by a systems analyst. Contains no
'technical
> > >stuff' such as record layouts or abends. It is written in English
rather than
> > >jargon.
> > >3.  Low level program spec. Usually written by an intern or junior
programmer.
> > >This one is full of technical detail, sometimes has pseudo-code.
> > >
> > >The programmer can use the low level spec or ignore it. Low level specs
are
> > >often full of errors. When there is a question about what the change is
intended
> > >to accomplish, the high level design is 'the bible'. If there is a
conflict
> > >between high level design and business requirement (this happens
rarely), the
> > >business requirement prevails.
> > >
> > >Although they are formally structured, these environments do not have
the
> > >authoritarian attitude you describe. 'Do as you're told and shut up' is
found in
> > >UNstructued environments, where order comes from personal relationships
because
> > >better tools are absent.
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > What do you think a programmer should do when given a clear,
unambiguous,
> > direct and legal instruction by a duly-authorised corporate superior?
> >
> > DD

jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight) wrote:

>I don't get the tap dancing on this issue. 
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>just keep on keepin on.

You just descibed a hierarchical organization. In my experience, the decision
process has been networked. You and your supervisor together go to a systems
analyst or Subject Matter Expert, who makes a determination based on facts and
policy rather than politics. He usually has the courtesy to explain the decision
by citing relevant documents. The consultation is often done via email rather
than in person. 

>docdwarf@panix.com wrote in message news:<b8p6it$r8a$1@panix1.panix.com>...

takes

the

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Go To" and "Structured programming"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T08:28:01-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8to8h$qql$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b8if76$gdc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b8p6it$r8a$1@panix1.panix.com> <8a2d426e.0304302141.47b7b970@posting.google.com> <3eb1fde5.30851164@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3eb1fde5.30851164@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight) wrote:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>policy rather than politics.

This seems a sidestep, Mr Wagner... whether it is one's direct supervisor 
giving a clear, direct, unambiguous and legal instruction or a systems 
analyst giving a clear, direct, unambiguous and legal instruction or a 
'Subject Matter Expert' giving a clear, direct, etc...

... and whether the determination is made before or after a discussion, or 
a coffee-break or a take-out Chinese lunch of K'ung Pao Brocolli... or 
before or after a discussion of the merits of various schools of 
programming structure...

... it boils down to something asking a programmer to do something which
said programmer Just Doesn't Like.  In this case - a GO TO directing
program execution to an -ABEND routine - the matter involved is one of
'mere style' but that is the heart of the matter: someone with the
authorisation to direct the use of a programmer's time as a corporate
resource directs the programmer to do something said programmer Just 
Doesn't Like.

How does one respond to being told to do something one Just Doesn't Like?  
That, of course, depends on the someone.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Go To" and "Structured programming"

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

- **Message-ID:** `<3EB4203A.8AEEEC5D@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<b8if76$gdc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b8p6it$r8a$1@panix1.panix.com> <8a2d426e.0304302141.47b7b970@posting.google.com> <3eb1fde5.30851164@news.optonline.net> <b8to8h$qql$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EB406BA.3020201@Knology.net>`

```


LX-i wrote:

> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> > How does one respond to being told to do something one Just Doesn't Like?
>
> As a military member, I believe the appropriate response is "Sir, yes sir!"
>

Agreed. It comes out sounding like above, but as an ex-military man I was
always mentally spelling it as :-

                "Cur, yes cur ! Three bags full cur !".

BTW - are you actually in uniform or are you a 'civvy'. Long after I left the
RAF my boss an ex-airman (your PFC) and yours truly an ex-sergeant - we both
visited a military training establishment at Blandford Forum in Dorset.
Primarily run by two RAF flight lieutenants (Education branch).

The students were 'hossifers' from all three services primarily being trained
as analysts; possibly with some programming being thrown in. Can't have
officers and gentlemen dirtying their hands - onerous tasks such as operating
the computer - that was left to a hariy-assed RAF flight sergeant and his
minions, corporals and a bunch of AC-plonks !

The two flight-lewies latched on to the fact that the visiting duo were ex RAF
- but the rules of gentlemanliness forbade them the opportunity to ask, "So
what were you.....?"

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Go To" and "Structured programming"

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> LX-i wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Primarily run by two RAF flight lieutenants (Education branch).

I'm in uniform - SSgt (E-5) in the USAF.

> The students were 'hossifers' from all three services primarily being trained
> as analysts; possibly with some programming being thrown in. Can't have
> officers and gentlemen dirtying their hands - onerous tasks such as operating
> the computer - that was left to a hariy-assed RAF flight sergeant and his
> minions, corporals and a bunch of AC-plonks !

In the USAF, the programmers are in the "communications" career field. 
Our current officer in charge (OIC) was over a base communications 
squadron before coming to our shop.  The comm squadron is responsible 
for phones, mobile radio, LAN, etc.  Quite a bit different than what we 
do (systems analysis, design, and lots o' coding).  To his credit, he's 
done a great job, but I think its strange that the career field is that 
broad.
```

I'm the only person in my shop who's not retired military.  They're all

(O-5).  So, although I'm the only military, I'm still expected to be 
courteous.

Of course, they're all pretty laid back - most of the "directives" that 
I end up having to "shut up and color" on are from outside our office. 
It's not too back - heck, I've stayed here over 5 years, and am about 
the only military person in our office (our shop + all other on the 
project) who wouldn't love to PCS...

>>like something...)

>

I was thinking more "AWOL"...  :)
