jallan wrote:
snip...
>>That doesn't bother me because language hasn't produced an adequate substitute
>>for math. Romans tried and failed miserably with their XVCII dates.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> The first two methods are ideographic.

fwiw: XVCII is roman numeral nonsense.  The most similar meaningful
roman numeral sequence is XCVII, but this is equivalent to 97.
MDII is the roman numeral equivalent of 1502.

~~LF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 52)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T11:47:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com>`

```
jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message
news:<3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net>...
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >> Nor do I care what you think. Hopefully another reader, perhaps a
neophyte,
>> >> will
>> >> learn that > represents more than 'less typing' or 'a question of style'.
Its
>> >> use is a linguistic issue.
>> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> That doesn't bother me because language hasn't produced an adequate
substitute
>> for math. Romans tried and failed miserably with their XVCII dates.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>in English as "one thousand five hundred and two").

The three are equally precise for expressing a single number. The superiority of
"1502" becomes evident when we do arithmetic operations on it, for example
adding 9 to get 1511. 

>But alphabetic sytems seem to have preferred to use ideographs for
>numbers. The surviving system in Greek and Hebrew both use alphabetic
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>printing.

We see that in street addresses such as 'Five Hundred Park Avenue'. We don't
normally do arithmetic on street addresses except when we compare them. In that
case we mentally convert them to numbers to figure out that 'Four Hundred' is
south of 'Five Hundred'. 

>If you claim that "greater than" is innately better than the ideograph
>">", why not also claim that in mathematics "one thousand five hundred
>and two" is  superior to "1502"?

As I said, the difference is arithmetic. 

>"GT" or even "G" are in any ideographic when used to mean "greater
>than"?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>represent.

I don't like that notation because a single typing error can change GT to LT or
GE. One must inspect the two symbols as closely as >. 

'Greater' had redundancy built in. An error in one letter cannot change it to a
word that compiles. 

>On the other hand ">" works equally well (or badly if you don't like
>symbols) in any language.

What about the remaining hundreds of words in COBOL's lexicon? Would you create
an ideograph for each? Doing so would make COBOL as cryptic as APL. 

>One could easily invent a mnemonic numbering system for English, for
>example 1502.6 represented by "on.fi.ze.tw;si"? Would some such system
>be superior to the Arabic numerals? It is still ideographic, of
>course.

That's basically what numerals are. They started as letters.

We all know the frustration of dialing telephone numbers like 1-800-FLOWERS.
They turn us into hunt-and-peck typists. I wish they'd never put letters on the
telephone keypad. 

>Or is there an advantage for quick mental interpreation when
>ideographs look like ideographs and cannot be confused with alphabetic
>symbols?

Yes. I wish the symbols were more distinct. Some fonts make it difficult or
impossible to distinguish 1 (one) from l (lowercase L). That's a problem in file
names, where the symbol could represent either.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 53)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T18:25:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9u1mj$67o$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

On 14-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> I don't like that notation because a single typing error can change GT to LT
> or
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> word that compiles.

But we type in the number 123, when we meant 124.   Or we wrote HEADER-1 when we
meant HEADER-2.   It's easy enough to read >, we don't need to inspect it.


> Yes. I wish the symbols were more distinct. Some fonts make it difficult or
> impossible to distinguish 1 (one) from l (lowercase L). That's a problem in
> file names, where the symbol could represent either.

I remember trying to teach old time typists to not use l for 1, or o for 0.

But we have this problem with words as well.   Lots of ambiguity in real life.
  I try to state numbers from left to right if someone is writing them down,
don't say one-hundred-fifteen, especially when I mean 100-15.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 53)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T07:38:04+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9u60i$osh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>"XVCII" is just as precise in meaning as "1502" and "fifteen hundred
>>and two".

Actually it doesn't.  RW probably didn't know that it was an invalid number 
when he wrote it.  RW: what number does it represent ?

> The three are equally precise for expressing a single number. The
> superiority of "1502" becomes evident when we do arithmetic operations on
> it, for example adding 9 to get 1511.

Roman numeral arithmetic is just as easy to anyone who has practiced it as 
arabic notation aritmetic is to you.  The differerce is only in what one 
has been taught.  It is also just as easy to do octal, hexadecimal or any 
other given equal amounts of training and practice.


> LT or GE. One must inspect the two symbols as closely as >.

People who have learnt to recognise the symbols do not need to 'inspect 
closely' to see what it means.  If you need to think about which <> is 
which then perhaps you should use them more, you certainly need to for C.

> That's basically what numerals are. They started as letters.

No they didn't, not the arabic ones anyway.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 57)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-16T10:09:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec4b595.68064449@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <b9u60i$osh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b9uaji$9et$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2ecfa.156728865@news.optonline.net> <ba078p$7ak$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 14-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>sounding choice of the three.

Ok, preferred by me. I prefer it because:

'equals' is not standard COBOL. I don't think it's a good idea to extend the
standard unless you get something valuable in return. 

'is equal to' supplies is as a verb. But we don't need another verb because
COBOL uses IF as a verb. In English we can use other tenses and cases for is. We
can say 'a used to be equal to b' or 'a will soon be equal to b' or 'a is almost
equal to b'. In COBOL the only option is present tense is, which makes it a
noise word which supplies no information. 

'equal to' vs. 'equal' lacks the compelling arguments above. Some would say 'to
b' is counterproductive because a search on 'to b' seeking places where b is
modified will return false hits on equal tests. I use 'equal to' because it
seems more grammatical, supplying a preposition to the comparative adjective.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

news:3ec2ecfa.156728865@news.optonline.net...

> The preferred syntax is 'equal to', not 'is equal to' nor 'equals'.

In standard COBOL, I would expect "if a equals b ..." to receive a syntax
error.  As to the others:

*The* preferred syntax, or *your* preferred syntax?  If the former,
according to which authority?   I see no such preference in any standard, or
in any manual documenting an implementation of that standard.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>in any manual documenting an implementation of that standard.

Howard Brazee raised the same objection. Quoting my answer to him:

Ok, preferred by me. I prefer it because:

'equals' is not standard COBOL. I don't think it's a good idea to extend the
standard unless you get something valuable in return. 

'is equal to' supplies is as a verb. But we don't need another verb because
COBOL uses IF as a verb. In English we can use other tenses and cases for is. We
can say 'a used to be equal to b' or 'a will soon be equal to b' or 'a is almost
equal to b'. In COBOL the only option is present tense is, which makes it a
noise word which supplies no information. 

'equal to' vs. 'equal' lacks the compelling arguments above. Some would say 'to
b' is counterproductive because a search on 'to b' seeking places where b is
modified will return false hits on equal tests. I use 'equal to' because it
seems more grammatical, supplying a preposition to the comparative adjective.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 58)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-16T14:21:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba2s5m$dhq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <b9u60i$osh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b9uaji$9et$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2ecfa.156728865@news.optonline.net> <ba08lv$787$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ec4b5be.68104893@news.optonline.net>`

On 16-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> 'is equal to' supplies is as a verb. But we don't need another verb because
> COBOL uses IF as a verb. In English we can use other tenses and cases for is.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'equal to' vs. 'equal' lacks the compelling arguments above.

It seems to be the opposite argument - you're picking the compromise position.
Nothing wrong with that - but nothing particularly right with it either.

> Some would say 'to b' is counterproductive because a search on 'to b' seeking
> places where b is

Does Hamlet say 'to b' or not 'to be'?

What's the question?

> modified will return false hits on equal tests. I use 'equal to' because it
> seems more grammatical, supplying a preposition to the comparative adjective.

To me, "is equal to" seems more grammatical, and certainly more English like.  
If "English like" is valued, it is the obvious choice.  If "English like" is not
valued, then the symbol "=" is the obvious choice.

But I won't claim any of these is "The preferred syntax" unless I make it very
clear who is doing the preferring.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 60)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-18T21:46:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305182046.6ecfee00@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <b9u60i$osh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b9uaji$9et$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2ecfa.156728865@news.optonline.net> <ba08lv$787$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ec4b5be.68104893@news.optonline.net> <ba2s5m$dhq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6ca9b.97736635@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >To me, "is equal to" seems more grammatical, and certainly more English like.  
> >If "English like" is valued, it is the obvious choice.  If "English like" is
>  not
> >valued, then the symbol "=" is the obvious choice.
 
> I supported the reasons behind my preference. You didn't.

In what way is "seems more grammatical" _not_ a supporting reason ?

In what way is 'if .. not valued' _not_ a supporting reason ?


> I thought it was understood that all postings here are the opinion of the
> author. Why is it necessary to post disclaimers like 'in my opinion' and 'in my
> limited experience'? 

It is _not_ 'understood' when a statement is made as if it were a
statement of fact or of absolute truth.

Most people would say: "A good way of doing this is ..."

You tend to say: "The best way of doing this is ..."

Use of the definite article and superlatives tends to deny that it is
merely an opinion.


> The rest is all personal opinion.

I know that what you say is just opinions, sometimes it is hard to
convince you that this is the case.  Certainly you act as if what you
say is facts.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 60)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-19T15:11:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

On 17-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >To me, "is equal to" seems more grammatical, and certainly more English like.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I supported the reasons behind my preference. You didn't.

What was my preference?   I gave reasons for a couple of choices - the English
like, and the International like.  I read your reason for neither, and it seemed
that you didn't want to be too international, nor two English like.   And I
missed your reason entirely.

> >But I won't claim any of these is "The preferred syntax" unless I make it
> >very clear who is doing the preferring.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> my limited experience'?

Normally.   But the very syntax of "The preferred syntax is" implies that the
entity doing the preferring isn't the speaker - unless someone is Royalty using
the royal "we".
```

Robert Wagner wrote:

> Once again, my reason is dislike for symbols because they seem
> linguistically backward.

It is only your opinion that they seem backward.  You tried to invent a 
justification for this based on a superficial and shallow argument about 
something quite different, and showed that you knew little about it.  Cobol 
does not rely on 'linguistics', it does rely on mathematics.

One could equally argue, with as much relevence, that road signs in europe 
went from words to symbols and thus '>' is more 'advanced' than the words.

simply a matter of style.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 64)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T08:11:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bag4ud$25t2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec6ca9b.97736635@news.optonline.net> <baas6m$ejv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec9cf5f.64521958@news.optonline.net> <bae206$lln$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecb0f5c.146451151@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ecb0f5c.146451151@news.optonline.net...

>> Cobol
> >does not rely on 'linguistics', it does rely on mathematics.
>
> It would help if COBOL knew more about both.

Help *what*?

The rules for the COBOL language are more precise and more predictable than
that of any natural language.  Every COBOL construct is expected to mean
exactly one, and only one, thing taken in context, and that is most
definitely *NOT* true for natural languages.  What is it about historical,
comparative or computational linguistics that the COBOL language (or any
implementor's COBOL compiler) needs to know about?

I would agree, by the way, that COBOL's long-standing focus on
English-speaking cultures and American conventions in particular has
resulted in some significant shortcomings in the applicability of the
language to other cultures, but I would also assert that the standards
committee has put forth a great deal of effort to eliminate many of those
shortcomings.   I think it ought to be just as easy for a programmer to
"float" the currency symbol "Bs." when printing financial reports as it
currently is to do so using the currency symbol "$".

But I don't think that's so much a *linguistics* issue as it is a *cultural*
one.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 53)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T20:14:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net>`

```
I will respond in one place to both your previous posts to prevent
unnecessary splitting of the discussion.

Roget Wagner wrote:
> jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>> Yet that would miss the point of why mathematical ideographic
notation
>> has increased. It is a visual language, more distinct from speech
than
>> sign-language is from speech, in which one can visually grasp
complex
>> relationships in one single view, relationships that are much
harder
>> to comprehend if represented serially in speech.

> Good point .. and perhaps a good argument for other programming languages. 
> But COBOL is modelled on English.

The exact modelling of various features in COBOL may be good or bad.
If an argument works for mathematics and other programming languages,
then one should show why that argument does not apply to COBOL.

But COBOL's long-winded style of syntax has not been copied by any
other programming language that I know of. Few see it as a virtue.

That "IS GREATER THAN" fits COBOL's verbosity more than ">" is just as
good a reason not to use it as to use it.

>>> I dispute that.The symbol > is no more (or less) precise than the
words
>>> 'greater than'.
>>
>> Ah, but word take longer to process mentally than a single symbol.

> I find words faster to comprehend. We don't read a letter at a time, we read 
> the whole word at once.

Words still take, on the average, longer to recognize than a single
familiar symbol.

In fact people read sometimes by entire phrases, sometimes by words,
sometimes by parts of words, sometimes by invidividual letters,
depending on how familiar to them the phrases, words, parts of words,
and alphabetical clusters that make up the words are.

>> Would you refer to write "greater than" instead of "GT"? 

> Yes, because a typo will be detected by the compiler.

A slip of logic which produces "IS GREATER THAN" when "IS LESS THAN"
is meant will not be spotted by the compiler.

If the programmer misspells "IS GREATER THAN", the compiler will catch
it, but normally the programmer will look no further than the spelling
error, will not recheck the logic.

I think one could make a case for "GT" and "LT" over ">" and "<", in
that the latter two are not so mnemonic and look more alike especially
to many who are slightly dyslexic (as well as those who are more
severely dyslexic).

But could one make the same case for "IS EQUAL TO" or even "EQ" over
"="? Empty little words get it the way.

>Do you agree with standard use in geometry of _d_ for "diamater", _r_
>for "radius", but argue against use of the _pi_ symbol for the
>relation between radius/diameter and circumference.

> Pi is universally known, but generally I don't like Greek letters in math.
> They look like the author is showing off or making a class distinction
> between educated and uneducated.

I don't think use of Greek letters is what separates those who know
calculus from those who do not, or the use of special characters in
HTML from those who know HTML for those who do not, or the use of
special characters in linguistics from those who know linguistics and
those who do not.

Learning such characters is one of the least difficult things about
learning those disciplines. Learning the special characters in c is
perhaps the least difficulty in learning c.

One could esasly replace symbols in c that one finds to short and
confusing by words which could be translated back into symbols by the
compiler using preprocessor #define statement.

I've never seen it done.

>> What would you use instead? What English word or expression would
you
>> subtitute?

> The letters 'pi'.

Syntax error!!  The letters "pi" mean p times i.

The use of single alphabet letters for variables and constants in
common algebraic notation is principally why Greek letters and
typographically variant  letters are used for special cases.

robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net>...
> jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> adding 9 to get 1511. 

Yes. Therefore ideographs *can* be superior.

But in computer languages, the computer does the mathematical work. We
don't need to line up the numbers in columns, so we could spell out
numbers in words without worrying about ease of calcuation, if
redundancy in typing is helpful.

> We see that in street addresses such as 'Five Hundred Park Avenue'. We don't
> normally do arithmetic on street addresses except when we compare them. In that
> case we mentally convert them to numbers to figure out that 'Four Hundred' is
> south of 'Five Hundred'. 

Agreed. 

Writing "500" instead of "five hundred" avoids mental conversion of
words into concept.

Similarly writing "=" instead of "is equal to" avoids mental
conversion of words into concept.

The counter-argument is that here the concept is more precisely
indicated by the the word "equal", that we have less need of a symbol.

The counter-counter-argument is that "=" and other symbols produce
statements that are shorter and more easily grasped.

In part the use of symbols in mathematics comes because of algebraic
use of letters as variables. Abbreviations were considered desirable,
but "eq" means e times q.

Of course "eq." is not equal to "eq", or perhaps preferrably "eq." ï¿½=
"eq" or "eq." != "eq".

But is an appended dot enough difference for immediately clarity?
Underling doesn't work well in hard type. I supposed some such
notation as |eq| might have been used for operators.

Instead special symbols, simpler in form than most letters, were
devised one by one, and those found most useful fell into common use.

Such symbols help distinguish operators and relational indicators from
the data.
 
> What about the remaining hundreds of words in COBOL's lexicon? Would you
> create an ideograph for each? Doing so would make COBOL as cryptic as APL. 

Why not, as a variant of COBOL?

But in fact the most general use for symbols in programming languages
is as operators. Commands and structural indicators and variables are
mostly indicated by words.

There are languages like Postscript and Lisp that make almost no use
of symbols. But such exceptions don't make a case that using symbols
is a backward step. These may be the backward languages. Perhaps their
creators just happend to share your particular (but possibly
objectively unjustified for most) dislike of symbols. Or perhaps for
the particular philosphy behind those languages and the use to which
they were to be put symbols were not the way to go.

With the age of Unicode upon us there is unbounded freedom to use more
symbols in new languages. That will certainly happen. Whether such use
catches on depends on how many people like using those languages, and
imitate their use of symbols.

> >One could easily invent a mnemonic numbering system for English, for
> >example 1502.6 represented by "on.fi.ze.tw;si"? Would some such system
> >be superior to the Arabic numerals? It is still ideographic, of
> >course.
 
> That's basically what numerals are. They started as letters.

Irrelevant.

First, Roman numerals are believed to predate the Roman alphabet,
being ideographs, I for one finger, V thumb and other fingers spread,
X for two of V (one above the other, the bottom one inverted), L
probably as a variant form for V, and C as two L's combined (one above
the other, the top one inverted).

Each later fell together with the character it most resembled in the
Roman alphabet.

The so-called Arabic numerals can be traced back to Hindu forms, but
their ultimate origin is lost in antiquity. The earliest known forms
are not obviously connected to Hindu letter forms.

In any case, the standard numeric digits associated with modern Latin
alphabets are ideographs, whatever their origin, just as &, #, @, $,
%, +, =, \, ~ and many other symbols are ideographs, whatever the
characters from which they derived once were.

Use of variables such as I, J, and K for first counter, second
counter, and third counter, are ideographic. The meanings of these
letters here have nothing to do with their phonetic values or with
words in the language.

Your original claim was that using ideographs in mathematics and
programming was step backwards. You wish not to include numbers in
this, which is fair enough.

Though numeric ideographs were not part of the original Phoenician
alphabet from which it appears all later non-syllabary alphabets
derive by direct descent or by imitation, ideographic renderings for
numbers spring up almost everywhere beside the phonetic scripts.

The Canaanite Ugartic cuneiform alphabet (http://www.jhu.edu/ice/ufu)
had a separate set of digits
(http://www.jhu.edu/ice/ufu/numerics.html).

The Old Persian cuneiform alphabet, a syllabary apparently invented
about 512 BCE, contains numeric characters separate from the syllabic
characters. See http://std.dkuug.dk/jtc1/sc2/wg2/docs/n2583.pdf.

It seems that however reasonable a phonetic script might be for normal
use, still additional ideographic symbols for numbers are devised or
adapted to go beside it.

Other ideographs are also used on occasion, but we find no case out of
the many alphabets and syllabaries where the users begin introducing a
large number of additional non-numeric ideographs for commonly used
words, despite the saving in writing and perhaps greater ease of
reading.

Phonetic notation seems to suffice for almost everything but numbers,
even when so badly out of synchronization with the spoken language as
in modern English.

But we do find many abbreviations. Using abbreviations partly
counteracts the long-windedness of phonetic notations. Books before
moveable type used abbreviations far more widely than we do today.

Sometimes a word or name used particular text will be used often in
that text yet will *never* appear in full spelling. You are just
supposed to know that "G." means "Gauuain" or "Gauuains" or "Gawain"
or "Galvain" or whatever variant spelling you feel like.

Some of our current ideographs are distorted verions of such
abbreviations and others are probably so

Yet it is significant that most of them have to do with weights and
measures and working with numbers.

Beginning in the Renaissance ideographs started appearing in
mathematics.

See http://www.roma.unisa.edu.au/07305/symbols.htm for a brief
discussion.

Why?

Because ideographs do make mathematical argument and the mathermatical
templates called forumlas clearer to the understanding, and enable a
reader to more easily follow mathematical reasoning. If not, why did
mathematicians introduce them, and then introduce more and more them,
despite the problems in publishing them properly?

The notation "5 ï¿½ 8 + 5 - 3 = 42" is less lengthy and leaps into the
mind more easily than "5 multiplied by 8 plus 5 minus 3 is equal to
42". There is further confusion possible in a notation like "ab plus b
minus c is equal to d".

Different mathematicians used different symbols, but none to my
knowledge eschewed the use of ideographs so obviously, on the whole,
despite what might be particular misuses, improved mathematical
clarity.

Can you find any mathematician who wants to drop ideographs (except
for numbers) from mathemtical notation? I am sure one can find
complaints of overuse and misuse. Style suggestions indicate, for
example, that any special symbols used in an article or long
mathematical argument should normally appear at least twice.

I would not mind find and reading something by a mathematician who
felt that mathematical notation would be improved by dropping almost
all ideographs other than numbers. It would be interesting reading.

But would you prefer that COBOL's COMPUTE statement did not used the
normal English arithemtic operators, that it used the corresponding
English words instead?

Would page on page of mathematical formula be better comprehended if
the calculations were rendered in the English words that might be used
to read those calculations aloud, except for numeric digits?

I can understand personally preferring the long forms for conditions
in COBOL. Nothing is completely open-and-shut and personal tastes
unaccountable differ.

But I don't see any justification that such forms are, in general,
innately superior to single ideographs, even in the context of COBOL.

For me they leap out from the fog of variables and keywords in any
computer language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 54)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-17T19:18:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com>`

```
jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>Roget Wagner wrote:
>> jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:
>

>>> What would you use instead? What English word or expression would
>you
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Syntax error!!  The letters "pi" mean p times i.

Implied multiplication mandates single letter names. That would be unacceptable
in a programming language. 

>The use of single alphabet letters for variables and constants in
>common algebraic notation is principally why Greek letters and
>typographically variant  letters are used for special cases.

The solution is names bigger than one letter and explicit multiplication.

>> >> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> >> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> The three are equally precise for expressing a single number. The superiority
of
>> "1502" becomes evident when we do arithmetic operations on it, for example
>> adding 9 to get 1511. 
>
>Yes. Therefore ideographs *can* be superior.

Ideographs are superior for numbers because they make it easier to perform
operations on the 'words'. It does not follow that ideographs are superior for
words that will not be transformed. 

>But in computer languages, the computer does the mathematical work. We
>don't need to line up the numbers in columns, so we could spell out
>numbers in words without worrying about ease of calcuation, if
>redundancy in typing is helpful.

We do that when we give names to variables and constants. When a program uses
the value of pi in multiple places, it is more economical to type the letters
'pi' than to keep repeating 3.1415926. 

>Writing "500" instead of "five hundred" avoids mental conversion of
>words into concept.
>
>Similarly writing "=" instead of "is equal to" avoids mental
>conversion of words into concept.

Both must be converted to the concept of equality. The symbol '=' is not a
concept.

>The counter-argument is that here the concept is more precisely
>indicated by the the word "equal", that we have less need of a symbol.
>
>The counter-counter-argument is that "=" and other symbols produce
>statements that are shorter and more easily grasped.

If shorter is more easily grasped, why did COBOL replace the period with wordy
delimiters? Because people complained that a period is too easy to overlook.

C programmers solved the problem by spending an entire line on the terminating
symbol. 

>In part the use of symbols in mathematics comes because of algebraic
>use of letters as variables. Abbreviations were considered desirable,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>"eq" or "eq." != "eq".

Following your logic, "!=" means 'not and equal' (and is logical
multiplication). Thus 'a != true' could be expanded to 'not a and a = true',
which would always be false. 

>Instead special symbols, simpler in form than most letters, were
>devised one by one, and those found most useful fell into common use.

If their use is so common, why is the symbol for not equal -- an equal sign with
a slash through it -- missing from my keyboard?

>Such symbols help distinguish operators and relational indicators from
>the data.

By that argument, reserved words such as TO and FROM should use ideographs to
distinguish them from data names. 'Gee, I saw 'ADD A TO B' and thought he was
adding it to a variable named TO.'

>> What about the remaining hundreds of words in COBOL's lexicon? Would you
>> create an ideograph for each? Doing so would make COBOL as cryptic as APL. 
>
>Why not, as a variant of COBOL?

Because it would be cryptic; that's why not.

>But in fact the most general use for symbols in programming languages
>is as operators. Commands and structural indicators and variables are
>mostly indicated by words.

Symbols are most widely used as scope delimiters. In COBOL, it's limited to
parentheses and the comment indicator. In many other languages they are also
used to delimit procedural blocks. When used in this way, they are punctuation
symbols. 

Because symbols are used as arithmetic operators, it does not necessarily follow
that they are superior for all operators. The common word operator does not
constitute an argument. 

>There are languages like Postscript and Lisp that make almost no use
>of symbols. But such exceptions don't make a case that using symbols
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>they were to be put symbols were not the way to go.

A quick Web search found this example of Lisp, which uses lots of symbols.

(find '(A B)'((A B) (C D) (E F)) ==> NIL
(find '(A B)'((A B) (C D) (E F) :test #'equal)
  ==> (A B)
(defun Almost-Equal (Num1 Num2)
  (<= (abs (- Num1 Num2)) 0.1))
(find pi '(2.9 3.0 3.1 3.2 3.3) 
      :test #'Almost-Equal) ==> 3.1

>In any case, the standard numeric digits associated with modern Latin
>alphabets are ideographs, whatever their origin, just as &, #, @, $,
>%, +, =, \, ~ and many other symbols are ideographs, whatever the
>characters from which they derived once were.

Your example confuses the difference between ideograms and logograms. The
symbols &, @, $, >, %, + and = are logograms -- they represent words. The others
are ideograms -- they do not represent words. Punctuation symbols are all
ideograms, notwithstanding the fact they have names. 

I have no objection to punctuation symbols, numerals nor arithmetic operators.
The topic of this discussion is logographic symbols such as > and = used in
place of words. Let's try to stay focussed on that rather than restating the
issue with generalizations about all symbols .. in the hope no one will notice
logograms being swept in with the other symbols. 

>Use of variables such as I, J, and K for first counter, second
>counter, and third counter, are ideographic. The meanings of these
>letters here have nothing to do with their phonetic values or with
>words in the language.

I concur, and use that style for subscripts.

>It seems that however reasonable a phonetic script might be for normal
>use, still additional ideographic symbols for numbers are devised or
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>reading.

Now you are supplying evidence in support of my argument. You must be an
academician. Non-academics don't usually do that.  :)

>Phonetic notation seems to suffice for almost everything but numbers,
>even when so badly out of synchronization with the spoken language as
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>moveable type used abbreviations far more widely than we do today.

I can remember the ampersand used in ordinary text. More recently it has fallen
from use.  

Why is there no corresponding symbol for OR outside math? That always struck me
as odd. 

>Sometimes a word or name used particular text will be used often in
>that text yet will *never* appear in full spelling. You are just
>supposed to know that "G." means "Gauuain" or "Gauuains" or "Gawain"
>or "Galvain" or whatever variant spelling you feel like.

Modern (and not so modern) style rules require the abbreviation to be spelled
out the first time it's used.

We don't do it with widely known abbreviations from Latin  -- vs., etc., et al.

>Some of our current ideographs are distorted verions of such
>abbreviations and others are probably so
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>minus c is equal to d".

Most programmers object to the omission of parentheses. I do strongly.
Mathematicians seem to be unbothered. 

>Different mathematicians used different symbols, but none to my
>knowledge eschewed the use of ideographs so obviously, on the whole,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>mathematical argument should normally appear at least twice.

No, but programming is somewhat different from math. We deal with large networks
of programs passing messages to each other .. through both space and time. Math
doesn't have a concept like disk storage. We deal with data structures more
complex than a matrix. Our languages are 'meta-' or self-aware when they perform
operations on the language itself (e.g. inheritance).

>But would you prefer that COBOL's COMPUTE statement did not used the
>normal English arithemtic operators, that it used the corresponding
>English words instead?

No. I do not object to arithmetic symbols. I object to paradigm shifts when
their only purpose is to reduce typing. 

Apologies for the delay in responding. I was unusually busy this week.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 55)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-05-18T16:04:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0305181504.611b8836@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net>...
> jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> in a programming language. 

But I was talking about mathematics.

Would you want to coin a word like
"circumference-to-diameter-relation" to describe what is now indicated
by the Greek lower-case letter _pi_? Would you ike to write that out
in full every time it is referenced in mathematics or in a programming
language?

You might of course use some such abbreviation as CDR in programming
for "circumference-to-diameter-relation".

If you use "pi" in a programming language, you are accepting the
mathematical use of a Greek letter. The variable name "pi" is only a
typographical makeshift in ASCII to indicate the true symbol, and the
symbol itself says nothing about its meaning.

(The Greek lowercase letter _pi_ actually was available on the
Commodore line of computers, on their keyboards, and was used in
Commodore Basic as an unchangeable constant with its normal value of
3.1415926...)

> >The use of single alphabet letters for variables and constants in
> >common algebraic notation is principally why Greek letters and
> >typographically variant  letters are used for special cases.
> 
> The solution is names bigger than one letter and explicit multiplication.

It is a solution only if there is a problem.

Algebraic variable notation is the foundation on which modern
mathematics (including calculus) and logic (including propositional
calculus and set theory) is built.

There is no one single notation. Individuals continually niggle at the
notations or invent partially new ones.

There are continual attempts at improvement.

But a constant in all I have seen (except for a few ad hoc inventions
for a particular purpose) is staying *close* to the principle of one
symbol for one concept, a single symbol for each variable and
constant, a single symbol for each operator.

Also in spoken English something like "five oranges" is normal. So is
"five sixes" which is multiplication. If I don't actually know whether
it is sixes or eights or what not, I might say "five somethings". In
algebra a "something" is a variable, hence the notation "5a". An
explicit multiplication sign would add nothing. Similarly, if I don't
even know how many "somethings" I have, then I can write "ba" in
algebraic notation, indicating that I have b a's.

Explicit multiplication is only needed sometines when arabic numbers
appear in the equations, as "5ï¿½2ab" (which I could also write "2a5b"
and so forth).

> >> >> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> >> >> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> words that will not be transformed. 

If you mean transformed on the computer, all tokens in source code
have to be transformed, either compiled or interpreted into executable
machine instructions, both characters representing digits and
characters representing operators and characters indicating relations.

If you mean transformed in the mind, let us consider the symbol "/".

The example statement I will use would probably have variables in at
least one postion in most real situations, but I will use decimal
numbers instead here to make things simpler. The following is a
logical statement that would not be allowed in COBOL, but makes my
point:

If 5/10 = 1/2

How do you transform this into English words? Some possibilities:

If five-tenths is equal to one-half
If five divided by ten is equal to one divided by two
If five-tenths is equal to one divided by two
If five divided by ten is eqaul to one-half
If five is to ten as one is to two

The last example is worth looking at. Shouldn't the notation be "If
5:10 = 1:2 ..." Well anywhere that a colon used as a ratio operator it
can be replaced by a division operator without changing the value of
the equation in any way.  Indeed, the symbol ":" is the normal symbol
for division in Latin alphabet languages other than English.

Accordingly more and more "/" is used mathematics instead of ":" and
certainly more than "ï¿½" because it is more visibly a variant way of
writing the normal numerator_then_underscore_then_denominator under
that.

My last translation of the sample statement can be stated a little
less idiomatically as:

If five to ten is as one to two

Moving the verb to the position of the equal sign transforms the
English phrase to a phrase that means exactly the same in English but
in which the corresponding elements of meaning match the order for the
mathematical notation.

It could be expanded further:

If five in size to ten is as one in size to two

This may make it more clear how "/" can be both ratio and division
though it is no longer idiomatic English.

So what does "/" mean? There is no single word in English. The various
expressions which can be used to translate an expression containing it
do not *intuitively* have the same meaning in English to most.

The symbol "/" and other symbols used in mathematical and logical
notation are not logographs, except by chance in particular languages,
but attempts at pure ideographs, expressing relationships to data that
may not be easily or precisely expressible in words, or not so easily
in all languages.

The typography of the system and derived rules for manipulating that
typography allows for transformations that are relatively simple when
working with the symbols but for most very difficult to comprehend
when working with the ideas the symbols represent.

Rigorous proofs and transformations are possible in such typographical
systems because of strict rules governing allowable typographical
transformations without the slipperiness of spoken language with its
multiple meanings for the same words and its irrgularities of
expression.
 
> >But in computer languages, the computer does the mathematical work. We
> >don't need to line up the numbers in columns, so we could spell out
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'pi' than to keep repeating 3.1415926. 

The symbol 3.1415926 is not much longer than the length of average
variable in a program, perhaps even shorter for some programs.

You want to use "equal" instead of "=" because "equal" is longer and
as the supposed virture of redundancy. But then you want to use a
short variable for a long numeric string. That last part makes sense
to me. But it disagrees with your position on "equal" versus "=".

The real reason to use variables and symbolic constants is not length,
but the principal of separating data from program flow. The less one
hardcodes "magic" values through the code the more general a program
can be and the more easily it can be adapted to another use.

(Also, many compilers will complain about undefined variables, but not
about literals, which many typos in variables are caught where typos
in literals are not.)

But short variables/constants are good, then just consider "=" as a
kind of variable/constant that contains the concept _equal_. What is
the difference?
 
> >Writing "500" instead of "five hundred" avoids mental conversion of
> >words into concept.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> concept.

I did not claim "=" *is* a concept.

The symbol "=" is used to *represent* the concept of equalty.

The words "is equal to" are also used to *represent* the concept of
equality.

But the symbol "=" in terms of words might be rendered in English as
"is equal to", "is", "is the same as", "gives", "makes", "evaluates
to", "equals" and a large number of other expressions, and the same in
other languages. Similarly 93 in French is "quatre-vingt treize",
words literally translated into English as "four-twenty thirteen" and
more idiomatically as "four score and thirteen".

Mathematical and logical ideographs try to get beyond words and are
transformed and manipulated by precise rules that act on the
typography of the mathematical or logical expression.

> >The counter-argument is that here the concept is more precisely
> >indicated by the the word "equal", that we have less need of a symbol.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> delimiters? Because people complained that a period is too easy to overlook.

It is too easy to overlook.

> C programmers solved the problem by spending an entire line on the terminating
> symbol. 

A fix in COBOL might have been to allow it to period to sit on a line
by itself.

But there was the additional bother that nested if's could not have
priods of their own.

That added a level of difficulty to the contruction of nested if's in
COBOL not found most other languages, indeed *no* other language of
any following purporting to be for general use.

> >In part the use of symbols in mathematics comes because of algebraic
> >use of letters as variables. Abbreviations were considered desirable,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> which would always be false. 

No. "!=" is intended to be recognized as the normal "not equals" used
in c just as "ï¿½=" is the normal "not equals" use EBDIC machines for
REXX. The "not" is applied to the "equals" and modifies it just as "a"
is applied to "b" in something like "ab". "Not" inverts the meaning.

Taking "!" to mean not and (for typographical reasons because of the
character set avaialbe) "->" to mean "implies", in certain notations
one can quite quite sensibly and obviously notate things like A = !B
-> A != B.

> >Instead special symbols, simpler in form than most letters, were
> >devised one by one, and those found most useful fell into common use.
> 
> If their use is so common, why is the symbol for not equal -- an equal sign with
> a slash through it -- missing from my keyboard?

For the same reason many other useful symbols and characters are
missing from you keyboard: the limited number of code points available
in the ASCII character set and the EBCDIC character set, and space on
the keyboard. Of course under Unicode I can now get the standard "not
equals" symbol easily.

By your logic, why are the "<" and ">" symbols on your keyboard, if
not to use for their original intent.

> >Such symbols help distinguish operators and relational indicators from
> >the data.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> adding it to a variable named TO.'

To replace "TO" in COBOL by a right arrow and "FROM" by a left arrow
would not harm or hurt in my opinon, provided those characters were
easily accessible by an editor through a keyboard.

They mean the same and are as readible.

Some programming languages do use "B += A" which means *exactly* the
same thing as "ADD A TO B". I think that notation clearer. You may
not. It does help distinguish operators and relational indicators from
the data.

> >> What about the remaining hundreds of words in COBOL's lexicon? Would you
> >> create an ideograph for each? Doing so would make COBOL as cryptic as APL. 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

My point is if someone *wants* to do this, why not? As to cryptic, it
would depend how cryptic the symbols were. The English alphabet was
cryptic to all of us at one time. Symbols might be far less crytic to
a person who didn't know English.

Most of the world's inhabitants don't know English.

"Cryptic" is very subjective. 

I have seen hexidecimal notation decried as cryptic and elitist in
much the way way you decry Greek symbols in mathematics. But you know
yourself that hexidecimal numbers are far easier to work with for many
purposes in programming, that once you understand them and have reason
to use them you won't want to use decimal numbers for some purposes.
 
> >But in fact the most general use for symbols in programming languages
> >is as operators. Commands and structural indicators and variables are
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> constitute an argument. 

It also does not follow that they are not superior.

The symbols are generally used and known.

They are pictographically indicative of whether the data to the left
or right is   greater or smaller in size.

There is no more chance of making a typographical error with them than
between numeric digits which also adjoin each other on the keyboard.

> >There are languages like Postscript and Lisp that make almost no use
> >of symbols. But such exceptions don't make a case that using symbols
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>       :test #'Almost-Equal) ==> 3.1

Indeed it does. I don't know what I was thinking of since I have
worked in Lisp. Probably thinking of its lack of mathemtical
capability and forgetting the other  symbols

So does that leave only Postscript which uses fewer symbols than
COBOL? (I'm not going to count languages like PCL which don't have
branches.)

> >In any case, the standard numeric digits associated with modern Latin
> >alphabets are ideographs, whatever their origin, just as &, #, @, $,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> ideograms, notwithstanding the fact they have names. 

I am not concerned with punctuation symbols at all, with parentheses
of any kind or periods or comment indicators.

Almost all, if not all logoraphs are also ideographs. But ideographs
are not all logogrpaphs. The symbol ">" is usually rendered as
"greater than" in English, which is two words, not one. It can also be
rendered in English as "more than", "bigger than", "larger than", "of
greater size than" and so forth, phrases that would not be rendered
the same in a logographic system for English.

I included "/" and "~" for their ideographic use as operators. The
first I have already discussed at length. The tilde indicates
approximation in some notation systems and "not" in others. It is used
to mean binary not in c and logical not in REXX for the PC.

> I have no objection to punctuation symbols, numerals nor arithmetic operators.
> The topic of this discussion is logographic symbols such as > and = used in
> place of words. Let's try to stay focussed on that rather than restating the
> issue with generalizations about all symbols .. in the hope no one will notice
> logograms being swept in with the other symbols. 

Again, almost all logographs are also ideographs.
 
> >Use of variables such as I, J, and K for first counter, second
> >counter, and third counter, are ideographic. The meanings of these
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I concur, and use that style for subscripts.

But then, why not use something like FIRST-COUNTER, SECOND-COUNTER,
THIRD-COUNTER as redundantly excellent variable names, since I, J, and
K could be so easily typoed as one another? All three adjoin on the
standard keyboard and are typed with the same finger in touch-typing.

You are using the redundancy to avoid typos argument only with the
characters "=", ">", and "<", and seeminly for "=" only when used in a
condition, not when used after COMPUTE.

Why are these uses less desireable than normal use of "+", "-", "*",
and "/" which are also subject to typos?

> >It seems that however reasonable a phonetic script might be for normal
> >use, still additional ideographic symbols for numbers are devised or
> >adapted to go beside it.

This discussion has made me realize the extent to which phonetically
based writing systems have generally not introduced other symbols,
except for numbers, again and again, and for abbreviations of full
words, some of which have become symbols.

But it has also made me realize the extent to which relationships
connected to numbers also cry out for ideaographic symbols. Just as
numbers are real parts of the reality of the universe, so are their,
relationships with one another.

Mathematics has increased in power in direct proportion to the
increased power of its symbolic representation. So also with logic.
 
> >
> >The notation "5 ï¿½ 8 + 5 - 3 = 42" is less lengthy and leaps into the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mathematicians seem to be unbothered. 

Very well, I will rewrite it.

The notation "(5 ï¿½ 8) + 5 - 3 = 42" is less lengthy and leaps into the
mind more easily than "(5 multiplied by 8) plus 5 minus 3 is equal to
42". There is further confusion possible in a notation like "ab plus b
minus c is equal to d".

Note that "ab" is *obviously* more tightly bound than "a  + b" in
algebraic notation. Adding parentheses also would be silly. And the
vertical notation for division in which the divider is placed under a
dividend but division is then done removes another need for
parentheses.

> >Different mathematicians used different symbols, but none to my
> >knowledge eschewed the use of ideographs so obviously, on the whole,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> operations on the language itself (e.g. inheritance).

Programming deals with large networks and and so programming languages
should not use the symbols "<", ">", and "=" in conditions? I miss
your logic.
> 
> >But would you prefer that COBOL's COMPUTE statement did not used the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> their only purpose is to reduce typing. 

What paradigm shift? 

Most current programming languages, *including* COBOL, allow or demand
symbols to indicate most operators.

There is no paradigm shift.

The characters "<", ">" and "=" have been part of COBOL from the
beginning.

Fortran, the only earlier high-level computer language that had been
implemented, used ".LT.", ".GT.", ".EQ." in conditions, but it was not
taken as a model here.

COBOL does give you a variant notation to use if you wish, the longer
"[IS] GREATER [THAN]", "[IS] LESS [THAN]", "[IS] EQUAL [TO]", which
you prefer, but don't seem to be able to justify beyond personal
preference.

It seems special pleading when you apply arguments about redundance to
these alone when you admit to being quite happy to use other symbols,
quite happy with variables like I, J, K which can be as easily
mistyped.

But why did the CODASYL people put two separate syntaxes for
conditions into COBOL? Nothing of the kind was done elsewhere in
COBOL, other than the variations allowed where particular words are
optional and may be omitted.

It might be that there was strong disagreement among those who created
COBOL on this issue, disagreement only appeased by allowing both
sytaxes.

But in fact if "<" and ">" were to be made part of the language, there
was also a very pratical reason why there *had* to be an alternate
syntax.

In 1960 when COBOL was introduced, there were a large number of
varying character sets on varying platforms, many of which did not
have "<", ">" and even "=". Platforms that had them could not
necessarily always print them out on available printers. Not all print
bands contained all characters in the platform supported set.

In the early 50's the IBM BCDIC character set had none of these. When
Fortran came along "+", "=", "(" and ")" were put in the place of "&",
"#", "%" and a square lozenge symbol respectively creating a separate
version of BCDIC for scientific use but still retaining the old
version for commerical uses.

Eventually "<" and ">" and some other symbols like ":" and ";" were
added to both.

Accordingly a commercial IBM shop could *perhaps* print "<" and ">",
but probably could not print "=" or "+". A Fortran programmer in such
an establishment just had to know that "&" meant "+", that "#" meant
"=", that "%" meant "(" and that the funny lozenge shape meant ")",
provided the printer to be used could print those characters either,
or print all of them.

Some non-IBM machines used the Fieldata character set, which did have
all the necessary characters for Fortran uniquely encoded as well as
"<" and ">".

But there were various other now-forgotten proprietary character sets,
peculiar to particular systems or particular software.

Considering that, some at least of the creators of COBOL must have
felt *very* strongly about including ">", "<", and "=" as COBOL
characters, even though the fall-back syntax would be necessary in
environments where those symbols were not well supported or not
supported at all.

All the symbols I have mentioned were of course on the board to be
included in the new IBM eight-bit character set EBCDIC. Meanwhile
development of ASCII was underway, only completed in 1963.

About the inclusion of "<" and ">" in ASCII, according to
http://www.cs.tut.fi/~jkorpela/latin1/ascii-hist:

<< The inclusion of < and > characters was considered as necessary for
COBOL programming.

Reference: Minutes of ECMA TC-1 and ASA X3.2 joint meeting, March 8-9,
1962, CBI 67, box 1, folder 3. >>

EBCDIC followed. Even in the 80's in many mainframe shops the print
bands used in printing out listings often did not support "<", ">" and
"=".

You have yet to provide *any* evidence that ideographs instead of
words in the Latin alphabet or another phonetically based script is a
bad thing in mathematics or logic or programming or any other
technical discipline.

If you actually meant only that certain *kinds* of ideographs are not
so desireable as words spelled out, again you have yet to provide any
evidence of how this might be determined, that is, for example, why
"<" is more undesireable than "+".

You have hinted that what works for other languages might not be
desireable for COBOL, but have given no rationale why that should be.

Jim Allan
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 56)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T16:42:41+00:00

```
jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message
news:<3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net>...
>> jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:
>> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Implied multiplication mandates single letter names. That would be
unacceptable
>> in a programming language. 
>
>But I was talking about mathematics.

Equations such as c=b ln(1+s/n), v=ir and e=mc� would be less memorable if they
were written in Greek.

>Would you want to coin a word like
>"circumference-to-diameter-relation" to describe what is now indicated
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>symbol itself says nothing about its meaning.

The letters pi and uppercase sigma (summation) are unique in that respect
because we were taught them in high school. They are special cases. 

>(The Greek lowercase letter _pi_ actually was available on the
>Commodore line of computers, on their keyboards, and was used in
>Commodore Basic as an unchangeable constant with its normal value of
>3.1415926...)

On my Microsoft calculator, the nomenclature is PI.

>> >The use of single alphabet letters for variables and constants in
>> >common algebraic notation is principally why Greek letters and
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>constant, a single symbol for each operator.

I think mathematical statements are so abbrerviated because they frequently
transformed and rewritten manually. Thus brevity is an asset.

That reason doesn't apply to programming, where readability is more valuable
than writeability. A commercial program is written once; it is read hundreds of
times. 

>Also in spoken English something like "five oranges" is normal. So is
>"five sixes" which is multiplication. If I don't actually know whether
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>algebraic notation, indicating that I have b a's.

That feature is called elision. Multiplication is there, and being implied by
omission. It would also be correct to say 'five of something' or 'five instances
of something', just as it would be mathematically correct to say 5�a. 

COBOL is one of the few programming languages that offers elision, as in 'if a
equal to 1 or 2 or 3'. 

>> >> >> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> >> >> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >> The three are equally precise for expressing a single number. The
superiority
>>  of
>> >> "1502" becomes evident when we do arithmetic operations on it, for example
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> operations on the 'words'. It does not follow that ideographs are superior
for
>> words that will not be transformed. 
>

>If you mean transformed in the mind, let us consider the symbol "/".
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>If five is to ten as one is to two

In programming, the second interpretation is used. 

>The last example is worth looking at. Shouldn't the notation be "If
>5:10 = 1:2 ..." Well anywhere that a colon used as a ratio operator it
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
>expression.

COBOL is similar to math in that respect. It looks like English but its grammar
is rigorously defined. 

Micro Focus permits 'if (5/10) = (1/2)', I don't use it because that _is_
slippery. Unlike math, computers do not have infinite precision. One does not
know the size nor rounding (if any) of the intermediate numbers being compared.
Basic, C and others are likely to use floating-point intermediates. In that
case, there is a high probability they will not be exactly equal. 

When applying tests to inexact numbers, a range test (almost equal, close
enough) is more appropriate than testing for equality.

>> >But in computer languages, the computer does the mathematical work. We
>> >don't need to line up the numbers in columns, so we could spell out
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>to me. But it disagrees with your position on "equal" versus "=".

My objection is to the context in which symbols are used. In 'if invoice-date
less than date-today', we see all words. In 'if invoice-date < date-today', we
must mentally shift gears from words to symbol to words. To what end? Just to
save typing. We don't write English that way, even though the meaning would be
understood by most readers. Fifty years ago, it was common to see & in place of
and. Now that is considered bad style. 

I do not object to the = following COMPUTE because the statement to its right
will probably contain mathematical symbols. It is an assignment operator in a
math statement, not a relational operator in a logic statement.

>The real reason to use variables and symbolic constants is not length,
>but the principal of separating data from program flow. The less one
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>in literals are not.)

Not so with Fortran and Basic. They say, 'Ohh, a new variable I haven't seen
before. I'll set it up.' That's what aversion to typing give us.

>But short variables/constants are good, then just consider "=" as a
>kind of variable/constant that contains the concept _equal_. What is
>the difference?

I use short-named variables ONLY when they have limited scope .. roughly, when
all the code can be seen on one screen. I would never pass a short-named
variable to another procedure, especially implicitly as in PERFORM. Why? Because
when seen out of context, its meaning is unknown. 

I don't think this argument applies to symbols vs. words. There is no ambiguity
in =.  My objection is that it's a different writing system.

>> C programmers solved the problem by spending an entire line on the
terminating
>> symbol. 
>
>A fix in COBOL might have been to allow it to period to sit on a line
>by itself.

It can. This is valid, but seldom used:

if a = b
   set in-balance to true
else
   set in-balance to false
.

>But there was the additional bother that nested if's could not have
>priods of their own.

>That added a level of difficulty to the contruction of nested if's in
>COBOL not found most other languages, indeed *no* other language of
>any following purporting to be for general use.

Agreed, that's the major objection to periods. Before explicit delimiters, we
sometimes were forced to write out-of-line procedures just to get a nested block
of code. For example, 

if a = b
   if c = d
      (action)
   end-if
   set in-balance to true
else

>Some programming languages do use "B += A" which means *exactly* the
>same thing as "ADD A TO B". I think that notation clearer. You may
>not. It does help distinguish operators and relational indicators from
>the data.

But 'B++=A' replaces B with A+1, which is quite different. The notation seems
error-prone to me. 

>> >> What about the remaining hundreds of words in COBOL's lexicon? Would you
>> >> create an ideograph for each? Doing so would make COBOL as cryptic as APL.

>> >
>> >Why not, as a variant of COBOL?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>a person who didn't know English.

His own language would be better, and more Cobol-like. 

>Most of the world's inhabitants don't know English.

Programming is in English because we did much of the seminal work. In times
past, other disciplines required knowledge of Latin or German for the same
reason. WMK mentioned that Micro Focus offers COBOL in other languages.

>So does that leave only Postscript which uses fewer symbols than
>COBOL? (I'm not going to count languages like PCL which don't have
>branches.)

Assembly languages use few symbols. I can't think of any others. 

>> >In any case, the standard numeric digits associated with modern Latin
>> >alphabets are ideographs, whatever their origin, just as &, #, @, $,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> symbols &, @, $, >, %, + and = are logograms -- they represent words. The
others
>> are ideograms -- they do not represent words. Punctuation symbols are all
>> ideograms, notwithstanding the fact they have names. 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>the same in a logographic system for English.

"Exceeds" is one word. 


>> >Use of variables such as I, J, and K for first counter, second
>> >counter, and third counter, are ideographic. The meanings of these
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>standard keyboard and are typed with the same finger in touch-typing.

Rather than I, J, K, I use the first letter of the word it represents: r for
row, c for column, s1 for first sum, s2 for second sum. That gives it a little
meaning when read in context. 

>You are using the redundancy to avoid typos argument only with the
>characters "=", ">", and "<", and seeminly for "=" only when used in a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>and "/" which are also subject to typos?

I don't object to arithmetic symbols because of the advantages of math and
because anyone reading it will know high school algebra, at least. But few
readers will have experience with logical algebra. 

It always struck me as odd that so many programmers have trouble with logic.
Logic is our bread and butter; it's what programs are made out of. Yet many of
them are confused by NOT or more than 2-3 tests in a conditional. They call it
"too complex". They don't see logic as a form or math; they try to get by with
English language skills. 

If they did, we'be discussing symbols for AND, OR and NOT rather than GREATER.
When a logic statement is transformed, those are the operators which change.
Relational operators remain unchanged. 

>This discussion has made me realize the extent to which phonetically
>based writing systems have generally not introduced other symbols,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>increased power of its symbolic representation. So also with logic.

I concur in re math. Symbolic logic is not commonly taught nor understood. As I
said, people don't see it as a form or math. 

>The notation "(5 � 8) + 5 - 3 = 42" is less lengthy and leaps into the
>mind more easily than "(5 multiplied by 8) plus 5 minus 3 is equal to
>42". There is further confusion possible in a notation like "ab plus b
>minus c is equal to d".

But that's exactly how many/most programmers read a logical statement. 



>> >But would you prefer that COBOL's COMPUTE statement did not used the
>> >normal English arithemtic operators, that it used the corresponding
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>What paradigm shift? 

Words to symbol to words.

>Most current programming languages, *including* COBOL, allow or demand
>symbols to indicate most operators.

Not the important logical operators: AND, OR and NOT. Suppose math used symbols
for some operators and words for others. You wouldn't be pleased with that
notation. 

>COBOL does give you a variant notation to use if you wish, the longer
>"[IS] GREATER [THAN]", "[IS] LESS [THAN]", "[IS] EQUAL [TO]", which
>you prefer, but don't seem to be able to justify beyond personal
>preference.

I tried to give reasons above: 
.. An incomplete notational system.
.. Not as familiar as math to many/most readers.


>But why did the CODASYL people put two separate syntaxes for
>conditions into COBOL? Nothing of the kind was done elsewhere in
>COBOL, other than the variations allowed where particular words are
>optional and may be omitted.

COBOL provides 'wordy' arithmetic as an alternative to math-like COMPUTE.

ADD 1 TO a
COMPUTE a = a + 1

>It might be that there was strong disagreement among those who created
>COBOL on this issue, disagreement only appeased by allowing both
>sytaxes.

Probably so.

>But in fact if "<" and ">" were to be made part of the language, there
>was also a very pratical reason why there *had* to be an alternate
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
>"<" is more undesirable than "+".

Again, because many programmers do not see logic thru mathematical eyes. They
use conversational skills to understand it.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 57)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T17:20:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bado49$pq4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305181504.611b8836@posting.google.com> <3eca52b4.98147976@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 20-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> My objection is to the context in which symbols are used. In 'if invoice-date
> less than date-today', we see all words. In 'if invoice-date < date-today', we
> must mentally shift gears from words to symbol to words.

We do?  I just read them.   Sure "<" is not spelled phonetically, but lots of
words aren't spelled phonetically, so we just have to recognize these words.   
I do read it as "is less than", including the word "is", and when I spell it out
I include the "is", as that's how I say that expression in English.   When I
read " IF invoice-date less than", I back track and add the "is" as well.

> To what end? Just to save typing.

So you have said.   Others have said it is to be more easily read.   I don't
know how to resolve who is correct here.

> We don't write English that way, even though the meaning would be
> understood by most readers.

> Fifty years ago, it was common to see & in place of  and. Now that is
> considered bad style.

Interesting, I wasn't aware of those.   Is this "it is considered" a reference
to your opinion, or is it a reference to some authority this time?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 57)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T16:20:54-07:00
