- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305201520.b6c65ef@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305181504.611b8836@posting.google.com> <3eca52b4.98147976@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> Equations such as c=b ln(1+s/n), v=ir and e=mcï¿½ would be less memorable if
> they were written in Greek.

And who actually knows that the 'c' stands for the Latin word
celeritas ?
 
> The letters pi and uppercase sigma (summation) are unique in that respect
> because we were taught them in high school. They are special cases. 

Weren't you taught delta (increment) and ro (density) and several
others, or did you drop out before you got that far ?

Those two may be unique [sic] to _your_ schooling, not to mine.
 
> On my Microsoft calculator, the nomenclature is PI.

I am not sure that I would use anything from MS as an example of
'standard' in any wider sense than it is what MS do.
 
> I think mathematical statements are so abbrerviated because they frequently
> transformed and rewritten manually. Thus brevity is an asset.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> of times. 

I find "if ( balance-total > credit-limit )" to be _MORE_ readable
than using words such as 'greater than'.  Thank you for providing an
argument to support my use of symbols.

> Micro Focus permits 'if (5/10) = (1/2)', I don't use it because that _is_
> slippery. Unlike math, computers do not have infinite precision. One does not
> know the size nor rounding (if any) of the intermediate numbers being compared.
> Basic, C and others are likely to use floating-point intermediates. In that
> case, there is a high probability they will not be exactly equal. 

Actually the rules are quite adequately defined for those that bother
to read the manual instead of just guessing what might happen.

C is not 'likely to' use anything that is not part of the definition
of what it will use.
 
> My objection is to the context in which symbols are used. In 'if invoice-date
> less than date-today', we see all words. In 'if invoice-date < date-today', we
> must mentally shift gears from words to symbol to words. 

_YOU_ may have to 'shift gears'.  I do not.  In fact I find that the
word version is harder to understand at a glance.

What you fail to realise is that we all recognise what we are used to.
 If you are used to the words then you will recognise them readily. 
If I am used to the symbols then I will recognise them readily.  It is
a matter of training the brain to do the recognition and conversion
for you.

To me APL looks like chicken scratchings, to those who have learnt it
they see concise and instantly meaningful code.

> Fifty years ago, it was common to see & in place of
> and. Now that is considered bad style. 

By whom ?  It is still very common in, say, company names: Smith & Co.

> I do not object to the = following COMPUTE because the statement to its right
> will probably contain mathematical symbols. 

The statement to the right will _also_ contain words.

> It can. This is valid, but seldom used:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> .

In what way is it 'seldom used' ?  _All_ my full stops in procedure
division are either at the end of the procedure label (paragraph name)
or on a line by itself.  The implication of your example is that the
full stop is an alternative to the end-if.

> But 'B++=A' replaces B with A+1, which is quite different. The notation seems
> error-prone to me. 

Certainly your understanding of the language is error prone.  'B++=A'
is invalid because B++ is an rvalue and the assignment operator
requires an lvalue to the left of it.  There are rules governing how
these expressions can be assembled, you obviously need to learn some
of these.

> Assembly languages use few symbols. I can't think of any others. 

Which particular 'assembly language' of the many hundreds of quite
different ones do you think uses 'few symbols' ?
 
> Rather than I, J, K, I use the first letter of the word it represents: r for
> row, c for column, s1 for first sum, s2 for second sum. That gives it a little
> meaning when read in context. 

I, J, K are traditionally used as counters because in earlier
languages variable names beginning with these letters were implicitly
Integer while other letters were implicitly real.  It is an idiom.

> It always struck me as odd that so many programmers have trouble with logic.
> Logic is our bread and butter; it's what programs are made out of. Yet many of
> them are confused by NOT or more than 2-3 tests in a conditional. They call it
> "too complex". They don't see logic as a form or math; they try to get by with
> English language skills. 

I find it odd that you find it possible to argue both sides
alternately.  On the one hand you claim 'linguistics' and want Cobol
to be english, then later you claim exactly the opposite.

> If they did, we'be discussing symbols for AND, OR and NOT rather than GREATER.

I am quite happy to use &&, || and ! when required.

> When a logic statement is transformed, those are the operators which change.
> Relational operators remain unchanged. 

Not necessarily.  Transform < to >=, > to <=.  You do it one way,
others can do it another, it is a matter of style.

> >The notation "(5 ï¿½ 8) + 5 - 3 = 42" is less lengthy and leaps into the
> >mind more easily than "(5 multiplied by 8) plus 5 minus 3 is equal to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> But that's exactly how many/most programmers read a logical statement. 

Som may, you seem to.  I don't, others don't.  Depends on what they
have trained their brains to recognise.

> Not the important logical operators: AND, OR and NOT. Suppose math used symbols
> for some operators and words for others. You wouldn't be pleased with that
> notation. 

Only until I got used to it <shrug>.  In fact if that is what they did
do then I _would_ be used to it.  In fact they _do_ use sine, cosine,
tan, log etc which _are_ words and it _doesn't_ displease me.

I do find that the arguments that you attempt to contrive are rather
shallow.

You argue about how programmers 'see' things when it is only your own
experience you are relying on, and mine is the opposite.  You argue
about how maths doesn't use words when I know it does.


> I tried to give reasons above: 
> .. An incomplete notational system.
> .. Not as familiar as math to many/most readers.

They are reasons, just not cogent ones. 
 
> >In the early 50's the IBM BCD character set had none of these. When
> >Fortran came along "+", "=", "(" and ")" were put in the place of "&",

Actually FORTRAN used .EQ. .GT. for exactly that reason.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 58)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T11:46:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eccaeab.252786284@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305181504.611b8836@posting.google.com> <3eca52b4.98147976@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305201520.b6c65ef@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>celeritas ?

Very few. 

>> The letters pi and uppercase sigma (summation) are unique in that respect
>> because we were taught them in high school. They are special cases. 
>
>Weren't you taught delta (increment) and ro (density) and several
>others, or did you drop out before you got that far ?

Delta was taught in calculus, which most students don't take. I've not seen ro
used for density in physics; is that from engineering?

>> On my Microsoft calculator, the nomenclature is PI.
>
>I am not sure that I would use anything from MS as an example of
>'standard' in any wider sense than it is what MS do.

A hasty generalization. Because you don't like their operating system (or
whatever), that invalidates everything they say and do. 

>> Micro Focus permits 'if (5/10) = (1/2)', I don't use it because that _is_
>> slippery. Unlike math, computers do not have infinite precision. One does not
>> know the size nor rounding (if any) of the intermediate numbers being
compared.
>> Basic, C and others are likely to use floating-point intermediates. In that
>> case, there is a high probability they will not be exactly equal. 
>
>Actually the rules are quite adequately defined for those that bother
>to read the manual instead of just guessing what might happen.

The manual for which compiler? My Cobol beautifier and date functions have run
on more than six platforms. 

>> if a = b
>>    set in-balance to true
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>full stop is an alternative to the end-if.

Full stop was the only if terminator before 85. It could still be used that way.


Since reading CLC, my periods are all on the same line with paragraph names. 

>> 'B++=A'
>is invalid because B++ is an rvalue and the assignment operator
>requires an lvalue to the left of it.  

Whoops, sorry. Make that C = B++ = A.

>> Assembly languages use few symbols. I can't think of any others. 
>
>Which particular 'assembly language' of the many hundreds of quite
>different ones do you think uses 'few symbols' ?

All that I know use a comma between operands,  brackets or parentheses around
modifiers, and quotes around literals. Excepting conditional assembly, those are
the only symbols commonly used. Note that all three are punctuation, not
logograms. 

>> Rather than I, J, K, I use the first letter of the word it represents: r for
>> row, c for column, s1 for first sum, s2 for second sum. That gives it a
little
>> meaning when read in context. 
>
>I, J, K are traditionally used as counters because in earlier
>languages variable names beginning with these letters were implicitly
>Integer while other letters were implicitly real.  It is an idiom.

Outside programming, the idiom for ordinality is n, as in 'the nth case'. The
letter n is also within the Fortran/Basic range for integer. 

>> It always struck me as odd that so many programmers have trouble with logic.
>> Logic is our bread and butter; it's what programs are made out of. Yet many
of
>> them are confused by NOT or more than 2-3 tests in a conditional. They call
it
>> "too complex". They don't see logic as a form or math; they try to get by
with
>> English language skills. 
>
>I find it odd that you find it possible to argue both sides
>alternately.  On the one hand you claim 'linguistics' and want Cobol
>to be english, then later you claim exactly the opposite.

I argue for math notation where appropriate, English elsewhere. Logic is really
math but there are two problems preventing math-like notation:

.. No symbols for AND, OR and NOT (in COBOL)
.. Many/most programmers don't think of logic as math, don't know how to
manipulate it using algebra

>> If they did, we'be discussing symbols for AND, OR and NOT rather than
GREATER.
>
>I am quite happy to use &&, || and ! when required.

If COBOL supported those symbols, I wouldn't object to >. 

>> When a logic statement is transformed, those are the operators which change.
>> Relational operators remain unchanged. 
>
>Not necessarily.  Transform < to >=, > to <=.  You do it one way,
>others can do it another, it is a matter of style.

I prefer the simplicity of 'not <' because many/most programmers think '>=' is a
compound condition containing OR. Also because COBOL is unsymmetrical; it
doesn't offer '<>'.

Using 'not <' makes the transformation rule regular; using '>=' makes it
irregular. Don't you agree that regularity is a Good Thing in math?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T14:11:56+00:00

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eccf646.271119787@news.optonline.net...

> Suppose COBOL offered the - symbol for subtraction ...

Interesting supposition.   I've never run into an implementation that
didn't.

> (that's what comparison is) ...

Lemme get this straight.  The condition "AN-ALPHANUMERIC-ITEM IS NOT EQUAL
TO ANOTHER-ALPHANUMERIC-ITEM" actually uses the SUBT operator and not the
CREL operator?

And all this time I've thought character comparison operators were
appropriate for this.  Silly me.

> while making you use words for addition and multiplication.

...  as for example "COMPUTE X = Y - (Z MULTIPLIED-BY Q) INCREMENTED-BY M"?

> Would you use such an incomplete system? I wouldn't; I'd use words for
everything.

Have at it.  You may have fun trying to find  very many compilers that will
accept your oh-so-perfect source, though.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T00:43:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ecd6b01.301006757@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305181504.611b8836@posting.google.com> <3eca52b4.98147976@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305201520.b6c65ef@posting.google.com> <3eccaeab.252786284@news.optonline.net> <bailrb$dq9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccf646.271119787@news.optonline.net> <baivk4$14cu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>didn't.

It was a hypothetical. 

>> (that's what comparison is) ...
>
>Lemme get this straight.  The condition "AN-ALPHANUMERIC-ITEM IS NOT EQUAL
>TO ANOTHER-ALPHANUMERIC-ITEM" actually uses the SUBT operator and not the
>CREL operator?

Exactly. Deep down in the hardware, the compare instruction is doing a
subtraction. 

>And all this time I've thought character comparison operators were
>appropriate for this.  Silly me.

During 'all this time' you should have checked with chip designers there at
Unisys or read their documentation. You would have learned your intuition is
incorrect. Their CREL is doing a SUB under the covers. 

>> while making you use words for addition and multiplication.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>accept your oh-so-perfect source, though.

No trouble at all. Every COBOL compiler supports >, < and = for subtraction, but
only OR and AND for addition and multiplication.
```

Robert Wagner wrote:


> Suppose COBOL offered the - symbol for subtraction (that's what comparison
> is) while making you use words for addition and multiplication. Would you
> use such an incomplete system? I wouldn't; I'd use words for everything.

But it _does_ do that.  It offers symbols for * - / + ** and words for 
FUNCTION SQRT(), FUNCTION LOG().

Your arguments always seem to be so superficial and arbitrary.  Try 
thinking them through for a while before contriving nonsense arguments.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 62)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T09:14:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ecdce79.8521556@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eccaeab.252786284@news.optonline.net> <bailrb$dq9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccf646.271119787@news.optonline.net> <bajdfm$uqv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>FUNCTION SQRT(), FUNCTION LOG().

You don't get it. Yes, COBOL has symbols for math operations but not for logical
operations. 

>Your arguments always seem to be so superficial and arbitrary.  Try 
>thinking them through for a while before contriving nonsense arguments.

Your arguments are superficial (A) because you don't think them through (B). 

If we're interested in establishing A, and B is offered as evidence, the
statement "A because B" is an argument.  If we're trying to establish the truth
of B, then "A because B" is not an argument, it  is an explanation. The Ad Hoc
fallacy is to give an after-the-fact explanation which does not apply to other
situations.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T16:08:20-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ECD2E34.7040606@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305181504.611b8836@posting.google.com> <3eca52b4.98147976@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305201520.b6c65ef@posting.google.com> <3eccaeab.252786284@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
 > riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
 >> robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
 > Delta was taught in calculus, which most students don't take.

Capital delta, for increment or difference, was very definitely
introduced in my 10th grade "pre-calculus" / trigonometry math class.
It was a prereq for 11th grade beginning calculus, which was a prereq
for 12th grade advanced calculus.  And except for 11th grade I wasn't in
the most advanced math track in my high school.

Lowercase delta, for partial differentials, didn't come up until 12th
grade, in advanced calc, AP chemistry, and AP physics.  Ditto capital
pi, for product.  Let's see... lowercase alpha, "is proportional", 11th
/ 12th grade physics.  Lowercase lambda, "wavelength", 11th / 12th grade
physics.  Lowercase mu, "micro", 11th / 12th grade physics.  Capital
omega, "Ohm", 11th / 12th grade physics.  I vaguely recall several
others showing up - lowercase nu, lowercase sigma, lowercase phi all
have hazy math associations.

Oh, and of course we'd been using lower-case theta for angle since
forever - probably saw that by 9th grade geometry at the latest, but
likely before then.

I don't recall seeing ro used in either of my high school physics
classes - may have always just used m/v for density - but I may have
forgotten.

It may well be true that most students don't take calculus, but it's not
safe to infer from that that most don't see delta used in their math
classes.  I might have even seen it in 8th grade algebra; certainly we
used the concept for such exercises as determining slope.  (Hmm, maybe
even in 7th grade intro to algebra.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T15:00:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305221400.4df2a279@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305181504.611b8836@posting.google.com> <3eca52b4.98147976@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305201520.b6c65ef@posting.google.com> <3eccaeab.252786284@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>>> .. and e=mcï¿½ would be less memorable if they were written in
Greek.
> >
> >And who actually knows that the 'c' stands for the Latin word
> >celeritas ?
> 
> Very few. 

And yet it doesn't seem to be 'less memorable' for being written in
Latin, why would it be so if it was in Greek ?

The others ?  I've forgotten already, they couldn't be less memorable
to me.
 
> >> The letters pi and uppercase sigma (summation) are unique in that respect
> >> because we were taught them in high school. They are special cases. 
> 
> Delta was taught in calculus, which most students don't take. 

So you do agree that pi and sigma were not unique [sic].  Yet again
you make superficial and nonsense arguments which are clearly wrong
(to us).

> I've not seen ro used for density ...

I am sure there are many things that you've not seen.  Sometimes you
assume that means they don't exist.

> >I am not sure that I would use anything from MS as an example of
> >'standard' in any wider sense than it is what MS do.
> 
> A hasty generalization. 

No, not hasty.  One that has been built up over many years and with
many examples.

> >> Basic, C and others are likely to use floating-point intermediates. In that
> >> case, there is a high probability they will not be exactly equal. 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> on more than six platforms. 

Your assertion that 'C and others are _likely_ to use floating-point'
in any situation for which this use is not defined is not supported by
anything other than your lack of knowledge in this area.

 
> >In what way is it 'seldom used' ?  _All_ my full stops in procedure
> >division are either at the end of the procedure label (paragraph name)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Full stop was the only if terminator before 85. It could still be used that way.

Yes, but it is not an _alternative_.  You don't have to take the full
stop off when you add the END-IF.  You _can_ have both.

The issue was 'Full stops on their own line', these are not 'seldom'. 

> >> 'B++=A'
> >is invalid because B++ is an rvalue and the assignment operator
> >requires an lvalue to the left of it.  
> 
> Whoops, sorry. Make that C = B++ = A.

That is _still_ invalid.  For _exactly_ the same reason that I just
explained.  B++ is an rvalue and cannot be to the left of an assigment
operator, putting C = in front of the expression makes no difference
to this.  When you have grasped rvalues and lvalues then you can move
on to see why your assertion about confusing operators just doesn't
happen (to anyone with two clues).

> All that I know use a comma between operands,  brackets or parentheses around
> modifiers, and quotes around literals. Excepting conditional assembly, those are
> the only symbols commonly used. Note that all three are punctuation, not
> logograms. 

Which leaves about 500 to check before you can say what 'assembly
languages' do. Or are you relying on a sample of 3 to extrapolate to
everything else, again ?

In fact I can find  assemblers that use # & + (addition) -
(subtraction) * (multiply) / (divide) and if I looked hard enough I
know that I will find dozens of other usages.

Once again if you haven't seen it you claim it doesn't exist.
 
> Outside programming, the idiom for ordinality is n, as in 'the nth case'. The
> letter n is also within the Fortran/Basic range for integer. 

Your point being ?

> > On the one hand you claim 'linguistics' and want Cobol
> >to be english, then later you claim exactly the opposite.
> 
> I argue for math notation where appropriate, English elsewhere. 

It does seem to be quite arbitrary sometimes.  For example you avoid
'>' except when you feel like using it.

> .. No symbols for AND, OR and NOT (in COBOL)

<shrug> get over it, most of us cope.

> .. Many/most programmers don't think of logic as math, don't know how to
> manipulate it using algebra

Perhaps because they have never needed to learn some set of rules. 
They just write code that works, and write it another way that works
if necessary.

> If COBOL supported those symbols, I wouldn't object to >. 

Yeah, right.

> I prefer the simplicity of 'not <' because many/most programmers think '>=' 
> is a compound condition containing OR. 

They do ?  IF ( a >= b )
           IF ( a > b ) OR ( a = b )

Well waddaya know.

> Also because COBOL is unsymmetrical; it doesn't offer '<>'.

I am surprised that you don't dump the whole thing and go and do PERL.

> Using 'not <' makes the transformation rule regular; 

That depends on the rules being used.  You have contrived _your_ set
of rules based around 'adding not' or 'removing not'.  A different set
of rules may use other substitutions and still be 'regular'.

> using '>=' makes it
> irregular. Don't you agree that regularity is a Good Thing in math?

Actually 'regular' just means 'by regulation' or following a set of
rules.  Whether it is 'irregular' depends entirely on the actual
rules.  It only seems that '>=' makes it irregular because your own
set of rules doesn't include it.  My set of rules does include it and
so '>=' _is_ regular (to me).

Once again you show your limitations and then try to contrive
arguments to support them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 55)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-19T15:16:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<baash6$el5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 17-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Ideographs are superior for numbers because they make it easier to perform
> operations on the 'words'. It does not follow that ideographs are superior for
> words that will not be transformed.

Numerals are a subset of all ideographs, and finding examples of this subset
where we agree that ideographs are superior certainly does not imply that ALL
cases are superior.   But one contrary example does disprove that "words are
superior to ideographs".

What are "words that will not be transformed", in this context?    Is the symbol
">" transformed in some different aspect than the symbol "7" is?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 56)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T10:22:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec9d016.64704698@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebd5572.186196194@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <baash6$el5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:


>What are "words that will not be transformed", in this context?    Is the
symbol
>">" transformed in some different aspect than the symbol "7" is?

If a Boolean transformation is performed, the symbol ">" will not be changed.
What will be changed are the words AND, OR and NOT.
```

robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >But Robert must sit and stare at a "<" for a few seconds to determine
> >if it means "is less than" or "is greater than".  Because he has
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The topic is words vs. logographs. If you can't think of a relevent response,

Well actually it is relevant.  You are trying to convince us that
words are 'better' and it was you that said you have to inspect these
symbols closely because you mix them up.

Starting from a conclusion that '>' is confusing (though only to you
as it happens) you have contrived all sorts of meaningless and
irrelevant arguments in an attempt to explain why _we_ should use
words.

You obviously have a very strong emotional attachment to a whole
jumble of nonsense reasons, from what the Goths did to how the
keyboards are laid out. All of which are basically trying to show that
it is not your (RW) fault that you can't cope with <>.

> posting an ad hominem does not add to your credibility nor provide useful
> information to others.

Actually it is not ad hominem if it is true.

> >This has been a great thread.  I've always wondered who used those
> >bulky, spelled-out conditionals.  Now I know.
> 
> You haven't learned anything. 

Oooooo, a put down.

> All you've done is created a public record making you look immature.

He may actually be young, his father was probably too young to have
been around when Cobol was all uppercase, on punch cards, and used
'spelled-out conditionals' because the print chain didn't have
adequate symbols.  But that was all nearly 40 years ago.  Most of us
have moved on since then.
```

```
On Wed, 21 May 2003 06:54:27 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
wrote:

> tukla_ratte@tukla.net (Tukla Ratte) wrote:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

< snip >

> >> >>In what way is using such symbols as '>', '=', '*',etc in programming
> >> >>a 'paradigm shift' ?  These have been used in programming since well
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> The topic is words vs. logographs. If you can't think of a relevent response,

My response was relevant.  Jeff stated how symbolic conditionals are
easier for him to spot in a piece of code.  I mentioned (in case he
had missed it in this enormous thread) that you have told us how you
have to mentally "switch gears" from words to symbols and back to
words.  You have repeated this numerous time, making it sound like it
is a great effort for you.  If that is not true, then perhaps you
should stop talking about "switching gears".

FWIW, *I* have to "switch gears" three times when I see "IF A NOT LESS
THAN B".  First, I have to backtrack and put in the missing "IS" after
the "A" to make it grammatical.  Then I have to convert the "LESS
THAN" to "<" in my head.  Then I have to apply the "NOT".  On the
other hand, I find the equivalent conditional "IF A >= B" immediately
readable.

I've always assumed that the reason for this is because I used a more
symbol-oriented language (BASIC) when first learning to program.
Unlike you, I'm not trying to invent a reason why my preference is
inherently "better" for *everyone*.  It obviously isn't.

> posting an ad hominem

Is it technically an ad hominem when I'm not debating you argument?
Much more intelligent people than I have already refuted your claims.

> does not add to your credibility nor provide useful
> information to others.

How is summarizing your own statements regarding your difficulty
reading symbols not providing useful information?

> >This has been a great thread.  I've always wondered who used those
> >bulky, spelled-out conditionals.  Now I know.
> 
> You haven't learned anything. All you've done is created a public record making
> you look immature. 

LOL!  Google Groups contains years of postings I've made making myself
look immature.  You'll have to try harder than that to insult me.

Besides, *your* public record shows your ignorance of linguistic
development, both natural and computer; your limited knowledge of
mathematical notation; your contradictory attitudes toward symbols
used in different contexts; and your habit of making absolute
statements about what is "correct" and what is "generally accepted"
without posting references or without making it clear that you are
really only stating your own personal preference.

And that's just in two recent threads!
```

>In article <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>>tukla_ratte@tukla.net (Tukla Ratte) wrote:

>>>FWIW, *I* have to "switch gears" three times when I see "IF A NOT LESS
>>>THAN B".  First, I have to backtrack and put in the missing "IS" after
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>If you find >= superior to not <, you've destroyed your credability in re
logic.
>
>Mr Wagner, please be so kind as to demonstrate how one's ability to 
>readily recognise a set of symbols has anything, whatsoever, to do with 
>logical capabilities... at your leisure, of course.

I already have .. in a post showing how to mechanically invert a complex logical
statement by changing '<' to 'not <' and 'not <' to '<'. 

If we were required to change '<' to '>=', the transformation would be more
prone to error.
```

Robert, I don't know what "it" you are talking about in "It's not my way, it

It is my impression (from the MAJORITY of posts to this NG *and* the
majority of code that I have seen over the years) that the use of ">" and
"<" rather than "greater than" and "less than" is the "norm".

When it comes to arithmetic, other than the "most simple cases", I STRONGLY
suspect that the use of COMPUTE with the mathematical symbols + - * and /
are SIGNIFICANTLY more common than a SERIES of arithmetic statements to
perform the same "task" - and of course there is no COBOL "alphabetic word"
to accomplish what ** does.

If you mean that something else is the "norm," can you clarify of what you
are speaking?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 69)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T06:20:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>is the norm."

The subject was how to how to reverse the truth of a conditional. I had written:

> He doesn't know how to transform logical statements. If he did, he'd
> understand why not < is superior to >=

My argument was you cannot write NOT >= (nobody here seems to know that).
Reversing its truth requires changing a relational operator, in this case to <.
In all other cases except >= and its companion, changes are made to logical
operators; relational operators are left unchanged. 

When I said "it is the norm", I meant that's how it is done in math.

>It is my impression (from the MAJORITY of posts to this NG *and* the
>majority of code that I have seen over the years) that the use of ">" and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>perform the same "task" - 

I don't dispute either.

>and of course there is no COBOL "alphabetic word" to accomplish what ** does.

Yes there are:

compute a = b ** c

compute a = exp(log(b) * c)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 70)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T15:47:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 26-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> My argument was you cannot write NOT >= (nobody here seems to know that).
> Reversing its truth requires changing a relational operator, in this case to
> <.
> In all other cases except >= and its companion, changes are made to logical
> operators; relational operators are left unchanged.

Generally, I make extensive use of the CONTINUE/ELSE structure available in
CoBOL when I want to reverse the logic from a conditional.

IF A => B
      CONTINUE
ELSE
    ...
END-IF

or better yet in this case

IF A < B
   ...
END-IF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

- **Message-ID:** `<3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net>`

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 26-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>END-IF

That looks clumsy, like the code is tripping over itself .. or like the
programmer couldn't figure out how to reverse the condition.  It is equivalent
to the following, with two fewer lines of code:

IF NOT (A -> B)
   ....
END-IF

>or better yet in this case
>
>IF A < B
>   ...
>END-IF

Yep, that's the right way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

> >      CONTINUE
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

But it's clear, can be easily reversed, and it is easy to replace the CONTINUE
with code.   A maintenance programmer doesn't get slowed down a second, as he is
with the NOT logic.

Clumsy is in the eye of the beholder - but clarity is pretty obvious.

- **Message-ID:** `<vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> >But it's clear, can be easily reversed, and it is easy to replace the
CONTINUE
> >with code.   A maintenance programmer doesn't get slowed down a second,
as he
> is
> >with the NOT logic.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> To me, it seems clearer to say the condition you want rather than the
condition
> you don't want.

The condition one may want is 'A >= B', when it is clear
that 'if A >= B, do not process'. This may appear in
specifications or other documents as a negative, such as;
exclude, reject, etc.

In these cases, it is clearer to write:

if A >= B
  continue *> exclude, reject, etc.
else
  *> default process
end-if

>
> Maintenance programmers slowed down by NOT are the kind who hold their
heads and
> say 'I'm so confused by logic'. I teach them to understand it.

Maintenance programmers may be slowed when a program
does not clearly implement a specification. This is not a question
of style, nor a matter of logic, it matters not that the example is
simple, it is whether the original programmer did their job well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 75)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T11:10:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

>
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net...

>> To me, it seems clearer to say the condition you want rather than the
>condition
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>end-if

In that case the program would perform an error handler; it wouldn't continue.

>>
>> Maintenance programmers slowed down by NOT are the kind who hold their
>heads and
>> say 'I'm so confused by logic'.

>Maintenance programmers may be slowed when a program
>does not clearly implement a specification. This is not a question
>of style, nor a matter of logic, it matters not that the example is
>simple, it is whether the original programmer did their job well.

As a matter of style and communication, I put the normal or most common case
first. For example,

if  transaction-type = 'a' or 'b' or 'c'
    if  store-number not = spaces
        perform process transaction
    else
        perform reject-no-store
    end-if
else
    perform reject-invalid-type
end-if

-- better yet --

set no-error to true
perform validate-1
if  no-error
    perform validate-2
    if  no-error
        perform validate-3
    end-if
end-if
if  no-error
    *> default processing
else
    perform report-error
end-if

A 'positive thinking' program puts normal processing in the forefront. A
'negative thinking' program talks about all the things that might go wrong then,
almost as an afterthought, does normal processing if it cannot find anything to
complain about. 

Someone reading a program for the first time wants to know 'what does this
program do?'. Making him wade through exception tests before getting to 'the
meat' doesn't answer the question  clearly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 76)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T08:09:58-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:

[snip]

>As a matter of style and communication, I put the normal or most common case
>first.

[snip]

>A 'positive thinking' program puts normal processing in the forefront. A
>'negative thinking' program talks about all the things that might go wrong then,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>meat' doesn't answer the question  clearly. 

How very interesting... different ways to the same end and different ways 
to a different end.

I was taught, lo, those many years ago, to start coding by Knowing Your
Data.  After you got to Know Your Data then the first condition to code
for was the most common... in order to save machine-time.  Do most of your
customers come from states for which you have to charge sales-tax on a
purchase?  Fine, early in the code gets written

MOVE G1752-PURCH-ST  TO  WS-TST-ST.
IF NOT VALID-STATE
    PERFORM 81653-INVALID-ST-RTN  THRU  81653-EX
    GO TO (current-paragraph-label)-EXIT.
IF STATE-TAX-NEEDED
    SEARCH ALL WS-STATE-TBL
        AT END PERFORM 81654-TBL-NEEDS-UPDT  THRU  81654-EX
        WHEN G1752-PURC-ST = WS-TAXING-ST (ST-IDX)
            MOVE LIT-Y                       TO  WS-CHARGE-TAX-FL
            MOVE WS-STATE-TAX-RATE (ST-IDX)  TO  WS-TAXRATE-USED.

(One might ask the need for setting a flag with LIT-Y when it is obvious 
that WS-TST-ST is 88'd... this is because LIT-Y and WS-CHARGE-TAX-FL are 
single character fields and WS-TST-ST is multi-character; a 
single-character test compiles to a CLI and a CLI is at least three times 
faster than a CLC)

(Notice also two different error-routines, one for a state not found in 
the 88s for WS-TST-ST and another for an out-of-date table of state tax 
rates... the advantages and disadvantages of such a structure might be 
reserved for later discussion.)

(Notice also that I could not help myself and coded, first and foremost, a 
negative conditional check on the state's validity... maybe I had better 
stop here... naaaahhhhhh.)

Anyhow... if the program in question is aways down deep in processing, 
after the edit programs have been run, then there's less of a case to be 
made for checking for the purity of the data and more of a case to be made 
for checking in order of frequency of occurrence; if you are generating 
offers to be mailed to the subscribers to Teen Droolers magazine you 
waste fewer CPU cycles looking for WS-AGE-RANGE-12-17 first and if you are 
generating offers to be mailed to the subscribers to Geezer Droolers it 
makes more sense to check for WS-AGE-RANGE-GT-60.

Likewise... if you are writing one of the edit programs that is higher up 
in the processing stream (or an online data-entry program) then it makes 
more sense to check for the most frequent error first... consider:

MOVE A15SSNI  TO  WS-TST-SSN.
IF WS-TST-SSN = SPACES OR HIGH-VALUES OR LOW-VALUES
    MOVE WS-NEED-SSN-MSG  TO  A15MESSO
    MOVE LIT-M            TO  A15-ACT-COD
    MOVE -1               TO  A15SSNL
    PERFORM 61528-SEND-MAP
    GO TO 71425-RETURN-TRANSID.

IF DUMMY-SSN-ENTERED
**NO MORE DUMMY SSN (111111111, 222222222 ETC) AS PER SFA 12/85)
    MOVE WS-NO-DMY-SSN-MSG  TO  A15MESSO
    MOVE LIT-M              TO  A15-ACT-COD
    MOVE -1                 TO  A15SSNL
    PERFORM 61528-SEND-MAP
    GO TO 71425-RETURN-TRANSID.

MOVE WS-TST-SSN  TO  WS-FRAMTXN-KEY.

EXEC CICS READ FILE('FRAMTXN')
               RIDFLD(WS-FRAMTXN-KEY)
...etc.

These rules, of course, were from the days when Every I-O Cost A Lot, 
Every CPU Cycle Cost A Bit Less (But Still Cost *Something*) and Every 
Data-Entry Operator Would Get Up For Lunch In Mid-Screen.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 77)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T10:13:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>>'negative thinking' program talks about all the things that might go wrong
then,
>>almost as an afterthought, does normal processing if it cannot find anything
to
>>complain about. 
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>to a different end.

I used to do it for speed; now I do it for clarity. In this case the end is the
same, but I've seen lots of Bad Code forged on the anvil of 'efficiency'.
Pre-optimization is bottom-up design .. the machine tail wagging the system dog.
We should design top-down, later optimize only the points of congestion. 

>I was taught, lo, those many years ago, to start coding by Knowing Your
>Data.  After you got to Know Your Data then the first condition to code
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>faster than a CLC)

You're bit-twiddling while overlooking better speed improvement available
through 'caching'. It is likely the state will be the same as the previous one
. perhaps the previous line on the same invoice, perhaps the previous customer
because customers tend to be clustered by geography. I would write it:

if  purchase-state not = previous-state or previous-state = spaces
    move purchase-state to previous-state
    evaluate true
        when  not valid-state
              perform report-invalid-state
              move spaces to previous-state
        when not state-tax-needed
              move zero to tax-rate-used
        when other
              search all state-table
                   at end
                       perform table-needs-update
                   when taxing-state (state-index) = purchase-state
                       move state-tax-rate (state-index) to tax-rate-used
               end-search
        end-evaluate
end-if

That's not only faster but also easier on the eye. 

>(Notice also two different error-routines, one for a state not found in 
>the 88s for WS-TST-ST and another for an out-of-date table of state tax 
>rates... the advantages and disadvantages of such a structure might be 
>reserved for later discussion.)

Yes, also thousands of city and county sales taxes. Calculating US sales tax
isn't as simple as searching a table of states. 

>(Notice also that I could not help myself and coded, first and foremost, a 
>negative conditional check on the state's validity... maybe I had better 
>stop here... naaaahhhhhh.)

I fixed that problem for you.  <g>

>Likewise... if you are writing one of the edit programs that is higher up 
>in the processing stream (or an online data-entry program) then it makes 
>more sense to check for the most frequent error first

This is a good example of sacrificing clarity by  'pre-optimizing' code that
needs no optimization. There is no point in 'optimizing' error detection because
errors are infrequent. The vast majority of transactions will pass through all
the tests. 

<ugly code snipped>

>These rules, of course, were from the days when Every I-O Cost A Lot, 
>Every CPU Cycle Cost A Bit Less (But Still Cost *Something*) and Every 
>Data-Entry Operator Would Get Up For Lunch In Mid-Screen.

If you wrote pseudo-conversational, lunch didn't cost anything. If you'd used
'caching', IO would have been less expensive because you would have done fewer
of them. If you were truly concerned about CPU cycles, you would have optimized
CICS. *It* was the 'MIPS sink', much more than application programs. I used to
front-end CICS functions and provide equivalent  functionality with 100 times
the speed. 

Bottom-up saves two clocks with a CLI while top-down makes the CICS beast run 20
times faster. My can discuss my documentation later. 

Speaking of documentation, yesterday I stumbled into this management article:

http://www.bcentral.com/articles/special/boss.asp
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 78)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T07:24:55-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>I used to do it for speed; now I do it for clarity.

Mr Wagner, the very next sentence here indicated what I was taught 'lo, 
those many years ago' and 'what was written'... nothing whatsoever 
indicates that this is what is done now.

>In this case the end is the
>same, but I've seen lots of Bad Code forged on the anvil of 'efficiency'.

I've seen lots of Bad Code written for a variety of reasons.

>Pre-optimization is bottom-up design .. the machine tail wagging the system dog.
>We should design top-down, later optimize only the points of congestion. 

Plural majestatus est, Mr Wagner... please note that I was relating 
anecdotes about what I was taught 'lo, those many years ago' and not 
seeking to make recommendations of any kind.

Also please note that COBOL code has a habit of... hanging around for a 
few years.  It might just be that someone ('oh, do it For The Newbies!') 
reading here might have seen constructs *exactly* like those I post and 
wondered 'what is the reason for doing it that way?'... and such a reason 
might be found here.

>
>>I was taught, lo, those many years ago, to start coding by Knowing Your
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>through 'caching'.

No, Mr Wagner, I am making sure that I use the cheapest, fastest set of 
instructions... every CPU-cycle costs, remember?

>It is likely the state will be the same as the previous one
>. perhaps the previous line on the same invoice, perhaps the previous customer
>because customers tend to be clustered by geography.

It is 'possible', Mr Wagner, not 'likely'... I have seen situations where 
customers are geographically grouped and where they are geographically 
random (last name sort, SSN sequence, etc.)  This is part of 'Konwing Your 
Data', as mentioned above.

>I would write it:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>That's not only faster but also easier on the eye. 

Depends on *whose* eye, Mr Wagner... what is 'easier on the eye', Samuel
Taylor Coleridge or e e cummings?  It also relies on compiler-mechanisms
that were not available 'lo, those years ago' and as such I could not have
been taught it... but that's all right, I was taught that some people need
such constructs... I was taught that some folks need more than 16K of
core, too.

>
>>(Notice also two different error-routines, one for a state not found in 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>I fixed that problem for you.  <g>

By coding it second... trying to slip it through later in the code, when 
QC's eyes are tired, obviously.

>
>>Likewise... if you are writing one of the edit programs that is higher up 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>If you wrote pseudo-conversational, lunch didn't cost anything.

Mr Wagner, pardon my subtlety; not only was I pointing out that one *had* 
to code psuedoconversationally in order to allow for such a lunch but that 
the date-time stamp of every record had to be checked before update in 
order to insure that a lunch did not occur between the READ and the 
UPDATE.


>If you'd used
>'caching', IO would have been less expensive because you would have done fewer
>of them.

Mr Wagner, you seem to be confusing 'overall IO costs' - which might be 
lower - with 'cost per IO', which is a function of hardware.  Be that as 
it may... 'caching' costs core and core costs money, as well; there is a 
fine art to balancing the two about which Many Great Tomes have been 
written.

>If you were truly concerned about CPU cycles, you would have optimized
>CICS.

I do not know who is being referred-to as 'you' here; I do know that 
operating-system optimisation was not, in the shops in which I worked, 
usually the province of those who were tasked to write applications.  
Granted that at times there would be crossover between the groups... but 
the fundamental approaches seemed different.  Apps jockies were concerned 
with getting the users Something To Do Their Jobs With, systems jockies 
were concerned with 'bit twiddling' and trying to make the machine run 
infinitely fast with an infinitely small load.

>*It* was the 'MIPS sink', much more than application programs. I used to
>front-end CICS functions and provide equivalent  functionality with 100 times
>the speed. 

How very nice for you... not every shop I worked in seems to have had the 
benefit of your unique combination of skills and experiences.

>
>Bottom-up saves two clocks with a CLI while top-down makes the CICS beast run 20
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.bcentral.com/articles/special/boss.asp

Mr Wagner, of what is this 'documentation'... the quality of writing that 
fills in-flight magazines?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 79)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T17:55:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>>I used to do it for speed; now I do it for clarity.
>
>Mr Wagner, the very next sentence here indicated what I was taught 'lo, 
>those many years ago' and 'what was written'... nothing whatsoever 
>indicates that this is what is done now.

That was not clear. I thought 'lo, those many years ago' was intended to support
its veracity. People say 'I learned long ago that ...', as though old knowledge
is better than new. 

>>I would write it:

<snip>

>>That's not only faster but also easier on the eye. 
>
>Depends on *whose* eye, Mr Wagner... what is 'easier on the eye', Samuel
>Taylor Coleridge or e e cummings? 

Coleridge didn't write in upper case. If he had, Hallmark wouldn't have copied
his style. 

Why is it ok when mainframers write in upper case, but not ok when I write in
lower case? 

>>>(Notice also that I could not help myself and coded, first and foremost, a 
>>>negative conditional check on the state's validity... maybe I had better 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>QC's eyes are tired, obviously.

Are you saying QC would reject code because it contains NOT? The pathology (sick
logic) is worse than I dreamed. Ok, I'll rewrite it without any nots, and
without evaluate. 

if  purchase-state = previous-state and previous-state > spaces
    continue
else
    move purchase-state to previous-state
    if  valid-state
        if  state-needed
            search all state-table
                at end perform table-needs-update
                when taxing-state (state-index) = purchase-state
                   move state-tax-rate (state-index) to tax-rate-used 
             end-search    
        else
             move zero to tax-rate-used
    else
        perform report-invalid-state
        move spaces to previous-state
end-if

>Mr Wagner, pardon my subtlety; not only was I pointing out that one *had* 
>to code psuedoconversationally in order to allow for such a lunch but that 
>the date-time stamp of every record had to be checked before update in 
>order to insure that a lunch did not occur between the READ and the 
>UPDATE.

Timestamp raises questions about clock resolution and multitasking. Someone
might update the record between your read and your write. Using LOCK raises more
issues .. like it tends to be slow and buggy. 

On database (at least) the technique I favor uses a SERIAL field that increments
by one on each update (via a trigger). Make that field the last one in the key.
Now an extra read is unnecessary. When you say UPDATE, you'll get 'key not
found' if it has been updated since you read it.

Finding a record (row) has been updated is not necessarily a 'start over' error.
Suppose you're doing order entry, the customer orders 1 widget, you check
inventory and find there are 10,000 on the shelf, give the thumbs up. The order
taker chats with the customer five minutes before hitting enter. Meanwhile, 20
order takers have drawn down the available inventory. You re-read the inventory
and learn there are now 9,980 widgets available. There's no need to annoy the
order taker with this information because the order is still fillable. You
simply update the inventory and continue.

>>If you'd used
>>'caching', IO would have been less expensive because you would have done fewer
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>it may... 'caching' costs core and core costs money, as well;

I was using 'caching' in a more generic sense of remembering. The table lookup
above is caching the previous lookup. IO against reference files are doing the
same thing. An IO module can be called with repeated requests for the same
record (due to closeout sale on widgets). If it already has the record, it
shouldn't do another read.  

>>If you were truly concerned about CPU cycles, you would have optimized
>>CICS.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>infinitely fast with an infinitely small load.

I managed medium-sized shops (6-20) where I did both. 

>>Speaking of documentation, yesterday I stumbled into this management article:
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>fills in-flight magazines?

Everything executives know about computers, they learned from in-flight
magazines.
```

Ask someone who has said you did something 'not ok', Mr Wagner... I do not

>
>>>>(Notice also that I could not help myself and coded, first and foremost, a 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Are you saying QC would reject code because it contains NOT?

>The pathology (sick
>logic) is worse than I dreamed.

We have worked in different shops, Mr Wagner... perhaps you mgith get out 
more.

>Ok, I'll rewrite it without any nots, and
>without evaluate. 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>might update the record between your read and your write.

That might just possibly be a reason why one codes a check of it before an 
update, Mr Wagner... isn't that just *clever*?

>Using LOCK raises more
>issues .. like it tends to be slow and buggy. 

There are shops where LOCKing is frowned upon for those very reasons, Mr 
Wagner.

>
>On database (at least) the technique I favor uses a SERIAL field that increments
>by one on each update (via a trigger). Make that field the last one in the key.

Database?  What kind of database is supported by a mainframe with 16K of 
core?

>>>If you'd used
>>>'caching', IO would have been less expensive because you would have done fewer
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>I was using 'caching' in a more generic sense of remembering.

Oh, I see... you, in the course of a technical discussion, decide to take 
a term with a technical definition and use a non-technical definition... 
it should have been so *very* obvious.

>The table lookup
>above is caching the previous lookup. IO against reference files are doing the
>same thing. An IO module can be called with repeated requests for the same
>record (due to closeout sale on widgets). If it already has the record, it
>shouldn't do another read.  

No, Mr Wagner, an IO module cannot be called... none was written as no 
manager wanted to supply the resources out of his budget.

>
>>>If you were truly concerned about CPU cycles, you would have optimized
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>I managed medium-sized shops (6-20) where I did both. 

As my Sainted Paternal Grandfather - may he sleep with the angels! - used 
to say, 'Never use yourself as a comparative, you'll only be 
disappointed.'  A bit of basic logic applied to my statement might 
generate the conclusion that the shops where I worked were not the ones 
that you managed... a bit more logic might generate the conclusion that 
shops where you did *not* manage far outnumber the ones where you did and 
that the world is larger than that with which you might be familiar.

>
>>>Speaking of documentation, yesterday I stumbled into this management article:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>magazines. 

Since this statement can neither be proven nor disproven, Mr Wagner, it 
may be considered as having no weight for or against your arguments.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Multi Users (was Alphabets)

_(thread depth: 80)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T19:56:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED90560.E81E873B@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7hmm$65p$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed842a9.118939465@news.optonline.net> <bba3e7$c7m$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed8d114.155403457@news.optonline.net>`

```


Robert Wagner wrote:

>
> On database (at least) the technique I favor uses a SERIAL field that increments
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Robert,

I know you were being hypothetical in your example above - BUT - there is a real
world problem with this multi-user game.

My own experience, so long ago I can't even remember the coding. Designed/programmed
a production accounting system for a local agricultural fertilizer firm. RM/COBOL on
Radio Shack Model 16s using MS Xenix (read 'Unix').

Four lassies taking orders. First time implemented with more than one user, I looked
in horror as the system froze ! Internally 'exploding' I demonstrated aplomb by
bullshitting my way through the problem, and did a quick fix then re-compiled. I
*think* it was associated with record locking. Totally forgotten the details but the
memory stuck in my head like the proverbial to a blanket. Multi-user - a whole ball
of wax all on its own.

Multi-user came up as a topic here about 2-3 years ago. I cited a hypothetical,
based on above memory  -  Flossie is entering a bunch of invoices, many for the same
vendor. With one entry on the screen not completed, she dives off for a coffee and a
chat-fest. You know what's coming next - somebody else is also trying to access the
vendor record while she is AWOL.

Used to be a great contributor here, Ken Foskey from Sydney, Kangaroo-land - both
MVS (I think) and bloody good on OO theory, which he was studying for a late degree.
BEZOOOOM ! Ken jumps in. My theoretical, he can cite an actual case in Australia
where such a string of events shut down some 6 or 10 warehouse locations !

In your example they've got 10K on the shelf. (Hasn't that outfit heard of JIT
<G>).  Suppose they were down to 5 and take 10 orders -  Order Taker #1 is in for a
surprise if he is lackadaisical on hitting the <OK> button.

Yes - there are all sorts of solutions to multi-user, and I've now doubt you can
come up with some. Others will also probably make a case.

Just wonder - can I test others' reaction to the following. No, I'm not going to say
who, although the COBOL documentation is on the Web if you search. To overcome the
multi-user problem, Programmer X gets over it by opening a file and closing a file
per transaction. Some may react in horror at opening/closing - but it sure gets rid
of the headache.

Gotta watch even the hypotheticals - some nit picker will jump in <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 76)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T14:04:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb7ocr$rvk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 30-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> A 'positive thinking' program puts normal processing in the forefront. A
> 'negative thinking' program talks about all the things that might go wrong
> then,
> almost as an afterthought, does normal processing if it cannot find anything
> to complain about.

My programs don't think.  They just do as they're told.

> Someone reading a program for the first time wants to know 'what does this
> program do?'. Making him wade through exception tests before getting to 'the
> meat' doesn't answer the question  clearly.

Saying
IF  my-account < 5000
or  my-account > 5999
   continue
else
    perform y
end-if

Is just a different syntax to say the same thing as
IF not ( my-account < 5000 or my-account > 5999)
   perform y
Endif

or
IF  my-account > 4999 and my-account < 6000)
   perform y
Endif

With the advantage that it the continue is already a stub for when the business
rules change.

You're the first person I have come across who thinks the first example is
somehow more difficult to read, but I have come across quite a few people who
find NOT syntax can get hard - especially with compound conditions.  The third
example looks good, except that we are considering the 5xxx range, and those
numbers were not shown.

And if I write with the possibility of change, the CONTINUE stub works well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 77)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T10:13:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed87e80.134260617@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <bb7ocr$rvk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 30-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>numbers were not shown.

I agree. A range test should show the limits, not numbers outside the range. I'd
write it something like this:

>IF  my-account >= 5000 and <= 5999
>   perform y
>Endif



>
>And if I write with the possibility of change, the CONTINUE stub works well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 78)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T14:40:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305311340.637138ed@posting.google.com>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> I agree. A range test should show the limits, not numbers outside the range.
> I'd write it something like this:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Endif


This is an example of why you have little credability for you 'best
practice' here.

You write casual, poor code that fails to convey the essence of the
actual _business_ problem.  It seems that you original attempt was
designed around your preference (newly gained) to use > rather than
using >=, which you complained may confuse because of some contrived
reason.

Then you just casually change to using >= when it is pointed out that
the values actually have some meaning.  Do you write all your code
with someone peering over your shoulder telling you how it should be ?

Or do you always write casually bad code because you concentrate on
the form rather than the content ?

And if you would write '>=' then what _was_ the point of your complete
rant over what the Goths did ?

Was all that just another completely pointless wagner rant ?
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED7F3D9.7060701@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> A 'positive thinking' program puts normal processing in the forefront. A
> 'negative thinking' program talks about all the things that might go wrong then,
> almost as an afterthought, does normal processing if it cannot find anything to
> complain about. 

I love that - may I quote you to others?

I've found the easiest way to all-but-eliminate NOT logic is to use 
88-levels, with true and false conditions named.  That way, instead of 
saying "If Not WS-File-Open", it's "If WS-File-Not-Open".  A subtle 
difference, but it makes a world of difference when you start stringing 
together compound conditions.
```

- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <banspq$6hg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfabd5.130744221@news.optonline.net> <baorcc$knc$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed00d5d.155715291@news.optonline.net> <bapo8t$p3t$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed0ff38.217639364@news.optonline.net> <barv8b$b50$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed187e1.252629954@news.optonline.net> <bb01ar$re$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed40410.415518198@news.optonline.net> <bb2gad$a7m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ed5d4b8.43939830@news.optonline.net> <vdc6jpba6c6i25@corp.supernews.com> <3ed71bec.43474368@news.optonline.net> <3ED7F3D9.7060701@Knology.net>`

```
LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>> A 'positive thinking' program puts normal processing in the forefront. A
>> 'negative thinking' program talks about all the things that might go wrong
then,
>> almost as an afterthought, does normal processing if it cannot find anything
to
>> complain about. 
>
>I love that - may I quote you to others?

I'd be honored. 

>I've found the easiest way to all-but-eliminate NOT logic is to use 
>88-levels, with true and false conditions named.  That way, instead of 
>saying "If Not WS-File-Open", it's "If WS-File-Not-Open".  

It still has 'not'. A better choice would be WS-File-Closed.

I write return codes like this:

05  my-return-code   comp  pic s9(04).
     88  no-error          value zero.
     88  fatal-error        value 100 thru 9999.   
     88  out-of-balance value 20.
     88  out-of-memory  value 100.

 set no-error to true
 if  no-error
     perform a
     if  no-error
         perform b
         if  no-error
             perform c

I don't find 'not logic' hard to understand. One way to eliminate 'not' is the
suffix '-free' (which I detest). Rather than no-error, say error-free. Doesn't
it sound bright and preppy?
```
