###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 67)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T08:07:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0305250707.5f9c8714@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecebe13.69868299@news.optonline.net> <bant9h$8hr$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ecfb622.133381125@news.optonline.net> <baorsh$nvn$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed0748e.182136306@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3ed0748e.182136306@news.optonline.net>...
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
…[65 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you for eliciting that tergiversation.

Both wrong.

English quite happily uses a noun as an adjective. Forms like "invoice
date", "birth date", "severance date", "mail date" or "mailing date",
"acceptance date" are normal and acceptable in English.

Since spaces are not allowed in variable names in most languages, if
we wish to use such phrases as variable names, we must connect them,
either directly abutting them as INVOICEDATE, InvoiceDate, or using a
connector symbol which must be the hyphen in COBOL and is often the
underbar in other computer languages.

The hyphen does not indicate reordering.

We would similarly use hyphen if we wanted to use instead
DATE-OF-INVOICE as a variable name in COBOL and use date_of_invoice or
possibly DateOfInvoice in some other languages.

Jim Allan
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 61)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T07:05:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bailg3$t2m$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0305102213.463660bf@posting.google.com> <3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <b9m745$akc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <baddsl$m0a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net...

> All alphabetic words are phonetic, by definition. Sure there are spelling
> irregularities and, yes, it's not perfectly phonetic because there are
more
> phonemes than letters in the alphabet.

WHAT????

TZQBLVMRF is an "alphabetic word" perfectly functional in the COBOL
language.  There is no requirement WHATEVER in COBOL that any character in a
COBOL word represent a *sound*.   There is no requirement that *anything* in
COBOL represent a sound, in English, Swahili, Tagalog, Tlingkit or Hill
Bondo.  None.

"Phonetic" and "phonemic" have *very* different meanings.  The "t" in "tell"
and the "t" in "battle" as pronounced in the US are represented by different
symbols in the International Phonetic Alphabet.  The former would, I
believe, most correctly be represented as an alveolar aspirated stop (t with
a following superscript h); the latter in standard American English would be
unaspirated (no superscript h), but in some dialects is more correctly a
glottal stop (represented by something that looks like a question mark
without the dot underneath).  They are all the same *phoneme* in American
English.

Neither one has anything *whatever* to do with COBOL.

I'm beginning to think I insulted Gilderoy Lockhart a few weeks ago.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 61)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T08:39:49+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> I've seen shop standards which prohibited inline literals. I guess they
> want to be prepared in case the value of ONE changes from 1 to 2. <g>

In the days of 16K machines and crude compilers a new entry in the literal 
table would be created for each occurance, even if it had the same value as 
a previous one.

So if there were several ADD 1    TO .. then there would be several '1's in 
the literal table.  Putting a 77 ONE PIC <appropriate> VALUE 1. and ADD ONE 
.. saved many vital bytes.

Later compilers became sophisticated enough, when given enough memory, to 
ensure no duplication in the literal table so the saving was nullified.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 62)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T10:30:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ecf312d.99338917@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>.. saved many vital bytes.

This calls to mind the RCA 301 -- the most inept computer ever made, seemingly
designed by managers rather than engineers. It had a disk drive make by
Wurlitzer which looked and worked exactly like a juke box. When it detected a
tape read error, the machine stopped and a red light came on. The operator was
expected to 'fix' the error in memory and resume at the next instruction. Its
card reader and printer were equally ideosyncratic. 

I called it CADET for Can't Add Doesn't Even Try because it didn't know how to
do arithmetic with logic gates. Instead it had 'arithmetic tables' at a fixed
location in (unprotected) low memory. To do 1+ 2, it indexed into a two
dimensional table and found the answer to be 3. Any application program could
alter the table. 

Those were the Good Old Days, when men were men (and some of the women were
too). Where's Mr. Peabody and his time machine?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ RCA301 was Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 63)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T18:20:01-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED13381.EED05A14@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecf312d.99338917@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> 
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> card reader and printer were equally ideosyncratic.

Ah Yes, the old MRPE, DPE and the tapes with connections for threading
that I can't describe.  The shop I was at used the IBM 1402 for the card
reader punch and a drum printer on which you could execute print and
blow fuses by blanking out the print table that was in high memory.
> 
> I called it CADET for Can't Add Doesn't Even Try because it didn't know how to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> alter the table.

On the other hand the branch instruction V1
atore-next-ins-addr,branch-location was ideal for the COBOL perform.  It
also had some nice move and scan instructions.  I found it easier to
program in machine code than use their assembler which didn't know how
to origin data areas to an even hundreds boundary.  I also liked the RPG
for it which somewhat resembled the much later DYL260 from DYLAKOR.
> 
> Those were the Good Old Days, when men were men (and some of the women were
> too). Where's Mr. Peabody and his time machine?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RCA301 was Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 64)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T03:17:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed18028.250653258@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecf312d.99338917@news.optonline.net> <3ED13381.EED05A14@istar.ca>`

```
"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> This calls to mind the RCA 301 -- the most inept computer ever made,
seemingly
>> designed by managers rather than engineers. It had a disk drive make by
>> Wurlitzer which looked and worked exactly like a juke box. When it detected a
>> tape read error, the machine stopped and a red light came on. The operator
was
>> expected to 'fix' the error in memory and resume at the next instruction. Its
>> card reader and printer were equally ideosyncratic.
>
>Ah Yes, the old MRPE, DPE and the tapes with connections for threading
>that I can't describe. 

The tapes had a hook, eye, leader system for mounting. It never worked right.
The first time I saw an operator dismount a tape, I couldn't believe my eyes. He
took a pair of scissors and cut the tape. To mount the next tape, he spliced it
to the leader. 

>The shop I was at used the IBM 1402 for the card
>reader punch and a drum printer on which you could execute print and
>blow fuses by blanking out the print table that was in high memory.

You missed out on the RCA card reader. It had three speeds. The programmer had
to upshift from one to the next. In high speed, it couldn't stop on one card. It
was driving the computer like a check sorter does. Here comes a card image,
ready or not.

The programmer had to know when the last card was near so he could start
downshifting. A marker was inserted a few hundred cards from the end. 

> I also liked the RPG
>for it which somewhat resembled the much later DYL260 from DYLAKOR.

Thank you. I designed and wrote DYL240, the first in the series, around 1967. A
guy named Bill Newcomer stole it and started DYLAKOR to market it. Mine was a
JIT compiler; it wrote generated code into memory and branched to it. Later
versions went to interpreted free-form code.

I had not seen fixed-form RPG at the time. That it came out similar was a
coincidence.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ 78-Levels (Was: Alphabets)

_(thread depth: 62)_

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T21:48:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> So if there were several ADD 1    TO .. then there would be several '1's in 
> the literal table.  Putting a 77 ONE PIC <appropriate> VALUE 1. and ADD ONE 
> ... saved many vital bytes.

This brings up a question...  I was reading through our COBOL manual, 
and saw the description of a 78-level "literal" definition.  It looks 
like a way to define a constant, but there's not much in the 
documentation about what exactly you're defining.  The syntax it gives is

78  literal-name VALUE [IS] literal-1.

So, I imagine one could do something similar to what you did above with...

78  One Value 1.

(Having the added bonus, I suppose, that this cannot be changed by 
procedure division code, whereas the 77-level could).

Anyone...  Is this how they work?  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 78-Levels (Was: Alphabets)

_(thread depth: 63)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T05:07:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED03162.D1E58E75@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net>`

```


LX-i wrote:

> Richard Plinston wrote:
> > So if there were several ADD 1    TO .. then there would be several '1's in
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You didn't say which compiler - but it is a feature of Micro Focus, as an
extension, to declare CONSTANTS  - and yes, they are cast in stone and can't be
changed.

78 MyValue            value 22.
78 MyText              value "Now is the time for all good men to etc.....".
78 Myfield               pic x(MyValue).

Add MyValue to NumberX giving NumberY
move Mytext to TextField OR display MyText

The new COBOL 2002 Standard has the word CONSTANT :-

01 MyText CONSTANT value 22.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 78-Levels (Was: Alphabets)

_(thread depth: 64)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T18:41:13+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bapoh3$p3t$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net> <3ED03162.D1E58E75@shaw.ca>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> The new COBOL 2002 Standard has the word CONSTANT :-
> 
> 01 MyText CONSTANT value 22.

30 years or more ago the ICL XE13 compiler family (XE.. 11, 13, 16, 18, 20, 
A0,KB, etc.) had CONSTANT SECTION which made all declarations as constant 
values that could not be changed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 78-Levels (Was: Alphabets)

_(thread depth: 65)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T23:15:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED14AF9.C20A1F7@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net> <3ED03162.D1E58E75@shaw.ca> <v401dvccouopn7ln5722is15s7ue7thp2u@4ax.com>`

```


Binyamin Dissen wrote:

> On Sun, 25 May 2003 05:07:12 GMT "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Binyamin,

I thought that X-Li might ask, but here's an example. It's OO, but the same principle
applies if this was in Global WS  :-

*>------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "messageBox".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Working-storage section.
78 MC                          value 9.
78 ML                          value 35.
01 wa.
  05 pic x(ML) value "   ".                            *>  1
  05 pic x(ML) value "Make can't be blank   ".         *>  2
  05 pic x(ML) value "Frequency can't be zero ".       *>  3
  05 etc....
  05 pic x(ML) value "OK to delete this record ?".     *>  9
01 wb redefines wa.
   05 ws-message occurs MC pic x(ML).

copy "mssgparm.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ws==.
01 ws-response                      pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ws-WindowTitle   pic x(15) value "Probe records".

Linkage section.
01 lnk-response          pic x(4) comp-5.

Procedure Division returning lnk-Response.

 initialize ws-messageparams

 if  ValidationFlag = MC
     set TypeInformation to true
     set YesNo           to true

 else set TypeCritical   to true
      set OK             to true
 End-if

 move ws-Windowtitle to MessageLabel

 string
  ws-message(ValidationFlag)          delimited by size
  x"0d0a"                             delimited by size
  x"00"                               delimited by size
  into MessageText
 End-string

 invoke ErrorMessages "showMessage"
    using os-Parent, ws-MessageParams, returning lnk-Response

End Method "messageBox".
*>------------------------------------------------------------

Current literal length is 35. If I wanted to add message # 10 which had a literal of
45, I could make just two changes :-

78 MC                          value 10.
78 ML                          value 50.  (could be 45, but for expansion....).

Seems J4 agrees with you about the word CONSTANT. Now example above. Micro Focus in
early version of OO allowed for W/S in methods. If you had a variable MyField both
global and local, the local overrides the global while in the method.

J4 has decided that W/S cannot be in methods so my example above is screwed if I
substitute "Local-Storage Section". Although my Constants (78s) are OK, the
WA table of literals becomes gibberish.

Somebody wrote to J4 about Constants and I followed up tout suite !  In example above
substitute :-

-  "Local-Storage Section"
- 01 wa CONSTANT. *> at the 01 Level for a table.
  05 pic x(ML) value "   ".                                        *>  1
  05 pic x(ML) value "Make can't be blank   ".         *>  2
  05 etc....

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 78-Levels (Was: Alphabets)

_(thread depth: 65)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T07:44:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bavtk3$vc2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net> <3ED03162.D1E58E75@shaw.ca> <v401dvccouopn7ln5722is15s7ue7thp2u@4ax.com>`

```
Significant consideration is being given to expanding the 2002 standard
syntax, or more probably functionality, to include the ability to provide
"read-only" records.  Tentative plans for the COBOL futures workshop
discussed earlier in this forum and scheduled for Wednesday June 25 in Las
Vegas (originally the last day of COBOLExpo 2003) currently include
presentation of a paper that discusses that very subject.   Personally, I
find 78-levels, the idea of the CONSTANT SECTION, and the current
elementary-constants-only syntax of the '2002 standard way too restrictive.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:v401dvccouopn7ln5722is15s7ue7thp2u@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 25 May 2003 05:07:12 GMT "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
>    [ snipped ]
>

an
> :>extension, to declare CONSTANTS  - and yes, they are cast in stone and
can't be
> :>changed.
>
> :>78 MyValue            value 22.
> :>78 MyText              value "Now is the time for all good men to
etc.....".
> :>78 Myfield               pic x(MyValue).
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Director, Dissen Software, Bar & Grill - Israel

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 78-Levels (Was: Alphabets)

_(thread depth: 65)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T16:15:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb02vk$1nb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net> <3ED03162.D1E58E75@shaw.ca> <v401dvccouopn7ln5722is15s7ue7thp2u@4ax.com>`

```

On 25-May-2003, Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

> :>01 MyText CONSTANT value 22.
>
> Prefer that over 78 level.

Me too.   I don't use a compiler that has constants, but one use I could have
for a constant is as an initialized record or detail line.   As such, I want it
to be at a 01 level with filler values corresponding to the record where the
constant data will be moved to.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 78-Levels (Was: Alphabets)

_(thread depth: 65)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T15:03:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305271403.21aa04f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net> <3ED03162.D1E58E75@shaw.ca> <v401dvccouopn7ln5722is15s7ue7thp2u@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote

> :>You didn't say which compiler - but it is a feature of Micro Focus, as an
> :>extension, to declare CONSTANTS  - and yes, they are cast in stone and can't 
> :>be changed.

> A 78 with a picture? How does that work?

> :>01 MyText CONSTANT value 22.
> 
> Prefer that over 78 level.

78 lavels aren't constants, they are named literals.  Literals are, of
course, constant too.  Named literals have the advantage that they can
be used wherever a literal is required, not just as a constant value
where a variable is specified.

For example:

           78  Table-Size        VALUE 120.

           01  Table-Occurs        PIC S9(4) VALUE Table-Size.
           01  Table-A.
               03  Table-Item      OCCURS Table-Size.
                ...

              PERFORM ... UNTIL Table-Index > Table-Size

Then one place can change all dependancies for the table.

Arithmetic can also be done so that a 78 level is defined before a
record defining the start address, another defines the end address and
the difference gives a literal of the record size.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 78-Levels (Was: Alphabets)

_(thread depth: 66)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T17:23:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb0okc$3d3$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net> <3ED03162.D1E58E75@shaw.ca> <v401dvccouopn7ln5722is15s7ue7thp2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0305271403.21aa04f@posting.google.com>`

```
Although what you say may OR MAY NOT be correct for Fujitsu, it certainly is
NOT true for Micro Focus.  78-levels are definitely "constants".  This is
why (to go back to one of the earlier questions), it is valid in Micro Focus
(but I don't know about Fujitsu) to code:

78  Const1  Value 12.
01  Elem  Pic X(Const1).

At least in "COBOL terms" one may *not* use a "literal" in the replication
factor of a PICTURE clause - but it is "reasonable" to use a constant there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 78-Levels (Was: Alphabets)

_(thread depth: 67)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T18:39:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305271739.39370a99@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <bajcj2$uf5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ED02EFE.5010600@Knology.net> <3ED03162.D1E58E75@shaw.ca> <v401dvccouopn7ln5722is15s7ue7thp2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0305271403.21aa04f@posting.google.com> <bb0okc$3d3$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote 

> 78  Const1  Value 12.
> 01  Elem  Pic X(Const1).
> 
> At least in "COBOL terms" one may *not* use a "literal" in the replication
> factor of a PICTURE clause - but it is "reasonable" to use a constant there.

Unfortunately the use of 'constant' refers to several different
mechanisms.

For example CONSTANT SECTION, and from what I gather an '02 CONSTANT
clause, the defined name may only be used as if it were a
working-storage item (and thus may have a picture) that may not be
changed.  A 78 isn't that, it is like a #define in C, perhaps they
should have been called macros as the value is substituted at compile
time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 61)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T07:50:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0305250650.1f65b40d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <baddsl$m0a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net>...

> All alphabetic words are phonetic, by definition. Sure there are spelling
> irregularities and, yes, it's not perfectly phonetic because there are more
> phonemes than letters in the alphabet.

No.

Whose definition?

Having more phonemes than letters in the alphabet does not prevent
phonemic notation. One can use digraphs, as English does with TH and
CH, as other language versions of the Latin alphabet do more
consistantly with those or other characters. The Greeks represented
the NG sound of "wringer" by _gg_ in their alphabet.

One can also expand the alphabet with letters like ï¿½ and ï¿½ and ï¿½ and
ï¿½, or use diacritics as ï¿½, ï¿½, ï¿½.

The reason English spelling is so erratic has more to do with
vagueries of its spelling history, changes in pronunciation, and
conservatism in spelling. Some spellings descend from outright errors
such as the pseudo-learned spelling "island" for the proper "iland"
because ignorant pedants thought that "island" must be a shortening of
"isle-land". It isn't.

But by modern English dictionary *definition* _island_ is the proper
alphabetic spelling for the word we pronounce as if it were written
_eye-land_, regardless that it was never a phonetic spelling for the
word.

Alphabetic words, that is words indicated in alphabetic/syllabic/abjad
scripts, may also be represented by abbreviations, such as Mr., Ave.,
PhD, and even lb. for "pound(s)" or Xmas for "Christmas".

That these are most avoided in formal writing doesn't invalidate the
principle. Abbreviations are greatly used in field names and variable
names, such as AMT-PAYED, ADDR1, ID-NUN.

Modern writing also tends to contain acronyms that may be expanded to
the words which the acronym abbreviates or may be pronounced as if the
acronym was a word.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 62)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T10:02:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb05nv$15ab$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <299f1138.0305130818.6c218a3@posting.google.com> <3ec21484.101307304@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <baddsl$m0a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305250650.1f65b40d@posting.google.com>`

```

"jallan" <jallan@smrtytrek.com> wrote in message
news:299f1138.0305250650.1f65b40d@posting.google.com...
> Some spellings descend from outright errors
> such as the pseudo-learned spelling "island" for the proper "iland"
> because ignorant pedants thought that "island" must be a shortening of
> "isle-land". It isn't.

Not sure that's the right etymology for the spelling of "island".  My
dictionary indicates that Old French "isle" came from Latin "insula", and if
memory serves the Great English Vowel Shift would account for the transition
from "eel" to "iyl" in pronunciation.  I think it more likely that the
*spelling* was influenced by the Normans.  The beginning of OE "iland" would
have been pronounced, I believe, approximately the same as OF "isle" at that
time.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T19:48:01+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> I was disabused of that notion months ago. I'm just explaining the reasons
> for my  preferences. If you don't want to consider reasonable 
> explanations, 

Oh, I considered them, I put down an example that you may write and how I 
would do it.  I found that your 'reason' was wrong (for me), I could 
understand my example far more quickly and easily.  I found no difficulty 
at all in using all the keys on the keyboard and felt no need to restrict 
myself to 26 or so.

Now it may be that your results would be the opposite, because that is what 
you are used to.

> go ahead and write code the way you always have.

I certainly do not write 'the way I always have', and never have done.  My 
style changes as I find improvements and take from other languages that I 
learn.

I just see no benefit at all from the use of words for = < > etc., not 
since these symbols became available on the printers and card punches 35 
years ago or so.  Of course before that I would have used them.
```

>

for

Ah, "there's the rub"

You start out with some semblance of rationality.  You acknowledge that you
have learnt NOT to try and force your STYLE on others.  You then indicate
that you were simply explaining your preference.

  So far you are totally reasonable and what you are saying is appropriate
and accepted by many/most posters.

Then you RUIN it by assuming that explanations of YOUR style preferences
should impact how other code.

Once you "internalize" that stating your preferences, even explaining your
preferences is perfectly reasonable BUT that expecting others to agree that
YOUR STYLE PREFERENCES are "better" than their style preferences will result
in flames, "ad hominem" comments, and the general "hope that you will go
away"!!!!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PREFERENCES vs "better" styles (was: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 60)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net...
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>away"!!!!

I see your point in re arrogance. But my most severe critic, Richard, didn't
object. He said his style is constantly evolving (as is mine), he just disagreed
on the particular points we were discussing. That's rational. 

If someone convinced me that his particular style point was better than mine,
I'd adopt it. My preferences are not carved in granite. IMO, there is too little
discussion of style in CLC BECAUSE of the emotional defenses it evokes. 

"Men cling to arguments like a drunk clings to a lamppost .. not for
illumination but for support."  -- Giles StAubin

Since reading here, I've changed my style of periods to 

 . a-paragragh. 

No more in-line periods. They appear only on paragraph headers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PREFERENCES vs "better" styles (was: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 61)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T07:55:18+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<baj9vl$tf0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net> <bag7pe$p1r$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eccb112.253400801@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>> If you don't want to consider reasonable explanations,
>>> go ahead and write code the way you always have.

> I see your point in re arrogance. But my most severe critic, Richard,
> didn't object. 

I found your parting shot to be marginal.  It _almost_ sounded like 'If you 
don't want to do it my better way, carry on writing like you did in the 
60s.'.

> He said his style is constantly evolving (as is mine), he
> just disagreed on the particular points we were discussing. That's
> rational.

Not only did I disagree about the style of using words, but I thought that 
your arguments attempting to support this style were fatuous, arbitrary and 
irrelevant.
 
> If someone convinced me that his particular style point was better than
> mine, I'd adopt it. My preferences are not carved in granite. IMO, there
> is too little discussion of style in CLC BECAUSE of the emotional defenses
> it evokes.

If people (such as you) just said 'this is the style that I like' then 
there would be positive discussion.  However, when attacks are made on 
particular style points attempting to force them to change, then you will 
get responses.
 
> "Men cling to arguments like a drunk clings to a lamppost .. not for
> illumination but for support."  -- Giles StAubin

Yes, I have noticed how long you will hang on to an argument about things 
that are completely irrellevant, such as 'linguistics' and what the Goths 
did.
 
> Since reading here, I've changed my style of periods to
> 
>  . a-paragragh.
> 
> No more in-line periods. They appear only on paragraph headers.

I dislike that entirely.  It is an improvement over your previous style of 
a full stop on the end of the last statement of a paragraph. In fact you 
had made a point of objecting to full stops at the end of statements 
because they may be missed when indenting.

Howver, the full-stop in front of the next procedure label has the 
structural failing that it is syntactically part of the previous procedure 
but occurs in the lines of another.  You are physically overlapping the 
extents of the procedures.

There is also a discontiuity in that the first label does not have a full 
stop in front of it and the full stop for the last procedure does not have 
a following label to put it in front of.

As a practical issue I sometimes build programs by having 'stubs' for 
paragraphs that I am performing but haven't yet written.  A procedure may 
be empty but an empty one does not have a full stop.  Other times I may 
want to comment out the code in a procedure leaving the label to be 
performed.  Commenting out lines is easy - keyboard macro - and I would 
also comment out the full stop that is the end of the procedure that is on 
its own line.  However with the style you adopted it makes too much work - 
_because_ you have overlapped the procedures.

But that's OK.  If I ever have to work on code that does have this style I 
can just make up a keyboard macro and redo the whole program.  If I don't 
have to work on it I don't care.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PREFERENCES vs "better" styles (was: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 62)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T22:17:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ecfe051.144181751@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net> <bag7pe$p1r$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eccb112.253400801@news.optonline.net> <baj9vl$tf0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>60s.'.

If it sounded like that, I apologize. I wasn't thinking of the 60s, I was
thinking of <ahem> 'unusual' stylistic preferences. I won't add fuel to the fire
by giving examples of some I've seen.

>> If someone convinced me that his particular style point was better than
>> mine, I'd adopt it. My preferences are not carved in granite. IMO, there
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>get responses.

It is impossible to advocate one style without disparaging its corollary. I
thought full stops vs. explicit terminators was such an either/or choice.


>> Since reading here, I've changed my style of periods to
>> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>because they may be missed when indenting.

Well, moving the full stop to the next paragraph answers that objection.

>Howver, the full-stop in front of the next procedure label has the 
>structural failing that it is syntactically part of the previous procedure 
>but occurs in the lines of another.  You are physically overlapping the 
>extents of the procedures.

So what? If EVERY paragraph name has a full stop, one can cut and paste
paragraphs all day without error. Just make sure there's a full stop at the
bottom. Better, put a name on it:

. end-of-program.

Standard COBOL will not compile an empty paragraph, but most compilers will. If
that gives you heartburn, add EXIT.

>There is also a discontiuity in that the first label does not have a full 
>stop in front of it and the full stop for the last procedure does not have 
>a following label to put it in front of.

The first one does. I omit the full stop on the procedure division header, which
has a side benefit of permitting me to cut and paste the parameter list to or
from the call. 

I'm considering omitting the first paragraph name. It has no function. It is
NEVER referenced. Why is it there?

>As a practical issue I sometimes build programs by having 'stubs' for 
>paragraphs that I am performing but haven't yet written.  A procedure may 
>be empty but an empty one does not have a full stop.  

Sure it does, on the next paragraph or end-of-program.

Other times I may 
>want to comment out the code in a procedure leaving the label to be 
>performed.  Commenting out lines is easy - keyboard macro - and I would 
>also comment out the full stop that is the end of the procedure that is on 
>its own line.  However with the style you adopted it makes too much work - 
>_because_ you have overlapped the procedures.

I don't follow. Can you provide an example?

I put the full stop in column 2, which gives the option of commenting the entire
paragraph including its name. 

>But that's OK.  If I ever have to work on code that does have this style I 
>can just make up a keyboard macro and redo the whole program.  If I don't 
>have to work on it I don't care.

My editor doesn't have a macro language of its own. One can write macros in
COBOL, C or any other language that compiles to an .exe. I load them like dll's.

Of course it also has keystroke-recording macros, but their ability to make
decisions is limited to Find and FindNext (using regular expressions). They
cannot compare across line breaks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PREFERENCES vs "better" styles (was: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 63)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T18:37:44-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<baos7o$pi6$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eccb112.253400801@news.optonline.net> <baj9vl$tf0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecfe051.144181751@news.optonline.net>`

```
In article <3ecfe051.144181751@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:

[snip]

>It is impossible to advocate one style without disparaging its corollary.

Mr Wagner, this is wrong.  Consider:

'I advocate (solution x) as it produces the most efficient machine-code.'

'I advocate (solution y) as it produces the most easily-maintainable 
program source code.'

Both of these express advocacy based on a criterion; neither states that 
any other criterion is inferior.

On the other hand, Mr Wagner, were you to have said (note the use of the
subjunctive) '*I* (i.e., Mr Wagner) find it impossible to advocate one
style without disparaging its corollary' then you might have come closer
to a statement of fact.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PREFERENCES vs "better" styles (was: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 63)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net> <bag7pe$p1r$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eccb112.253400801@news.optonline.net> <baj9vl$tf0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecfe051.144181751@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 24-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> It is impossible to advocate one style without disparaging its corollary. I
> thought full stops vs. explicit terminators was such an either/or choice.

I apologize for not being more clear in my discussions.   Do you have any advice
on how I could have improved on my communication skills to make sure I was not
making this an either/or choice?



> Standard COBOL will not compile an empty paragraph, but most compilers will.
> If that gives you heartburn, add EXIT.

If I am modifying a program with EXIT type logic and get compiler warnings for
empty paragraphs, I do not insert EXIT, I insert CONTINUE.   This has the
further advantage in that I can have:

 EXIT-PARAGRAPH.
     CONTINUE.
D   DISPLAY "Reached the exit".

And have it work whether or not I have WITH DEBUGGING MODE on.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T07:21:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0305250621.3fea0e32@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <bae29l$lpi$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner orginally posted at
http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3eba027b.118744531%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain

> The only reactionary languages, to my knowledge, are Chinese,
> math and programming.  
> Programmers use symbols such as = and > to represent words without
> understanding that they're taking step backward in linguistics. They think
> all that matters is less typing. 

Many have disagreed.

I argued that most of the ideographic symbols used mathematical
notations and programming (and logic and chemistry and linguistics)
are not a step backward in respect to understanding in those
disciplines, but a step forward, despite any particular misuse or
abuse that might be decried.

Modern mathematics and logic largely grew from and fed back into
improvements in notation.

There were statements from individual programmers against the canard
that those using symbols (and those who created so many programming
languages that use symbols) "think all that matters is less typing."

Many, including myself, find that use of symbols adds clarity, that
">=" is clearer than "ge" even though it involves more typing, and far
clearer than a long phrase of words in the same symbols used for
variables.

The ideographic symbols are easier to parse mentally when considering
relation concepts than are words.

You have admitted this Roger, for Arabic digits making up numbers and
for  common arithmetic symbols, that those those who invented these 
notations and have spread them and those who use them do not "think
all that matters is less typing".

Yet it seems to be, as a matter of principle, that "less typing" or
less writing, brevity in notation, is a good thing, *if it does not
lead to confusion*. Who for example would want to be forbidden to use
the COMPUTE statement in COBOL, to be forced to work out any
calculation line by line?

If I can express myself more clearly and make do with less characters
by using Arabic digits and arithmetical operators instead of spelling
the statements out in full, of course I should go with the shorter and
clearer notation when the exact words that people use to read my
notation are unimportant.

Yet you resisted claims that exactly the same logic applies to use of
the symbols "<", "<=", ">", ">=" and to the symbol "=" when it is used
in a condition.

You do admit to using these symbols in conditions at times when they
make the condition clearer to you at
http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3ebe5d08.253684134%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain

> I sometimes use symbols in conditions when doing so makes the statement fit on
> one line. They also seem clearer in a range test, because the limits are closer
> together: if foo not < 2 and not > 5. But using = and > routinely offends my
> linguistic sense.

You posted at http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3ec2273b.106098230%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain

> I find words faster to comprehend.

Yet you finds the Arabic ideographic digits superior (for most
purposes) to words and arithmetic symbols acceptable and do not want
to replace them with spelled-out words.

You talked about redundancy at
http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3ec21484.101307304%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain

> I don't like that notation because a single typing error can change > GT to LT or
> GE. One must inspect the two symbols as closely as >. 

> 'Greater' had redundancy built in. An error in one letter cannot change it to a
> word that compiles. 

But again that argument against using "<", ">" and relational "="
applies ust as well against the symbols your happy to accept, the
Arabic digits, the arithmetic operators, and single letter variables
I, J, K used as counters. These last ought to be very easy to mistype,
all three adjoining and struck by the same finger by a touch-typist on
a standard keyboard.

From http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3eca52b4.98147976%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain

> From That feature is called elision. Multiplication is there, and being implied by
> omission. It would also be correct to say 'five of something' or 'five instances
> of something', just as it would be mathematically correct to say 5ï¿½a. 

> COBOL is one of the few programming languages that offers elision, as > in 'if a
> equal to 1 or 2 or 3'. 

First, I pointed out that "multiplication" (if you must call the
normal use of numbers to indicate how many "multiplication") is
regularly  indicated in algebraic notation by placing letters against
each other without spacing.

Calling the most normal syntactic way that a language or notation
represents something is elision is very odd.

It odd to claiming that in English spelling the normal lack of
hyphenation between syllables is elision. Putting multiplication dots
between all members of a term in algebraic notation would be as
needlessly redundant and unattractive as hyphenating all English words
in spelling.

But the elision of COBOL mentioned is real elision, following a
pattern of elision in English.

Why is this done? Just to avoid typing? Do you use this feature?

(Personally, I see nothing wrong with it conceptually, and nothing
*much* wrong with it in COBOL's implementation. The difference in
treatment of NOT before <= and >= as against the other operators is
inconsistant.)

>> If five-tenths is equal to one-half
>> If five divided by ten is equal to one divided by two
>> If five-tenths is equal to one divided by two
>> If five divided by ten is equal to one-half
>> If five is to ten as one is to two

> In programming, the second interpretation is used. 

No.

My point is that in programming (as well as in mathematics) all these
interpretations are *equally* valid for a reader for parsing and
pondering the meaning of the code and also for working the other way,
for determining how to code a calculation.

Something close to the second statement would be the most normal
transformation of the symbols when reading aloud or silently to
oneself. But it doesn't have to be the one used when another one makes
more sense to the reader in a particular context.

Restricting oneself to a single word-by-word interpretation of symbols
nullifies some of their value. They are ideographs, not logographs.
Similarly in translation for another spoken language into English one
does not usually proceed word for word, and may at times quite
correctly translate the same word in the source language by different
words in English.

> My objection is to the context in which symbols are used. In 'if invoice-date
> less than date-today', we see all words. In 'if invoice-date < date-today', we
> must mentally shift gears from words to symbol to words. To what end?  Just to
> save typing.

Again and again and again you don't notice that your arguments apply
just as cogently to the symbols you accept.

If gears are shifted, they are shifted for me just as much and exactly
in the same way for:

COMPUTE SALES-TAX = SALES-TTL - REFUND * SALES-TAX-RATE

You accept that Arabic numerals are clearer, arithmetic operators are
clearer, but seem unable to grasp that myself and others, for *exactly
the same reasons*, also find  other symbols also clearer in a
logical/mathematical context than the corresponding spelled-out words.

I vaguely recall at some time in primary school coming to the
realization that mostly one read from pages word for word exactly what
the pages said, but that for numbers, and especially for dollar
amounts, one read not the exact words but extracted the meaning from
characters that represented the idea and then expressed (silently or
aloud) that idea in *any* words that fit.

The sentence "I spent $10.25" might be read aloud as "I spent ten
dollars and twenty-five cents" or as "I spent ten twenty-five".

I don't ever recall such shifting of gears troublesome. I you do, then
you may indeed have some reason for preferring the spelled-out words
to symbols. But that can't apply to those of us who feel the opposite
about shifting into fast gear, and it doesn't logically explain why
you aren't bothered by gear shifting in other cases of ideographs.

Again that tired accusation (denying all words to the contrary and
your own experience with Arabic numbers and arithmetic operators) that
people *only* use symbols to save typing, not because they are
clearer.

The question is why certain symbols are not clear to you.

>> (Also, many compilers will complain about undefined variables, but
not
>> about literals, which many typos in variables are caught where
typos
>> in literals are not.)

> Not so with Fortran and Basic. They say, 'Ohh, a new variable I haven't seen
> before. I'll set it up.' That's what aversion to typing give us.

Garbage.

That is what people experimenting with new computer languages give us
and will continue to give us, flaws as well as virtues.

When I use any language with a compiler allows enabling checking for
predefining of variables, I always turn it on. Visual Basic's compiler
has an "/optionexplicit" setting. In modern Fortran all variables must
be defined.

For languages that don't insist on it, I still always try to
pre-define variables, even in the smallest temporary two-line program.
I use prefix camel notation in FoxPro to avoid type problems and scope
problems and possible misidentitication of a memory variable with a
field name. My counters are m.lnI, m.lnJ, m.lnK.

I do *whatever* extra typing is necessary to ensure clarity and
safety, clarity for me.

I rightly won't do extra typing for a more profuse notation when a
more concise notation seems clearer to me.

If you felt spelled-out words are superior and that extra typing
always  helps clarity, you could avoid COMPUTE altogether in COBOL,
just use the ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, and DIVIDE statements and do a
PERFORM loop to caculate _to the power of_.

Does the new COMPUTE command in COBOL only save typing? Or does it
also make things clearer when several calculations can be put on one
line instead of each one on a separate line? All other general
computer langauges have a syntax similar to COBOL's COMPUTE statement?

Verbosity is not always clarity, may often be the opposite of clarity.
Look at legal jargon. Look at COBOL, blamed for its needless
verbosity, a reasonable experiment at the time. But it is not one that
*any* other creators of computer languages have felt worth imitating,
so far as I know.

> "Exceeds" is one word. 

You miss the point that an ideograph can often be expressed in one
word, but it does not always have to be expressed in the same one
word.

> I tried to give reasons above: 
> .. An incomplete notational system.
> .. Not as familiar as math to many/most readers.

Finally some reasons.

First, the notation system expressible by symbols is only incomplete
just the same way that the notation in most computer languages is
incomplete. Even c has the sizeof operator which is a word not a
symbol. (And $ was actually available! How did Kernaghan and Ritchie
miss it?) There is nothing corresponding to the ** operator in c. You
have to use a function.

COBOL actually has a reasonably complete set of arithmetic operators
represented by symbols. It has a complete set of equality/inequality
operators represented by symbols. Only the three Boolean operators are
represented by words. All their spelled-out words are very short and
perhaps therefore do not so much call out for symbols as some other
operators.

But I can't understand the logic that because COBOL doesn't use
symbols for all operators, I should therefore as much as possible not
use symbols for any of them, even though I find expressions with
symbols clearer. The more logical approach is to take what I can get.

If COBOL did introduce such symbols as & | ~, would the
equal/inequality operators suddenly become acceptable to you, or would
you still refuse to use them because & | ~ are not familar to generic
readers, whoever they are.

As to "<" and ">" not being familiar to most readers, your suggestion
that "EQUAL" should be distinguished from "=" as relative "EQUAL"
versus assignment "=" is not familiar to the same kind of readers who
would not be familiar with "<" and ">".

Again your arguments conflict with each other.

I think it probably took me about ten minutes when first encountering
"<" and ">" along with "<=" and ">=" in BASIC to become totally
familiar with them.

How many readers of COBOL code are not familiar with these symbols? Do
you advocate avoiding them just on the chance that some non-programmer
might want to read COBOL code and might not understand those symbols?

(Should mathematicians then avoid them and any other *special* symbols
for the same reasons?)

By the same logic arithmetic signs should never have been introduced
in schools because people were previously unfamiliar with them.

A few new symbols in any programming language are no more likely to
cause difficulties than the spelled-out keywords that the reader is
unfamiliar with and differences in syntax from what the reader is used
to.

But in fact the symbols "<" and ">" are familar to almost anyone who
has worked with any notation involving types of inequality.

They are used in all spreadsheets I am familiar with, in most computer
languages, including almost all those taught at schools below
unversity level, including COBOL. (HTML and related SGML languages
would be would be exceptions.)

Should Microsoft and other spreadsheet manufacturers implement
"greater than or equal" as an option in their spreadsheets to aid all
those using them who have found ">=" to so hard to grasp, to help
those who want to read the formulas that use them without difficulty?

Do you have similar problems with * for multiplication and ** or ^ for
_to the power of_?

I've worked with people on spreadsheets, and as you indicate, it is
AND and OR and NOT that cause trouble there and in programming
languages.

Yet these operators are spelled out in full in Excel and in many
programming languages.

No-one using spreadsheets seems to have problems with something like
">=". If there is a problem it is with the concepts behind them, not
with the symbols, for example the normal forgetting that the opposite
of "<" is not ">" but ">=".

COBOL allowed from its beginnings:

A = B OR NOT >= C OR D

You would seem to prefer:

A EQUAL TO B OR NOT GREATER OR EQUAL TO C OR D

Or perhaps you doen't like the compressed notation which saves typing,
and would prefer people to output:

A EQUAL TO B OR A NOT GREATER OR EQUAL TO C OR A GREATER OR EQUAL TO D

Cobol also allows something closer to what other languages would
consider a more normal notation:

A = B OR A NOT >= C OR A >= D
or
A = B OR NOT A >= C OR A >= D

All the symbol notations get rid of those stupid extra OR's that
appear in the full word notation, the OR's that one might say when
pronouncing the symbols, but which only clutter up the notation when
written.

The word notation may be more readible to some if it comes to simply
reading the words, but it is not more *parsible* to most when one
wants to study such a line of code for its meaning.

"GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO" is not idiomatic English in any case.

It is a mathematics jargon phrase used to transliterate directly the
mathematical notation equivalent to ">=".

In English people (outside of a mathematics or programming environment
influenced by this notation) do not normally say something like "an
offer greater or equal to five thousand dollars" but rather "an offer
of five thousand dollars or more" (suggesting some kind of range
notation) or "an offer of at least five thousand dollars".

The phrase "at least" is better in expressing the meaning of the
operator in something close to a single word. Unfortunately it is not
intuitive that an operator ideograph ">=" with a greater-than sign in
it should be acurrately expressable by a phrase containing "least".
Yet it is.

If COBOL were really English-like, we might have had something like:

EQUALS which when negated could be expressed as DIFFERS FROM,
LESS THAN which when negated could be expressed as AT MOST,
MORE THAN which when negated could be expressed as AT LEAST.

Instead we have literal transliterations of the operator symbols.

Might as well use the symbols themselves, especially as the words were
quite possibly only introduced into COBOL as substitutes to use in
environments where the symbols themselves did not display or print
properly.

(I probably normally subvocalize something like "greater-equal" for
">=" for that operator if I at all say it rather than just see the
concept.)

It seems from the discussion that any mental shifting of gears that
might occur between processing spelled-out words and processing
ideographs doesn't bother most people here. It is usually not
noticeable to us, just as we don't notice it when reading $500.00 or
-25.5% or "Smith & Co."

It also appears that most people here find the symbols clearer than
the words despite your continued claim that people *only* use them
because they are shorter.

As far as I know, no other language has imitated COBOL in introducing
verbose phrases for equality/inequality operators. Why not?

Your problems with "<", ">" and "=" in conditions are not supported by
any rationale that holds against the widespread and honest perception
of myself and others that statements containing them *are* more
readable than statements containing words, are more quickly and easily
understood.

That is what counts most.

Logic about why we should not find such statements more readable can't
prevail against the fact that we do, especially when these rationales
mostly apply equally to the arithmetic notation that you aren't
bothered by.

From your arguments I get the impression that you have an irrational
dislike for symbols (which is logically not worse than an irrational
liking for symbols). You are slow to take to them.

The dislike can be overcome in individual cases by familiarity, as it
has been with Arabic digits and arithmetic symbols. But you have
trouble seeing that many others (presumably the majority to judge from
the use of symbols in programming languages) don't have any difficulty
in accepting symbols as long as new ones don't come too quickly in too
great a quantity. But the same is true for new spelled-out words.

But if you find that Arabic digits and arithmetic symbols are an
improvement over spelled-out words, even if you generally dislike
symbols, then you should be able to understand that if you had been
introduced to "<" and ">" earlier and made to use them, you would feel
exactly the same about those symbols for the same reasons, and also
with symbols for "and", "or" and "not".

Some, probably most of us, generally dislike hiding operators in words
just as with arithmetic operators. Symbols help the mind to parse the
expression, which is why arithmetic operators and the other operators
were adopted in use for notation long before computers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 60)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T01:50:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed15160.238675055@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <bae29l$lpi$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305250621.3fea0e32@posting.google.com>`

```
jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>Robert Wagner orginally posted at
>http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3eba027b.118744531%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Many have disagreed.

Are you saying the greater than number of people believing something, the more
likely it is to be true? That's he fallacy of argumentum ad populum. 

>I argued that most of the ideographic symbols used mathematical
>notations and programming (and logic and chemistry and linguistics)
>are not a step backward in respect to understanding in those
>disciplines, but a step forward, despite any particular misuse or
>abuse that might be decried.

I think those ideographs are a carry-forward from the days when statements were
handwritten. 

Chemists no longer use the old count-the-atoms notation such as C6 H12 O6.
Glucose, fructose and galactose all have that same formula. They use graphics to
show structure, or at least they show each radical separately. 

>Modern mathematics and logic largely grew from and fed back into
>improvements in notation.

Following that logic, we should we use one character variable names, because
that's what they use in math.

>If I can express myself more clearly and make do with less characters
>by using Arabic digits and arithmetical operators instead of spelling
>the statements out in full, of course I should go with the shorter and
>clearer notation when the exact words that people use to read my
>notation are unimportant.

>Yet you resisted claims that exactly the same logic applies to use of
>the symbols "<", "<=", ">", ">=" and to the symbol "=" when it is used
>in a condition.

A hasty generalization. Because ideographs are good for numbers does not show
they are good for other purposes. 

>You do admit to using these symbols in conditions at times when they
>make the condition clearer to you at
>http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3ebe5d08.253684134%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain
>
>> I sometimes use symbols in conditions when doing so makes the statement fit
on
>> one line. They also seem clearer in a range test, because the limits are
closer
>> together: if foo not < 2 and not > 5. But using = and > routinely offends my
>> linguistic sense.

I wish we could use THRU in IF, as we can in EVALUATE and VALUE. 

>From
http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3eca52b4.98147976%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain
>
>> That feature is called elision. Multiplication is there, and being implied by
>> omission. It would also be correct to say 'five of something' or 'five
instances
>> of something', just as it would be mathematically correct to say 5�a. 
>
>> COBOL is one of the few programming languages that offers elision, as > in
'if a
>> equal to 1 or 2 or 3'. 
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>each other without spacing.

That forces you to use single-character variable names. 

>It odd to claiming that in English spelling the normal lack of
>hyphenation between syllables is elision. Putting multiplication dots
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Why is this done? Just to avoid typing? Do you use this feature?

Yes, as much as possible. I miss it when programming in other languages. I keep
thinking how ironic their supporters call Cobol verbose as I'm duplicating the
subject over and over. 

>(Personally, I see nothing wrong with it conceptually, and nothing
>*much* wrong with it in COBOL's implementation. The difference in
>treatment of NOT before <= and >= as against the other operators is
>inconsistant.)

That's a good reason to avoid <=.  <= means NOT >. NOT <= would be NOT NOT >.
Double negatives are not permitted in COBOL, which is why NOT <=  is not
permitted.

>> My objection is to the context in which symbols are used. In 'if invoice-date
>> less than date-today', we see all words. In 'if invoice-date < date-today',
we
>> must mentally shift gears from words to symbol to words. To what end?  Just
to
>> save typing.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>COMPUTE SALES-TAX = SALES-TTL - REFUND * SALES-TAX-RATE

I see that. You were so distracted by gear shifting that you created a program
bug. You're about to charge sales tax on the refund rather than the sale.

>You accept that Arabic numerals are clearer, arithmetic operators are
>clearer, but seem unable to grasp that myself and others, for *exactly
>the same reasons*, also find  other symbols also clearer in a
>logical/mathematical context than the corresponding spelled-out words.

We get something in return for math notation: the ability to maniuplate and
transform statements, not to mention adding a column of numbers. When
manipulating logic statements, changes are to the logical operators AND, OR and
NOT; there is no need to change relational operators (assuming we avoid >=). In
other words, they chose the wrong three words to express symbolically. 

I wouldn't object to > if we had symbols for AND, OR and NOT. Then we would have
a complete notational system for logic. Suppose Cobol provided a symbol for
subtraction (comparison) while making you write words for addition (or) and
multiplication (and). Would you support using such an incomplete notation?

>Again that tired accusation (denying all words to the contrary and
>your own experience with Arabic numbers and arithmetic operators) that
>people *only* use symbols to save typing, not because they are
>clearer.

>The question is why certain symbols are not clear to you.

I don't find the symbol > any more nor less clear than 'greater'. They mean the
same thing. 

>>> (Also, many compilers will complain about undefined variables, but
>not
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>and will continue to give us, flaws as well as virtues.

They did it to avoid the 'trouble' of defining variables. 

>When I use any language with a compiler allows enabling checking for
>predefining of variables, I always turn it on. Visual Basic's compiler
>has an "/optionexplicit" setting. In modern Fortran all variables must
>be defined.

Yes, we learned that the hard way. 

Modern Fortran sounds like an oxymoron. 

>I do *whatever* extra typing is necessary to ensure clarity and
>safety, clarity for me.

Most experienced programmers do the same. We learned that the hard way too. 

>If you felt spelled-out words are superior and that extra typing
>always  helps clarity, you could avoid COMPUTE altogether in COBOL,
>just use the ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, and DIVIDE statements and do a
>PERFORM loop to caculate _to the power of_.

I don't object to arithmetic symbols. 

>Does the new COMPUTE command in COBOL only save typing? Or does it
>also make things clearer when several calculations can be put on one
>line instead of each one on a separate line?

I agree. COMPUTE is not new. 

>Verbosity is not always clarity, may often be the opposite of clarity.
>Look at legal jargon. Look at COBOL, blamed for its needless
>verbosity, a reasonable experiment at the time. But it is not one that
>*any* other creators of computer languages have felt worth imitating,
>so far as I know.

I dispute the assertion that COBOL is verbose. Write the same program in COBOL
and C. You'll find the line count about the same, unless you contrive a case to
make one language look better. 

>> I tried to give reasons above: 
>> .. An incomplete notational system.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>have to use a function.

Nor does C any string handling. How unreasonable is that in a general purpose
language? C doesn't have SEARCH, you're forced to roll your own. It punishes you
for writing structures by requiring full-path qualification, even though the
variable name is unique. How's that for discouraging good programming practice?

>COBOL actually has a reasonably complete set of arithmetic operators
>represented by symbols. It has a complete set of equality/inequality
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>operators.

After 40 years, we're accustomed to AND, OR and NOT. <shrug>

>But I can't understand the logic that because COBOL doesn't use
>symbols for all operators, I should therefore as much as possible not
>use symbols for any of them, even though I find expressions with
>symbols clearer. The more logical approach is to take what I can get.

I'm more of a purist. 

>If COBOL did introduce such symbols as & | ~, would the
>equal/inequality operators suddenly become acceptable to you, or would
>you still refuse to use them because & | ~ are not familar to generic
>readers, whoever they are.

If COBOL offered those symbols, I'd use all symbols in conditionals. 

Now that you mention it, Micro Focus has the equivalent of #define. I wonder if
I could create the symbols that way.

>As to "<" and ">" not being familiar to most readers, your suggestion
>that "EQUAL" should be distinguished from "=" as relative "EQUAL"
>versus assignment "=" is not familiar to the same kind of readers who
>would not be familiar with "<" and ">".

I didn't argue for a context-free language. On the contrary, I've argued against
it here. Every C programmer has made this mistake at least once 'if (a = b) ...'
The compiler happily does the assignment, then tests for truth. Or 'if (a-string
== b-string) ...', the compiler compares two pointers. What poor design.

>I think it probably took me about ten minutes when first encountering
>"<" and ">" along with "<=" and ">=" in BASIC to become totally
>familiar with them.

Fair enough. That answers my objection about symbols being unfamiliar to
novices. 

>How many readers of COBOL code are not familiar with these symbols? Do
>you advocate avoiding them just on the chance that some non-programmer
>might want to read COBOL code and might not understand those symbols?

I'll grant they are widely known, and withdraw my argument on that ground.

>COBOL allowed from its beginnings:
>
>A = B OR NOT >= C OR D

Syntax error. You can't put NOT in front of >=. 

>You would seem to prefer:
>
>A EQUAL TO B OR NOT GREATER OR EQUAL TO C OR D

I would prefer 

A EQUAL TO B OR LESS THAN C OR D
-or-
A = B OR < C OR D

>Cobol also allows something closer to what other languages would
>consider a more normal notation:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>A = B OR NOT A >= C OR A >= D

Too verbose, and another bug.  Neither is equivalent to the statemenets above.

above: 	A = B OR A < C OR A < D
below: 	A = B OR A < C OR A NOT < D 

>All the symbol notations get rid of those stupid extra OR's that
>appear in the full word notation, the OR's that one might say when
>pronouncing the symbols, but which only clutter up the notation when
>written.

Another good reason to avoid >=. Take for example:

A GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO  B AND A EQUAL TO C

does it mean
(A > B) OR (A = B AND A = C)

One would think so, by applying the normal rules of COBOL logic. But it doesn't,
it evaluates this way

(A > B OR A = B) AND (A = C)

The OR in GREATER OR EQUAL is not a normal OR. Does that clarify? I contend it
will confuse people.

>The word notation may be more readible to some if it comes to simply
>reading the words, but it is not more *parsible* to most when one
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Yet it is.

What's wrong with 'an offer not less than five thousand dollars'? On the trading
floors they say 'five thousand dollars or better', where or better is often
omitted or changed to 'limit'. It means five thousand or more if I'm the seller;
five thousand or less if I'm the buyer. If you call your broker and say 'sell
one hundred MSFT limit 24', that means 24 or higher. It might be trading at 25
when you call, in which case you'll get close to 25. But in a thinly traded
stock, say Joes Computer and Bait Shop, there may be only two buy offers open --
one at 17 and the other at 25. If the 25 is filled before your order hits the
floor, you don't want to be matched with the 17, you want your order to stay
open waiting for a buyer. 

We can say the same thing in SQL:

select max(price) in (select price from open-buy where price >= 24)

>If COBOL were really English-like, we might have had something like:
>
>EQUALS which when negated could be expressed as DIFFERS FROM,
>LESS THAN which when negated could be expressed as AT MOST,
>MORE THAN which when negated could be expressed as AT LEAST.

You got 'em backwards. The negative of LESS THAN is AT LEAST. 

>As far as I know, no other language has imitated COBOL in introducing
>verbose phrases for equality/inequality operators. Why not?

You would say because they were invented after keypunch machines and old
printers were obsolete. I would say because programmers imagine themselves to be
mathematicians. 

>Your problems with "<", ">" and "=" in conditions are not supported by
>any rationale that holds against the widespread and honest perception
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>were adopted in use for notation long before computers.

Thank you for the LONG, well-reasoned article.
```

```

On 25-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Are you saying the greater than number of people believing something, the more
> likely it is to be true? That's he fallacy of argumentum ad populum.

While that is a fallacy, it is reality that the more people disagree with a
statement, the greater is the need for the person making the statement to back
up his statement - if he wants to be listened to.


> That's a good reason to avoid <=.  <= means NOT >. NOT <= would be NOT NOT >.
> Double negatives are not permitted in COBOL, which is why NOT <=  is not
> permitted.

I don't get this.   You could just as validly say that > means NOT <=.   Maybe
someone who has worked on the CoBOL standards committee could verify your claim
as to their reasoning.


> >COMPUTE SALES-TAX = SALES-TTL - REFUND * SALES-TAX-RATE
>
> I see that. You were so distracted by gear shifting that you created a program
> bug. You're about to charge sales tax on the refund rather than the sale.

He's about to compute sales tax on the refund rather than the sale.   That is
not the same thing as charging it.   Whether or not it is a program bug cannot
be determined by this one line.
```

What 'maths' did the Romans try to produce a substitute for with 'XVCII' 
[sic].

In what way did they 'fail miserably' ?

Notes: * Roman notation is not just for dates.
       * Can we do maths on dates in Roman notation ?
       * XVCII is an invalid number

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 52)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T11:47:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <b9q0b7$uj0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>[sic].

Ok, it wasn't a substitute for anything, but it was still a flawed system. How
hard is the concept zero?

I wonder how Romans did engineering, logistics and business with an inadequate
numeric system. 

>In what way did they 'fail miserably' ?
>
>Notes: * Roman notation is not just for dates.
>       * Can we do maths on dates in Roman notation ?
>       * XVCII is an invalid number

The system failed because it's no good for arithmetic. You can't add a column of
Roman Numerals. The reason it survived for writing dates (only) is because we
don't usually do arithmetic on them. As I said, you can't add 1 to a date. 

>>If using a logogram such as > offends you, how do you feel about using a
>>logogram such as 5?

I have no objection to numerals. 

>What about " ' , . @ $ ! ? * & ; : %  ?

No objection except &, and = used as a relational operator. If we spelled out
the others, they would be hard to distinguish from the words they punctuate. 

Currency symbols aren't very useful. In the financial services industry, we use
abbreviations such as DM, JY and NZD.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 53)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T07:49:18+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9u6lk$p5r$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <b9q0b7$uj0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Ok, it wasn't a substitute for anything, but it was still a flawed system.
> How hard is the concept zero?

The Romans had the concept of zero, they just didn't have a symbol for it 
that was used in their numbering system.  That is, they had a numbering 
system that didn't need zero.
 
> I wonder how Romans did engineering, logistics and business with an
> inadequate numeric system.

Because it wasn't inadequate.

> The system failed because it's no good for arithmetic. You can't add a
> column of Roman Numerals. 

No. No. _YOU_ can't add a column of Roman numbers, _I_ can.  The Romans 
could, and do subtraction, multiplication and division (the last being a 
bit long winded but works).

> The reason it survived for writing dates (only)
> is because we don't usually do arithmetic on them. As I said, you can't
> add 1 to a date.

MMIII + I -> MMIV

That works fine.  Geez Robert do you run around in a funk on New Year's Eve 
because you don't know what next year will be called until the Calendars 
arrive ?
    

>> What about " ' , . @ $ ! ? * & ; : %  ?

> No objection except ...

So you object to the ones you have to 'inspect closely' because you haven't 
learnt to recognise them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 55)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T18:15:03-07:00

robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >So you object to the ones you have to 'inspect closely' because you haven't 
> >learnt to recognise them.
> 
> Remarks like this are simply out of touch with reality. 

It was your claim that you had to inspect > closely.  Actually you
had, as you often do, implied that everyone had to inspect closely
when I certainly do not need to, recognising them at a glance.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 55)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T14:06:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba06t2$744$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ebf01e7.295898087@news.optonline.net> <b9ocfi$bmk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec03239.24014939@news.optonline.net> <b9q0b7$uj0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ec21f7c.104115258@news.optonline.net> <b9uag5$9ed$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec2f444.158595845@news.optonline.net>`
