<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b9863u$kg3$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <b97do4$q33$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>,
> Richard Plinston  <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Have you thought the same way about this for decades? <g>

I notice that you changed from 'thought' to 'see' and 'seeing'.
The meaning is different in my interpretation.

There is a thing about which I have thought the same for more
than 25 years. When I was more youthful, I was confused by
by what my elders were saying. Since that time, I have learned
much. Now it is clear, they have always been wrong.

The thought has not changed; but I do see things differently.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(thread depth: 45)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T09:28:35-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b98da3$68h$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b97do4$q33$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b9863u$kg3$1@panix1.panix.com> <vbfcrh17pbqgf9@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <vbfcrh17pbqgf9@corp.supernews.com>,
Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b9863u$kg3$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>Have you thought the same way about this for decades? <g>

Me?  I don't think about it hardly at all.

>
>I notice that you changed from 'thought' to 'see' and 'seeing'.
>The meaning is different in my interpretation.

Meaning, as Wittgenstein tells it, is the result of interpretation; I was 
using 'see' in the senses of 'to grasp something mentally' or 'to make 
investigation or inquiry' (http://www.m-w.com) with an eye towards 
generating a conclusion.

>
>There is a thing about which I have thought the same for more
>than 25 years. When I was more youthful, I was confused by
>by what my elders were saying. Since that time, I have learned
>much. Now it is clear, they have always been wrong.

Ahhhh... so now that you are older you conclude that older people have 
always been wrong?

>
>The thought has not changed; but I do see things differently.

In this last sentence it appears to me as though you are using 'see' as a
variant of 'interpret' (again, with the sense of 'coming to a
conclusion'); it seems that if the interpretation is different then the
thoughts (and ordering/'weighting' of the thoughts) which one uses to
arrive at an interpretation would be different... unless, of course, the
same thoughts given the same ordering/'weighting' would allow one to 
arrive at different interpretations/conclusions... in which case 
reproducibility of results might become an issue.

DD
```

<G>
> >> >
> >> >I'll be 19 till I die.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> What can be found here?  On the one hand a young'un had the ability to
see
> >> something with the eyes of a person of advanced years... and that can
be a
> >> wonderful, impressive thing.
> >>
> >> On the other hand... a person of advanced years is seeing something the
> >> same way as a young'un would, ignoring the experience of the
intervening
> >> decades... and that can be a sad thing.
> >>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> generating a conclusion.

'[Ha]ve thought' (past tense) 'think' (present tense) as in 'to
have as an opinion' (http://www.m-w.com). 'I've thought the
same way ...' means, to me, 'I've had the same opinion ....'

> >
> >There is a thing about which I have thought the same for more
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> always been wrong?

With respect to 'a thing about which I have thought the same'
and with similar things about which I hold the same opinion,
some older people were always (and are) wrong.

> >
> >The thought has not changed; but I do see things differently.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> conclusion');

I am.

> it seems that if the interpretation is different then the
> thoughts (and ordering/'weighting' of the thoughts) which one uses to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> reproducibility of results might become an issue.

It is also true that I think differently today--more logical,
less emotional--and therefore see things differently.
Thus from the emotional "What the hell are they thinking?",
I am now able to see patterns (logic) that suggest their
thinking was (and is) in err.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(thread depth: 47)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T22:48:35-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b99s63$b5k$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <vbfcrh17pbqgf9@corp.supernews.com> <b98da3$68h$1@panix1.panix.com> <vbfio4110kqs7f@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <vbfio4110kqs7f@corp.supernews.com>,
Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b98da3$68h$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>same way ...' means, to me, 'I've had the same opinion ....'

See above about 'the result of interpretation'; I was using 'think' to 
convey a variety of mental processes of which opining is but one.

>
>> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>some older people were always (and are) wrong.

But now that you are older you are of the set of 'some older people'... so 
perhaps you are wrong.

>
>> >
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>thinking was (and is) in err.

So... it seems a contradiction, then, to say 'I'll be 19 till I die' and 
then follow it with 'I think differently today than I did at 19'... then 
again, 19-year-olds have been known to contradict themselves readily.

DD
```

> >> using 'see' in the senses of 'to grasp something mentally' or 'to make
> >> investigation or inquiry' (http://www.m-w.com) with an eye towards
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> convey a variety of mental processes of which opining is but one.

It seems the intent was lost to me. Given the variety of
mental processes and the general nature of 'this thing',
it seems reasonable that any person may have, for the
same 'thing', 'thought the same way' with one mental
process and thought in different ways with other mental
processes. Thus one may have thought often, and in
different ways, of 'life' and consistently held that 'life sucks'
then in later years acknowledge that 'I've had a good life'
due to that which happened while 'life sucked'.

> >
> >> >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> perhaps you are wrong.

Popper might suggest that we can never be certain we have
the right answers; but my answers will do until something
better comes along.

> >
> >> >
> >> >The thought has not changed; but I do see things differently.
> >>
> >> In this last sentence it appears to me as though you are using 'see' as
a
> >> variant of 'interpret' (again, with the sense of 'coming to a
> >> conclusion');
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> arrive at an interpretation would be different... unless, of course,
the
> >> same thoughts given the same ordering/'weighting' would allow one to
> >> arrive at different interpretations/conclusions... in which case
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> again, 19-year-olds have been known to contradict themselves readily.

It would seem a contradiction, however it was, as shown
above, Mr. Plinston, not I, who said 'I'll be 19 till I die.'
```

>mental processes and the general nature of 'this thing',

>

…[27 more quoted lines elided]…

A good thing that he didn't do that, then!

DD

> >> >> >>
> >> >> >> Hmmmmmm... consider:  A person of advanced years says 'I've
thought the
> >> >> >> same way about this thing ever since I was a young'un!'
> >> >> >>
> >> >> >> What can be found here?  On the one hand a young'un had the
ability to see
> >> >> >> something with the eyes of a person of advanced years... and that
can be a
> >> >> >> wonderful, impressive thing.
> >> >> >>
> >> >> >> On the other hand... a person of advanced years is seeing
something the
> >> >> >> same way as a young'un would, ignoring the experience of the
intervening
> >> >> >> decades... and that can be a sad thing.
> >> >> >>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> Meaning, as Wittgenstein tells it, is the result of interpretation;
I was
> >> >> using 'see' in the senses of 'to grasp something mentally' or 'to
make
> >> >> investigation or inquiry' (http://www.m-w.com) with an eye towards
> >> >> generating a conclusion.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Glad I had the opportunity to clarify the matter.

Upon reflection, after posting, I realized that I should have
written: It seems the intent was lost on me.

This is just a 'small change', only two letters, but the
difference in meaning reveals a 'bug' in my previous
statement and I apologize for the error.

> >Given the variety of
> >mental processes and the general nature of 'this thing',
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> only six words in English.

But does not provide a structure to compare two different
interpretations of 'I've thought the same way about this thing'.

> >
> >> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> Ahhhh... so now that you are older you conclude that older people
have
> >> >> always been wrong?
> >> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> But now that you are older you are of the set of 'some older people'...
so
> >> perhaps you are wrong.
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> those 'they'... how will you know that something better has come along?

The original 'they' referred to 'my elders' who made statements
I found counfusing. I can never be a member of the set consisting
of 'my elders'. Hence, I am not now, and will never be, one of
those 'they'.

On the other hand, if I am older than my years and make
statements that I find confusing, then I would be a member
of that set until such time as my thoughts became clear.

Come to think of it, that would be the same point that I
may know that something better has come along!

> >
> >> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> In this last sentence it appears to me as though you are using 'see'
as a
> >> >> variant of 'interpret' (again, with the sense of 'coming to a
> >> >> conclusion');
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> thoughts (and ordering/'weighting' of the thoughts) which one uses
to
> >> >> arrive at an interpretation would be different... unless, of course,
the
> >> >> same thoughts given the same ordering/'weighting' would allow one to
> >> >> arrive at different interpretations/conclusions... in which case
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> So... it seems a contradiction, then, to say 'I'll be 19 till I die'
and
> >> then follow it with 'I think differently today than I did at 19'...
then
> >> again, 19-year-olds have been known to contradict themselves readily.
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Such things happen, in my experience, and are dealt with.

…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>interpretations of 'I've thought the same way about this thing'.

It just possibly might if it is the same person whose interpretation 
changes from 'great hardship' to 'entertainment'; consider old Army 
buddies reminiscing laughingly five decades later about uncomfortable 
fox-holes.

>
>> >
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…

You can be, however, a member of the set of people who are of the age 
which you considered 'my elder' when you generated the conclusion...

'they'?

>On the other hand, if I am older than my years and make
>statements that I find confusing, then I would be a member

You stated earlier, though, that folks of that age were clearly wrong, 
didn't you?

>

>may know that something better has come along!

Glad that's cleared up, then!

DD

> >> >> >> generating a conclusion.

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

to
> >> >> convey a variety of mental processes of which opining is but one.
> >> >
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> that 'Great hardships make for later entertainments'... but that
requires
> >> only six words in English.
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> fox-holes.

Form follows function. I could have written: One may have
thought often, and in different ways, about 'life' and consistently
held that 'life sucks and then you die'. [Note that I use the word
'about' where the earlier text had 'of'. I did so to make it
consistent with '... about this thing ...'. There is no change in
intent.]

> >
> >> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> >> Ahhhh... so now that you are older you conclude that older people
have
> >> >> >> always been wrong?

people'... so
> >> >> perhaps you are wrong.

('they'
> >> having been defined by you as those 'older people') and you are now one
of
> >> those 'they'... how will you know that something better has come along?
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> which you considered 'my elder' when you generated the conclusion...

I could be, in the absence of other qualification.

> or are you stating that you've implemented a 'sliding window' for defining
> 'they'?

No. The qualifying phase 'who made statements I found
confusing' would keep me from being a member of the set.

> >
> >On the other hand, if I am older than my years and make
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> didn't you?

No. As a result of my learning much, it became clear that
those 'who made statements I found confusing' when I was
'more youthful', had been 'always wrong' with respect to the
'thing about which I have thought the same for more than
25 years'.

I have also learned to not use unrestricted generalizations!

> >
> >Come to think of it, that would be the same point that I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

I can assure you that the me of then would have had great
difficulty following this conversation and would have been
unable to deal with the ideas and concepts the me of now
regularly considers. However, the me of then would not have
been confused by a reasonable explanation with respect to a
'thing about which I have thought the same for more than 25 years'.
It being the unreasonableness of the statements of 'my elders'
that caused the confusion in the first place.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(thread depth: 55)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T23:38:00-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9f7qo$qu2$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <vblhsbeo2p3ed0@corp.supernews.com> <b9ent5$r9t$1@panix1.panix.com> <vbltc3c0kme9b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <vbltc3c0kme9b@corp.supernews.com>,
Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b9ent5$r9t$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[163 more quoted lines elided]…
>regularly considers.

That's very nice, I am sure, but... I asked something entirely different.  
I did *not* ask 'of what can you assure me?', I asked 'How do you know?'

>However, the me of then would not have
>been confused by a reasonable explanation with respect to a
>'thing about which I have thought the same for more than 25 years'.
>It being the unreasonableness of the statements of 'my elders'
>that caused the confusion in the first place.

How odd... I did not know that statements caused confusion, I thought that 
attempting to interpret statements might cause confusion.

DD
```

> >> >> 'they'?
> >> >

> >> And how, pray tell, do you know that you, now, are not making
statements
> >> found confusing by the you of then?
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> I did *not* ask 'of what can you assure me?', I asked 'How do you know?'

Temporal cohesion. Statements I make now do not exist
for the me of then. Or, perhaps there was an implication
lost on me, such as: 'making [the same] statements' or
'statements [that would have been] found', etc. As to 'making
[the same] statements', I know that I am not, because I have
opposed the statements for over 25 years. As to 'statements
[that would have been] found', I answered that with my
assurance; that is, the assurance that I know who the me of
then was. As to any other implication, ....

> >However, the me of then would not have
> >been confused by a reasonable explanation with respect to a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> attempting to interpret statements might cause confusion.

Statements from implicitly trusted and respected sources
are the proximate cause of confusion when those statements
conflict with a source that must be trusted. The conflict may be
resolved by a loss of trust and respect for those making the
statements. While the statements are still in conflict with the
source that must be trusted, there is no longer any confusion;
the statements, and those making them, are just plain wrong.

When, above, I wrote 'I was confused by what my elders were
saying' and 'who made statements I found confusing', it was
not a difficulty in understanding the text itself, it was my
inability, at that time, to resolve statements from two trusted
sources. [Here, think 'confused' in the sense of 'puzzled', as in,
"People I trust and respect would not make false statements
or lie to me; thus I was confused by what they said.]

I used 'my elders' in the sense of '[those] having authority by
virtue of age and experience' (http://www.m-w.com) and, by
implication, to be deserving of my trust and respect; not
merely those who were older.
```

Precisely... there's no control on the experiment of a given human life.

>Or, perhaps there was an implication
>lost on me, such as: 'making [the same] statements' or
>'statements [that would have been] found', etc. As to 'making
>[the same] statements', I know that I am not, because I have
>opposed the statements for over 25 years.

Oh... so you *have* thought about them that way for years.

>As to 'statements
>[that would have been] found', I answered that with my
>assurance; that is, the assurance that I know who the me of
>then was.

It might provide enjoyment to keep in mind the concept of 'the folly of a 
previous decade'... the sort of association generated by looking at one's 
own actions in the past and concluding 'Oh, that was just my folly of a 
previous decade'...

... and keeping in mind that ten years hence - if one is still capable of 
thinking along such lines - what is thought and done *now* might be, then, 
'the folly of a previous decade'.

>As to any other implication, ....
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>conflict with a source that must be trusted.

Even when no interpretation is attempted?  How fascinating... it seems, 
then, that when a statement is made in a forest and nobody is around to 
hear it then it may still cause confusion!

>The conflict may be
>resolved by a loss of trust and respect for those making the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>sources.

When you state 'my inability... to resolve' it would seem that you were 
interpreting the statements (using 'interpret' in the sense of 'to explain 
or tell the meaning of').

>[Here, think 'confused' in the sense of 'puzzled', as in,
>"People I trust and respect would not make false statements
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>merely those who were older.

Ahhhhhh... glad you clarified that you were using definition 3; I read 
what you had written in the sense of definition 2 ('one who is older').

DD

…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> thinking along such lines - what is thought and done *now* might be, then,

> 'the folly of a previous decade'.

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> How odd... I did not know that statements caused confusion, I thought
that
> >> attempting to interpret statements might cause confusion.
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> hear it then it may still cause confusion!

In a forest, a loud verbal statement may confuse the animals.

It is central to the meaning of conflict that two things come
into opposition. For statements, the conflict cannot occur
until the statements are understood* and found to be in
opposition. It is the finding of opposition, rather than of
agreement, that creates the possibility of confusion. When
the statements come from two different trusted and respected
sources, there is a period of indecision until the conflict is
resolved in favor of one or the other. It is during this period
that confusion reigns.

*The interpretation occurs in the understanding, in the
sense of 'personal interpretation', of the statements.
[Definition from RHCD.]

> >The conflict may be
> >resolved by a loss of trust and respect for those making the
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> or tell the meaning of').

I probably should have used 'to reconcile statements' in the
sense of 'harmonize (to bring into agreement)'. What I had
in mind was 'to resolve the conflict' in the sense of 'to deal
with [the conflict] conclusively; solve'.

But, as I wrote above, understanding in the sense of
'personal interpretation' precedes reconciliation (or resolution).
Thus, by my understanding (interpretation of) the texts, I knew
the statements were in conflict but could not comprehend why
both were claimed to be true. [Definitions from RHCD.]

> >[Here, think 'confused' in the sense of 'puzzled', as in,
> >"People I trust and respect would not make false statements
> >or lie to me; thus I was confused by what they said.]

It seems, in the above, I used implicit scope termination
of the quote. Thus I have mentioned something ... well ...
COBOL-like. <g>

> >I used 'my elders' in the sense of '[those] having authority by
> >virtue of age and experience' (http://www.m-w.com) and, by
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T09:40:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9ivh8$h29$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <vbnt4b8bfc4ibe@corp.supernews.com> <b9hmff$8gd$1@panix1.panix.com> <vbpngrpvu3sb19@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <vbpngrpvu3sb19@corp.supernews.com>,
Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b9hmff$8gd$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[223 more quoted lines elided]…
>In a forest, a loud verbal statement may confuse the animals.

Or it may not... it might be best to stick with one's own species when it 
comes to questioning these matters.

>
>It is central to the meaning of conflict that two things come
>into opposition. For statements, the conflict cannot occur
>until the statements are understood* and found to be in
>opposition. 

[snip]

>*The interpretation occurs in the understanding, in the
>sense of 'personal interpretation', of the statements.
>[Definition from RHCD.]

You say that it is the attempt to 'understand' which causes the conflict; 
you further state that you are using 'understanding' in the sense of 
'personal interpretation'.

I stated several lines earlier that I thought it was 'attempting to 
interpret' which caused the confusion; it appears that you are in 
agreement with me, then.

>
>> >The conflict may be
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>sense of 'harmonize (to bring into agreement)'.

According to the AHD this would have made no difference, as def. 2 of 
'reconcile' is 'to settle or resolve'.

>What I had
>in mind was 'to resolve the conflict' in the sense of 'to deal
>with [the conflict] conclusively; solve'.

And, again, this would seem to require an interpretation.

>
>But, as I wrote above, understanding in the sense of
>'personal interpretation' precedes reconciliation (or resolution).

This seems to be in agreement with my questioning how the situation could 
exist with a lack of interpretation.

>Thus, by my understanding (interpretation of) the texts, I knew
>the statements were in conflict but could not comprehend why
>both were claimed to be true. [Definitions from RHCD.]

Hence... it was not the statements which caused the confusion but the 
interpretation.  This appears to agree with my earlier supposition that it 
is not the statements which are the cause but the interpretation of them.

DD
```

If you refer to my earlier comment, you will see that I did not
say 'the statement ... caused the confusion'; but rather 'the
unreasonableness of the statement ... caused the confusion'.

It is in my use of 'unreasonableness' that interpretation is
found.

Had I mentioned this earlier, I would have missed an enjoyable,
thought-provoking discussion that reinforced the value of
having and using a good reference to promote clarity in thought
and I thank you for that.

Now, if I can only find some enjoyable way for receiving
reinforcement of the rules for punctuation.

Ahhhh... I was referring to your earlier comment where you said 'When I 
was more youthful, I was confused by what my elders were saying.'

>
>It is in my use of 'unreasonableness' that interpretation is

In that reasoning requires interpretation it wouls seem such, aye.

>
>Had I mentioned this earlier, I would have missed an enjoyable,
>thought-provoking discussion that reinforced the value of

>and I thank you for that.

Shucks... you'se jes' easily amused, that's all.  As I've stated 
previously I try to hold to the standard employed by Euclid... and that 
means first come the definitions.

( http://aleph0.clarku.edu/~djoyce/java/elements/bookI/bookI.html )

>
>Now, if I can only find some enjoyable way for receiving

What about Sylvie`, the naughty French Punctuation Maid?

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb83df8.2884529@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b96fja$ddq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb6fb51_1@127.0.0.1> <b974ng$m10$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3EB72D0F.A0856EB9@shaw.ca> <b97do4$q33$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>James J. Gavan wrote:

>> Perhaps the group should be re-christened comp.lang.cobol.oldfarts <G>

How about comp.lang.cobol.grouchy.old.men?

>I'll be 19 till I die.

I looked, acted and fealt like 30 until a year ago. Then I got old seemingly
overnight .. not mentally, but physically and athletically. At 50 I was winning
A and open category raquetball tournaments against 20 year olds. If I tried to
play now, I wouldn't beat a C player. I'll be 60 next week.

I hope age doesn't hit you all at once.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(thread depth: 44)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T03:24:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c31447$ef21a920$2191f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b96fja$ddq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb6fb51_1@127.0.0.1> <b974ng$m10$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3EB72D0F.A0856EB9@shaw.ca> <b97do4$q33$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb83df8.2884529@news.optonline.net>`

```
Check out:  www.lef.org

Have you had your thyroid checked? Blood tests are inaccurate.  Taking your
temperature under your underarm first thing in the morning is more
accurate. Check out "Hypo-Thyroidism: The Unsuspected Illness" by Broda O.
Barnes and Lawrence Galton. This can creep up on you and make you believe
it is old age.

Of course it could be something else, but I would NOT just assume it is old
age. Get a physical and get yourself checked out. I once saw a documentary
about a South American indian group. This 65 year old guy went hunting and
shot a monkey with a slow acting poison. He followed it for several miles
and it died 50 to 70 feet up a tree. He shimmied up the trunk (there were
no branches down naer the ground) and I could not believe it.

Jumping to conclusions about your health is very unwise.

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in article
<3eb83df8.2884529@news.optonline.net>...
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> I looked, acted and fealt like 30 until a year ago. Then I got old
seemingly
> overnight .. not mentally, but physically and athletically. At 50 I was
winning
> A and open category raquetball tournaments against 20 year olds. If I
tried to
> play now, I wouldn't beat a C player. I'll be 60 next week.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(thread depth: 44)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T04:11:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB88392.7E726E40@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b96fja$ddq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb6fb51_1@127.0.0.1> <b974ng$m10$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3EB72D0F.A0856EB9@shaw.ca> <b97do4$q33$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb83df8.2884529@news.optonline.net>`

```


Robert Wagner wrote:

> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> I hope age doesn't hit you all at once.

Largely depends on the individual and lifestyle. What happened in your case between
50 and 60 - did you ease off on the exercise ? I'm sure state of mind can also be a
factor. In your case, I do *sense* some past frustration.

As for it coming on all at once. While never an athlete in my youth, although I
swam, non competitively as a teenager, living on the coast, my current exercise is
down to doggie walking. (I once reluctantly ran in the 100 yards at school and came
in third - there were three of us).

My best friends and I take a daily trip down by the river - meet a Scottish buddy -
he's about 65/67 and between us we sort the world out on a daily basis - I'm sure
our solutions are much better than those made at 1600 Pennsylvania Avenue, the UN,
NATO  or EU and most certainly better than those made by the morons in our
Parliament in Ottawa !

I don't think I'm going ga ga yet - still got my wits, although some days I don't
want to think intensely about computer logic.

Physically I'm fine - don't even know what I've got - fibrillation like George Bush
Senr. No big deal, pop about six pills a day. One had a fancy name Coumadin, (aka
Warfarin) - Perhaps they think I'm a rat <G>. Love gardening; get 'happily'
physically tired. If fatigued snooze for about an hour to recharge the batteries.

For a real uplifter, get in the car and head west and just look at those
magnificent mountains as we hit the brow of a hill - it is a beauty that just can't
be purchased.

There's one truly delicious aspect of ageing - all those loveable memories you have
of the past, particularly those beings, family, actors, playwrights, composers,
political figures etc., many perhaps now dead, you think of them almost 'lovingly'.
You have an encyclopaedia of them stored in your memory.

It's no handicap at the moment - but the odd creaks come and go. Oh I wish I had
some WD40 to occasionally oil the joints !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3EB72D0F.A0856EB9@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > I was reminded of Luis Ponce de Leon, who returned to his University
after
> > > five years of imprisonment and torture by the Inquisition, and resumed
his
> > > lectures with the words: "As we were saying yesterday..."
> >
> > You must be older than I thought, no wonder your father tried to teach
you
> > Latin.
>
> I *think* he was reminiscing about UK. As to age, I've now got it tied
down
> about your Dad. The clue was when you said "Staging Post". I certainly
knew of
> him, but wise man, he stayed away from the HQ building - but I have no
> recollection of what he looked like. Now that I also know you are a
military
> brat <G> like Alistair up there in Scotland, in descending order :-
>
> Me, 70 +, Pete around 60 and you around 50 ? Doesn't contribute now, but
Warren
> Simmons ex-Standards is 80+.
>
> Both you and Pete rang a bell for me when you mentioned ICL and PLAN.
>
> I *think* it was Donald's father that did the Latin teaching, the base
stone for
> correct grammatical usage. (Taught by Christain Brothers, I never learned
Latin
> !). His dad was RCAF from WW2. Age wise I'm guessing he's somewhere there
in
> between you and Pete, seeing as he got some initial instruction from Grace
> Hopper.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

I'm 56.  Did my first degree in psychology (pre-theological BA), then saw
the light and did a computer science degree.  My dad was RCAF, flying
officer early in life, retired in 1946 with 6 years service, then an
engineer, then a priest for the last 20 years of his life.  My mother was
Irish protestant, my grandfather protestant IRA, and my maternal grandmother
was English Roman Catholic.  I had an interesting ubringing, with a fair
amount of precision with nuance drilled into me.

Donald
(I swear, it is all true)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(thread depth: 43)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T06:15:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB74F55.54C10131@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0305042123.4977db1c@posting.google.com> <3eb66609_2@127.0.0.1> <b96fja$ddq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb6fb51_1@127.0.0.1> <b974ng$m10$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3EB72D0F.A0856EB9@shaw.ca> <ZdHta.3270$vS4.260675@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

> >
> > Perhaps the group should be re-christened comp.lang.cobol.oldfarts <G>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> (I swear, it is all true)

It may well be true but Jeez, "my grandfather protestant IRA", that *definitely*
should be supported with an explanation <G>.

Now my father born Dublin, domiciled Liverpool, joined British Army, I think
about the age of 16 but too young to be sent overseas in WW1. (I suppose like
his dad, Battle of Omdurman,  he patriotically took the King's shilling).

When southern Ireland got independence, as a youngster, he supported Michael
Collins, against de Valera (don't ask me which was which). Natch! They were
thrilled to get kids with military training. So they line him up with others in
front of disassembled bren guns. "On the mark, assemble the bren gun !".

He was so adept, they had him with a red-cross band on his arm and doing the
cooking. See it's genetic. Now you know why I am a *natural*  rocket scientist
<G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting algorithms

_(thread depth: 42)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T07:21:57-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b985sl$j8b$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb6fb51_1@127.0.0.1> <b974ng$m10$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3EB72D0F.A0856EB9@shaw.ca>`

```
In article <3EB72D0F.A0856EB9@shaw.ca>, James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

[snip]

>I don't think the ex-sergeant DD is too 'youthful' either.

zzzzzZZZZZzzzz... zzzzZZZAAAAWWWWKKKHHhhhhh... huh? whuh?  Sorry, I was 
just... going over some sorting-algorithms, did I miss something?

Age?  All relative, as Einstein said... absolutely right, too!  Why, I 
remember the music of Beethoven... never heard the man perform it himself, 
though...

... and speaking of which... I recall, a few decades back, hanging around 
the Neighborhood Local I frequented at the time... one of the Regulars was 
a lass from a family that had provided her with a small trust-fund, enough 
so that she didn't have to work... but not enough so that she could afford 
to go to a better bar.  Spent most of her time moderately... 
anaesthetised... but underneath the haze beat a more-or-less good heart.

She kind of disappeared for a while... showed up after a month-and-a-half 
with a bit of a limp, seems the driver of the car she was in was even more 
intoxicated than she and the accident kept her at home after her 
hospital-stay.  She was going on about the miscellaneous aches and pains 
of post-accident life - I was considered a 'cousin' in this, having lived 
through a motorcycle-accident - and how the creakiness was a nuisance, 
that it just wasn't *right* to be twenty-five years old and have to just 
*sit down* after walking a few miles... that she was just Too Young for 
this...

... and at that point I smiled and interjected 'Too young?  You wouldn't
have said that about a twenty-five-year-old when you were seventeen, would
you?'

DD
```

I am MUCH older than that...

You may have missed the famous remark by Don when Roman Arithmetic was being
discussed here: "Why don't we just ask Pete. He was there..."

This led to a full length article at:
http://66.152.52.10/archives/v4/v40405.asp which might bring a smile...

Pete.

> indicator...<G>)

Ah, well, that wasn't a 1901A then, it was a 1901 and it was July or
August 1968. Crown Lynn had built a new octagonal building on the
triangle in New Lynn especially for the new baby. ICL had agreed to
offset the cost by repurchasing time which was to be used by the ICL
Bureau.  I worked in that building for ICL from September 1968 until
the Bureau moved to the 1903A at the Auckland Star.

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0305061412.48d5ff35@posting.google.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote
>
> > > > We had a new 1901A that was delivered from Putney.
> >
> > Yes, a holding company set up by Crown Lynn called "Consolidated Brick
and
> > Pipe Investments". I think it was around 1967, but I'm not sure (I'm
trying
> > to remember based on the wife I had at the time and that is not a
reliable
> > indicator...<G>)
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> the Bureau moved to the 1903A at the Auckland Star.

I thought your name was vaguely familiar...<G>. I worked for Eric Mancer (a
really good Boss, though he's probably dead now...) in the same building,
around the same time. I think you're right and we "upgraded" to a 1901A some
time after the machine was installed. I remember when they laid the
foundations for that "beautiful" building. You may remember it stood on
conrete piles? The first one they hit with a pile driver and it disappeared!
Seems the site was an ancient swamp...<G>. I remember moving into the new
building when it was finished and I remember writing  COBOL modules to test
the new disk drives when they were installed (Enter PLAN "SEEK" <G> powerful
stuff...). I left to get IBM experience (which was essential in those days)
on the new 360-30 at Lucas in Newmarket... Seems like another lifetime
(actually, it was...<G>).

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T08:04:16+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8rulb$p9a$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3EAE9D89.8000708@newsguy.com> <217e491a.0304291706.9829@posting.google.com> <3EAFBB59.4050904@newsguy.com>`

```
Michael Wojcik wrote:

> strcmp is not suitable as a comparison function for qsort.  qsort passes
> to its comparison function pointers to the elements being compared.  If
> you are sorting an array of strings in C, you're really sorting an array
> of pointers to char; qsort will pass to the comparison function two
> pointers to pointers to char, which strcmp will not operate on properly.

Of course if you sort an array of pointers then a comparison function will 
be required to dereference the pointers which strcmp won't do.  However for 
sorting an array of, say, structs where the key is the first field and is a 
string (eg a code) then strcmp will sort correctly.
 
> If you're sorting an array of char, strcmp will get pointers of the
> right type, but obviously it won't do what you want, since it'll be
> trying to treat your chars as strings.  (Not a problem, of course, if
> there are no duplicate values, since strcmp's comparison will stop on
> the first byte.  But not a good idea in the general case.)

An 'array of char' is a string.  What are you trying say ?  Stop on the 
first byte ?  where is that from ?

> The qsort discussed in the second edition of Kernighan and Richie,
> section 5.11, isn't the standard C library qsort (and hence is illegal
> for any conforming program in a hosted environment - it's not a good
> choice for the name of an example function; probably held over from K&R1).

Given that K&R was the _definition_ of C then whatever is in K&R _was_ C 
(of course this situation hass been superceeded).

> Also, strcmp is the wrong type for a qsort comparison function.  qsort's
> comparison function parameter is "int (*)(const void *, const void *)" -
> a pointer to a function taking two void pointers to data it will not
> modify, and returning an integer.  strcmp is "int strcmp(const char *,
> const char *)".  

Just put in a replacement prototype.  It may then give a warning but it 
compiles and runs correctly with, say, gcc.

> a conforming implementation has to invoke the comparison
> function in a way that's compatible with a call to strcmp (though it
> won't work correctly when it gets there).

In what way does it 'not work correctly' ?

struct item { char key[10], char data[20] } table[] = {
    { "DDD", "DDD" }, { "AAA", "AAA" }, { "CCC", "CCC" };

int strcmp(const void *, const void *);
qsort(table, 3, 30, strcmp);

Compiles and runs correctly (with warning <shrug>).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T16:26:02-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB2D45A.2070500@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3EAE9D89.8000708@newsguy.com> <217e491a.0304291706.9829@posting.google.com> <3EAFBB59.4050904@newsguy.com> <b8rulb$p9a$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
  > Michael Wojcik wrote:
  >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
  > correctly.

Drat.  Right you are, thanks to the fact that a pointer to a structure
is equivalent to a pointer to its first element (after any necessary
type conversions).  I was only thinking about sorting an array of
pointers to char.  (Largely because this is a comp.lang.c FAQ; lots of
people try to use strcmp as the comparison function when sorting an
array of strings declared as an array of pointers to char.)

  >> If you're sorting an array of char, strcmp will get pointers of the
  >>  right type, but obviously it won't do what you want...
  >
  > An 'array of char' is a string.

Well, only if it's null-terminated; the standard's specific about what
does and doesn't constitute a string.

  > What are you trying say ?

I was noting that strcmp, used as the comparison function to qsort, will
receive arguments of the proper type if the array being sorted is an
array of char (as opposed to an array of pointers to char).  It was an
aside, and not a particularly useful one.

  >> The qsort discussed in the second edition of Kernighan and Richie,
  >> section 5.11, isn't the standard C library qsort (and hence is
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
  > _was_ C (of course this situation hass been superceeded).

The second edition of K&R (which is the one I cited) was never the
definition of C; when it appeared, the C89 standard existed.  I no
longer have a copy of K&R1, so I don't know whether it had the qsort
example in question.  (Probably it did.)

  >> Also, strcmp is the wrong type for a qsort comparison function.
  >
  > Just put in a replacement prototype.  It may then give a warning but
  > it compiles and runs correctly with, say, gcc.

Supplying a prototype for a function reserved to the implementation
invokes Undefined Behavior.  Since gcc is only a conforming
implementation under rather particular conditions, what it does isn't
particularly germane.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T17:42:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb3f9ff.86002436@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3EAE9D89.8000708@newsguy.com> <217e491a.0304291706.9829@posting.google.com> <3EAFBB59.4050904@newsguy.com> <b8rulb$p9a$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Michael Wojcik wrote:

>Of course if you sort an array of pointers then a comparison function will 
>be required to dereference the pointers which strcmp won't do.  However for 
>sorting an array of, say, structs where the key is the first field and is a 
>string (eg a code) then strcmp will sort correctly.

It will sort correctly only if the key is null terminated. If not, you'll
probably get a protection exception. Even if they are strings, they're probably
all the same length.  Memcmp() is a more generalized solution, especially when
sorting COBOL data. 

>> If you're sorting an array of char, strcmp will get pointers of the
>> right type, but obviously it won't do what you want, since it'll be
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>first byte ?  where is that from ?

He meant it stops on the first unequal byte.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T15:04:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305031404.12be6d5b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3EAE9D89.8000708@newsguy.com> <217e491a.0304291706.9829@posting.google.com> <3EAFBB59.4050904@newsguy.com> <b8rulb$p9a$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb3f9ff.86002436@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >sorting an array of, say, structs where the key is the first field and is a 
> >string (eg a code) then strcmp will sort correctly.
> 
> It will sort correctly only if the key is null terminated. 

In C a 'string' _is_ null terminated, that is why I used the term
'string'.

> If not, you'll
> probably get a protection exception. Even if they are strings, 

If they are not null terminated then they are not 'strings'.

> Memcmp() is a more generalized solution, especially when
> sorting COBOL data. 

Memcmp() cannot be used directly in qsort() as strcmp() can be.


> He meant it stops on the first unequal byte.

In which case using strcmp() on 'array of char' without null probably
_won't_ give a protection exception.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T02:22:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb4614e.10262701@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3EAE9D89.8000708@newsguy.com> <217e491a.0304291706.9829@posting.google.com> <3EAFBB59.4050904@newsguy.com> <b8rulb$p9a$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb3f9ff.86002436@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305031404.12be6d5b@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 


>> Memcmp() is a more generalized solution, especially when
>> sorting COBOL data. 
>
>Memcmp() cannot be used directly in qsort() as strcmp() can be.

You are correct. Memcmp() requires a length which qsort() doesn't supply.

>> He meant it stops on the first unequal byte.
>
>In which case using strcmp() on 'array of char' without null probably
>_won't_ give a protection exception.

Perhaps that's why he qualified with 'if there are no duplicate keys'.
```

Array of strings:

  char  stable[][20] = { { "DDD  XYZ" },
                         { "AAA  AASD" },

works fine (as expected).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T16:35:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB2D67F.1060603@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <3EAE9D89.8000708@newsguy.com> <217e491a.0304291706.9829@posting.google.com> <3EAFBB59.4050904@newsguy.com> <217e491a.0305011535.726041f3@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
 > Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote
 >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
 > works fine (as expected).

Aside from the illegal redefinition of strcmp, I agree.  When I wrote
"array of strings" I was thinking "array of pointers to char", and
disregarding the obvious alternative of array of array of char.  I was
thinking of the comp.lang.c FAQ on the topic and didn't consider long
enough before posting.  Pure foolishness on my part.

(I believe replacing those last two lines in your code with:

    qsort(stable, 4, 20, (int (*)(const void *, const void *))strcmp);

makes it conformant, since void* and char* are guaranteed to have the
same representation.  Assuming the translation unit as a whole is
conformant and you've included stdlib.h and string.h, of course.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T10:35:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb0d6e3.567635714@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <3EAE9D89.8000708@newsguy.com> <217e491a.0304291706.9829@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote 
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>one too.

No, I'll post the C source code used to test it 12 years ago, copied from my
archive with NO modification.

Following that is source code for the 16-bit assembly language program which ran
23x faster, then the testbed. Note that it is generalized to accept any size key
and record, multiple keys, and sorts a list rather than a table. Programs were
compiled with MS C, optasm and Realia COBOL. 

After you read it and verify results, an apology might be in order.


/*  Test timing of Quicksort       R. Wagner  10/29/91  */
#define ENTRIES 1000
#define SIZE    10

 typedef struct{char Key[SIZE];
               }  array;

 array SortArray[ENTRIES];
 array HoldArray[ENTRIES];
 int i, j, debug;
 typedef union {char *ptr;
                int  num;} iptr;
 iptr debug1, debug2, debug0;
 long random = 123456789;
 char random_char[34];
 typedef struct{int Def[5];} def;
 def definition={SIZE, ENTRIES, 1, 1, SIZE};

void SORTQ(array *ptr, def *def);

  int cmp_function(const void *a, const void *b){
//   if (debug   < 50){
//       debug++;
//       debug1.ptr = a;
//       debug2.ptr = b;
//       printf ("%i %i\n", (debug1.num-debug0.num)/10,
(debug2.num-debug0.num)/10);}
     return(memcmp(a,b,SIZE));
  }

void main() {
     for (i=0; i<ENTRIES; i++){
       random = (random * 15245) + 12345;
       ltoa(random, random_char, 10);
// itoa(i, random_char,10);
// memcpy(&HoldArray[i].Key[4-strlen(random_char)],random_char,
strlen(random_char));
       memcpy(&HoldArray[i], random_char, SIZE);
     }
     debug0.ptr = (char *) SortArray;
//   for (j=0; j<20; j++){
       memcpy(SortArray, HoldArray, ENTRIES * SIZE);
       qsort(SortArray, ENTRIES, SIZE, cmp_function);
//     SORTQ(SortArray, &definition);
//   }
}

         title  SORT4
         comment \
         Function      Sorts an array  - multi-key - very high speed
         Author        Robert Wagner
         Date Written  11/29/91
         Comment       Hybrid model. Call with far pointers. Chain-link
                       pointers in your data structure contain offset only.
                       The assumption is that the data structure will fit in
                       64k. Caution: first entry cannot be at offset zero.

           Input:  ds:si (param 2) points to a structure describing the array
                   es:di (param 1) points to the array (must fit in 64k)
                         first two bytes of each entry are used for
                         pointer forward, offset portion only
           See also:     SORT4FAR, not restricted to 1 segment, will sort
                         up to 1 meg of data.
         \

            include macros.inc

            public  SORT4, _SORT4

pointer struc
lsw          dw    ?
pointer ends

entry       segment at 0        ; the entries to sort
link         pointer<>
data         db    ?
entry        ends

description segment at 0        ; move to ss
es_first     pointer<>          ; return (pointer to first)
es_len       dw    ?            ; size of an entry
es_count     dw    ?            ; number of entries
es_keys      dw    ?            ; number of keys following (1-25)
es_key       dw    25 dup(?, ?) ; loc (rel to 1) and size
description ends

sseg        segment at 0        ; ss:[bp] points here
ss_start    label byte
ss_head        pointer  256 dup(<>)
ss_tail        pointer  256 dup(<>)
ss_ref         pointer<>
ss_ref1        pointer<>
ss_high_speed  dw  ?
ss_cols        dw  ?
ss_desc_start  label byte
ss_first       pointer<>         ; copy of description structure
ss_len         dw  ?
ss_count       dw  ?
ss_keys        dw  ?
ss_key         dw  25 dup(?, ?)
ss_end       label byte

ss_data        dd  ?            ; pushed
ss_description dd  ?
sseg         ends

head            equ    ss:ss_head[bp]
tail            equ    ss:ss_tail[bp]
head_bx         equ    ss:ss_head[bx]
tail_bx         equ    ss:ss_tail[bx]
ref             equ    ss:ss_ref[bp]
ref1            equ    ss:ss_ref1[bp]
first           equ    ss:ss_first[bp]
high_speed      equ    ss:ss_high_speed[bp]
cols            equ    ss:ss_cols[bp]
len             equ    ss:ss_len[bp]
count           equ    ss:ss_count[bp]
keys            equ    ss:ss_keys[bp]
key             equ    ss:ss_key[bp]
key_bx          equ    ss:ss_key[bx]
ptr_data        equ    ss:ss_data[bp]
ptr_description equ    ss:ss_description[bp]

MIN_COLS        equ    5

SORT4seg segment public 'CODE'
            assume cs:SORT4seg, ss:sseg

_SORT4      proc  far                     ; entry point for C (model LARGE)
            push  bp
            mov   bp, sp
            pushm ds, si, es, di
            cld
            lds   si, dword ptr ss:[bp+10]
            les   di, dword ptr ss:[bp+6]
            call  SORT4
            popm  ds, si, es, di
            mov   sp, bp
            pop   bp
            ret
_SORT4      endp

SORT4       proc  far                     ; entry point for asm & Cobol
            pushm bp, ds, si, es, di
            sub   sp, ss_end - ss_start
            mov   bp, sp
            lds   si, ptr_description     ; copy desciption to stack
            push  ss                      ;  due to scarcity of registers
            pop   es
            lea   di, ss_desc_start[bp]
            mov   cx, (ss_end - ss_desc_start) / 2
       rep  movsw
            zero  ax
            mov   first.lsw, ax
            les   di, ptr_data           ; make sure first entry is not at 0
            test  di, di
            jz    #to_exit
            mov   ax, count
            cmp   ax, 1                  ; make sure params are valid
            jbe   #to_exit
            mov   ax, keys
            cmp   ax, 1
            jb    #to_exit
            cmp   ax, 25
            ja    #to_exit
            jmp   #start
#to_exit:   jmp   #exit

#start:     zero  cx                     ; setup local variables
            cmp   keys, 1
            jne   #not_hs
            mov   cx, key+2              ; if only one key
#not_hs:    mov   high_speed, cx         ; high_speed contains its length
            mov   cx, word ptr key+2
            cmp   cx, MIN_COLS
            jbe   #cols
            mov   ax, count       ; determine how many columns to sort
            mov   cx, 2
            cmp   ax, 10
            jbe   #cols
            inc   cx              ; 3 cols
            cmp   ax, 100
            jbe   #cols
            inc   cx              ; 4 cols
            cmp   ax, 1000
            jbe   #cols
            inc   cx              ; 5 cols
#cols:      mov   cols, cx
            push  ss                     ; clear tail
            pop   es
            lea   di, tail
            mov   cx, size ss_tail / 2
            zero  ax
       rep  stosw
            call  initialize_pointers
            call  radix_sort
            cmp   keys, 1
            jne   #ins
            cmp   word ptr key+2, MIN_COLS
            jbe   #exit
#ins:       call  insert_sort
#exit:      mov   ax, first.lsw
            les   di, ptr_description
            mov   es:es_first.lsw[di], ax
            mov   sp, bp
            add   sp, ss_end - ss_start
            popm  bp, ds, si, es, di
            ret
SORT4       endp

initialize_pointers proc near
            les   di, ptr_data
            mov   first.lsw, di
            mov   ax, di
            mov   bx, len
            mov   cx, count
#loop0:     cmp   cx, 10
            jbe   #loop
        rept 10                          ; unrolled loop
            add   ax, bx
            stosw
            mov   di, ax
        endm
            sub   cx, 10
            jmp   #loop0
#loop:      add   ax, bx
            stosw
            mov   di, ax
            loop  #loop
            sub   di, bx
            zero  ax
            stosw
            ret
initialize_pointers endp

radix_sort  proc  near
            lea   dx, data-2
            add   dx, key
            add   dx, cols
            lds   si, ptr_data
            mov   si, first.lsw
            zero  ax                    ; run thru building up to 256 strings
            mov   cx, cols
#loop0:     pushm cx, dx
            even
#loop1:     mov   bx, dx
            mov   al, ds:[si][bx]       ; pickup a byte
            mov   di, ax
            shl   di, 1
            mov   bx, si
            xchg  bx, tail.lsw[di]
            test  bx, bx
            jz    #zero                 ; if tail is not zero
            mov   ds:[bx], si
#cont:      mov   si, ds:[si]           ; on to next entry
            test  si, si
            jnz   #loop1
            jmp   #relink
#zero:      mov   head.lsw[di], si
            jmp   #cont

#relink:    zero  si                    ; relink into one chain
            mov   cx, size ss_tail / 2
            zero  ax
            push  ss
            pop   es                  ; point to tail end of tail array
            lea   di, ss_tail[bp] + size ss_tail - 2
            std                       ; scanning backwards
            even
#loop3: repe scasw                    ; find a non-zero
            je    #loop3c
            add   di, 2               ; correct overshoot
            inc   cx
            mov   bx, es:[di]         ; point to last entry in string
            mov   ds:link.lsw[bx], si ; link to head of next string
            mov   si, es:ss_head-ss_tail[di]  ; set new head
            stosw                     ; clear tail
            loop  #loop3

#loop3c:    cld
            popm  cx, dx
            dec   dx
            loop  #loop0
            mov   first.lsw, si

            ret
radix_sort  endp

insert_sort proc near
            lds   si, ptr_data
            les   di, ptr_data
prev     equ si
curr     equ di
            mov   curr, first.lsw
            zero  prev
            lea   dx, data-1
            add   dx, key
            jmp   #break
            even
#loop1:     mov   ax, si
            mov   bx, di
            add   si, dx
            add   di, dx
            mov   cx, cols
       repe cmpsb                      ; compare prev to curr
            mov   di, bx
            mov   si, ax
            je    #same
#break:     mov   ref.lsw, prev
#cont1:     mov   prev, curr
            mov   curr, ds:[curr]          ; ds:link[curr]
            test  curr, curr
            jnz   #loop1
            ret

#same:      mov   ax, si
            mov   bx, di
            mov   cx, high_speed
            jcxz  #not_hs
            add   si, dx
            add   di, dx
       repe cmpsb
#cont:      mov   di, bx
            mov   si, ax
            jbe   #cont1
            jmp   #outseq
#not_hs:    call  cmp
            jmp   #cont
#not_hs1:   call  cmp
            jmp   #cont3

#outseq:    mov   ax, ds:link.lsw[curr]   ; delink curr
            mov   ds:link.lsw[prev], ax
            mov   prev, ref.lsw         ; backup to ref point
            test  prev, prev
            jz    #first1
            jmp   #cont4
#osloop:    mov   ax, si                  ; compare
            mov   bx, di
            mov   cx, high_speed
            jcxz  #not_hs1
            add   si, dx
            add   di, dx
       repe cmpsb
#cont3:     mov   di, bx
            mov   si, ax
            ja    #found
#cont4:     mov   ref1.lsw, prev        ; on to next
            mov   prev, ds:link.lsw[prev]
            test  prev, prev
            jnz   #osloop
#found:     mov   ax, prev
            mov   prev, ref1.lsw
            test  prev, prev
            jz    #first
            mov   ds:link.lsw[prev], curr
#cont2:     mov   ds:link.lsw[curr], ax
            jmp   #cont1

#first1:    mov   prev, first.lsw
            mov   ref1.lsw, 0
            jmp   #osloop
#first:     mov   ax, first.lsw
            mov   first.lsw, curr
            mov   prev, curr
            jmp   #cont2
insert_sort endp


cmp         proc  near
;  Input: ds:si and es:di point to the two entries to cmp
;  Output: flags (ja, jb, je)
;  called for multi-key comparisons
#sortn:     pushm ax, bx, cx
            mov   cx, keys      ; number of keys
            mov   bx, bp        ; index to key
#loop1:     pushm si, di, cx
            mov   ax, key_bx
            mov   cx, key_bx+2
            add   ax, offset data - 1
            add   si, ax
            add   di, ax
       repe cmpsb
            popm  si, di, cx
            jne   #exit
            add   bx, 4
            loop  #loop1
            cmp   cx, cx         ; set equal flag
#exit:      popm  ax, bx, cx
            ret
cmp         endp

SORT4seg ends

         end

macros.inc:

pushm       MACRO  p1, p2, p3, p4, p5, p6, p7, p8, p9, p10, p11, p12
             IRP pn,<p1, p2, p3, p4, p5, p6, p7, p8, p9, p10, p11, p12>
              IFNB <pn>
               push pn
              ENDIF
             ENDM
            ENDM

popm        MACRO  p1, p2, p3, p4, p5, p6, p7, p8, p9, p10, p11, p12
             IRP pn,<p12, p11, p10, p9, p8, p7, p6, p5, p4, p3, p2, p1>
              IFNB <pn>
               pop  pn
              ENDIF
             ENDM
            ENDM

zero        MACRO  p1, p2, p3, p4, p5, p6, p7, p8, p9, p10, p11, p12
             IRP pn,<p1, p2, p3, p4, p5, p6, p7, p8, p9, p10, p11, p12>
              IFNB <pn>
               xor  pn, pn
              ENDIF
             ENDM
            ENDM

testbed:

*$CALL,NOLIST,NOMOVELINK
 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID.                                 SORTY4.
 AUTHOR.                                     Robert Wagner.
 DATE-WRITTEN.                               11/29/91.
*REMARKS. Tests SORT4.                       1000  3000  looks
*   Highly optim Shell      Sort1 (asm)       .25  1.00  70,000
*   C intrinsic             qsort (C)         .33  1.20  46,000
*                           qsort (C)-mm      .35  1.30
*                           qsort (C)-ml      .46  1.55
*   Cobol intrinsic         SortC (cbl)       .60  1.45
*   QuickSort in C          qqsort(C)         .33
*                           qqsort(C)-ml      .43
*   QuickSort w/improvementsqqsort(C)         .22
*                           qqsort(C)-ml      .31
*   Same in asm             Sort2 (asm)       .15   .51
*   Radix sort              rxsort(C)         .22
*                           rxsort(C)-ml      .36
*   Radix w/pointers        rxsort4(C)        .13
*   Radix/exchange w/ptrs   radix3(C)         .055
*   Same in asm             sort3(asm)        .023  .12
*                           sort3far -ml      .036  .21  16,384=1.20 secs
*   Radix/insert w/ptrs     sort4             .020  .09   radix w/pointers
*                           sort4far -ml      .034  .18  16,384=1.00 sec

 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
 SOURCE-COMPUTER.                            IBM-PC.
 OBJECT-COMPUTER.                            IBM-PC.

 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  unqualified-variables.
     05  random-number-x.
         10  random-number                         pic  9(16).
     05  dos-time.
         10  filler                                pic  x(04).
         10  random-seed                   comp-5  pic s9(09).
     05  work-pointer.
         10  work-offset                           pic  x(02).
         10  filler                                pic  x(02).
     05  n                                 comp-5  pic s9(04).
     05  temp1  pic x(04).
     05  temp2  pic x(04).
 01  to-sort1.
     05  ds-first                                  pic  x(02).
     05  ds-length          value 12       comp-5  pic s9(04).
     05  ds-count           value 1000     comp-5  pic s9(04).
     05  ds-keys            value 1        comp-5  pic s9(04).
     05  ds-key             value 1        comp-5  pic s9(04).
     05  ds-key-length      value 10       comp-5  pic s9(04).
 01  big-table.
     05  big-entry  occurs 1000 indexed x.
         10  pointerfwd                            pic  x(02).
         10  big-data                              pic  x(10).
 LINKAGE SECTION.
 01  prev-entry.
     05  prev-link                                 pic  x(02).
     05  prev-data                                 pic  x(10).
 01  curr-entry.
     05  curr-link                                 pic  x(02).
     05  curr-data                                 pic  x(10).

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-DRIVER SECTION.
 MAIN-LINE.
     call 'MLI_GETADDR' using big-table, work-pointer
     call 'dos_get_time' using dos-time
     move random-seed to random-number
     move low-values to big-table
     perform varying x from 1 by 1 until x > ds-count
         perform randomize
         move random-number-x to big-data (x)
     end-perform
     move 'eyecatcher' to big-data (1)
     display 'start sort'
     perform 1000 times
         call 'SORT4' using big-table to-sort1
     end-perform
     display 'sort done'
     move ds-first to work-offset
     call 'MLI_SETLINK' using work-pointer, prev-entry
     move prev-link to work-offset
     call 'MLI_SETLINK' using work-pointer, curr-entry
     perform varying x from 2 by 1 until x greater ds-count
         if  prev-data greater than curr-data
             set n to x
             display 'bug ' n
                 space prev-data space curr-data
         else
             move prev-data to temp1
             move curr-data to temp2
             if  temp1 = temp2
              display prev-data space curr-data end-if
         end-if
         call 'MLI_SETLINK' using work-pointer, prev-entry
         move prev-link to work-offset
         call 'MLI_SETLINK' using work-pointer, curr-entry
     end-perform
     stop run.
 randomize.
     multiply 15245 by random-number
     add      12345 to random-number.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>> Suggesting I biased the tests, intentionally or not, is dubious and a bit
>> slanderous. 
>
>In what way would explaining a slow result be 'slanderous'.  In what way

You said "that may explain how he made it run so slowly." The word "made" rather
than 'found (that it)' implies intentional bias. 

>Now that I have discussed strcmp(), are you going to acknowledge your 
>further error in "qsort() and the lack of a standard function to do that." ?

inside memcmp or strcmp? No wonder the C sort runs so slowly.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T17:50:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305031650.662b02c0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3eb0d6e3.567635714@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305011806.1b19135f@posting.google.com> <3eb32d30.33563343@news.optonline.net> <b8vf89$9e7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eb400c0.87731089@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >further error in "qsort() and the lack of a standard function to do that." ?
> 
> You are correct about avoiding a call by coding memcmp as the callback
> function.

That would constitute a further error on your part. memcmp() requires
a third parameter.  It is only strcmp that can be used directly by
qsort(), which is why I was quite specific about strcmp().

> I doubt one call would make much difference. 

From another thread on the same subject:

RP>For a default compare use strcmp() if appropriate.  That is _why_
RP>strcmp returns the results it does.

RW> That's not a qsort default. It still wastes time calling you so
you can
RW> waste more time calling strcmp. 

Once again you argue both sides of an issue alternately without
noticing that it makes you look foolish.

> Have you ever looked at the code
> inside memcmp or strcmp? No wonder the C sort runs so slowly.

The 'code inside' depends on the implementation.  In the
implementations that I care about it is fine.

And, given that you could use your own compare if you wished then what
conclusion about qsort() can be found by looking at the code of
something completely different ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T02:56:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c30f8d$1815e7c0$d8c2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <3EAE9D89.8000708@newsguy.com> <3eaf22e6.456005592@news.optonline.net>`

```
The only sort that is N lg N on "any" input is Heapsort.  I would not call
Quicksort N lg N because its worst case behavior is much slower than that.

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in article
<3eaf22e6.456005592@news.optonline.net>...
> Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> >good short discussion of the tradeoffs in Sedgewick's _Algorithms_
books.)
> 
> I cited Sedgewick. Straight radix is O(cN), where c = number of bytes in
the
> key. The code I posted did a radix sort on the first seven bytes to get
entries
> in approximate order (as it turned out, exact order) followed by a
modified
> insertion sort to shuffle nearby entries. I anologized this to using a
card
> sorter and collater. 
> 
> It turned out to be seven times faster than whatever Micro Focus
provides, which
> I'm guessing is an NlnN performer, probably Quicksort. My results are
invariant
> to key size whereas Micro Focus' performance is erratic when key size is
> changed.  Granted, mine cannot sort floating-point nor negative integers
as the
> Micro Focus sort can. OTOH, mine could easily be modified to sort a
linked list.
> 
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bitwise would be too slow because it requires an iteration per bit. I
considered
> going the other way  -- a card sorter with 64K pockets. 
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> It's not a career, it's a demonstration that NlnN algorithms such as
Quicksort
> are NOT the fastest. It was a prompt to CS types to get over their
fascination
> with Quicksort and recursion. 
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T16:11:42-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB2D0FE.6070409@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <3EAE9D89.8000708@newsguy.com> <3eaf22e6.456005592@news.optonline.net> <01c30f8d$1815e7c0$d8c2f943@chottel>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
 > The only sort that is N lg N on "any" input is Heapsort.  I would not
 > call Quicksort N lg N because its worst case behavior is much slower
 > than that.

Well-written Quicksort avoids pathological behavior and is O(NlgN) for
arbitrary input.  See Sedgewick or another decent algorithms book for
the details.  Mergesort is also O(NlgN).  And, as we've been arguing
elsethread, so is Radix Exchange sort (which shouldn't be surprising
since it's quite similar to Quicksort).
```

```
Well in thinking about this later, in using this notation, I would prefer
to specify whether talking about the average or the worst case. IIRC Unix
Review contained an article on an application experiencing very poor
performance using the UNIX sort, which was a quicksort which selected
pivots at random. So even though the worst case or a pretty bad case may be
a rare occurence, it can still occur.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T13:18:16-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-D67304.13181603052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8b881d.147942768@news.optonline.net> <b6hi7f$nv0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e8cc4e8.229053531@news.optonline.net> <b77c9f$211$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <joe_zitzelberger-BA7DE7.19304412042003@corp.supernews.com> <3e98df71.30393272@news.optonline.net> <b815pt$362$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea5c65f.9265079@news.optonline.net> <b865nj$ete$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ea71d51.97072274@news.optonline.net> <3EAE9D89.8000708@newsguy.com> <3eaf22e6.456005592@news.optonline.net> <01c30f8d$1815e7c0$d8c2f943@chottel> <3EB2D0FE.6070409@newsguy.com> <01c31114$b6ff2b20$6c95f243@chottel>`

```
Selecting pivots at random fixes half the problem.  Using a simple 
(Bubble, Network, etc) sort when the partitions are of small size fixes 
the other half.  The magic number depends, but is usually somewhere less 
than 10 elements per partition.

A well coded quicksort can accept worst case (inorder or reverse order) 
data without behaving in the worst case manner.

FTR -- the C library function "qsort()" is NOT a quicksort, though it 
can be.  That is totally implementor defined.  "qsort()" could be bubble 
sort, or a call to an external program like syncsort.

> to specify whether talking about the average or the worst case. IIRC Unix
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…

```
See UNIX Review, Feb. 1992, Software Explorium, "The trouble with
Quicksort", page 85 by Jon Bentley.

Describes problematic inputs for Quicksort and possible solutions.

See UNIX Review, Aug. 1992, Software Explorium, "History of a Heapsort",
page 71 by Jon Bentley.

Describes more problematic inputs for Quicksort and describes a version of
Heapsort that  "makes it pretty competitive with Quicksort in average
running time".

<a id="t-f9a35f5394"></a>
## Thread: PLUG: txt2pdf 6.5 — 1 this month / 3 total

_Thread started [2003-04](2003-04.md#t-f9a35f5394)._

#### ↳ Re: txt2pdf 6.5

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T15:49:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb0994f_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<f2a191bd471c95dc3308e9a61a7f9441.93245@mygate.mailgate.org>`

```
I had a look at this as I might have a need for something along these lines
shortly.

Here are my findings <G>:

As I am not interested in running Perl on my system at the moment I
downloaded the executable only for Windows as a self-extractor.

It extracted and installed correctly under XP Pro. No problem.

I then downloaded the documentation as a WinZIP and that also was no
problem.

The HTML documentation looks pretty comprehensive and it had a link saying
"Using txt2pdf from COBOL" (or somesuch...) which I took. It took me to the
aboutlegacycoding site but returned a 404., so no joy there...

I decided to just test it so I did.

I tried it with a word document and it failed... well, of course, it is
called TXT2pdf, after all...<G>

I converted the word document to RTF and tried it on that... no go.

Converted it to plain text... it worked.

Good job.

Then I got to thinking...

The main reason I want to produce something as PDF is because it has images
or diagrams in it (maybe a Company logo) and I'd like it to be viewable on
all platforms.

If I can only convert TEXT why would I bother converting it at all? TEXT IS
viewable on all platforms.

If I really want it to look like a WORD document, containing images, (and I
do...) then I can convert it to HTML and that is also viewable on all
platforms (well, all the platforms I'm interested in...).

I guess I'm just missing something here, but unless this tool can convert a
document in RTF or DOC format (which may contain some images), what useful
purpose does it serve?

Pete.

"Sanface Software" <sanface@sanface.com> wrote in message
news:f2a191bd471c95dc3308e9a61a7f9441.93245@mygate.mailgate.org...
> We would like to announce txt2pdf 6.5 version.
> http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
