<a id="t-03adc2546e"></a>
## Thread: Performance of OCCURS DEPENDING ON / Dynamic System — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Performance of OCCURS DEPENDING ON / Dynamic System

- **From:** david_marek@standardlife.com (David Marek)
- **Date:** 2003-05-01T23:52:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f4e4a1b3.0305012252.7f41c7d5@posting.google.com>`

```
We are writing a calculation system that uses a central accumulation
routine with a large I/O.

This is made up of 2 or 3 dimensional arrays - 12 indicies are uses throughout
the 106 arrays, and to cater for different types of contract, the arrays are 
allocated dynamically.   This is done by defining the indicies and deriving the
values for a particular case, then using an Assembler program to calculate the 
size of each array and allocate the storage accordingly.  The addresses of the 
arrays (01 levels) are stored in pointer variables, then whhen the COBOL wants
to access the storage, SET ADDRESS commands associate particular storage with 
the corresponding array.

The arrays are defined like :

01  CI-CHG-ARRAY.                                   
    05 FILLER                 OCCURS 1440 DEPENDING ON    
                                          MAX-MTH.        
       10 FILLER              OCCURS 250  DEPENDING ON    
                                          MAX-FUND.     
          15 CI-CHG                       COMP-1.


For most cases, the values of the indicies will be fairly low, but we have
to allow for the maximum.  The idea was that the system will size the arrays
and compare to a pre-defined limit on a table - if the size is less than the 
limit then the case is deemed to be small enough to run through the 'fast' 
transaction, if the size of the case exceeeds the limit, then the 'slow' 
transaction is used, with more memory available, hence the need for dyncamic
arrays.

However, when we're testing the prototype we're finding that using 'OCCURS 
DEPENDING ON' increases performance by about 2-3 times, which is unacceptable.

Does anyone know why the ODO would affect performance so much?

Are there any other ways to achieve what we want i.e. a system that can dynamically
define the mask for a piece of storage?


Thanks,
```

#### ↳ Re: Performance of OCCURS DEPENDING ON / Dynamic System

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T02:04:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8t5al$e98$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<f4e4a1b3.0305012252.7f41c7d5@posting.google.com>`

```
My GUESS is that this is an IBM mainframe environment.  If not, please tell
us which compiler and environment you are talking about.

Nested ODO's is an IBM extension - and is (as you have found out) a fairly
"complex" construct for the compiler to handle.  I assume you are using the
latest IBM compiler (Enterprise COBOL) and are using the OPT compiler
option.  There have been some optimization improvements for ODO's from
release to release, so this MIGHT improve your performance.

My suggestion (at first glance - and there may be better ways of doing this)
would be to

 A) make the lowest level a fixed format OCCURS
 B) either keep the top level an ODO - or (possibly even better) make it a
fixed OCCURS also - but never "access" beyond the limit used by your
assembler routine for acquiring storage.  As the table must be in Linkage
Section (as you use a SET ADDRESS OF to get its addressability), then you
shouldn't be in any problem if you define the table as "too large" but do
not try to ACCESS any storage beyond what your Assembler obtained.

Again, there may well be some "better design" approaches for this
situation - but without knowing all the details, it is hard to come up with
something that will meet your run-time performance requirements.
```

#### ↳ Re: Performance of OCCURS DEPENDING ON / Dynamic System

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T21:16:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb4313d.100146155@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<f4e4a1b3.0305012252.7f41c7d5@posting.google.com>`

```
I do it by making the second dimension another table, like this:

01  CI-CHG-ARRAY.                                   
    05 FILLER                 OCCURS 1440 DEPENDING ON    
                                          MAX-MTH.
         10  link-to-funds    pointer.
 01  FUND-ARRAY.        
       10 FILLER              OCCURS 250  DEPENDING ON    
                                          MAX-FUND.     
          15 CI-CHG                       COMP-1.

Then change:

move CI-CHG (MTH, FUND) to ...

to:

set address of FUND-ARRAY to link-to-funds (MTH)
move CI-CHG (FUND) to ..

This technique provides an infinite number of dimensions. You're not limited to
the COBOL max and your speed problem will vanish. 


david_marek@standardlife.com (David Marek) wrote:

>We are writing a calculation system that uses a central accumulation
>routine with a large I/O.
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>Are there any other ways to achieve what we want i.e. a system that can
dynamically
>define the mask for a piece of storage?
>
>
>Thanks,
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Performance of OCCURS DEPENDING ON / Dynamic System

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-05T09:35:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b963rs$305b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<f4e4a1b3.0305012252.7f41c7d5@posting.google.com> <3eb4313d.100146155@news.optonline.net>`

```
Just as a caution:  "OCCURS <integer-1> DEPENDING ON data-name-1" is an
implementation-specific  extension.  In any OCCURS format in which DEPENDING
appears, TO must appear also, in '68, '74, '85 or '02 standard COBOL, and
any implementation that relaxes that rule does so at the expense of
portability.  See for example ISO/IEC 1989:2002 Page 306, 13.16.36 OCCURS
clause, Format 2 general format (note the absence of square brackets in the
salient area of this format), and Page 307-308, syntax rule 21, which
applies to Format 3 and explains the need for square brackets in that
format.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eb4313d.100146155@news.optonline.net...
> I do it by making the second dimension another table, like this:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> This technique provides an infinite number of dimensions. You're not
limited to
> the COBOL max and your speed problem will vanish.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >This is made up of 2 or 3 dimensional arrays - 12 indicies are uses
throughout
> >the 106 arrays, and to cater for different types of contract, the arrays
are
> >allocated dynamically.   This is done by defining the indicies and
deriving the
> >values for a particular case, then using an Assembler program to
calculate the
> >size of each array and allocate the storage accordingly.  The addresses
of the
> >arrays (01 levels) are stored in pointer variables, then whhen the COBOL
wants
> >to access the storage, SET ADDRESS commands associate particular storage
with
> >the corresponding array.
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >For most cases, the values of the indicies will be fairly low, but we
have
> >to allow for the maximum.  The idea was that the system will size the
arrays
> >and compare to a pre-defined limit on a table - if the size is less than
the
> >limit then the case is deemed to be small enough to run through the
'fast'
> >transaction, if the size of the case exceeeds the limit, then the 'slow'
> >transaction is used, with more memory available, hence the need for
dyncamic
> >arrays.
> >
> >However, when we're testing the prototype we're finding that using
'OCCURS
> >DEPENDING ON' increases performance by about 2-3 times, which is
unacceptable.
> >
> >Does anyone know why the ODO would affect performance so much?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Performance of OCCURS DEPENDING ON / Dynamic System

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T03:00:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb8696b.14009046@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<f4e4a1b3.0305012252.7f41c7d5@posting.google.com> <3eb4313d.100146155@news.optonline.net> <b963rs$305b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>Just as a caution:  "OCCURS <integer-1> DEPENDING ON data-name-1" is an
>implementation-specific  extension.  In any OCCURS format in which DEPENDING
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>format.

Slow execution is due to ODO on the outer dimensions. For instance, MOVE CI-CHG
(1000, 1) requires the code to go through outer-dimension (1) through
outer-dimension (999), adding their sizes to determine where outer-dimension
(1000) starts. The table of pointers I suggested eliminates that calculation. 

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3eb4313d.100146155@news.optonline.net...
>> I do it by making the second dimension another table, like this:
…[83 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Performance of OCCURS DEPENDING ON / Dynamic System

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T09:27:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9bc5o$jfg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<f4e4a1b3.0305012252.7f41c7d5@posting.google.com> <3eb4313d.100146155@news.optonline.net> <b963rs$305b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eb8696b.14009046@news.optonline.net>`

```

I wrote:

> >Just as a caution:  "OCCURS <integer-1> DEPENDING ON data-name-1" is an
> >implementation-specific  extension.  In any OCCURS format in which
DEPENDING
> >appears, TO must appear also, in '68, '74, '85 or '02 standard COBOL, and
> >any implementation that relaxes that rule does so at the expense of
> >portability.  See for example ISO/IEC 1989:2002 Page 306, 13.16.36 OCCURS
> >clause, Format 2 general format (note the absence of square brackets in
the
> >salient area of this format), and Page 307-308, syntax rule 21, which
> >applies to Format 3 and explains the need for square brackets in that
> >format.

Robert Wagner replied:

> Slow execution is due to ODO on the outer dimensions. For instance, MOVE
CI-CHG
> (1000, 1) requires the code to go through outer-dimension (1) through
> outer-dimension (999), adding their sizes to determine where
outer-dimension
> (1000) starts. The table of pointers I suggested eliminates that
calculation.

Ummm... Exactly which question are you answering here?   I don't recall
having *asked* one.

Whether  OCCURS integer-1 DEPENDING ON data-name (without TO integer-2) is
fast or slow in a given implementation on the outer, inner, or any other
dimension has nothing whatever to do with whether it's standard COBOL.  My
point was that it isn't, and that because it's a vendor-specific
implementation it does whateve the whims of the implementor have decided
it's going to do in the manner in which they've decided it's going to do it.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

<a id="t-dfc9d5c28e"></a>
## Thread: What statement is executed next? — 29 msgs

### What statement is executed next?

- **From:** setar@mindspring.com (STR)
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T11:15:27-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8623d3bf.0305021015.559061f7@posting.google.com>`

```
A beginner's question.  

Given the following code, what statement is executed after the GO TO
400-EXIT Statement in the 400-END paragraph?  Does control return to
the 400-END paragraph to execute the next statement or since the exit
statement is reached does the control return to the next statement of
the 200-TEST paragraph?

Thanks in advance.
----------------------------------------

200-TEST
02168
02169             PERFORM 400-HISTORY THRU 400-EXIT.
02170             MOVE RAS-TEST TO STR-TEST.

400-HISTORY.
02168
02169              MOVE ZEROS TO STR-KEY.
02170              MOVE RAS-MEMBER TO STR-MEMBER.
02171              IF KJ-YR NOT NUMERIC MOVE ZEROS TO KJ-YR.
02176              IF ARJFILE-STATUS NOT EQUAL "00"
02177                  DISPLAY "BAD START ARJFILE, STATUS= "
ARJFILE-STATUS
02178                           UPON CONSOLE
02179                  GO TO 400-END.

400-END.
02332              GO TO 400-EXIT.
02333
02334
02335              IF KJ-TOTAL-PGS (SUB) NOT = ZEROS
02336                    MOVE KJ-TOTAL-PGS (SUB)         TO
DETAIL4-PGS.
02340              COMPUTE KJ-TRANS (SUBH) =
02341                  KJ-USED (SUBH) + KJ-OTHER (SUBH)
02343              IF KJ-TOTAL-TRANS (SUBH)  NOT = ZEROS
02344                    MOVE KJ-TOTAL-TRANS (SUBH)           TO
DETAIL4-TRANS.

400-EXIT.
02403              EXIT.
```

#### ↳ Re: What statement is executed next?

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T11:57:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8uf3k$nf3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<8623d3bf.0305021015.559061f7@posting.google.com>`

```
I think this is a trick homework question.  "EXIT" is indeed a statement,
but it "serves only to enable the user to assign a procedure-name to a given
point in the program."  Thus, the next *executable statement* that is
executed at the completion of GO TO 400-EXIT is, in my view, MOVE RAS-TEST
TO STR-TEST.

If the teacher is trying to confuse you by implying 'When you do a "GO TO",
where do you land when you "COME BACK"?'  The answer is 'You don't "COME
BACK".  You *never* "COME BACK".  You are lost forever 'neath the streets of
Boston.  "PERFORM" is for coming back.'

    -Chuck Stevens

"STR" <setar@mindspring.com> wrote in message
news:8623d3bf.0305021015.559061f7@posting.google.com...
> A beginner's question.
>
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> 02403              EXIT.
```

##### ↳ ↳ [OT] 'Neath the streets of Boston

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T16:26:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37-dnVzrhOIGfy-jXTWJkQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<8623d3bf.0305021015.559061f7@posting.google.com> <b8uf3k$nf3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens"
>
> If the teacher is trying to confuse you by implying 'When you do a "GO
TO",
> where do you land when you "COME BACK"?'  The answer is 'You don't "COME
> BACK".  You *never* "COME BACK".  You are lost forever 'neath the streets
of
> Boston.  "PERFORM" is for coming back.'
>

During the last election, I was telling some of our younger employees about
"Campaign Songs" and mentioned "The MTA." They never heard of it.

So I hie myself down to Borders Music and prowl around looking for it
(urban, folk, etc.). Can't find it.

Finally I screw up my resolve and approach the employee with the least scary
spiked hair (although he did have very many body parts pierced) and asked
where I might find Kingston Trio offerings.

"Cool, man," says he. "Lemme show ya!"

He leads me to the "Classics" section and lays hands on the exact CD I
wanted.

"Classics!" Indeed.

For the children:

Spoken:
These are the times that try men's souls. In the course of our nation's
history, the people of Boston have rallied bravely whenever the rights of
men have been threatened. Today, a new crisis has arisen. The Metropolitan
Transit Authority, better known as the M. T. A., is attempting to levy a
burdensome tax on the population in the form of a subway fare increase.
Citizens, hear me out! This could happen to you!

(Eight bar guitar, banjo introduction)

Well, let me tell you of the story of a man named Charley on a tragic and
fateful day.
He put ten cents in his pocket, kissed his wife and family, went to ride on
the M. T. A.

Chorus:
Well, did he ever return? No, he never returned and his fate is still
unknown.
He may ride forever 'neath the streets of Boston. He's the man who never
returned.

Charlie handed in his dime at the Kendall Square Station and he changed for
Jamaica Plain.
When he got there the conductor told him, "One more nickel." Charlie
couldn't get off of that train.

(Chorus)

Now, all night long Charlie rides through the station, crying, "What will
become of me?!!
How can I afford to see my sister in Chelsea or my cousin in Rocksbury?"

(Chorus)

Charlie's wife goes down to the Sculley Square Station every day at quarter
past two,
And through the open window she hands Charlie a sandwich as the train comes
rumblin' through.

(Chorus)

Now, you citizens of Boston, don't you think it's a scandal how the people
have to pay and pay?
Fight the fare increase! Vote for George O'Brien! Get poor Charlie off the
M. T. A.

(Chorus)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] 'Neath the streets of Boston

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T14:49:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8up5b$ulm$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<8623d3bf.0305021015.559061f7@posting.google.com> <b8uf3k$nf3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <37-dnVzrhOIGfy-jXTWJkQ@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:37-dnVzrhOIGfy-jXTWJkQ@giganews.com...

<lyrics of what I knew as "The Ballad of the MTA">:

I think there are some place-name spelling errors:  Kendel, Roxbury, Scully.
Great job otherwise, though.

As to classics:  Feh.  I'm one of those idiots that thinks Englebert
Humperdinck actually wrote an opera.  And there's a popular melody I know
that, when I hear the chorus, I think "Aura Lee, Aura Lee, ... " instead of
"Love me tender, love me true ...".   Not quite old enough to remember when
"To Anacreon in Heav'n" was popular, though I have heard the melody a time
or two in my day!

       -Chuck Stevens
```

recording using the melody attributed to Henry Clay Work (who also wrote
such favorites as 'My Grandfather's Clock' and 'Marching Through 
Georgia')... more importantly, though, listen to the voice... that 
*voice*, nasally and adenoidal... they don't make 'em like that any more, 
by gum.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What statement is executed next?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T16:01:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305041417.69bb9fb8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8623d3bf.0305021015.559061f7@posting.google.com> <3eb2fe81$1_2@127.0.0.1> <8a2d426e.0305031908.c72712b@posting.google.com> <3eb53461_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote

> My response was based on what he posted, yours went further.

> > You're correct only if 200-TEST wasn't PERFORMed.

> > > > 400-EXIT.
> > > > 02403              EXIT.


It also depends on what follows line 02403.  One may expect that it is
another paragraph name or perhaps the end of the source file.  In fact
the EXIT is a no-op (and is thus not 'executed') and any further code
in the 403-Exit paragraph will be executed next.  Of course 'further
code' may give a compile error or warning, depending on the usual
constraints.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What statement is executed next?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T19:00:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0305041800.7f833aa5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8623d3bf.0305021015.559061f7@posting.google.com> <3eb2fe81$1_2@127.0.0.1> <8a2d426e.0305031908.c72712b@posting.google.com> <3eb53461_2@127.0.0.1> <217e491a.0305041418.3fe255cb@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0305041418.3fe255cb@posting.google.com>...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> in the 403-Exit paragraph will be executed next.  

Hi Richard,

From what I remember, how EXIT performs depends on how the associated
pgraph(s) was (were) entered. If via a PERFORM stmt EXIT will behave
as a branch back to the NSI following the PERFORM. If entered via a GO
TO or a "fall through", it behaves as a "noop". I haven't looked at
the code generated (if any) by an EXIT stmt, but I do know the
behavior in an MVS/mainframe environment is as I've outlined above. In
this case the pgraphs were entered via a PERFORM, so control should
return to the stmt following the PERFORM, n'est-ce pas?
  
Of course 'further
> code' may give a compile error or warning, depending on the usual
> constraints.

In an MVS level mainframe environment, allowing "further code" was
introduced in COBOLII. Prior to that an "exit pgraph" was allowed to
contain only 1 stmt, the EXIT stmt.

I've always assumed the "further code" had to precede the EXIT stmt.
I'll have to check that out.

Regards, Jack.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What statement is executed next?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T14:52:44+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b94jnr$jp1$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<8623d3bf.0305021015.559061f7@posting.google.com> <3eb53461_2@127.0.0.1> <217e491a.0305041418.3fe255cb@posting.google.com> <8a2d426e.0305041800.7f833aa5@posting.google.com>`

```
Jack Sleight wrote:

> From what I remember, how EXIT performs depends on how the associated
> pgraph(s) was (were) entered. If via a PERFORM stmt EXIT will behave
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'll have to check that out.

"""An EXIT statement serves only to enable the user to assign a 
procedure-name to a given point in the program. Such an EXIT statement has 
no other effect on the compilation or execution of the program."""

The return to the PERFORM is done because the end of the PERFORM range has 
been reached, in other words the next label after the range specified in 
the PERFORM (or THRU).  Paragraphs are not supposed to be empty so, in 
order to have a pargraph label as the last point in a PERFORM range there 
must be a statement in it.  The EXIT statement is a no-op 'noise word'.  
Because it must have been in its own paragraph it _appeared_ to be 
actioning the return to the PERFORM.

       PERFORM A THRU B
       ...


     A.
        ...
     B.
        EXIT.
        DISPLAY "This will display".
     C.

Should 1) give a compile error (if enforced).  2) execute the DISPLAY 
statement (if not enforced).

However, as allowing other statements in a paragraph with an EXIT is 
non-standard ('85 and '74, maybe other) then the implementation may do 
anything, including, as you say, acting as a return to the PERFORM and 
skipping anything following.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What statement is executed next?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T12:58:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305071158.ff26b9e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8623d3bf.0305021015.559061f7@posting.google.com> <3eb53461_2@127.0.0.1> <217e491a.0305041418.3fe255cb@posting.google.com> <8a2d426e.0305041800.7f833aa5@posting.google.com> <b94jnr$jp1$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <8a2d426e.0305052003.4d841a8b@posting.google.com> <7xVta.390679$gl2.13987889@castor.casema.net>`

```
"Werner Spreeuwenberg" <wspreeuw@wanadoo.nl> wrote 

> Have you guys ever tried reading the Assembler listing of your pgm ??? See
> my other mail.

Yes I have thank you.  What I haven't done is look at the assembler
listing for your program from your compiler.

When I looked at mainframe compilers 30 years ago I found that an EXIT
statement in its own paragraph was replaced by a place holder that was
overwritten by a return address.  However I also noticed that an empty
paragraph was replaced by the same place holder, and a paragraph with
statements had a place holder at its end without an EXIT being there.

I drew the conclusion that the EXIT statement actually had no
relationship to the creation of the place holder at all - at least on
the compilers that I used.

Later (25 years ago) we moved to compilers on systems that prevented
self modification of code.

Now, what did you want to show me ?
```

#### ↳ Re: What statement is executed next?

- **From:** "Werner Spreeuwenberg" <wspreeuw@wanadoo.nl>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T21:38:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oFWsa.374358$gl2.13327171@castor.casema.net>`
- **References:** `<8623d3bf.0305021015.559061f7@posting.google.com>`

```
Firstly, It's bad programming to make use of a GO TO in Cobol. In most
companies it's NOT allowed. Your code should therefor read:
> 02176              IF ARJFILE-STATUS = "00"
> 02177                  DISPLAY "BAD START ARJFILE, STATUS= "
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>                         etc, etc


But, to answer your question : The GO TO paragraph-name statement translates
to a Branch in machine language. The 400-EXIT paragraph name is in fact a
full-word carrying the address of the instruction following that point. The
next statement to be executed will be the EXIT statement. The EXIT statement
will also execute a branche statement according to a return-adress it saved
whilst doing the original PERFORM statement at line number 02169.


"STR" <setar@mindspring.com> schreef in bericht
news:8623d3bf.0305021015.559061f7@posting.google.com...
> A beginner's question.
>
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> 02403              EXIT.
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Portability of 100 percent display was Re: Style (was: Sets and subsets

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T15:39:07-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB2C95B.9000009@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3EA55E79.6090702@newsguy.com> <3ea5ed65.19257366@news.optonline.net> <3EA69C04.7090208@newsguy.com> <3ea88130.21326254@news.optonline.net> <b8ab71$1dv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <b8h8s8$c6r$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eac5f18.274780521@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304272216.24c66f64@posting.google.com> <3ead12ba.320773761@news.optonline.net> <3EAE88F6.8687CB15@istar.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-F1F6F8.22043829042003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
 > In article <3EAE88F6.8687CB15@istar.ca>, "Clark F. Morris, Jr."
 > <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
 > the XML standard.

Moreover, the claim "XML ... presumes a code set" is incorrect.  XML
permits explicitly declaring the encoding in the XML Declaration which
begins the document prolog, if present.  An XML document must contain a
prolog, which must contain an XML Declaration, which must contain an
encoding declaration, if the document does not begin with a UTF-16 Byte
Order Mark (BOM) and is not UTF-8 encoded.

Since the presence of a BOM is equivalent to explicitly declaring that
the document is UCS-2 encoded, the only encoding XML is willing to
assume is UTF-8.  Explicitly declaring the encoding is preferred.

See
<http://www.w3.org/TR/2000/REC-xml-20001006.html#NT-EncodingDecl>.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability of 100 percent display was Re: Style (was: Sets and subsets

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T18:16:37-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-2872ED.18163702052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3EA55E79.6090702@newsguy.com> <3ea5ed65.19257366@news.optonline.net> <3EA69C04.7090208@newsguy.com> <3ea88130.21326254@news.optonline.net> <b8ab71$1dv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eaaa1e8.160795747@news.optonline.net> <b8h8s8$c6r$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eac5f18.274780521@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304272216.24c66f64@posting.google.com> <3ead12ba.320773761@news.optonline.net> <3EAE88F6.8687CB15@istar.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-F1F6F8.22043829042003@corp.supernews.com> <3EB2C95B.9000009@newsguy.com>`

```
In article <3EB2C95B.9000009@newsguy.com>,
 Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
>  > In article <3EAE88F6.8687CB15@istar.ca>, "Clark F. Morris, Jr."
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> <http://www.w3.org/TR/2000/REC-xml-20001006.html#NT-EncodingDecl>.

Appendix F discusses the BOM for IBM EBCDIC (4c-6f-a7-94) with the 
encoding production providing the proper code page.

See 
<http://www.w3.org/TR/2000/REC-xml-20001006.html#sec-guessing-no-ext-info
>

## Thread: Way to view Line Sequential files in Windows using Cobol — 31 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Way to view Line Sequential files in Windows using Cobol

- **From:** dhirschman@omdcorp.com (Dave Hirschman)
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T15:18:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<623abf2.0305021418.59836a0c@posting.google.com>`

```
I am looking for some code or software to incorporate in an
application written in Microfocus Net Express 3.1 that will allow a
user to display Line sequential files correctly generated by Cobol.
Most of the Windows routines either display boxes for the carriage
returns/linefeeds or the alignment is not correct. Also the utility
would need to have the ability to print the file correctly and have
search capabilities. Any input would be greatly appreciated. Thanks in
advance.
```

- **References:** `<623abf2.0305021418.59836a0c@posting.google.com>`

```


Dave Hirschman wrote:

> I am looking for some code or software to incorporate in an
> application written in Microfocus Net Express 3.1 that will allow a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> advance.

It smells like, looks like, is it..... homework ? You state N/E V 3.1,
but are you using the University Edition ? Show us code that you have
written so far, including record formats. Do you want to display in a DOS
text window or are you asking about GUIs. From what appears to be a lack
of expertise at the moment, I would suggest you are way off track thinking
about 'Windowing'.

May be wrong on some of the above observations - but feel free to 'fill us

Jimmy

Jimmy,
This would be for a commercial application. The reason for the
original question was that we have something that does this but Dialog
(part of Net Express) has a limit of 32768 lines in the buffer for the
scrollable window to display. This equates to about 500 pages in a
report which in some cases is not enough. Thanks again.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Way to view Line Sequential files in Windows using Cobol

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T17:25:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB3F7EC.B744ABCC@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<623abf2.0305021418.59836a0c@posting.google.com> <3EB2EE42.5F9D12DA@shaw.ca> <623abf2.0305030428.2da3fbf2@posting.google.com>`

```


Dave Hirschman wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:<3EB2EE42.5F9D12DA@shaw.ca>...
> > Dave Hirschman wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> report which in some cases is not enough. Thanks again.

Dave,

Sorry for that - but your question was so paraphrased, plus the mention of CRLF as a
problem that it led me to believe it was a beginner 'struggling'.

Ahhh . That upper limit - that's Dialog System you are using - correct ? I have never used
it, but I believe it has been considerably enhanced since Pete used the DOS version.

Two possibilities, if you are comfortable with INVOKING from Dialog System :-

- Pete's component  - he provides the Fujitsu runtime (free) so no hassle there.

- Using OO/GUIs -  Create a Listbox using the Dialog Editor and put the transactions into
an Ordered or Sorted collection. In theory you have all of memory to play with. The only
limiting factors are an object reference for the collection itself, additional object
references for each element, and naturally the actual length of each record (element) in
the collection.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Way to view Line Sequential files in Windows using Cobol

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T11:21:00+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb2fd59_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<623abf2.0305021418.59836a0c@posting.google.com>`

```

"Dave Hirschman" <dhirschman@omdcorp.com> wrote in message
news:623abf2.0305021418.59836a0c@posting.google.com...
> I am looking for some code or software to incorporate in an
> application written in Microfocus Net Express 3.1 that will allow a
> user to display Line sequential files correctly generated by Cobol.

These are records up to 255 bytes long terminated by x'090A', right?

> Most of the Windows routines either display boxes for the carriage
> returns/linefeeds or the alignment is not correct.

>Also the utility
> would need to have the ability to print the file correctly and have
> search capabilities.

I have a component (written in COBOL) that does exactly this. It provides a
re-sizable GUI window to list the file and you can specify the colour scheme
you want (default is Green on Black). The window border also includes a
printer icon and you can click this to print the file.

If you want searching  on any string in the file, and scrolling, I can
provide that too, but then the "window" becomes a console where you can
input search strings etc. (Or, you can pass a search string through the
component's properties, if you are invoking it from COBOL and have obtained
the required search string from elsewhere. The found string is highlighted
in the window and you can also specify context highlighting for a given
number of characters before and after the search term.)

The component installs and registers itself on your system. You access it
from COBOL using its Methods and Properties,  and the INVOKE verb, as
described in the accompanying HELP file.

Obviously, you can include it in your Web Pages also, running on Client or
Server, as well as the Windows desktop.

If you want it, send me your snail mail address and I'll despatch it on CD.
The price is $US100.00 and this includes airmail and help and support for
the lifetime of the software.

Pete.




 Any input would be greatly appreciated. Thanks in
> advance.

You mean licensing? I haven't decided yet. Probably not. I am more
interested in establishing goodwill and a potential market than I am with
creaming everything from day one. It costs me nothing, as it is developed
and in use in one of my own applications. I am not required to pay a license
for the support software so why would I charge a customer one? (I know... to
make money. There's a novel concept in IT <G>...)

Anyway, it is academic now, as the computer it is on, is somewhere on the
ocean and won't be here for 3 months.

Furthermore, you have now stated that you need to handle files in excess of
200MB. I doubt that, as it stands, it would do that. I could extend it to
use bit stream IO and grab large chunks of the file into memory, but then
you would have a memory eater that could impact your existing applications.

You have had some good advice.

I would go with connecting to the WP of choice through OLE/COM or using one
of the components Frederico outlined, or give Michael the job...

Pete.

Following on your answer to Jimmy.

You will most probabiliy be able to use the products from
FarPoint technologies. (www.fpoint.com), in particular the List Pro
control.
http://www.fpoint.com/products/list/details.html

Have a look at it and then tell us something.

Regarding your alignment complaint, I think this will be due to the
fact that you probably are using a proportional font. Choose a fixed
font like Courier and this should solve that part of the problem.

The above product is only an example. There are others, some better,
some worst.

#### ↳ Re: Way to view Line Sequential files in Windows using Cobol

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T14:36:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xZTsa.10298$Jy3.1069534@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<623abf2.0305021418.59836a0c@posting.google.com>`

```
It strikes me that the entire purpose of a "line sequential" file is to be
compatable with the system text editor. That is why the Unix version usually
differs from a Windows version.  "Edit" on a PC does a perfectly fine job of
examining such a file, as does NotePad or just about any source code editor.
I'd stick to allowing the user to specify their favourite text editor.

Donald

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> advance.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Way to view Line Sequential files in Windows using Cobol

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T03:32:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB4808F.177377D9@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<623abf2.0305021418.59836a0c@posting.google.com> <xZTsa.10298$Jy3.1069534@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb45212.6362336@news.optonline.net>`

```


Robert Wagner wrote:

> Excellent idea. He should 'execute' the file and let the registry specify which
> viewer to use. Use CreateProcess to feed the file name to cmd.exe.
>

Nice solution, perhaps - but it's relevance ? We haven't yet found out *exactly*
why he even wants to display the file !

Assumption (and makes an ASS-OF-U-AND-ME). Could it be that he wants to do
something other than just a simple display - select a record, perhaps change it,
delete it, hive it off to somewhere else. As he is an existing user of Dialog
System that suggests to me that is possibly the case.

The reason he can't do it in Dialog System - there's an upper limit  for creating a
Dialog System Table, a hard-coded limit to that particular tool  - which is not an
obstacle to GUIs and  OO collections. (In fact if I knew what he really wanted I
could illustrate he doesn't even need the GUI Dialog Editor - obviously he has to
create a background window as a Parent, but both that and the Listbox can be
created dynamically invoking the appropriate class/methos using x, y, w and h
co-ordinates. For gratis I could even give him MyDialog which I've previously
mentioned, containing the necessary 'general' methods. And if he looks back through
here, he can even pick up on my OO Class for Line Seq files).

Solutions without the spec don't help too much. Design FIRST; Programming SECOND.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Way to view Line Sequential files in Windows using Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T09:44:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ON8ta.50$ER4.20624@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<623abf2.0305021418.59836a0c@posting.google.com> <xZTsa.10298$Jy3.1069534@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb45212.6362336@news.optonline.net> <3EB4808F.177377D9@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3EB4808F.177377D9@shaw.ca...
>
>
> Robert Wagner wrote:
>
> > Excellent idea. He should 'execute' the file and let the registry
specify which
> > viewer to use. Use CreateProcess to feed the file name to cmd.exe.
> >
>
> Nice solution, perhaps - but it's relevance ? We haven't yet found out
*exactly*
> why he even wants to display the file !
>
> Assumption (and makes an ASS-OF-U-AND-ME). Could it be that he wants to do
> something other than just a simple display - select a record, perhaps
change it,
> delete it, hive it off to somewhere else. As he is an existing user of
Dialog
> System that suggests to me that is possibly the case.
>

But that is the point Jimmy.  Using a generic text editor is as "generic" as
you can get.  You can select a record, change it, delete it, insert a new
one, etc., etc., without having a clue in the world what the data you are
going to be dealing with looks like in advance.  The ultimate "patch"
software, when dealing with "line sequential" files is equivalent to the
ultimate text editor. Once you know what you want to change, where you want
to change it, and what you want to change it to, *then* you can get more
specific.  Until then, a text editor is the best.

Roberts suggestion of using the registry is one that I'm so so on.  The big
problem with it is that you have to standardize the file type and register
it.  That means that the text editor is "officially" the update method for
the data, and I am not sure I would go that far.  The text editor should be
the method of last resort, unless the line sequential file happens to be
source code.

Donald


Donald


> The reason he can't do it in Dialog System - there's an upper limit  for
creating a
> Dialog System Table, a hard-coded limit to that particular tool  - which
is not an
> obstacle to GUIs and  OO collections. (In fact if I knew what he really
wanted I
> could illustrate he doesn't even need the GUI Dialog Editor - obviously he
has to
> create a background window as a Parent, but both that and the Listbox can
be
> created dynamically invoking the appropriate class/methos using x, y, w
and h

back through
> here, he can even pick up on my OO Class for Line Seq files).
>
> Solutions without the spec don't help too much. Design FIRST; Programming
SECOND.
>
> Jimmy

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Way to view Line Sequential files in Windows using Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-05T04:21:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB5E33A.28FC743A@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<623abf2.0305021418.59836a0c@posting.google.com> <xZTsa.10298$Jy3.1069534@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb45212.6362336@news.optonline.net> <3EB4808F.177377D9@shaw.ca> <ON8ta.50$ER4.20624@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
> news:3EB4808F.177377D9@shaw.ca...
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald

Agreed - but with a slightly different twist. Very likely that a particular
end-user has one text editor that is his/her preference, i.e., one (s)he is
comfortable with. To keep the thing 'integrated' within the application, I have
a Default file - one Relative  record which contains as additional information
the pathname for their favourite editor - could be NotePad, WordPad, Word or
something considerably more flexible than any of those - particularly 'width' of
lines.

Converting RM to MF I generate a series of intermediary Line Sequentials plus
error files, as appropriate. Display them in a listbox, (check the Filename
Class in N/E V 3.0), having previously used wildcards to get the set of files
you want, then display them in a Listbox. Select from Listbox and the
appropriate file gets invoked to open, edit or print, using OLE as appropriate.

BUT - we still don't know exactly what he wants to do - for example, what isn't
lining up, why is CRLF a problem ? An extreme case - is he going to screw up an
audit trail - not likely with a Line Sequential, but we are still guessing.

Jimmy
```

Jimmy,

I basically would like to give the end-user the ability to view the
print file, search for text and scroll. Currently the end-user can do
this. I do not want to give them the ability to edit/change the print
file. If there would be a way to select text and print only the
selected text that would be good also. ie: print the total page only
that is at the end of the report file. My main problem is the Dialog
limit and how to get around that. Some of the print files are over 250
MB in size. I don't see this size file being stored completely in
memory either. I was just looking at editors to see what posibilities
they had but they provide too much functionality and have different
problems. Thanks

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-05T20:19:19+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2kcdbvs7l19v5in3c1rr83g9mr3ja44m4i@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<623abf2.0305021418.59836a0c@posting.google.com> <xZTsa.10298$Jy3.1069534@news20.bellglobal.com> <3eb45212.6362336@news.optonline.net> <3EB4808F.177377D9@shaw.ca> <ON8ta.50$ER4.20624@news20.bellglobal.com> <3EB5E33A.28FC743A@shaw.ca> <623abf2.0305050548.2466941a@posting.google.com>`

```
On 5 May 2003 06:48:33 -0700, dhirschman@omdcorp.com (Dave Hirschman)
wrote:

>"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:<3EB5E33A.28FC743A@shaw.ca>...
>> Donald Tees wrote:
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>problems. Thanks
If this is what you intend then you have a few problems to solve.
The main one is "do I load the whole file?". This is fine if the file
is small, but with 250 MB there is a big overhead.
If you don't load the whole file, how do I search it? It is "almost"
obvious that in this case you will not be able to do it using whatever
control you use, so you will need to do this yourself.

It can be done, but I don't know of any control out there that will
allow for such a big file and at the same time allow for search and
print.

Just as an example FarPoint Spread Control allows for a maximum of 2
billion lines, with the amount of data dependent on memory (but it is
a memory eater!!), and this one will allow you to search, select and
print either the selection or the whole thing.

You can also choose not to load the whole file, and still scroll
up/down, but the search option will only search the loaded lines, and
the same goes for the printing bit. Nevertheless if your average file
is below 1 milion and the average size below 100MB then this is doable
with this control (and as I said before there are others out there, so
search and try them).


And I am sure James would gladly do it for you (for a fee).

FF
Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

```


Dave Hirschman wrote:

>
> I basically would like to give the end-user the ability to view the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> problems. Thanks

OK - now we're getting there - a print Preview, and as a little bonus thrown in, the
ability to 'block print' or 'page print'.

The options suggested so far, Pete's component, Robert/Donald's suggestion of calling
favourite editor would do it for you, but I get the impression you want to keep the user's
sticky little fingers off the editing features <G>.

Bearing in mind you want to View and Print - one further option, (although you can't avoid
'editing' showing up as a feature),  and well documented. with examples. Check the White
Papers at the M/F site - looking for one by Wayne Rippin where he illustrates using either
Word or Excel. You write your printfile to Word and then open Word as a viewer.

All the documentation for any methods you might need associated with Word are taken from
the IDE dropdown menu for Type Library Assistant. (If Word is not your tool of preference
for w/p, check out the Type Library Assistant to see which other products are listed -
quite a large list).

Possible shortcut to all this - go with Pete for $100 <G>

HTH

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Way to view Line Sequential files in Windows using Cobol

- **From:** "David Sands" <david.sands@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T09:24:36+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b97r2t$49t$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<623abf2.0305021418.59836a0c@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Dave,

You could have a look at the GUI Class library "RichEditField" control that
comes with Net Express.

This has the facility to load a text file via the "openTextFileZ" method.
There is a "findTextZ" method to support a basic search and you could use
"disable" to editing of the data. Printing can be done via the "print"
method.

If its a COBOL type report file you could also set the font to be fixed
rather than proportional so tabular type data will line up.

There is a demo in DialogSystem\CommonControls that shows Rich Edit Controls
in use.

HTH
David.

news:623abf2.0305021418.59836a0c@posting.google.com...
> I am looking for some code or software to incorporate in an
> application written in Microfocus Net Express 3.1 that will allow a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> advance.

## Thread: Period rules — 10 msgs

### Period rules

- **From:** "Craig Franck" <craig.franck@verizon.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T03:08:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%oGsa.32775$xw4.26477@nwrdny01.gnilink.net>`

```
I am learning Cobol from "TYC in 21 Days" and "The
Revolutionary Guide to Cobol" (the compiler is down-
loadable from the wrox site), and neither book seems to
have a summary of when to use a period, and when not
to. I can program in several other languages, and it seems
as if the period is a statement delimiter like the semicolon
in C, but sometimes it's like a curly brace in that it delimits
the end of a  block (like when you put a period after the
last statement rather than use an END-IF). I find myself
adding periods to get code to compile, but am not
always sure why they are needed. (It seems like the compiler
pays more attention to periods than keywords when it
parses the syntax.)

WROX says "a  period signifies the end of a paragraph
(and as you will see later, can be the end of a sentence or
of some statements);"

From what I can gather, what goes between a PERFORM/
END-PERFORM is not a compound statement (WROX
claims it is in syntax diagrams), but rather PERFORM/
END-PERFORM is a single statement, so placing a
period inside causes the compiler to think it came to the
end of a statement prematurely.

Below is a simple program with some error messages
from the compiler that came with the WROX book.
If someone could comment and point out exactly
how periods fit into the syntax when in the Procedure
Division, that would be great.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. HELLO.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77 Counter PIC 999 VALUE 0.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      * First Hello call
       PERFORM HELLO.
       PERFORM UNTIL Counter = 10
      * Second HELLO call.
           PERFORM HELLO
      * A period               here^ gives "misplaced scope
      * delimiter" error msg
           ADD 1 TO Counter
      * A period              here^ gives "Hello is undefined
      * in this scope," but identifies the problem as
      * being with the first HELLO call as well as second
       END-PERFORM.
       PROGRAM-DONE.
           STOP RUN.

       HELLO.
           DISPLAY "HELLO".
```

#### ↳ Re: Period rules

- **From:** "Anon" <anon@anon.org>
- **Date:** 2003-05-02T23:16:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8vfqr$kab$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<%oGsa.32775$xw4.26477@nwrdny01.gnilink.net>`

```
A period in COBOL is not like a statement delimiter in C.
The only place a period is required in the procedure division
is procedure division itself, paragraph names and a period must
be the last thing in a paragraph. You can do everything else without
any periods. However, if used a period functions as a hard
scope terminator. A complex series of nested if's can be
terminated with one period instead of a long series of
end-if. I've sometimes had long series of IF, IF, IF ELSE
IF ELSE ELSE IF etc. One period, splat ends the whole structure
without a bunch of END-IF END-IF END-IF etc. or a
whole bunch of braces as in C. Whether this is good or not
depends on who you ask. As you discovered a period will also
terminate the scope of an in-line PERFORM or other verb.
General rule, a period hard ends whatever is above it.
Don't put a period in the middle of something.
These are all functionally equivalent, that is
move, add, divide are subject to "if a = b"

if a = b
  move c to d y
  add f to g
  divide f by g giving x rounded remainder z.

if a = b
  move c to d y
  add f to g
  divide f by g giving x rounded remainder z
end-if

if a = b
  move c to d y
  add f to g
  divide f by g giving x rounded remainder z
end-if. <-
          this period is allowed but not required
          unless it is the end of the paragraph



As an aside, COBOL does not have to be in all upper-case if
you have anything newer than a 20 year old compiler. Also,
data-names (variables) are not case sensitive as they are
in C. Counter = countER = CoUnTeR, it is all refers to the
same variable.

> I am learning Cobol from "TYC in 21 Days" and "The
> Revolutionary Guide to Cobol" (the compiler is down-
…[60 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Period rules

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T08:53:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB3C9C7.7030204@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<%oGsa.32775$xw4.26477@nwrdny01.gnilink.net>`

```
If only you knew the debate that has occurred here over this issue... 
but I'll try to explain it for you in a nutshell.  :)

Craig Franck wrote:
> WROX says "a  period signifies the end of a paragraph
> (and as you will see later, can be the end of a sentence or
> of some statements);"

This is the key.  A period (full stop in UK and NZ) delimits a sentence, 
just as it does in English.  Prior to COBOL 85, you had to end a 
conditional statement (among other statements, such as file i/o) with a 
period.  However, COBOL 85 introduced "scope delimiters" which you can 
use to terminate a conditional (again, among other things).  This opened 
up some flexibility as to when you have to use periods.

There are two prevailing philosophies.  The first is similar to 
pre-COBOL 85, except that the scope delimiter is included.  The second 
is based on the rule that a paragraph must end in a period.  Using this 
style, you put a period and a space -before- each paragraph name (and 
one immediately after the name).  So, under concept one, you might have 
code like...

v (column 7)
  Procedure Division.

  Driver-Paragraph.
      Perform Hello.
      Perform 10 Times
          Perform Hello
      End-Perform.

  Stop-Run-Paragraph.
      Stop Run.

And with the other, you would have...

                    v note omitted period
  Procedure Division

  . Driver-Paragraph.
      Perform Hello
      Perform 10 Times
          Perform Hello
      End-Perform

  . Stop-Run-Paragraph.
      Stop Run

  .
  ^ note ending period

Personal preference - #2.  You can cut and paste code between 
paragraphs, add If statements, and all that stuff, without having to 
worry about whether you deleted periods or moved them to the proper line.
```

#### ↳ Re: Period rules

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T13:56:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-C575D9.13561703052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<%oGsa.32775$xw4.26477@nwrdny01.gnilink.net>`

```
Ahh, what a can of worms this could open.  What follows is my opinion on 
periods.

A period is an absolute scope terminator, once used it stops everything.  
This caused some problems in previous versions of Cobol when people 
tried to add structure to their code.

So in 1985 the explicit scope terminators arrived to correct this 
problem.  They are quite similar to the end-curly-brace from C, and all 
scope initiating keywords have an implicit start-curly-brace.

For example, in the case of C, a "while (cond) {  statements  }" would 
be identical to the cobol "Perform cond statements End-Perform", with 
the implicit start-curley-brace between the cond and the statements.

To anyone who groks block structured languages this makes perfect sense.  
Each Keyword starts a new scope, and is terminated by the cooresponding 
End-Keyword.

If all of this sounds too simple, you are right.  What the 85 standard 
did not do is remove the nuclear bomb-like power of periods to terminate 
scope, so now you could nest keywords and still stop everything with a 
single period.  

If you had the statements in C or COBOL:

   if(cond1){        if cond1
      statements        statements
      if(cond2){        if cond2
         statements        statements
      }                 end-if
   }                 end-if

you would expect that the start-curley-braces, explicit or implicit, 
would have a 1 for 1 pairing with the end-curley-braces.  But because of 
the periods absolute scope destructive power, this is not true, you 
could have:

   if cond1
      statements
      if cond2
         statements .    <=====this period is now 2 "end-ifs"

What is more, anything above the first if is also terminated.  This is 
the real problem that I have with thier use.  As a practical matter, 
when they are used nesting beyond a single level is rarely used, and 
structure suffers because of it.

There are several other problems with their indiscriminant use like 
source maintenance errors and cut&paste scope errors.

There is good news in all of this though -- there are only two places 
you need to use a period in post-85 cobol.  The first is after a 
paragraph-name label, the second is at the end of the paragraph.

If you avoid periods in  all other places, and use explicit scope 
terminators (END-*) instead your code will not suffer from these 
problems.

My best advice is to let periods, as scope terminators, whither and die.

Regards,

Joe Zitzelberger

In article <%oGsa.32775$xw4.26477@nwrdny01.gnilink.net>,
 "Craig Franck" <craig.franck@verizon.net> wrote:
>
> I am learning Cobol from "TYC in 21 Days" and "The
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> Division, that would be great.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Period rules

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T21:44:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0305032044.12a34a9e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<%oGsa.32775$xw4.26477@nwrdny01.gnilink.net> <joe_zitzelberger-C575D9.13561703052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi Craig,

If you want to avoid the confusion while learning the basics, my
advice is to abandon the periods except at end of pgraphs and after
pgraph heads and to terminate a COPY stmt (they're required). Use
scope delimiters where appropriate and if multiple END-IFs get
tedious, use a period in those situations if you want to. You DON'T
have to be "religious" about ant of this.

It's also a good idea to put the periods used in the P DIV on a
separate line. It's another way of avoiding copying a period into the
middle of stmt when "borrowing" code from another part of your pgm and
causing an error.

Here's a rewrite of your code (without the comments) using what I said
above:

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           PERFORM HELLO
           PERFORM UNTIL Counter = 10
              PERFORM HELLO
              ADD 1 TO Counter
           END-PERFORM
           .
       PROGRAM-DONE.
           STOP RUN
           .
       HELLO.
           DISPLAY "HELLO"
           .

Try it, you'll sleep better. :)

Regards, Jack.


Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message news:<joe_zitzelberger-C575D9.13561703052003@corp.supernews.com>...
> Ahh, what a can of worms this could open.  What follows is my opinion on 
> periods.
…[95 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Division, that would be great.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Period rules

- **From:** "Craig Franck" <craig.franck@verizon.net>

```
"Jack Sleight" <jacksleight@hotmail.com> wrote

> Here's a rewrite of your code (without the comments) using what I said
> above:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>            .

Everything now makes much more sense.

I think what was giving me the most trouble was, once you
insert the paragraph "PROGRAM-DONE.," a period now
becomes necessary after END-PERFORM because the
STOP RUN is now associated with PROGRAM-DONE.

I should have realized this because in the code

        Proc-A.
            display "In Proc-A".
        End-Proc-A.

"End-Proc-A" is only logically associated with "Proc-A;" it's
not the end of Proc-A, but rather the beginning of another
paragraph. This was itself a revelation from the day before:
The next label/paragraph name acts as a return statement did
in early BASIC when it just had GOSUB's with line numbers,
but it also can become a target itself with

        Perform End-Proc-A

The text of both books made this clear, but I guess I just lapsed
into that because it's how most other program language are
structured. I'm surprised the Cobol doesn't have an explicit
"return" statement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Period rules

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T16:30:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EKeta.177$J%5.53400@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<%oGsa.32775$xw4.26477@nwrdny01.gnilink.net> <joe_zitzelberger-C575D9.13561703052003@corp.supernews.com> <8a2d426e.0305032044.12a34a9e@posting.google.com> <5udta.40913$xw4.12164@nwrdny01.gnilink.net>`

```
"Craig Franck" <craig.franck@verizon.net> wrote in message news:5udta.40913

>
> The text of both books made this clear, but I guess I just lapsed
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Craig Franck

<G>, well, it does, it just does not have anything to do with paragraphs.
The "goback" statement, the "exit method" statement, the "exit program"
statement, and the "stop run" statement can are all examples of explicit
return statements. There is also an implied return statement at the end of
every program ... but they all return from *programs/subprograms* rather
than paragraphs.

In fact, the paragraph name and the paragraph that go with it are NOT
subroutines in the generally accepted meaning of the word.  The paragraph
name is simply a label for all the code that follows it up to, but not
including, the next paragraph name.

If you simply list statements with interspersed paragraph names, the
statements will execute one after the other in an identical fashion to a
basic program with all lines numbered.  The paragraph names in and of
themselves are simply place markers.

The big difference between cobol and other languages is not the return
mechanism, but the calling methodology ... the perform statement.  If you
want to compare it to basic, think of an entirely new type of "call", where
you can specify both the start and the end of the subroutine in the call
statement.  For example "call line 300, treating line 556 as the return".
That would be the rough equivalent of the "perform thru" in Cobol.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Period rules

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T18:38:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305041738.df95bf7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<%oGsa.32775$xw4.26477@nwrdny01.gnilink.net> <joe_zitzelberger-C575D9.13561703052003@corp.supernews.com> <8a2d426e.0305032044.12a34a9e@posting.google.com> <5udta.40913$xw4.12164@nwrdny01.gnilink.net>`

```
"Craig Franck" <craig.franck@verizon.net> wrote 

> I'm surprised the Cobol doesn't have an explicit
> "return" statement.

It may be that you think of C's (for example) return as being 'go back
from whence you came', and thus should be in other languages.  This is
not strictly so.

A C void routine (function) need not have a return statement, the end
of the routine will cause the 'from whence you came'.

void testme()  {  puts("in testme"); }

The return statement indicates the _value_ that is to be returned, and
thus a non-void function must have a return statement.

As a Cobol PERFORMed paragraph cannot return a value there is no need
for an explicit 'return' statement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Period rules

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Craig Franck" <craig.franck@verizon.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-05T02:37:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j7kta.49243$J27.6132@nwrdny02.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<%oGsa.32775$xw4.26477@nwrdny01.gnilink.net> <joe_zitzelberger-C575D9.13561703052003@corp.supernews.com> <8a2d426e.0305032044.12a34a9e@posting.google.com> <5udta.40913$xw4.12164@nwrdny01.gnilink.net> <217e491a.0305041738.df95bf7@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote

> "Craig Franck" <craig.franck@verizon.net> wrote
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> thus a non-void function must have a return statement.

It's actually a quirk of C that

int f1(void) {}
int f2(void) {return;}

are identical and only invoke undefined behavior if you try
to use the return value.

> As a Cobol PERFORMed paragraph cannot return a value there is no need
> for an explicit 'return' statement.

That's true, and as someone else pointed out, it's only when
a paragraph is performed that you get control returned.
Otherwise, paragraphs just fall straight thru.
```

## Thread: Deleting "invalid" character from a file — 9 this month / 11 total

### Deleting "invalid" character from a file

- **From:** datacraft@netzero.net (L V Squitin)
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T05:56:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<be9d5d6a.0305030456.23e527e9@posting.google.com>`

```
I support an old PC application developed under Ryan Macfarland COBOL
vers 6.10. I am having a problem processing external input files that
contain the character hex 1A (decimal 26) ASCII right arrow.
As far as I can tell, RM/COBOL treats this character as an End-of-File
marker, and does not read beyond it.
The characted appears once every 15 records or so.
I would appreciate any suggestions on dealing programatically with
this problem. Manually editing the file to delete the offending char
is not an option.
```

#### ↳ Re: Deleting "invalid" character from a file

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T13:10:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IOicnYeWV6itmymjXTWJhQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<be9d5d6a.0305030456.23e527e9@posting.google.com>`

```

"L V Squitin" <datacraft@netzero.net> wrote in message
news:be9d5d6a.0305030456.23e527e9@posting.google.com...
> I support an old PC application developed under Ryan Macfarland COBOL
> vers 6.10. I am having a problem processing external input files that
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> is not an option.

"1A" IS an end-of-file marker.

Can you get to the source code? If so, you might be able to redefine the
file attribute to be a non-text file, whereupon, I bet, the compiler will
use the length of the file as a limit instead of stopping at EOF.

Failing that, you might have to pre-process the file with another program to
change intermediate "1A" to a space.
```

#### ↳ Re: Deleting "invalid" character from a file

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T22:05:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb43ba2.617122@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<be9d5d6a.0305030456.23e527e9@posting.google.com>`

```
datacraft@netzero.net (L V Squitin) wrote:

>I support an old PC application developed under Ryan Macfarland COBOL
>vers 6.10. I am having a problem processing external input files that
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>is not an option.

I write a FILTER program which can remove them, or translate them to something
else. Send an email and I'll reply with a copy, including source. It's written
in COBOL. 

In the bad old days of DOS 1.0, 1A was the end of file marker on line sequential
files. 

Alternatively, you could define the file as record sequential, record size 512
(a disk sector), and parse records yourself using UNSTRING DELIMITED BY x'0D0A'.
```

written
> in COBOL.

sequential
> files.

        You would still need to consider Tabs.  Bother.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Deleting "invalid" character from a file

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T02:03:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebae561.26410746@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<be9d5d6a.0305030456.23e527e9@posting.google.com> <3eb43ba2.617122@news.optonline.net> <MrOcnWkbrb98aSSjXTWcpg@comcast.com>`

```
"Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>        You would still need to consider Tabs.  Bother.

Actually, 'do it yourself expansion' is a FEATURE in re tabs. There are two tab
conventions, not one. COBOL uses the text-editor convention where there is a tab
every 8 cols. Files produced by spreadsheets and the like use a 'tab delimited'
format which has one tab between columns, no matter how far apart they are. 

How do you know where columns begin? You guess. I usually make them 10 or 20
cols wide.
```

#### ↳ Re: Deleting "invalid" character from a file

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T01:21:39+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d6g8bv899f2nvcpudepbi7gjkoqq8p8pe8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<be9d5d6a.0305030456.23e527e9@posting.google.com>`

```
On 3 May 2003 05:56:49 -0700 datacraft@netzero.net (L V Squitin) wrote:

:>I support an old PC application developed under Ryan Macfarland COBOL
:>vers 6.10. I am having a problem processing external input files that
:>contain the character hex 1A (decimal 26) ASCII right arrow.
:>As far as I can tell, RM/COBOL treats this character as an End-of-File
:>marker, and does not read beyond it.
:>The characted appears once every 15 records or so.
:>I would appreciate any suggestions on dealing programatically with
:>this problem. Manually editing the file to delete the offending char
:>is not an option.

What does the SELECT and FD look like?
```

_Thread started [2003-04](2003-04.md#t-0d2696a5cf)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style (was: Sets and subsets

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T17:42:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb3d538.76586182@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eac5f18.274780521@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304272216.24c66f64@posting.google.com> <3ead12ba.320773761@news.optonline.net> <b8jtd4$982$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eaf1cbf.454430474@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304292251.431ce5cf@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

>> XML files are usually not read by an application program. They are read by a
>> tool which loads them into a database by translating XML field names, read
from
>> a dictionary, into database field names. When the input contains undefined
>> names, the program reports them, someone updates the dictionary, and the load
is
>> rerun. 
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>process that data.

Repition is not a problem because databases handle repitition with lists rather
than arrays. Additional information in the form of structural relationships can
usually be discarded. My database structure is adequate for _my_ needs

>> >But only useful for exchanging data when an application is agreed between 
>> >partners.
>> 
>> The relationship can be more casual, thanks to human intervention and the
>> dictionary. When we get previously undefined 'Product SKU', we can equate it
to
>> 'product'.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>The same problems occur.

Suppose an untagged file contains a column of six digit codes. What are they? I
take ten samples and look them up in reference files, finding one is a valid
Sedol code (stocks), three are valid Canadian postal codes, zero are valid type
E UPCs and seven are valid NAICS industry codes. Am I to conclude it must be a
NAICS code? This is guesswork.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style (was: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T07:51:02+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b916kq$5bl$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b7tepn$9tb$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eaf1cbf.454430474@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304292251.431ce5cf@posting.google.com> <3eb3d538.76586182@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>It is no different from what has occurred with previous 'standard'
>>interchange formats such as EDIFACT.  A basic mechanism extracts the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> to conclude it must be a NAICS code? This is guesswork.

And if the XML has <code>786532</code> then you are no better informed as 
to what it is. Guessing is a poor substitute for definition.

With EDIFACT it may be RFF+XXX,786532' where XXX is _defined_ as qualifying 
a specific value.

As I say.  When there is a specific application defining an XML document 
then you can be sure as to what the values represent, exactly as can be 
done with EDIFACT message definitions.  Without that XML gives the 
_appearance_ of being understandable without, in many cases, the substance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Style (was: Sets and subsets

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-04T02:22:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb473a9.14962492@news.optonline.net>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>to what it is. Guessing is a poor substitute for definition.

XML wouldn't be tagged 'code', it would be tagged whatever they use internally.
If it's an external standard, it would be labeled 'NAICS code'. If it labelled
generically 'product code', that probably means internally assigned. 

>With EDIFACT it may be RFF+XXX,786532' where XXX is _defined_ as qualifying 
>a specific value.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>_appearance_ of being understandable without, in many cases, the substance.

<sigh> Technocrats will never relinquish the illusion they have all bases
covered, past and future.
```

## Thread: NetExpress problem — 9 msgs

### NetExpress problem

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T14:32:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6WTsa.10296$Jy3.1069100@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
I am doing some work with NetExpress, and have run into a rather strange
thing that I cannot figure out.  The "make" facility has suddenly stopped
doing a rebuild for most of the file dependancies.

I've done the obvious things, like told it to check all file dependancies,
and the structure seems to be fine ... the copies all appear to be correct
in the list view of the project structure.  I also checked the dates of the
offending object modules to assure myself that I had not done a build with a
forward date.  As far as I can see, everything appears in order ... but I
can still change a copybook file and the project does not re-compile the
source module containing it.

The NetExpress Version is 3.00.  Since a full rebuild takes about twenty
minutes, this is becoming a real pain in the butt.  Anybody have any ideas?

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress problem

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-03T20:46:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB42714.4290CD1D@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<6WTsa.10296$Jy3.1069100@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

> I am doing some work with NetExpress, and have run into a rather strange
> thing that I cannot figure out.  The "make" facility has suddenly stopped
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald

Firstly, greatly surprised you were even using N/E, then was thrown by your use
of the word "make", assuming you were going from command line.

However I read on and you are creating a project with a controlling App file -
correct - so you are doing it from the IDE with a Build or Build All ? Now
sometimes, and very rarely, things just seem to get out of sync - can't think
when I had the last one. They are a pain in the butt and you have to get really
innovative to find them.

Now I do a lot of to-ing and fro-ing between my MSCOBOL V.5 DOS Text Editor and
the N/E IDE. I would say 99.9% of the time the N/E IDE recognizes I've updated a
source outside of N/E.

By no means a complete list, but some suggestions :-

- Are there any errormessage files (xxxxx.msg from memory), kicking around in
the Debug directory - delete 'em and try a Rebuild. (As appropriate delete any
int or gnt files - but you will find you will have to exit N/E to do this).

- Delete the copyfile from the Project IDE - do a Rebuild All, (or Rebuild just
this program, if only one is affected) and you are obviously going to get errors
because the copyfile is missing. Add the copyfile back into the project and
Rebuild All again. Most times this gives you the complete solution.

To ensure it's happening as you want - on adding or deleting, I make the project
change and get out of the IDE so that I know the alterations will get included
in the App file before it closes. Then reopen the IDE, in theory, with those
alterations included.

I know above is a bit weird - but it's you trying to beat the logic of the IDE
<G>

If above doesn't work and you are using University Edition, there is a separate
query area on Answer Exchange covering UE - where you could post your question.

Jimmy
```

Donald Tees wrote:

> I have about 600 of them that have suddenly stopped working ...

Oooooh ! Copyfiles I hope, not source.

> > If above doesn't work and you are using University Edition, there is a
> separate
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald

For Home Page :
http://microfocus.com/index.asp?bhcp=1

Assuming it is University Edition :
http://microfocus.com/academic/academicprograms_support.asp

Assuming it is not University Edition :
http://www.cobolportal.com/microfocusforum/

One other possibility - depending upon how many separate source programs there
are, not copyfiles. A bit extreme - completely remove project, set up a new
project and add your source back in - no need to do this for copyfiles as their
inclusion is triggered by adding the appropriate source program to the project.
Then do a complete Rebuild.

Closing thought - if you did try deleting from the project for say one program -
remove the source from both the left and right panes in the IDE. Subsequently of
course, re-adding to the project puts it in the left tree pane and
alphabetically in the right Listbox pane.

Jimmy
```
