###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress problem

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T05:59:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB74B9D.6AAD0A22@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<6WTsa.10296$Jy3.1069100@news20.bellglobal.com> <3EB42714.4290CD1D@shaw.ca> <D9uta.1454$ER4.180393@news20.bellglobal.com> <3EB6E543.7B1A8B96@shaw.ca> <yeGta.3349$VJ.289903@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
> news:3EB6E543.7B1A8B96@shaw.ca...
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ouch ! Ouch !

> > For Home Page :
> > http://microfocus.com/index.asp?bhcp=1
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Certainly as a last resort - there's no way you want to bugger around if it
still leaves
you at square one !

>
> > Closing thought - if you did try deleting from the project for say one
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald

I forget the actual sequencing, because it's only rarely I've had to do it. Now
my memory may not be correct on this one, but assume you remove the alphabetical
source from the right pane - I think because it is in the left-hand tree you are
going to get some sort of error message. The left-hand tree is what the
application is being built from..

However, if you add or 'put back' a source in the right-hand pane it should
AUTOMATICALLY get added to the left-hand tree. If you add back into the right
pane for something which is *still* in the left-hand tree - not quite sure what
happens.

I'm assuming for your problem you are going with the IDE defaults - I just don't
mess around with them. Just in case, from the IDE Menu Bar check out Animate
settings and the Pull-down Project Menu, the various sub-menu items,  and see if
you are set to defaults. Another thought, are you switching to and fro between
Debug and Release modes - or are you currently solely in Debug mode for testing
with the Animator ? Conceivably, although it shouldn't, but to-ing and fro-ing
*might* just create a problem.

Somebody did raise a similar query to yours recently on Answer Exchanges (for V
3.1), and M/F admitted there were some problems when using OO (which is what I
guess you are doing) - but what the problem was/is - I'm not sure. Other than
very infrequent "obscure" problems which I mentioned having, I don't have
problems with OO-ing - certainly not a whole application going belly up !.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress problem

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T01:45:47-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Q1idnboCPs-0ciSjXTWcqQ@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<6WTsa.10296$Jy3.1069100@news20.bellglobal.com> <3EB42714.4290CD1D@shaw.ca> <D9uta.1454$ER4.180393@news20.bellglobal.com> <3EB6E543.7B1A8B96@shaw.ca>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3EB6E543.7B1A8B96@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> One other possibility - depending upon how many separate source programs
there
> are, not copyfiles. A bit extreme - completely remove project, set up a
new
> project and add your source back in - no need to do this for copyfiles as
their
> inclusion is triggered by adding the appropriate source program to the
project.
> Then do a complete Rebuild.


    This is a known problem with NetExpress.  Sometimes
the APP files become corrupted, and their is no
way to correct them, short the above.

    If you keep the source files in a dir by themselves,
you can recreate an APP fairly easily by selecting
ALL, and adding them all at one time.

    But this does nothing for all of the customizations
you may have done (ie this one is an INT, this one is a GNT, etc etc).

    A "rebuild" would be nice.


>
> Closing thought - if you did try deleting from the project for say one
program -
> remove the source from both the left and right panes in the IDE.
Subsequently of
> course, re-adding to the project puts it in the left tree pane and
> alphabetically in the right Listbox pane.
>
> Jimmy
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress problem

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T09:54:50-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<6WTsa.10296$Jy3.1069100@news20.bellglobal.com> <3EB42714.4290CD1D@shaw.ca> <D9uta.1454$ER4.180393@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald:

Have you tried deleting the entire "DEBUG" directory for the project in
question? If not, you might try that...

IMO, one of the major problems with NetExpress is the IDE getting screwed up
with dependencies...I have some projects similar to yours where they will
not rebuild if a dependent copybook changes, etc...I have others that
rebuild everything in the project even thou nothing changes...I have
projects with non-existing members listed as part of the project on the
right hand side of the IDE...I can go on, but you get my point...

Recreating major production projects because the IDE has problems is
certainly no fun...

kenmullins
newnan ga USA
```

<a id="t-fc8787ee51"></a>
## Thread: Access VSAM stats within a COBOL module — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Access VSAM stats within a COBOL module

- **From:** studlow1@hotmail.com (The Mean Farmer)
- **Date:** 2003-05-05T09:14:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e0465864.0305050814.539f177b@posting.google.com>`

```
Is there a way to read the attributes of a VSAM file in a COBOL
program and utilize those statistics??

I have a program that runs in 3 sepearte batch cycles. In 1 of them
this file carries about 1.5 million records, in the other 2 cycles it
has only one HIGH-VALUES record in it.

The program accesses the file Randomly and the other inputs are sorted
prior to execution.

I am thinking that if I can get a read on how many records are in the
file, and if it is 1 record, then I can verify that it is a
HIGH-VALUES record and not make the and IO call 950,000 times in the
program if it is.

I am also looking at reading the file sequentially and using TPS logic
on it to find EOF and then shut off the READ.

Any other ideas on how to verify this? I would love to get rid of
950,000 IO calls to a virtually empty file.
```

#### ↳ Re: Access VSAM stats within a COBOL module

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-05T19:22:14+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fp3dbv4584nh60a53qt7g33i4d9muuv79l@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0305050814.539f177b@posting.google.com>`

```
On 5 May 2003 09:14:04 -0700 studlow1@hotmail.com (The Mean Farmer) wrote:

:>Is there a way to read the attributes of a VSAM file in a COBOL
:>program and utilize those statistics??

:>I have a program that runs in 3 sepearte batch cycles. In 1 of them
:>this file carries about 1.5 million records, in the other 2 cycles it
:>has only one HIGH-VALUES record in it.

:>The program accesses the file Randomly and the other inputs are sorted
:>prior to execution.

:>I am thinking that if I can get a read on how many records are in the
:>file, and if it is 1 record, then I can verify that it is a
:>HIGH-VALUES record and not make the and IO call 950,000 times in the
:>program if it is.

:>I am also looking at reading the file sequentially and using TPS logic
:>on it to find EOF and then shut off the READ.

Or you can read it sequentially and see if the first record is the high values
record. That will give you the answer using simple COBOL.

:>Any other ideas on how to verify this? I would love to get rid of
:>950,000 IO calls to a virtually empty file.
```

#### ↳ Re: Access VSAM stats within a COBOL module

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-05T12:50:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vbd5k8f9r2f287@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0305050814.539f177b@posting.google.com>`

```

The Mean Farmer <studlow1@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:e0465864.0305050814.539f177b@posting.google.com...
> Is there a way to read the attributes of a VSAM file in a COBOL
> program and utilize those statistics??
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 950,000 IO calls to a virtually empty file.

If the file is made OPTIONAL then, where it is to contain only
a HIGH-VALUES record, do not make the file available and the
OPEN will show file status '05'. If file status is '05', don't read
the file; just indicate the record was not found, or whatever.

This, I think, is a standard COBOL solution and I am not
certain as to what changes may be required in JCL.
```

#### ↳ Re: Access VSAM stats within a COBOL module

- **From:** Gerry Wolfe <gerry.wolfe@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-05T16:10:59-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<q5hdbv0chsb7113198kinh6fp7s7ubvr14@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0305050814.539f177b@posting.google.com>`

```
An alternative method would be to include an IDCAMS REPRO step with
COUNT=2 to a DUMMY output file... if there is only 1 record present,
then the IDCAMS step will return CC=0004 which could be tested for to
bypass or execute a subsequent step.

rgds, g

On 5 May 2003 09:14:04 -0700, studlow1@hotmail.com (The Mean Farmer)
wrote:

>Is there a way to read the attributes of a VSAM file in a COBOL
>program and utilize those statistics??
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>950,000 IO calls to a virtually empty file.
```

#### ↳ Re: Access VSAM stats within a COBOL module

- **From:** David_G_Clark@brown.edu (David_G_Clark)
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T11:33:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9aqtg$cri$1@saturn.services.brown.edu>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0305050814.539f177b@posting.google.com>`

```

	If you're on MVS then check out Batch LSR.  This would speed up the 
processing even when the file has real records in it.   The reads would still 
be done,  but the physical IO would be eliminated for the case of one high 
value record.  Only the JCL would need to be changed once the subsystem for it 
is activated.   


In article <e0465864.0305050814.539f177b@posting.google.com>, 
studlow1@hotmail.com says...
>
>Is there a way to read the attributes of a VSAM file in a COBOL
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>950,000 IO calls to a virtually empty file.
```

<a id="t-d1a3d5861d"></a>
## Thread: Calendar Program — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Calendar Program

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T15:52:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b997b1$28k$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Someone asked for a "Calendar generator" relatively recently in this NG.  I
remembered that the Report Writer "product" that is available for several
compilers (and which includes both extensions to Standard RW - and features
from the 2002 Standard) had such a program.  I have now received permission
to post this code to the NG.  I hope that whoever asked the original
question is still watching.  (I can't find the old post).  Please feel free
to use or pass this code on.  For information on the RW product that uses
this as their IVP (Installation Verification Program) see:

   http://www.spc-systems.com/

        ***

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  CXRIVP01.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER.  IBM-370.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER.  IBM-370.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO UT-S-CALENDAR.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  PRINT-FILE
           LABEL RECORDS OMITTED
           RECORDING MODE IS F
           REPORT IS CALENDAR.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  WORKING-LOCATIONS.
           05  TODAYS-DATE.
             07  YEAR-NO            PIC 99.
             07  FILLER             PIC X(4).
           05  START-DAY            PIC 99 COMP.
           05  MONTH-NO             PIC 99 COMP.
           05  DAY-COUNT            PIC 99 COMP.
           05  LEAP                 PIC 9  COMP.
           05  TEMP                 PIC S9(9) COMP.
           05  MONTH-NAME-TABLE.
             07  FILLER             PIC X(54)  VALUE
           " JANUARY  FEBRUARY   MARCH    APRIL      MAY     JUNE".
             07  FILLER             PIC X(54)  VALUE
           "   JULY    AUGUST SEPTEMBER OCTOBER  NOVEMBER DECEMBER".
           05  FILLER REDEFINES MONTH-NAME-TABLE.
             07  MONTH-NAME         PIC X(9)  OCCURS 12.
           05  MONTH-DAYS-TABLE     VALUE "312831303130313130313031".
             07  DAYS-IN-MONTH      PIC 99  OCCURS 12.
           05  DAY-TABLE.
             07  DAY-NUM            PIC 99 COMP   OCCURS 42.
       REPORT SECTION.
       RD  CALENDAR
           PAGE LIMIT 60    FIRST DE 7.
       01  TYPE PH  LINE 2.
           05  COL 50           VALUE "CALENDAR FOR".
           05  COL + 2          VALUE "19"  WHEN YEAR-NO > 50
                                      "20"  WHEN OTHER.
           05  COL 65           PIC 99    SOURCE YEAR-NO.
       01  MONTH-BLOCK TYPE DE  REPEATED 4 TIMES EVERY 30 COLUMNS.
         03  LINE + 4  COL 6    PIC X(10)   MONTH-NAME (MONTH-NO).
         03  LINE + 2  COL 1    VALUE "  S  M  T  W  TH F  S ".
         03  LINE OCCURS 6      VARYING SUNDAY FROM 1 BY 7.
           05  COL 2  OCCURS 7  STEP 3  VARYING DAYNO FROM SUNDAY
                      PIC Z9    DAY-NUM (DAYNO)   BLANK WHEN ZERO.
       01  TYPE PF.
         03  LINE.
           05  COL 35           "PRODUCED USING COBOL REPORT WRITER ON".
           05  COL + 2          PIC <X(9)B<99B9999   FUNCTION MDATE.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       CALENDAR-START.
           ACCEPT TODAYS-DATE FROM DATE
           DIVIDE 4 INTO YEAR-NO GIVING TEMP REMAINDER LEAP
           COMPUTE TEMP = (1461 * YEAR-NO) / 4
           IF   LEAP = 0 MOVE 29 TO DAYS-IN-MONTH (2)
           ELSE ADD 1 TO TEMP.
           DIVIDE 7 INTO TEMP GIVING TEMP REMAINDER START-DAY
           OPEN OUTPUT PRINT-FILE
           INITIATE CALENDAR
           PERFORM PRINT-MONTH VARYING MONTH-NO FROM 1 BY 1
           UNTIL MONTH-NO > 12
           TERMINATE CALENDAR
           CLOSE PRINT-FILE
           STOP RUN.
       PRINT-MONTH.
           MOVE LOW-VALUES TO DAY-TABLE.
           PERFORM STORE-DAY VARYING DAY-COUNT FROM 1 BY 1
           UNTIL DAY-COUNT > DAYS-IN-MONTH (MONTH-NO)
           GENERATE MONTH-BLOCK
           DIVIDE 7 INTO START-DAY GIVING TEMP REMAINDER START-DAY.
       STORE-DAY.
           ADD 1 TO START-DAY
           MOVE DAY-COUNT TO DAY-NUM (START-DAY).
```

---

<a id="t-9570d28feb"></a>
## Thread: Calendar Program — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Re: Calendar Program

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T16:04:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b99808$gql$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Here is a slightly "enhanced" version of the program that I just posted to
the NG.  This version includes comments and uses some additional functions
and extensions.  Again, for additional information on the product that uses
this as an IVP, see:
 http://www.spc-systems.com

            ***

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  CXRIVP01.
      *
      *COBOL REPORT WRITER DEMONSTRATION PROGRAM.
      *THIS PROGRAM PRINTS A CALENDAR FOR THE CURRENT YEAR.
      *
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO CALENDAR.
      *
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  PRINT-FILE
           REPORT IS CALENDAR.
      *
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  WORKING-LOCATIONS.
      *31DEC1899 WAS A SUNDAY. THIS AVOIDS NEEDING TO KNOW BASE DATE
           05  SOME-SUNDAY                    PIC 9(8) VALUE 18991231.
           05  NEW-YEARS-DAY                  PIC 9(8).
           05  NEW-YEARS-DAY-AN REDEFINES NEW-YEARS-DAY.
             07  YEAR-NO                      PIC 9(4).
             07  MONTH-DAY                    PIC X(4).
           05  START-DAY                      PIC 99 COMP.
           05  MONTH-NO                       PIC 99 COMP.
           05  DAY-COUNT                      PIC 99 COMP.
           05  MONTH-NAME-TABLE.
             07  FILLER                       PIC X(54)  VALUE
           " JANUARY  FEBRUARY   MARCH    APRIL      MAY     JUNE".
             07  FILLER                       PIC X(54)  VALUE
           "   JULY    AUGUST SEPTEMBER OCTOBER  NOVEMBER DECEMBER".
           05  FILLER REDEFINES MONTH-NAME-TABLE.
             07  MONTH-NAME                   PIC X(9)  OCCURS 12.
           05  MONTH-DAYS-TABLE     VALUE "312831303130313130313031".
             07  DAYS-IN-MONTH                PIC 99  OCCURS 12.
           05  DAY-TABLE.
             07  DAY-NUM                      PIC 99 COMP   OCCURS 42.
      *
       REPORT SECTION.
       RD  CALENDAR
           PAGE LIMIT 60    FIRST DE 7.
       01  TYPE PH  LINE 2.
           05  COL 50           VALUE "CALENDAR FOR".
           05  COL 63           PIC 9(4)  SOURCE YEAR-NO.
           05  COL + 3          VALUE "(LEAP YEAR)"
                                PRESENT WHEN DAYS-IN-MONTH (2) = 29.
       01  MONTH-BLOCK TYPE DE  REPEATED 4 TIMES EVERY 30 COLUMNS.
         03  LINE + 4  COL 6    PIC X(10)   MONTH-NAME (MONTH-NO).
         03  LINE + 2  COL 1    VALUE "  S  M  T  W  TH F  S ".
         03  LINE OCCURS 6      VARYING SUNDAY FROM 1 BY 7.
           05  COL 2  OCCURS 7  STEP 3  VARYING DAYNO FROM SUNDAY
               PIC Z9  BLANK WHEN ZERO SOURCE DAY-NUM (DAYNO).
       01  TYPE PF.
         03  LINE.
           05  COL 35           "PRINTED USING COBOL REPORT WRITER ON".
           05  COL + 2          PIC <X(9)B<99B9999   FUNCTION MDATE.
      *
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       CALENDAR-START.
           MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO NEW-YEARS-DAY-AN
           MOVE "0101" TO MONTH-DAY
           IF   FUNCTION MOD (YEAR-NO 4) = 0
           AND (FUNCTION MOD (YEAR-NO 100) > 0
                          OR (FUNCTION MOD (YEAR-NO 400) = 0))
                MOVE 29 TO DAYS-IN-MONTH (2).
           COMPUTE START-DAY = FUNCTION MOD
                 (((FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE (NEW-YEARS-DAY)) -
                   (FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE (SOME-SUNDAY))) 7)
           OPEN OUTPUT PRINT-FILE
           INITIATE CALENDAR
           PERFORM PRINT-MONTH VARYING MONTH-NO FROM 1 BY 1
                                 UNTIL MONTH-NO > 12
           TERMINATE CALENDAR
           CLOSE PRINT-FILE
           STOP RUN.
       PRINT-MONTH.
           MOVE LOW-VALUES TO DAY-TABLE
           PERFORM STORE-DAY VARYING DAY-COUNT FROM 1 BY 1
           UNTIL DAY-COUNT > DAYS-IN-MONTH (MONTH-NO)
           GENERATE MONTH-BLOCK
           COMPUTE START-DAY = FUNCTION MOD (START-DAY 7).
       STORE-DAY.
           ADD 1 TO START-DAY
           MOVE DAY-COUNT TO DAY-NUM (START-DAY).
```

---

<a id="t-a2fb228655"></a>
## Thread: Radix Sort a la RW (a New Thread) — 21 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Radix Sort a la RW (a New Thread)

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T23:16:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c31425$684b0bc0$2495f243@chottel>`

```
Robert,

I tried your Radix Sort but received the following output:

radix sort
test failed 003662442790187170712366172910
               003662437202251720399880311638
test failed, count is 000000162
   1.33 secs

Please note that I edited this output to line up the keys to make it easier
to read.

The only changes I made were to remove comments at the end of lines and I
shifted lines to get rid of compiler warnings, plus I used a different
timing method. I desk checked and did not see where any lines were missing
or changed.  Anyway I will post the modified program as a reply to this
post.

Also if I understand you code correctly, the time displayed is the total
time for 10 (repeat-factor = 10) sorts and NOT the average time for one
sort.
```

#### ↳ Re: Radix Sort a la RW (a New Thread)

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T23:18:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c31425$ab5f4980$2495f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c31425$684b0bc0$2495f243@chottel>`

```
Here is the program:

//JOBNAME JOB (ACCT,'ROOM'),'HOTTEL RADIXSRT',MSGCLASS=S,CLASS=K
//PROCLIB JCLLIB ORDER=USERID.DVL.PROC
//        SET TITLE1='PFX.RADIXSRT'
//OUTPUT INCLUDE MEMBER=DESTCH
//*-------------------------------------------------------------------*
//*        COBOL II COMPILE                                           *
//*-------------------------------------------------------------------*
//STEP01   EXEC  COB3CLG,LIBRARY='DVL.SOURCE.LIBMAST',PROG=IEFBR14,
//    OPT='OPTIMIZE(FULL)',LOPT='AMODE=31,RMODE=ANY'
//COMP.SYSIN DD  *
       identification division.
       program-id. RADIX.
       author. Robert Wagner.
      * Testbed for radix-sort.
      * results: Sparc Ultra 10, 450 MHz, 10 x sort
      *
      *    N log(N)      16.75
      *    radix cN+      2.40
      *
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01  test-data GLOBAL.
           05  n                           comp  pic s9(09).
           05  first-n                     comp  pic s9(09).
           05  radix-columns               comp  pic s9(02).
           05  card      occurs 50000.
               10  sort-key.
                   15  key-column occurs 30      pic x(01).
           05  next-ns.
               10  next-n occurs 50000     comp  pic s9(09).
           05  next-n-1s.
               10  next-n-1 occurs 50000   comp pic s9(09).

       01  init-data.
           05  init-n                      comp  pic s9(09).
           05  init-first value 1          comp  pic s9(09).
           05  init-columns value 7        comp  pic s9(02).
           05  init-card occurs 50000.
               10  init-key-1                    pic v9(18).
               10  init-key-2                    pic v9(12).
           05  .
               10  init-next occurs 50000  comp  pic s9(09).
           05  .
               10  init-next-1 occurs 50000 comp pic s9(09).

       01  timer-variables.
           05  repeat-factor value 10      comp  pic s9(09).
           05  previous-key                      pic  x(30).
           05  card-count                  comp  pic s9(09).
           05  start-time                  comp  pic  9(18).
           05  end-time                    comp  pic  9(18).
           05  elapsed-time                      pic  z(04).99.

       procedure division.
           perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 50000
               compute init-key-1 (n) = function random
               compute init-key-2 (n) = function random
               compute init-next (n) = n + 1
               compute init-next-1 (n) = 0
           end-perform
           move zero to init-next (50000)

*******    display 'N log(N) sort'
*******    perform timer-on
*******    perform repeat-factor times
*******        move init-data to test-data
*******        call 'MF-SORT'
*******    end-perform
*******    perform verify-sort
*******    perform timer-off

           display 'radix sort'
           perform timer-on
           perform repeat-factor times
               move init-data to test-data
               call 'RADIX-SORT'
           end-perform
           perform verify-sort
           perform timer-off

           goback
           .
       verify-sort.
           move zero to previous-key, card-count
           move first-n to n
           if  n equal to zero
               display 'test failed, no first'
           end-if
           perform until n equal to zero
               if  sort-key (n) less than previous-key
                   display 'test failed '
                       previous-key space sort-key (n)
                   move zero to n
               else
                   move sort-key (n) to previous-key
                   move next-n (n) to n
                   add 1 to card-count
               end-if
           end-perform
           if  card-count not equal to 50000
               display 'test failed, count is ' card-count
           end-if
           .
        timer-on.
           call 'TASKTIME' using start-time
           .
        timer-off.
           call 'TASKTIME' using end-time
           compute elapsed-time = (end-time - start-time) / 1000000
           display  elapsed-time ' secs'
           .

*******identification division.
*******program-id. MF-SORT.
******* Performance of a typical N log(N) sort.
*******procedure division.
*******
******* if you do not have MF, call qsort() or other
*******  sort card on ascending sort-key
*******
*******  perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 50000
*******      compute next-n (n) = n + 1
*******  end-perform
*******  move zero to next-n (50000)
*******  goback.
*******end program MF-SORT.

       identification division.
       program-id. RADIX-SORT.
      * Demo radix sort
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01  radix-variables.
           05  c                         comp   pic s9(02).
      * reverse this if your machine is small-endian
           05  pocket                    comp   pic s9(04).
           05       redefines pocket.
               10                               pic  x(01).
               10  pocket-column                pic  x(01).
           05  swap-count                comp   pic s9(09).
           05  last-n                    comp   pic s9(09).
           05  previous-n-0              comp   pic s9(09).
           05  previous-n-1              comp   pic s9(09).
           05  previous-n-2              comp   pic s9(09).
           05  previous-card.
               10  previous-key                 pic  x(30).
           05  radix-pockets.
               10  radix-pocket occurs 256.
                   15  first-in-pocket   comp   pic s9(09).
                   15  last-in-pocket    comp   pic s9(09).

       procedure division.
      *  one iteration per column. Speed is cN
           perform varying c from radix-columns by -1 until c < 1
      *  RUN through the input string, tossing entries into pockets
               move low-values to radix-pockets
               move zero to pocket
               move first-n to n
               perform until n equal to zero
      *   determine pocket
                   move key-column (n, c) to pocket-column
                   add 1 to pocket
                   if  first-in-pocket (pocket) equal to zero
                       move n to first-in-pocket (pocket)
                   end-if
                   move last-in-pocket (pocket) to last-n
      *   link to previous last in pocket
                   if  last-n not equal to zero
                       move n to next-n-1 (last-n)
                   end-if
      *   make this last in pocket
                   move n to last-in-pocket (pocket)
                   move next-n (n) to n
               end-perform
      *  GATHER - link tail of each pocket to head of next
               move zero to first-n, last-n
               perform varying pocket from 1 by 1 until pocket > 256
                   move first-in-pocket (pocket) to n
                   if  n not equal to zero
                       if  first-n equal to zero
                           move n to first-n
                       end-if
                       if  last-n not equal to zero
                           move n to next-n-1 (last-n)                  ast
                       end-if
                       move last-in-pocket (pocket) to last-n
                   end-if
               end-perform
               move zero to next-n-1 (last-n)
      *  MOUNT - input is now in the new order
               move next-n-1s to next-ns
           end-perform

      *  If radix-columns = 30, the above is all you need.
      *  By itself, it runs faster than the MF sort when radix-columns <
18.
      *  The following is optimization. It permits radix-columns
      *  to be set less than 30 for speed. It then merges out of order
      *  cards into place. It almost always works in two iterations,
      *  will run once if the above radix put entries into perfect
sequence.
      *  On the test data, this happens with radix-columns is 7 or more.
      *
      *  MERGE out of order cards
      *  This is a modified insertion sort which does not
      *  require backward links. When finding a step down, it searches
      *  forward looking for a step up, then exchanges the big record
before
      *  the step down with the little record before the step up, then
takes
      *  a step backward. It cannot work in one pass because the step back
      *  cannot find (n - 2) when there are two consecutive steps back.
      *  I don't think this is a documented algorithm; I just made it up.
      *
*******  move 1 to swap-count
*******  perform until swap-count = zero
*******      move zero to previous-key, previous-n-0, previous-n-1
*******      move zero to swap-count
*******      move first-n to n
*******      perform until n equal to zero
*******          if  sort-key (n) less than previous-key
*******              perform until n equal to zero or
*******                  sort-key (n) not less than previous-key
*******                  move n to previous-n-2
*******                  move next-n (n) to n
*******              end-perform
      *  exchange two cards
*******  call 'cbl_xor' using card (previous-n-1) card (previous-n-2)
*******       by value 30
*******  call 'cbl_xor' using card (previous-n-2) card (previous-n-1)
*******       by value 30
*******  call 'cbl_xor' using card (previous-n-1) card (previous-n-2)
*******       by value 30
      *  alternative to the above 3 lines, if you don't like xor
      *              move card (previous-n-1) to previous-card
      *              move card (previous-n-2) to card (previous-n-1)
      *              move previous-card to card (previous-n-2)
*******              move previous-n-0 to n
*******              if  n equal to zero
*******                  move previous-n-1 to first-n, n
*******              end-if
*******              add 1 to swap-count
*******          end-if
*******          move previous-n-1 to previous-n-0
*******          move n to previous-n-1
*******          move sort-key (n) to previous-key
*******          move next-n (n) to n
*******      end-perform
*******  end-perform
*******
           goback.
       end program RADIX-SORT.
       end program RADIX.
//LKED.SYSLIB  DD
//             DD
//             DD DSN=HLQ.DVL.BATLOAD,DISP=SHR
//             DD DSN=USERID.TOOLS.LOAD,DISP=SHR
//LKED.SYSLMOD DD DSN=&&LOADLIB(RADIXSRT),DISP=(OLD,PASS)
//LKED.SYSIN DD *
  NAME RADIXSRT(R)
//GO.SYSUDUMP DD SYSOUT=*
//GO.SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*
//GO.SYSOUT   DD SYSOUT=*
//GO.SYSDBOUT DD SYSOUT=*

```

>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>post.

It failed because you commented out the insertion sort. To test it without the
insertion sort, change init-columns to 30. It will not run as fast, but it
should work.

With the random numbers I got from MF, the radix sort did the whole job when
sorted on 7 positions. Evidently that's not the case with the random numbers you
got from IBM. 

>Also if I understand you code correctly, the time displayed is the total
>time for 10 (repeat-factor = 10) sorts and NOT the average time for one
>sort.

Correct. It might be better to divide the elapsed time by 10 and change he edit
picture to show three digits (or more) to the right of the decimal.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix Sort a la RW (a New Thread)

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T22:15:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c314e6$06292820$4accf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c31425$684b0bc0$2495f243@chottel> <3eb8ee7f.48081818@news.optonline.net> <01c3148e$0019aca0$9cccf943@chottel>`

```
I finally got the Radix sort to work and compared it to two other sorts. 
One was a recursive Quicksort which is a slightly modified version of
JAZQSORT that Joe Z. posted here recently. I changed it to work with a 30
character key.  The other is Leif Svalgaard's optimized version of
Quicksort from the CobolReport web site. The times below are the total
times for 10 sorts of 50,000 records with 30 byte keys:

RW's Radix Sort:  1.27 secs
JAZQSORT:         4.58 secs
Leif's Quicksort:   1.03 secs

All are pretty decent results but the difference is much smaller than
Robert claimed.

It occurred to me while doing this that reference modification would be a
good way to pass parameters to a generalized sort to specify where the sort
keys are located.

<snip>
```

Ouch!  That recursion really does make a difference.

In my defense JAZQSORT was written for clarity and had no
optimizations at all.  I just took a look at Leif's psuedo-recursive
one and it had a few clarity issues, though it obviously beat the
pants off of my raw quicksort.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix Sort a la RW (a New Thread)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T22:49:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c318d8$80a67120$d5caf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c31425$684b0bc0$2495f243@chottel> <3eb8ee7f.48081818@news.optonline.net> <01c3148e$0019aca0$9cccf943@chottel> <01c314e6$06292820$4accf943@chottel>`

```
Here are more comprehensive test results of RW's radix sort. The previously
posted results were for the radix-columns default value of 7. The following
results are for 2 to 30.  As far as I can tell setting radix-coulumns to
one gives an excellent simulation of an infinite loop.

radix sort
radix-columns = 02,    88.04 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 03,     6.81 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 04,     1.79 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 05,     1.33 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 06,     1.18 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 07,     1.62 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 08,     1.70 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 09,     1.82 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 10,     2.41 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 11,     2.60 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 12,     2.13 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 13,     2.56 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 14,     2.62 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 15,     3.38 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 16,     3.03 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 17,     3.50 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 18,     3.17 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 19,     3.82 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 20,     3.72 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 21,     3.86 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 22,     4.10 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 23,     4.97 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 24,     4.53 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 25,     4.46 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 26,     4.33 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 27,     4.82 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 28,     5.16 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 29,     4.96 secs (for 000000010 sorts)
radix-columns = 30,     5.34 secs (for 000000010 sorts)


Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in article
<01c314e6$06292820$4accf943@chottel>...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix Sort a la RW (a New Thread)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T19:50:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305121850.d182006@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<01c31425$684b0bc0$2495f243@chottel> <3eb8ee7f.48081818@news.optonline.net> <01c3148e$0019aca0$9cccf943@chottel> <01c314e6$06292820$4accf943@chottel> <01c318d8$80a67120$d5caf943@chottel>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote 

> radix-columns = 30,     5.34 secs (for 000000010 sorts)

> > Leif's Quicksort:   1.03 secs

Are these equivalent timings ?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix Sort a la RW (a New Thread)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T03:07:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c318fc$9fd57ea0$a18bf243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c31425$684b0bc0$2495f243@chottel> <3eb8ee7f.48081818@news.optonline.net> <01c3148e$0019aca0$9cccf943@chottel> <01c314e6$06292820$4accf943@chottel> <01c318d8$80a67120$d5caf943@chottel> <217e491a.0305121850.d182006@posting.google.com>`

```
Depends what you mean by equivalent. When radix columns is set to 30 then
the follow up insertion sort is not needed (according to RW's comments).
However Leif's quicksort also switches to an insertion sort as a speed up
when the partitions become small.  All of the timings posted are equivalent
in the sense that they measure the time to sort 50,000  30 byte records  10
times.

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in article
<217e491a.0305121850.d182006@posting.google.com>...
> "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote 
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix Sort a la RW (a New Thread)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T03:17:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c318fe$034ddc60$a18bf243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c31425$684b0bc0$2495f243@chottel> <3eb8ee7f.48081818@news.optonline.net> <01c3148e$0019aca0$9cccf943@chottel> <01c314e6$06292820$4accf943@chottel> <01c318d8$80a67120$d5caf943@chottel> <217e491a.0305121850.d182006@posting.google.com> <01c318fc$9fd57ea0$a18bf243@chottel>`

```
In case I was not completely clear.  If I understand RW's algorithms, the
radix columns controls the "initial shuffle" and the insertion sort finishs
off the job. In every case that was posted, in the end,  the records are
sorted into order on all 30 bytes.

Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in article
<01c318fc$9fd57ea0$a18bf243@chottel>...
> Depends what you mean by equivalent. When radix columns is set to 30 then
> the follow up insertion sort is not needed (according to RW's comments).
> However Leif's quicksort also switches to an insertion sort as a speed up
> when the partitions become small.  All of the timings posted are
equivalent
> in the sense that they measure the time to sort 50,000  30 byte records 
10
> times.
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix Sort a la RW (a New Thread)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T03:44:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c31901$b3ab1020$0693f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c31425$684b0bc0$2495f243@chottel> <3eb8ee7f.48081818@news.optonline.net> <01c3148e$0019aca0$9cccf943@chottel> <01c314e6$06292820$4accf943@chottel> <01c318d8$80a67120$d5caf943@chottel> <217e491a.0305121850.d182006@posting.google.com> <01c318fc$9fd57ea0$a18bf243@chottel> <01c318fe$034ddc60$a18bf243@chottel>`

```
In another previous thread when I posted timing results, a number of people
wrote to list all of the things that affect these timings and basically
threw up their hands saying they were not worthwhile. To be fair I guess
that is my interpretation of their remarks.

For example if you look at my original post and my more recent one, you
will find two different results for the case where radix columns = 7. One
was 1.27 secs and the other was 1.62 seconds. This clearly shows variable
results. For those interested in a different approach which takes this into
consideration I would recommend the following article:

"An Elementary C Cost Model" by Bentley, Kernighan, and Van Wyk. UNIX
Review Vol. 9 No. 2.

The subtitle is "You may not know the exact cost of a gallon of gas, but
you can get close enough to not have to wash dishes at a service station."

Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in article
<01c318fe$034ddc60$a18bf243@chottel>...

finishs

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

We expect such inconsistencies in timing tests. They're not a fatal or even a
serious flaw. Perhaps another job started while your test was running. When in
doubt, I run the test several times and use the LOWEST (not average) duration. 


>
>Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in article
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix Sort a la RW (a New Thread)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T02:46:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec17a77.61864273@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<01c31425$684b0bc0$2495f243@chottel> <217e491a.0305121850.d182006@posting.google.com> <01c318fc$9fd57ea0$a18bf243@chottel> <01c318fe$034ddc60$a18bf243@chottel> <b9q0s7$uqo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Charles Hottel wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> > > > Leif's Quicksort:   1.03 secs

I did not claim radix-columns = 30 is optimal. A fair comparison would be
against:

radix-columns = 06,     1.18 secs (for 000000010 sorts)

With a little tuning, I'm confident that could be reduced by a factor of 5-8.
These results are not measuring the algorithm so much as the generated code. You
can be sure Leif's code was tuned. 

My 23:1 was comparing assembly language to C-generated code. A ratio between 2:1
and 3:1 is common in such comparisons. Thus 23:1 translates to 9:1 when both are
written in a HLL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Radix Sort a la RW (a New Thread)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T02:46:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec17134.59492232@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<01c31425$684b0bc0$2495f243@chottel> <3eb8ee7f.48081818@news.optonline.net> <01c3148e$0019aca0$9cccf943@chottel> <01c314e6$06292820$4accf943@chottel> <01c318d8$80a67120$d5caf943@chottel>`

```
It appears you got the insertion sort working and the times you posted include
it. If not, the radix times would be monotonically ascending. If true, the
optimum radix span is 6. 

Why isn't it faster than Lief's Quicksort? To answer that we'd have to look at
generated code. Perhaps use of subscripts causes time-consuming calls to library
functions. That's what I found with Micro Focus running on a RISC Solaris. Using
indexes or pointers might solve the problem, as it did on Solaris. Was bounds
checking turned off in both cases? 

A generalized radix-insertion sort would try various radix column factors, as
you did, on a small sample .. perhaps 100 items, then use the fastest for the
larger population. 


"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote:

>Here are more comprehensive test results of RW's radix sort. The previously
>posted results were for the radix-columns default value of 7. The following
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
```

<a id="t-80769d203a"></a>
## Thread: Low level variables hold strange values — 6 msgs

### Low level variables hold strange values

- **From:** vze33wre@verizon.net (Mason)
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T20:53:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<549cd16b.0305061953.6ab02487@posting.google.com>`

```
I am using NetExpress as my interface, trying to get a fairly simple
program to work correctly.  One of the problems I have is with the
following code:

01 STUDENT-RECORD.
    05 SR-RCD                   PIC 9(2).
    05 FILLER                   PIC X(3)  VALUE SPACES.
    05 SR-STUDENT-NAME.
        10 SR-FIRST-LETTER      PIC X(1).
        10 SR-NAME-REMAIN       PIC X(19).
    05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)  VALUE SPACES.
    05 SR-CORR-ANS              PIC 9(3).
    05 FILLER                   PIC X(11) VALUE SPACES.
    05 SR-CTRL-NUMBER.
        10 SR-CTRL-NON-CHECK.
            15 SR-CTRL-1        PIC 9(1).
            15 SR-CTRL-2        PIC 9(1).
            15 SR-CTRL-3        PIC 9(1).
            15 SR-CTRL-4        PIC 9(1).
            15 SR-CTRL-5        PIC 9(1).
        10 SR-CTRL-CHECK        PIC X(1).
    05 FILLER                   PIC X(32) VALUE SPACES.
           
I show the whole variable for completeness sake, but I'm having a
problem with the level 15 variables.  In my test case, SR-CTRL-NUMBER
is equal to 654321, SR-CTRL-NON-CHECK is equal to 65432, SR-CTRL-1 is
equal to 6, and SR-CTRL-CHECK is equal to 1, all of which is correct. 
However, none of the other SR-CTRL-# numbers have the correct value. 
They have the following values:

2 = >
3 = 9
4 = 0
5 = 5

I'm having problems with other variables in the program as well.  Has
anyone seen or heard of something like this?  I'm fairly new to the
language, so I could be missing something relatively obvious.  Oh, I'm
using the compiler's run-time value displaying ability to look at the
variable's values, in case that makes any difference.

Thanks for any pointers.

Mason
```

#### ↳ Re: Low level variables hold strange values

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T04:23:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EB8863E.306A9D14@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<549cd16b.0305061953.6ab02487@posting.google.com>`

```


Mason wrote:

> I am using NetExpress as my interface, trying to get a fairly simple
> program to work correctly.  One of the problems I have is with the
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mason

As per many beginners you have fed us the bit "you think we need" - my
suspicion is in your procedural code where you MOVE.

Now there is an easy way to resolve this - use the Animator in Step mode,
line by line, do queries on input or moveable fields and the results you
get in you student record. Follow the displays in the Animator - and I've
no doubt you will probably resolve it yourself.

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Low level variables hold strange values

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-06T23:30:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9a266$2al$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<549cd16b.0305061953.6ab02487@posting.google.com> <3EB8863E.306A9D14@shaw.ca>`

```
My guess is that the problem is that you have a line sequential input file,
but have allowed the Select/Assign to default to "record sequential".  Try
putting  "Organization is Line Sequential" in your input file's
Select/Assign clause and see if that fixes your problem.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Low level variables hold strange values

- **From:** vze33wre@verizon.net (Mason)
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T10:45:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<549cd16b.0305070945.3751e8f8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<549cd16b.0305061953.6ab02487@posting.google.com> <3EB8863E.306A9D14@shaw.ca> <b9a266$2al$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Mr. Klein, you hit the nail right on the head.  Thanks!

Mason

"Richard Maher" <maher_rj@hotspamnotmail.com> wrote in message news:<b7bvm4$7oj$1@titan.btinternet.com>...
> Hi,
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Regards Richard Maher

Depending on what you are trying to do, you may want to look at

  USAGE POINTER
  USAGE POINTER-64

http://h71000.www7.hp.com/doc/73final/6296/6296_profile_015.html#index_x_446

USAGE POINTER is 32 bits on OpenVMS Alpha and OpenVMS VAX and 64 bits
on Tru64 UNIX. USAGE POINTER-64 is 64 bits on OpenVMS Alpha.

- Don

<a id="t-654ab61554"></a>
## Thread: Theory of Systems — 2 msgs

### Theory of Systems

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-07T19:03:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NVadnZuFKPRxAySjXTWcpA@giganews.com>`

```
Just laid hands on another copy of "Systemantics: How Systems Work and
Especially How They Fail," which, when I first read it many years ago
encouraged me to become an asparagus farmer.

In summary, here are the "Rules" (like for dating) thoroughly explained in
this slim tome:

1. Systems in General work poorly or not at all.
   a. Nothing complicated works.
   b. Complicated systems seldom exceed 5% efficiency.
2. New systems generate new problems.
   a. Systems should not be unnecessarily multiplied.
3. The total amount of anergy (the amount of human energy required to reach
a goal) in the universe is constant.
   a. Systems operate by redistributing anergy into different forms.
4. Systems tend to grow, and as they grow, they encroach.
   a. Systems tend to expand to fill the known universe.
   b. The system itself tends to expand at 5-6% per year.
5. Systems display antics.
   a. Complicated systems produce unexpected outcomes.
   b. The behavior of a complicated system cannot be predicted.
   c. A large system, produced by expanding a smaller system, does not
behave like the smaller system.
6. Complex systems tend to oppose their own proper function.
  a. Systems get in the way.
   b. The system always kicks back.
   c. Positive feedback is dangerous.
7. People in systems do not do what the system says they are doing.
8. The system itself does not do what it says it is doing.
   a. The function performed by a system is not operationally identical to
function of the same name performed by a human.
9. Fundamental Law of Administrative Working (FLAW):
   a. Things ARE what they are reported to be.
   b. The real world is what is reported to the system.
   c. A system is no better than its sensory organs
   d. To those within a system, outside reality tends to pale and disappear.
10. Systems attract systems people.
11. The bigger the system, the narrower and more specialized the interface
with individuals.
12. A complex system cannot be MADE to work. It either works or it doesn't.

   a. Pushing on the system doesn't help. It just makes things worse.
13. A simple system, designed from scratch, sometimes works.
   a. A simple system may or may not work.
14. Some complex systems actually work.
   a. If a system is working, leave it alone.
15. A complex system that works is invariably found to have evolved from a
simple system that worked.
16. A complex system designed from scratch never works and cannot be patched
up to make it work. You have to start over with a working simple system.
   a. Programs never run the first time. Complex programs never run.
17. Functional Indeterminacy Theorem (FIT): In complex systems, malfunction
and even total nonfunction may not be detectable for long periods, if ever.
18. Large complex systems are beyond the human capacity to evaluate.
19. (Newton's law): A system that performs a certain function or operates in
a certain way will continue to operate in that way regardless of the need or
of changed conditions.
   a. A system continues to do its own thing, regardless of need.
20. Systems develop goals of their own the instant they come into being.
21. Intrasystem goals come first.
22. Complex systems usually operate in failure mode.
23. A complex system can fail in an infinite number of ways.
24. The mode of failure of a complex system cannot ordinarily be predicted
from its structure.
25. The critical variables are discovered by accident.
26. The larger the system, the greater the probability of unexpected
failure.
27. "Success" or "Function" in any system may be failure in the systems to
which the system is connected.
   a. In setting up a new system, tread softly. You may be disturbing
another system that is actually working.
28. When a fail-safe system fails, it fails by failing to fail-safe.
29. Complex systems tend to produce complex responses (not solutions) to
problems.
30. Great advances are not produced by systems designed to produce great
advances.
31. Systems run better when designed to run downhill.
32. Loose systems last longer and work better.
   a. Efficient systems are dangerous to themselves and to others.

Advanced Concepts
1. Everything is a system.
2. Everything is part of a larger system.
```

Pete.

<a id="t-bccf988b5a"></a>
## Thread: Heroin response from Bayer — 12 msgs

### Heroin response from Bayer

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T14:23:14+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eb9bf7e_1@127.0.0.1>`

```
I have received a response from Bayer regarding my questions on their
involvement with heroin.

I should state that, having been involved with this company, it is only fair
that I should declare an interest.

In my experience they are one of the most ethical of the Global Corporations
(sadly we can't say the same for some of the other Pharmaceuticals who won't
even publish their accounts because they know the numbers would provoke
outrage around the World...) and I have been amazed at the responsibility
and goodwill shown by both their Management and Shareholders.

(I recounted elsewhere the incident I witnessed at one of their AGMs in
Cologne where the shareholders voted for foreign workers (in their own
Countries) to be paid the minimum German wage (in some cases a princely sum
by local standards), and the Company had already provided decent canteens
and medical care (without being required to) to these workers.)

Bayer are a good Company. (and I mean the word in every sense we have
discussed <G>).

Here's the response:

=======================  Begin quoted response
=============================
Dear Peter Dashwood,

First let me say thank you for your e-mail and your request regarding
Heroin.

Yes, it is right that the active substance diacetylmorphine was introduced
to the market as a pharmaceutical product in 1898 under the trade name
"Heroin." The substance was welcomed by the medical community due in
particular to its qualities as a pain-reliever and cough remedy.

Over the course of the 20th century, "Heroin" went from being a registered
trademark for the active ingredient diacetylmorphine to a generic term - a
fate suffered by a number of well-known trademarks. Whenever the abuse of
the drug diacetylmorphine came up in the past - and the topic goes back a
long way - people falsely used Bayer's registered trade name rather than the
difficult-to-pronounce chemical name.

Until roughly a half century ago, Bayer made repeated efforts to combat the
improper use of this trade name so as not to be brought into association
with the sale of illegal narcotics - but to no avail. In 1940, Bayer ceased
its production of Heroin, which had been taking place on a limited scale
anyway. The registered trademark has since expired.

In our 100 year celebration of our Chemical Research we published an
overview of our compounds - and Heroin was mentioned. Here in Germany it is
well known that Heroin was produced by Bayer. We also supported for example
a book written by Michael de Ridder "Heroin - from a pharmaceutical compound
to a drug" which was published by Campus. As you could see we are not trying
to hide it.

All the best from Germany,

Christina Sehnert

________________________________________

Bayer HealthCare

Corporate Communications

Bayer AG

Geb. Q 30

51368 Leverkusen

Tel.: +49 214/30-66102

Fax: +49 214/30-71640

E-Mail: christina.sehnert.cs@bayer-ag.de

Internet : http://www.bayer-ag.de

=============================== end quoted response
============================

So, Robert was "right" about heroin ("wrong" about opium <G>). And this is
yet another company that has had its trademark converted into general use.
(In this case a rather unfortunate association...)

(I believe that is the point that the thread was addressing anyway.)

Pete.

```

You're new here, right, Peter <G>?

This Newsgroup shares a common interest in COBOL, but if you spend any time
here you will find that the topics covered are far more diverse than
commercial computer programming...

Normally, it is polite to prefix posts that are NOT about COBOL with the
letters "OT". On this occasion, I forgot <hangs head in shame...>

Frequenting this newsgroup will either expand your horizons or drive you
crazy (or possibly both); any effect on your knowledge of COBOL will be
purely coincidental <G>

Pete.

<a id="t-6b1c0957a0"></a>
## Thread: XML proposal initial input for future COBOL Standard — 30 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### XML proposal initial input for future COBOL Standard

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T06:26:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9df60$b4s$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
(Sent to various NG's)

For those of you interested in COBOL and XML, you might want to see an
"initial" paper proposing full (???) XML support for a future COBOL
Standard.  This paper may be viewed at:

     http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/files/03-0094.doc

Notice particularly the statement,

"This paper is based on a working implementation.

Since WG4 and J4 are evaluating alternative approaches for XML, I have not
identified the pages on which the changes should be made, but left this to
the imagination of the reader.  I am willing to develop this working paper
into a formal proposal if this is the approach that is chosen."

As I am sending a copy of this note to IBM-MAIN, it should be noted that
this is a QUITE different approach than that currently available in
Enterprise COBOL.

I would think that if "you" have input on this (or another) approach to XML
support in "native" COBOL, that J4 and WG4 would be interested in it.  You
can contact the J4 Chair, the WG4 convenor, and the author of this paper
(all of whom are the same person) by emailing:

   Don.Schricker <at> microfocus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: XML proposal initial input for future COBOL Standard

- **From:** joe_zitzelberger@vsam.org (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T10:29:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c99d007f.0305080929.4d0d8bd1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b9df60$b4s$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
This is interesting, and looks rather useful, but there is a huge hole
in it -- it builds internal trees.  SAX exists for a reason --
sometimes you need to parse huge documents.  Trying to fit all things
into a DOM is going to fail in many cases.

I rather like it, but as I routinely use huge files I don't think I
would find it very useful.  Is this the full idea for Cobol and XML,
or is there a SAX-like model that would also be included in a
different proposal?

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<b9df60$b4s$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>...
> (Sent to various NG's)
> 
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>    Don.Schricker <at> microfocus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: XML proposal initial input for future COBOL Standard

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>

```
IBM's XML PARSE only *reads* XML documents and seems rather cumbersome to
me. The extended file-io approach of Micro Focus makes the reading, writing
and manipulation of XML documents easy. BTW the IBM-style XML PARSE will
also be supported so watch this space.

www.microfocus.com

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: XML proposal initial input for future COBOL Standard

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T19:01:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EBBF733.F5378775@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<b9df60$b4s$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <c99d007f.0305080929.4d0d8bd1@posting.google.com> <b9gdd8$24o$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```


Paul Barnett wrote:

> IBM's XML PARSE only *reads* XML documents and seems rather cumbersome to
> me. The extended file-io approach of Micro Focus makes the reading, writing
> and manipulation of XML documents easy. BTW the IBM-style XML PARSE will
> also be supported so watch this space.
>

I picked up on Don Schricker's draft prior to Bill's posting, in the course of
looking for something else. I know nothing of XML, (which I don't currently have
a need for), other than a cursory read of Will Price's second book on Web
Programming.
However, I *did* like Don's overall approach, and of course this will be
'dotted' and "I'd' through J4 discussion.

My only suggestion to Don was his return error or status codes - he had some as
negatives. For 'familiarity' why not group those codes in the same fashion as
file-status.

If COBOL is to survive, and not be seen as a dinosaur by the youngsters, then it
has to play catch-up with new concepts as they arrive. This can't happen as soon
as somebody 'splashes' new technology - we have to wait for maturation to see if
it is going to last.

The problem is, when did XML first surface - i.e., when was it acknowledged as a
generally accepted technology. Having got to the stage, 'This is worth a look',
it's then in two phases, Phase I - J4 discusses then goes concrete on a
proposal, (emphaisis - within a targetted timeframe), and Phase II when do
compiler vendors make it available. Some vendors get *real* courageous and jump
in with an intermediate extension - I can think of two <G>.

As the opposite to the above, there are those who contend, "Nah, nah, nah. Don't
screw around with COBOL. You can call language/software X or Software Y  to do
the same thing". But - the same argument could have been made for (R)DBMS and
SQL - to my mind the ability to include SQL statements gelled into COBOL source,
makes complete sense - the two dovetail beautifully together.

Before Pete explodes on seeing the magic characters "J4", let's see if I can get
him to reflect <G>. (a) J4 is the only game in town, and (b) J4 is not 'Us"
(self appointees) and 'Them" (the compiler vendors). It *IS* the vendors. Just
did a quick check on current membership :-

Status:  October 10, 2002

J4 has presently 9 voting members and 5 advisory members.  The voting members
are:

(Compiler Vendors) - 6

- Fujitsu Software Corporation; Hewlett-Packard Company; IBM Corporation; ITSCJ
(Japan's own 'ISO/J4' and he works for Hitachi); Micro Focus Inc., and Unisys
Corporation;

("Independents") -  3

- EDS, Karlins'Korner; and Victor Consulting.

Changes since last year:

Membership dropped from 11 to 9.  HP bought Compaq. Individual member Stephen
Spiro did not pay his INCITS Service fee, which was too high for him as an
individual to bear. (I sympathize for Stephen - it cost me a real bunch of money
to stand in for him at Newbury in 2000 - air fares at the beginning of the
summer season. And the hotel room, I think I was buying it, not renting the
firiggin thing !).

We know EDS is a big player, but I'm not sure about the other two. However, 3
out of 9 hardly constitutes a bunch of self appointees.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

You have no cause for concern, Jimmy.

I DID reflect on what I think about J4 and nothing happened...

They are as relevant in my life as the RSPB (Royal Society for the
Protection of Birds), or the local UFO Society (meets every month in
Tauranga.)

While I'm sure both of these are worthwhile organisations that provide a
useful service to their members, they don't figure large in MY life...

Guess I moved on...<G>

a member (apart from interest in COBOL and paying the exorbitant fee, or
being sponsored by a Corporation that looks good and gives credibility to
J4? If these things are no longer true then I would have to review my

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: XML proposal initial input for future COBOL Standard

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T19:39:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EBEA310.9331B3B2@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<b9df60$b4s$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <c99d007f.0305080929.4d0d8bd1@posting.google.com> <b9gdd8$24o$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3EBBF733.F5378775@shaw.ca> <3ebd8d77_1@127.0.0.1>`

```


"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:

>
> I DID reflect on what I think about J4 and nothing happened...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Now you've truly got me upset <G>. Being animal/country lovers we belonged to RSPB -
not in the serious sense that we trudged the countryside in 'wellies' poking
binoculars in every direction looking for 'boids'. We had membership, magazines, books
and bird song tapes etc. I even had a tie with their crest, an avocet I think. My son
took a fancy and pinched it from me. (I could digress about the diversity of birds I
see here in Calgary but it's not germane <G>).

>
> While I'm sure both of these are worthwhile organisations that provide a
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Bill, (or Chuck) can flesh out the specifics - not that you are really interested. But
yes a commitment to attend 'n' number of meetings per year, annual fee for membership
and up front money for each meeting. Bloody costly to the small guy. So far as the
compiler vendors are concerned,  pay according to the rules and nominate one of their
staff to attend on their behalf.  Note some are not active participants  - Liant
(RM/COBOL) and Acu for two. They are still around  in an observer 'capacity'.

Assuming there were 20 COBOL compiler vendors, and they wanted to participate - to my
knowledge there's nothing to stop them participating. KOBOL or Tiny COBOL people could
be members, with voting rights, if they wished. Obviously NICTS or ultimately ANSI
have come up with a charter of house rules, but I doubt there is a physical limitation
to membership.

Certainly as a result of something I posted on CONSTANTS at the 01 Level (the only way
to have a table of literals in a method now that Method Working-Storage has been
scotched), I had a query for clarification from one of the Acu people - I spelled it
out, and he personally agreed.

So as not to digress here - see the postscript about COBOL history.

Now the latter three I mentioned, (the independents)  - yes they are corporates
(unlike Bill and Stephen when they participated). So they 'pays yer money' and join.
In that sense you could call them 'self appointees'. EDS I mentioned, but Dennis their
member is selected to represent their company; John Piggott SPC (in Wimbledon)
represents his 'own' company I believe. His particular baby, Report Writer - which
gets you and me terribly excited - but he certainly contributes on other aspects of
the language. The third, Bob Karlin of Kalrlin's Korner - guess he represents himself,
and sometimes Janet ??? (whom you know from NZ), stands in for him when he can't make
it.

I don't think it is in any sense a 'closed shop'. I was amazed when Thane once
commented itemizing the companies. Dramatically reduced from household names that you
and I knew back in the sixties or seventies. Two examples - ICL now part of Fujitsu,
not quite clear on second, but it appears Siemens in Germany is also writing a
compiler with OO, as part of the Fujitsu empire. For F/J - Thane is their front man;
used to be Artur Reiman (Reitman ?).

Let's keep it simple - just concentrating on J4 - both you and I have expressed some
strong criticisms of the J4 organization, not personalities. It really boils down to
any administrative body must on a cyclic basis, have fresh blood. Occurs at various
levels of government, starting at the bottom from village pump up through townships
and city level, all the way to national governments. Thirteen years of Maggie Thatcher
- too long. Ronnie Reagan by law was limited to a max of 8 years. Similarly here in
Canada, much too much governance by the Liberals, (and the last conservative I voted
for was Winston Churchill !), particularly our latest clown Chretien who insists on
hanging in there until 2004. Snag is here there isn't a credible opposition.

I wrote to you privately ages ago, that like the US presidency there should be a time
limit on J4 membership - not necessarily corporations, but the individuals that
represent those corporations. Your response, that would kibosh good people like Bill -
well that's now happened, probably due to a combination of finance and health.
I don't know the longevity of the current J4 team, but at least three are new boys,
Chuck for Unisys,  Thane for Fujitsu, and Dennis  representing EDS.

It's only the newer and younger blood coming in that can take a fresh look and perhaps
revamp the machinery. Take this particular thread - XML - there's no way 9 individuals
can be omnipotent about all aspects of computing, and I doubt that any claim to be.
XML, to my mind would best be tackled by recruiting a small specialized group of XML
users. The precedence for that the - OOCTG back in '93 which gave us OO. As I
responded to Robert - following on from the OOCTG it was J4 that gave the kick-start
to OO - otherwise you and I wouldn't be using it now.

Worth bearing in mind this thread wouldn't have existed if Bill hadn't brought it to
our attention. It's listed there in the J4 site - but unless you continuously go
looking....?  I sensed a feeling of frustration from John Piggott, when some 18 months
ago,  he proposed J4 teleconferencing as an alternative to swanning around the globe
to different locations. There were two replies, both negative, immediately jumping
into all the minuses, without first using his suggestion as a trial balloon.. Seemed
like a bloody good idea to me, particularly as I recall a teleconference a little
while back in London, bringing various Arab groups together - Jeez ! If you can do it
with a bunch of crafty rag heads - anybody should be able to do it !.

Jimmy

*********

Thought you and others might find the following bit of 'history' interesting. The
source is Jerry Garfunkel's 'COBOL 85 Examples' (real neat little paperback for verb
syntax) :-

1. CODASYL 1959 - Secretary of Defense, USAF, US Steel Corp., DuPont Co., Esso
Standard Oil, Nat. Bureau of Standards, USN, Sperry Rand (Grace Hopper), IBM Corp.

2. It was probably from the spec done by their Short Range Committee that you  first
used as COBOL - N.Bureau of Standards, USAF, Honeywell, RCA, Burroughs, USN, Sylvania,
IBM, Sperry Rand

2. CODASYL 1985 - Burroughs, Control Data, Data General, Deloitte Haskins and Sells,
Digital Equip, Gen Services Admin,Honewell Info Sys, IBM, Jerome Garfunkel Associates
, (his brother is the 'Garfunkel' in Simon and Garfunkel), Micro Focus, Nat. Bureau of
Standards, Nat Computing Centre UK (my buddies in Manchester), NCR, Norfolk Southern,
Sperry Corp, Tandem Computers, US Army, USAF, USN, Wang Laboratories

3. ANSI COBOL Committee 85 - Burroughs, Control Data, Digital Equip, DECUS (DEC Users
Society), Hewlett-Packard, Jerome Garfunkel Assoc., Martin Marietta, McDonnell
Douglas, Nashoba Systems, Nat. Bureau of Standards,  NCR Corp, Online Systems Support,
Perkin-Elmer, Ryan-McFarland, Sperry Corp, Travellers Ins. Co., ( ** see below on
Travellers), US Army, USAF, Wang Laboratories

4. ISO COBOL Committee as of 1986 - Austria, Canada, France, Germany, Netherlands, UK,
US

One all prevailing thing in this is that from a US perspective they had to come up
with something acceptable to DofD to obtain military contracts - hence getting the
good housekeeping seal of approval via ANSI..

I highlighted Travellers Insurance above - that's hilarious. Without gong into the
nitty gritty, their head honcho in DP, (but of course the SOB was a bloody-minded
Irishman <G>), strongly objected to changes proposed, which he contended would put him
through massive re-writes. He went on a paper campaign which finished up with a couple
of ballots, plus he threatened to sue each individual member of what we now call J4 -
that made them do a double-take. That is one of the reasons that there is always that
backward compatibility in new features introduced to the language.

The jazz about that secretive mob in Geneva - ISO - Again something Gerry briefly
covers in his book. This is speculative on my part, having read his text. It wasn't
that the Americans were arrogant but naive. Following on from the success of COBOL
under the ANSI umbrella, they got the idea why not take it to the Europeans, (Hey ! It
works in the good 'ole US of A !), and suggest it as an ISO standard. Rather like the
EU is currently contemplating a tit for tat tax of US goods, (things like the US tax
embargo on foreign steel imports), I get the impression they said "Screw you. We will
look at it and do it in our own way", What the actual detail was I don't know, but
Gerry was one of the US presenters, I believe  - and he summarizes that by writing :-

"See Computer World March 1984.........." and concludes, "Some anti-COBOLists
referred to the significant action taken by the Vienna group as the Valentine's Day
Massacre, 1984".

No wonder ISO/WG4 is a pain in the ass !.
--------------
```

Oops! Sorry <G>...no slur on the RPBS was intended (or the UFO society, for
that matter). In fact, faced with being forced to join RPBS or J4 I'd go
with the birds every time...<G>

> >
> > While I'm sure both of these are worthwhile organisations that provide a
> > useful service to their members, they don't figure large in MY life...
> >
<snip>
>
> Bill, (or Chuck) can flesh out the specifics - not that you are really
interested.

(Guys, please don't waste your valuable time responding; Jimmy is right, I'm
not interested...)

It is true that I am no longer interested in what J4 or ANSI or ISO do, as I
believe it is all irrelevant (and sadly, has made COBOL so...), however,
that is just one man's opinion, and I WAS interested in your historical
postscript, Jimmy.

Thanks for that (and, BTW, I received the personal copies of this mail.)

Pete.

<snip of very interesting COBOL background, which shows the steady decline
of the Language once it was handed to standards committees...>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: XML proposal initial input for future COBOL Standard

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T10:28:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-404ED9.10280510052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b9df60$b4s$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <c99d007f.0305080929.4d0d8bd1@posting.google.com> <b9gdd8$24o$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
In article <b9gdd8$24o$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>,
 "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote:

> IBM's XML PARSE only *reads* XML documents and seems rather cumbersome to
> me. The extended file-io approach of Micro Focus makes the reading, writing
> and manipulation of XML documents easy. BTW the IBM-style XML PARSE will
> also be supported so watch this space.

I've not used the XMLParse from Enterprise Cobol (as my shop likes to be 
a few releases behind on the compilers for stability).  But I do use SAX 
all the time -- it makes great sense and is easy to use.

While DOM is great for small documents, it simply cannot handle large 
documents.  SAX excels at both.

For those not familiar, SAX (Simple API for XML) is a event call-back 
model.  The parser is provided with a program (or paragraph in IBMs 
case) that can accept events.  They are simple and limited in number:

  Start Document Event  -  No parms, raised at beginning of doc
 
  End Document Event    -  No parms, raised at end of doc

  Start Element         -  Parms are element name with namespace
                           info and any attributes
  
  End Element           -  Parm is element name

  Character             -  Parm is character data (CDATA,
                           Comments, Whitespace or plain chars).
  
  Processing Instruction-  Parms are target and Data
   
It reads sequentially, firing events as they are recognized, and allows 
for unlimited document sizes to be processed.

While SAX says nothing about writing XML, the methods needed are similar 
to the ones used to read XML.  Although, XML is so simple and the rules 
so well defined that a utility to generate XML is a trivial programming 
task. 

There is a corresponding description for an XML data writer, which I 
jokingly call SOX (Simple Output for XML), available from the creator of 
SAX, David Megginnson.  See www.saxproject.org for more detail.

My other concerns about the DOM model (which I have also never used) 
described in the posted document are two.

The record oriented IO style of READ/WRITE/DELETE/etc and the record 
description seem designed to build very flat, record-like documents.  I 
tried to imagine how to describe an XD record that would process the 
schema for schema and the complexity worried me a bit.  I don't really 
have enough familiarity with this product to make an accurate judgment 
on that, just gut instinct.

The second, and more serious concern, is that the described DOM model 
violates XML standards in several small, but serious ways.  It applies 
the namespace of group items to subordinate items; it allows multiple 
root elements; it ignores PIs outside of root elements; it treats the 
xmlDecl as a processing instruction.  Also, while strictly optional, 
this spec does not mention comments at all.  Are they not supported?  
They can sometimes be quite useful.

All things considered, I think I might like this DOM approach.  But I'm 
not crazy about it being the ONLY thing available.  SAX has great 
utility as well for a different set of problems.  I would like to see 
them both in the next version of Cobol.

Regards,

Joe Zitzelberger
```
