###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: XML proposal initial input for future COBOL Standard

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T22:25:59-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-AB4969.22255910052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b9df60$b4s$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <c99d007f.0305080929.4d0d8bd1@posting.google.com> <b9gdd8$24o$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <joe_zitzelberger-404ED9.10280510052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EBD55C2.33D634F8@shaw.ca>`

```
In article <3EBD55C2.33D634F8@shaw.ca>,
 "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> Now having gone to great lengths to itemize pros and cons - which is 
> interesting
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

I was thinking about writing up some detailed concerns with the original 
paper and sending them to the author.  That is what I might do about the 
suggested DOM/Cobol interface.  And, as I stated, they are mostly minor 
quarks -- overall it looks useful.  

But I don't have MicroFocus and I haven't the time to fully evaluate it, 
I'll leave that to those that have used the MF XML DOM and wish to rave 
or rant about it.

As to the need for SAX-like treatment of XML documents.  Well, I could 
do nothing.  IBM has a SAX/Cobol interface that I know as well as I know 
the MicroFocus one -- that is, by word of documentation only.

I'm sure the international computing giant, IBM, will be able to use 
their seat on the committee to include some variant of their own parser.   
Even without my help.

There are three things that I see that I could do:

   - Mark up the proposal with additions for sequential, SAX-like, 
parsing.

   - Employer permitting, I could write up my own solution for dealing 
with XML documents in a SAX-line manner.

   - I could dream up some third-way of dealing with large XML documents 
in a sequential manner from Cobol.


Assuming I did one of these three I would have to present it in some 
fashion to the J4 committee.  My monthly homeowners association meeting 
leaves me dreaming of claymores and assault rifles for days -- how am I 
supposed to survive an interaction with the famously troublesome Cobol 
standards committee?  

Also, I have no plans to be in Las Vegas for that meeting.  And, if I 
understand my marriage vows correctly, I'm prohibited from entering that 
city unescorted.

Finally, while I really think it would be cool to have XML handling in 
native Cobol, and if it is going to be put in, it ought to be complete.  
Parsing XML is really the sort of thing that is best handled by a 
component (msxml for example).  With object-oriented Cobol soon to be 
universally available the point might well become moot.  

What would you suggest I do?
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T19:56:17+00:00

- **References:** `<b9df60$b4s$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <c99d007f.0305080929.4d0d8bd1@posting.google.com> <b9gdd8$24o$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <joe_zitzelberger-404ED9.10280510052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EBD55C2.33D634F8@shaw.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-AB4969.22255910052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```


Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

>
> I was thinking about writing up some detailed concerns with the original
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> What would you suggest I do?

Excellent for being positive ! Completely forget about any reference to Micro
Focus - it just doesn't enter into the picture - this is for a COBOL standard
not an M/F standard.

As regards XML, this is not like suggesting "Howz about adding this additional
line to the existing EVALUATE syntax ?" You have an idea and it is fairly easy
to follow through. XML is a different baby altogether - a discrete topic all by
itself. Unless you are totally immersed in XML 24/7, perhaps not likely that one
is an 'expert', and even if you are, there's probably another 'expert' who can
put you straight <G>

You have experience of the topic, can see pitfalls, and have ideas for
improvements. Do a paper, but qualify your comments highlighting, for example
that you can't comment on COM or DOM as appropriate. Then just lead through with
your observations on Don's paper, followed by your own suggestions.

Do it something like as follows :-

Subject : xxxxx

Author : Joe and e-mail address

Date :

References : J403-???? - Title of Don's paper - Don Scrhicker

Then go for it. Send it to Don as a submission, he allocates the next sequential
Document Number, and for your sins, you are now in the J4 archives <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: XML proposal initial input for future COBOL Standard

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T15:37:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9mcb1$8fa$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b9df60$b4s$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <c99d007f.0305080929.4d0d8bd1@posting.google.com> <b9gdd8$24o$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <joe_zitzelberger-404ED9.10280510052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EBD55C2.33D634F8@shaw.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-AB4969.22255910052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EBEA704.4B1F0B7@shaw.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-537A95.16122011052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe (and generally posted so others can see),

  Yes there is a COBOL-thing at the end of COBOL Expo June 23-25 when they
plan on talking about "future of COBOL"

HOWEVER, anyone may at ANY TIME submit a paper (as Jimmy lists below) on ANY
topic to J4.  Such a paper will be discussed at J4's next scheduled meeting.

Furthermore, one need not "join" (or commit ANY resources to) J4 in order to
"participate" in developing some feature that they have significant
knowledge or expertise on.   Such participation may be done via email,
electronic documents *OR* by attending (without fee - but paying for
expenses) any or all of a J4 meeting that occurs "near" them or at a
convenient time.

To give examples:

 - Much of the arithmetic enhancements and new functions of the 2002
Standard were developed by a non-J4 member
 - Almost all of the 2002 Report Writer enhancements came from a non-J4
member

It is up to any person with "good ideas" how much they do and how much they
"turn over" to J4 for development.  You can simply outline your ideas (or
problems with an existing proposal) or you may go into detailed "change
"abc" in line 1 on page 234 to "xyz" detailed instructions" - it is UP TO
YOU.
```

Well, if they come to Tauranga, I have a bottle of Jack they can share...Or
tea and biscuits even...<G>


> To give examples:
>
>  - Much of the arithmetic enhancements and new functions of the 2002

Is it fair to say this was an "ex-member"?

Who had no vested interest in people using RW?

> It is up to any person with "good ideas" how much they do and how much
they

Fair enough.

Two questions...

1.In their entire history, have they ever "taken on board" or implemented
ANYTHING that was put forward in this way?
2. Did they ever give any credit to the person(s) concerned?

(the point here is WHY? would anyone contribute if they receive neither
recompense nor recognition? You COULD argue that people should do it for
love of COBOL. But that argument is decidedly shaky as long as large sums
are charged for the documents produced, and the right to membership;
notwithstanding the claim to be a "non-profit" organisation.)

Pete.

- **References:** `<b9df60$b4s$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <c99d007f.0305080929.4d0d8bd1@posting.google.com> <b9gdd8$24o$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <joe_zitzelberger-404ED9.10280510052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EBD55C2.33D634F8@shaw.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-AB4969.22255910052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EBEA704.4B1F0B7@shaw.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-537A95.16122011052003@corp.supernews.com> <b9mcb1$8fa$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3ebefc9a_2@127.0.0.1>`

LX-i wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> > LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message news:<3EBEB767.7020903@Knology.net>...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> I thought you were a proponent of safe SAX...

Witticisms from the USAF <G>. Of course your usage of the word 'safe' in above is correct.
However, I couldn't abide an early road safety slogan up here :-

                Drive safe.

You can (1) carry a safe, (2) wear a safe, (3) even make sure it's safe, but (4) there's no
bloody way you can drive a safe !

Jimmy - ex-RAF apprentice

Post-WW2 - We wuz kids 15 - 17, trained in particular areas, electrical, radio, engine and
airframe mechanics, admin etc.,  anywhere from 15 to 24 months training, before we were
unleashed on the air force, proper. Some of the pre-war mechanics volunteered and were trained
as aircrew, but went back to their trades. When WW2 came, they were immediately drafted into
aircrew, primarily as pilots and navigators.

For Richard and his dad down there in NZ . I was an Admin apprentice, (secretarial plus
shorthand/typing, as opposed to accounting or supplies(equipment)) - we wore a black and white
chequered hat band.
```

## Thread: Convert Fujitsu to DBASE files — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Convert Fujitsu to DBASE files

- **From:** "TeckDesign" <francis@teckdesign.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T12:47:41+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Bvrua.344$R86.457832@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```
I need a tool to convert Fujitsu Cobol files to Dbase.
I know a tool from the SiberSystem, but i  think that it is very expensive.
Who knows another process to convert indexed files in Fujitsu to Dbase.



Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Convert Fujitsu to DBASE files

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T07:12:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CKycnZYahvJh1CejXTWJjw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<Bvrua.344$R86.457832@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```

"TeckDesign" <francis@teckdesign.com> wrote in message
news:Bvrua.344$R86.457832@newsserver.ip.pt...
> I need a tool to convert Fujitsu Cobol files to Dbase.
> I know a tool from the SiberSystem, but i  think that it is very
expensive.
> Who knows another process to convert indexed files in Fujitsu to Dbase.

It shouldn't be too hard to write your own: Dbase files are mostly just a
text file with a single header record at the front defining the file layout.

We have a program to go the other way (Dbase to text) that we've often used
to convert customer files as input to our system.

For that matter, you can read your text file into Excel and have Excel save
the result as a DB2, 3, or 4 output. If your file is a Fujitsu indexed file,
you'll have to squirt it out to a text file first.
```

#### ↳ Re: Convert Fujitsu to DBASE files

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T13:52:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8itua.10075$%_3.5793455@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

> I know a tool from the SiberSystem, but i  think that it is very
expensive.

Start here...general advice on "COBOL-created data for non-COBOL
applications"

http://www.providerpaymentpartner.com/tsihome_html/downloads/C2IEEE.htm


MCM

```

## Thread: Is there anyone who can tell me more aboute TELON and it's relationship whith COBOL — 3 msgs

### Is there anyone who can tell me more aboute TELON and it's relationship whith COBOL

- **From:** "Reda" <Bendraou_Reda@yahoo.fr>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T17:03:59+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9dre3$12fg$1@vishnu.jussieu.fr>`

```
I juste want to now , what exactly, the relationship between a COBOL Program
et TELON? is it the way to provide an GUI to  an application COBOL or What?

thank's in advance for who gives me answer!
```

#### ↳ Re: Is there anyone who can tell me more aboute TELON and it's relationship whith COBOL

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <don.leahy@nospamwhatever.leacom.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T14:56:37-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GLxua.7306$ER4.654303@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<b9dre3$12fg$1@vishnu.jussieu.fr>`

```
"Reda" <Bendraou_Reda@yahoo.fr> wrote in message
news:b9dre3$12fg$1@vishnu.jussieu.fr...
> I juste want to now , what exactly, the relationship between a COBOL
Program
> et TELON? is it the way to provide an GUI to  an application COBOL or
What?
>
> thank's in advance for who gives me answer!
>
>
Telon is a product that generates Cobol or PL/1 code.  In its earliest form
its purpose was to assist in the creation of online (IMS or CICS) screens by
hiding many of the environmental details from the programmer, who was then
free to concentrate on the business logic (well, in theory anyway).   Since
then it has evolved to support other platforms such as AS400, Unix, DOS,
OS/2 and possibly more.  I don't know if it generates GUI screens, the
version I am familiar with is strictly character based.

The vendor is CA, and you can probably find out more on their website.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is there anyone who can tell me more aboute TELON and it's relationship whith COBOL

- **From:** manri02@ca.com (Richard Mann)
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T05:07:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<581bd9fe.0305090407.4734ec4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b9dre3$12fg$1@vishnu.jussieu.fr> <GLxua.7306$ER4.654303@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Don Leahy" <don.leahy@nospamwhatever.leacom.ca> wrote in message news:<GLxua.7306$ER4.654303@news20.bellglobal.com>...
> "Reda" <Bendraou_Reda@yahoo.fr> wrote in message
> news:b9dre3$12fg$1@vishnu.jussieu.fr...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> The vendor is CA, and you can probably find out more on their website.

Mr. Leahy has correctly summarized TELON (the full name these days is
Advantage CA-Telon Application Generator).  It was the market leader
world-wide in the 80's and early 90's for a code generator which
assists in the development of "green-screen" and batch programs for
several IBM mainframe environments, as well as iSeries, UNIX and
Windows platforms.  In addition to generating environmental details
for the programmer, it also allows for high-level specification of
data access for a variety of methodologies, including IMS/DB, DB2,
Datacom/SQL, IDMS/SQL, and VSAM for OS/390, as well as DB2 on iSeries
and Windows, and Oracle and Ingres on UNIX.

As far as GUI is concerned, CA-Telon has supported for several years
an interface with the Jacada product, which allows the
Internet-enabling of existing applications, with no changes to
existing legacy code.  In addition, CA will soon be providing a
similar interface with the Advantage Gen product.

Typical CA-Telon customers are those with very large programming
shops: government agencies, insurance companies, banks, airlines, oil
companies, etc.  These companies have typically built thousands of
programs using Telon, with some shops even today having as many as 600
Telon programmers.

For more information,
http://www3.ca.com/Solutions/ProductFamily.asp?ID=1527
```

## Thread: Net Express question. — 3 msgs

### Net Express question.

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T12:15:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jovua.11058$VJ.631345@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
I am trying to read a tab delimited file under MF NetExpress.  How is the
best way to do it?  I wanted to read it using organization line sequential,
but that changes all my tabs to spaces.  Is there a way to turn this
"feature" off?

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express question.

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T13:27:12-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9e407$f5e$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net>`
- **References:** `<jovua.11058$VJ.631345@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:jovua.11058$VJ.631345@news20.bellglobal.com...
> I am trying to read a tab delimited file under MF NetExpress.  How is the
> best way to do it?  I wanted to read it using organization line
sequential,
> but that changes all my tabs to spaces.  Is there a way to turn this
> "feature" off?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

See the NE help file for the following run time switches...This can also be
controlled on a file by file basis via the call x'91' function which is also
in the help file...

T - Tab Switch
 The tab switch is set off (-T) by default. The tab switch compresses extra
spaces to tab characters (x"09"), for line sequential files. This saves
space in the file. If you write a file with +T set, you must use +T when
reading the file and if you write a file with -T set, you must use -T when
reading the file.

N - Null Switch
 When +N is used (which is the default), all characters less than x"1B" are
preceded by a null byte (x"00"). If -N is used, characters less that x"1B"
are treated as control characters; for example, x"1A" is treated as the
end-of-file marker. If a file is created from an application running
with -N, the file must be read back with an application running the same
way; the same is true for files created from applications running with +N.
```

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

```

"scarface" <scarface@scarface.com> wrote in message
news:b9e5a7$pe3$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg...
> My company are migrating almost everything from their
> BIG irons into Unix. Does it mean that in terms of
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have ported Cobol programs from IBM mainframe to Unix (Sun Systems) with
few problems. The biggest issue was moving to an Oracle database from VSAM
files.

In fact many report programs that read flat files compiled and fuctioned
correctly with
only one or two lines of code having to be changed.

I would suspect that you credit card system is written in Cobol and could be
ported to
a unix platform with similar ease depending on which database is being used
on each end. The
other big issue could be datacom.
```

#### ↳ Re: Are Unix boxes gonna replace Mainframes?

- **From:** Steve Thompson <n_o_spam.steve_t@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T01:00:36-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EBB35F4.1090503@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<b9e5a7$pe3$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg>`

```
scarface wrote:
> My company are migrating almost everything from their
> BIG irons into Unix. Does it mean that in terms of
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

In a word, no.

Take a G6 chipset IBM mainframe. CPU bus to C-Store (RAM for you 
PC types) is 256 Bytes wide. Compare that to 64 bits wide. Cache 
lines are 256 bytes in the G6 chips. A CPU's Cache is > 256K and 
there are two of them (I bank and D bank) -- I can't remember 
what the actual cache size is, been working too many late nights.

Speaking of memory, mainframes have memory CENTRAL to the system, 
not the bus. That is, most non-mainframe systems make the bus 
central to the machine. As a result, n CPUs are able to 
communicate with C-STORE simultaneously (not pseudo 
simultaneously) with y I/O channels (typically connected through 
an IOP - I/O Processor).

1 => n <= 16    CPUs
1 => y <= 128   Channels

Now, given this, how many transactions a second can the mainframe 
do compared to an AIX box?

Well, where I am right now, a z/800-02 is blowing the doors off 
every RAPTOR (biggest AIX box IBM makes) in the house. And they 
are doing everything they can to handicap the mainframe in favor 
of "open systems".  BTW - we are doing in excess of 2M 
transactions in a business day, and the other guys can't hit 50% 
of that (note our transactions are complex and involve 
communications to outside entities). And, we can't seem to load 
the production LPAR beyond 35%!
```

#### ↳ Re: Are Unix boxes gonna replace Mainframes?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T14:00:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9gc9q$95t$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b9e5a7$pe3$1@mawar.singnet.com.sg>`

```
A machine can be BIG iron and run Unix both.   Heck, Crays ran a version of
Unix.

With IBM supporting the use of Linux on its mainframes, why do you assume it is
an either/or situation?

You could just as easily ask "Are Unix boxes gonna replace PCs?".
```

#### ↳ Re: Looking for source code

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-08T12:39:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9ebpi$2o4v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<s9yua.569220$0M1.564682@telenews.teleline.es>`

```
Compliant with ANSI X3.23-1974, and should be compilable with any compiler
compliant with subsequent standards:

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000300 DATA DIVISION.
000400 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000500 ONLY-PARAGRAPH.
000600     STOP RUN.

       environment division.
       configuration section.
       special-names. decimal-point is comma
                      crt status is ws-escape.
       input-output section.
       file-control.
             copy   arqven.ext.
       data division.
       file section.
       copy  vendedor.ext.

       working-storage section.
       01  ws-avanca               pic x         value spaces.
       copy   dthrstat.ext.

       screen section.
           copy  ajuda.ss.
           copy  mensa.ss.

       procedure division.
       inicio section.
           open     input arqven
           if        ws-status not = "00" and "05"
                     move     "ARQVEN  " to ws-nomarq
                     perform  erro-status1
                     close     arqven
                     display  spaces at 0101
                     exit program.

           move  1  to ws-indt
           perform salva-tela
           display tela-ajuda
           move    spaces  to  vnd-nome
           start   arqven key  is not less than vnd-nome
           move    zeros      to ws-coord
           move    99         to ws-cod03
           move    spaces  to  ws-avanca
           perform until ws-cod03  = zeros
             if      ws-avanca = spaces
                     move "A"  to ws-avanca
             else
                     move    spaces  to  ws-avanca
                     accept  ws-avanca  at 2479    with auto
             end-if
             evaluate  true
              when  ws-avanca = ("A" or "a") and
                                (ws-status not = "10" and "46")
                                or ws-avanca = "p" or "P"
                   if  ws-avanca = "P" or "p"
                       move  2  to ws-indt
                       perform salva-tela
                       display tela-mensa
                       move    spaces  to  vnd-nome
                       move    99      to  ws-status
                       perform until ws-status  = "00" or "02"
                          move  spaces  to  vnd-nome
                          accept  vnd-nome  at 2220
                          start   arqven key  is not less than vnd-nome
                          if      ws-status not = "00" and "02"
                                  display
                   "O Nome que foi Digitado nao esta Cadastrado <Enter>"
                                  at 2020
                                  display  vnd-nome   at 2220
                          end-if
                       end-perform
                       move  2  to ws-indt
                       perform recupera-tela
                   end-if
                 move    06      to  ws-lin
                 perform  until ws-lin > 20
                    perform leitura-vendedor-next
                    if   ws-status = "10" or "46"
                         if      ws-lin < 20
                                 move    ws-lin to ws-lin ws-linh
                                 move    20     to ws-col
                                 perform varying ws-lin from ws-linh
                                                 by 1 until ws-lin > 20
                                    display ws-brancos at ws-coord with
                                                          size 59
                                 end-perform
                         end-if
                         move  99      to ws-lin
                         move  zeros   to ws-status
                         move  spaces  to  vnd-nome
                    else
                         move    20      to  ws-col
                         display vnd-chave   at ws-coord
                         move    27      to  ws-col
                         display "-"         at ws-coord
                         move    29      to  ws-col
                         display vnd-nome    at ws-coord
                         add     1  to  ws-lin
                    end-if
                 end-perform
              when  ws-avanca = ("R" or "r")
                 move    20      to  ws-lin
                 perform  until ws-lin < 06
                    perform leitura-vendedor-prev
                    if   ws-status = "10" or "46"
                         if      ws-lin > 06
                                 move    ws-lin to ws-lin ws-linh
                                 move    20     to ws-col
                                 perform varying ws-lin from ws-linh
                                                 by -1 until ws-lin < 06
                                    display ws-brancos at ws-coord with
                                                          size 59
                                 end-perform
                         end-if
                         move 01       to ws-lin
                         move  zeros   to ws-status
                         move  "zzzz"  to  vnd-nome
                    else
                         move    20      to  ws-col
                         display vnd-chave   at ws-coord
                         move    27      to  ws-col
                         display "-"         at ws-coord
                         move    29      to  ws-col
                         display vnd-nome    at ws-coord
                         subtract  1  from   ws-lin
                    end-if
                 end-perform
             end-evaluate
           end-perform
           move  1  to ws-indt
           perform recupera-tela.
       fim-processamento.
           close      arqven
           exit program.

       leitura-vendedor-next.
           read      arqven next with ignore lock at end
                     move  "10" to  ws-status.
           if        ws-status not = "00" and "02" and "10" and
                                     "23" and "46"
                     move    "ARQVEN  " to ws-nomarq
                     move     "N" to ws-dispmsg
                     perform erro-status1
                     go   fim-processamento.

       leitura-vendedor-prev.
           read      arqven previous ignore lock.
           if        ws-status not = "00" and "02" and "10" and
                                     "23" and "46"
                     move    "ARQVEN  " to ws-nomarq
                     move     "N"       to ws-dispmsg
                     perform erro-status1
                     go   fim-processamento.

           copy      taberros.ext.
           copy      salvale.ext.
```

Hi,

I'm stuck in a bunch of Cobol data files of an old system.
Unfortunately it contains all data which would be needed to make business a
little more efficient. Is there any way to access Cobol data files? I was
thinking of a library (maybe for any script language like python...) or an
ODBC way? Even a simple conversion tool would help.

I already googled two possibilities: www.ratrix.com and www.siber.com
Their offers sound good, but a little bit to expensive for our small
company.

The system is SCO Unix running Microfocus Prolog 3.4. We don't have any
sources.

Appreciating every answer

Bernd

Hello,

Is there any tool available to create data model automatically from Cobol source?

Is there any way to design database without going manually through Cobol programs?

All help appreciated.

Thanks a lot.

Ganesh
```

#### ↳ Re: How to create RDBMS (or data model) from Cobol ptograms?

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T11:34:05+12:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3ebc3ad3$1_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<5477459a.0305090618.7d3e8367@posting.google.com>`

I have a tool (ISAM2RDB) that will analyse the COBOL source definition of
ISAM files and generate a relational database in third normal form.

This tool has been used in the live environment and enabled 50 ISAM files to
be converted in 3 days.

If you are interested, mail me privately with some details of the number of
files and maybe a couple of COBOL source definitions and I'll send you a
database by return.

Pete.

### Date checks

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9gi6q$bfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
What do people here use to test for valid dates, assuming that they have
FUNCTIONS, but not TEST- functions?

I've used lots of hard coded code in the past, with different degrees detail.
```

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EBBD730.3070009@Knology.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> What do people here use to test for valid dates, assuming that they have
> FUNCTIONS, but not TEST- functions?
> 
> I've used lots of hard coded code in the past, with different degrees detail.

For Y2K, we came up with one big monster proc that has validation in it.

snippets, since I'm the current custodian of it.  Or, I'd also e-mail it 
to you if you'd like to see the whole thing.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Date checks

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T16:55:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9gmij$d5t$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b9gi6q$bfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3EBBD730.3070009@Knology.net>`

```

On  9-May-2003, LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:

> > I've used lots of hard coded code in the past, with different degrees
> > detail.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> to you if you'd like to see the whole thing.

I've generally used overkill in code to verify that a date is, in fact, a valid
date.   It's not hard to code, I have done so a bunch of times.   But if I had
available a TEST-DATE function, I would use it.  And I was wondering what kinds
of checks people have?   Would you OK February 30?  Do you check for leap years?
  Does your check for leap years include 2000?   Do you use a month table?

Has your methodology changed over the years?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Date checks

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T22:15:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EBC6EB5.1070809@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<b9gi6q$bfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3EBBD730.3070009@Knology.net> <b9gmij$d5t$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On  9-May-2003, LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>   Does your check for leap years include 2000?   Do you use a month table?

Feb 30, no.  Leap year check, yes, and it doesn't just code for 2000 - 
it divides the year by 400. (so, it'll work for 1600 and 2400 as well). 
  Our month table is actually a "month info" table, which has the 
abbreviation, the number of days, the first and last ordinal/julian 
(whatever you like to call them) days for that month, the fiscal 
quarter, the actual quarter, etc.

> Has your methodology changed over the years?

Well, since my years with COBOL started in 1998, I can say "no, not 
really".  :)  But, I am looking forward to the TEST- functions to 
validate dates - they'll probably cut this monster proc way down to size.
```

project I was on had better performance and did exact checking (1900 is
not a leap year but 2000 and 2400 are).

#### ↳ Re: Date checks

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T12:45:09-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9glul$ju8$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b9gi6q$bfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <b9gi6q$bfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>What do people here use to test for valid dates, assuming that they have
>FUNCTIONS, but not TEST- functions?
>
>I've used lots of hard coded code in the past, with different degrees detail.

I try to use a method that works and that is comfortable to my client...  
but I'm easy that way.  The easiest example that comes to my mind
extemporaneously is (given a YYYYMMDD field) -

01  WS-MISC-STUFF.
...
    05  WS-YYYYMMDD.
        10  WS-YYYY PIC 9(4).
        10  WS-MM   PIC 99.
            88  VALID-MONTH VALUES 01 THRU 12.
        10  WS-DD   PIC 99.
            88  VALID-DAYS VALUES 01 THRU 31.
    05  WS-MO-TBL.
        10  FILLER PIC 99 VALUE 31.
        10  FILLER PIC 99 VALUE 28.
        10  FILLER PIC 99 VALUE 31.
...
    05  REDEF REDEFINES WS-MO-TBL.
        10 MAX-DAYS-IN-MO OCCURS 12 PIC 99.

...

    MOVE INPUT-DATE TO WS-YYYYMMDD.
    IF WS-YYYYMMDD NUMERIC AND VALID-MONTH AND VALID-DAYS
        PERFORM VALIDATE-DATE THRU VD-EX
    ELSE
        SET NONNUM-DATE TO TRUE
    END-IF.
...
VALIDATE-DATE.

    SET DATE-INVALID TO TRUE.
    MOVE ZEROES TO WS-JUNK02.
    DIVIDE WS-YYYY BY 4 GIVING WS-JUNK01 REMAINDER WS-JUNK02.
    IF WS-JUNK02 = 0
        MOVE ZEROES TO WS-JUNK03
        DIVIDE WS-YYYY BY 400 GIVING WS-JUNK01 REMAINDER WS-JUNK03.
    IF WS-JUNK02 = 0
        MOVE 29 TO MAX-DAYS-IN-MO(2).
    IF WS-JUNK03 = 0
        MOVE 28 TO MAX-DAYS-IN-MO(2).
    IF WS-DD > MAX-DAYS-IN-MO(WS-MM)
        PERFORM TOO-MANY-DAYS-RITUAL THRU TMDR-EX
    ELSE
        SET DATE-VALID TO TRUE.

VD-EX.
    EXIT.

The flaws in this code are obvious, of course... all caps, periods galore,
PERFORM THRU, it is limited to a particular calendar ('What happens when
we sell this code to an Israeli company?'), a paragraph-name that might
cause an adolescent to giggle ('He named it VD... haw haw haw!')...

... it also, more-or-less, works... and should be readily understood at 
3:am by a 2-year maintenance coder.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Date checks

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T17:16:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EBBDEA3.9326F213@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<b9gi6q$bfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> What do people here use to test for valid dates, assuming that they have
> FUNCTIONS, but not TEST- functions?
>
> I've used lots of hard coded code in the past, with different degrees detail.

Please do your own homework <G>
```

#### ↳ Re: Date checks

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T10:43:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9gpct$1ddg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b9gi6q$bfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
If you have the intrinsic functions INTEGER-OF-DATE and DATE-OF-INTEGER from
the '89 Intrinsic Function Amendment, and the original date THE-DATE  you
want to test is in YYYYMMDD format, it may be possible to verify the
validity of THE-DATE by testing to ensure that the value contained in
THE-DATE is the same as the result of FUNCTION DATE-OF-INTEGER (FUNCTION
INTEGER-OF-DATE (THE-DATE)).  In the MCP/AS  implementation of '85 COBOL,
INTEGER-OF-DATE returns a zero for any out-of-range argument, in which case
DATE-OF-INTEGER returns 16001231.  I would expect something analogous from
anyone whose function doesn't fall over (though the returned value from
INTEGER-OF-DATE may not be zero, it can't represent a value corresponding to
something that isn't a date to begin with, certainly not the [invalid] date
you started with).

    -Chuck Stevens

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:b9gi6q$bfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
> What do people here use to test for valid dates, assuming that they have
> FUNCTIONS, but not TEST- functions?
>
> I've used lots of hard coded code in the past, with different degrees
detail.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Date checks

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T13:37:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9gsi7$o04$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b9gi6q$bfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b9gpct$1ddg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck,
  You are correct that this *MIGHT* work (your word).  Unfortunately, I
think that Howard works in an environment that does a "fully ANSI '89
conforming" thing of "issuing a run-time error" when an invalid argument is
passed to the INTEGER-OF-DATE intrinsic function.

Therefore, this solution wouldn't work (for him) or anyone else that works
in such an environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Date checks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T16:46:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9h7kt$fjp$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b9gi6q$bfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b9gpct$1ddg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b9gsi7$o04$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <b9gu69$g65$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard (who probably knows this already) and others (who may not),

  The following solution is NOT a generic solution. (As far as I know, other
than "table of date checks" there is no generic solution before the 2002
Standard TEST-functions.) It will, however, work with either Micro Focus
(only products where LE callable services are available) or IBM environments
(I think even VA COBOL - but I am not certain about OS/400 or COBOL Set for
AIX).

There is, however,  a "question" that I would think arises with this
"solution", i.e.

   IF you are going to use LE callable services to test if a date is valid,
why not use them for everything???

Anyway, here is a program that will test (in an IBM or Micro Focus
environment) if a date is a valid date in YYYYMMDD format (including
leap-days and other oddities).  If you want to test for a YYYYDDD format, I
think you can figure out the required modification.

(see:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/CEEA3130/3.5.24.3
for source of this program and check your SCEESAMP library for machine
readable version.  I have removed comments in program below)

            IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
            PROGRAM-ID. CBLDAYS.
            DATA DIVISION.
            WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
            01  CHRDATE.
                02  Vstring-length      PIC S9(4) BINARY Value 8.
                02  Vstring-text.
                    03  Vstring-char    PIC X
                                OCCURS 1 TO 8 TIMES
                                DEPENDING ON Vstring-length
                                    of CHRDATE.
            01  PICSTR.
                02  Vstring-length      PIC S9(4) BINARY Value 8.
                02  Vstring-text.
                    03  Vstring-char    PIC X
                                OCCURS 1 TO 8 TIMES
                                DEPENDING ON Vstring-length
                                    of PICSTR.
            01  LILIAN                  PIC S9(9) BINARY.
            01  FC.
                02  Condition-Token-Value.
                COPY  CEEIGZCT.
                    03  Case-1-Condition-ID.
                        04  Severity    PIC S9(4) BINARY.
                        04  Msg-No      PIC S9(4) BINARY.
                    03  Case-2-Condition-ID
                              REDEFINES Case-1-Condition-ID.
                        04  Class-Code  PIC S9(4) BINARY.
                        04  Cause-Code  PIC S9(4) BINARY.
                    03  Case-Sev-Ctl    PIC X.
                    03  Facility-ID     PIC XXX.
                02  I-S-Info            PIC S9(9) BINARY.
            PROCEDURE DIVISION.
            PARA-CBLDAYS.
     *   fill in date to test
                MOVE "20030532"
                    TO Vstring-text of CHRDATE.
           *   fill in format of date to test
                MOVE "YYYYMMDD"
                        TO Vstring-text of PICSTR.
                CALL "CEEDAYS" USING CHRDATE, PICSTR,
                                     LILIAN, FC.
                IF  CEE000 of FC  THEN
                    DISPLAY Vstring-text of CHRDATE
                        " is A VALID date with Lilian Value: " LILIAN
                ELSE
                    DISPLAY "Input Date is not valid, Check return code for
reason"
                        Msg-No of FC UPON CONSOLE
                    STOP RUN
                END-IF
                GOBACK.
```

>                         TO Vstring-text of PICSTR.

#### ↳ Re: Date checks

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T11:27:55+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebc3967_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b9gi6q$bfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:b9gi6q$bfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
> What do people here use to test for valid dates, assuming that they have
> FUNCTIONS, but not TEST- functions?
>
> I've used lots of hard coded code in the past, with different degrees
detail.

Here is the full source code of the main routine I use.

It handles different format dates and has proven useful.

The code here has been moved to a component and has performed successfully
in a live environment for the last 2 years.

(The predecessor of this module ran for 7 years before that...).

Check out the comments in the Linkage Section, which explain what it does.
Basically, you give it a "suspected date" in any one of 5 different formats.
It then "fills in" the other 4 formats. If the suspected date isn't, then it
sets a return code and all the dates will be blank.

By posting it here I am placing it in the public domain and take no
responsibility for its use. No claims of any kind will be entertained. You
use it at your own risk.

Enjoy!

Pete.

========================  source code ==============================

 identification division.
 program-id.     DATEFMT.
 installation.   Prima Computing.
 author.        Peter E. C. Dashwood.
 date-written.   Apr 98.
*
*remarks.
*
*    This program has been superseded by the DATEFMT component.
*
*
*    In this version, Y2K compliance has been ensured, a serial
*    date representing the number of days since 1 January, 1601
*    is also returned for use in date arithmetic,
*    and the code has been totally rewritten,
*    and uses intrinsic COBOL functions to cut down the size of
*    source code.
*
*     PARAMETERS: See description in LINKAGE...
*
*    *******************************************************
 environment division.
 configuration section.
 source-computer. IBM-PC.
 object-computer. IBM-PC.
*------------------------  DATA  DIVISION  ---------------------
 data division.
 working-storage section.
 01  ws-date-area.
    12 wda-yyyy         pic x(4).
    12 wda-yyyy-num redefines wda-yyyy pic 9(4).
    12 wda-mm         pic x(2).
    12 wda-mm-num redefines wda-mm pic 9(2).
    12 wda-dd         pic x(2).
    12 wda-dd-num redefines wda-dd pic 9(2).
 01  ws-date-area-num redefines ws-date-area
           pic 9(8).
*
 01  ws-process-ind        pic s9 comp-5 value zero.
*
*
 01  ws-today         pic x(21).
*   Format of ws-today is as returned by COBOL CURRENT-DATE
*   intrinsic function:
*
*   YYYYMMDDHHMMSSSS+hhmm
*
*   (hhmm following sign are hours and minutes ahead or behind
*    GMT, if this available from the OS, otherwise they are zero)
*
*
 01  ws-year-start        pic x(8).
 01  ws-year-start-num redefines ws-year-start pic 9(8).
 01  ws-year-start-int        pic s9(7) comp.
*
*
 01  ws-now         pic x(8).
 01  ws-now-num redefines ws-now pic 9(8).
 01  ws-now-int         pic s9(7) comp.
*
*
 01  ws-days         pic 999 value zero.
 01  ws-days-x redefines ws-days pic xxx.
*
*
 01  ws-month         pic xx value zero.
 01  ws-month-num redefines ws-month pic 99.
*
*
 01  table-of-months.
    12 filler         pic xxx value 'JAN'.
    12 filler         pic xxx value 'FEB'.
    12 filler         pic xxx value 'MAR'.
    12 filler         pic xxx value 'APR'.
    12 filler         pic xxx value 'MAY'.
    12 filler         pic xxx value 'JUN'.
    12 filler         pic xxx value 'JUL'.
    12 filler         pic xxx value 'AUG'.
    12 filler         pic xxx value 'SEP'.
    12 filler         pic xxx value 'OCT'.
    12 filler         pic xxx value 'NOV'.
    12 filler         pic xxx value 'DEC'.
 01  filler redefines table-of-months.
    12 month-name        pic xxx
           occurs 12
           indexed by month-index.
*
*
 01  table-of-month-days.
    12 filler         pic xx value '31'.
    12 filler         pic xx value '??'.
    12 filler         pic xx value '31'.
    12 filler         pic xx value '30'.
    12 filler         pic xx value '31'.
    12 filler         pic xx value '30'.
    12 filler         pic xx value '31'.
    12 filler         pic xx value '31'.
    12 filler         pic xx value '30'.
    12 filler         pic xx value '31'.
    12 filler         pic xx value '30'.
    12 filler         pic xx value '31'.
 01  filler redefines table-of-month-days.
    12 month-days        pic xx
           occurs 12
           indexed by md-index.
*

 01  dont-care   pic s9(4) comp-5.
 01  leap-year-maybe              pic 99 comp-5.
*
 linkage section.
*
*
*     PARAMETERS: (any one parameter can be input; all other
*     parameters filled in and returned by DATEFMT).
*     The parameters are all contained in a single block.
*
*     The parameters consist of 5 different dates and a return
*     code.
*
*    Date-1: yyyymmdd (database format)
*    Date-2: yyyyddd (julian date format)
*    Date-3: dd/mm/yy (mainframe date format) Date-3 will
*     default to the current century. If
*     this is NOT what is wanted, use a
*     different date format which includes
*     the century, e.g.yyyymmdd
*
*    Date-4: dd-mmm-yyyy (international display date format)
*     The month can be any mix of upper or
*     lower case. If it is not passed, it
*     will be returned as capitalised lower
*     case. e.g.Aug,Feb, etc.
*
*
*    Date-5: integer (7 digit 4 byte binary integer
*     representing the number of days since
*     1st January, 1601.
*     7 digits covers us for 27,378.5 years..
*
*
 01  DATEFMT-parms.
   12 lnk-return-code        pic x.
      88 returned-ok       value zero.
      88 bad-date-1       value '1'.
      88 bad-date-2       value '2'.
      88 bad-date-3       value '3'.
      88 bad-date-4       value '4'.
      88 bad-date-5       value '5'.
      88 lnk-no-parms       value '6'.
      88 lnk-internal-error    value '7'.
*    Date-1: yyyymmdd (database format)
*    Date-2: yyyyddd (julian date format)
*    Date-3: dd/mm/yy (mainframe date format)
*    Date-4: dd-mmm-yyyy (international display date format)
*    Date-5: integer (7 digit 4 byte binary integer
   12 lnk-yyyymmdd        pic x(8).
   12 lnk-yyyyddd        pic 9(7).
   12 lnk-yyyyddd-x redefines lnk-yyyyddd   pic x(7).
   12 lnk-mfdate         pic x(8).
   12 lnk-dd-mmm-yyyy        pic x(11).
   12 lnk-integer        pic s9(7) comp.
   12 lnk-integer-x redefines lnk-integer   pic x(4).
*
/
 procedure division using DATEFMT-parms.
 main section.
 main-start.
    set returned-ok  to true
    perform a-initialise
    perform b-processing
    .
 return-to-caller.
    exit program.
*-----------------------------------------------------------------
 a-initialise section.
 a-0100.
    if lnk-yyyymmdd = spaces
       if lnk-yyyyddd = zeroes
        OR
   lnk-yyyyddd-x = spaces
   if lnk-mfdate = spaces
      if lnk-dd-mmm-yyyy = spaces
         if lnk-integer = zero
      OR
     lnk-integer-x = spaces
     set lnk-no-parms to true
     move 6 to ws-process-ind
     go to a999
         else
     set bad-date-5 to true
     move 5 to ws-process-ind
         end-if
      else
         set bad-date-4 to true
         move 4 to ws-process-ind
      end-if
   else
      set bad-date-3 to true
      move 3 to ws-process-ind
   end-if
       else
   set bad-date-2 to true
   move 2 to ws-process-ind
       end-if
    else
       set bad-date-1 to true
       move 1 to ws-process-ind
    end-if
    move function current-date  to ws-today
    move ws-today (1:8)        to ws-now
    compute ws-now-int =
  function integer-of-date (ws-now-num)
    move ws-now         to ws-year-start
    move '0101'         to ws-year-start (5:4)
    compute ws-year-start-int =
  function integer-of-date (ws-year-start-num)

    .
 a999.
    exit.
*-----------------------------------------------------------------
 b-processing      section.
 b000.
    evaluate ws-process-ind
    when 1
   perform b001-yyyymmdd
    when 2
   perform b002-yyyyddd
    when 3
   perform b003-mfdate
    when 4
   perform b004-dd-mmm-yyyy
    when 5
   perform b005-integer
    when 6
   go to  b999
    when other
   set lnk-internal-error to true
   go to  b999
    end-evaluate
*
*  Validate ws-date-area...
*
    if wda-yyyy (3:2) = zero
       divide wda-yyyy-num by 400
       giving dont-care REMAINDER leap-year-maybe
    else
       divide wda-yyyy-num by 4
       giving dont-care REMAINDER leap-year-maybe
    end-if
    if leap-year-maybe = zero
       move 29 to month-days (2)
    else
       move 28 to month-days (2)
    end-if
    if wda-mm < '01'
   OR
       wda-mm > '12'
   go to  b999
    end-if
    set md-index to wda-mm-num
    if wda-dd < '01'
   OR
       wda-dd > month-days (md-index)
   go to  b999
    end-if
    compute lnk-integer =
   function integer-of-date (ws-date-area-num)

    if lnk-integer = zero
*  intrinsic function failed...bad date is already set so exit
       go to b999
    else
       set returned-ok        to true
    end-if
*   fill all areas in the linkage block....
    if lnk-yyyymmdd = spaces
       move ws-date-area        to lnk-yyyymmdd
    end-if
*
*
    if lnk-yyyyddd  = zero
       compute ws-days =
       (lnk-integer - ws-year-start-int) + 1
       string
   ws-date-area (1:4)
      delimited by size
   ws-days-x
      delimited by size
         into lnk-yyyyddd
       end-string
    end-if

*
*
    if lnk-mfdate  = spaces
       string
   ws-date-area (7:2)
      delimited by size
   '/'
      delimited by size
   ws-date-area (5:2)
      delimited by size
   '/'
      delimited by size
   ws-date-area (3:2)
      delimited by size
         into lnk-mfdate
       end-string
    end-if

*
*
    if lnk-dd-mmm-yyyy  = spaces
       move ws-date-area (5:2) to ws-month
       set month-index to ws-month-num
       string
   ws-date-area (7:2)
      delimited by size
   '-'
      delimited by size
   month-name (month-index)
      delimited by size
   '-'
      delimited by size
   ws-date-area (1:4)
      delimited by size
         into lnk-dd-mmm-yyyy
       end-string
       move function lower-case (lnk-dd-mmm-yyyy (5:2)) to
     lnk-dd-mmm-yyyy (5:2)
    end-if
    .
 b999.
    exit.
*-----------------------------------------------------------------
 b001-yyyymmdd         section.
 b001-note.
*
*   This section creates ws-date-area from lnk-yyyymmdd
*
    move lnk-yyyymmdd        to ws-date-area
    .
 b001.
    exit.
*-----------------------------------------------------------------
 b002-yyyyddd         section.
 b002-note.
*
*   This section creates ws-date-area from lnk-yyyyddd
*
    compute lnk-integer =
  function integer-of-day (lnk-yyyyddd)
    compute ws-date-area-num =
  function date-of-integer (lnk-integer)

    .
 b002.
    exit.
*-----------------------------------------------------------------
 b003-mfdate         section.
 b003-note.
*
*   This section creates ws-date-area from lnk-mfdate
*
*   As currently written the routine defaults to the
*   current century. If this is not what is required,
*   user must specify a different format which includes century.
*   (e.g. yyyymmdd)
*
*
    move lnk-mfdate (1:2)       to wda-dd
    move lnk-mfdate (4:2)       to wda-mm
    move ws-today   (1:2)       to wda-yyyy (1:)
    move lnk-mfdate (7:2)       to wda-yyyy (3:)
    .
 b003.
    exit.
*-----------------------------------------------------------------
 b004-dd-mmm-yyyy         section.
 b004-note.
*
*   This section creates ws-date-area from lnk-dd-mmm-yyyy
*

    move lnk-dd-mmm-yyyy (1:2)  to wda-dd
    set month-index        to 1
    search month-name
       at end
   move zero        to ws-date-area
   go to b004
       when
   month-name (month-index) =
   function upper-case (lnk-dd-mmm-yyyy (4:3))
   continue
    end-search
    set ws-month-num        to month-index

    move ws-month        to wda-mm
    move lnk-dd-mmm-yyyy (8:4)  to wda-yyyy
    .
 b004.
    exit.
*-----------------------------------------------------------------
 b005-integer         section.
 b005-note.
*
*   This section creates ws-date-area from a passed date
*   integer. (The integer will be the number of days since
*   01/01/1601)
*
    compute ws-date-area-num =
  function date-of-integer (lnk-integer)
    .
 b005.
    exit.
*-----------------------------------------------------------------
*-----------  end of program  ------------------------------------

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

component version uses the same logic.

I'm curious to know if COBOL really is as transportable (without taking any
special care to make it so) as we all think...

Pete.

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3EBED3B9.A22BB2F0@shaw.ca...
>
>
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:
>
> > If anybody actually compiles this code and tries it, could you let me
know?
> >
> > I'd be interested to see what platforms it runs on.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'll give it a shot for you in Net Express. Didn't look in detail but have
it
> squirreled away. (Reason - I'm only interested currently in verifying
input of YYMM
> and a quick bit of logic to add the century). I'll get back to you about
the compile.
>
> This is back to something I wrote some time back. I really would like to
see a library
> of COBOL examples. (DD could then write 'Please do your own homework
having taken a
> look at URL xxxxx)  J4 folks - I know it appeared as an item one time on
your agenda -
> what happened. Just fizzled out ?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Date checks

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T22:39:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC17041.53045FB0@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<b9gi6q$bfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ebc3967_2@127.0.0.1> <3ebd88d2_1@127.0.0.1> <3EBED3B9.A22BB2F0@shaw.ca> <3ebef762_2@127.0.0.1>`

```


"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Thanks Jimmy.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Compiled as sweet as a nut in seconds. Thought I'd better give it a call. It must be
aeons since I wrote a non-OO call - so initially I was thrown as you can see from
following code by Calling XXX Using AAAA Returning BBBB when in fact you are sending
and receiving the same block. Initially I was getting a space x'00' for the return
code.

I'm fairly sure it was related to OO specifically, but M/F cautioned against using the
same sending and receiving fields - results can be unpredictable. So I've got into the
habit of 'sendings' and 'receivings' as being separate entities - more often than not
I have more than one return value under a Level 01, e.g. :-

Local-storage section.
01 ls-values.
    05 ls-return-code                pic 9(03).
    05 ls-CollectionObject        object reference.

invoke MyCollectionClass "create" using A,B,C, Returning ls-values

if ls-return-code <> zeroes
  (OR more accurately I use 88s and have "if File/Table-Error......)
   we gotta problem

Jimmy

***** Here's what I used :-

Program-id. CallDateFmt.

Working-Storage section.
01  DATEFMT-parms.
  12 lnk-return-code        pic x.
     88 returned-ok       value zero.
     etc....
  12 lnk-yyyymmdd        pic x(8).
  etc...

Procedure Division.

move "20030727"      to lnk-yyyymmdd
move  2003198         to lnk-yyyyddd
move "27/07/03"       to lnk-mfdate
move "27-Jul-2003"  to lnk-dd-mmm-yyyy
move 1234567         to lnk-integer
call "DateFmt" using DateFmt-Parms.
*>returning lnk-return-code

STOP RUN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Date checks

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T12:19:17+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec188d8_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<b9gi6q$bfd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ebc3967_2@127.0.0.1> <3ebd88d2_1@127.0.0.1> <3EBED3B9.A22BB2F0@shaw.ca> <3ebef762_2@127.0.0.1> <3EC17041.53045FB0@shaw.ca>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3EC17041.53045FB0@shaw.ca...
>
> Compiled as sweet as a nut in seconds. Thought I'd better give it a call.
It must be
> aeons since I wrote a non-OO call - so initially I was thrown as you can
see from
> following code by Calling XXX Using AAAA Returning BBBB when in fact you
are sending
> and receiving the same block. Initially I was getting a space x'00' for
the return
> code.
>

Ah, old habits die pretty easily it seems <G>.

It is certainly true that I have to stop and think on the rare occasions I
need to make a procedural call now, too...

The secret here is NOT to specify RETURNING (the code does not "return"
anything, it just updates the parameter block).

> I'm fairly sure it was related to OO specifically, but M/F cautioned
against using the
> same sending and receiving fields - results can be unpredictable. So I've
got into the
> habit of 'sendings' and 'receivings' as being separate entities - more
often than not
> I have more than one return value under a Level 01, e.g. :-
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> invoke MyCollectionClass "create" using A,B,C, Returning ls-values

Yes, this is certainly good practice for OO and I do the same.

It is not applicable to DATEFMT.

>
> if ls-return-code <> zeroes
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Here's a sample of how it works...

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

01   date-handling    pic x(15) value "DATEFMT   ".  *> I use a dynamic call
but it isn't mandatory...


PROCEDURE DIVISION.
...
move spaces to DATEFMT-parms
*>
*> Set up only ONE date. It can be any of the 5...
*>
move "20030727"      to lnk-yyyymmdd *> this is an example...
call DATEFMT
      using DATEFMT-parms
      on exception *> do something dreadful...
end-call
if NOT returned-OK
   *> The date passed was not valid. Take whatever action you need to...
end-if
*> At this point ALL 5 dates are available in DATEFMT-parms
*> and they are all valid representations of the date you passed
*> including the lnk-integer which will be the number of days since
01/01/1601
*> until the date specified.

I think the 88 level "returned-ok" is NOT a good name and people using the
code might want to change it to something more meaningful and date related
(maybe "date-ok") or somesuch.

It is interesting how we focus on the problem at the time. When I first
wrote this code I was having all kinds of trouble getting the bloody Call to
work (it was MicroFocus code originally and I had some trouble with run time
switches (I was using extended memory under DOS (remember that <G>?)...I
would call the module and never receive control back), so it was a "major
breakthrough" if the call returned to my program OK. As a result, I labelled
the event as "returned-ok" having lost sight of the real problem, which was
about dates and not about grappling with the environment...

We all have 20/20 vision in hindsight...<G>

Anyway, thanks for taking the time to try it, Jimmy and for posting the
results. I hope you find the module useful.

I note with some regret that Don, who prompted this thread by asking what
code people use, has not acknowledged. I was hoping he might have some
comment.

(Last time I respond to your requests, Don <G>...)

Pete.

PS. I have moved to another Usenet feed and am having no trouble seeing your
posts now, Jimmy. Thanks for posting privately as well; there is no longer
necessity.

It's interesting... the old feed was direct from Usenet and cost me around
$US20 a month. They kept moving the forum between two servers and it was
necessary to look at both of them to get the truly up-to-date picture. The
new supplier offers a choice of 3 servers, 2 of which have synchronized CLC
on them (so I only ever need to look at one, but there is a backup if it
goes down). The cost is $6 a month...go figure.
```

<a id="t-1c850ce752"></a>
## Thread: maintenance problems — 1 msgs

### maintenance problems

- **From:** "Carole Devillers" <carole@raincode.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T17:54:52+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebbcfbe$0$6524$afc38c87@sisyphus.news.be.easynet.net>`

```
Hi,

If you are concerned about maintenance problems this
will interest you.

It is frequently experienced that 70% of the total costs of a
system are devoted to maintenance and adjustements. Several
studies have also demonstrated that restructuring legacy
applications leads to a reduction of 40 to 50% in maintenance
time and cost. Restructuring is obviously a cost-effective
investment.

RainCode Online is a new, recently launched service. It is an
online COBOL restructuring service: it reduces maintenance
costs by renovating legacy COBOL code, and giving it a second
life. After transformation, applications become much more
readable and intelligible, with less GOTOs, a better structure
and a better performance.

Have a look at this short example of how RainCode Online
can transform a piece of badly structured COBOL code.
Evaluation is free, try and connect to www.raincode.com/online


A1001.
       MOVE     1              TO X-WRK-J.
       GO TO    A1003.
A1002.
       ADD      1              TO X-WRK-J.
A1003.
       IF         10         < X-WRK-J THEN
         GO TO    A1006
       END-IF.
       IF    FLAG(X-WRK-J) = "0" THEN
         NEXT SENTENCE
       ELSE
         GOTO    A1004
       END-IF.
       MOVE  "X" TO TABLE(X-WRK-J).
A1004.
       IF   FLAG(X-WRK-J) NOT = "0" THEN
         NEXT SENTENCE
       ELSE
         GOTO A1005
       END-IF.
       MOVE "Y" TO TABLE(X-WRK-J).
A1005.
       GO TO A1002.
A1006.
       EXIT.


Becomes:


PERFORM WITH TEST BEFORE
       VARYING X-WRK-J FROM 1
                       BY 1
       UNTIL 10 < X-WRK-J
       IF FLAG(X-WRK-J) = "0" THEN
           MOVE "X" TO TABLE(X-WRK-J)
       ELSE
           MOVE "Y" TO TABLE(X-WRK-J)
       END-IF
END-PERFORM.
```

### COBOL Expo in Las Vegas - June 23rd through 25th

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>

```
Dear COBOL Professional:

REGISTER FOR COBOL EXPO AS SOON AS POSSIBLE!!

There are only a few more days to register for COBOL Expo and receive
the early bird discount of $100.

NOTE:  SAVE AN EXTRA $100!!!!  If you use the Flexus keycode of FLE100
to register for COBOL Expo, we will reimburse you an ADDITIONAL $100
toward your registration.  You can apply the reimbursement to your
annual software maintenance, purchase of a new product or simply ask
us to send you a check.

We are doing this in order to try to help get as many people to attend
as possible.

We believe that an annual COBOL conference is good for the COBOL
programming community and want to do everything we can to promote the
conference.

You can register at http://www.CobolExpo.com

COBOL Expo 2003 will be held in Las Vegas, NV at the University of
Nevada, Las Vegas on Monday June 23rd through Wednesday, June 25th.



Bob Wolfe

When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Expo in Las Vegas - June 23rd through 25th

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T13:25:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<46s1cvk02l642ghmhrrifut2fiajfp9oji@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5aknbvk7lrt68rok9nml57835liqtoefo9@4ax.com> <HEMxo5.Iut@news.boeing.com> <5m7vbv80vpiark2ukkg8s3n1get8t8n2kr@4ax.com> <3ec03f6e.27395908@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

>Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Would Nomex (flame resistant) coveralls be appropriate attire?

Robert:

Nomex certainly affords standard protection against flames, but for
superior protection, you should consider PBI as the preferred material
for your coveralls.

If you are also concerned about flame resistance as well as puncture
resistance, then Kevlar is what you are seeking as your material of
choice.

And if your priorities do not include puncture resistance, but you
want the highest flame resistance possible, just purchase a proximity
suit.

The only problem is that in your efforts to protect yourself from the
flames, you will succumb to heat stroke by wearing such attire in the
average, late June Las Vegas temperature.

Drink lots of fluids.

;-)



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

Charles:

adequate protection against heat and flames.

His primary concern was to survive the flames of those who do not
share his opinions in this great forum.

Please don't misunderstand...I LIKE the Kodiac protection wear.
Perhaps the best thing to do is don the Kodiac suit and then wear a
proximity suit over the top of it.

This combination would likely allow you to survive a dynamite-laden
cargo aircraft crash into a gasoline tank farm.

....or maybe even an upset win at a children's sports event.

;-)




Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

## Thread: Hangs up printing — 1 msgs

### Hangs up printing

- **From:** mailjamez@email.com (Niko)
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T09:11:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<32765ce3.0305090811.23bacb5f@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi all,
I never posted on this ng, and I don't know if this is the right place
for my problem, so I go on...
I use an application written in Acu-Cobol, client/server app wich use
acuserver/acuconnect system, here is my problem: my client is a win 98
2nd edition, when I try to print certain data with this application my
pc hangs up; I used task manager to understand, and I saw when
printing my pc load WINOA386.MOD, that hangs up WRUN32.EXE (the acu
runtime). So, to get back in control of my pc I must kill wrun32.exe
process, and nothing is printed. The big problem is that what I wrote
happens only on ONE CLIENT of my lan, so the problem isn't located on
my Linux server runnig acuserve neither in other client, only on THIS
client. Can someone help me? Who knows what WINOA386.MOD is used for,
and why when I print acucobol loads it in memory ??? There's another
strange thing: when printing from all others client of the same lan,
all goes fine and the others client doesn't load WINOA386.MOD when
printing !!! Any idea ?

thanks in advance

Jamez
mailjamez@email.com
```

<a id="t-f87892a8b6"></a>
## Thread: Net Express Revisited — 3 msgs

### Net Express Revisited

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T12:47:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VXQua.12070$VJ.782837@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Well after half a day of fiddling, I managed to find a way (with a EXTFH.CFG
file) to prevent Net Express from changing the tabs in my data to spaces.
Unfortunately, it will now not allow me to use TABS in source code, so it
looks like I either cannot use any existing source on my system, or I cannot
use any data on my system. Is there a way to have it treat source code as
normally, and still read data on the same machine without re-configuring?

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express Revisited

- **From:** "Jean Villemaire" <jean.villemaire@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T07:51:57-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9o1t6$rnt$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<VXQua.12070$VJ.782837@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald,

From your post I am presuming that you are using the EXPANDTAB=OFF file
handler parameter.

A possible solution would be to set this parameter for your line sequential
file only instead of setting it globally.

Your EXTFH.CFG would look something like this;

[XFH-DEFAULT}
BASENAME=ON

[MYFILENAME.EXTENSION]
EXPANDTAB=OFF

Hope this helps.



"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:VXQua.12070$VJ.782837@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Well after half a day of fiddling, I managed to find a way (with a
EXTFH.CFG
> file) to prevent Net Express from changing the tabs in my data to spaces.
> Unfortunately, it will now not allow me to use TABS in source code, so it
> looks like I either cannot use any existing source on my system, or I
cannot
> use any data on my system. Is there a way to have it treat source code as
> normally, and still read data on the same machine without re-configuring?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Net Express Revisited

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T08:30:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KtMva.732$z13.151536@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<VXQua.12070$VJ.782837@news20.bellglobal.com> <b9o1t6$rnt$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
Hi Jean.  So THAT is how you do it.  Thank you.  However, I did a work
arround.  I defined the file as sequential with a one byte record, and just
read it a line at a time scanning for the CR and LF to.

Donald

"Jean Villemaire" <jean.villemaire@microfocus.com> wrote in message
news:b9o1t6$rnt$1@hyperion.microfocus.com...

<a id="t-e060b7c0cb"></a>
## Thread: How to check a filesize in JCL — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to check a filesize in JCL

- **From:** sahus@hotmail.com (Srini)
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T14:23:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<186e13cf.0305091323.11a9e443@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi JCL Guru's

I would appreciate if you can provide a solution to my problem

Is there a way to determine a filsize in a JCL Step. Basically number
of records in the file.

e.g:-
My job requires to execute STEP1 if there are no records in a
TSOID.FILE.NAME else STEP2.

Right now we are calling a cobol program to do this. Its kind of
getting messy since we are doing this a lot more times than i wanted
it to.

The effort I put on this problem before I posted here is to use
SYNCSORT, FILESZ parameter but this is not really helping my cause.

Thanks for your time
Srini
```

#### ↳ Re: How to check a filesize in JCL

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T20:18:46-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-C233C3.20184609052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<186e13cf.0305091323.11a9e443@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <186e13cf.0305091323.11a9e443@posting.google.com>,
 sahus@hotmail.com (Srini) wrote:

> Hi JCL Guru's
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Srini

The only way to make a decision in JCL is based on a return code of a 
previous step.  So you will really need three steps.  The first should 
execute some utility, IDCAMS or IEBGENER can give you an RC=4 if you try 
to copy an empty dataset, or you can roll your own with assembler -- 
open the file and see if the first thing is an end of file marker.

The execute STEP1 with a COND=(STEPTEST,EQ,4) and STEP2 with a 
COND=(STEPTEST,NE,4)...

Hope that helps.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to check a filesize in JCL

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-05-09T19:30:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0305091830.334c3f31@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<186e13cf.0305091323.11a9e443@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Srini,

Use this step before your other 2 steps.

//  EXEC IDCAMS  
//SYSIN DD *
 PRINT INDATASET=('your.data.set') COUNT(1)

If 'your.data.set' exists and contains no data, the step RC=4.
If 'your.data.set' does not exist             , the step RC=12.
If 'your.data.set' exists and contains data,    the step RC=0.

Now code the COND param of you subsequent steps accordingly.

Regards, Jack.


sahus@hotmail.com (Srini) wrote in message news:<186e13cf.0305091323.11a9e443@posting.google.com>...
> Hi JCL Guru's
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Srini
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to check a filesize in JCL

- **From:** "scarface" <scarface@scarface.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T02:53:58+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pan.2003.05.10.18.53.42.924618@scarface.com>`
- **References:** `<186e13cf.0305091323.11a9e443@posting.google.com> <8a2d426e.0305091830.334c3f31@posting.google.com>`

```
On Fri, 09 May 2003 19:30:47 -0700, Jack Sleight wrote:

> Hi Srini,
> 
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Srini

or you can do a repro

  ..
  REPRO INDA('IN.FILE') OUTDA('OUT.FILE') COUNT(1)

  This will fail if IN.FILE is empty and Jacks solution
  applies here as well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to check a filesize in JCL

- **From:** "David Binkowski" <dcbinkowski@ameritech.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T23:11:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VGfva.11898$%_3.6358467@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<186e13cf.0305091323.11a9e443@posting.google.com> <8a2d426e.0305091830.334c3f31@posting.google.com> <pan.2003.05.10.18.53.42.924618@scarface.com>`

```
Hey Srini, does your last name begin with a "B" ?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to check a filesize in JCL

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T14:01:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0305101301.79d933c2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<186e13cf.0305091323.11a9e443@posting.google.com> <8a2d426e.0305091830.334c3f31@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Srini,

Just a clarification of the phrase "contains no data". 

Not every "empty" file returns a valid EOF indication when a read
operation is performed against it.

A file that has been allocated space but never opened and closed for
output operations returns an error indication when a read is
attempted. In this situation the IDCAMS step described below returns
an RC=12.

If the file contained records and all were susequently deleted, or the
file was opened and closed for output operations without writing a
record to the file, then the IDCAMS step returns a RC=4.

Jack.

jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight) wrote in message news:<8a2d426e.0305091830.334c3f31@posting.google.com>...
> Hi Srini,
> 
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Srini
```

#### ↳ Re: How to check a filesize in JCL

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T22:31:08-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EBF076C.75413BAF@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<186e13cf.0305091323.11a9e443@posting.google.com>`

```
Srini wrote:
> 
> Hi JCL Guru's
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> of records in the file.

You wouldn't be in Columbus OH would you?

You will want to execute IKJEFT01 (TSO in batch). There you
will want to run either a REXX or CLIST that will query the
type of file (LISTCAT). If VSAM, you can get back the number
of records. If xSAM, then you can find out how much of the
file is used. 

I think the command you are after is STATE.

At any rate, you can make these determinations from with the
the REXX or CLIST and cause the RC to be set accordingly
which you can then process using JCL IF logic.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to check a filesize in JCL

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T13:43:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0305121243.203ea712@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<186e13cf.0305091323.11a9e443@posting.google.com> <3EBF076C.75413BAF@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Srini,

If you're still out there, drop us a line to clarify what you want to
know.

You ask how to detemine the # of recs in a file, but show an example
that requires to know if the file is "empty" or not.

As you can see from the replies, each requires a different solution.
Actually Steve's solution solves both interpretations, but, in my
opinion, is overkill as an "empty" file solution. (No offense
intended, Steve.)

Regards, Jack. 


Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<3EBF076C.75413BAF@ix.netcom.com>...
> Srini wrote:
> > 
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> attorney's fees) for collecting this fee.
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2003-05-10T08:27:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1052555163@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

:>don't want to admin their database, and hopefully it will be small enough
:>for embedded systems built with J2ME.

Then why do you care about the structure?

VSAM is a base part of the operating system!
```

:>>:>>>written, how read, records added and deleted, and such?
  
:>>:>> Why not use an ODBC driver?  You can find some links to them here:
  
:>>:>> http://ourworld.compuserve.com/homepages/Ken_North/odbcvend.htm
  
:>>:>> If you still want to write your own, almost all IBM manuals are available
:>>:>> online. Look here for instructions on how to find them:

:>>:>I don't want the ODBC for it implies I have a much larger database hanging
:>>:>around someplace. I want something like a very small VSAM or dbm for small
:>>:>projects that don't require large databases, contract programs where I
:>>:>don't want to admin their database, and hopefully it will be small enough
:>>:>for embedded systems built with J2ME.

:>> VSAM is a base part of the operating system!

:>I care because I don't have a vsam library on unix or embedded devices.

Why do you wish to use the VSAM structure rather than some structure you
define yourself?

VSAM is a sort of jack-of-all-trades structure - you would be better off
defining something relevant to your application.
