In reality I don't care if its vsam, isam, or something else. I really
want key/value pairs stored on disk such that the manipulating
code is small and the stored values are small. For embedded systems
I expect maybe a few thousand records with maybe 100 bytes of length
each. For small applications on my servers it could be more or less.
What I don't want is a full DBMS. If I could get a very light version
of unix dbm I'd be quite satisfied.

Mike
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: vsam algorithms?

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T01:51:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebd7b87.195946839@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<vbq3gg54g6mdf3@corp.supernews.com> <3ebd4a27.183305146@news.optonline.net> <vbqoqe7rfdq10@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Mike <Mike@mikee.ath.cx> wrote:

>In article <3ebd4a27.183305146@news.optonline.net>, Robert Wagner wrote:
>> Mike <Mike@mikee.ath.cx> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>for embedded systems built with J2ME.

In that case, get a COBOL compiler. They all come with a VSAM-like file system
out of the box. There is no need to write your own. 

Dave Sokol, one of the two authors of Realia COBOL, told me they spent more time
on the file system than on the COBOL compiler. Developing your own file system
is not a weekend project.
```

```

:>I want to implement my own vsam system. Where can I find the algorithms

#### ↳ Re: vsam algorithms?

- **From:** Steve Thompson <s_thompson#NO#SPAM_@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T22:38:20-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EBF091C.6CD3861F@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<vbq3gg54g6mdf3@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Mike wrote:
> 
> I want to implement my own vsam system. Where can I find the algorithms
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mike

I've read the whole set of answers here and in ASM370.

I think what you want is BDAM, or Relative record
processing.

OTOH - If you use xSAM (where x is B or Q), and you have the
understanding of NOTE & POINT, you can build a key
record(s), read that into an incore table and before closing
the data set, write that table back out. This way you can
read direct and re-write the disk data.

HOWEVER, with 100 byte records, your DASD efficiency with
3380/90 type drives will be abysmal.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Program (URGENT and IMPORTANT)

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T09:29:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EBE5E4F.4040507@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<2586396.1046409100@dbforums.com> <yLqdnZe4PubiICCjXTWcoQ@comcast.com>`

```
member425738@dbforums.com wrote:
>Is there someone who can write a program for me in COBOL that will
>calculate the area of TRIANGLE, area of RECTANGLE and area of CIRCLE. I
>know its too much of asking but i need it, Please help me.

Yes, there is.  However, you don't learn if someone here does it.  You 
should be able to find a "shell" of a COBOL program (with the four 
divisions) in a textbook, the language reference manual of the compiler, 
even online.

To do what you need, you'll need to define picture clauses for your 
variables.  You'll also probably want to make them bigger than your 
biggest final expected value, as many of the math verbs calculate 
"intermediate values" that can be larger than the final value.

In the procedure division, check out the ACCEPT verb to ask the user 
whether they want a triangle, rectangle, or circle, and to prompt them 
for the dimensions of what they've selected.  Then, check out the ADD, 
SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, DIVIDE, and COMPUTE verbs for actually doing the 
calculations.  Again, you should be able to find these in a textbook or 
language reference manual.  If you have neither, here's a link to a 
COBOL language reference manual (it's the one we use at work)...

http://public.support.unisys.com/2200/docs/ix80/PDF/78310448-002.PDF

(You'll have to define PI manually, unless you've got a COBOL 
2002-compliant compiler.  The rules for picture clauses should describe 
how to do decimal numbers.)

Once you get something put together, you'll find folks here more than 
willing to help you learn - but, we like to see that the person asking 
for help has expended a bit of effort on learning it themselves first.

Good luck...
```

<a id="t-3277892029"></a>
## Thread: NDM help — 1 msgs

### NDM help

- **From:** "scarface" <scarface@scarface.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T13:14:23+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pan.2003.05.11.05.14.12.428766@scarface.com>`

```
Hi,

We are using NDM (Network Data Mover) to transfer/handoff
files across multiple platforms. One problem we have is when
we tried sending a dataset to a WinNT box, the number of
records is twice the size of the original. Ours is an OS/390
host mainframe. NDM requires that a receiving NDM is also
running in the destination machine.

Our scripts doesn't have RUN TASK, it's just a place COPY time
output dispositions as RPL (replace). Only problem is according
to the NDM stats at the destination, they are receiving the 
correct number of records.

Also confirmed is that there are no jobs, programs, running
to reprocess the file in the destination machine.

Any help is highly appreciated. Thank you in advance.

Regards,
Mel
```

<a id="t-fb4018c768"></a>
## Thread: [OT] An Olde Joke — 12 msgs

### [OT] An Olde Joke

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T11:22:28-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9lprk$as$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

... told me, long ago, by one with greater familiarity about such things 
than I.  As with many jokes, though, there might be a grain of truth to 
it.

A group of rabbis were once arguing about a manifestation of Divine Intent 
in a bit of Scripture.  The old one argued that it was one way, the three 
younger ones were in agreement in their disagreement with him.  Finally 
the 'leader' of the younger set said 'Look, you say it is one way, we say 
it is the other; there are three of us and only one of you, we must be 
right.'

At that moment the sky grew dark... and in the clouds there appeared a 
hole through which came a single sunbeam which illuminated the old 
rabbi.  From the sky came a deep, peace-filled voice which stated 'The old 
one, truly, is right'...

... and the sky clouded over and there was silence.

... and in this silence the 'leader' of the younger rabbis said 'But 
*still*...!'

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] An Olde Joke

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Z8qdnRy-OvXHCSOjXTWJjg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<b9lprk$as$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b9lprk$as$1@panix1.panix.com...
>
> ... told me, long ago, by one with greater familiarity about such things
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> *still*...!'

---
On the lower East Side, an elderly Jew is at prayers:

"Baruch HaShem, Master of the Universe, all my life I've been a good Jew.
I've raised my children to be good Jews. I attend shule, I keep the
commandments. I've never asked anything for myself. Now I'm asking. In
return for a lifetime of obedience in your service, I want one little thing:
I want I should win the New York state lottery. I know you can fix it.
Amen."

The next day.

"Baruch HaShem, Master of the Universe. I didn't win! Maybe you forgot...."

This sequence repeats for several days, the demands becoming more intense.
Finally:

"Baruch HaShem, Master of the Universe. I'm tired of waiting. You can fix it
so I win the lottery. I want you to do it now! I insist!!!"

The room darkens. A slow, sibilant, low voice is heard:

"Moshe. Don't you think, first, you should buy a ticket?"

----
Jewish jokes are great. They deal a lot with food, relationships
(particularly with God), and poke fun at their own stereotypes.

Marvin and Benjamin meet at their high-school reunion.

Marvin: "So, how's things?"
Ben: "Last year we had a fire at my furniture store. But, Lord be praised,
we had insurance. Everything is OK now. And you?"
Marv: "As you know, I moved to Florida. Last year we had a hurricane and it
wiped out my wholesale kitchen utensil business. But, Lord be praised, we
had insurance. Everything is OK now."
...
Ben: "So, tell me, how do you have a hurricane?"
```

Perhaps the most important thing is to have the ability to be able to laugh at
oneself. Although it appears inherited, just my guess, that a large amount of
humour originates as a diversion from mild to to large degrees of oppression.
Perhaps with Jewish jokes, czarist Russia and the pogroms ? Ireland - well what
can I tell youse. England - by far and away the majority of comedians are from
the industrial north -
the wise cracks being an outlet against the miserable image of 'dark Satanic
Mills' and the Depression.

In my part of north east London when I was a kid, Islington and Finsbury Park,
there was a Yiddish theatre that produced plays in Yiddish. No doubt not too
many Jews would want to visit Finsbury Park right now - with the mad mullah in
the mosque spewing hate against anything that is not Islam.

All humours come in flavours, including Jewish. Don't much care for the Don
Rickles approach, which is 'cruel' as well as abrasive. My favourite quote is
from
George Burns. "When we sit down to dinner as a family, I'm the only one that
wears a hat". His beloved Gracie and the kids were catholics <G>

Pete's heard and seen this one before. It was from a 30-second TV skit going way
back (perhaps 'Laugh-In', I don't remember) :-

Our hero trapped in an enclosed  room is about to be attacked by Count Dracula,
(played by Marty Feldman - he of the huge hard-boiled egg eyes). Our hero looks
around desperately and grabs a crucifix for protection.

Says Count Dracula, "Oi Vey! 'Ave I got a supriseeeee for yew !".

Jimmy

<a id="t-d331f547c6"></a>
## Thread: CICS, CALL and other statements — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### CICS, CALL and other statements

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-11T15:22:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9mbeg$320$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
A new thread with "truth" vs "rumor" (or memory)

1) All releases of COBOL since VS COBOL II, R1 (c. 1984) have FULLY
supported both dynamic and static CALL statements in COBOL programs run
under CICS.

2)Before that, the documentation indicated that some CALLs were
unsupported - and there was no documented way to CALL a subprogram that
issued EXEC CICS statements.

3) With VS COBOL II (mid-80's - almost 20 years ago), IBM introduced a
"separate" run-time system for some (not all) COBOL functions.  This
continues thru today with LE.

4) For at least the last 10 years, IBM has documented the advantages (in
SOME situations) of using CALL statements over EXEC CICS LINK statements.
On the other hand, EXEC CICS LINK statements are *still* required for some
ILC (Inter-language Communication) situations under CICS.

5) With OS/VS COBOL (and earlier), there were several COBOL statements that
were required O/S versus CICS managed "getmains"  (e.g. INSPECT).  With VS
COBOL II (again mid-80's) IBM provided run-time routines that used
CICS-appropriate run-time support for these COBOL statements - and they have
been fully supported for almost 20 years now.

6)  No release of "mainframe" (MVS, VSE, OS/390, z/OS) CICS has supported
"traditional" COBOL access to files, e.g. Select/Assign, OPEN, CLOSE, READ,
WRITE, REWRITE.  In stead, there are CICS provided facilities that *MUST* be
used to perform such operations on any type of mainframe file (as opposed to
database - Use of EXEC SQL, EXEC DLI, CALL "CBLTDLI", etc provide database
access)

7) As a *minor* exception to the statement above, IBM provided (since the
late 80's) an interface to the COBOL *SORT* statement.  IBM, itself, does
*not* provide any support for SORT under CICS - but does document the
run-time interface that may be provided by 3rd party vendors or in-house.
Also, for about the last 3-5 years (I don't remember the exact release), IBM
added support for the COBOL DISPLAY statement - which uses CICS services to
send output to an appropriate CICS resource.

    ***

From an historical point of view, I know of several shops that violated the
DOCUMENTED restrictions on using CALL statements or O/S-getmain-related
statements with OS/VS COBOL or DOS/VS COBOL and CICS.  *ALL* such sites
(that I know of) had SERIOUS migration problems when moving to either the VS
COBOL II or LE run-time environment where such violations of the documented
restrictions lead to run-time problems or other serious side effects.

And finally, as far as I know (and I certainly could be mistaken on this),
CICS is *still* one of the best performing and most heavily used
"transaction processing" systems in use today.  With its current interfaces
to the web, ATM and similar terminals, and a variety of other I/O systems,
its power and robustness is not questioned by either its supporters or
detractors.
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS, CALL and other statements

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T13:17:22+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebef607_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b9mbeg$320$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b9mbeg$320$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...

<snipped interesting and succinct summary of some CICS history>

> And finally, as far as I know (and I certainly could be mistaken on this),
> CICS is *still* one of the best performing and most heavily used
> "transaction processing" systems in use today.  With its current
interfaces
> to the web, ATM and similar terminals, and a variety of other I/O systems,
> its power and robustness is not questioned by either its supporters or
> detractors.
>

I have a few observations on CICS. (I feel qualified to comment, having used
it (only on mainframes) for around 10 years.)

I also worked for IBM (on contract) at Hursley park (where CICS was
invented) and had coffee with one of the original team that wrote it...does
that count <G>?

1. CICS is a VERY stable Transaction Processor.

2. It is NOT the best transaction processor that has ever been available,
but it is certainly one of the oldest still available.

3. IMS/DC is/was a better transaction processor but it lost popularity due
to its unfortunate association with IBM's hierarchical database offering.
(Sadly, IMS was also a perfectly good database system but the superiority of
Relational over Hierarchic has gradually established itself...there are
still many fierce debates in pubs about this, though; I was in one recently
<G>)

The fundamental requirement for ANY transaction processor is the ability to
store "terminal dependent " data across dequeue boundaries. In other words,
the system can carry on processing other threads while a user is thinking or
entering data at his terminal (which is said to be "dequeued"). When he hits
Enter, the thread "reawakens" and obtains the previously stored data from
that particular terminal. In this way, the "conversation" continues as a
logical chronistic stream. (The difference between the way that CICS
achieves this and IMS/DC achieves it, is the basis for my statement that I
prefer IMS/DC above. It is also the basis for the terms
"pseudo-conversational" and "conversational" when it comes to discussing
these things, but as these same terms have different meaning to an IMS or a
CICS programmer, it causes more arguments than it resolves...<G>)

Anyway, for around 20 years I worked in both these environments and came to
know and understand them fairly well.

Then I moved to Client Server and am currently involved in constructing a
Web Portal that also has to manage multiple threads across multiple
"terminals" (actually, individual Browsers on individual clients). I was
pleased to find that, as expected in the Object Oriented world of modern
programming, there is something called a Session Object that I can use to
store "terminal dependent" data in, just like you would with CICS DFHCOMM
area or IMS/DC's SPA. (I am using ASP (amongst other things...JavaScript,
HTML, and COBOL for the CGI/ASAPI code) and this object is available in this
environment).

I was pretty excited, as to me, this represented "familiarity" with what I
would expect. I gratefully started stashing various variables in the Session
Object.

To my horror, I found that this object was unstable! As soon as I ran a
COBOL CGI program the Session Object was reset, as the system considered it
was now in another session...<G>! I frantically phoned a few people who know
a lot more about this environment than I do (and are not blinkered by having
worked for decades on mainframes <G>) and they made noises like the
mechanics do when you take your car in for what you hope will be a standard
service, but there is much groaning and chin rubbing and a general feeling
of despondency... Well...yes, we've had problems with the stability of the
Session Object (even though we're not using much CGI/ASAPI and certainly not
COBOL)...it's a bit of a problem sometimes...MicroSoft have some advice,
here's a URL...and a NewsGroup... (I checked out the newsgroup and found
many posts complaining about "lost" Session Objects. MS solution was to use
cookies...(not everyone accepts cookies so this is not an ideal
solution...)).

By now I was devastated, but resolved to solve the problem myself. So I
wrote a version of "CICS" that runs on the WEB in a client server
environment and is stable...(Well, of course, I didn't REALLY write CICS,
but I built a mechanism that works the same way CICS does and appears to be
stable. It certainly ensures that threads running on the target Web Server
do NOT lose data across dequeue boundaries...<G>) And it DOESN'T require the
use of client cookies (which is one possible solution to the problem), or
storing stuff directly in TCP/IP Headers (which is another solution to the
problem).

Then a couple of days ago I made an architecture change and needed to
regenerate some nested CGI programs with the Fujitsu Web Software. Not being
particularly stressed, I took my time and noticed some check boxes I had
never used before... One of them said "Clear session object..." It was the
default...<G>

The Moral of this story is that sometimes under pressure it is easy to miss
something that may save you a HUGE amount of heartache later. When you are
using complex tools, it is worth investing some time in getting to know them
THOROUGHLY before rushing to war with them...<G>

The NEXT Web Portal I do might use the Session Object; this one will use my
"CICS simulator". It hasn't been tested under fire yet, but in simulation it
behaves very well.

So, there is a place for CICS knowledge even in this day and age...<G>

I agree that CICS will be with us for some time yet, Bill.

Pete.

http://www.newsfeed.com The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World! >100,000 Newsgroups
---= 19 East/West-Coast Specialized Servers - Total Privacy via Encryption =---
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CICS, CALL and other statements

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T02:10:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c3182b$6fc13820$7593f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<b9mbeg$320$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3ebef607_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
Each CICS region runs under it own task control block (TCB) and CICS
provides multitasking for the transaction ids being executed.  The reason
some COBOL facilities were not available under CICS is because they issued
a supervisor call (SVC).  This SVC was a direct request to the operating
system for services, bypassing CICS, and in fact it would cause the entire
CICS region to go into a wait state until the SVC request was complete.
This was extremely NOT GOOD!  Well at least it used to be that way, today
the preceding would be considered somewhat of a simlpification.  In time
some of these restrictions have relaxed, and now we have terminal owing
regions (TORs), appication owning regions (AORs), function shipping etc.

Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in article
<3ebef607_2@127.0.0.1>...

systems,

I

> "pseudo-conversational" and "conversational" when it comes to discussing
> these things, but as these same terms have different meaning to an IMS or
a
> CICS programmer, it causes more arguments than it resolves...<G>)
> 
> Anyway, for around 20 years I worked in both these environments and came
to
> know and understand them fairly well.
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> HTML, and COBOL for the CGI/ASAPI code) and this object is available in
this
> environment).
> 
> I was pretty excited, as to me, this represented "familiarity" with what
I
> would expect. I gratefully started stashing various variables in the
Session
> Object.
> 
> To my horror, I found that this object was unstable! As soon as I ran a
> COBOL CGI program the Session Object was reset, as the system considered
it
> was now in another session...<G>! I frantically phoned a few people who
know
> a lot more about this environment than I do (and are not blinkered by
having
> worked for decades on mainframes <G>) and they made noises like the
> mechanics do when you take your car in for what you hope will be a
standard
> service, but there is much groaning and chin rubbing and a general
feeling
> of despondency... Well...yes, we've had problems with the stability of
the
> Session Object (even though we're not using much CGI/ASAPI and certainly
not
> COBOL)...it's a bit of a problem sometimes...MicroSoft have some advice,
> here's a URL...and a NewsGroup... (I checked out the newsgroup and found
> many posts complaining about "lost" Session Objects. MS solution was to
use
> cookies...(not everyone accepts cookies so this is not an ideal
> solution...)).
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> but I built a mechanism that works the same way CICS does and appears to
be
> stable. It certainly ensures that threads running on the target Web
Server
> do NOT lose data across dequeue boundaries...<G>) And it DOESN'T require
the
> use of client cookies (which is one possible solution to the problem), or
> storing stuff directly in TCP/IP Headers (which is another solution to
the
> problem).
> 
> Then a couple of days ago I made an architecture change and needed to
> regenerate some nested CGI programs with the Fujitsu Web Software. Not
being
> particularly stressed, I took my time and noticed some check boxes I had
> never used before... One of them said "Clear session object..." It was
the
> default...<G>
> 
> The Moral of this story is that sometimes under pressure it is easy to
miss
> something that may save you a HUGE amount of heartache later. When you
are
> using complex tools, it is worth investing some time in getting to know
them
> THOROUGHLY before rushing to war with them...<G>
> 
> The NEXT Web Portal I do might use the Session Object; this one will use
my
> "CICS simulator". It hasn't been tested under fire yet, but in simulation
it
> behaves very well.
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

In article <3ebef607_2@127.0.0.1>,
Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>(The difference between the way that CICS
>achieves this and IMS/DC achieves it, is the basis for my statement that I
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>CICS programmer, it causes more arguments than it resolves...<G>)

During an interview I was asked Standard CICS Question #37, 'What is 
psuedoconversational mode?'

I responded 'Psuedoconversation is what I have about the weather with 
strangers at a truck-stop...

... no, it is the technique used for decreasing use of system resources by
releasing a thread used by a transaction to allow, usually, for user
interaction at a terminal...'

I got the contract.

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CICS, CALL and other statements

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T16:49:31+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9n98k$p3n$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b9mbeg$320$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3ebef607_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

> (I checked out the newsgroup and found many posts complaining about "lost"
> Session Objects. MS solution was to use cookies...(not everyone accepts
> cookies so this is not an ideal solution...)).

There are very many problems with forms and CGI.  With Cookies at least the 
latest is sent, when an updated Cookie is sent to the client you can be 
sures that an old one will not reappear, they also expire.  Putting hidden 
fields in the form is also a partial solution but what happens when the 
user does a few 'back's and resends an old form ?

I use both Cookies and hidden fields and generate a new hidden code for 
each new form (where appropriate) and store these in a session database. 
When a hidden code is 'used up' by the form returning and the data being 
processed correctly, I delete that hidden code so that a resending of the 
same form can be rejected as 'already processed'.  This saves duplication.

I suspect CICS wouldn't have this problem as the screens get overwritten 
and a user cannot resend an old one.

Modern browers cater for having lists of sites where Cookies are allowed or 
disallowed.  This can be built up by having an 'ask' flag set.  My own 
browsing is done with Cookies off but certain sites are set to allow them.
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS, CALL and other statements

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T02:19:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebeef71.291171143@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b9mbeg$320$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>A new thread with "truth" vs "rumor" (or memory)
>
>1) All releases of COBOL since VS COBOL II, R1 (c. 1984) have FULLY
>supported both dynamic and static CALL statements in COBOL programs run
>under CICS.

Static is a no-brainer, CICS has always supported it. I was unaware they
supported dynamic since 1984. I worked on a VSE 'mainframe' at the time and saw
no mention of it in that realm. 

>2)Before that, the documentation indicated that some CALLs were
>unsupported - and there was no documented way to CALL a subprogram that
>issued EXEC CICS statements.

Sounds vaguely familiar. 

>3) With VS COBOL II (mid-80's - almost 20 years ago), IBM introduced a
>"separate" run-time system for some (not all) COBOL functions.  This
>continues thru today with LE.

You mentioned that in an earlier post. IBM manuals do not make it clear that
you're invoking a 'CICS function'. It looks like you're calling an OS function.
>
>4) For at least the last 10 years, IBM has documented the advantages (in
>SOME situations) of using CALL statements over EXEC CICS LINK statements.
>On the other hand, EXEC CICS LINK statements are *still* required for some
>ILC (Inter-language Communication) situations under CICS.

Some situations would be those where you care about speed. If you're going to
call the thing once only, LINK is fine. 

>6)  No release of "mainframe" (MVS, VSE, OS/390, z/OS) CICS has supported
>"traditional" COBOL access to files, e.g. Select/Assign, OPEN, CLOSE, READ,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>access)

You mean temporary storage queues. Ok. 

>From an historical point of view, I know of several shops that violated the
>DOCUMENTED restrictions on using CALL statements or O/S-getmain-related
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>restrictions lead to run-time problems or other serious side effects.

I know of one shop that saved $4M/yr by volating the restrictions. For six
years. After I left, its IT budget went from $1M to $7M and its response time
went to hell. 

They didn't have serious problems migrating to MVS, they just unplugged my tool
and everything reverted back to IBM standard. 

>And finally, as far as I know (and I certainly could be mistaken on this),
>CICS is *still* one of the best performing and most heavily used
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>detractors.

Compared to what? 

I don't see many Web sites 'powered' by CICS.
```

-Paul

#### ↳ Re: CICS, CALL and other statements

100% Spot on.

The single most irritating problem with CICS is solely the cost of running
in on a mainframe. Running it under AIX or even Winders is a lot cheaper,
but you loose an awful lot.

BTW: There is no CICS offering anymore, the current version is called
Transaction Server.

-Paul

<a id="t-c0ae58ad00"></a>
## Thread: Attention COBOL Newbies ... — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Attention COBOL Newbies ...

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T18:11:53+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ebf3b0c_1@127.0.0.1>`

```
Hi!

This is the major COBOL Newsgroup on Usenet.(there are a few others but they
don't get the traffic this one does.)

If you are learning COBOL or have just finished training and are looking for
examples, encouragement and help, it is a good place to lurk. (Read the
"rules" in the COBOL FAQ before you ask for help with homework...)

There are highly experienced people here who collectively know just about
everything there is to know about COBOL.

However, there are a few pitfalls also.

The Newsgroup, as well as being a place for exploring technical issues, is
also a place where people who share an interest in COBOL will seek some
light intellectual relief by opening discussions that are way off topic
(however, they are often interesting and amusing). Be prepared to find stuff
that has only a tenuous relationship with COBOL.(These threads are supposed
to be prefixed with "OT" (off topic) but sometimes we forget...<G>)

Like all groups of people, there is a spectrum of personalities here.

Before you start, here is some advice:

1. If you read or see anything here that conflicts with what your tutor is
telling you, show it to him and get his arguments.

2. Do NOT be "bullied" by people here into a certain style of coding. The
ONLY style of coding that is acceptable while you are training, is the one
being taught to you. Once you have finished the course, and written a number
of COBOL programs, make up your own mind about questions of style. There are
MANY ways to write COBOL; they are not "right" or "wrong" (provided the
program does what it is supposed to, consistently) but they ARE a matter of
personal preference.
Many factors will influence your personal "style" as it develops;
(installation standards, methods of formatting code so it is easier to read,
consistent ways to use complex constructs within the language, are just a
few) and your style will change as you become a mature professional.

3. Don't be afraid to contribute and, if you see something that puzzles you,
ask about it.

Finally, this is an unmoderated forum. People can post whatever they like
(even if it exceeds good taste...). If you get "flamed", don't respond. If
you see stuff that upsets you, ignore it. Remember you are NOT required to
respond to anything posted here (although sometimes it is very hard not
to...<G>)

Above all, think for yourself and make your own decisions about what "works"
for you.

Enjoy!

Pete.





----== Posted via Newsfeed.Com - Unlimited-Uncensored-Secure Usenet News==----
http://www.newsfeed.com The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World! >100,000 Newsgroups
---= 19 East/West-Coast Specialized Servers - Total Privacy via Encryption =---
```

## Thread: OT (?) - COBOL and Linguistics — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT (?) - COBOL and Linguistics

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T11:00:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9ogff$5bq$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
This morning, I realized that I have replied to several "linguistics-only"
threads withOUT prefixing the subject line with "OT". (Thanks, Pete - for
your note to "Newbies" that reminded me of this.)

I thought that I would post this note for a few reasons.

- My undergraduate degree (too many years ago to think about) was in
Linguistics and French.  Therefore, my "passion" for linguistics pre-dates
my "passion" for COBOL.

- When I see notes with "false" or "misleading" or "misinformed" statements
about *NATURAL* languages, I usually want to "jump in" and provide
corrections or clarifications (just as I do with such statements about
COBOL).

- Anyone with a modicum of knowledge of linguistics knows that there are
SIGNIFICANT differences between:
     - "natural" and "artificial" languages
     - "spoken" and "written" languages
     - "structural" and "historical" linguistics
     - "semantics" and "syntax"

- The "modern" theories of linguistics (of "natural languages") has been
significantly impacted by computers (e.g. attempts for computerized
translations of both written and spoken languages, statistics, etc).
Similarly, modern "artificial languages" (such as computer programming
languages) have been influenced by theoretical linguistics.  The fact that
the two disciplines have impacted each other does NOT (IMHO) mean that they
should be "confused". What is true for one is not necessarily true (nor is
it necessarily false) for the other.

    ***

Again, sorry for not indicating "OT" for linguistic-only posts to the NG.  I
will probably continue to "jump in" on linguistic threads (as such really DO
interest me) - but I will try and remember to make certain that they are
noted as Off-Topic for those who want to skip them.
```

> ...
> I am unaware of any rules or even general principles for artificial
languages.
> Their grammar seems to be created ad hoc by technicians, later documented
a
> posteriori. COBOL stands above the others in its concern for consistency.

No Sir. There are some other languages which *also* have a high internal
consistency. Ada, for instance. However, 100 % Consistency you will
find nowhere with man-made things, and flame-wars about which of
the 99 % consistency languages are most consistent lead to nowhere.

On a second thought: The Turing-machine is 100 % consistent. But who
wants to program with that?

Herwig

#### ↳ Re: Utility to convert assembler record layout to Cobol

- **From:** Steve Burns <"stephen.d.burns2 <at> boeing.com">
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T19:05:21+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<HEsFox.7uD@news.boeing.com>`

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Utility to convert assembler record layout to Cobol

- **From:** temp4339@msn.com (Bob)
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T06:34:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ea2dfd4.0305130534.21cd763b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5ea2dfd4.0305120812.23f44d77@posting.google.com> <01c318da$cce36e60$d5caf943@chottel>`

```
This is a one time thing. The whole record layout is 675 lines long. A
part of it is below:

EXTRCD   DSECT ,                   USER EXTRACT RECORD        
V0410003
EXTLEN   DS    XL2                 LENGTH OF THIS RECORD
         DS    XL2                 UNUSED
EXTTYPE  DS    X                   01 = MASTER EXTRACT ONLY
*                                  04 = SCHEDULE RECORD ALSO PRESENT
         DS    XL3                 ... RESERVED
*
ESEXTRCT EQU   *                   SCHEDULE RECORD EXTRACT PORTION
ESEEVTNO DS    F                   EVENT NUMBER
ESEFLAG1 DS    BL1                 FLAG BYTE
* THIS FLAG BYTE COMES FROM THE EMR.
*        EQU                       X'80' = EMR FLAG BIT       
V0400004
ESEHAVOK EQU                       X'40' = OPERATOR HAS OK'D DISPATCH
ESENOTFY EQU                       X'20' = NOTIFY OPERATOR OF DISPATCH
ESEGETOK EQU                       X'10' = GET OPER OK TO DISPATCH
...
ESESDATE DS    PL4                 SCHEDULE DATE FOR THIS SQ RE
ESERDATE DS    PL4                 REAL DATE RECORD CREATED    
ESEDDATE DS    PL4                 LAST DISPATCH DATE          
ESEDTIME DS    XL4                 DISPATCH TIME               
ESESTIME DS    H                   STATUS TIME                 
ESETIME1 DS    H                   EARLY DISPATCH TIME         
ESETIME2 DS    H                   NORMAL DISPATCH TIME        
ESETIME3 DS    H                   LATE DISPATCH TIME - URGENT 
ESETIME4 DS    H                   MUST END TIME               
ESETIME5 DS    H                   NOT AFTER TIME              
ESETYPE  DS    B                   EVENT TYPE 01/02/03/04      
ESETAPES DS    B                   TAPE DRIVES REQUIRED        
...
         DS    0X                  - SCHEDULE VIEW FIELDS      
ESEJCLRB DS    F                   RBN TO JCL OVERRIDE         
ESEOJOBN DS    CL8                 JOB NAME FROM SJB           
ESEOUSRI DS    CL8                 USER ID FROM SJB            
ESEOJKEY DS    XL4                 JOB KEY FROM SJB            
ESEOIOT  DS    XL4                 MTTR OF IOT FROM IOT        
ESEOASID DS    XL2                 ASID FROM SJB               
ESERTIME DS    XL2                 DISPATCH TIME - USED BY 02M 
         DS    XL12                SOME SLACK                  
*                                                              
ESERCDL  EQU   *-ESEXTRCT                                      
         SPACE 3                                               
EXTWHEN  DS    CL(SEGMXEWH)        WHEN CLAUSE SEGMXEWH=1360   
         SPACE 3                                               
EMEXTRCT DS    0F                  EVENT MASTER RECORD EXTRACT 
EMERECL  DS    XL4                 STORAGE NEEDED FOR RECORD   
EMEEVENT DS    F                   EVENT NUMBER                
EMEETYPE DS    B                   TYPE OF EVENT RECORD        
...
...
***********************************************************    
******      EVENT MASTER RECORD - JOB EVENTS      *********    
***********************************************************    
         SPACE 3                                               
*                                                              
* RECORD TYPE X'01' - JOB RECORD                               
*                                                              
EX1RCD   DS    0F                 MINIMUM LENGTH=41 BYTES MAX=1
EX1JOB   DS    CL8                 DOS JOB NAME FOR THIS EVENT 
EX1FLAG1 DS    B                   FLG BYTE                    
EX1SLI   EQU                       X'00' = SLI BOOK            
EX1ICCF  EQU                       X'01' = EX1SNAME IS ICCF NAM
EX1LIBR  EQU                       X'02' = EX1SNAME IS LIBRARIA
EX1OWL   EQU                       X'03' = OWL LIBRARY MEMBER  
...
***********************************************************    
****** EVENT MASTER RECORD -  SYSTEM COMMANDS     *********    
***********************************************************    
         SPACE 3                                               
         ORG   EX1RCD                                          
EX7RCD   DS    0C                  SCOM EVENT (SYSTEM COMMANDS)
EX7ITMCT DS    XL2                 NUMBER OF SCOM ENTRIES      
EX7LEN1  EQU   *-EX7RCD                                        
EX7START DS    0X                  START OF COMMAND ENTRIES    
*  OCCURS EX7ITMCT TIMES                                       
EX7DSECT DSECT ,                                               
EX7TYPE  DS    X                   TYPE OF COMMAND             
EX7PCOM  EQU   1                   POWER COMMAND               
EX7ZCOM  EQU   2                   ZEKE COMMAND                
EX7VCOM  EQU   3                   VM COMMAND                  
EX7SCOM  EQU   4                   SYSTEM COMMAND              
EX7SRESP EQU   5                   SYSTEM RESPONSE COMMAND     
EX7ZACK  EQU   6                   ZACK COMMAND                
EX7TEXT  DS    CL60                COMMAND TEXT                
EX7LENG  EQU   *-EX7DSECT          LENGTH OF COMMAND ENTRY     

Thanks, Bob

#### ↳ Re: Utility to convert assembler record layout to Cobol

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T09:25:46-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-217CAC.09254613052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<5ea2dfd4.0305120812.23f44d77@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <5ea2dfd4.0305120812.23f44d77@posting.google.com>,
 temp4339@msn.com (Bob) wrote:

> My boss has asked me to look for a utility to convert an Assembler
> record layout to a Cobol layout. I know some rudimentary assembler but
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks, Bob


IIF the assembler layout is very well defined, that is, actual types are 
used instead of "C" for everything.  And the duplication factors and all 
line up.  Then you can do this with the macro preprocessor.

Set up a simple assembler shell, copy in the layout, and repeatedly call 
a macro with each field in order. 

Write the macro, perhaps EMITCBFL, to use the various symbolic attribute 
checks like, for example the type is T'fldname.  I believe that all the 
information you need is available for each field, just format up a text 
line and punch it.

Hope this helps
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Utility to convert assembler record layout to Cobol

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T21:19:01+03:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7kd2cvga7uq6qur0t8vkco0vv0jg0qcal8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5ea2dfd4.0305120812.23f44d77@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-217CAC.09254613052003@corp.supernews.com> <5ea2dfd4.0305131013.21d9c159@posting.google.com>`

```
On 13 May 2003 11:13:31 -0700 temp4339@msn.com (Bob) wrote:

:>>Binyamin Dissen <bdissen@dissensoftware.com> wrote

:>>If you have the skill sets available to convert the assembler program
:>that
:>>processes this record, the conversion of the data record is trivial.
:>>
:>>And if you do not, what is the point?

:>We don't have the skill set or the program. We were given the record
:>layout from a third party product as a way to get information that
:>they do not provide in a report format. We wanted to use this to get
:>the information and come up with a report on our own.

PLn = PIC S9(n*2-1) COMP-3.

H = PIC S9(4) COMP.

F = PIC S9(9) COMP.

CLn = PIC X(n).

XL(whatever) is hard to say. Some appear to be flags, some appear to be
numerics.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Utility to convert assembler record layout to Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T02:28:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c31a89$659967e0$088bf243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<5ea2dfd4.0305120812.23f44d77@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-217CAC.09254613052003@corp.supernews.com> <5ea2dfd4.0305131013.21d9c159@posting.google.com> <7kd2cvga7uq6qur0t8vkco0vv0jg0qcal8@4ax.com>`

```
You may have to make some changes to this depending upon how
you see the fields being used. Some fields defined with signs
may not need to be treated as signed fields. I hope this helps you
get started.

Some working storage fields are fields referred to but which had
no definition in your post. Others are lengths of various records
that your COBOL program may not need.

Addressing fields in assembler is done using a base register and
displacement method. Each field in a DSECT has a known displacement
from the start of the DSECT. The base register associated with a
DSECT is specified via a USING directive to the assembler.

A DSECT in assembler is simply a map of storage and is associated
with a USING that specifies a register. For example USING EX7DSECT,2
would associate register two with the DSECT. When fields in the DSECT
are referenced they would then be assembled using register two as a
base register until another USING for the DSECT is encountered.
Therefore by placing a address in register 2 you can make the DSECT
reference or map storage starting at that address.

In COBOL a DSECT is equivalent to a definition in the LINKAGE-SECTION.
Suppose you had the following field defined:

       01  R2-PTR      USAGE IS POINTER.

and further suppose that it contains the starting address from which
you want the EX7DSECT to start mapping from. You can accomplish this
with:

           SET ADDRESS OF EX7DSECT TO R2-PTR.



       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  EX7ITMCT                               PIC 9(04).
       01  SEGMXEWH                               PIC 9(04).
       01  ESERCDL                                PIC 9(04).
       01  EX7LEN1                                PIC 9(04).
       01  EX7LENG                                PIC 9(04).
       LINKAGE-SECTION.
       01  EXTRCD-USER-EXTRACT-RECORD.
           05  EXTLEN-EXTRACT-LENGTH              PIC S9(04) COMP.
           05  FILLER                             PIC S9(04) COMP.
           05  EXTTYPE-EXTRACT-TYPE               PIC X.
               88  MASTER-EXTRACT-ONLY            VALUE X'01'.
               88  SCHEDULE-RECORD-PRESENT        VALUE X'04'.
           05  FILLER                             PIC X(03).
      *
           05  ESEXTRCT-SCHEDULE-REC-EXTRACT.
               07  ESEEVTNO-EVENT-NUMBER          PIC S9(09) COMP.
               07  ESEFLAG1-FLAG-BYTE             PIC X.
                   88  EMR-FLAG-BIT               VALUE X'80'.
                   88  ESEHAVOK-OPER-OK-DIS       VALUE X'40'.
                   88  ESENOTFY-NOTIFY-OPER-DISP  VALUE X'20'.
                   88  ESEGETOK-GET-OPER-OK-DISP  VALUE X'10'.
               07  ESESDATE-SCHEDULE-DATE-THIS-SQ PIC S9(07) COMP-3.
               07  ESERDATE-REC-CREATE-DATE       PIC S9(07) COMP-3.
               07  ESEDDATE-LAST-DISPATCH-DATE    PIC S9(07) COMP-3.
               07  ESEDTIME-DISPATCH-TIME         PIC S9(04) COMP.
               07  ESESTIME-STATUS-TIME           PIC S9(04) COMP.
               07  ESETIME1-EARLY-DISPATCH-TIME   PIC S9(04) COMP.
               07  ESETIME2-NORMAL-DISPATCH-TIME  PIC S9(04) COMP.
               07  ESETIME3-LATE-DISPATCH-TIME    PIC S9(04) COMP.
               07  ESETIME4-MUST-END-TIME         PIC S9(04) COMP.
               07  ESETIME5-NOT-AFTER-TIME        PIC S9(04) COMP.
               07  ESETYPE-EVENT-TYPE             PIC X.
                   88  ESETYPE-EVENT-TYPE-01      VALUE X'01'.
                   88  ESETYPE-EVENT-TYPE-02      VALUE X'02'.
                   88  ESETYPE-EVENT-TYPE-03      VALUE X'03'.
                   88  ESETYPE-EVENT-TYPE-04      VALUE X'04'.
               07  ESETAPES-TAPE-DRIVES-REQUIRED  PIC X.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SCHEDULE VIEW FIELDS                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           07  ESEJCLRB-RBN-TO-JCL-OVERRIDE       PIC S9(09) COMP.
               07  ESEOJOBN-JOB-NAME-FROM-SJB     PIC X(08).
               07  ESEOUSRI-USERID-FROM-SJB       PIC X(08).
               07  ESEOJKEY-JOB-KEY-FROM-SJB      PIC X(04).
               07  ESEOIOT-MTTR-OF-IO-FROM IOT    PIC X(04).
               07  ESEOASID-ASID-FROM-SJB         PIC X(02).
               07  ESERTIME-DISP-TIME-USEDBY-02M  PIC X(02).
               07  FILLER                         PIC X(12).

I am unsure how to interpret EXTWHEN so I am including one
definition if it is a fixed length and another (commented out)
if it is a variable length.


       01  EXTWHEN                                PIC X(1360).
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *01  EXTWHEN.                                                   *
      *    05  FILLER                             PIC X               *
      *            OCCURS 1 TO 1360 TIMES DEPENDING ON SEGMXEWH.      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           05  EMEXTRCT-EVENT-MASTER-REC-EXTRACT.
               07  EMERECL-STORAGE-NEEDED         PIC S9(09) COMP.
               07  EMEEVENT-EVENT-NUMBER          PIC S9(09) COMP.
               07  EMEETYPE-TYPE-OF-EVENT-RECORD  PIC X.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *     EVENT MASTER RECORD - JOB EVENTS                          *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *     RECORD TYPE X'01' - JOB RECORD                            *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  EX1RCD.
           05  EX1JOB-DOS-JOB-NAME-THIS-EVENT     PIC X(08).
           05  EX1FLAG1-FLAG-BYTE                 PIC X.
               88  EX1SLI-SLI-BOOK                VALUE X'00'.
               88  EX1ICCF-EX1SNAME-IS-ICCF-NAME  VALUE X'01'.
               88  EX1LIBR-EX1SNAME-IS-LIBRARIA   VALUE X'02'.
               88  EX1OWL-OWL-LIBRARY-MEMBER      VALUE X'03'.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *     EVENT MASTER RECORD -  SYSTEM COMMANDS                    *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  EX7RCD REDFINES EX1RCD.
           05  EX7ITMCT-NUMBER-SCOM-ENTRIES       PIC X(02).
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *     START OF COMMAND ENTRIES                                  *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           05  EX7START.
               07  EX7DSECT OCCURS 1 TO ???? TIMES
                            DEPENDING ON EX7ITMCT.
                   09  EX7TYPE-TYPE-OF-COMMAND    PIC X(01).
                   88  EX7PCOM-POWER-COMMAND      VALUE X'01'.
                   88  EX7ZCOM-ZEKE-COMMAND       VALUE X'02'.
                   88  EX7VCOM-VM-COMMAND         VALUE X'03'.
                   88  EX7SCOM-SYSTEM-COMMAND     VALUE X'04'.
                   88  EX7SRESP-SYS-RESPONSE-CMD  VALUE X'05'.
                   88  EX7ZACK-ZACK-COMMAND       VALUE X'06'.
               07  EX7TEXT-COMMAND-TEXTPIC X(60).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           MOVE LENGTH OF ESEXTRCT-SCHEDULE-REC-EXTRACT TO ESERCDL.
           MOVE LENGTH OF EX7RCD TO EX7LEN.
           MOVE LENGTH OF EX7DSECT TO EX7LEN.


<7kd2cvga7uq6qur0t8vkco0vv0jg0qcal8@4ax.com>...
> On 13 May 2003 11:13:31 -0700 temp4339@msn.com (Bob) wrote:
> 
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Utility to convert assembler record layout to Cobol

- **From:** joe_zitzelberger@vsam.org (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T10:46:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c99d007f.0305140946.2ad16a3a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5ea2dfd4.0305120812.23f44d77@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-217CAC.09254613052003@corp.supernews.com> <5ea2dfd4.0305131013.21d9c159@posting.google.com>`

```
I hope this helps some.  In several cases, the fields are bit switches
-- there is no nice way to do this in plain Cobol, LE offers services
for it though.

If all you have is the one copybook and no assembler programmers then
coding up a general purpose tool is way overkill.  Just hand converting
the one will be easier.


01  EXTRCD.

*EXTLEN   DS    XL2                 LENGTH OF THIS RECORD
*         DS    XL2                 UNUSED
*EXTTYPE  DS    X                   01 = MASTER EXTRACT ONLY
*         DS    XL3                 ... RESERVED

	05  EXTLEN          Binary PIC S9(4).
	05                  Binary PIC S9(4).
	05  EXTTYPE                PIC X.
*                                  04 = SCHEDULE RECORD ALSO PRESENT
	05                         PIC X(3).
*
*
*ESEXTRCT EQU   *                   SCHEDULE RECORD EXTRACT PORTION
*ESEEVTNO DS    F                   EVENT NUMBER
*ESEFLAG1 DS    BL1                 FLAG BYTE
* THIS FLAG BYTE COMES FROM THE EMR.
*         EQU                       X'80' = EMR FLAG BIT       
*ESEHAVOK EQU                       X'40' = OPERATOR HAS OK'D DISPATCH
*ESENOTFY EQU                       X'20' = NOTIFY OPERATOR OF DISPATCH
*ESEGETOK EQU                       X'10' = GET OPER OK TO DISPATCH

    05  ESEXTRCT.
	  10  ESEEVTNO      Binary PIC S9(8).
	  10  ESEFLAG1             PIC X.
*...
*ESESDATE DS    PL4                 SCHEDULE DATE FOR THIS SQ RE
*ESERDATE DS    PL4                 REAL DATE RECORD CREATED    
*ESEDDATE DS    PL4                 LAST DISPATCH DATE          
*ESEDTIME DS    XL4                 DISPATCH TIME               
*ESESTIME DS    H                   STATUS TIME                 
*ESETIME1 DS    H                   EARLY DISPATCH TIME         
*ESETIME2 DS    H                   NORMAL DISPATCH TIME        
*ESETIME3 DS    H                   LATE DISPATCH TIME - URGENT 
*ESETIME4 DS    H                   MUST END TIME               
*ESETIME5 DS    H                   NOT AFTER TIME              
*ESETYPE  DS    B                   EVENT TYPE 01/02/03/04      
*ESETAPES DS    B                   TAPE DRIVES REQUIRED        

      10 ESESDATE          Packed-Decimal   PIC S9(7).
	  10 ESERDATE      Packed-Decimal   PIC S9(7).
	  10 ESEDDATE      Packed-Decimal   PIC S9(7).
	  10 ESEDTIME      Binary           PIC S9(8).
	  10 ESESTIME      Binary           PIC S9(4).
	  10 ESETIME1      Binary           PIC S9(4).
	  10 ESETIME2      Binary           PIC S9(4).
	  10 ESETIME3      Binary           PIC S9(4).
	  10 ESETIME4      Binary           PIC S9(4).
	  10 ESETIME5      Binary           PIC S9(4).
      10 ESETYPE                        PIC X.
	  10 ESETAPES                       PIC X.
	  
*...
*         DS    0X                  - SCHEDULE VIEW FIELDS      
*ESEJCLRB DS    F                   RBN TO JCL OVERRIDE         
*ESEOJOBN DS    CL8                 JOB NAME FROM SJB           
*ESEOUSRI DS    CL8                 USER ID FROM SJB            
*ESEOJKEY DS    XL4                 JOB KEY FROM SJB            
*ESEOIOT  DS    XL4                 MTTR OF IOT FROM IOT        
*ESEOASID DS    XL2                 ASID FROM SJB               
*ESERTIME DS    XL2                 DISPATCH TIME - USED BY 02M 
*         DS    XL12                SOME SLACK                  

      10  ESEJCLRB     Binary           PIC S9(8).
	  10  ESEOJOBN                      PIC X(8).
	  10  ESEOUSRI                      PIC X(8).
	  10  ESEOJKEY                      PIC X(4).
	  10  ESEOIOT                       PIC X(4).
	  10  ESEOASID                      PIC X(2).
	  10  ESERTIME                      PIC X(2).
	  10                                PIC X(12).

*                                                              
*ESERCDL  EQU   *-ESEXTRCT                                      

...This will be replaced with the length of ESEXTRCT


*EXTWHEN  DS    CL(SEGMXEWH)        WHEN CLAUSE SEGMXEWH=1360   

...No way to know what this means, what is SEGMXEWH???



*EMEXTRCT DS    0F                  EVENT MASTER RECORD EXTRACT 
*EMERECL  DS    XL4                 STORAGE NEEDED FOR RECORD   
*EMEEVENT DS    F                   EVENT NUMBER                
*EMEETYPE DS    B                   TYPE OF EVENT RECORD        
*...

* The sync might be a problem here, if it is, adjust with filler.

    05  EMEXTRCT         SYNC.
	  10  EMERECL        Binary PIC S9(8).
	  10  EMEEVENT       Binary PIC S9(8).
	  10  EMEETYPE              PIC X.


***********************************************************    
******      EVENT MASTER RECORD - JOB EVENTS      *********    
***********************************************************    
*                                                              
* RECORD TYPE X'01' - JOB RECORD                               
*                                                              
*EX1RCD   DS    0F                 MINIMUM LENGTH=41 BYTES MAX=1
*EX1JOB   DS    CL8                 DOS JOB NAME FOR THIS EVENT 
*EX1FLAG1 DS    B                   FLG BYTE                    
*EX1SLI   EQU                       X'00' = SLI BOOK            
*EX1ICCF  EQU                       X'01' = EX1SNAME IS ICCF NAM
*EX1LIBR  EQU                       X'02' = EX1SNAME IS LIBRARIA
*EX1OWL   EQU                       X'03' = OWL LIBRARY MEMBER  
*...

    05  EX1RCD.
	  10  EX1JOB                PIC X(8).
	  10  EX1FLAG1              PIC X.
	  

***********************************************************    
****** EVENT MASTER RECORD -  SYSTEM COMMANDS     *********    
***********************************************************    
*         ORG   EX1RCD                                          
*EX7RCD   DS    0C                  SCOM EVENT (SYSTEM COMMANDS)
*EX7ITMCT DS    XL2                 NUMBER OF SCOM ENTRIES      
*EX7LEN1  EQU   *-EX7RCD                                        
*EX7START DS    0X                  START OF COMMAND ENTRIES    
*  OCCURS EX7ITMCT TIMES                                       
*EX7DSECT DSECT ,                                               
*EX7TYPE  DS    X                   TYPE OF COMMAND             
*EX7PCOM  EQU   1                   POWER COMMAND               
*EX7ZCOM  EQU   2                   ZEKE COMMAND                
*EX7VCOM  EQU   3                   VM COMMAND                  
*EX7SCOM  EQU   4                   SYSTEM COMMAND              
*EX7SRESP EQU   5                   SYSTEM RESPONSE COMMAND     
*EX7ZACK  EQU   6                   ZACK COMMAND                
*EX7TEXT  DS    CL60                COMMAND TEXT                
*EX7LENG  EQU   *-EX7DSECT          LENGTH OF COMMAND ENTRY     


    05  EX7RCD   redefines EX1RCD.
	  10  EX7ITMCT     Binary PIC S9(4).
	  10  EX7START     Occurs 0 to ??? 
	                   Depending on EX7ITMCT.
	    15  EX7TYPE           PIC X.
		  88  EX7-Power-Cmd         Value '1'.
		  88  EX7-Zeke-Cmd          Value '2'.
		  88  EX7-VM-Cmd            Value '3'.
		  88  EX7-System-Cmd        Value '4'.
		  88  EX7-System-Resp-Cmd   Value '5'.
		  88  EX7-Zack-Cmd          Value '6'.
        15  EX7TEXT           PIC X(60).
```

## Thread: New copy In Micro Focus Cobol — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### New copy In Micro Focus Cobol

- **From:** MFDEVP <member27090@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T16:15:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2869138.1052756111@dbforums.com>`

```

I have a problem in Micro focus Cobol program, my changes are not
immediately applying - If I add my changes and compile the program
some times it is applying immediately and some times it is taking some
time (10 mints to 1 hr).I don't know why this is happening , could any
body please expalin me the problem ?
```

#### ↳ Re: New copy In Micro Focus Cobol

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T23:51:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC02FAC.9E31D689@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<2869138.1052756111@dbforums.com>`

```


MFDEVP wrote:

> I have a problem in Micro focus Cobol program, my changes are not
> immediately applying - If I add my changes and compile the program
> some times it is applying immediately and some times it is taking some
> time (10 mints to 1 hr).I don't know why this is happening , could any
> body please expalin me the problem ?

I think you will have to expand on your problem a little to give us some
more clues. Is the compiler Net Express (University or Professional
editions) ? The only thing I can immediately offer, where you refer to '10
minutes to an hour', is that somehow your source file has got a glitch,
(perhaps some hidden character or text you can't see). Pure speculation
but that might cause the compiler to loop endlessly trying to check
something.

Based on speculation - put some *deliberate* error lines in your source,
which will cause the compiler to display errors. You can then 'watch' to
see where the compiler is perhaps 'pausing'. If you hit true frustration,
try 'chopping' the program up, say doing a compile just containing
Paragraph 1. If that comes out 'clean' now add the source for Paras 2 and
3 and do a fresh compile.  Extremely tedious - but might help you if in
fact a glitch in the source was the problem.

Coding in OO I've been totally mystified where a perfectly valid method
(paragraph/sub-program) has been rejected. Cut it out, compiles clean. Now
add it back in and it compiles clean again !

If truly stumped go to the Micro Focus Answer Exchange and ask there.
Before you ask, the URLs are :-

For Home Page :
http://microfocus.com/index.asp?bhcp=1

Assuming it is University Edition :
http://microfocus.com/academic/academicprograms_support.asp

Assuming it is not University Edition :
http://www.cobolportal.com/microfocusforum/

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-3f3b3c9ad7"></a>
## Thread: More from SYSTEMANTIC — 1 msgs

### More from SYSTEMANTIC

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T15:33:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O82dndv-D5OxmF2jXTWcqw@giganews.com>`

```
The book by John Gall

Since we've proven:

SYSTEMS NEVER REALLY DO WHAT WE WANT THEM TO DO

We offer a primer of What Can Be Done.

Briefly, cope. On rare occasions, prevail.

Basic Rule:
Do Without A System If You Can

a. New problems are created by its presence.
b. It kicks back, gets in the way, and opposes its own proper function.
c. Your own perspective becomes distorted by being in the system.
d. You become anxious and push on it to make it work.
e. Eventually you come to believe that the misbegotten product it so
grudgingly delivers is what you really wanted all along.

At this point encroachment has become complete. You have become absorbed.
You are now a Systems-person.

In formal language:

Many Functions Are Intrinsically Unsuited To The Systems Approach.

(Anyone who has tried to operate an umbrella in a high wind has an intuitive
feel for what is involved here.)
```

## Thread: Alphabet vs. logograph (was Sorting algorithms) — 1 msgs

### Re: Alphabet vs. logograph (was Sorting algorithms)

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-12T14:22:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9p3b7$12ji$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

I wrote:  I

> The invention of Cyrillic based
> on Greek as a part of Cyril and Methodius' missionary work to the Slavic
peo
> ple in the ninth century is undisputed as historical fact.

Actually I misspoke.  What Cyril and Methodius seem to have invented in the
9th century is Glagolitic script, based apparently on Greek cursive.
Cyrillic came in a generation or so later, based on Greek uncials, and had
pretty much replaced Glagolitic by the 12th century.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

Just to let the curious know...  The SORT with input and output 
procedures works under Unisys 2200 UCOB (the one with HMP IX 5.1).  I 
think I remember someone saying that it wasn't implemented yet, but it 
works like a charm.  :)

#### ↳ Re: Follow-Up: SORT tables on Unisys 2200

```
What wasn't implemented was
    SORT <table-name> <key-structure>.
It's possible to accomplish the same thing using input and output procedures
with COBOL of any vintage since no later than the '68 standard inclusive, so
long as that implementation has the SORT module.

    -Chuck Stevens

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Follow-Up: SORT tables on Unisys 2200

- **From:** codecrafters@cix.compulink.co.uk
- **Date:** 2003-05-18T18:31:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba8ji4$iqa$1@thorium.cix.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<b9r085$2d0l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
> What wasn't implemented was
>     SORT <table-name> <key-structure>.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> long as that implementation has the SORT module.

This is true but perhaps unhelpful :-)

I fervently hope that the mainframe implementation of SORT <table-name> 
doesn't invoke the system sort to do the job - it's rather like swatting a 
fly with a Buick.  The whole point of this extension is to let you sort a 
table easily and - I hope - quickly. Use of a full-blown system sort would 
generally preclude the latter. I've used the comb sort many times where I 
could have used the system sort as described, and performance (on the 
mainframe) has always been significantly better.

Just FWIW, you understand.    

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Follow-Up: SORT tables on Unisys 2200

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-19T12:00:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bab9ju$1ptk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b9r085$2d0l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ba8ji4$iqa$1@thorium.cix.co.uk>`

```

<codecrafters@cix.compulink.co.uk> wrote in message
news:ba8ji4$iqa$1@thorium.cix.co.uk...

> This is true but perhaps unhelpful :-)
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Just FWIW, you understand.

There are almost always ways to "beat" generic code.  Such is the nature of
optimization.  I am not yet convinced that the labor costs associated with
developing a hand-crafted in-memory sort are  unconditionally justified by
improved performance over the "system sort as described".   I know of at
least one "system sort" implementation that is heavily used in system
software and *specifically* allows the specification of its commands and
parameters so that the sort is performed without any I/O operations at all.
Is that so bad?

I suspect your reaction to the use of the "system sort" may either be based
on an implementation in which significant optimization techniques are
unavailable in the software, or on insufficient information about the
available mechanisms for improving performance of that "system sort".

I believe, in the application with which I am most familiar, that the number
of cases in which a "hand-crafted" sort -- particularly one involving a
large table with a complicated key structure -- would *dramatically*
outperform a system sort with a couple of optimizations and the use of input
and output procedures, or a compiler-generated "table sort" based on that
mechanism -- would be so few as to cause me to recommend to our users
"unless you can prove it's broke, don't fix it."  The cost of development of
a "handmade" sort is a whole lot higher than having the SORT verb accomplish
the same thing.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

## Thread: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans — 291 msgs

### [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T09:18:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com>`

```
As you may know, 50-odd Texas House Democrats skedaddled for parts unknown
yesterday bringing business in the legislature to a standstill for lack of a
quorum.

One Republican house member has already created a "most wanted" deck of
cards, a la Iraqi fugitives.

The Speaker of the House has ordered the Department of Public Safety
(troopers) and the Texas Rangers to arrest instanter any decamped members
and bring them to the capitol forthwith.

A large bunch of runaways is reported ensconced in a motel in Oklahoma,
taking their meals at a nearby Denny's. The motel is reportedly surrounded
by Texas Rangers (one*) in an unmarked car. A plane is standing by.
Extradition has been requested of the governor of Oklahoma and the feds have
been alerted. The Speaker of the House needs to fetch only nine of the
miscreants to reach a quorum.

Already a wanted poster/website is up:

http://www.homestead.com/derelictlegislators/

The main beef of the Democrats is a proposed redistricting plan that would
switch as many as ten U.S. congressional seats from Democrat control to
Republican. Plus the decision to quit serving grits in the House cafeteria.


-----
* In the early 20th Century a riot broke out in Galveston. The mayor sent an
urgent telegram to the headquarters of the Texas Rangers asking for
immediate assistance in quelling the disturbance. The next morning, a single
Ranger got off the train. The shocked mayor exclaimed: "There's only ONE of
you?!!"

The ranger replied: "Well, there's only one riot."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T14:34:12-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vc53566lp323d4@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net>,
> LX-i  <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> system to change it or find another place more to your liking.'

There are some things one may have great difficulty
'learn[ing] to live with', as in 'a thing about which I have
thought the same for more than 25 years'.

Where the system is defective, 'work[ing] within' may be
nearly impossible. For example, the Constitution of the
United States describes a republican form of government
within a federal system. Article III delegates, to the
Judicial branch, the power to decide 'all cases ... arising
under this Constitution, the laws of the United States, and
....' Article VI, Clause 2, states that 'This Constitution, and
the laws of the United States which shall be made in
pursuance thereof; and ... shall be the supreme law of the
land ....' The use of 'pursuance' provides sufficient authority
for the judicial branch to review (judicial review) the laws
of the United States, to determine whether those laws shall
become the supreme law of the land, and this it has refused
to do. Furthermore, the Fifth Amendment, states that
'... [no person shall] be deprived of life, liberty, or property
without due process of law ....' It is in due process that
judicial review is mandatory, in that, no person should
be charged with the violation of, or cowered into obeying,
a law that is uncontitutional. Finally, the absence of
judicial review changes the form of government from
republican to authoritarian, thus requiring that the people
prove, to a reluctanct court, that a law is unconstitutional;
rather than, a willing court using its delegated power to
prevent the unconstitutional exercise of power by the
Congress.

Finding 'another place more to your liking' has its
difficulties, as well. The migration laws of Austrailia, for
example, provide, as I recall, eight categories. The
category that applies to this discussion is political
asylum. For Austrailia to accept the politically disaffected
of the United States would be to more than double its
population within a year. Thus, for Austrailia to publicly
grant political asylum to one, it would, thereby, risk the
consequences of granting asylum to many and, in time,
alter its own political and social structures and its
relations with the United States.

While I acknowledge that you did not say the alternatives
'learn to live with it, work within the system to change it or
find another place more to your liking' would be easy, it is,
nontheless, true that the alternatives offered may not
provide a solution to some problems.

> >
> >(I'd better stop before I really get started - this is CLC, not
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T16:10:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i1ydnVax3NEgLV-jXTWJhA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <vc53566lp323d4@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

>  It is in due process that
> judicial review is mandatory, in that, no person should
> be charged with the violation of, or cowered into obeying,
> a law that is uncontitutional.

(I think you meant 'cowed' instead of 'cowered' -- but 'coward' also works.)

When a bill is passed, the resulting law is presumed constitutional and must
be followed until a court of competent jurisdiction nullifies the law in
question.

When someone says "I have been visited by the Holy Spirit and spook told me
this law is unconstitutional and therefore not to be obeyed," he is more
properly termed a Baptist than a legal scholar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <vc53566lp323d4@corp.supernews.com> <b9uu2u$7cm$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b9uu2u$7cm$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <vc53566lp323d4@corp.supernews.com>,
> Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
[snip]
> >There are some things one may have great difficulty
> >'learn[ing] to live with', as in 'a thing about which I have
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> thread which went on, some might say, far too long.

Ahh! The American colonist in 1776, having perceived
"a long train of abuses and usurpations" should have
'learn[ed] to live with it' by changing their thought?

It didn't work for them. It won't work for me.

[snip]
>
> >Article III delegates, to the
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> wrong again!'?

Sometimes I agree. Other times I disagree. In any case,
I learn.

This Fall I expect a determination as to whether one case
will be presented before a local inferior court. This will
involve a new interpretation of the Tenth Amendment
and a complaint that I was denied due process by the
absence of judicial review.

This Summer, with or without the aid of counsel, I plan on
filing suit against the United States, in Washington, D.C.,
on, possibly, two issues. One, the Ninth Amendment -- one,
seeking an injunction. I am pursuing other avenues that
may make the suits unnecessary.

Then, depending upon the outcomes, I will, once again,
agree or disagree. And, the system may be changed, or not.

> [snip]
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Americans, the expressing of a rather... curious taste.

It is a matter of perspective. American citizenship is
retained. Residence is granted until the reasons for
the political disaffection cease. As a guest, I need
only comply with the law of the host country. It does not
matter if the law is essentially the same. The host
country has not abused my rights and usurped my
powers. The central issue is physical separation from
the abusive environment.

That I perceive the environment as abusive need only
reflect my unique nature, an ability to see the pattern,
understand it and how it affects me, and to react to it.
My inability to accept or adapt to it may be unique,
as well, though I doubt it.

I finally decided to stay and fight, in the courts. It is the
American thing to do.
```

The Fall issue may be resolved before trial, for other
reasons, though I would prefer it to continue.

I do not expect public hearings, this Summer. Serving
notice is like setting off a firecracker, it gets their attention.
Before any hearing, the information will be examined by
their experts and either will be accepted, or ... well ...
accepted. Having accepted the information, action will be
taken. It is likely that the particulars will be seen only by
lawyers and experts; though I expect the resulting changes
to be significant and public.

> >
> >> [snip]
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

I do not perceive the situation to be that different. One
exception is that soldiers will not arrest me for libel
and sedition or other charges and therefore I will not
be subject to imprisonment or execution for exercising
freedom of speech.

I would characterize both governments as having
committed 'a long train of abuses and usurpations'
evincing a design to subject the people to the rule
of the government. The Crown was, at that time and
may be seen as, open about it because of its
presumed right; the U.S. Congress uses subterfuge
supported by the courts.

The result is similar, even if the means are different.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans
