"LX-i" <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message
news:3EC4402B.2050708@Knology.net...
>
<snipped stuff on Social Security in America; an oxymoron if ever I heard
one...<G>>

> Providing for the national ("common" in the Constitution) defense is one
> of the Constitutionally-mandated functions of our Federal government.  I
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well spoken, Daniel, and I understand your concerns.

Here's a thought...

"(as we're now starting to get a bit of a pinch with missiles and such).  "

I understand tomahawks cost over $1,000,000 each. In Afghanistan they were
used to target the homes of the Taliban and Al Queida leadership.

Now, I don't know what real estate costs in Afghanistan, but wouldn't it
have been much cheaper to just buy the properties and send in the
bulldozers...<G>?

After Iraq I guess the inventory is showing serious depletion. Of course, if
nuclear weapons had been deployed that would not have been a problem. (It
has been calculated that full scale Global nuclear War could not last more
than 4 days due to the rapid destruction of infrastructure and resources;
after 4 days there's nothing left to fight with or over...- The Third World
War -). As nuclear weapons were NOT deployed, the game came down to
conventional weapons. As noted, these weapons are becoming horrifically
expensive.

So, next time the US decides to (rightly or wrongly) get involved in a War,
why not get the potential combatants round a table, drink a few beers (or
coffee, if they're non-alcoholic types), play a few hands of poker, and
agree that the conflict will be limited to bows and arrows?

This would give the under-priveleged nations as much right to fight a war as
the technically advanced ones, and everyone would give great kudos to the US
for being so sporting... Of course it would set Humanity back around 500
years, but as we still seem Hell bent on War as a solution to our
differences, that is no more than we deserve <G>.

Another thought I had that could save a lot of resource, was that the Armed
Forces should be composed of people over 50. (The ones who have made their
contribution and are therefore expendable). We should be PROTECTING the
potential reformers, movers and shakers of Society, (the youth), not sending
them off to War...

Sergeant: "You asleep on duty again, Smith?"

Smith: "It ain't my fault, Sarge. You scheduled the patrol during my nap
time... Anyway, none of the boys are up to hiking through minefields. Wilson
lost his walking stick, Johnson can't move with arthritis,  Brown tried to
pull the pin out of a grenade with his teeth, like he saw Audie Murphy do in
the movies...ended up throwing his teeth at the enemy and the exploding
grenade took out him and Simpson... we've had a hard day."

Sergeant: "Yeah, I guess you're right. Let's pack it in and head back to
base...now where the Hell is that base...Christ, this Alzheimers is a
curse..."


> In my opinion, national defense and internal infrastructure are two of
> the best uses of my tax money.  Social programs are, for the most part,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I believe you may modify this position as you get older, Daniel. (I did
<G>).

Sadly, there are always those in our Society who simply cannot "earn their
keep in a free market".

It would be immoral (given that we live in an affluent society) to simply
let these people die. If a nation generates so much wealth, there is an
argument that says it should care for at least the basic requirements of all
of its citizens.(that includes the freeloaders, deadbeats, no-hopers, and
anti-socials, simply by right of birth. Hey, the law protects the right of
children to their birthright, why not Society protecting its "children"?)

Suppose that all Social programs were based on charity as you suggest, and
nobody donated? Should we simply revert to the law of the jungle? (I mean
the one about the strong surviving at the expense of the weak, not Kipling's
Law of the Jungle ("which is as Old and as True as the sky, and the wolf
that shall keep it will prosper, but the wolf that shall break it, must
die..."))

This post is not intended to undermine or cast aspersions in any way on the
fine young people (of whatever country's Armed Forces) who are the first to
give their lives in the defence of their way of life.

I just wish they were directed by people with more sense. (I'm not
necessarily talking about the Military Command here, although, they get it
wrong often enough to not be above criticism...the problem is when political
leaders commit young lives for doubtful goals.)

As an ex-soldier myself, I remember what it's like when you're twenty years
old and someone puts a powerful weapon in your hand.  (I couldn't wait to
get to Viet Nam and utilise what I'd been taught...so Gung-Ho it was unreal.
Fortunately, I never had to go. I have nothing but respect for those who
did.)

Now, in my late fifties, having enjoyed a rich and varied life (which other
men died in conflict so I could), I realise this is not the solution.

There IS a time when war is inevitable and it is important that all of us
should be up to that challenge. Not least, we should ensure that those in
our Armed Forces are adequately trained, equipped, and funded.

But we have to be VERY sure there is absolutely no alternative.

Besides, war has changed a lot over the centuries... Consider this...

"Then up spake brave Horatius
 The Captain of the Gate
 'To every man upon this Earth
  Death cometh, soon or late.
  And how can man die better
 Than facing fearful odds,
 For the ashes of his Fathers,
 And the Temples of his Gods.' "

Horatius, Herminius, and Spurius Lartius held back the Tuscan army on the
one bridge over the Tiber that led to Rome (it was just wide enough for
three people) while their compatriots sawed it through. The Tuscans were
very sporting about it and sent men in threes against them.

Delightful image after the first three were quickly despatched by our
heroes...

"And none there was amongst them
 Would face that bold attack,
 While those behind cried "Forward!"
 And those before cried "Back!" "

After they lost a few, they sent one of their heroes (the Lord of Luna) who
wounded Horatius severely in the side and received Horatius' sword through
his head for his trouble... As the bridge was about to go, the other two ran
back, but Horatius, hampered by his wound, had to jump in the Tiber and try
to swim, encumbered by his armour. I won't tell you what happened; you can
read it for yourself in Lord MacCaulay's "Lays of Ancient Rome" (and no,
that wasn't Cleopatra <G>).

See, we don't do stuff like that any more... As we have degraded the
"nobility" of war you could argue that we no longer have the right to wage
it... (however, it is a philosophical argument and probably not worth the
effort of minds that can post several hundred mails to a thread about the
origins of Language (all of which is easily ascertainable in 5 minutes with
a decent encyclopaedia and/or GOOGLE), and their possible connection to
COBOL...<G>)

Pete.









>
> -- 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT]The Cost of War.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-16T19:19:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC53605.BD92AC5E@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC28EDE.C8467E13@istar.ca> <FAEwa.297$vk3.68131@twister.austin.rr.com> <joe_zitzelberger-86822F.08405315052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EC42356.9040708@Knology.net> <3EC42ECF.DF9CA73A@shaw.ca> <3EC4402B.2050708@Knology.net> <3ec4c675_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`

```


"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:

> "LX-i" <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message
> news:3EC4402B.2050708@Knology.net...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

<snip>

Yes Daniel's thoughts were expressed with sincerity. And as you pointed out,
when we were younger, possibly we thought along similar lines about the war
machine. But I would love to see a US school text on world history, to establish
whether there is any bias. I'll bet there is.

As an illustration. In my time we had an official set of texts for UK history. -
BUT- I didn't read the identical texts that you would have done at an Anglican
or  non-denominational school. We read the same texts but the specifically 'anti
Papist' bits were removed. That's not to say they were censored. They still
accurately reported history, but excluded the 'digs' inserted by the protestant
authors against 'Romanism'. I can't remember specifics, but you will appreciate
how a protestant author might play up the fact that Guy Fawkes was a
catholic.(He was the only sensible guy to set foot in the House of Commons <G>).

Loved your take on war <G>

You touched on the topic in passing - an alternative to war. This is where
'Western' nations have been sadly morally lacking. As has been demonstrated only
too well, both the League of Nations and United Nations, (both 'incorporated' by
the 'West') have been completely impotent as regards war or staving off wars.

The UN does a good administrative, international job for things like UNESCO, WHO
etc., but is absolutely kiboshed on preventing aggressive acts  Ignore the
rights and wrongs of Gulf War 2, but it was political interest, ( MONEY) which
came into play with the French and Russians trying to stop Resolution 1441 being
used by the Coalition of the Willing. Quite simply, if Iraq Saddam style was
replaced by 'Iraq Model 2', they would have no call on the huge gobs of money
they poured into Saddam's regime. (Even that's complicated; go back to the
'eighties, and the US was dishing out money to Saddam as a counter to the
Iranian revolution).

To get Iraq Reconstruction going, the US and UK have just fine tuned their
latest proposal to the UN to be accommodating to the French and Russians - 'You
will be able to make a claim on what the previous regime owed you from future
generated oil revenues". Seems a real pity to me - much better if the Iraqis
were given a clean slate.

However as my Scottish buddy suggests, as a counter to losing that old regime's
debt, the Russians, (as the current second largest producers), could turn on
the  taps and release real chap oil - truly hurting the Saudis and their Wahhabi
fundamentalists. The U.S. needs that situation like a hole in the head, with its
attendant destabilization of the Saudi monarchy.  Politics, politics,
politics.....

As to the morality of the West -

1931 - Japanese aggression in China - not a damn thing done
1930s - Nazis come to power and well prior to WW2 'labour camps' introduced.
While not 'refined' to the degree of the Holocaust, both US and UK intelligence
were feeding back data to their governments.

Side step WW2, (Chamberlain doing his tippy-toeing through the German
Chancellery)  - pass on to subsequent - Cambodia, the dismemberment of
Yugoslavia resulting in the ethnic/religious cleansings, the murderous loss of
life amongst the blacks in Africa, and our very latest - we have now discovered
the 'Killing Fields' in Iraq.

In the West we saw all this coming before many of the events occurred. Much
debating in the UN, but nothing effective ever came out of the deliberations,
and proposals always snarled by a particular country's fear of a precedent being
set. With an eye on Tibet, our Chinese friends were never enthused about the
concept of toppling totalitarian regimes. Same of course with the previous USSR.

With the Iron Curtain threat no longer in existence, one would have thought the
UN could function in a more meaningful manner. But now we find the world split
into groups like Third World, seething with resentment at First World affluence,
and the latest, Arabic nationalism + Islamic fundamentalism.

I can't offer a solution. But one thing's for sure. The structure and operation
of the UN has to alter. Until the structure changes the only recourse,
reluctantly,  is to go to war. War is never a solution because it occurs after
tragic events have already happened.

To suggest that we could have a world without wars is a Utopian dream. As
Desmond Morris points out, the howling fans supporting the home team at a soccer
match is nothing more than a safety valve for tribal warfare. Now there's a
thought. Europeans, Arabs, Africans, Asians, Aussies, Kiwis, S. Americans all
play soccer.
Now if we could persuade our American cousins to play *real* football (soccer) -
that might be a start. Problem is of course, they'd get into a hell of a fight
as to who implements it under their Constitution, the Feds or the States <G>.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT]The Cost of War.

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba3ehf$kgg$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC28EDE.C8467E13@istar.ca> <FAEwa.297$vk3.68131@twister.austin.rr.com> <joe_zitzelberger-86822F.08405315052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EC42356.9040708@Knology.net> <3EC42ECF.DF9CA73A@shaw.ca> <3EC4402B.2050708@Knology.net> <3ec4c675_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3EC53605.BD92AC5E@shaw.ca>`

```

On 16-May-2003, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> To suggest that we could have a world without wars is a Utopian dream

Yep.   Throughout history the relationship between governments has been like the
relationships between gangs and gang members, or maybe members of a wolf pack.
 The big guy sets the rules and pushes the little guys in line.   The gang
members say they're on the little guy's side, when they can do so without risk.
 If the little guy does fight, he doesn't do it mano a mano, that would be crazy
- so when he fights dirty (by the big guy's definitions), the big guy criticizes
him for being a coward.   Occasionally the top dog wolf gets nipped, and to show
his power and keep control, he attacks the first available victim.

A generation later the players are different, but their behaviors are the same.
```

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

- **Message-ID:** `<3ec5fbee.44820199@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC28EDE.C8467E13@istar.ca> <FAEwa.297$vk3.68131@twister.austin.rr.com> <joe_zitzelberger-86822F.08405315052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EC42356.9040708@Knology.net> <3EC42ECF.DF9CA73A@shaw.ca> <3EC4402B.2050708@Knology.net> <3ec4c675_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3EC53605.BD92AC5E@shaw.ca> <ba3ehf$kgg$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>Yep.   Throughout history the relationship between governments has been like
the
>relationships between gangs and gang members, or maybe members of a wolf pack.
> The big guy sets the rules and pushes the little guys in line.   The gang
>members say they're on the little guy's side, when they can do so without risk.
> If the little guy does fight, he doesn't do it mano a mano, that would be
crazy
>- so when he fights dirty (by the big guy's definitions), the big guy
criticizes
>him for being a coward.   Occasionally the top dog wolf gets nipped, and to
show
>his power and keep control, he attacks the first available victim.

Good analogy. 

I work with mutual fund holdings (the stocks the own). This is public
information filed with the SEC on form 13F. 

We're all familiar with mutual funds geared to the middle class. They have Web
pages and 800 numbers. They invest in recognized brand names such as Microsoft,
Dell, Toys R Us, Lowe's and Amazon. Names which give middle class investors a
warm fuzzy feeling. 

There is another class of mutual funds that's bigger than Vantage 500 and
Fidelity Magellan ($50B). They don't call themselves mutual funds, don't have
Web pages nor 800 numbers, you've never heard of them. They are managed by
'perservation of capital' companies with lineage to Rothschild/Rockefeller/JP
Morgan. In other words Old Money. To participate, one must submit to an
in-person interview, meet the minimum requirement -- typically $1M or at least
$100K plus current income. It is instructive to see which investments give Old
Money a warm fuzzy feeling. It's Lockheed-Martin, Grumman, Exxon, Mobil --
weapons and oil. 

The brief Iraq war was about weapons and oil. We did it for rich people.
```

Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
> "LX-i" <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message
> news:3EC4402B.2050708@Knology.net...
> 
> <snipped stuff on Social Security in America; an oxymoron if ever I heard
> one...<G>>

When I first got out of high school, I had a work scholarship at a local 
community college.  I was assigned to Student Services, which does 
registration, ID cards, etc.  Whenever I handed someone their blank 
(pre-photo) card, I asked them to sign it and verify their social 
insecurity number.  You'd (or you might not) be surprised how few people 
caught that.  (Of the ones who did, the people who were most amused were 
mid-50s ladies...)

> Here's a thought...
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> bulldozers...<G>?

The problem is that they can outrun the bulldozers...  :)

> So, next time the US decides to (rightly or wrongly) get involved in a War,
> why not get the potential combatants round a table, drink a few beers (or
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> differences, that is no more than we deserve <G>.

Well, this violates Patton's principle (paraphrased) - "Your job is not 
to die for your country - your job is to make that other SOB die for 
his."  And, I think you'd have a lot fewer volunteers for our 
all-volunteer force.  (And besides, unless you're going to pop the 
canopy on an F/A-22 and shoot out the top, that leave my branch of the 
service out!)

> Another thought I had that could save a lot of resource, was that the Armed
> Forces should be composed of people over 50. (The ones who have made their
> contribution and are therefore expendable). We should be PROTECTING the
> potential reformers, movers and shakers of Society, (the youth), not sending
> them off to War...

heh - interesting.  I'm not quite sure how strong our military would be, 
but interesting...  ;)

>>In my opinion, national defense and internal infrastructure are two of
>>the best uses of my tax money.  Social programs are, for the most part,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> <G>).

That's what people told me over 10 years ago, and if anything I've 
gotten more conservative.  It's amazing what having children will do to you.

> Sadly, there are always those in our Society who simply cannot "earn their
> keep in a free market".
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> children to their birthright, why not Society protecting its "children"?)

A hand up, not a hand out.  People's positions in life are a direct 
result of the choices they made.  In an affluent society, I have no 
problem helping out once for a short period of time (government charity, 
if you will).  The problem with this is that very quickly it becomes an 
"entitlement".  Why should some 18-year-old burger-flipper buy me 
prescription drugs just because I was foolish with my money and didn't 
invest properly so I would have it when I needed it?  They shouldn't - 
I'm not "entitled" to prescription drugs for free just because I reach a 
certain age.

> Suppose that all Social programs were based on charity as you suggest, and
> nobody donated? Should we simply revert to the law of the jungle?

Why would nobody donate?  People generally feel much more benevolent to 
other people than they do toward their government (relatives excluded).

> There IS a time when war is inevitable and it is important that all of us
> should be up to that challenge. Not least, we should ensure that those in
> our Armed Forces are adequately trained, equipped, and funded.

Yes - and yet it's difficult to convince some people that this 
preparation must be done during peacetime...  I'm not sure how much of 
the Clintonian slashing of the military you were privy to down under, 
but the US military is much "leaner" than it used to be.

> See, we don't do stuff like that any more... As we have degraded the
> "nobility" of war you could argue that we no longer have the right to wage
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL...<G>)

I don't know - there's about 70 new messages in this group, and probably 
55 of them are "[OT] Legislative Shenanigans"...  I guess everyone's 
getting tired of alphabet soup.  Of course, in a political discussion, 
it's almost inevitable that eventually, there will be the thread-killing 
reference to the former leader of the Third Reich...  :)
```

```


LX-i wrote:

A bit of a pre-amble to the following quote. I get the impression that you are
'enjoying' the military, but that's perhaps because you are cocooned in EDP,
away from the day-to-day bullshit ? (Here's me thinking you are a young 'un, cos
you said so; then comes mention of the two kids - so I'm going to guess - near
thirty ?)

I had also assumed the US military was well paid, (well GI Joe in the UK during
WW2 was considered as such - 'Over-paid, over-sexed and over here' <G> - but
probably the over-paid bit was relative to their income compared to a Tommies
pay). I was surprised to see your reference and Joe's to military pay. Certainly
during my time the pay *was* poor and always lagged behind civilians in catch-up
increases.

Also surprised me that Pete Dashwood started out with a military career. An
absolute non-starter, he would just have been too much of a rebel <G>. While no
rebel, I couldn't hack the stupidity, and as part of the British caste system -
the SNOBBERY. So although twice recommended for a cadetship to RAF Cranwell (our
RAF equivalent to Sandhurst or West Point), and subsequent to that again
recommended for a Permanent Commission -  I'm real glad I didn't get a
commission - I would have been a square peg in a round hole.

I did my stint 1948 to 1961, (becoming an apprentice required me to sign on for
the long haul), then got the hell out of it. Apart from giving up the best
(youthful) years of my life, I would sum up my military career in one word -
B-O-R-I-N-G.   I would not make a good poster boy for military advertising.

>
> Yes, but do we have what we need to do it again?  That's the problem -
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ike's phrase ' the military industrial machine', they'd LUV you for saying the
military should be planned on 10 times a Gulf War. He denies it vociferously,
but Rumsfeld wanted Gulf War2 on the cheap, and claims Tommy Franks got all he
needed. I very much doubt that and it did get tricky there for a while - the
supply lines from Qatar/Basrah heading north were not protected, obviously due
to lack of manpower. (We will only confirm this from the history books some 10
years hence).

While I wouldn't attempt to put a size on US military strength, can't help but
feel it is overblown, given there is no longer a USSR. Perhaps some of the
military budget might be better spent on FOREIGN POLICY, looking inwards first
at some of the  US corporations and their over-aggressive foreign trading
practices, (invisibly) supported by the potential of US military might. (That
last is not a black and white, but an attempt to paraphrase without going into
umpteen paragraphs).

The Western colonialism of the Middle East started at the end of WW1.
From 1918 until roughly 1950 - the whole of the MIddle East was British turf,
also some French, as in Syria. Never been to Iraq but from my days in Egypt
(51/54), I remember names like Basrah, Sharjah (that might have been Jordan) and
one which didn't come up during Gulf War 2, Habbaniyah - my guess that's what we
now call
Baghdad International Airport.

Subsequent to 1950 the same game was being played, just that the Westerners took
on different nationalities over the years. Consider from recent history, Shah of
Persia and replacement by the fundamentalist Iran. Then the hatred of Osama bin
Laden, railing against the US but also the utter contempt for the Saudi royal
family. US business(foreign polic) interests led to both situations. We can see
with hindsight that a different perspective in US foreign policy *might* have
avoided both - most importantly 9/11.

Now you statement, "That way, you not only have the power to send a
message, you have the power to say, "And to the rest of you..."

Careful now ! I do hope your, "And the rest of you ..", is solely directed at
any other non comprehending rag-heads in the Middle East. If unfortunately it it
intended to be more 'universal' in application - then watch out - there's a few
here spread around the globe will get real pissed off  !.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

LX-i wrote:

> James J. Gavan wrote:
> >
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Good thinking Batman ! Around about '57 at Northolt I was talking to another
ex-apprentice who was just leaving at the age of 30. He was into radar etc. He was
more than bright, because, ( probably at the age of 25),  he was awarded the BEM
(British Empire Medal), for work he had done maintaining radar equipment when the
Russkies sealed off Berlin - the Berlin Airlift. (JFK, 'Ich bin ein Berliner ').

The RAF also had 'On the JOb Training' for tecchies, (which I'm sure you have), where
they went off to manufacturers for on site training - the only name immediately coming
to mind, Plessey. Obviously the scheme had an attraction to both the military and the
manufacturers, plus the latter were keeping their eyes peeled for the 'bright ones'.
He too had immediate offers of three jobs as he left.

I nearly forgot, at the age of 30 he was a flight sergeant, (three chevrons/stripes
and a crown). That ws quite remarkable; normally not until you were well over 35 +.
The English caste system - he was awarded the BEM. Now had he been an officer it would
have been the MBE (Member of the British Empire), referred to as 'My Bloody Effort' as
opposed to senior officers, say wing commander, (Lt. Col)  upwards, where they would
be awarded the OBE (Order of the British Empire), also referred to as 'Others Bloody
Effort'.

>
> > Now you statement, "That way, you not only have the power to send a
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> our lives to stop it.

Phew! Thank God for that. But you didn't have to publicly warn the Iranians and
Koreans in advance. Canada - well Thane Hubbell's dad used to be USAF, based in
Eastern Montana,  pointing nukes at the Great White North - so that ain't new <G>..

Jimmy

> The RAF also had 'On the JOb Training' for tecchies, (which I'm sure you have), where

We mostly go to Atlanta for training we can't get here.  However, our 
organization has been try to pool training requirements from all the 
offices and see if it won't be cheaper to pay Oracle (for example) to 
send one person here than it would be to send 12 people to Atlanta for 
the week.

When I first got here, I had my beginner/intermediate/advanced COBOL 
training (3 weeks), put on by a local guy who had been enlisted, gotten 
out, and started a company doing web-based design back before it was 
really hot, and funded the company (before all that got going) by 
providing local training to the government folks.  The first class was 
downtown, the last two were held on base.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT]The Cost of War.

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-19T13:12:07+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec82ca8_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC28EDE.C8467E13@istar.ca> <FAEwa.297$vk3.68131@twister.austin.rr.com> <joe_zitzelberger-86822F.08405315052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EC42356.9040708@Knology.net> <3EC42ECF.DF9CA73A@shaw.ca> <3EC4402B.2050708@Knology.net> <3ec4c675_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3EC58DD4.9080606@Knology.net> <uapccvsujqibdf2sjmf22in2knki4368cv@4ax.com> <3EC71390.9040101@Knology.net> <3EC7F47C.2A64546E@shaw.ca>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3EC7F47C.2A64546E@shaw.ca...
<snip>
>
> Also surprised me that Pete Dashwood started out with a military career.
An
> absolute non-starter, he would just have been too much of a rebel <G>.

I was conscripted, Jimmy. It wasn't a career choice. I was already
programming NCR 315s at the time and had recently married. They had a ballot
system where you registered at age 19 (I was late, and registered at age 20)
and could be called up  the following year.

They pulled the birth dates out of a barrel. I was born at midnight so I
technically had two chances...<G> I complained about this when I registered
and the guy there said: "OK, pick one..." I chose the 20th. They had the
ballot and pulled out the 20th (but the 21st was never extracted...fickle
finger of Fate, I guess <G>). I managed to get it deferred for a year but by
the time I was 22 there was no avoiding it. (Well, I guess I could've gone
to live in Australia, but I considered the Army (or working at
MacDonald's... or "anything") to be preferable to doing THAT <G>).  I was
kind of hoping there might be a change of Government and they would abolish
it (there was, and they did, but it was too late, I was already in camp...).

They gave us a couple of weeks of basic, then IQ and personality tests and
took the top 5% to OCTU for Officer training. The deal was that if we wanted
to, we could join up full time with a commission. In the meantime we were
"acting" officers (I don't know about the others, but I should've got the
Academy Award for my performance <G>...)

As for being a rebel, I guess that is fair. I don't rebel against Authority
as such, but I do like to explore alternative ways of doing things. (They
had the results of my personality tests and they still took me...I guess
they were really desperate <G>)

As I said before, I actually enjoyed (for the most part; some of it was
bloody awful) my time in the military and I look on it as a positive
experience in my life. If we had been required to go to Viet Nam (we were
trained as if we WERE going, but NZ never sent conscripts (Oz did...it was
tragic.) I was definitely up for it... (My wife was having kittens at the
thought and my military service put quite a strain on our marriage; I wasn't
there and she needed to have me there...another story).

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT]The Cost of War.

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-19T10:02:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-F03521.10022219052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC28EDE.C8467E13@istar.ca> <FAEwa.297$vk3.68131@twister.austin.rr.com> <joe_zitzelberger-86822F.08405315052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EC42356.9040708@Knology.net> <3EC42ECF.DF9CA73A@shaw.ca> <3EC4402B.2050708@Knology.net> <3ec4c675_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3EC58DD4.9080606@Knology.net> <uapccvsujqibdf2sjmf22in2knki4368cv@4ax.com> <3EC71390.9040101@Knology.net> <3EC7F47C.2A64546E@shaw.ca>`

```
In article <3EC7F47C.2A64546E@shaw.ca>,
 "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> I had also assumed the US military was well paid, (well GI Joe in
> the UK during WW2 was considered as such - 'Over-paid, over-sexed
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> catch-up increases.

I had a disturbing realization while doing my taxes in mid-1991.  Total 
compensation for my service was slightly more than the current minimum 
wage.  Were actual hours worked used, and considered for overtime 
purposes, it would have been far less.

My monthly pay was about $700 for Desert Shield/Storm.

It took hazardous duty pay to bring the total up to "slightly more", 
without it it would have been "slightly less".  This is the $110 that 
the US government pays its troops when they are faced with the prospect 
of grevious bodily harm.  In my case it was a bit less than 1/6 of my 
monthly pay.

I mentioned this to my father at a later date -- surprise -- he was paid 
the same amount of hazardous duty pay in Korea in 1950.  At the time, 
his monthly pay was about $40, making the combat pay a 300% raise.  In 
40 years the powers that be had not see fit to alter it.

Somewhere in the middle of that Congress got around to raising the 
hazardous duty pay -- to $150/month.

Overpaid?  Not!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT]The Cost of War.

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-18T14:05:20-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-F42275.14052018052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC28EDE.C8467E13@istar.ca> <FAEwa.297$vk3.68131@twister.austin.rr.com> <joe_zitzelberger-86822F.08405315052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EC42356.9040708@Knology.net> <3EC42ECF.DF9CA73A@shaw.ca> <3EC4402B.2050708@Knology.net> <3ec4c675_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3EC58DD4.9080606@Knology.net> <uapccvsujqibdf2sjmf22in2knki4368cv@4ax.com>`

```
In article <uapccvsujqibdf2sjmf22in2knki4368cv@4ax.com>,
 SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:
> 
> The Clintonian slashing, as you put it, was initiated by Ronald Regan,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> change the military.


You are confusing the cold war drawdown with the Clintonian "unfunded 
missions" of the 90's.

GB-I did start a reduction and/or move to reserves of about 1/3 of the 
active force.

Clinton continued that plan and cranked up the operational tempo without 
providing additional funds.  Under his direction we had temporary, but 
expensive, missions in Somalia, Hatti, Macedonia, Bosnia, Yemen, 
Afganistan, Iraq and others.  Most of the funds for that ended up coming 
from the normal peacetime operating budgets.

My favorite is his exausting our supply of Tomahawks on the eve of his 
impeachment and not planning to replace them.  (as previously noted, 
they are quite expensive)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-16T14:33:01+00:00

<grin> Well, at the risk of showing a U.S. Centric attitude, I don't much care what other
countries do because I don't have to directly pay their tax bill. On the other hand, you
Canadian types get really decent health care for what you pay out, while I shell out
over $6K per year directly out of my paycheck for family health care insurance.
That's in addition to the taxes and other expenses taken out.

That's just one example.

So just out of curiosity, why don' t you e-mail your guess as to what political affiliation
I belong to? It would be interesting to see how well you can peg it. :)


-Paul

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC5183B.8BD44115@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC28EDE.C8467E13@istar.ca> <FAEwa.297$vk3.68131@twister.austin.rr.com> <joe_zitzelberger-86822F.08405315052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EC42356.9040708@Knology.net> <3EC42ECF.DF9CA73A@shaw.ca> <anYwa.7414$9X1.195264@twister.austin.rr.com> <3EC45E79.39BE6466@shaw.ca> <6k4xa.11409$ql5.362009@twister.austin.rr.com>`

Paul Raulerson wrote:

> <grin> Well, at the risk of showing a U.S. Centric attitude, I don't much care what other
> countries do because I don't have to directly pay their tax bill. On the other hand, you
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Generally speaking our health care system does work well, although like any social program it does have
its bumps and warts. Here in Alberta within recent months, a 21-year old taken seriously ill, (I think
something like appendicitis - but I'm not sure). He lived in the boonies was shipped to Calgary and
shuffled from hospital emergency ward to emergency ward. (Bit like Christmas, 'There was no room at the
inn.........'). Then taken back to his town hospital in High River and subsequently died. OK, so it was an
administrative cock-up - but lack of facilities, through lack of funding, were contributory.

No different to the States - constant argie bargie between the Feds and provinces. By constitution
provinces are *responsible* for health along with education. Feds as the national tax gatherers dish out
funds. They have progressively reduced their financial involvement, putting the onus on the provinces to
find their own funding  to make it work, (i.e. Provincial Sales Tax - which we don't have in Alberta
because of the gobs of oil).

There is never a finite solution to welfare programs; start out with a philosophical approach and then
figure out can you fund it, and can you keep funding it ! Naturally the 'Left', (NDP) says "Pour more
money in", while the 'Right' like our Fraser Institute Think Tank in Vancouver says, "Privatize it". The
latter argument not being dissimilar to, 'Get rid of all taxes and you can rest assured the wealthy upper
10%  will make the economy boom".

>
> So just out of curiosity, why don' t you e-mail your guess as to what political affiliation
> I belong to? It would be interesting to see how well you can peg it. :)
>

Nah. Not gonna touch that one - wasn't aimed at you  specifically <G>. More accurately I should have said,
"who initiates a thread, and of what political persuasion, are the majority who jump in.....". I think DD
has made some astute comments - but I'm not going to attempt to guess what his political persuasion is
either. As 'the King of England', like the last one, he should be a conservative in thought - but I don't
think that would be correct for our current royal member contributing to this Newsgroup <G>

Jimmy
```

inflammatory. Basically, we should have the right to choose our own charities.

why they are taking our money.

on vacation for a decade or two, I don't know how to go about changing it.

"go west young man!" and all that jazz. Unfortunately, the only thing west
these days is still - California.

Better, perhaps, that Taxassachutest in the East though.

And we do have Taxas here in the middle.

-Paul

"senior citizens and welfare mothers."

Those two segments take a chunk of the tax proceeds, but not even close to 70%.
In fact, blaming the intolerable tax burden on those two groups is just rhetoric and
usually used as a shock tactic.

In fact, if taxes were used merely to support those two groups at the same level they
receive now, the average tax burden would be only about 6% of what it is now, or less.
Probably could be a lot less than that, even if my rough calculation above is off by 50%.

-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-18T23:00:29-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-CD5577.23002918052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <FAEwa.297$vk3.68131@twister.austin.rr.com> <joe_zitzelberger-86822F.08405315052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EC42356.9040708@Knology.net> <ba1e63$b0f$1@panix1.panix.com> <FrYwa.7418$9X1.200368@twister.austin.rr.com> <joe_zitzelberger-A5EB18.00163116052003@corp.supernews.com> <rf4xa.11407$ql5.361408@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
Your rough calculation is off by way more than that.  The CBO lists 
numbers on the "historical budget data" at http://www.cbo.gov.  All 
numbers are in P(9).

Non-Discretionary spending (aka Entitlements) for 2002 (Table 9)

 � Social Security            452.5
 � Medicare                   253.7
   Medicaid                   147.5
 � Other means-tested         138.5
 � Retirement & Disability     96.1  �  
  �Other non-means-tested      70.8 
   Unemployment Comp           50.6  �  
   Farm Price Supports         13.9  �  
   Deposit insurance           (1.0)

The grand total for all of this spending that is done using a twisted 
interpretation of the interstate commerce clause or the and "other 
things necessary" catch all of article 2, section 8, is 1.2 trillion.

Discretionary spending for 2002 (Table 7):

   Defense        348.9
   International  26.2
   Domestic       359.2 

Much of the discretionary spending is also in the above category of 
being constitutionally questionable.  But this grand total is 734 
billion.

Even if I stipulate that all discretionary spending has proper 
constitutional mandate the level of entitlement spending is 62%.

(These guys make Enrons accounting department look lame.  The numbers 
listed in the entitlement line items are just the checks mailed out -- 
they hide all the 'administration fees', 'personnel', 'buildings', etc 
for the entitlement programs inside domestic discretionary spending.  
Easily the other 8% I claim, probably more.)

The CBO also lists 171 billion of debt service to bonds (Table 5), 
mostly purchased by the Social Security Administration -- required by 
law to "invest" its current surpluses only in government bonds.  

(This is an accounting trick that makes Enron and Worldcomm look 
amateur.  The federal government forces itself to buy bonds with any 
excess funds that it is prohibited by law from spending.  Then it takes 
the bond income, which it is allowed to spend, and spends it.  But it  
gives itself an IOU that agrees to pay itself back, with interest.

My statement of "senior citizens and welfare mothers" fits the top four  
line items of entitlement spending.    Those first four line items 
comprise an outlay of 992.2 billion of 1196.6 billion entitlement 
spending.  Since when is it improper to generalize about something based 
on an 83% sample?

Ok, I've posted official numbers supporting my claims.  Can you come up 
with some official numbers to support your claim that 94% of all taxes 
are used for something else???



In article <rf4xa.11407$ql5.361408@twister.austin.rr.com>,
 "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:

> "senior citizens and welfare mothers."
> 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> > programs comprise most of that 70%?
```

"Does anyone know where to get a good COBOL compiler?"
Me - "Corporate Welfare sucks, but Fujitsu has a free one for download"

"Why won't this code compile?
     If X <> Y
         Perform Aw-Crap"
Me - "Your real problem is the corporate welfare in the US"

But, if your beef is with my unqualified "always", I'll qualify by 
saying that I always decry corporate welfare whenever it is the topic of 
discussion.  :)

>>>>I would love to see all forms of welfare end.  My personal peeve is the 
>>>>the farm subsidies and price supports leftover from FDL's New Deal.  
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

aforementioned "involuntary servitude to senior citizens"...  :)

There are those that describe any payment from government to corporation

On the other end, when goverment is paying corporations for goods or

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-18T14:07:18-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba8i4m$8ta$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC591F6.1050807@Knology.net> <ba55tc$c19$1@panix1.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-B5A9DE.13174418052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <joe_zitzelberger-B5A9DE.13174418052003@corp.supernews.com>,
Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
>In article <ba55tc$c19$1@panix1.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>Welfare?  I would be curious to see what exactly you view as welfare.

Granted that the figures are a bit dated... but consider what the Boston 
Globe had to say in 1996, from

http://www.corporations.org/welfare/globe2.html

--begin quoted text:

Every year, an estimated $150 billion - in the form of direct federal 
subsidies and tax breaks that specifically benefit businesses - is 
funneled to American companies. Critics call it ``corporate welfare.'' 

The $150 billion for corporate subsidies and tax benefits eclipses the
annual budget deficit of $130 billion. It's more than the $145 billion 
paid out annually for the core programs of the social welfare state: Aid 
to Families with Dependent Children (AFDC), student aid, housing, food and 
nutrition, and all direct public assistance (excluding Social Security and 
medical care). 

[snip]

The subsidies range from $1.4 billion annually in price supports for large 
sugar farming interests; to nearly $2 million to help McDonald's market 
Chicken McNuggets in the Third World; to $20,000 for golf balls that 
defense manufacturer Lockheed Martin billed the federal government as an 
``entertainment'' expense. 

[snip]

After World War II, the nation's tax bill was roughly split between 
corporations and individuals. But after years of changes in the federal 
tax code and international economy, the corporate share of 
taxes has declined to a fourth the amount individuals pay, according to 
the US Office of Management and Budget.

--end quoted text

>
>There are those that describe any payment from government to corporation 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>that will never be used by anyone comes to mind as a perfect example.

Something along those lines, yes.  The government needs pencils, for 
example, and those are usually bought from corporations.  The government 
does not seem to need to defray the costs of mergers and acquisitions, 
which it pays for by tax-breaks.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-18T22:54:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-CFE9F3.22545318052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC591F6.1050807@Knology.net> <ba55tc$c19$1@panix1.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-B5A9DE.13174418052003@corp.supernews.com> <ba8i4m$8ta$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
In article <ba8i4m$8ta$1@panix1.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
> Granted that the figures are a bit dated... but consider what the Boston 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> medical care). 

This is interesting.  Recently I have posted to this thread the current 
numbers from the CBO on the various welfare programs.  All of the above 
mentioned are lumpped in the tiny "other" catagory and all are dwarfed 
by Social Security, Medicare and Medicade.  I note that they lump 
"medicare and medicaid into a single, generic "medical care" grouping.

This seems like the famed liberal media bias attempting to avoid 
mentioning that all those means-tested social welfare programs chewed up 
almost 200 billion in 1996 (also excluding Social Security and Medicare).

That doesn't make the claim of 150 billion in tax breaks sound so bad.


> [snip]
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> ``entertainment'' expense. 

I'm right there.  Lets cut off the sugar subsidies and golf balls.


> [snip]
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> --end quoted text

The other way to look at that is:

'since 1945, bracket creep and poorly designed tax laws have raised  
individual contributions to 75% of receipts.'

These things are easy to twist...since both the corporate and individual 
tax rates are far greater than either was in 1946 all you can really say 
is congress was not as effective at raising corporate tax as it was 
individual tax.

> >There are those that describe any payment from government to corporation 
> >as welfare, regardless of goods or services received.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> which it pays for by tax-breaks.

My favorite, which also happens to be a big "corporate welfare" issue 
here in west georgia is "Greenspace".  Hopefully to improve the 
environment, space out industry, and make all the cities of Georgia look 
nicer.

The way the program works, if a corporation will set aside an acre, and 
maintain it, as a public park, they will receive a tax break on that 
acre.  The idea is to make industrial parks look nice and be useful to 
the public.  

Several corporations around here have set up fountains, picnic tables, 
well landscaped areas for free public use.  There is even one with a 
nice bicycle/roller-blade/jogging path.

The city and state recives value (landscaping service, land, security, 
reduced parks and rec budget, lower concentrations of emissions, etc), 
but many call this "corporate welfare".

The fact that the government asked them for some service (set aside 
land, locate in a depressed area) should be enough to free them from the 
charge of "corporate welfare", but often that little fact is ignored.  
That is my first test for corporate welfare.

As to mergers and acqusitions, well, why does the government help you 
defray the cost of your house (via interest payment deductions)?  The 
grouping is somewhat unclear...are we talking about interest on 
financing to buy other businesses?  Government often uses tax breaks to 
encourage behaviours it approves of (like home ownership).  I will 
suggest my second test for corporate welfare -- if the break is applied 
equally to all entities, it is not welfare.

How do you feel about these two criteria?  

It is not welfare if:

   It is provided in response to government request.
   It is given equally to all and not a super-targeted break.

Would you add others?
```

- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-CFE9F3.22545318052003@corp.supernews.com> <ba9hqa$5i2$1@panix1.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-7B4C25.01005419052003@corp.supernews.com> <baap1p$eor$1@panix1.panix.com> <c99d007f.0305190916.4a7a140e@posting.google.com>`

```


Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

Joe - lost your connection to 'West Wing'. Don't think it's quite as bad as you
think.

Firstly - West Wing for me - an excellent cast with a fine-tuned script, and  I
really enjoy the character inter-plays. I'm not naive enough to think this is
the *real* thing. Although on a retrospective documentary, various occupants
from both parties in the White House didn't blast it as being inaccurate.

Doesn't matter to me whether the Prez is Republican or Democrat - it gives an
interesting insight to how things are done/not done. On reflection it is like a
modern version of Shakespeare's 'Much Ado About Nothing'.

The voting public. Low turn-outs for elections prevail in all the democracies,
UK, US and Canada etc......... So if you offered a free course on US
Constitution 101, (and state subsidized food of course <G>), how many takers do
you think you would get ?

Originally intended as pure entertainment, it does allow, (if they can break
themselves away from 'Survivor', 'Desert Island Special - Do you want to screw
my Boyfriend ?', and all the other current crap), to get at least some insight
into how their country works. Makes a non threatening background to popping a
can of beer or stuffing their faces with pop corn..

Jimmy

PS: Our long May weekend, so having 'sent' this, we are off heading West, to
take a look at the mountains - Eat your heart out <G>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** joe_zitzelberger@vsam.org (Joe Zitzelberger)

- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC591F6.1050807@Knology.net> <ba55tc$c19$1@panix1.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-B5A9DE.13174418052003@corp.supernews.com> <ba8i4m$8ta$1@panix1.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-CFE9F3.22545318052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```


Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

> This seems like the famed liberal media bias attempting to avoid
> mentioning that all those means-tested social welfare programs chewed up
> almost 200 billion in 1996 (also excluding Social Security and Medicare).
>

You folks fall back on this one like a hoary chestnut. While the Canwest Group
here in Canada is definitely controlled by Izzy (Israel) Asper and his son who
are devout Liberals, rest assured my local paper the Calgary Herald, which they
own, is most definitely CONSERVATIVE, and semi-sympathetic to the folks to the
right of them, the Reform aka Alliance Party..

As for the 'talking heads' are you trying to suggest that the pair of clowns
Kudlow and Kramer are Liberals or lefties ? I don't know which is which, nor
care, but how the hell did the bald headed, bearded, high-pitched voiced geek
get to be a TV personality.

Similar claims are made about CBC up here showing leftist political bias -  I
never watch CBC enough to care - but I think I would dispute that.

Although PBS displays some of the shows par excellence, the 'Liberal' jibe is
often aimed at them too. In the case of PBS, perhaps they just get too deep into
a topic, searching out truths. Does that make them biased. Biased or not, we do
know that the Right would be quite happy to shut them down by killing Federal
funding.

The BBC has a long history of factual reporting without bias. I pick up BBC
World News at 18:00 via PBS, with anchors in Washington DC and London. It
wouldn't surprise me at all that the Right in the US would claim that their
coverage also shows bias on things American.

Jimmy
```

In article <3EC903C4.B877B778@shaw.ca>,
 "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> right of them, the Reform aka Alliance Party..


I'm not sure what I am falling back on.  I catch the author hiding 51.6 
Billion dollars that plainly alters the point of that paragraph 
specifically and calls the entire article into question.

Neither you or Mr. Dwarf have offered any possible explanation for the 
50 Billion Mr. Sennott has hidden.  Up front his article says that 
"critics" have identified "an estimated $150 billion...`corporate 
welfare.'"  

We have to read much more to get actual names of these critics and their 
numbers.  The 'conservative' Cato institute suggest the real number is 
about $75 Billion.  While the Ralph Nader's group the 
explicitly-NOT-labeled-liberal group Center for the study of Responsive 
Law claims $167 Billion.  Mr. Sennott does say that the 167B is "a 
figure most experts consider high".

But we never find the source of $150 Billion?  Did Mr. Sennott just pick 
a point between 75 and 167 that felt good to him?  

Many of his examples are in the for 'so and so company was eligible for 
X, they didn't take it, but many other companies do'.  Often the 
examples sound as if they fail my first criteria -- that the government 
actually asked them to locate in a blighted area, or research a specific 
subject area, in exchange for a tax break.

Finally, Mr. Sennott seems unable to imagine why any company would ever 
want to leave the his state unless a federal tax incentive was involved.  
I have news for him -- there is a great tax incentive for anyone to 
leave the Peoples Republic of Massachusetts, it is the states own tax 
system.

Mr. Sennott is lacking in many things.  But I will forgive them all if I 
can just find out two simple answers:

   The CBO reports 196B spent on means-tested social programs (e.g. 
Welfare) in 1996 -- Mr. Sennott reports 145B, which 51B did Mr. Sennott 
remove from that figure and why.

   Mr. Sennott prominently reports a figure of 150B as the amount of 
"corporate welfare".  He cites two figures, 167B which he also says is 
"too high" and 75B, which he does not say is "too low", but he doesn't 
use it.  His entire article is based on a number quite close to the one 
that is "too high".  How did he arrive at this number.  

I have two specific cites of highly questionable numbers, with primary 
source evidence to back up said question.  Both of you have suggested 
that this is just another right wing rant on my part, but neither have 
attempted to explain the discrepancies or attempted to provide other 
cites of 'corporate welfare'.

Are you afraid your numbers will not stand up to question?


> The BBC has a long history of factual reporting without bias. I pick up BBC
> World News at 18:00 via PBS, with anchors in Washington DC and London. It
> wouldn't surprise me at all that the Right in the US would claim that their
> coverage also shows bias on things American.

Does Paul Adams know?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T00:31:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-92736D.00315321052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-CFE9F3.22545318052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EC903C4.B877B778@shaw.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-820843.00031920052003@corp.supernews.com> <badcnh$8rm$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
In article <badcnh$8rm$1@panix1.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> In article <joe_zitzelberger-820843.00031920052003@corp.supernews.com>,
> Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'forgive all'; fixing a target makes the moving of it a bit more obvious.


In rebuttal of my assertion that "corporate welfare" is nowhere near the 
problem that "individual welfare" is you posted a story with two very 
important numbers comprising its lead.  One was just wrong, not an 
estimate, just plain wrong.  Inaccurate since 1992.  And possibly the 
target of some journalistic shenanigans on the part of Mr. Sennott.   
The other figure was completely unsourced.

I was willing to write this off with a smart-aleck crack about the 
liberal media, hinting that I might like some clarification from another 
source for those numbers.  In fact, I tried several times.  I most 
certainly did not want to come out and say something as blunt as "Mr. 
Dwarf, do you have any cites, with *accurate* and *sourced* information, 
that corporate welfare is such a great amount compared to individual 
welfare?"

Instead of producing some more credible source, you counter with a 
suggestion that my 'standards' will not allow me to see any figures I do 
not wish to see. While I do have an "opinion" about the matter under 
discussion, and I have made that opinion clear in no uncertain terms,  I 
have quite explicitly eschewed any reference from organizations like 
ReasonPPF, Cato, NR, ATR, etc, because I knew they would be questioned 
as biased.  I limited myself to value-neutral primary sources like the 
CBO and the SSA.

OTOH, Mr Dwarf, you have seen fit to cite an article featured 
prominently on a site, corporations.org, that lists among its explicit 
goals to "stop privatization/resocialize systems"; "public financing of 
elections"; "30 hour work week" and "universal health care".  Which of 
us shows a bias?

The article also shows prominently on "www.ratical.org" and 
"www.citizen.org", both are interesting sites, and readers should decide 
for themselves if they are biased or not.

I am certain that you will call into question the fact that the Boston 
Globe published this story, and that is indeed fair to do so.  But the 
fact remains that the article is an obvious favorite of interest groups 
that could reasonably be called 'biased' in some direction.  I would 
think that someone who has condiments that are my age would have learned 
to believe no more than half of what he reads.

My 'standards' are simple, in fact, they are the usual journalistic 
standards.  Facts must be able to be verified independently via the 
original source.  If "corporate welfare" is actually the problem you 
have said is that original source should be quite easy to find.  Such a 
primary sources might be the IRS, a corporation that took a deduction 
and admitted it, a cite from the tax code allowing such welfare; etc.

Sources with an obvious bias, such as "www.corporations.org" or 
"www.cato.org" will necessarily draw extra scrutiny.  Though in a pinch, 
I would accept a fact if two obviously competing agenda groups (like 
Corporations.org -&- Cato.org) agreed on said fact.  But primary sources 
are better, and anonymous sources like "unnamed critics" are still a 
no-no.

Seriously Mr Dwarf, did you really expect me to accept as unquestionable 
fact a story featured on sites like "www.corporations.org" and written 
by a winner (for an unrelated story) of the "James M. Aronson Award for 
_Social_Justice_Journalism_"?



> [snip]
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> liberal media bias'

You are quite right.  Please forgive me for lumping you in with that.  
You were quite explicit in not suggesting it was a right-wing rant as 
you took your royal services elsewhere.

> and 'hippies'.

Do you prefer the "Woodstock generation"?  Is there anything besides 
that overused "baby boomer" term that I might otherwise use.  I had not 
intended "hippies" to cause any irritation or offense.  Being a die-hard 
fan of both Volkswagens and the Dead it is actually a term I use 
somewhat enviously.

(* as opposed to the I.S. to S.C. and W.M. comments, which were intended 
to evoke an equal and opposite reaction to my own bimonthly reaction.  
When I intend to offend, readers know...;-))

For some silly reason, perhaps the Ford-era mayonnaise jars that you 
still posses, I had thought you were born before the 1946 population 
explosion.
```

As the King of England We have little need for such things.

rather than the other, Mr Zitzelberger?

>think that someone who has condiments that are my age would have learned 
>to believe no more than half of what he reads.

This seems to be a variant of 'If folks was as smart as me...'

>

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>and admitted it, a cite from the tax code allowing such welfare; etc.

Ahhhh, *finally*!  Mr Zitzelberger, this is interesting... on the one hand

on... some criteria you demand line-item citings from budgets.

>
>Sources with an obvious bias, such as "www.corporations.org" or 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>no-no.

Mr Zitzelberger, I believe that anything done by humans is done with a 
bias... but that's my belief, aye.

>
>Seriously Mr Dwarf, did you really expect me to accept as unquestionable 
>fact a story featured on sites like "www.corporations.org" and written 
>by a winner (for an unrelated story) of the "James M. Aronson Award for 
>_Social_Justice_Journalism_"?

Mr Zitzelberger, I did not expect you to trot out 'fellow traveller' 
arguments, no... but how good of you to have done so!

>
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>you took your royal services elsewhere.

Mr Zitzelberger, what kind of evaluation is that?  First you come up with 
'fellow-traveller' accusations and now a statement of 'You were quite 
explicit in not suggesting'?  Are you accustomed to dealing with people 
who do other than laugh at such things?

Here, watch the example and see how it can be done with a bit more sting:  
'Mr (X), the kind of reaction you evidence has been seen most often in
older, married, repressed homosexuals who have not come to grips with
their orientation, express their frustration by beating their wives and
children in secret and afterwards, as all involved weep (for a variety of
reasons), read to them from a Book of Religious Writings.'

As you can see, Mr Zitzelberger, this appears to do rather... little in 
the furthering of Reasoned Discourse... but it's so *colorful*, isn't it?

>
>> and 'hippies'.
>
>Do you prefer the "Woodstock generation"?

No, Mr Zitzelberger... I prefer not to interrupt sentences and, as shown 
by the example I gave above, I prefer to avoid such rhetoric, entire.

>Is there anything besides 
>that overused "baby boomer" term that I might otherwise use.

Find your own terms to express your views, Mr Zitzelberger.

>I had not 
>intended "hippies" to cause any irritation or offense.  Being a die-hard 
>fan of both Volkswagens and the Dead it is actually a term I use 
>somewhat enviously.

Mr Zitzelberger, I do not believe I showed irritation or offense; I 
believe that I reported your use of it, that is all.

>
>(* as opposed to the I.S. to S.C. and W.M. comments, which were intended 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>explosion.

What are here, Mr Zitzelberger, are words on the UseNet; try to address 
those, rather than the birth-date you have imagined for their author... 
the more one deals with them, directly, the less, possibly, one might 
invoke groups.

Now, as for Mr Sennott's sources... I don't know what *you* consider to be 
'diligent research', Mr Zitzelberger, but I have a tendency to perform 
what might be called 'dilettante research'.  I checked the Boston Globe's 
website and sent off an email to their Editorial Department, asking them 
for Mr Sennott's email address... by the time they replied I'd also found 
it myself, posted in a variety of places on the Web.

(I will now violate email etiquette by posting private mail publicly)

I sent Mr Sennott an email, asking:

--begin quoted text:

Good day!  I was researching some data on corporate welfare and I came
across a series you wrote in 1996.  I know this was a few years ago but I
was wondering if you could answer two short questions as to the source of
your date:

1) The CBO (Congressional Budget Office) reports $196B spent on
means-tested social programs (e.g. Welfare) in 1996 while you reported a
figure of $145B.  It is understood that CBO figures are subject to
revision after issue; what was the source of your figure?

2) You reported a figure of $150B as the amount of "corporate welfare";
you compare this to two figures, $167B which you said was "too high" and
$75B, which you did not use.  How did you arrive at the figure of $150B?

I thank you for your time in considering this request; it is greatly
appreciated!

--end quoted text

... and a few hours later I received a reply:

--begin quoted text:

Hi. I am now based in London and for the five years previous was based in
Jerusalem, so all of those notes and that data are far from my memory.
Unfortunately, any notes or back up documents I would have saved from that
series would be in storage. I am sorry to be of more  help.
Charles Sennott
Boston Globe - London Bureau

--end quoted text

... and there you have it, Mr Zitzelberger.  Nothing whatsoever to back up 
the figures except the status of their publication... and besides, too, 
those figures are quoted by... You Know What Kind of organisations, as 
well.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T09:24:45-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-236439.09244523052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-820843.00031920052003@corp.supernews.com> <badcnh$8rm$1@panix1.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-92736D.00315321052003@corp.supernews.com> <bag3eh$n6l$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

In article <bag3eh$n6l$1@panix1.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> [snip]
> >I was willing to write this off with a smart-aleck crack about the 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> them, say, a presumption of accuracy.

The figures made it by the fact-checkers at the Boston Globe.  The paper 
was rocked by several scandals for fabrications a few years after the 
story in question.

Two star columnists, one a Pulitzer nominee, were both canned for 
repeated, blatant fabrication and plagiarism.  The questions always was 
-- where were the fact-checkers.  In both cases it took external 
entities to raise the red flag.

The only reason I remember this five year old scandal is because it is 
back in the headlines once again.  Joseph Blair was recently busted by 
The New York Times for fabrication of his stories.  He trained as an 
intern for the Boston Globe in the mid-1990s, and the paper is now going 
back and reviewing all the things he did for accuracy.

The Boston Globe had a very severe problem, pervasive, systemic problem 
with fact-checking.  Specifically, that they did not do much of it at 
all.  I like to think that they have gotten their act together and 
cleaned house a bit since the summer of 1998.  But this story ran in 
1996.

In light of the recent coverage of Blair and the historical problems at 
the Globe, I almost thought you were making a joke by posting a story 
from that paper.  That is why I immediately started checking some of 
numbers.

Some, like the sugar subsidy are very easy to check (and it has grown to 
1.9B - 2.0B since), some, as I have stated, are not easy to find.

I would not go as far as put the Boston Globes creditability on par with 
the National Enquirer, but it ranks quite poorly as major media outlets 
go.  You ask for a presumption of accuracy for a story published at a 
paper with obviously broken fact-checking and rampant fabrication by its 
journalists, this is not easy.  Involvement in one scandal would be 
enough to raise serious questions, involvement in three raises a 
presumption of inaccuracy.


> Mr Zitzelberger, I made no such suggestion; please address what I wrote, 
> not what you have wished me to have written.  Unretracted figures 
> published in a front-page article in a major-market newspaper do not seem 
> to be sufficiently accurate for you; I have asked you for the quality of 
> answer which might be.  You've not supplied it.

I've repeatedly asked for cited and sourced data -or- corroboration from 
another source.


> >While I do have an "opinion" about the matter under 
> >discussion, and I have made that opinion clear in no uncertain terms,  I 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> call them 'value-neutral' is, according to some, Just Plain Wrong.  

Figures of actual dollars spent in 1996 are exactly known by October of 
1996, give or take a few weeks.  By July of 1996 the figure is not know 
exactly, but neither should they be off by 50 Billion, or 25% of the 
total.

How we interpret the number will add value.  But the number itself is 
just the number.


> >I would 
> >think that someone who has condiments that are my age would have learned 
> >to believe no more than half of what he reads.
> 
> This seems to be a variant of 'If folks was as smart as me...'

Nope.  This is a variant of the old saying "Don't believe half of what 
you read, and none of what you hear."  Printing is easy, but it does not 
guarantee truth to the item printed.


> >My 'standards' are simple, in fact, they are the usual journalistic 
> >standards.  Facts must be able to be verified independently via the 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> on... some criteria you demand line-item citings from budgets.

I have checked those and other items.  The only other mention I found 
about the Golf Balls was a broken link, but since it was .00002 billion 
I directed my attention to other items listed.  I am looking for support 
for two figures I find questionable -- in other words, I'm doing my own 
fact-checking.


> >Sources with an obvious bias, such as "www.corporations.org" or 
> >"www.cato.org" will necessarily draw extra scrutiny.  Though in a pinch, 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> arguments, no... but how good of you to have done so!

If the facts are correct the spin put on them is irrelevant.  If the 
facts are incorrect then it is more properly called propaganda.  When an 
article uses uncited, unsourced figures (e.g. "critics claim it is 
150B") and the article is an obvious favorite groups on the Ralph Nader 
wing of the discussion, I consider it questionable.  

As I said previously, if groups on the opposite end of the discussion 
agree with Naderite groups that 150B is the number then it becomes less 
of an issue.  If they do not agree, and no source for that number can be 
found, I still consider it an issue.  In all cases, an actual source is 
best.

My problem with a 'social justice' award is the very nature of 
journalism.  A journalist is supposed to report on the world.  A 
journalist that is trying to change the world is something different 
entirely.


> >> [snip]
> >>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> the furthering of Reasoned Discourse... but it's so *colorful*, isn't it?

Mr Dwarf, you have a very colorful way of saying 'and with that comment 
I will now direct my royal attentions elsewhere' -- sometimes this is 
hard to interpret without facial expressions, voice tone, etc.  I 
originally read it as a "here he goes again..." sort of comment.  It 
certainly looked like one, but I'm experience speaking with royalty  is 
quite limited and I am sure I am wrong, please forgive my presumption.



> ... and there you have it, Mr Zitzelberger.  Nothing whatsoever to back up 
> the figures except the status of their publication... and besides, too, 
> those figures are quoted by... You Know What Kind of organisations, as 
> well.

Every organization that wishes should be able to use those facts and 
figures, regardless of their political leanings.  My concern is with 
organizations of one political leaning using figures that have no  
support.

To promote a non-fact into a fact, simply because it has been printed 
somewhere, is fraught with problems.  Printing is cheap Mr. Dwarf.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T17:54:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ECA6839.976C6432@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC591F6.1050807@Knology.net> <ba55tc$c19$1@panix1.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-B5A9DE.13174418052003@corp.supernews.com> <ba8i4m$8ta$1@panix1.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-CFE9F3.22545318052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EC903C4.B877B778@shaw.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-820843.00031920052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```


Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

> In article <3EC903C4.B877B778@shaw.ca>,
>  "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> > here in Canada <snip>.....

> I'm not sure what I am falling back on.  I catch the author hiding 51.6
> Billion dollars that plainly alters the point of that paragraph
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You missed the point entirely. Not once did I make reference to Budget figures,
my observation was on the phrase, "This seems like the famed liberal media bias
attempting ......".

Either you or the Boston Globe may be correct, depending upon what spin you take
on the figures. Frankly I don't care. But does one writer's interpretation
constitute 'media bias' ? To have any impact, is the Boston Globe a 'national'
that can be easily purchased in say Tennessee, California or Texas - or is it
written with a local bias ?

No matter what the philosophy or figures, both are subject to interpretation.
Hopefully readers of the Boston Globe, or the Calgary Herald for that matter,
digest contents and make their own judgements. Incidentally, what does keep the
Herald 'lively' is that there are arguments, printed side-by-side offering both a
right and left wing spin.

"Left wing bias......",  whether down there or up here is a constant bogey
offered by the Right, and to mind smacks of McCarthyism and the communist ogre.

I wonder whether you would consider the following from to-day's paper as showing
a left wing bias, (repeated as an extract from the Vancouver Sun)  I have
paraphrased for shortness, but retained the context :-

*******
"The American military and scientific teams hunting for Saddam Hussein's supposed
WMD (weapons of mass destruction) are being pulled out of Iraq frustrated and
empty-handed after a month of fruitless searching.

The teams called the 75th Exploitation Task Force, have examined almost all of
the nearly 100 sites, identified by intelligence agencies before the invasion as
the most likely  places for Saddam to have hidden WMD. They have found no trace
of the weapons or development programs........<They found nothing>......

The hunt will be continued by another team, the Iraq Survey Group, but most of
the weapon experts are being withdrawn for lack of anything to do.

.....American military is perplexed and puzzled..... A sense of unease about the
justification for the invasion came through when Maj. Gen. David Petraeus,
commander 101st Airborne Div talked to reporters in N. Iraq earlier this week.
'There's no question there were chemical weapons years ago', he said. 'I just
don't know whether is was all destroyed years ago'.

< a reference to UK concerns and does lack of WMD evidence constitute a breach of
international law>

....... Others are going one step further and saying intelligence reports were
made to fit the highly ideological stance of top advisers to U.S. President GWB.
Anything that didn't fit what the Bush advisers wanted to hear - which appears to
have been a good deal of the intelligence product - was discarded.

..... Seymour Hersh <a veteran journalist in U.S , who has reported on
administration bloopers for past 30 years > says the key intelligence analysis
group in preparation for the Iraq invasion was the Office of Special Plans in the
Pentagon, headed by Abram Shulsky, a long-time political ally of of key Bush
hawks Rumsfeld, Wolfowitz and Perle.

<Goes on to say adoption of Chalabi, (You know, the guy who in absentia, has a 20
year odd sentence hanging over his head in Jordan for financial finagling), and
his exiled opposition group, the Iraqi National Congress added to the self
delusion.>

<Concludes - original reasoning for GW2 was WMD but is now shifting to the fact
Saddam was a monstrous dictator>"

******

So you know where I'm at . I supported invasion of Iraq, but NOT based on WMD,
rather that we already *knew* Saddam was a monster, and in the long term things
could only get worse - but it was Daddy Bush who should have taken him out 10
years back.

Reading the article above, I discern no "media bias" and for my money it is fact
rather than fiction. Seeing the body language on TV of the three cowboys referred
to above in the White House - I have no problem with the assertions.

Told ya. U.S.A. will be SOL on credibility if you don't turn up those WMDs.

Jimmy
```

On 18-May-2003, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> Every year, an estimated $150 billion - in the form of direct federal
> subsidies and tax breaks that specifically benefit businesses - is
> funneled to American companies. Critics call it ``corporate welfare.''

One trouble with such statements is that someone who wants to make this
"corporate welfare" look huge, they include tax breaks.   Lots of us don't see
tax breaks as welfare (and we accept tax breaks ourselves when we can).

Another trouble is when we get local.   We don't mind spending tax money to
build an infrastructure to attract the right kind of jobs to our neighborhood.
The difficulty is in defining "our neighborhood".
```
