```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>>There has -got- to be a better
>>system than we have.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> doing what?'

Taxation

>>So what you're saying is that you see taxes of government taking what's
>>already theirs?
> 
> 
> That certainly does seem to be a conclusion, aye.

This is why we will invariably reach an impasse on this topic.  :) 
Which is cool - if all of us agreed on everything, we'd all be 
redundant.  Since the ownership of the money in question is fundamental 
to any tax policy or discussion (and definitely important when you start 
trying to figure out "equitable" taxes), we'll never arrive at the same 
destination.

>>>An increase in circulating money supply without an associated  increase in
>>>available goods and services is said by economists to most frequently
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> then the result would be inflation.

I'm guessing that the intent of turning up the presses would be that if 
people had more money, more goods and services would be offered.  Else, 
why would a government intentionally start inflation?

> Each Federal Reserve Bank (there are twelve of them, last I looked) has 
> its own home page... to make things even more intricate there does not 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> off to you:

Thanks for being so sporting...  :)  I was getting nowhere fast.  I 
really don't mind being proven wrong, as long as I can learn in the process.

> http://www.ustreas.gov/education/fact-sheets/currency/distribution.html
> 
> .... where there is nothing about winning, nothing about trophies... just 
> dry facts.

So, this seems to be saying that every year, they "guess" how much money 
they'll need, order it, print it, and hold it, waiting for banks to come 
ask for it.  That's interesting - a lot can happen in a year.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T06:33:37-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bad0a1$4f$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC98B47.5090804@Knology.net> <bac6vn$iig$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC9AE1B.1050506@Knology.net>`

```
In article <3EC9AE1B.1050506@Knology.net>,
LX-i  <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>>>There has -got- to be a better
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Taxation

All right... what are you seeing as the criteria for 'better' when it 
comes to systems of taxation?

>
>>>So what you're saying is that you see taxes of government taking what's
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>destination.

Given the facts presented - that government determines the supply of
money, that government physically creates and distributes it, that
disposition of it and that government disposes of it when government feels 
the need to... what aspects of 'ownership' are left to anyone else?  How 
can one come to a conclusion that there are aspects of ownership outside 
of these?

(Notice, by the bye, that I am not arguing 'beliefs' or 'my view' or 'This 
I Hold True'... what are presented here seem to be matters of fact)

>
>>>>An increase in circulating money supply without an associated  increase in
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>why would a government intentionally start inflation?

There have been reasons in the past - the classic was 'spending your way
out of debt' - but no matter what the reason the end results with which I
am familiar have been the same, a ruined economy, a devestated currency
and years of reconstruction.

(A delightful side-point in this is the concept of seigniorage, the 
difference between the value of money and the cost to produce it.  Having 
a debt in a currency which one controls is *such* a tricky matter!)

>
>> Each Federal Reserve Bank (there are twelve of them, last I looked) has 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>really don't mind being proven wrong, as long as I can learn in the process.

The learning is, to me, the most enjoyable part... others here may speak 
of 'winning'; I prefer Jefferson's 'lighted taper' analogy.

>
>> http://www.ustreas.gov/education/fact-sheets/currency/distribution.html
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>ask for it.  That's interesting - a lot can happen in a year.

A lot can be kept from happening, as well... I recall, a few years back, 
some folks were getting a bit riled up because the Fed, anticipating folks 
withdrawing bunches of cash because they feared a collapse of the banking 
system due to Y2K bugs (remember those heady days?), printed up a bunch of 
new currency and put it into warehouses.

Some folks pointed to this as a Sure Sign of inflation and ruin to come; 
no amount of persuasion seemed to be sufficient to convince them that if 
the money never saw circulation it would have no economic effects.

DD
```

Actually, about 16 or 17 percent of the federal budget is spent on the

less that senior NCO or officer rank qualifed for food stamps, WIC, etc.  
Yet somehow, whenever the topic of funding comes up people are quick to 
say "If we just cut defense spending..."

$400 billion out of $2.1 trillion is a sizable chunk, but it is half the 
annual outlay for Social Security and Medicare.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-16T10:09:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec451ce.42517668@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC28EDE.C8467E13@istar.ca> <FAEwa.297$vk3.68131@twister.austin.rr.com> <joe_zitzelberger-86822F.08405315052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>In article <FAEwa.297$vk3.68131@twister.austin.rr.com>,
> "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>citizens and welfare mothers.

As a practical matter, elected politicians (not government) are following the
incentives we provided, are 'working from within the system' as we set it up. 

Politicians' first, perhaps only, objective is reelection. The best way to
accomplish that is by building a constituancy who will vote for the candidate or
party because they feel like they got more money than they gave. 

Suppose a party, lets call them Democrats, takes from 20% of the population and
gives to 50% (while ignoring the other 30%). The 20% might be pissed off and vow
to never vote for a Democrat, but the 50% will reelect them. 

In fact, the upper 1% (in income) of the US population pay 36% of Federal income
tax, and 23% of all Federal taxes. The upper 10% pay well over 50% of Federal
taxes. 

http://www.cbpp.org/4-16-02tax.htm

Our system of government encourages higher taxes, higher entitlements, and two
economic classes, with at least 51% of the population in the lower class. This
has nothing to do with idealism, everything to do with pragmatism.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-18T11:44:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba89ph$nso$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC5991D.6050903@Knology.net> <ba573p$ghv$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC7122A.2090403@Knology.net>`

```
In article <3EC7122A.2090403@Knology.net>,
LX-i  <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>>>>.... and given the role of corporations in politics... it might be
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Man - two apologies in 2 messages...  Sorry...

Most gracious of you.

>
>Are you implying by the above that you think it is not good for the 
>economy to encourage business growth?

Implication is in the mind of the beholder, of course... but thanks much 
for causing me to take a closer look at what I am doing, such examination 
can be valuable.

I would say that 'business' is like cutting open a person's flesh... it is 
a neutral act.  A cut in the skin can be done during an assault; this is 
generally considered 'not a good thing'.  A cut in the skin can also be 
done by a surgeon performing an emergency appendectomy in order to save a 
life; this is generally considered 'a good thing'.  The cutting, however, 
is neutral.

Likewise with 'business'... buying, selling, indulging in the exchange of 
goods and services, etc. can be 'not a good thing' or 'a good thing'.

>Or are you just curious as to 
>which political party is generally thought of as more pro-business?

I am aware that, frequently, in the United States of America the 
Republican party is thought of as being more pro-business... but as stated 
above, 'doing business' is a neutral act.  Generating fruits and 
vegetables, for instance, is a 'business', and allowing folks to get food 
more readily and for less cost is generally thought of as 'a good thing' 
while killing songbirds with pesticides is generally thought of as 'a bad 
thing'.

Gets kind of... complicated, no?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-18T18:20:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<z4WcnSgpx8SiiFWjXTWJkg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC5991D.6050903@Knology.net> <ba573p$ghv$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC7122A.2090403@Knology.net> <ba89ph$nso$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com>
>
> I would say that 'business' is like cutting open a person's flesh... it is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> is neutral.

There are governments where 'business' is a 'bad thing' unless engaged in
solely by the government. Even in the US, only the government is allowed to
run lotteries, sell auto license plates, execute prisoners, and several
other (otherwise lucrative) sidelines.

>
> Likewise with 'business'... buying, selling, indulging in the exchange of
> goods and services, etc. can be 'not a good thing' or 'a good thing'.

Oh but 'business' is always a 'good thing' from an economic perspective. In
every free exchange the wealth of all the participants increases.

>
>  Generating fruits and
> vegetables, for instance, is a 'business',

No. Not unless the produce can be exchanged for something of value.

> and allowing folks to get food
> more readily and for less cost is generally thought of as 'a good thing'

Getting food with greater difficulty and at greater cost can be 'a good
thing' too. Such as '100% Organic" foods or imported rarities.

> while killing songbirds with pesticides is generally thought of as 'a bad
> thing'.

Would be. Except it's a terrible waste to kill songbirds with pesticides. It
takes too much time. You have to hold them under, one at at time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T18:15:08-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC2CDFC.2090806@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net>,
> LX-i  <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> system to change it or find another place more to your liking.'

I'm happy with where I am.  (And, like I said, I realize that I'm a 
government employee, although we kept working during the government 
shutdown.)  I'm working within the system to change it, as a Republican 
(not Libertarian).  But, regardless of its odor, you can't argue that 
it's untrue...  :)

My gripe is not with taxes, but with the system.  Governments must 
collect money from its citizens (especially one "of the people, by the 
people, for the people") to fund common services.  However, this list of 
"common services" for which Americans are now taxed has grown into a 
humongous kluge of services, defense, and social programs.  In the 
process, the Federal government controls more and money.

Then, you add on the extreme legislators (on both sides) who put 
behavior caveats on Federal money (i.e., you will not get Federal 
highway funds unless your legal drinking age is 21), and you get a 
Federal government much larger than I believe any of the founders 
envisioned.  (And yes, I know there's nothing "extreme" about my example 
above, but there are others that are.)

I think a smaller government is the way to go (and not Clinton's 
definition of "fewer employees") - a government that does what it must 
to provide for the safety and common defense of its people, and stays 
out of the way otherwise.  Smaller government means a government smaller 
in scope, not personnel (though certainly personnel will follow).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T21:19:42-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vc5qtaf2rfc85e@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC2CDFC.2090806@Knology.net>`

```

LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message
news:3EC2CDFC.2090806@Knology.net...
[snip]
>
> Then, you add on the extreme legislators (on both sides) who put
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> above, but there are others that are.)

It is an example of usurpation and is a violation of the
guarantee clause (Article IV, Section 4) in which "The
United States shall guarantee to every State in this
Union a Republican form of government, ...."

In this case, the people delegate to their States the
power to establish a minimum drinking age. Then for
the United States, to establish a minimum drinking
age by act of Congress, is to renege on the guarantee
clause by denying the power of the people to delegate
who is to establish the minimum drinking age. This
means the act of Congress is unconstitutional to
the extent that it attempts to establish a minimum
drinking age.

The guarantee clause has not been fully tested by the
Supreme Court. Many State cases relied on the Tenth
Amendment, which has not been properly interpreted.

Of course, all of this is my opinion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T20:31:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC2EDE4.2000707@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC2CDFC.2090806@Knology.net> <vc5qtaf2rfc85e@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message
> news:3EC2CDFC.2090806@Knology.net...
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Amendment, which has not been properly interpreted.

But, they're not -actually- establishing the drinking age.  They're just 
saying, if you want any money, you've got to behave.  I'm not sure that 
caveats on money are unconstitutional, but if the voters got upset and 
"threw the bums out" the first chance they got, those who would replace 
them would surely get the message.  Of course, this assumes an informed 
electorate...

> Of course, all of this is my opinion.

And it's a very good one - I'm sure a lawyer could make a case for a "de 
facto" law or something for the "caveat" I'm describing above.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T22:35:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EYKcnV0lGL1jl16jXTWJlg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC2CDFC.2090806@Knology.net> <vc5qtaf2rfc85e@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
>
> The guarantee clause has not been fully tested by the
> Supreme Court. Many State cases relied on the Tenth
> Amendment, which has not been properly interpreted.
>

The Tenth Amendment (for our non-US friends: "Powers not delegated to the
United States by this Constitution, nor prohibited by it to the States, are
reserved to the States respectively, or to the people") is as dead as the
amendment outlawing alcoholic beverages.

You're correct: The Tenth has not been properly (or ever) interpreted
because, to the judiciary, it doesn't make a lick of sense.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vc74dpn8s9im1b@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC2CDFC.2090806@Knology.net> <vc5qtaf2rfc85e@corp.supernews.com> <EYKcnV0lGL1jl16jXTWJlg@giganews.com>`

```

JerryMouse <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:EYKcnV0lGL1jl16jXTWJlg@giganews.com...
>
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> United States by this Constitution, nor prohibited by it to the States,
are
> reserved to the States respectively, or to the people") is as dead as the
> amendment outlawing alcoholic beverages.
>
> You're correct: The Tenth has not been properly (or ever) interpreted
> because, to the judiciary, it doesn't make a lick of sense.

< http://www.access.gpo.gov/congress/senate/constitution/toc.html >
Document 'con021.pdf', 12 pages.

In the entire discussion of the interpretation of the Tenth
Amendment, there is no reference to powers reserved
'to the people'. The discussion is extensive with respect
to the relations between the United States and the States;
but has nothing concerning the people.

In a Republican form of government, supreme power is
retained by the people. This concept was well understood
and was expressed in the Declaration of Independence.
The words appear in the Constitution, itself. Yet, the Court
seems unable to take the position that
-- The people delegated powers to the United States
-- The people prohibited exercise of some powers by
    the States
-- The people delegated powers to the States
-- The people retained all other powers for their use
    to delegate or exercise at their discretion.

This view of the Tenth Amendment would greatly curtail
the ability of Congress to interfere with the power of the
people to make choices for themselves.

I see the problem as interest groups influencing the
members of Congress to make laws that impose
the views of these groups upon the whole of the
population. Remove the ability of Congress to make
such laws and the bulk of the problem goes away.
```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
>

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Nah. Interest groups - and their money - are a couterweight to the unwashed

sometimes another. It mostly averages out.

have for dinner) or you can have freedom. Pick one.

- **Message-ID:** `<vc8jc1ab8nsv88@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC2CDFC.2090806@Knology.net> <vc5qtaf2rfc85e@corp.supernews.com> <EYKcnV0lGL1jl16jXTWJlg@giganews.com> <vc74dpn8s9im1b@corp.supernews.com> <2sCcneuXy8YBIF6jXTWJkw@giganews.com> <vc82at9o257r05@corp.supernews.com> <kTOdnanMHOa4o1mjXTWcoQ@giganews.com>`

JerryMouse <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:kTOdnanMHOa4o1mjXTWcoQ@giganews.com...
>
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > Congress-critters have to contantly balance the desires of the
'special
> > > interests' and the uninformed public. Sometimes things swing one way,
> > > sometimes another. It mostly averages out.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Special interests are OK at the state and local level?

Congress was given an extraordinary power to tax,
then arrogated an extraordinary power to spend and
control people.

When special interest groups influence members of
Congress they have a small audience and access to
a lot of money to spend and power to control people.

States don't have nearly as much power to tax and
spend or to control. These powers are better controlled
by the people. There are also many more legislators
to influence.

Thus, the ability of special interest groups to force
the people to adhere to their views is reduced.

Restoring the limits on Congressional power will
force special interest groups to move to the state
and local level where they can be watched more
closely.
```

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:ba0868$7ho$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 14-May-2003, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Of course, they can't set a national speed limit either - but they can
bribe the
> states into compliance!!!

The National government of the United States extends
externally to the relations with other nations.

The Federal government extends internally to the States.

Hence, the phrase should be 'unconstitutional Federal
speed limit'.

Beginning in the late 1890's, rulings of the Court began
to blur the distinction between the National and Federal
governments. By 1937, the distinction all but disappeared.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T15:50:27-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba15jj$sdt$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC189B6.3010209@Knology.net> <b9te87$2ih$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC2CDFC.2090806@Knology.net> <vc5qtaf2rfc85e@corp.supernews.com> <ba0868$7ho$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <vc82arptui0d04@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Problem I see with this whole discussion is that what is constitutional at
any given moment is precisely what the Supreme Court decides is
constitutional at that moment.  It's not clear to me that the concept of
"judicial review" is itself constitutional, however clear it might have been
to Marshall in 1803.  The only ways we, as citizens, have to decide in any
sort of semi-permanent way what is constitutional and what is not is or not
are through amendments to the constitution.  Though reversal of a final
constitutionality decision on a given topic might be rare, it's not
unthinkable; generally speaking, if the Supreme Court decides that a law
(federal or otherwise) making cutting your toenails on Thursday a capital
crime is constitutional, it's constitutional, until such time as somebody
can convince the justices to revisit the issue *and* reverse the prior
ruling.   Regardless of whether *you* or *I* think such a law is
constitutional or not.

    -Chuck Stevens


"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:vc82arptui0d04@corp.supernews.com...
>
> Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

Exactly.

There's no such thing as an 'unconstitutional law,' for if it's
unconstitutional, it's not a law.

Laws don't get born unconstitutional - they get denominated as such by the
courts.

<a id="t-8dd69a03c0"></a>
## Thread: OT: Sharks, Lawyers, Barristers — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Sharks, Lawyers, Barristers

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T17:05:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC121F3.FD7A50B1@shaw.ca>`

```
Howard, can't recall which thread you asked in.

So Michael answered 'lawyer' but I think my 'solicitor' is more accurate
- more 'genteel' and  much closer in definition to the ladies of the
night who prowl the sidewalks <G>

Pure guess - part of the hierarchy (caste) system in England.
Lawyers/solicitors are the minions allowed to advise you on divorce,
property exchange, notaries public etc., but oh never allowed to
represent you in criminal cases. They take your case 'upstairs' to
barristers, who represent you in the courts - at a handsome fee of
course.

Have to do your own homework on this one. When it started no idea, but I
think it is associated with the Inns of Court in London's Gray's Inn
area close to the Old Bailey. You do your grunt work as a lawyer, become
a partner in a prestigious law firm, don your stupid wig and gown and
get to perform in front of a guy up on the bench also bewigged and
begowned (in red).

Think of dear Old Rumpole of the Bailey. Even more hilarious - do you
recall from years ago an episode of LA Law. A Brit barrister shows up in
an LA court bewigged and begowned <G>.

From a small fact index, may be slightly out of date with what occurs
now as this is from about 1945/46  :-

Qualified member of that branch of the legal profession which has the
exclusive right to plead in the superior courts of law in England and
Ireland. In Scotland he is termed an advocate.

The above then cross-referenced me to 'Law' :-

.....Barristers and solicitors, who plead in the courts as advocate or
counsel, or otherwise act as a client's legal agent, form the two great
branches of the profession. To qualify for a call to the English Bar
takes at least three years, and the candidate must become a student of
one of the four Inns of Court in London - the Inner Temple, Middle
Temple, Lincoln's Inn and Gray's Inn. A large proportion of law students
who now enter the Inns have taken a university degree. .... a certain
number of dinners must be attended in the hall of an Inn to "keep
terms". The higher grades in the profession - King's(Queen's) counsel
(K.C/Q.C) or judge - are open only to those who have established a good
practice.

To become a solicitor (once called an "attorney"), the usual method is
for a youth to be articled to a member of the profession for five years;
or he may serve in a solicitor's office as an ordinary clerk for 10
years. There are three examinations,
(controlled by the Incorporated Law Society). Passing the intermediate
and final examinations, and the payment of certain fees, admit the
candidate to practice. (See also Courts of Justice)       - Hey ! I
ain't typing that as well !

Not deadly accurate, but looks like my guess was pretty close. Step by
step, the whole biz., in its current form, probably dates from Tudor
times. Think on 'Man for All Seasons'. Thomas More as the Lord
Chancellor (of the Exchequer),  was responsible for the Courts Chancery,
and prior to the Reformation there were the Ecclesiastical Courts..

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Sharks, Lawyers, Barristers

- **Date:** 2003-05-13T17:23:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC129CB.E5C17301@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3EC121F3.FD7A50B1@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> Howard, can't recall which thread you asked in.
>
> So Michael answered 'lawyer' but I think my 'solicitor' is more accurate
> - more 'genteel' and  much closer in definition to the ladies of the
> night who prowl the sidewalks <G>

A friend of mine (then just a female lawyer - now a judge so I won't name
her) was once walking in Gas Town (a slightly seedy part of Vancouver) from
a client's office back to hers.  She happened to meet the Chief Justice who
asked her "Miss ****, what are you doing in an area like this?".  She
sweetly replied "Soliciting, M'Lud."

As you are aware, a Canadian lawyer puts on his business card "Barrister and
Solicitor" since the profession is not divided.

> Pure guess - part of the hierarchy (caste) system in England.
> Lawyers/solicitors are the minions allowed to advise you on divorce,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> course.

I think that this has been changed recently in respect of some offences.

> To become a solicitor (once called an "attorney"), the usual method is
> for a youth to be articled to a member of the profession for five years;
> or he may serve in a solicitor's office as an ordinary clerk for 10
> years.

I think that's out of date too now.  Normally a law degree would be obtained
prior to articling.
```

Ed Guy wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" wrote:

Haven't been to Vancouver for over a decade - but loved it. Always managed to be
there when it wasn't raining. ( When I asked a former Portsmuthian about the
rain, he replied, "You ain't seen nuthin !"). I gather that at least in the
daylight hours, having been spruced up, Gas Town is an interesting place to
visit.

<a id="t-30fda30dc6"></a>
## Thread: May 13th IBM Announcements and COBOL — 4 msgs

### May 13th IBM Announcements and COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-13T13:37:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9re13$rr5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
A couple of (indirect) implications for COBOL (and PL/1 - because of LE
statements) users on IBM mainframes from today's announcements from IBM.

From:
  http://www.ibmlink.ibm.com/usalets&parms=H_203-13 1

"The following actions are planned to be effective with z/OS V1.6: ...

Language Environment's use of Run Time Library Support (RTLS) will be
withdrawn. This function is used primarily in run-time migration. Given the
stability and the upward compatibility being provided by the Language
Environment run-time library in recent releases of OS/390 and z/OS, this
functionality is no longer required. "

and from
    http://www.ibmlink.ibm.com/usalets&parms=H_203-128

"In order to support the C/C++ for z/VM compiler and updated runtime
libraries for C/C++, COBOL, and PL/I, the Language Environment component has
been updated to the level shipped with z/OS V1.4 and is integrated in z/VM
V4.4."

    ****

I *assume* (no statement from IBM to this effect) that the latter statement
means that the final "end" of IBM COBOL for OS/390 & z/OS can now be
announced.  It already has a "limited" life-expectancy under z/OS, but until
LE was upgraded under z/VM, IBM wasn't able to offer Enterprise COBOL for
that platform.  Now that LE is upgraded, I would expect (but have no "inside
information" to this effect) that a release of Enterprise COBOL for z/VM
should be coming sooner than later.
```

## Thread: A series decimal arithmetic was Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: A series decimal arithmetic was Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T11:22:52-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC2513C.27FAB23A@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8d513b.5582563@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304041259.54d8d7cd@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net> <b6neh4$fi7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f9b30.79006238@news.optonline.net> <b6s5g6$2v3c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3E91A5CF.D7C18000@istar.ca>`

```
What hardware (microcode or chip) support is there for decimal
arithmetic in the A/NX/LX series and in whatever the Univac 1100/2200
evolved into?
```

#### ↳ Re: A series decimal arithmetic was Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T11:44:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9u2q7$1na5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8d513b.5582563@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304041259.54d8d7cd@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net> <b6neh4$fi7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f9b30.79006238@news.optonline.net> <b6s5g6$2v3c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3E91A5CF.D7C18000@istar.ca> <3EC2513C.27FAB23A@istar.ca>`

```
Arithmetic on the MCP/AS system is done in binary.  There was some
discussion close to twenty years ago about the possibility of building
hardware to do decimal arithmetic directly as the V Series did, but
ultimately the architects reached the conclusion that much better "bang for
the buck" could be obtained by improving the decimal-to-binary and
binary-to-decimal conversion operators, and that the costs of a decimal
arithmetic box on the MCP/AS system could not be justified in terms of
performance gain.

I can't speak to the OS2200/IX line.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
news:3EC2513C.27FAB23A@istar.ca...
> What hardware (microcode or chip) support is there for decimal
> arithmetic in the A/NX/LX series and in whatever the Univac 1100/2200
> evolved into?
>
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A series decimal arithmetic was Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T12:16:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9u4mi$1ohi$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8d513b.5582563@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304041259.54d8d7cd@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net> <b6neh4$fi7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f9b30.79006238@news.optonline.net> <b6s5g6$2v3c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3E91A5CF.D7C18000@istar.ca> <3EC2513C.27FAB23A@istar.ca> <b9u2q7$1na5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
I wrote:

<< Arithmetic on the MCP/AS system is done in binary.  >>

A clarification:  it's done in the native word-oriented format of the
machine.  Technically, the word contains a signed binary mantissa and a
signed exponent, the exponent representing the power of 8 by which the
mantissa is to be scaled.  Canonic single-precision integers form a subset
of that format, having an exponent of zero and the binary representation of
the value right-justified as the mantissa in the word.  Most COBOL-oriented
calculations start out with integer values and the results are integerized
(with rounding or with truncation as appropriate in context) before (and if)
they are to be converted back to some sort of external-decimal format.  The
"odometer effect" on intermediate results as they are stored in destinations
is handled as part of the scaling process through explicit instructions.

The B5/6/7000 series had an architectural anomaly with conversion
(determination of the sign of a decimal item was made based on contents, not
on PICTURE); so long as the data matched the PICTURE, everything was OK, but
if it didn't, the results presented some problems.  This anomaly was
resolved in the A Series by the addition of instructions that explicitly
stated whether there was a sign, and if so where it was located, for the
input conversion and pack operations.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A series decimal arithmetic was Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii

- **From:** gary drummond <gdrumm@sbcglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T02:27:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ECC35DC.9392FF57@sbcglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3e8d513b.5582563@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0304041259.54d8d7cd@posting.google.com> <3e8ef7a0.37127268@news.optonline.net> <b6neh4$fi7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e8f9b30.79006238@news.optonline.net> <b6s5g6$2v3c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3E91A5CF.D7C18000@istar.ca> <3EC2513C.27FAB23A@istar.ca> <b9u2q7$1na5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b9u4mi$1ohi$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> 
> I wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>     -Chuck Stevens

The 1110 allowed 511/512? (I have trouble counting... :>) digits
in native ISO ASCII in decimal math hardware instructions. If I
could find my books, I could tell you if they had "packed" decimal
instructions, and the length of the addends/etc...

The follow-on systems supported the previous instructions, and also
added more of the IBM-type decimal instructions. All of them required
loading a register (pair?) with a binary editing mask for the edit
instructions, but with about 43 registers, they could be loaded once
during initial load and left there.

I'll email any other "facts" if I can find my books.

Gary
```

## Thread: Mircofocus and html preprocessor — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Mircofocus and html preprocessor

- **From:** "Steve Rainbird" <steve.rainbird@nospam.mssint.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T15:15:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9tmj3$cie$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`

```
I am running "Micro Focus COBOL for UNIX         V4.1 revision 030" on a
Linux box

I am trying to compile a program using the htmp preprocessor.  If I put a
display of an external-form inside a if/end-if statement i get an error.

For example

-bash-2.05b$ cat example.cbl
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       77 77-if-field pic 9.
       01  output-form is external-form identified by "outpage1.htm".
           03  name-field2 pic x(30) identified by name.
       procedure division.
       a10.
           if 77-if-field = 1 then
              display output-form
           end-if
           stop run
           .
-bash-2.05b$ cob -x example.cbl -C "preprocess(htmlpp)" ACCCGI.o
Micro Focus Accept/Display CGI and Embedded HTML Preprocessor
Version 1.58 Copyright (C) 1984-1999 MERANT International Ltd.
Micro Focus CP
V990720 140438 Copyright (C) 1993-1999 Micro Focus Ltd.
    10     end-if
* 564-S**********
**
**    A scope-delimiter did not have a matching verb and was discarded.
cob: error(s) in compilation: example.cbl
-bash-2.05b$


It seems that the preprocessor is expanding the display statement and has
inserted a full stop somewhere.

Anybody heard of this before?

Is it a bug?

Is there a fix?


regards
```

#### ↳ Re: Mircofocus and html preprocessor

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T17:03:17+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9too6$jc8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<b9tmj3$cie$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`

```
Hi Steve.

This is a bug which appears to be corrected in later updates of OCDS for
Linux.

I'd suggest contacting Micro Focus SupportLine to obtain either 4.1.40 or
4.2, making it clear which OS (inc. version) you're running under, as the
later update has been rebased on Red Hat AS 2.1/SuSE 8.0.

Simon.
```

## Thread: OT - Arithmetic with Roman Numerals — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - Arithmetic with Roman Numerals

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T13:16:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9u169$i2e$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For information on HOW to do "Arithmetic with Roman Numerals" there are a
number of web pages.  One that I (personally) found interesting was:

     http://www.jimloy.com/arith/roman.htm

I found the statements,

"So, in Egyptian, numbers are longer than they are in Roman numerals. But,
it turns out that arithmetic is simpler. There are fewer carries and no
borrows (when adding)."

which may (or may not) have some relevance to "progression" of numeric
systems.

    ***

This subject is, however, TOTALLY "off-topic" (in the COBOL newsgroup) as

1)  The use of "numerals" in both English and COBOL is quite "stable" and
well understood by even beginning "users" of either.

2) All posters that I respect in this NG, agree that the choice of "symbols"
as opposed to "words" in COBOL (in cases where both are allowed) *is* a
matter of STYLE not "substance" (with issues of maintainability, inadvertent
errors, print chains, etc - being QUITE subjective).

3) COBOL's 40 year old history of being "English-LIKE" is neither a current
"design" criterion nor of significant import to MOST programmers.

4) COBOL has a "variety" of items for which *only* SYMBOLS are allowed
(e.g. the "::" inline OO invocator or the traditional "==" pseudo-text
delimiter)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Arithmetic with Roman Numerals

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T07:29:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba0889$6r3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b9u169$i2e$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3ec30525.162917435@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ec30525.162917435@news.optonline.net...

> >3) COBOL's 40 year old history of being "English-LIKE" is neither a
current
> >"design" criterion nor of significant import to MOST programmers.
>
> COBOL is obviously English-like and no amount of dancing can negate that.
The
> requirement for backward compatibility insures it will remain
English-like.

No official American or International standard for COBOL has in any way
denigrated the use of "non-English-like" constructs like "IF A > B".

The concept that the best COBOL is the most English-like COBOL *directly*
conflicts with the goals of J4, WG4, ISO, the non-English-speaking world,
and the international standards community.  "IF A > B" is arguably more
understandable to someone who does not speak or read English than "IF A IS
GREATER THAN B".

You may want to insist that COBOL programmers need to think just like good
ol' 'Murkins,  but such a goal strikes me, and I believe strikes many
outside the United States, as jingoistic at best.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Arithmetic with Roman Numerals

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

```

>news:3ec30525.162917435@news.optonline.net...

>No official American or International standard for COBOL has in any way
>denigrated the use of "non-English-like" constructs like "IF A > B".
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>GREATER THAN B".

The greater symbol has been in COBOL since the beginning; it did not come from a
recent internationalization effort. 

What about the hundreds of other English words in the COBOL vocabulary? Is there
a plan to let them be expressed in other languages? What about word order?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Arithmetic with Roman Numerals

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-17T22:10:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba6tje$tbf$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b9u169$i2e$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3ec30525.162917435@news.optonline.net> <ba0889$6r3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ec6b1e1.91406408@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ec6b1e1.91406408@news.optonline.net...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
>
> The greater symbol has been in COBOL since the beginning; it did not come
from a
> recent internationalization effort.
>
> What about the hundreds of other English words in the COBOL vocabulary? Is
there
> a plan to let them be expressed in other languages? What about word order?
>

In GENERAL (but certainly NOT in all cases) "internationalization"
implications are and will be considered in all ENHANCEMENTS to the Standard
COBOL language.

Micro Focus already provides a facility whereby (using "vocabularies") you
may use non-English words *and* syntax order for COBOL source code.

The IBM CCCA product *originated* as a product to support French Language
source code (key words, etc).  I don't know if it is still used for that
purpose in France (or parts of Canada) but it certainly continues that
functionality.
```

## Thread: x < y < z Syntax — 97 msgs

### x < y < z Syntax

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T18:31:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net>`

```
Does anyone on the standards committee know if the above syntax is or is 
ever planned to be sorted?  For example, something like the following...

If 3 < x < 11
     Perform Its-4-to-10
Else
     Perform Out-of-Range
End-If

Just curious...  Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: x < y < z Syntax

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T03:06:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec30201.162112774@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net>`

```
LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:

>Does anyone on the standards committee know if the above syntax is or is 
>ever planned to be sorted?  For example, something like the following...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Just curious...  Thanks!

You can do that now in two ways:

if x not < 4 and not > 10
     Perform Its-4-to-10
Else
     Perform Out-of-RangeEnd-If

- or - 

05  x pic ...
     88 in-range values 4 thru 10.

if in-range
    Perform Its-4-to-10
Else
     Perform Out-of-RangeEnd-If

Note that both explicitly refer to the limits, not some numbers outside the
limits.
```

#### ↳ Re: x < y < z Syntax

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-14T23:50:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9v6a1$mhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net>`

```
To the best of my knowledge, no one has suggested this to the Standards
Committees.  Besides other ways of doing it the following (IMHO fairly
"dangerous") use of "combined abbreviated conditions" should do what you
want (and even be SEMI-intuitive)

 If X > 3  and < 11
      Perform Its-4-to-10
 Else
      Perform Out-of-Range
 End-If
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: x < y < z Syntax

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T09:58:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vc77krkp2q4g8d@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net> <b9v6a1$mhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Or, in 2002 COBOL

evaluate X
  when 4 thru 10

should do it, safely.

Okay - I guess that's close enough.  :)  Although, what I had been doing

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: x < y < z Syntax

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T22:25:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC40FF2.EA77F0C7@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net> <b9v6a1$mhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3EC408A0.7020008@Knology.net>`

```


LX-i wrote:

> William M. Klein wrote:
> > To the best of my knowledge, no one has suggested this to the Standards
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> a little more intuitive...  Thanks!

I don't particularly like Bill's 'semi-intutive',  "If x >= 4 And x <=10".
It's not difficult, but if you are the poor sod doing maintenance at 02:00,
without first having had a black coffee as a pick-me-up  ???

Rick suggested :-

*>Or, in 2002 COBOL

*>evaluate X
*>  when 4 thru 10

*>should do it, safely.

The following is a 'play' on Rick's solution :-

01 X        pic 9(03).
    88 ValidRange        value 4 thru 10.

Move 9 to X
Evaluate true
    when ValidRange
             Perform Its-4-to-10
    when other.........
End-Evaluate

Conditionals with Level 88s can be quite useful.

Jerry and Pete - you can relax <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: x < y < z Syntax

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-16T00:31:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC42C11.20A08188@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net> <b9v6a1$mhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3EC408A0.7020008@Knology.net> <3EC40FF2.EA77F0C7@shaw.ca> <ba17od$6k3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Jimmy,
>    Besides the phrase
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bill,

I *did* read your whole message and noted the "(IMHO fairly  "dangerous") -
which is the element most likely to come into play at 02:00 <G>. As you say not
your choice, but it was an option.

For X-Li, perhaps I should add another option. For illustration, you can build
OO dictionaries holding a key and data, per element(row). The M/F support class
returns comp-5, so I have to show it below. Assume we are after a element with
the 'key' 'Davey Jones :-

Local -storage section.
01 ls-key    pic x(30).
01 ls-bool pic 99 comp-5.
    88 isTrue            value 1.
    88 isFalse            value 0.
.......
move 'Davey Jones' to ls-key
invoke MyDictionary "includesKey" using ls-key returning ls-Bool

if isTrue
   invoke self  "we-have-found-it"

else invoke self  "we-didn't-find-it"
End-if
End Method "xxxxx

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: x < y < z Syntax

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-16T08:27:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba301k$254h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net> <b9v6a1$mhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3EC408A0.7020008@Knology.net> <3EC40FF2.EA77F0C7@shaw.ca> <ba17od$6k3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:ba17od$6k3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...

> I am NOT (personally) a great fan of combined abbreviated conditions.  I
do,
> however, acknowledge their existence in standard COBOL.

In case anyone doubted my position on this:  I am (personally) an inveterate
FOE of abbreviated combined relation conditions (the "standard-ese" term).
While I acknowledge their existence in standard COBOL, I will generally
argue against their use and have formally argued (and will continue to
argue) against their extension beyond the context in which they are
currently allowed in the standard.

The COBOL meaning of a condition containing abbreviated combined relation
conditions is, as written, quite often at significant variance with the
ENGLISH meaning of that same statement because in English the omission of a
subject or both the subject and the relation implies a relationship between
the original subject and the object that is not implied in COBOL.   The
difference is not obvious on casual reading, particularly to the neophyte,
and is a frequent cause of bugs in user code, in my experience.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: x < y < z Syntax

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-16T16:04:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba3271$fo1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net> <b9v6a1$mhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3EC408A0.7020008@Knology.net> <3EC40FF2.EA77F0C7@shaw.ca> <ba17od$6k3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ba301k$254h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
One thing I do in COBOL is the following:

IF (A = B)
or (A = C)
or (A = D)


Instead of

IF A = B or C or D

The former code is completely unambiguous, it is easier to maintain (comment out
the 2nd line, or change the third A to X if you like).
```

Sorry Robert, you still don't understand the ANSI/ISO concept of "OBSOLETE".

As I understand the process, Obsolete features are not *automatically* dropped
in the following standard. The decision to drop is made on a case-by-case basis.


On a historic note, IBM's VS COBOL II V1.0 dropped (among others) OCCURS
DEPENDING ON, exponentiation and floating-point. (Why? Insurance companies need
all three.) Two years later, in V2.0, those features were restored.

- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net> <b9v6a1$mhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3EC408A0.7020008@Knology.net> <3EC40FF2.EA77F0C7@shaw.ca> <ba17od$6k3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ba301k$254h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ba3271$fo1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6da13.101698059@news.optonline.net> <ba6u3e$2vr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ec77927.142427415@news.optonline.net>`

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ec77927.142427415@news.optonline.net...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
> >"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
> >news:3ec6da13.101698059@news.optonline.net...
> >> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
<snip>
>
> As I understand the process, Obsolete features are not *automatically*
dropped
> in the following standard. The decision to drop is made on a case-by-case
basis.
>

I cannot speak for the future, but during discussion of this "topic" during
the development of the 2002 Standard, the opinion of the committee was that
being in the OBSOLETE category *does* "guarantee" that a feature will be
dropped in the next Standard.  ARCHAIC, of course is a totally different
thing - with NO guarantee that a feature will ever move from ARCHAIC to
OBSOLETE and then be removed.


>
> On a historic note, IBM's VS COBOL II V1.0 dropped (among others) OCCURS
> DEPENDING ON, exponentiation and floating-point. (Why? Insurance companies
need
> all three.) Two years later, in V2.0, those features were restored.

IBM's VS COBOL II was missing MANY features that IBM clearly indicated that
they INTENDED to introduce in a follow-on product.  Actually OCCURS
DEPENDING ON was fully supported in V1.  EXPONENTIATION was required for an
'74 Standard HIGH compiler - and IBM fully expected to support such.
```

How did debugging lines move from supported in the 85 standard to Obsolete in
the 2002, without going through Archaic? 

The 2002 standard says "Debugging lines and the DEBUGGING MODE phrase are
difficult to use and are rarely used." That's simply not true in my experience.
They are easy to use (and to compile) and they are frequently used  in mainframe
apps .. in lieu of the evanescent READY TRACE feature. 

I don't use nor support them, but any compiler which deleted them would hear a
maelstrom of protest from mainframe shops.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OBSOLETE vs ARCHAIC (in ANSI/ISO Standards) (was: x < y < z Syntax

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-18T20:32:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba9c6p$6js$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net> <b9v6a1$mhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3EC408A0.7020008@Knology.net> <3EC40FF2.EA77F0C7@shaw.ca> <ba17od$6k3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ba301k$254h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ba3271$fo1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6da13.101698059@news.optonline.net> <ba6u3e$2vr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ec77927.142427415@news.optonline.net> <ba8mmj$1ok$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3ec81e81.31622616@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ec81e81.31622616@news.optonline.net...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>
<snip>
> How did debugging lines move from supported in the 85 standard to Obsolete
in
> the 2002, without going through Archaic?
>
> The 2002 standard says "Debugging lines and the DEBUGGING MODE phrase are
> difficult to use and are rarely used." That's simply not true in my
experience.
> They are easy to use (and to compile) and they are frequently used  in
mainframe
> apps .. in lieu of the evanescent READY TRACE feature.
>
> I don't use nor support them, but any compiler which deleted them would
hear a
> maelstrom of protest from mainframe shops.

There is NO requirement that a feature be designated as ARCHAIC before being
designated as OBSOLETE.  It would NOT surprise me, if "character-based
Accept/Display/Screen Section" goes into the OBSOLETE category in the next
Standard - without ever being called "OBSOLETE".  The two categories really
are VERY different in "intent" (as well as definition).

An ARCHAIC item is designated as such (as I understand it) *IF* it is
 - in "relatively common usage"
 - as a "substitute" functionality (either in COBOL itself or in some/most
operating systems
 - is considered a "bad" coding technique  (often as it is
"counter-intuitive" or "error-prone")

There is NO guarantee that such a feature will soon (or even EVER) move to
the OBSOLETE category and then be removed.  HOWEVER, if its usage *does*
become uncommon and it is still considered "less desirable" - THEN it will
considered for being OBSOLETE.

    ***

As far as debugging lines go (and their usage in mainframe shops), if a
compiler supports the 2002 Standard "conditional compilation" feature, it
would be TRIVIAL to create software that would automatically convert a
program with debugging lines (when removed from the Standard and
implementations) to one with conditional compilation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OBSOLETE vs ARCHAIC (in ANSI/ISO Standards) (was: x < y < z Syntax

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Rosa Fischer <Rosa.Fischer@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T08:28:09+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ECB1C79.64474EC4@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net> <b9v6a1$mhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3EC408A0.7020008@Knology.net> <3EC40FF2.EA77F0C7@shaw.ca> <ba17od$6k3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ba301k$254h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ba3271$fo1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6da13.101698059@news.optonline.net> <ba6u3e$2vr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ec77927.142427415@news.optonline.net> <ba8mmj$1ok$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3ec81e81.31622616@news.optonline.net> <ba9c6p$6js$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" schrieb:

> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
> news:3ec81e81.31622616@news.optonline.net...
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

It's hard to understand that the standard comittee made debugging lines obsolete
and at the same time invented a new format of debugging lines for free format
(floating debugging indicator) and rules how to compile fixed format debugging
lines as free format debugging lines.
In most cases when an element becomes obsolete and is not supported in the next
standard it becomes a vendor supplied extension in most compilers.

Have a nice day
Rosa
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OBSOLETE vs ARCHAIC (in ANSI/ISO Standards) (was: x < y < z Syntax

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T11:28:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bag9fl$9l9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net> <b9v6a1$mhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3EC408A0.7020008@Knology.net> <3EC40FF2.EA77F0C7@shaw.ca> <ba17od$6k3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ba301k$254h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ba3271$fo1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6da13.101698059@news.optonline.net> <ba6u3e$2vr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ec77927.142427415@news.optonline.net> <ba8mmj$1ok$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3ec81e81.31622616@news.optonline.net> <ba9c6p$6js$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ECB1C79.64474EC4@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
"Rosa Fischer" <Rosa.Fischer@fujitsu-siemens.com> wrote in message
news:3ECB1C79.64474EC4@fujitsu-siemens.com...
<snip>
> It's hard to understand that the standard comittee made debugging lines
obsolete
> and at the same time invented a new format of debugging lines for free
format
> (floating debugging indicator) and rules how to compile fixed format
debugging
> lines as free format debugging lines.
> In most cases when an element becomes obsolete and is not supported in the
next
> standard it becomes a vendor supplied extension in most compilers.
>
> Have a nice day
> Rosa
>

There were both  "philosophical" and a "practical" reasons for introducing
floating debugging indicator.

A) It was felt "important" that *all* fixed form reference format source
lines  be "logically converted" to free form before applying the rest of the
rules of 2002 Standard.  Among other things, this SIGNIFICANTLY improved the
"ease" of defining the rules for COPY and REPLACE statements.  Once this
decision was made, there had to be SOME way of indicating "debugging lines"
in free form reference format (and this couldn't depend on a "D" in column
7 - or even as the first "word" on a source line).

B) There was a conscience decision made that there should be a way to create
a single copy of ANY type of "valid" line - that could be used "correctly"
in BOTH fixed and free format - by leaving columns 1-7 "blank" and ending
before the R-Margin.  This meant that there needed to be a way of
designating debugging lines (in EITHER fixed or free format) with column 7
blank.

    ***

FINALLY, there was "mixed" opinion among the committees about "problems"
that occurred because some of the items designated as OBSOLETE in the '85
Standard were not "adequately" enhanced to match the rest of the Standard.
The most "obvious" problems related to the DEBUGGING special-register with
7-dimension tables and other "new in '85 features".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: x < y < z Syntax

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-19T09:40:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bab1el$1k55$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net> <b9v6a1$mhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3EC408A0.7020008@Knology.net> <3EC40FF2.EA77F0C7@shaw.ca> <ba17od$6k3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ba301k$254h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ba3271$fo1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6da13.101698059@news.optonline.net> <ba6u3e$2vr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ec77927.142427415@news.optonline.net> <ba8mmj$1ok$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3ec81e81.31622616@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ec81e81.31622616@news.optonline.net...

> How did debugging lines move from supported in the 85 standard to Obsolete
in
> the 2002, without going through Archaic?

The Archaic category was introduced in the '02 standard.

> The 2002 standard says "Debugging lines and the DEBUGGING MODE phrase are
> difficult to use and are rarely used." That's simply not true in my
experience.
> They are easy to use (and to compile) and they are frequently used  in
mainframe
> apps .. in lieu of the evanescent READY TRACE feature.
>
> I don't use nor support them, but any compiler which deleted them would
hear a
> maelstrom of protest from mainframe shops.

What you quoted is just the lead-in, and it looks to me like the argument is
being built in *increasing* order of weight.  The final argument is
summarized "Because conditional compilation is easier to use, provides all
of the functionality of debugging lines, is clearly defined, and is more
robust, there is no longer any need for debugging lines or for the DEBUGGING
MODE phrase to activate them."

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: x < y < z Syntax

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-19T09:32:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bab0v6$1jrl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net> <b9v6a1$mhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3EC408A0.7020008@Knology.net> <3EC40FF2.EA77F0C7@shaw.ca> <ba17od$6k3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ba301k$254h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ba3271$fo1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ec6da13.101698059@news.optonline.net> <ba6u3e$2vr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ec77927.142427415@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ec77927.142427415@news.optonline.net...

> As I understand the process, Obsolete features are not *automatically*
dropped
> in the following standard. The decision to drop is made on a case-by-case
basis.

Then you misunderstand the process.  In the middle of Page 5 in ISO/IEC
1989:2002 it states "Obsolete language elements will be removed from the
next revision of standard COBOL."

I think I may have previously given the impression in some of my posts in
this forum a while back that a feature -- I think it was debugging
indicators -- of a module that had been marked "obsolete" in the '85
standard -- the "debug" module -- was nonetheless retained in the '02
standard.  Bill Klein kindly corrected that impression because the feature,
though it *looked like* it was part of that module, was actually part of
"nucleus" COBOL.   Once an element of the language is marked "obsolete" in a
given revision of the standard, it is mechanistically deleted from the next
revision of the standard.  Ordinarily that's done as part of the process of
developing the *initial* version of the working draft of the revision.   In
the case of the obsolete elements listed on Page 834 of ISO/IEC 1989:2002,
they're already gone from the working draft for the next revision.  So far
as I know every feature shown in the Obsolete Language Element List on pages
XVII-81 through XVII-86 of ANSI X3.23-1985 was absent from ISO/IEC
1989:2002.  Whether one or another implementor elects to retain the feature
*as an extension* once it has been marked obsolete is immaterial to that
point.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

Chuck Stevens wrote:
> Also, whether or not it is rendered obsolete in a future standard, I will
> continue to argue against expansion of abbreviations in relation conditions
> in new contexts.

As the one who started this whole mess, I really appreciate all the 
comments.  I hadn't thought that a simple Evaluate statement with one 
When clause would do the same thing, but be much more elegant and clean. 
  I knew I could define an 88-level, and just check it.  And, I've 
actually removed what I now know is called abbreviated related 
conditions, because I looked at the code and thought "what is that doing?"

Now if we could just get "<>" to mean the same thing as "Not =", it 
wouldn't be so hard going back and forth between COBOL and VB...  :) 
(And get VB to drop the required "Then" after every "If"...)  I guess 
such is life for the multilingual programmer...
```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ec6df20.102990665@news.optonline.net...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
<snip>
> >
> >The COBOL meaning of a condition containing abbreviated combined relation
> >conditions is, as written, quite often at significant variance with the
> >ENGLISH meaning of that same statement because in English the omission of
a
> >subject or both the subject and the relation implies a relationship
between
> >the original subject and the object that is not implied in COBOL.   The
> >difference is not obvious on casual reading, particularly to the
neophyte,
> >and is a frequent cause of bugs in user code, in my experience.
>
> Can you provide an example?
>
> In IF a = b or c AND d = e or f, the implied relationship test of f is to
d, not
> a.

For the type of code that leads to problems (and in fact - at least as IBM
understood it - was a change between the '68 and '74 Standards), see the
section on the meaning of:

 A = B AND NOT LESS THAN C OR D

at:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igymg202/4.1.7

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: x < y < z Syntax

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-18T13:10:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec77e33.143720389@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net> <b9v6a1$mhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3EC408A0.7020008@Knology.net> <3EC40FF2.EA77F0C7@shaw.ca> <ba17od$6k3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ba301k$254h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ec6df20.102990665@news.optonline.net> <ba6ttj$qdn$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igymg202/4.1.7

I see. In my opinion, the example demonstrates two abuses of the feature:
implying he subject while changing the relational operator for multiple objects,
and mixing AND with OR in the absence of parentheses. I wish the standard
disallowed both, because they are error-prone. 

A meaningful use of 'implied subject only' is in range tests:
  a NOT < lower-limit AND NOT > upper-limit
but this is abusive:
  a NOT < lower-limit-1 AND NOT > upper-limit-1 OR NOT < lower-limit-2 AND NOT >
upper-limit-2

In the old days (68? 74?), we had what I call 'backward elision' of the logical
operator:
  a = b c d OR e
That was dropped in 85(?) without much user protest. I missed the loss but
managed to get over it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: x < y < z Syntax

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T10:15:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<badnqs$fln$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3EC2D1CA.3030203@Knology.net> <b9v6a1$mhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3EC408A0.7020008@Knology.net> <3EC40FF2.EA77F0C7@shaw.ca> <ba17od$6k3$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ba301k$254h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ec6df20.102990665@news.optonline.net> <ba6ttj$qdn$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ec77e33.143720389@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ec77e33.143720389@news.optonline.net...

> I see. In my opinion, the example demonstrates two abuses of the feature:
> implying he subject while changing the relational operator for multiple
objects,
> and mixing AND with OR in the absence of parentheses. I wish the standard
> disallowed both, because they are error-prone.

Changing the relational operator while retaining the same subject of the
relation has been EXPLICITLY allowed in COBOL for AT LEAST thirty-five
years.  Are you prepared to fix all the programs that have been using this
form of abbreviated combined relation conditions all these decades, at your
cost, in support of your wish?

And while the specifications have always *allowed* the use of parentheses to
override the default order of evaluation of relation conditions, there is
*no* requirement that parentheses be used when the default order is what is
desired, nor has there ever been.  Are you prepared personally to indemnify
all those companies who have programs with combined relation conditions that
have both AND and OR connectors, to cover the costs of having their
programmers insert parentheses in combined conditions, in support of your
wish?

In English, AND and OR have fundamentally the same "precedence".  In COBOL,
the precedence of AND is higher than that of OR.

In English, abbreviation of a condition (implied subject, with or without
implied relational operator) implies a hierarchical level for the entire
abbreviated condition.  In COBOL, there is no hierarchical association among
the expanded conditions.

These, I believe, are the source of the failings of abbreviated combined
relation conditions.  And these failings have been plaguing COBOL
programmers, best I can tell, since the days of COBOL-60.  Now is not the
time in the history of COBOL suddenly to require explicit parentheses in
combined relations using both AND and OR, or to forbid the elision of the
subject in an abbreviated condition without *also* eliding the relational
operator.

I don't like abbreviated combined relation conditions in the first place,
for the fundamental reason that *in English* (and for that matter in most
natural languages) the act of abbreviation implies a hierarchy that is not
reflected in COBOL.  It's not the combination of AND and OR (whose relative
precedence is well-defined in COBOL), nor is it the ability to assume the
subject while specifying the operator in an abbreviated combined relation
condition.  It's the fact that *in English* and (and for that matter all the
natural languages I know of) the act of abbreviation implies a hierarchy
that is not reflected in COBOL.

If back in 1960 the original framers of COBOL had said "IF A = B AND C = D
OR E OR > F" was equivalent, *because of the hierarchical grouping implied
by abbreviation*, to "IF (A = B) AND (C = D OR C = E OR C  > F)", I suspect
strongly I wouldn't object to abbreviated combined relation conditions.  But
they didn't.  And forty-three years after the fact is a bit late to
second-guess the fundamental way in which abbreviated combined relation
conditions are evaluated.

Making it a matter of policy to use parentheses to clarify, both to the
reader and to the compiler, the exact hierarchy in which the simple
conditions in a combined relation conditions are to be evaluated (with or
without the use of both AND and OR at any given hierarchical level, and with
and without the use of abbreviations) is a Good Thing, I believe.  Making it
mandatory for the user to do so, in a context in which no such requirement
currently exists, would be catastrophic to the future of COBOL.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: x < y < z Syntax

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T09:44:19+12:00

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:badnqs$fln$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...

< snipped excellent case>

> Making it a matter of policy to use parentheses to clarify, both to the
> reader and to the compiler, the exact hierarchy in which the simple
> conditions in a combined relation conditions are to be evaluated (with or
> without the use of both AND and OR at any given hierarchical level, and
with
> and without the use of abbreviations) is a Good Thing, I believe.  Making
it
> mandatory for the user to do so, in a context in which no such requirement
> currently exists, would be catastrophic to the future of COBOL.
>
Really good post, Chuck, spoiled by the last statement.

It wouldn't be catastrophic to the (arguable) future of COBOL.

It would just be downright stupid.

For the reasons you already covered.

The "future of COBOL" is NOT dependent on implied combined conditions; we
could lose them tomorrow and it would still have less effect on the future
of COBOL than the short-sightedness of some areas of the COBOL community...

Pete.
