#### ↳ Re: Evaluate or IF,s in Power Cobol

- **From:** "Craig S" <c10@elvis.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-18T18:11:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ku-cncT-D7tOjlWjXTWcpg@wideopenwest.com>`
- **References:** `<2883377.1053017801@dbforums.com>`

```
The IF in your example doesn't do the same thing as
the EVALUATE (when the variable is zero in the latter,
nothing is done).
```

#### ↳ Re: Evaluate or IF,s in Power Cobol

- **From:** gmspano <member9303@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-19T18:51:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2898119.1053370305@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2883377.1053017801@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi Carlos

Best solution is to use a field in the WS-Section defined like
01 DUMMY    PIC S9(9) COMP-5, then move the field control value using:

MOVE "TEXT" OF CMTEXT1 TO DUMMY
EVALUATE DUMMY
    WHEN  something
           do something
    WHEN  other
           do  other
END-EVALUATE.

This is because, as it has been explained by Jerry and  William, the
CMTEXT1 control treats its values as a text and using the solution above
you can resolve your problem...

Hope in this help.

Gianni
```

## Thread: Net Express V4 (???) — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Net Express V4 (???)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T14:47:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba0qsp$9r8$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For those who haven't see it, the "Micro Focus Net Express� 4.0" product
"fact sheet" is available at:


http://www.microfocus.com/products/netexpress/Net_Express4_Datasheet.asp

IMHO, there are a few "interesting" (some good - some bad) things said and
not said in this announcement.  For example:

1) (said) "Full support for Object Orientated COBOL (including ISO2002
Syntax) and COBOL development and debugging."

2) (NOT said) Full support for ISO 2002 non-OO syntax

3) (said) "Microsoft .NET Interoperability Through Support for COM(+)
Objects"

4) (NOT said) Microsoft .NET "participation" available
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express V4 (???)

- **From:** "Douglas Gallant" <no@spam.net>

- **References:** `<ba0qsp$9r8$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

MF is working on a product that will do that. Not sure how much longer it
has to bake.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Net Express V4 (???)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-15T21:47:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ba1jfa$klr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ba0qsp$9r8$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <anXwa.3630$5y5.2268@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
As far as the .NET issue, it appears to me (and I could be mistaken on
this - I only see the "fact sheet") that this product also doesn't produce
"object" that can inherit from the "official" .NET classes.   This is
certainly a major selling point of Fujitsu's .NET product.  I am *not*
saying that Micro Focus won't "get there" eventually, but it doesn't look
(ot me) as if V4 is much closer than the last release.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Net Express V4 (???)

- **From:** "Douglas Gallant" <no@spam.net>

```
NX V4 as shipped certainly does not target .NET directly. It claims to make
.NET-friendly COM components though. The NX for .NET product should do the
things you expect. I've seen it (haven't used it as much as I'd like) but of
course can't really comment due to NDA.

## Thread: OT: Haircuts — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Haircuts

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-16T13:20:19+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec43a18_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`

```
Being follicularly challenged (more so as years go by) it isn't very often I
actually need a haircut.

(I don't actually have haircuts any more, I have "search and destroy"
missions...)

Anyway, I decided to take a break from the intensive coding I am doing
currently (especially as I had a major breakthrough around 2:00 am this
morning and got something to work that had baffled me for a couple of days)
and go pamper myself...

It just so happened that on checking the e-mail before going out, there was
a "Friday Funny" which I reproduce here...


"Women on Haircuts"

Woman 1: Oh! That haircut is so cute!
Woman 2: You think so? I wasn't sure when she gave me the mirror. I mean,
you don't think it's too fluffy looking?
Woman 1: Oh God no! No, it's perfect. I'd love to get my hair cut like that,
but I think my face is too wide. I'm  pretty much stuck with this stuff I
think.
Woman 2: Are you serious? I think your face is adorable. And you could
easily get one of those layer cuts - that would look so cute I think. I was
actually going to do that except that I was afraid it would accent my long
neck.
Woman 1: Oh - that's funny! I would love to have your neck! Anything to take
attention away from this two-by-four I have for a shoulder line.
Woman 2: Are you kidding? I know girls that would kill for your shoulders.
Everything drapes so well on you. I mean, look at my arms - see how short
they are? If I had your shoulders I could get clothes to fit me so much
easier. (etc...)

"Men on Haircuts"

Man 1: Haircut?
Man 2: Yeah.

...Needless to say, I walked past the trendy up-market unisex "salon" and
and went to Bob the Kiwi Barber (who still has a red, white, and blue pole
outside his shop), where he cuts hair and provides file storage (always
struck me as a curious blend of occupations, but it works for him.)

Solved three more problems while sitting in his chair...
Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Haircuts

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-16T01:31:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-2BD042.01315316052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```

Reminds me of an incident that still haunts me.

I was working in a group of four women coding 370 assembler.  One had 
long, beautiful hair.

So one morning she arrives at work and goes cube to cube to get everyone 
to gush at her new Dorthy Hammel style.  The other three women in the 
group pretty much followed your script.  But when she got to my cube I 
said something, not meaning to be hurtful, to the effect of "but you had 
such pretty hair".

She really did have very pretty hair.  What was I suppose to say?

Ten years later I still hear about it...


In article <3ec43a18_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com>,
 "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> Being follicularly challenged (more so as years go by) it isn't very often I
> actually need a haircut.
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Haircuts

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-17T09:47:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec5f5b2.43223571@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ec43a18_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <joe_zitzelberger-2BD042.01315316052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>
>Reminds me of an incident that still haunts me.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>She really did have very pretty hair.  What was I supposed to say?

Whan woman asks a question like that she's seeking support, not information. 

Suppose a 250-pounder came to you wearing a new dress and asked 'Do I look fat
in this?' If you answered 'You'd look fat in any dress. If you want to look
slim, try losing 100 pounds.', you'd not only make an enemy for life but, more
to the point, you wouldn't be telling her something she didn't already know. 

She's soliciting your support. The woman you cited already *knew* her long hair
was beautiful. That's why she cultivated it for years. Now that she's made a
change, she want affirmation that it looks ok, especially to men. She got it
from women, but that's expected. You were the first man she asked and you let
her down. 

Next time, act preppie, positive, *aroused*. 

>
>Ten years later I still hear about it...
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Haircuts

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com>

BTW your "spiky haired friend", the one with face jewellery, "who looked the
least dangerous" <G>. What does he do for haircuts ?

My granddaughter, the one permanently dressed for Halloween, I guess she has to
be a fan of Judy Garland, 'Somewhere over the Rainbow". Colour is no problem.
You name it she has had it. Arrives one day looking like a green veggie,
followed by an appearance looking like a carrot., and if she is in a blue
mood.......... If we are *lucky* sometimes it's black !

She's also a fashion expert - pokes a hole through them and wears socks on her
arms !

"I'll never comprehend......",  he says to himself as he goes off into senility
<G>

Jimmy

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <3EC4D049.9090401@Knology.net>,
> LX-i  <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> course.

I wasn't thinking of any particular activities.  My thought was more 
along the lines of "We don't need any more money for school lunches, but 
we've got some infrastructure repairs that could use some funding". 
But, should states not be able to set policies based on the values of 
the majority in that state?  I thought diversity was a good thing... 
(Oh yeah, that's right - it doesn't apply to conservatives)

You really shouldn't charge the peasants such high usury, your 
highness...  :)
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-17T04:42:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F4jxa.18325$ql5.518508@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC4402B.2050708@Knology.net> <joe_zitzelberger-B97CC9.00224116052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EC4D049.9090401@Knology.net> <ba2k1p$h5s$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC58FE5.5080302@Knology.net>`

```
Ah- an advocate of the tyranny of the majority. The simple answer is "no."
The more complex answer requires a rather long ethical review of what
one considers "rights" vs. "privileges."

When a government is protecting a person (*a* person, as in singular!) in regard to their
rights, it is doing its job.

When a government is granting groups of people, or even individuals "privileges", it pretty much has grown into some kind of
oppressive monster, whether on the left end or the right end of the scale. Individuals, and their rights, fall smack dab right into
the middle of that scale, and that is
exactly where a government is least effective in controlling the everyday lives of a person.

As to states and their rights, I personally think that states are too large too, and those rights
should be held at a very local level. That way people can always move to another locality more to their liking.  Remember our
states, at least many of them, have larger budgets and more
politicians than many countries!

-Paul


"LX-i" <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message news:3EC58FE5.5080302@Knology.net...
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> > In article <3EC4D049.9090401@Knology.net>,
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-17T23:24:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EC70AFD.9090309@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC4402B.2050708@Knology.net> <joe_zitzelberger-B97CC9.00224116052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EC4D049.9090401@Knology.net> <ba2k1p$h5s$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC58FE5.5080302@Knology.net> <F4jxa.18325$ql5.518508@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
Paul Raulerson wrote:
> Ah- an advocate of the tyranny of the majority.

Wow - you completely misread that.  We have a republic, not a democracy, 
and I'm glad we do.

> As to states and their rights, I personally think that states are too large too, and those rights
> should be held at a very local level. That way people can always move to another locality more to their liking.  Remember our
> states, at least many of them, have larger budgets and more
> politicians than many countries!

I know we've got big states, but they are to whom the Constitution 
designates powers not delimited within itself.  :)  I do agree, though - 
you remember the blue/red map from Election 2000, right?  <toungue in 
cheek> Look how much better we'd be if electorate were based on land 
instead of people! :) </tongue in cheek>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-18T23:58:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-A9635A.23585318052003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC4402B.2050708@Knology.net> <joe_zitzelberger-B97CC9.00224116052003@corp.supernews.com> <3EC4D049.9090401@Knology.net> <ba2k1p$h5s$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC58FE5.5080302@Knology.net> <F4jxa.18325$ql5.518508@twister.austin.rr.com> <3EC70AFD.9090309@Knology.net>`

```
In article <3EC70AFD.9090309@Knology.net>,
 LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:

> Paul Raulerson wrote:
> > Ah- an advocate of the tyranny of the majority.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> instead of people! :) </tongue in cheek>

It used to be that way in part.  The Senate was intended to be the 
"states house" and a check on bread and circuses run amok in the 
"peoples house".

Sadly, this system failed about the same time the income tax was passed 
and we now have two houses of the people that can run amok with the 
public treasury -- and noone speaks for the several states anymore.

This is why most people are also completely unaware of their state 
legislatures -- they haven't mattered one bit since 1913.
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

```
In article <3EC58FE5.5080302@Knology.net>,
LX-i  <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> In article <3EC4D049.9090401@Knology.net>,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>I wasn't thinking of any particular activities.

You were just thinking of 'activities, themselves'?  (Sorry, I just got 
done responding to Mr Smith about 'government, itself'.)

>My thought was more 
>along the lines of "We don't need any more money for school lunches, but 
>we've got some infrastructure repairs that could use some funding". 

Or... 'the owner of the local dairy contributed to the losing political 
party but the owner of the construction company to the winner'.

>But, should states not be able to set policies based on the values of 
>the majority in that state?  I thought diversity was a good thing... 
>(Oh yeah, that's right - it doesn't apply to conservatives)

You thought wrongly, deviations from the policy of Encouraging Diversity 
will *not* be tolerated... but before you get off on a 'poor endangered 
white man's rant' let's get back to this idea of 'local control'.

As pointed out above there's local control... and local corruption, as 
anyone who's read Faulkner might be able to imagine.  One of the balances 
between local and centralised control is the balance between seeing the 
forest and the trees... locally some opinions might not receive a fair 
hearing because they run contrary to how the Local Powers wish to see 
themselves ('Infrastructure problems?  We would *never* let our schools 
reach such a condition, there *are* no infrastructure problems!')

As I recall it the reason that folks decided a State Police Force was 
necessary was, in part, due to the corruption of local constabularies; 
this is good to keep in mind.

>
>You really shouldn't charge the peasants such high usury, your 
>highness...  :)

We are not involved in matters of Mere Finance, that is the province of 
the Treasurer.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Legislative Shenanigans

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<4T2dna3ZZYcwY12jXTWJkQ@giganews.com> <3EC58FE5.5080302@Knology.net> <ba55gq$abb$1@panix1.panix.com> <3EC70C9C.8020702@Knology.net>`

```
In article <3EC70C9C.8020702@Knology.net>,
LX-i  <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>>>>>1)  The states know better what parts of their education systems need 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>:)

Glad you enjoyed.

>
>>>My thought was more 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Man, you're cynical...

What... you mean that the local dairy would not catch on to the trick used 
by the Tyson corporation?  This is *politics*, man... and there's 
something that's been said about those who love law and sausages.

>
>> As pointed out above there's local control... and local corruption, as 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>pothole is a lot easier that building a whole new road.)

Halfway right, perhaps... local corruption is easier to spot, sure.  What 
happens, however, when the local corruption extends to the mechanisms of 
enforcement?  Granted that it is a movie but there is a lovely scene in 
the film 'Ragtime' where a Crusading Reporter catches the Mayor at a 
banquet which (for the tastes of the times) is decidedly too racy and 
undignified for a man of his position.  When the Reporter threatens to 
report His Honor to the police the Mayor responds with 'The Chief is at 
that table, right over there.'

As I mentioned in another posting, I recall that the move towards the 
establishment of a State Police Force was prompted, in part, by the 
corruption found in local departments.  If your local departments are 
corrupt then the choice seems to be... learn to live with it, work within 
the law to change it or move to another place more to your liking.

Sound familiar?

>
>>>You really shouldn't charge the peasants such high usury, your 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>So sorry, your highness...  I shall direct my advice elsewhere....

We are certain that it shall be given *all* the consideration that it most 
assuredly deserves.

DD
```

>>>>along the lines of "We don't need any more money for school lunches, but 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> something that's been said about those who love law and sausages.

Ideally, it would be more about government than politics.  What do our 
schools need?  (all the following are hypothetical, but I know the first 
to be true...)  Schools in Alabama are falling apart.  Maybe schools in 
California aren't falling apart, but they need computers.  Schools in 
New Mexico have fine facilities and computers, but many of their folks 
are poor, and need help with lunch programs.  Now, these local decisions 
would benefit the construction industry in Alabama, the computer 
hardware industry in California, and the farmers in New Mexico.  But it 
doesn't mean they would have the leaders in their pockets.

(Of course, we've already established that what I'm advocating has never 
been successfully pulled off...)

> Halfway right, perhaps... local corruption is easier to spot, sure.  What 
> happens, however, when the local corruption extends to the mechanisms of 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> that table, right over there.'

I see your point.

> corrupt then the choice seems to be... learn to live with it, work within 
> the law to change it or move to another place more to your liking.
> 
> Sound familiar?

Wasn't it you that was chiding me for repeating myself?  ;)
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2003-05-19T08:49:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1053334185@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: Cobol: SQL — 7 this month / 22 total

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

_Thread started [2003-03](2003-03.md#t-7ab4e12491)._

#### ↳ Re: Cobol: SQL

- **From:** SergVSmola <member29849@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-19T09:54:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2896106.1053338068@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3e786cf9.2639044@news.newsguy.com>`

```

I tried to execute stored procedure 2 modes
* storedprocedure MB_PAY_PREDPR( "01.01.2003", "31.01.2003" )

1     EXEC SQL
         PREPARE( "MB_PAY_PREDPR" )
     END-EXEC.
     EXEC SQL
         EXECUTE ( "01.01.2003", "31.01.2003" )
     END-EXEC.

2     EXEC SQL
         CALL ("MB_PAY_PREDPR","01.01.2003", "31.01.2003")
     END-EXEC.

In all cases compiler makes message
JMN2633I-S  SQL STATEMENT IS INVALID. ODBC-7600E MESSAGE ACCESS ERROR.

Why SQL in invalid?
I still didn't find answer.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: SQL

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-19T10:53:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%I2ya.19002$%_3.8257528@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3e786cf9.2639044@news.newsguy.com> <2896106.1053338068@dbforums.com>`

```
Gee whiz, an ON TOPIC post. I can't resist even though I don't know the
whole answer...

"SergVSmola" <member29849@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:2896106.1053338068@dbforums.com...
>
> I tried to execute stored procedure 2 modes
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why SQL in invalid?

PREPARE is only used when you build an SQL statement within your program.
Stored procedures are executed simply by calling them with any parameters
needed; they are already PREPAREd.

The correct sequence should be something like...

MOVE        "01.01.2003" to VAR1
MOVE        "31.01.2003" to VAR2
EXEC SQL
   CALL "MB_PAY_PREDPR" (:var1, :var2 )
END-EXEC

Var1 and var2 must be SQL variables, usually defined with ...

EXEC SQL
   BEGIN DECLARE SECTION
01  FILLER
      05 VAR1        PIC whatever
      05 var2          PIC whatever
END- EXEC

(Sheesh, it's been so long I've forgotten how to do this)

I'm sure you can find more in your SQL precompiler  manual about "executing
stored procedures"


MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol: SQL

- **From:** SergVSmola <member29849@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T05:54:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2914330.1053669261@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3e786cf9.2639044@news.newsguy.com>`

```

I read help in NetCOBOL docs
Sample from documentation:

An Example of Calling a Stored Procedure
The following figure is a COBOL program that calls a stored procedure.

EXEC SQL BEGIN DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.
01 INPUT-VARIABLE PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
01 OUTPUT-VARIABLE PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
EXEC SQL END DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
EXEC SQL CONNECT TO DEFAULT END-EXEC.
MOVE 100 TO INPUT-VARIABLE.
EXEC SQL
CALL PROC (:INPUT-VARIABLE, :OUTPUT-VARIABLE)
END-EXEC.
EXEC SQL ROLLBACK WORK END-EXEC.
EXEC SQL DISCONNECT DEFAULT END-EXEC.
STOP RUN.
Sample program that calls a stored procedure

My program where I use calling a stored procedure:
000010 @OPTIONS MAIN, APOST
000011 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000020 PROGRAM-ID.    STO_PROC.
000030 ENVIRONMENT    DIVISION.
000040 DATA           DIVISION.
000050 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000060     EXEC SQL BEGIN DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.
000142 01 SQLSTATE    PIC X(5).
000143 01 SQLMSG      PIC X(128).
000144 01 VAR1        PIC X(10).
000145 01 VAR2        PIC X(10).
000147     EXEC SQL END DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.
000148
000149 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000200* EMBEDDED EXCEPTION DECLARATION
000201     EXEC SQL
000202       WHENEVER NOT FOUND GO TO :P-END
000203     END-EXEC.
000204* CONNECT TO THE DATABASE ON THE DEFAULT SERVER.
000205     EXEC SQL CONNECT TO DEFAULT END-EXEC.
000206* storedprocedure SR.MB_PAY_PREDPR( "01.01.2003", "31.01.2003" )
000230     MOVE "01.01.2003" to VAR1
000231     MOVE "31.01.2003" to VAR2
000282     EXEC SQL
000283       CALL SR.MB_PAY_PREDPR ( :VAR1, :VAR2 )
000285     END-EXEC.
000290*******************
000300****   NOTES   ****
000310*******************
000320*    This program makes connect to Oracle RDBMS trough ODBC
connection
000330*    server   - abs1
000340*    user id  - urv_1
000350*    password - *****
000360*    view     - SR.MB_PAY_PREDPR
000440*-----------------------------------------------------------------
000450 P-END.
000460* DETERMINE CONNECTION
000800     EXEC SQL ROLLBACK WORK END-EXEC.
000830* DISCONNECT FROM THE DATABASE.
000840     EXEC SQL DISCONNECT DEFAULT END-EXEC.
000880 END-PROC.

Compiler message is:

** DIAGNOSTIC MESSAGE ** (STO_PROC)
D:\fsc\PCOBOL32\samples\StoProc\StoProc.COB 26: JMN2633I-S  SQL
  STATEMENT IS INVALID. ODBC-7600E MESSAGE ACCESS ERROR.
STATISTICS: HIGHEST SEVERITY CODE=S, PROGRAM UNIT=1

line 26 is: 000283       CALL SR.MB_PAY_PREDPR ( :VAR1, :VAR2 )

why SQL STATEMENT IS INVALID?
what are errors: JMN2633I-S and ODBC-7600E MESSAGE ACCESS ERROR?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: SQL

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T02:16:10+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ece2a60_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3e786cf9.2639044@news.newsguy.com> <2914330.1053669261@dbforums.com>`

```
Serg,

1. Check that SR.MB_PAY_PREDPR  is a stored procedure and not simply a
Database VIEW.

2. Try calling the procedure with literals coded in the parameter list:
SR.MB_PAY_PREDPR( "01.01.2003", "31.01.2003" )

This will establish whether the problem is in the interface or the way the
variables are set up.

3. Read the stored procedure on the Oracle DB and check that the parameters
are defined in the order you are calling them, there are the correct number
of them, and they are of the correct type.

4. Make sure you are running in a Win32 environment.

What are you expecting this procedure to return?

Pete.

"SergVSmola" <member29849@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:2914330.1053669261@dbforums.com...
>
> I read help in NetCOBOL docs
…[77 more quoted lines elided]…
> Posted via http://dbforums.com
```

## Thread: OT - Humor (not REALLY a comment on some recent postings to CLC) — 42 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - Humor (not REALLY a comment on some recent postings to CLC)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-19T15:12:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<babdr7$i33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
From a usually "funny" source (as opposed to my usual - "usually reliable
sources").  I make *NO* claims that I have verified any (much less all) of
these "attributions".

    ***

Question: If you could live forever, would you and why?

Answer: "I would not live forever, because we should not live forever,
because if we were supposed to live forever, then we would live forever, but
we cannot live forever, which is why I would not live forever,"
--Miss Alabama in the 1994 Miss USA contest.

``````````````````````````````````

"Whenever I watch TV and see those poor starving kids
all over the world, I can't help but cry. I mean I'd love to be skinny like
that, but not with all those flies and death and stuff." --Mariah Carey

`````````````````````````````````

"Smoking kills. If you're killed, you've lost a very important part of your
life,"
--Brooke Shields, during an interview to become Spokesperson for federal
antismoking campaign.

`````````````````````````````````````````````````

"I've never had major knee surgery on any other part of my body,"
--Winston Bennett, University of Kentucky basketball forward.

`````````````````````````````````````````````

"Outside of the killings, Washington has one of the lowest crime rates in
the country,"
--Mayor Marion Barry, Washington, DC.

`````````````````````````````

"That lowdown scoundrel deserves to be kicked to death by a jackass, and I'm
just the one to do it,"
--A congressional candidate in Texas.

``````````````````````

"Half this game is ninety percent mental."
--Philadelphia Phillies manager, Danny Ozark

``````````````````````````````````````

"I love California. I practically grew up in Phoenix."
--Dan Quayle

``````````````````````````````````````````````````````````````````

"We've got to pause and ask ourselves: How much clean air do we need?"
--Lee Iacocca

`````````````````````````````````

"I was provided with additional input that was radically different from the
truth. I assisted in furthering that version."
--Colonel Oliver North, from his Iran-Contra testimony.

`````````````````````````````````````````

"The word "genius" isn't applicable in football. A genius is a guy like
Norman Einstein."
--Joe Theisman, NFL football quarterback & sports analyst.

````````````````````````````````````````````

"We don't necessarily discriminate. We simply exclude certain types of
people."
--Colonel Gerald Wellman, ROTC Instructor.

````````````````````````````````````````````````````````````````

"Traditionally, most of Australia's imports come from overseas."
--Keppel Enderbery

```````````````

"Your food stamps will be stopped effective March 1992
because we received notice that you passed away. May God bless you. You may
reapply if there is a change in your circumstances."
--Department of Social Services, Greenville, South Carolina

````````````````````````````````````````````

"If somebody has a bad heart, they can plug this jack in at night as they go
to bed and it will monitor their heart throughout the night.  And the next
morning, when they wake up dead, there'll be a record."
--Mark S. Fowler, FCC Chairman

````````````````````````````````````````````````

....Feeling smarter yet?
```

ago, advertising a local event:

course, I didn't.

    -Chuck Stevens

#### ↳ Re: OT - Humor (not REALLY a comment on some recent postings to CLC)

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T14:08:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6fakcv4eqgsj13u8m18q23ahiuun8g8hvf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<babdr7$i33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>From a usually "funny" source (as opposed to my usual - "usually reliable
>sources").  I make *NO* claims that I have verified any (much less all) of
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>--Miss Alabama in the 1994 Miss USA contest.

Despite the nonsense, it IS difficult to argue against her statement.

>"Whenever I watch TV and see those poor starving kids
>all over the world, I can't help but cry. I mean I'd love to be skinny like
>that, but not with all those flies and death and stuff." --Mariah Carey

Such compassion.

>"Smoking kills. If you're killed, you've lost a very important part of your
>life,"
>--Brooke Shields, during an interview to become Spokesperson for federal
>antismoking campaign.

Hmmmmm...isn't Brooke a Princeton University grad?  Perhaps they
should nullify her degree.

>"I've never had major knee surgery on any other part of my body,"
>--Winston Bennett, University of Kentucky basketball forward.

This is why the NCAA Clearinghouse has become more strict in approving
High School athletes for participation in NCAA athletics.  I happen to
know a family whose son is a GREAT athlete, but has the brains of a
rock.  He was denied athletic participation by the clearinghouse.

>"Outside of the killings, Washington has one of the lowest crime rates in
>the country,"
>--Mayor Marion Barry, Washington, DC.

Spoken like a true crackhead.

>"That lowdown scoundrel deserves to be kicked to death by a jackass, and I'm
>just the one to do it,"
>--A congressional candidate in Texas.

Hmmmmm....at least he is honest about his qualifications!  If he were
not a jackass, he wouldn't qualify for political office.

>"Half this game is ninety percent mental."
>--Philadelphia Phillies manager, Danny Ozark

In other words, 45% of the game is mental?

>"I love California. I practically grew up in Phoenix."
>--Dan Quayle

Ok, ok......so he can't spell and his geographical skills are weak.  I
like to look at the bright side, at least he's not a Purdue graduate.

>"We've got to pause and ask ourselves: How much clean air do we need?"
>--Lee Iacocca

Just enough to breathe.....no more, no less....and send the dirty air
to those neighborhoods which have lower Chrysler Minivan purchase
rates.  Lee Iacocca for EPA Director!!

>"I was provided with additional input that was radically different from the
>truth. I assisted in furthering that version."
>--Colonel Oliver North, from his Iran-Contra testimony.

Ollie should have verified his sources first.  Although it's not much
consolation, at least he didn't know he was telling a lie.

We have a local man running for mayor who clearly claimed to be a
college graduate.  He was not and obviously knew he was not.  When his
opponent proved that he was not, he said that he "embellished."  Since
when has telling a lie been reduced to embellishment?  A lie is a lie.

>"The word "genius" isn't applicable in football. A genius is a guy like
>Norman Einstein."
>--Joe Theisman, NFL football quarterback & sports analyst.

Yep....good old Norm......developed the Theory of Relativity
(essentially the theory that all relatives who visit for more than 2
days at your house become excessively annoying).  Norm was on the
faculty of Princeton.......The Princeton Institute of Mixology in
Dogpatch, Pennsyltucky.  Joe Theisman attended Notre Dame where he
received a BS QoP (Bachelor of Science Quarterback Option Play).

>"We don't necessarily discriminate. We simply exclude certain types of
>people."
>--Colonel Gerald Wellman, ROTC Instructor.

Obviously, because of Col. Wellman's involvement, they do not exclude
people who are adept at "doublespeak."

>"Traditionally, most of Australia's imports come from overseas."
>--Keppel Enderbery

It's always a safe bet to state the obvious.

>"Your food stamps will be stopped effective March 1992
>because we received notice that you passed away. May God bless you. You may
>reapply if there is a change in your circumstances."
>--Department of Social Services, Greenville, South Carolina

Think that anyone reapplied?

>"If somebody has a bad heart, they can plug this jack in at night as they go
>to bed and it will monitor their heart throughout the night.  And the next
>morning, when they wake up dead, there'll be a record."
>--Mark S. Fowler, FCC Chairman

Perhaps it might make more sense to attach the jack to a defibrilator
than a recording device.  It's really annoying to wake up dead.

>....Feeling smarter yet?

Not really.........I'm quite sure that I have made statements along
the same lines as the scholarly ones identified in the above
quotations.  This is why celebrity and fame is not always as wonderful
as it may seem.

Very happy to be a part of the non-famous majority.




Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

```

```
On Tue, 20 May 2003 14:08:52 GMT, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
enlightened us:

>"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
<snip>

>>"I love California. I practically grew up in Phoenix."
>>--Dan Quayle
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Aah..one of my favorite people he says sarcastically talking about Dan
Quayle.  Here's a few other Quaylisms:

"It was just a job. It wasn't any special interest in consumer
affairs. I needed a paycheck and the Attorney General said that I
would be best to go down there, because he knew I was anti-consumer.

-- Vice President Dan Quayle and talking about his job as Chief
investigator, consumer protection division of the Indiana Attorney
General's office from 1970-1971.

"I'll have to check with my dad."

-- Dan Quayle responding when an Indiana GOP county chairman asked him
to run for Congress in 1976. (reported by the Washington Post,
10/2/88)

"I know one committee I don't want -- Judiciary. They are going to be
dealing with all those issues like abortion, bussing, voting rights,
prayers. I'm not interested in those issues and I want to stay as far
away from them as I can."

-- Senator Dan Quayle (from `What a Waste it Is to Lose One's Mind' --
the Unauthorized Autobiography)

And speaking of Dan and college, here are a couple of things Dan had
to say about his college days:

"I was a less than serious student in college. If I had it to do over
again, I would be far more serious. I did play a lot of golf. But I
don't think that's any reflection on my ability to lead this nation."

-- Vice President Dan Quayle

"Looking back, I should have pursued philosophy and economics and
things of that sort in college more, but I didn't."

-- Vice President Dan Quayle

"I didn't pay a lot of attention as I should have in college."

-- Vice President Dan Quayle

And just where did Dan go to college you ask?  DePauw University where
he maintained a 2.4 GPA and got numerous D's in political science
which was his major.  [DePauw is a small, private college in
Greencastle Indiana that generally has an enrollment of less than
3,000.]

He got into the University of Indiana Law School despite a policy they
had to reject all applicants with averages below 2.6. Quayle told frat
brothers that he applied to law school because he heard that "lawyers
make lots of money and do little." The Dean of Admissions was G. Kent
Frandsen, a Republican city judge who was always endorsed by the local
Quayle-family-owned newspaper. The Quayles gave lots of money to the
law school, and the Admissions Dean later officiated at Quayle's
marrige, and served as his campaign manager in Boone County when
Quayle ran for senate. 

Now for Bob Wolfe and I, who are both Purdue alums, it is no surprise
that someone like Dan Quayle has an IU degree :)


<snip>

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Real Signs:
Dry Cleaner's, Bangkok:  DROP YOUR TROUSERS HERE FOR THE BEST RESULTS
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Humor (not REALLY a comment on some recent postings to CLC)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T21:03:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v42tcvs48fc6670h8h4dotuhnir2g51ijm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<babdr7$i33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <6fakcv4eqgsj13u8m18q23ahiuun8g8hvf@4ax.com> <50mscvcmjk3eq5gvmkh99lm6hh37aipji0@4ax.com> <arvscvods4m44aubekn1cp3fuc63nsj593@4ax.com> <bam0pl$1mm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 23-May-2003, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>do things I think are more important.

Hoawrd:

I have absolutely nothing against universities accepting donations
from rich families, football fans, corporations or foundations.  In
fact, I applaud those who choose to unselfishly contribute to higher
education.

I do find it unsettling when the donation has "strings attached."

I'm not sure that I consider your:

"I will work if you pay me money"

in the same realm as:

"I will pay you money if you allow my unqualified kid to be admitted."

In your case, you are exchanging your skills for money and then using
the money to pay for activities and things you enjoy.  That's
honorable.

In the case of a school, 99.9% of the students admitted earned their
admission.....just as you earned your money.

The stupid, lazy kid was the beneficiary of admission without having
worked for it.....even worse, he very likely bumped a smarter,
motivated kid out of the admissions process.  In my opinion, this is
not the same thing as working hard to earn an honest buck.

Rich daddy and mommy are also doing their child a terrible disservice.
They are teaching him or her that it's not important to work toward
your goals......just throw money.

The university which takes donations in exchange for admitting an
unqualified candidate is just as guilty.



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

```

On 23-May-2003, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:

> Rich daddy and mommy are also doing their child a terrible disservice.
> They are teaching him or her that it's not important to work toward
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> unqualified candidate is just as guilty.

So the university gets money to hire more professors.  The unqualified candidate
flunks out (if he passes, he is qualified).   And with the money, more needy
people get educations.

But we don't want to educate the needy badly enough to do anything that seems
less than honorable do we?   Our pride is more important than their educations.


> Rich daddy and mommy are also doing their child a terrible disservice.
> They are teaching him or her that it's not important to work toward
> your goals......just throw money.

Sure - but who cares if the rich kids don't go to universities appropriate to
their abilities?    It's the poor kids that I want to have the advantage of the
rich parents' money.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Humor (not REALLY a comment on some recent postings to CLC)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T14:59:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qae9dvk88i06d6uuls4ihb78ioq4pa8pvs@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<babdr7$i33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <6fakcv4eqgsj13u8m18q23ahiuun8g8hvf@4ax.com> <50mscvcmjk3eq5gvmkh99lm6hh37aipji0@4ax.com> <arvscvods4m44aubekn1cp3fuc63nsj593@4ax.com> <bam0pl$1mm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v42tcvs48fc6670h8h4dotuhnir2g51ijm@4ax.com> <bb05l2$3al$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 23-May-2003, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>less than honorable do we?   Our pride is more important than their educations.

Howard:

Pride has nothing to do with it.  Maintaining standards of excellence
has everything to do with it.

And if the university lowers their standards in exchange for cash to
accept the underqualified applicant, it is reasonable to assume that
the university will also allow the failing student to receive passing
grades so as to prevent the cash flow from drying up after one
semester.

In the US, society pays for education through tax dollars.  This
includes elementary, secondary and post secondary education.
Community colleges and state universities are funded through local,
state and federal funds.  Students can almost always enroll in
work-study programs and obtain education loans.  Almost every college
requires the parents to complete financial need forms in order to
determine the "expected family contribution."  Based upon the expected
contribution, those with a lower contribution are generally given
higher priority for need-based scholarships and grants.

Those qualified candidates who also happen to be needy can indeed
obtain an excellent college education through their local community
college or through a state university at a much lower cost than MIT,
Harvard, Yale or Princeton.

Needy individuals can also obtain an outstanding education through
non-publicly funded schools.  I happen to know a brilliant young lady
who was able to gain admission to an Ivy League school despite the
fact that her parents' income was very low.  She only pays $5,000 per
year because of educational grants.  Total Cost of Attendance at the
school is $38,000 per year.  She was both needy as well as qualified.
Grants and scholarships do allow the needy of society to obtain an
education at schools other than publicly funded schools.

Howard, I am not against contributions to universities.  Princeton has
a massive endowment and they offer grants to students which vary in
size based upon the income of the parents of the student.  This is
wonderful.  

I am 100% in favor of contributions to institutions of higher
learning.  In fact, I purchased my home from Northampton Community
College, because the family donated it in order to start a scholarship
fund in the name of their late father who was a trustee at the
community college.  It was gratifying to know that the proceeds of the
sale of my home went toward higher education.  I am against
contributions to universities in which "I'll give you money if you
admit my kid" strings are attached to the contribution....and it does
indeed happen.

Universities do themselves a tremendous disservice when they allow an
underqualified candidate to be admitted based upon a large donation
from a rich daddy.  They lower their own standards of excellence by
rejecting applicants who have proven their academic worth in favor of
those who have not.

This country offers a wonderful diversity of educational opportunities
for persons of all academic qualifications and all levels of income.
For those who truly want an education, it can be achieved.  There are
countless stories of highly motivated people with little or no income
who have achieved their academic goals through hard work and
diligence.

Persistence and determination are key factors.  Those who are not
motivated, should not be handed admission to a university nor handed
an education without some effort on their behalf.  An education should
be earned (i.e. DD'd mantra, "do your own homework").

>> Rich daddy and mommy are also doing their child a terrible disservice.
>> They are teaching him or her that it's not important to work toward
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>rich parents' money.

And that happens now, through the wonderful generosity of those
wealthy persons in society who contribute for philanthropic reasons.
My objection is based solely upon compromising the standards of higher
education by allowing admission to underqualified individuals in
exchange for a contribution.

If it is acceptable to compromise standards in exchange for cash,
perhaps we should allow rich daddies and mommies to have a "purchase
option" for their kids' medical board examinations, pharmacy board
examinations, certified professional engineering board examinations
and the bar examinations.  The money could go to underwrite senior
citizen medical costs, senior citizen prescription costs, costs to
repair our  transportation infrastructure and court appointed attorney
fees...all wonderful benefits to society.

....but then we would have more people dying on the operating room
table, more incorrect medication being dispensed, more unsafe bridges
and fewer competent attorneys for those unable to pay for their legal
defense.

Lowering standards in exchange for cash is just not an acceptable
alternative in my mind.




Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

> And if the university lowers their standards in exchange for cash to

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Humor (not REALLY a comment on some recent postings to CLC)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T20:26:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l30adv0k0f62al9qpjibq7nrhnjhcscles@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<babdr7$i33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <6fakcv4eqgsj13u8m18q23ahiuun8g8hvf@4ax.com> <50mscvcmjk3eq5gvmkh99lm6hh37aipji0@4ax.com> <arvscvods4m44aubekn1cp3fuc63nsj593@4ax.com> <bam0pl$1mm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v42tcvs48fc6670h8h4dotuhnir2g51ijm@4ax.com> <bb05l2$3al$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qae9dvk88i06d6uuls4ihb78ioq4pa8pvs@4ax.com> <bb2kov$c19$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 28-May-2003, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>I will agree with you.   I thought we were discussing the admissions standards.

Howard:

We were talking about admissions, but earlier, you mentioned that
allowing admission to the underqualified wasn't a problem because the
ones who failed would not be able to remain at the university anyway.
Passing or failing is pertinent to grade assignment standards, not
admission standards.

Lowering admission standards in exchange for cash is not a far cry
from nudging a failing student's grade up just a notch in order to
allow him or her to pass.

Standards are standards.........and an organization which proclaims
high standards and then bends them to suit their needs is guilty of
misrepresentation to their own customers...in this case, the students
and the employers who hire recent graduates.

If the cash is so critical to the operation of the university fulfill
their objective to educate the needy, a university willing to lower
their admission standards to obtain the extra cash would very likely
also be willing to bump a student's grades up from a failing grade to
a barely passing grade to allow the student to remain at the
university.  The student remains at the school......the school gets
daddy's money........the needy obtain their education.......the
university's purpose is fulfilled.

....and I'm quite sure that this does indeed occur at many
institutions on a regular basis.

>
>If the rich person gives enough money to allow the university to educate the
>needy - then I'm all for it.

You and I are already contributing toward this goal....through our tax
dollars which are distributed to local community colleges and state
universities.  The contributions and endowments from the wealthy
should be icing on the cake........not the cake itself.



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Humor (not REALLY a comment on some recent postings to CLC)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T20:56:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb37pb$j7u$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<babdr7$i33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <6fakcv4eqgsj13u8m18q23ahiuun8g8hvf@4ax.com> <50mscvcmjk3eq5gvmkh99lm6hh37aipji0@4ax.com> <arvscvods4m44aubekn1cp3fuc63nsj593@4ax.com> <bam0pl$1mm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v42tcvs48fc6670h8h4dotuhnir2g51ijm@4ax.com> <bb05l2$3al$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qae9dvk88i06d6uuls4ihb78ioq4pa8pvs@4ax.com> <bb2kov$c19$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <l30adv0k0f62al9qpjibq7nrhnjhcscles@4ax.com>`

```

On 28-May-2003, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:

> Standards are standards.........and an organization which proclaims
> high standards and then bends them to suit their needs is guilty of
> misrepresentation to their own customers...in this case, the students
> and the employers who hire recent graduates.

As important as standards are, people are more important.

> If the cash is so critical to the operation of the university fulfill
> their objective to educate the needy, a university willing to lower
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> institutions on a regular basis.

It could be - but that is a different problem.   The degree needs to be
valuable, which means it should be worth what it says it is worth.  If you want
to argue about graduation standards, I'm sure you can find someone to do so.  
(You sound like the person who says every heroin user started off drinking
alcohol).

> >If the rich person gives enough money to allow the university to educate the
> >needy - then I'm all for it.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> should be icing on the cake........not the cake itself.

I kind of prefer rich people contributing voluntarily than regular people
contributing because the state says we have to.  And each poor person with a
good education is not icing, nor even cake, but is bread and meat.
```

do not.

- **References:** `<babdr7$i33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <6fakcv4eqgsj13u8m18q23ahiuun8g8hvf@4ax.com> <50mscvcmjk3eq5gvmkh99lm6hh37aipji0@4ax.com> <arvscvods4m44aubekn1cp3fuc63nsj593@4ax.com> <bam0pl$1mm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v42tcvs48fc6670h8h4dotuhnir2g51ijm@4ax.com> <bb05l2$3al$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qae9dvk88i06d6uuls4ihb78ioq4pa8pvs@4ax.com> <bb2kov$c19$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <l30adv0k0f62al9qpjibq7nrhnjhcscles@4ax.com> <3ed5d197.43139375@news.optonline.net>`

On 29-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> I'm told by a recent Princeton graduate that getting in is the hard part. Once
> you're in, you can coast through with fluff classes or you can take legitimate
> classes, it's your choice. Some rich kids coast through.

I've read that this is more so in most countries - Japan being an example.  You
work very hard to qualify to get into the school you want, then you can take it
easy.

I am a big supporter of the USAmerican way - it is relatively easy to get in,
but graduating is harder.   This is more expensive, but the dividends are worth
it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Humor (not REALLY a comment on some recent postings to CLC)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T15:56:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qgadnXFeT7RkTU6jXTWJiQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<babdr7$i33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <6fakcv4eqgsj13u8m18q23ahiuun8g8hvf@4ax.com> <50mscvcmjk3eq5gvmkh99lm6hh37aipji0@4ax.com> <arvscvods4m44aubekn1cp3fuc63nsj593@4ax.com> <bam0pl$1mm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v42tcvs48fc6670h8h4dotuhnir2g51ijm@4ax.com>`

```

"Bob Wolfe" <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
>
> The stupid, lazy kid was the beneficiary of admission without having
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> unqualified candidate is just as guilty.

1. The rich kid didn't 'bump' the better qualified kid; were it not for the
'rich' kid, some number (one? ten? a hundred?) 'qualified' students wouldn't
have a chance. His parent's 'gift' enables the institution to provide room
for some number of less-wealthy students. College admission is not a zero
sum game (like, for example, the Civil War draft).

2. Their institution, their rules. No criticism should be levied against
those who play by the rules.

3. Some confuse "work" and "goals." The purpose - the goal - of an education
is the knowledge that comes from it (plus an invitation to certain jobs and
society circles). The goal is NOT "work." If a problem can be solved by
money or effort, one picks between the two methods. Did you build your own
house? Or did you pay a contractor? The result is the same: one path is
neither dirtier nor more righteous than the other.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Humor (not REALLY a comment on some recent postings to CLC)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T15:09:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vrj9dvc6kvh1i7o4vt275p4pn41a5df2lt@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<babdr7$i33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <6fakcv4eqgsj13u8m18q23ahiuun8g8hvf@4ax.com> <50mscvcmjk3eq5gvmkh99lm6hh37aipji0@4ax.com> <arvscvods4m44aubekn1cp3fuc63nsj593@4ax.com> <bam0pl$1mm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v42tcvs48fc6670h8h4dotuhnir2g51ijm@4ax.com> <qgadnXFeT7RkTU6jXTWJiQ@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

>
>"Bob Wolfe" <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>sum game (like, for example, the Civil War draft).

I disagree.  In fact, some of the best universities admit only 10% of
the total number of applicants.  I can assure you that some of those
who are admitted are less qualified academically than many who were
not admitted.

>2. Their institution, their rules. No criticism should be levied against
>those who play by the rules.

But if their rules result in "dumbing down" the student population,
they are only doing themselves a disservice.

>3. Some confuse "work" and "goals." The purpose - the goal - of an education
>is the knowledge that comes from it (plus an invitation to certain jobs and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>neither dirtier nor more righteous than the other.

The problem can indeed be solved by effort if the money is not
available.  Stories of hard working and diligent individuals who have
struggled to advance their education abound.

If the college admits lesser qualified individuals in exchange for
cash, why not just allow them to pay the $160,000 up front and give
them a degree?  In both cases, the cash is considered more important
then the effort being demonstrated by the individual.




Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Humor (not REALLY a comment on some recent postings to CLC)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T15:35:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb2kvm$c1h$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<babdr7$i33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <6fakcv4eqgsj13u8m18q23ahiuun8g8hvf@4ax.com> <50mscvcmjk3eq5gvmkh99lm6hh37aipji0@4ax.com> <arvscvods4m44aubekn1cp3fuc63nsj593@4ax.com> <bam0pl$1mm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v42tcvs48fc6670h8h4dotuhnir2g51ijm@4ax.com> <qgadnXFeT7RkTU6jXTWJiQ@giganews.com> <vrj9dvc6kvh1i7o4vt275p4pn41a5df2lt@4ax.com>`

```

On 28-May-2003, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:

> If the college admits lesser qualified individuals in exchange for
> cash, why not just allow them to pay the $160,000 up front and give
> them a degree?  In both cases, the cash is considered more important
> then the effort being demonstrated by the individual.

Because admission does not confer a degree.   A college degree doesn't indicate
how good someone was in high school, nor how good one's SATs were - it indicates
how good of a student one's college education was.

If that person gets admitted because of such a donation, or because one plays
good football, or because one is of a minority - but is able to compete, learn,
and get a degree - all that matters in the end is that one passed the courses
and learned became educated.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Humor (not REALLY a comment on some recent postings to CLC)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T20:42:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9t6advkkvk2rquj27db9soo90r1dtst556@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<babdr7$i33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <6fakcv4eqgsj13u8m18q23ahiuun8g8hvf@4ax.com> <50mscvcmjk3eq5gvmkh99lm6hh37aipji0@4ax.com> <arvscvods4m44aubekn1cp3fuc63nsj593@4ax.com> <bam0pl$1mm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v42tcvs48fc6670h8h4dotuhnir2g51ijm@4ax.com> <qgadnXFeT7RkTU6jXTWJiQ@giganews.com> <vrj9dvc6kvh1i7o4vt275p4pn41a5df2lt@4ax.com> <bb2kvm$c1h$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 28-May-2003, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>and learned became educated.

Howard:

I have no problem if one plays football well and is able to earn an
athletic scholarship.  In this case, the individual has earned the
scholarship.  Race is not an issue in this discussion.

I still contend that the university who would stoop to lower their
admission standards to gain a little rich kid will also lower their
passing standards for the same individual in order to keep the kid at
the school for four years.....or better yet......5 years!  

Wealth and those who use their wealth to lower academic standards is
the issue.  Those universities which choose to engage in this practice
certainly have the right to do so.

Obviously we must agree to disagree here.....my experience with those
who are born with a silver spoon in their mouths is that they are
generally lazy sloths who expect everything to be provided to them.

Those individuals who I have the greatest respect and admiration for
are those who have earned their accolades through persistence,
diligence and hard work......regardless of whether they were born into
wealth or poverty.



 



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> >1. The rich kid didn't 'bump' the better qualified kid; were it not for
the
> >'rich' kid, some number (one? ten? a hundred?) 'qualified' students
wouldn't
> >have a chance. His parent's 'gift' enables the institution to provide
room
> >for some number of less-wealthy students. College admission is not a zero
> >sum game (like, for example, the Civil War draft).
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> not admitted.

So what?

>
> >2. Their institution, their rules. No criticism should be levied against
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> they are only doing themselves a disservice.

They know that. But what if the 'dumbing down' is mandated by law? In some
places, state universities MUST admit the top 10% in each high school's
graduating class. It's provable that any in the top 50% of one school are
way brighter than any in the top 10% of another school. Yet these 10-percent
dummies get in while top-notch students languish flipping burgers.

>
> >3. Some confuse "work" and "goals." The purpose - the goal - of an
education
> >is the knowledge that comes from it (plus an invitation to certain jobs
and
> >society circles). The goal is NOT "work." If a problem can be solved by
> >money or effort, one picks between the two methods. Did you build your
own
> >house? Or did you pay a contractor? The result is the same: one path is
> >neither dirtier nor more righteous than the other.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> then the effort being demonstrated by the individual.

Because it's $160,000 for ADMISSION, not graduation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Humor (not REALLY a comment on some recent postings to CLC)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T15:23:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<os8cdvgtaagk61dpceg3mgnuuq8h637l1n@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<babdr7$i33$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <6fakcv4eqgsj13u8m18q23ahiuun8g8hvf@4ax.com> <50mscvcmjk3eq5gvmkh99lm6hh37aipji0@4ax.com> <arvscvods4m44aubekn1cp3fuc63nsj593@4ax.com> <bam0pl$1mm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v42tcvs48fc6670h8h4dotuhnir2g51ijm@4ax.com> <qgadnXFeT7RkTU6jXTWJiQ@giganews.com> <vrj9dvc6kvh1i7o4vt275p4pn41a5df2lt@4ax.com> <v7ednYntE7wfkEijXTWJiQ@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

>
>"Bob Wolfe" <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote in message
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>So what?

So........the rich kid, DID in fact bump the better qualified
kid....no question about it.

>> >2. Their institution, their rules. No criticism should be levied against
>> >those who play by the rules.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>dummies get in while top-notch students languish flipping burgers.

Mandatory "dumbing down" is not the topic of discussion......the issue
which is being debated is the practice of universities allowing
admission to underqualified applicants based solely upon a donation to
the university from a rich daddy.

I'm quite sure that this is not mandated by law.

>> >3. Some confuse "work" and "goals." The purpose - the goal - of an
>education
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Because it's $160,000 for ADMISSION, not graduation.

You miss my point.....and it's NOT $160,000 for admission.  It's only
$19,000 for admission for the first semester.  The kid has to maintain
a minimal GPA to continue.

My point:  The university who gets a nice $50,000 donation from rich
daddy in exchange for admitting his less-than-brilliant kid, will
certainly want to see that kid graduate.....so that they can tap daddy
for more money......if they lower their standards for admission in
exchange for cash why not fudge the kid's GPA to keep the well from
running dry?


Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

named after former presidents and vice-presidents.

There will be the Gerald Ford bond, which has no interest. 

And there will be the Bill Clinton bond, which has no principal.

<a id="t-4d8d9a3d8e"></a>
## Thread: OT - Humor (Part 2) — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - Humor (Part 2)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-19T15:28:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<babeol$67t$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
So, if you think you are so much smarter than those in my other "humor"
post, I also received the following "quiz" (again, I don't vouch for its
accuracy)

    ***

You don't have to be a rocket scientist to get these, but it helps!!!

    (Passing requires 4 correct answers)

1) How long did the Hundred Years War last?

2) Which country makes Panama hats?

3) >From which animal do we get catgut?

4) In which month do Russians celebrate the October Revolution?

5) What is a camel's hair brush made of?

6) The Canary Islands in the Pacific are named after what animal?

7) What was King George VI's first name?

8) What color is a purple finch?

9) Where are Chinese gooseberries from?

10) What is the color of the black box in a commercial airplane?

All done?

Check your answers below!






ANSWERS TO THE QUIZ














1) How long did the Hundred Years War last?
     *116 years

2) Which country makes Panama hats?
    *Ecuador

3) From which animal do we get cat gut?
    *Sheep and Horses

4) In which month did Russians celebrate the October Revolution?
    *November

5) What is a camel's hair brush made of?
    *Squirrel fur

6) The Canary Islands in the Pacific are named after what animal?
    *Dogs

) What was King George VI's first name?
    *Albert

8) What color is a purple finch?
    *Crimson

9) Where are Chinese gooseberries from?
    *New Zealand

10) What is the color of the black box in a commercial airplane?
    *Orange, of course.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - Humor (Part 2)

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T12:53:06+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ec979b6_3@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<babeol$67t$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Oh Dear,

sorry sent a blank response by accident...

this one is the real one...

I did quite well in your test, Bill, but I was APPALLED to see that the
Canary Islands, which, I visit fairly frequently, and are just off the West
Coast of
Africa (in the ATLANTIC Ocean), have now been relocated to the Pacific! (I
knew the Romans bred dogs there and the name comes from Latin Canis)...I
think you'll find the Romans didn't actually make it to the Pacific....There
is some controversial evidence that the Vikings and Egyptians MAY have, but
not the Romans...

If you are going to have a "pedant trap" type quiz, then we have a right to
expect the information to be correct in the questions (and answers).

I DIDN'T know the purple finch (but guessed it wasn't purple <G>), The camel
hair brush is NOT made from squirrel in Europe (it is ox, goat or pony, the
majority being pony (cheapest) and the ox hair is taken from behind the
ears). The rest of them I got right <G>.

I always enjoy quizes, but blatant inaccuracy (like the Canary Islands) kind
of sours the pleasure.

Pete.

- **References:** `<babeol$67t$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0305191804.1196f310@posting.google.com> <8a2d426e.0305192043.341ad5b3@posting.google.com> <vcla2ab8oe38af@corp.supernews.com> <baed2r$bg4$1@panix1.panix.com>`

On 20-May-2003, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> >OT: Budgerigar - an Australian Aboriginal word meaning "good food".
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'box of appetisers'.

My birds are Cockatiels.  When I am working on my computer at home they like to
sit on my legs.  This means I can't wear shorts around the house.  Sometimes the
one that has laid eggs picks on the other and we have to separate them (My wife
has her own computer two floors away).  I suspect the other one is male, but
don't know for sure (the 4 eggs we have seen are all sterile).   I do have to
check myself in the mirror before going out of the house, as I can't tell what
they do down my back when they are on my shoulders.

The (possibly male) one will stick his whole head in my mouth to grab some of
what I am eating.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Humor (Part 2)

_(thread depth: 6)_

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Humor (Part 2)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T17:30:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ECBB3F4.B64D2D7E@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<babeol$67t$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3ECB06C8.E1CE1780@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```


Rosa Fischer wrote:

> You could add the question "when is the october feast in Munich".
>
> Have a nice day
>
> Rosa

Answer : Oktober ?

Kind of reminds me, some forty years ago went into an ice cream parlour
in Cologne with my wife. A middle aged Belgian couple joined us at our
table.

With some six months in Germany with NATO, my Deutsch was limited to
"Funf litre bitte" That's the Italian Lambretta not the NSU version.
Hey, here was an opportunity to wax forth with my schoolboy French. .
"Bon jour. Qui demurez vous ?"

The answer was that they lived fairly close to Aix la Chapelle - but not
knowing the true French pronunciation of "Aix", I just didn't get it. So
this topic went on for a while as they tried to explain, with much of
the 'conversation' being hand signs.

A rather gruff German was sitting at an accompanying table. He could
stand this nonsense no more. He informed me with one word,  "AACHEN !".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-2014aee21b"></a>
## Thread: Original title of newsletter article: Is COBOL the 18-Wheeler of the Web? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Original title of newsletter article: Is COBOL the 18-Wheeler of the Web?

- **From:** richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com (Chris Richardson)
- **Date:** 2003-05-19T21:50:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b11feff.0305192050.9b4f2c7@posting.google.com>`

```
I read this article and thought I'd copy/paste the entire newsletter
article here to share with you all. I received it from
"Peter_Coffee@eletters.eweek.com".

<Begin Copy and Paste>
=========================================================
Is COBOL the 18-Wheeler of the Web?

Legacy code may be stuck at the truck stop if
tomorrow's coders choose other rides.

By Peter Coffee

If you're looking for a hot combination of highly employable
skills, consider writing code to provide Web services--in
COBOL.

Can a person build a 21st-century IT career on this 1960s
foundation? Well, foundations are better than shifting
sands. Legacy Reserves, a databank for over-35 IT pros,
cites Gartner estimates that retirement and death will
shrink the population of working COBOL coders by 13 percent
between 2002 and 2006, even while 15 percent of all new
applications are being written in the language--and quotes
the GIGA Group as predicting that "The most highly paid
programmers in the next ten years are going to be COBOL
programmers who know the Internet."

(Read about the origins of COBOL:)
http://eletters.eweek.com/zd/cts?d=79-31-6-7-75629-3628-1
(Read more COBOL facts:)
http://eletters.eweek.com/zd/cts?d=79-31-6-7-75629-3631-1

It doesn't matter whether you enter the world of Web
services through the door marked ".Net" or the one labeled
"Java" (and after all, the whole point is to avoid being
locked into either one). Fujitsu's NetCOBOL for .Net
produces Common Language Runtime code that integrates with
Microsoft languages like C# and Visual Basic .Net; Micro
Focus plans to do the same by summer. Fujitsu has also
commissioned a multimedia training course, with textbook,
for developers who want to learn more.

(Find out more about Fujitsu's NetCOBOL for .Net:)
http://eletters.eweek.com/zd/cts?d=79-31-6-7-75629-3634-1
(Check out the .NET Framework Developer's Guide to Common
Language Runtime:)
http://eletters.eweek.com/zd/cts?d=79-31-6-7-75629-3637-1
(Read "COBOL on .Net Next for Micro Focus":)
http://eletters.eweek.com/zd/cts?d=79-31-6-7-75629-3640-1
(Find out more about Fujitsu's training course:)
http://eletters.eweek.com/zd/cts?d=79-31-6-7-75629-3643-1

Meanwhile, at GigaWorld IT Forum last week in Phoenix, Micro
Focus rolled out its Enterprise Server platform for
COBOL/J2EE integration. LegacyJ Corp.'s  PERCobol takes
another road, compiling 15 dialects of COBOL source code to
Java Virtual Machine executables. And earlier this month,
Acucorp announced forthcoming release of its extend6 lineup
of COBOL-based integration tools for XML--along with file
system extensions for handling large objects, such as images
and other multimedia, in record sizes as large as 64
megabytes.

(Find out more about GigaWorld IT Forum:)
http://eletters.eweek.com/zd/cts?d=79-31-6-7-75629-3646-1
(Read more about Micro Focus' Enterprise Server:)
http://eletters.eweek.com/zd/cts?d=79-31-6-7-75629-3649-1
(Find out more about LegacyJ's PERCobol:)
http://eletters.eweek.com/zd/cts?d=79-31-6-7-75629-3652-1
(Find out more about Acucorp's extend6 series:)
http://eletters.eweek.com/zd/cts?d=79-31-6-7-75629-3655-1

One of my son's favorite T-shirts bears an IBM COBOL logo,
along with a cartoon drawing of a shark in sunglasses and a
quotation from one of my columns. Though it's often called a
dinosaur, I argued in that column that COBOL better
resembles the shark: It's been ruling its niche since the
beginning, with a plausible challenger yet to emerge.

(Read Artur Reimann's "COBOL, Language of Choice--Then and
Now: Looking Back to Get a Glimpse at the Future":)
http://eletters.eweek.com/zd/cts?d=79-31-6-7-75629-3658-1

But if you query Google with "Java," you'll get almost 34
million hits. Ask Amazon.com about Java books, and you'll
find more than 2,000 titles. Do the same things with "COBOL"
as the search term, and you'll get fewer than 1 million
Google results and only 700 books. I wonder what language is
being used to cut the paychecks of all those Java coders?

To paraphrase the old proverb about accomplishments and
credit, there's no limit to how much work a language can do
if its vendors and practitioners don't care who gets the
buzz. But if the COBOL community wants to know why it gets
so little street cred, perhaps it should take a collective
look in the mirror. Follow that first Google hit to The
COBOL Center, and you'll find a "COBOL News" list whose
leading items are mostly past their first birthday: Of the
six news items on that home page as of last week, only one
bears a 2003 date. Hype alone is certainly not sufficient,
but a modest amount may be a necessary catalyst to the
production of future talent.

(Check out The COBOL Center:)
http://eletters.eweek.com/zd/cts?d=79-31-6-7-75629-3661-1

It takes a while to refill the pipeline of critical skills,
after we notice that it's running dry. If we're going to
need people in, say, 2008 who have current knowledge of the
Internet and the Web, practiced skills in writing COBOL code
that can use those network resources, and five to 10 years
of experience in leading a development team, now is not too
soon to start developing those assets.

Draw me your COBOL road map:
mailto:peter_coffee@ziffdavis.com

=========================================================

<End Copy and Paste>
```

## Thread: ASCII on IBM-Host in COBOL — 2 msgs

### ASCII on IBM-Host in COBOL

- **From:** G.Chalupka@t-online.de (Guenter Chalupka)
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T04:42:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bd02de0b.0305200342.5f08fa4d@posting.google.com>`

```
Everyone,

I have the problem to write a DATASET of variable length in ASCII on
an IBM-Host in a COBOL-Program.
Seams to be easy:
I put    ALPHABET ALPHABET-NAME IS STANDARD-1 to the  SPECIAL-NAMES.
I put    FD DS-OUT
            RECORDING MODE IS V
            BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
            CODE-SET IS ALPHABET-NAME
            RECORD VARYING DEPENDING ON DS-OUT-LENGTH.
         01 DS-OUT-RECORD.
            05 DS-RECORD  PIC X(1)  OCCURS 9  TO 9000
                                    DEPENDING ON DS-OUT-LENGTH.

I put    the DCB-parameter OPTCD=Q in the JCL

but I get file-status 90 or 39 at the open depending on diferent
changes of the JCL.

What's my mistake?
Thank you for your assistance.

G.Chalupka
```

#### ↳ Re: ASCII on IBM-Host in COBOL

- **From:** wobconsult@sprynet.com (WOB Consulting)
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T17:46:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<173f80ae.0305201646.98bd33a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bd02de0b.0305200342.5f08fa4d@posting.google.com>`

```
G.Chalupka@t-online.de (Guenter Chalupka) wrote in message news:<bd02de0b.0305200342.5f08fa4d@posting.google.com>...
> Everyone,
> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> G.Chalupka

In the FD, try "RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE FROM 9 TO 9000 CHARACTERS
DEPENDING ON DS-OUT-LENGTH".

Define the 01 level as DS-OUT-RECORD PIC X(9000).

In WS, define DS-OUT-LENGTH as PIC 9(09) BINARY. Make sure it's
unsigned.

Move the length that you want to write to DS-OUT-LENGTH prior to the
actual WRITE instruction.

The length you had specified in DS-OUT-LENGTH will be the length of
the record that was written.

Bill
```

---
