## Thread: Cobfattr in Power Cobol — 12 msgs

### Cobfattr in Power Cobol

- **From:** Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T12:02:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2900664.1053432122@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi, If you  in DOS prompt

type Cobfattr  nameoffile > trash

you get all information about this file, but
if you  do this inside a PowerCobol Program
doesnt work .

Anybody he Knows why ?

by the way any other command works


 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01 Command-Line        PIC X(128).
 01 ReturnValue PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
 PROCEDURE       DIVISION.
     MOVE "Cobfattr nomeoffile > trash"   TO Command-Line
     INVOKE CmDDE1 "Execute"
       USING     Command-Line
                 POW-SWSHOWNORMAL
       RETURNING ReturnValue


C. Lages
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobfattr in Power Cobol

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T16:05:39+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jl9kcvkjq0h0k8c0dm67k3b0t9k3c1fctt@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<2900664.1053432122@dbforums.com>`

```
On Tue, 20 May 2003 12:02:02 +0000 Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com>
wrote:

:>Hi, If you  in DOS prompt

:>type Cobfattr  nameoffile > trash

:>you get all information about this file, but
:>if you  do this inside a PowerCobol Program
:>doesnt work .

:>Anybody he Knows why ?

:>by the way any other command works

:> WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
:> 01 Command-Line        PIC X(128).
:> 01 ReturnValue PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
:> PROCEDURE       DIVISION.
:>     MOVE "Cobfattr nomeoffile > trash"   TO Command-Line
:>     INVOKE CmDDE1 "Execute"
:>       USING     Command-Line
:>                 POW-SWSHOWNORMAL
:>       RETURNING ReturnValue

That is because COMMAND.COM does the redirection to support this.

As you are not invoking COMMAND.COM to process it, you must do the redirection
yourself, i.e., redirecting standard output to TRASH. 

Unfortunately I have misplaced my DOS BIBLE, but I am sure that there are
sites with the information on how to do the DOS call.

If you wish to be sloppy, you can invoke a batch file which does it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobfattr in Power Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T16:39:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<awSdneOKLd8e3lCjXTWJkA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<2900664.1053432122@dbforums.com> <jl9kcvkjq0h0k8c0dm67k3b0t9k3c1fctt@4ax.com> <bae1a1$lbk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5b8d7c7.0305210324.4a27cd6f@posting.google.com> <2911281.1053614966@dbforums.com>`

```

"Carlos lages" <member129@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:2911281.1053614966@dbforums.com...
>
> Paulo, using Pow-self  instead of POW-DDE  doesnt Work too
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You could write your own. Try something like this:

    SELECT MYFILE ASSIGN TO IDISAM
        ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL
        FILE STATUS IS MYFILE-FS.

FD  MYFILE.
01  MYREC.
   02  FILLER        PIC X(50).
  02  NUMREC-1  PIC S9(9) COMP-4.
  02  FILLER        PIC X(2).
  02  NUMREC-2  PIC 9(9) COMP-4.

LINKAGE SECTION.
01  FILE-REC-1            PIC S9(9) COMP-4.

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING FILE-REC-1.

OPEN INPUT MYFILE.
READ MYFILE.
CLOSE MYFILE.
IF MYFILE-FS NOT = '00'
    MOVE 0 TO NUMREC-1.
MOVE NUMREC-1 TO FILE-REC-1
GOBACK.

It looks like, from scanning the file header, Fujitsu has three locations
for number of records (these two at bytes 51 and 57 and another starting at
byte 267).

It may take some experimenting to discover which is the real one (or they
may be redundant).
```

- **References:** `<2900664.1053432122@dbforums.com> <jl9kcvkjq0h0k8c0dm67k3b0t9k3c1fctt@4ax.com> <bae1a1$lbk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5b8d7c7.0305210324.4a27cd6f@posting.google.com> <2911281.1053614966@dbforums.com> <awSdneOKLd8e3lCjXTWJkA@giganews.com> <2913486.1053646342@dbforums.com> <cAGdnXqH2aJ0DlCjXTWJkA@giganews.com> <2915058.1053691513@dbforums.com>`

```

"Carlos lages" <member129@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:2915058.1053691513@dbforums.com...
>
> I think will be easier,.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ok, ok, OK  I could do this in Power too, but hard work

No, it's a single subroutine compiled outside of PowerCobol. We have
HUNDREDS of special-purpose subprograms: Utilities (left-justify), IO (all
our file IO are in a subroutines), Conversion (Text-to-CSV), Date validation
and editing, etc. Hundreds.

You'll only need one more parameter: the name of the file.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobfattr in Power Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T17:38:59+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bakc64$c1a$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2900664.1053432122@dbforums.com> <b5b8d7c7.0305210324.4a27cd6f@posting.google.com> <2911281.1053614966@dbforums.com> <awSdneOKLd8e3lCjXTWJkA@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:

> You could write your own. Try something like this:
> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>     MOVE 0 TO NUMREC-1.

You are check the file status result from the CLOSE, I suspect that you 
should be checking after the READ.

> MOVE NUMREC-1 TO FILE-REC-1

You are accessing the record area after the file has been closed.  This is 
OK with some systems, such as MF.  It is not guaranteed that such action 
will work.  You should be doing the CLOSE later.

> It looks like, from scanning the file header, Fujitsu has three locations
> for number of records (these two at bytes 51 and 57 and another starting
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> may be redundant).

It is possible that one is the number of records including deleted records.
```

<a id="t-ae68449e7d"></a>
## Thread: Allocate statement — 7 msgs

### Allocate statement

- **From:** Rosa Fischer <Rosa.Fischer@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T15:33:39+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ECA2EB3.2BF57FB5@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
I'm working for a computer comany in Germany; today I found in the new
standard
"1) Arithmetic-expression-1 specifies a number of bytes of storage to be
allocated. If arithmetic-expression-1 does not evaluate to an integer,
the result is rounded up to the next whole number."
I'm not shure what's the meaning of it: when I write

allocate 80.01 characters returning pointerpp

do I get 81 characters?

The next question is:

01 recordnew based.
     02 par1 pic x(80).
     02 par2 pic xx.

set address of recordnew to pointerpp.
move "ab" to par2

when the allocate statement delivers 81 characters to last character of
newrecord is not assigned to this program. An exception will be raised
only if the implementor assigns not more than 81 characters. If the
implementor delivers space in multiples of doublewords, no error would
oocur. Must there be an exception raised in any case?

Thanks for your help in advance
Sincerely Rosa
```

#### ↳ Re: Allocate statement

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T17:07:06+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dfdkcvslqaoj3nd8crc26eljlnu4aqebjn@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3ECA2EB3.2BF57FB5@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
On Tue, 20 May 2003 15:33:39 +0200 Rosa Fischer
<Rosa.Fischer@fujitsu-siemens.com> wrote:

:>I'm working for a computer comany in Germany; today I found in the new
:>standard
:>"1) Arithmetic-expression-1 specifies a number of bytes of storage to be
:>allocated. If arithmetic-expression-1 does not evaluate to an integer,
:>the result is rounded up to the next whole number."
:>I'm not shure what's the meaning of it: when I write

:>allocate 80.01 characters returning pointerpp

:>do I get 81 characters?

I would expect so.

:>The next question is:

:>01 recordnew based.
:>     02 par1 pic x(80).
:>     02 par2 pic xx.

:>set address of recordnew to pointerpp.
:>move "ab" to par2

:>when the allocate statement delivers 81 characters to last character of
:>newrecord is not assigned to this program. An exception will be raised
:>only if the implementor assigns not more than 81 characters. If the
:>implementor delivers space in multiples of doublewords, no error would
:>oocur. Must there be an exception raised in any case?

No error will occur if the storage is allocated heap style on top of previous
storage either.

I would greatly doubt that the standard will require an error should a based
object be used improperly.
```

#### ↳ Re: Allocate statement

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T08:25:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bae2vl$m4m$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ECA2EB3.2BF57FB5@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
Rosa Fischer wrote:

> 01 recordnew based.
>      02 par1 pic x(80).
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> oocur. Must there be an exception raised in any case?

It is unlikely that an exception would be raised as the programmer error 
may not be detectable by the system.  Your example is one of the reasons 
that explicit dynamic allocation of memory should be avoided.  At the very 
least the allocation should be based on 'sizeof recordnew' rather than a 
literal.  Some later changes may add to the record definition without the 
programmer updating the literal, or even knowing that it is needed.

Memory overwrites are one of the hardest bugs to find because they can 
cascade until an obvious result which may be far from where the actual 
defect is.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Allocate statement

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-20T20:43:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bae412$1ad$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3ECA2EB3.2BF57FB5@fujitsu-siemens.com> <bae2vl$m4m$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

On 20-May-2003, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> Memory overwrites are one of the hardest bugs to find because they can
> cascade until an obvious result which may be far from where the actual
> defect is.

Unless you let the compiler do the work for you.   If you get a standard error
every time we try to access outside of a table's boundary, they are easy to
find.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Allocate statement

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T10:03:35+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bae8nc$oh0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ECA2EB3.2BF57FB5@fujitsu-siemens.com> <bae2vl$m4m$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <bae412$1ad$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

>> Memory overwrites are one of the hardest bugs to find because they can
>> cascade until an obvious result which may be far from where the actual
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> easy to find.

Dynamically allocated memory records are not a table accessed using 
subscripts or indexes.  The two mechanisms are distinct.

Your comment is exactly why I would advocate against using explicit dynamic 
allocation (malloc()) of records and for the use of tables.

Of course implicit dynamic allocation, such as is done by OO, is relatively 
safe because the system will keep track of object types and should give an 
error if an attempt is made to access an attribute that is not part of that 
object.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Allocate statement

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Rosa Fischer <Rosa.Fischer@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T06:42:03+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ECB039B.5DFCE9DE@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<3ECA2EB3.2BF57FB5@fujitsu-siemens.com> <bae2vl$m4m$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <bae412$1ad$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bae8nc$oh0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston schrieb:

> Howard Brazee wrote:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> object.

Thank you all for your answers. As a COBOL I agree with you to avoid dynamic
allocation of memory "allocate arithmetic expression characters". But when
implementing new statements in the COBOL compiler I have to know what the
standard makes me to do and what the application developer is expecting.

Have a nice day
Rosa
```

#### ↳ Re: Allocate statement

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T00:18:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<baf26m$3ss$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ECA2EB3.2BF57FB5@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
Yes, 80.01 "round up" to 81, so I would expect 81 "bytes" to be allocated in
your first question.  As far as the 2nd question goes, I would expect
"results are unpredictable" - and I would expect your "run-time" to do
roughly the same thing it would do with your '85 Standard compiler in the
following case:

Identification Division.
  Program-ID.  CALLER.
Data Division.
 Working-Storage Section.
77  10-bytes  Pic X(10).
Procedure Division.
 Main-Para.
       Move all "Z" to 10-Bytes
       Call "CALLEE" using 10-bytes
       Stop Run
         .
End Program CALLER.
Identification Division.
 Program-ID.  CALLEE.
Data Division.
 Linkage Section.
01  Passed-Data.
     05 REALLY-PASSED  Pic X(10).
     05 WHO-KNOWS        PIC X(02).
Procedure Division using Passed-Data.
 Main-Para2.
      Move "AB" to WHO-KNOWS
      Display Passed-Data
      Exit Program
          .
End Program CALLEE.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Survey for Programming Language Trends.

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T14:37:14-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ECD18DA.9020600@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<1b2f70c3.0305200633.7471ee7c@posting.google.com> <3ECA9BBA.9060DF20@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:

 > The survery is flawed.

They usually are.  If I had a dime for every half-assed survey of this
sort I've seen that assumes that every question could only possibly have
one of a handful of answers...

 > So I put 10% on ML (under the probably incorrect assumption that ML
 > stood for "Machine Language").

I haven't bothered looking at the survey, but I'd guess ML refers to the
language Standard ML, or more generally to the whole ML family.  The
"ML" stands for "Meta-Language".  It's a functional language that's
popular in some quarters largely because it's amenable to program
proofs, including automatic proving systems.

SML is a bit like LISP, in that it's a functional language (programs are
typically expressed as functions operating on the results of other
functions, as opposed to the one-instruction-after-another style of
"procedural" languages like COBOL).  However, it has strong
type-checking and strict evaluation, and a very un-LISP-like syntax, so
it's not much like LISP after all.  And pretty much entirely unlike
COBOL, or assembly (unless you've written assembly for some very unusual
processors...).

But since the results of this survey will no doubt be useless anyway, no
harm done.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Using reference modification in Inspect tallying command

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T15:46:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bam1e4$ehk$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<d4075dc9.0305202336.d82bd86@posting.google.com> <8a2d426e.0305211540.3ecaca8b@posting.google.com> <d4075dc9.0305212327.3edef096@posting.google.com> <FL4za.199$Eg1.116814957@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <d4075dc9.0305230221.17ee5ab6@posting.google.com>`

```
Eric,
  From page VI-95 of the '85 Standard, the following syntax rule appears

"(2) Identifier-3, . . . , identifier-n must reference an elementary item
described, implicitly or explicitly, as USAGE IS DISPLAY."

Where identifier-3 is the item in the phrase where you want to use reference
modification.  Now there are "certain" debates about when a reference
modified data item is and is not an "elementary item" - but I assume that
your compiler is assuming that it is not.

I would be interested in knowing which other compilers do and do not accept
a referenced modified field for that operand of INSPECT.
```

…[16 more quoted lines elided]…

I would expect something like this to return, given...

maybe?) within x:y of searchitem?

- **References:** `<d4075dc9.0305202336.d82bd86@posting.google.com> <8a2d426e.0305211540.3ecaca8b@posting.google.com> <d4075dc9.0305212327.3edef096@posting.google.com> <FL4za.199$Eg1.116814957@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <d4075dc9.0305230221.17ee5ab6@posting.google.com> <3ED0C58D.2020802@Knology.net> <d4075dc9.0305260226.5d592e62@posting.google.com>`

```
Eric, 

I hit the same problem on a project years ago using the Ca-Realia 4.2
(16 bit) compiler.   Here is my solution.

001440 01  SEARCH-FIELD.
001450     03  SF-20.
001460      04  SF-19.
001470       05  SF-18.
001480        06  SF-17.
001490         07  SF-16.
001500          08  SF-15.
001510           09  SF-14.
001520            10  SF-13.
001530             11  SF-12.
001540              12  SF-11.
001550               13  SF-10.
001560                14  SF-9.
001570                 15  SF-8.
001580                  16  SF-7.
001590                   17  SF-6.
001600                    18  SF-5.
001610                     19  SF-4.
001620                      20  SF-3.
001630                       21  SF-2.
001640                        22  SF-1.
001650                         23  FILLER PIC X.
001660                        22  FILLER PIC X.
001670                       21  FILLER PIC X.
001680                      20  FILLER PIC X.
001690                     19  FILLER PIC X.
001700                    18  FILLER PIC X.
001710                   17  FILLER PIC X.
001720                  16  FILLER PIC X.
001730                 15  FILLER PIC X.
001740                14  FILLER PIC X.
001750               13  FILLER PIC X.
001760              12  FILLER PIC X.
001770             11  FILLER PIC X.
001780            10  FILLER PIC X.
001790           09  FILLER PIC X.
001800          08  FILLER PIC X.
001810         07  FILLER PIC X.
001820        06  FILLER PIC X.
001830       05  FILLER PIC X.
001840      04  FILLER PIC X.

000670 01  SFF-1 PIC X(1).
000680 01  SFF-2 PIC X(2).
000690 01  SFF-3 PIC X(3).
000700 01  SFF-4 PIC X(4).
000710 01  SFF-5 PIC X(5).
000720 01  SFF-6 PIC X(6).
000730 01  SFF-7 PIC X(7).
000740 01  SFF-8 PIC X(8).
000750 01  SFF-9 PIC X(9).
000760 01  SFF-10 PIC X(10).
000770 01  SFF-11 PIC X(11).
000780 01  SFF-12 PIC X(12).
000790 01  SFF-13 PIC X(13).
000800 01  SFF-14 PIC X(14).
000810 01  SFF-15 PIC X(15).
000820 01  SFF-16 PIC X(16).
000830 01  SFF-17 PIC X(17).
000840 01  SFF-18 PIC X(18).
000850 01  SFF-19 PIC X(19).
000860 01  SFF-20 PIC X(20).


Figure out the length being searched and use it to decide which
inspect to use.
(Clear "Tally" first)

004500           EVALUATE S-LENG
004510             WHEN 1
004520                MOVE SF-1 TO SFF-1
004530                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-1
004540             WHEN 2
004550                MOVE SF-2 TO SFF-2
004560                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-2
004570             WHEN 3
004580                MOVE SF-3 TO SFF-3
004590                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-3
004600             WHEN 4
004610                MOVE SF-4 TO SFF-4
004620                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-4
004630             WHEN 5
004640                MOVE SF-5 TO SFF-5
004650                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-5
004660             WHEN 6
004670                MOVE SF-6 TO SFF-6
004680                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-6
004690             WHEN 7
004700                MOVE SF-7 TO SFF-7
004710                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-7
004720             WHEN 8
004730                MOVE SF-8 TO SFF-8
004740                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-8
004750             WHEN 9
004760                MOVE SF-9 TO SFF-9
004770                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-9
004780             WHEN 10
004790                MOVE SF-10 TO SFF-10
004800                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-10
004810             WHEN 11
004820                MOVE SF-11 TO SFF-11
004830                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-11
004840             WHEN 12
004850                MOVE SF-12 TO SFF-12
004860                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-12
004870             WHEN 13
004880                MOVE SF-13 TO SFF-13
004890                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-13
004900             WHEN 14
004910                MOVE SF-14 TO SFF-14
004920                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-14
004930             WHEN 15
004940                MOVE SF-15 TO SFF-15
004950                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-15
004960             WHEN 16
004970                MOVE SF-16 TO SFF-16
004980                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-16
004990             WHEN 17
005000                MOVE SF-17 TO SFF-17
005010                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-17
005020             WHEN 18
005030                MOVE SF-18 TO SFF-18
005040                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-18
005050             WHEN 19
005060                MOVE SF-19 TO SFF-19
005070                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-19
005080             WHEN 20
005090                MOVE SF-20 TO SFF-20
005100                INSPECT W-KEY TALLYING TALLY FOR ALL SFF-20
005110           END-EVALUATE

- **References:** `<d4075dc9.0305202336.d82bd86@posting.google.com> <8a2d426e.0305211540.3ecaca8b@posting.google.com> <d4075dc9.0305212327.3edef096@posting.google.com> <FL4za.199$Eg1.116814957@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <d4075dc9.0305230221.17ee5ab6@posting.google.com> <3ED0C58D.2020802@Knology.net> <d4075dc9.0305260226.5d592e62@posting.google.com>`

```
Eric wrote:
>>>I tried something like
>>>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> I just can't imagine it is not possible..

That is really strange.  Of course, it's a little more code, but you can 
make a "variable-length" item...  (Replace [Max-Nbr] below with the 
maximum size your length of the search item could be)

=-=-=-=-=

01  WS-SearchItem.
     03  WS-SearchItem-Tbl  Occurs 1 to [Max-Nbr] Times
                              Depending on y  Pic X(01).
...
Move searchitem (x:y) to WS-SearchItem

Inspect text Tallying idx For All WS-SearchItem

=-=-=-=-=

Maybe that would work...  The occurs...depending on actually makes 
WS-SearchItem a variable-length field.  Since you've already got y 
defined, it'll expand or shrink based on y, and you can move the text to 
it, and use it without reference modification in the inspect statement.

Let me know if this works...  You've got my curiosity up now.  :)
```

```
Thanx for all the solutions
The problem was that I could not use reference modification in a
inspect statement or a FUNCTION in ANS cobol AS400
I changed my object type to CBLLE and now stuff starts to work...
This is my code :

MOVE 1 TO X                                    
MOVE 9 TO Y.                                   
PERFORM UNTIL SEARCHITEM(Y:1) NOT = SPACE      
        SUBTRACT 1 FROM Y                      
END-PERFORM.                                   
                                               
PERFORM VARYING Z FROM 1 BY 1                  
      UNTIL Z > (FUNCTION LENGTH(WS-TEXT)- Y)  
    IF WS-TEXT(Z:Y) = SEARCHITEM(X:Y)          
       ADD 1 TO IDX                            
    END-IF                                     
END-PERFORM.                                   
                                               

Regards

Eric
```

Hmmm.

MOVE 0 TO idx
PERFORM VARYING z FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL z > (FUNCTION LENG(text) - y)
    IF text(z:y) = searchitem(x:y) ADD 1 TO idx END-IF
END-PERFORM.
```

#### ↳ Re: Using reference modification in Inspect tallying command

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T19:04:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed2650f.309260296@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<d4075dc9.0305202336.d82bd86@posting.google.com>`

```
evanstaalduinen@chello.nl (Eric) wrote:

>Hi !
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>searchitem(x:y).

Here's my solution:

01  search-key.
     05   key-byte occurs 1 to 99 depending on y pic x.

move searchitem(x:y) to search-key
move zero to idx
inspect text tallying idx for characters before initial search-key

- for higher speed -

move searchitem(x:y) to search-key
move 1 to idx
set x-text to 1
perform until idx > 999 or text(idx:y) = search-key
    search text-byte 
        when text-byte (x-text) = key-byte (1) set idx to x-text
    end-search
end-perform
subtract 1 from idx
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Using reference modification in Inspect tallying command

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T19:44:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0305261844.5af78aae@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d4075dc9.0305202336.d82bd86@posting.google.com> <3ed2650f.309260296@news.optonline.net>`

```
Since we're swapping recipes, here's mine (the microwave version).

move +1 to idx
perform until  idx > (length of text - y) or item-found
   if text(idx:y) = searchitem(x:y)
      set item-found to true
   else
      add +1 to idx
   end-if
end-perform

if item-found
   display 'Item found at position ' idx ' of text'
else
   display 'Item not found in text' 
end-if

BTW Eric, 

What do you mean by "doesn't" work? I assume it compiles OK, but the
execution isn't to your liking.

What are you getting? Garbage data? Data you don't expect? An abend? A
loop? ?? If you told us, it may provide a clue.

Regards, Jack

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

Thanks - I did...  :)  Which was nice, because the last trip we had to

And, to beat all, not only did I have fun after work on the beach, I

well-spent on that TDY.  (Although, as King, you probably don't actually 
pay taxes...)

Now, can I go on a rant about how, using 36-bit words, Pic 9(02) Binary 
and Pic 1(09) will both hold the value 102, but not in the Value clause? 
  (No, that wasn't one of the problems, but it was a bonus find...)  :) 
  And, by the same token, Pic 9(05) Binary and Pic 1(18) both describe 
the same size (half-word), but only one of these definitions will take 
the number 100001 nicely...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - See you Saturday

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T07:54:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bavu7r$vqs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3ECB5743.9000209@Knology.net> <bag3oq$p3q$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ED025D3.1010504@Knology.net>`

```

"LX-i" <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message
news:3ED025D3.1010504@Knology.net...

>   And, by the same token, Pic 9(05) Binary and Pic 1(18) both describe
> the same size (half-word), but only one of these definitions will take
> the number 100001 nicely...

That's not a surprise, presuming a COBOL compiler compliant with ANSI
X3.23-1985 or later.  USAGE BINARY is a standards-specified USAGE, and the
implementation is obligated to provide code to apply the odometer effect.

That's one of the areas the MCP/AS world had to deal with in migration from
COBOL74 to COBOL85, as that system's '74 implementation had USAGE BINARY as
a Unisys extension (and a very commonly used one, at that), and the odometer
effect *didn't* apply there (well, unless the item so declared was an
elementary 01-level item, as distinct from a 77-level item or an item within
a record), whereas it is required to apply unconditionally in '85.

We added the Unisys extension syntax USAGE BINARY EXTENDED and USAGE BINARY
TRUNCATED to both compilers to allow programmers the ability to specify
exactly what they wanted in a manner that would be applicable to either
dialect.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - See you Saturday

- **From:** "Johan den Boer" <me@knoware.nl>

<a id="t-82c78ca8e3"></a>
## Thread: NOMINAL KEY clause — 4 msgs

### NOMINAL KEY clause

- **From:** csdavis@csdbiz.com (Chuck Davis)
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T07:15:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a900f7ff.0305210615.6c52a62b@posting.google.com>`

```
We are converting a bunch of COBOL-74 programs to COBOL for MVS (COBOL
II). Many of these programs read a QSAM file "randomly" using a
relative record number using the NOMINAL key clause. Of course, the
ideal (and possibly only) technique is to move the data to VSAM ESDS
files using relative record number (RRN) keys. Is it possible in the
latest versions of COBOL to still read QSAM fixed-length records by
relative record number without converting the files to VSAM ESDS?
```

#### ↳ Re: NOMINAL KEY clause

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T11:17:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bag8qc$gpu$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<a900f7ff.0305210615.6c52a62b@posting.google.com>`

```
I thought that "Nominal key" only worked with ISAM files - not QSAM files.
Are you CERTAIN that it is working with QSAM?

See a discussion of ISAM migration issues at:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3mg10/4.1.4.1.
1

P.S. ACTUAL key was used for BDAM - if you happen to have any of those.
That is also discussed in the Migration Guide.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NOMINAL KEY clause

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T16:03:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0305211503.7dfc6d20@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a900f7ff.0305210615.6c52a62b@posting.google.com> <bag8qc$gpu$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
From what I can garner from SG28-6483-2 "IBM OS/VS COBOL Compiler and
Library Programmer's Guide", NOMINAL KEY is used for "Relative Files",
in addition to ISAM files.

Relative files are/were accessed sequentially (BSAM) and randomly
(BDAM) using NOMINAL KEY.

A more recent, but not current, manual that I have mentions "relative"
VSAM files being accessed randomly in a similar way using "RELATIVE
KEY". Makes me wonder if you might be able to access those files you
have using VSAM "RELATIVE KEY". It's worth a shot.

Regards, Jack.

> Are you CERTAIN that it is working with QSAM?
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…

csdavis@csdbiz.com (Chuck Davis) wrote:

>We are converting a bunch of COBOL-74 programs to COBOL for MVS (COBOL
>II). Many of these programs read a QSAM file "randomly" using a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

I think so. It used to work and IBM tends not to delete anything, much less a
file acces method.

I do it often with non-IBM compilers, not to read records but rather 512 byte
sector images (so I can parse records myself). The compiler thinks they're 512
byte fixed length records.

<a id="t-bfc910dc2b"></a>
## Thread: Simple EVALUATE question — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Simple EVALUATE question

- **From:** studlow1@hotmail.com (The Mean Farmer)
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T12:36:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e0465864.0305211136.35c6530e@posting.google.com>`

```
When inside an EVALUATE statement is the object interrogated for every
WHEN condition inside of the EVALUATE - even if it has already found
the condition.

Sounds crazy but here is the goal.

EVALUATE WS-MESSAGE-INDEX(MSG-INDX)            
   WHEN 001                                    
        MOVE HOLD-1-KOUNT    TO DLH-1-KOUNT    
        ADD +1               TO MSG-INDX       
   WHEN 002                                    
        MOVE HOLD-2-KOUNT    TO DLH-2-KOUNT    
        ADD +1               TO MSG-INDX       
   WHEN 003                                    
        MOVE HOLD-3-KOUNT    TO DLH-2-KOUNT    
        ADD +1               TO MSG-INDX       
.
.
. 
END-EVALUATE

If I enter the statement with WS-MESSAGE-INDEX(MSG-INDX) = to 001 I
enter the first WHEN condition, move the KOUNT, and incrment the
Subscript by +1.  Now the value of WS-MESSAGE-INDEX(MSG-INDX) = 002.

Will the WHEN 002 condition be executed, or will it just go to the
END-EVALUATE statement??

If not I will just put a PERFORM UNTIL around the EVALUATE statement
and repeat the code over, and over, and over and over ....

Thanks!!!
```

#### ↳ Re: Simple EVALUATE question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T14:46:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bagl32$962$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0305211136.35c6530e@posting.google.com>`

```
Good news, bad news, and medium news.

Good news - With the 2002 COBOL Standard, things are "well defined" and
probably work the way you want. See Page 818 where it states (in part) as a
SUBSTANTIVE change from the '85 Standard,

"25) EVALUATE statement, sequence of execution. The sequence of evaluation
of selection subjects and objects is now defined to be from left to right
and selection objects are evaluated as each WHEN phrase is processed. When a
WHEN phrase is selected, no more selection objects are evaluated."

Bad News - this is a substantive change from the '85 Standard because it did
NOT define EVALUATE this way, so a "conforming" compiler WAS supposed to
evaluate ALL expressions (even if an "earlier one" was true).

Medium News - I *beleive* (but am not positive) that several '85 Standard
compilers actually DO work the "new" way not the way that was "strictly
required" by the '85 Standard.  You probably need to "test" your specific
compiler and code to see how they handle this and NOT relie on it when
porting to other compilers.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Simple EVALUATE question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T15:22:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bagn6i$src$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0305211136.35c6530e@posting.google.com> <bagl32$962$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Although "true" my response was NOT responsive. After reading the other
responses, I realize that you *want* to execute multiple WHEN phrases
"sequentially".  As others have indicated, that doesn't happen (and won't
with the 2002 Standard).

Either your solution of putting this in a "loop" or changing to consecutive
IF (or even EVALUATE) statements will do what you want. Depending on the
rest of your application logic, you may also want to consider changing your
data items

 HOLD-1-KOUNT, HOLD-2-KOUNT, etc

to a table and use a loop with an index and not have either an EVALUATE or
an IF.
```

#### ↳ Re: Simple EVALUATE question

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T12:56:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<baglll$2i0t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0305211136.35c6530e@posting.google.com>`

```
Just so we are using the same terms:  In your example, WS-MESSAGE-INDEX
(MSG-INDX) is a "selection subject".  "001" is a "selection object" in
standards terms.

Selection subjects are evaluated once at the beginning of execution of the
EVALUATE statement.

The WHEN phrases are applied, right to left (or top to bottom, if you
prefer) against the values produced by the selection subject, until such
time as a WHEN phrase is satisfied, at which point no more WHEN phrases are
analyzed.

In your example, I would expect execution to proceed to the END-EVALUATE
statement once the MOVE and ADD associated with WHEN 001 are finished.

    -Chuck Stevens

"The Mean Farmer" <studlow1@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:e0465864.0305211136.35c6530e@posting.google.com...
> When inside an EVALUATE statement is the object interrogated for every
> WHEN condition inside of the EVALUATE - even if it has already found
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks!!!
```

#### ↳ Re: Simple EVALUATE question

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-21T22:58:53+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<semncvkle9teje82jb9268hq03apkm3fcq@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0305211136.35c6530e@posting.google.com>`

```
On 21 May 2003 12:36:32 -0700 studlow1@hotmail.com (The Mean Farmer) wrote:

:>When inside an EVALUATE statement is the object interrogated for every
:>WHEN condition inside of the EVALUATE - even if it has already found
:>the condition.

:>Sounds crazy but here is the goal.

:>EVALUATE WS-MESSAGE-INDEX(MSG-INDX)            
:>   WHEN 001                                    
:>        MOVE HOLD-1-KOUNT    TO DLH-1-KOUNT    
:>        ADD +1               TO MSG-INDX       
:>   WHEN 002                                    
:>        MOVE HOLD-2-KOUNT    TO DLH-2-KOUNT    
:>        ADD +1               TO MSG-INDX       
:>   WHEN 003                                    
:>        MOVE HOLD-3-KOUNT    TO DLH-2-KOUNT    
:>        ADD +1               TO MSG-INDX       
:>.
:>.
:>. 
:>END-EVALUATE

:>If I enter the statement with WS-MESSAGE-INDEX(MSG-INDX) = to 001 I
:>enter the first WHEN condition, move the KOUNT, and incrment the
:>Subscript by +1.  Now the value of WS-MESSAGE-INDEX(MSG-INDX) = 002.

:>Will the WHEN 002 condition be executed, or will it just go to the
:>END-EVALUATE statement??

"The EVALUATE statement provides a shorthand notation for a series of nested
IF statements."

Thus at most one WHEN clause will be executed.

:>If not I will just put a PERFORM UNTIL around the EVALUATE statement
:>and repeat the code over, and over, and over and over ....

You appear to be using the wrong tool. 

You should:

  IF EVALUATE WS-MESSAGE-INDEX(MSG-INDX) = 001
        MOVE HOLD-1-KOUNT    TO DLH-1-KOUNT    
        ADD +1               TO MSG-INDX       
  END-IF 
  IF EVALUATE WS-MESSAGE-INDEX(MSG-INDX) = 002	
        MOVE HOLD-2-KOUNT    TO DLH-2-KOUNT    
        ADD +1               TO MSG-INDX   
  END-IF 
  IF EVALUATE WS-MESSAGE-INDEX(MSG-INDX) = 003
        MOVE HOLD-3-KOUNT    TO DLH-2-KOUNT    
        ADD +1               TO MSG-INDX       
  END-IF 
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Simple EVALUATE question

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T11:46:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eccb1b6.253564601@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0305211136.35c6530e@posting.google.com> <semncvkle9teje82jb9268hq03apkm3fcq@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

>On 21 May 2003 12:36:32 -0700 studlow1@hotmail.com (The Mean Farmer) wrote:
>
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>  END-IF 

Delete the word EVALUATE in the above. It's not valid COBOL. 

Better yet, put it in a loop:

perform varying k from 1 by 1 until k > [max] or MSG-INDX
    if  WS-MESSAGE-INDEX(MSG-INDX) = k
        move HOLD-k-KOUNT (k) to DLH-k-KOUNT (k)
        add 1 to MSG-INDX
    end-if
end-perform
```

#### ↳ Re: Simple EVALUATE question

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T11:46:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eccb157.253470306@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0305211136.35c6530e@posting.google.com>`

```
studlow1@hotmail.com (The Mean Farmer) wrote:

>When inside an EVALUATE statement is the object interrogated for every
>WHEN condition inside of the EVALUATE - even if it has already found
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>and repeat the code over, and over, and over and over ....

COBOL goes to the end-evaluate after the first hit. You want to build your
'state machine' in the C language, where a case statement continues 'evaluating'
after a hit .. unless you say 'break'. Many many bugs have be created by
forgetting to write 'break'.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Simple EVALUATE question

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T10:52:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vcpp3bfce0sv7e@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0305211136.35c6530e@posting.google.com> <3eccb157.253470306@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eccb157.253470306@news.optonline.net...
> studlow1@hotmail.com (The Mean Farmer) wrote:
[snip]
> >
> >If not I will just put a PERFORM UNTIL around the EVALUATE statement
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'state machine' in the C language, where a case statement continues
'evaluating'
> after a hit .. unless you say 'break'. Many many bugs have be created by
> forgetting to write 'break'.

One might prefer to use COBOL to do the equivalent of C,
two methods come to mind.

I have used the second method to improve program
performance. These were the increases of 80% and
900% I had previously mentioned in the group.

Perform paragraphs only style.

W-SWITCH.
  EVALUATE WS-MESSAGE-INDEX(MSG-INDX)
     WHEN 001
          PERFORM W-001
     WHEN 002
          PERFORM W-002
     WHEN 003
          PERFORM W-003


   END-EVALUATE.

W-001.
     MOVE HOLD-1-KOUNT    TO DLH-1-KOUNT
     PERFORM W-002.
W-002.
     MOVE HOLD-2-KOUNT    TO DLH-2-KOUNT
     PERFORM W-003.
W-003.
     MOVE HOLD-3-KOUNT    TO DLH-3-KOUNT
     PERFORM W-004.

Peform sections style.

W SECTION.
W-SWITCH.
  GO TO W-001 W-002 W-003 ...
    DEPENDING ON MSG-INDX
  GO TO W-EXIT.
W-001.
     MOVE HOLD-1-KOUNT    TO DLH-1-KOUNT.
W-002.
     MOVE HOLD-2-KOUNT    TO DLH-2-KOUNT.
W-003.
     MOVE HOLD-3-KOUNT    TO DLH-3-KOUNT.


W-EXIT.
  EXIT.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Simple EVALUATE question

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T07:23:32+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<baj844$skb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0305211136.35c6530e@posting.google.com> <3eccb157.253470306@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> COBOL goes to the end-evaluate after the first hit. 

Correct.

> You want to build your
> 'state machine' in the C language, where a case statement continues
> 'evaluating' after a hit .. unless you say 'break'. 

No, that is wrong.  That is not how the C case (switch) statement works. It 
does not 'continue evaluating', it does not 'evaluate' each case after a 
hit.

In a C switch the case labels are just numeric labels.  The switch is, in 
effect, a go to depending on.  After the case label has been selected the 
code will continue to be executed until the end of the block or a break 
statement.

The presence or absence of other case labels is irrelevant to this 'drop 
through', they are not 'evaluated'.


> Many many bugs have be
> created by forgetting to write 'break'.

OTOH actions can be combined by ordering the case labels and having the 
drop throughs in a useful way.

Cobol allows several empty WHENs to use one set of actions.  One has to be 
careful that if no action is required on a particular case a CONTINUE is in 
place otherwise the next WHEN's actions are done.
```

#### ↳ Re: Simple EVALUATE question

- **From:** weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous)
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T14:37:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<balbn4$aev$4@news.utelfla.com>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0305211136.35c6530e@posting.google.com>`

```
The Mean Farmer <studlow1@hotmail.com> wrote:

: When inside an EVALUATE statement is the object interrogated for every
: WHEN condition inside of the EVALUATE - even if it has already found
: the condition.

EVALUATE stops at the first condition which evaluates as TRUE.


: Sounds crazy but here is the goal.

: EVALUATE WS-MESSAGE-INDEX(MSG-INDX)            
:    WHEN 001                                    
:         MOVE HOLD-1-KOUNT    TO DLH-1-KOUNT    
:         ADD +1               TO MSG-INDX       
:    WHEN 002                                    
:         MOVE HOLD-2-KOUNT    TO DLH-2-KOUNT    
:         ADD +1               TO MSG-INDX       
:    WHEN 003                                    
:         MOVE HOLD-3-KOUNT    TO DLH-2-KOUNT    
:         ADD +1               TO MSG-INDX       
: .
: .
: . 
: END-EVALUATE

: If I enter the statement with WS-MESSAGE-INDEX(MSG-INDX) = to 001 I
: enter the first WHEN condition, move the KOUNT, and incrment the
: Subscript by +1.  Now the value of WS-MESSAGE-INDEX(MSG-INDX) = 002.

: Will the WHEN 002 condition be executed, or will it just go to the
: END-EVALUATE statement??

: If not I will just put a PERFORM UNTIL around the EVALUATE statement
: and repeat the code over, and over, and over and over ....

Remove the EVALUATE statement and replace it with a PERFORM loop.
```

<a id="t-aeff71e2be"></a>
## Thread: Symbols vs Words — 67 this month / 71 total

### Symbols vs Words

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T15:10:47-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
A new thread - starting with a single question:

Mr. Wagner,
   Do you accept that the use of symbols as opposed to words to represent
the relational operators is a PREFERENCE in STYLE that you have and that
your reasons may or may NOT be overwhelming (deciding?) to others?

Comment:
  I fully support your use of words rather than symbols as a STYLE that best
meets your programming needs and approaches.  I do *not* agree that you have
provided any reasons that are sufficiently "telling" that they would change
when I use symbols versus words in MY PREFERRED STYLE.

If all of your posts are simply trying to explain why your style works for
you, that's fine.  If you think that you have reasons that should impact
other peoples preferred style, then you certainly have not succeeded so far
and others have explained why your reasons are not convincing to them.
Unless you have NEW arguments, then you can/should (IMHO) simply accept that
there is a difference in perceived advantages and disadvantages of each
style.
```

#### ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-05-22T21:16:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0305222016.4b42e1a8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill - I didn't read the original thread - being tied up with some
personal matters and not having time to dwell here - but I worked on
many systems where the IBM printer in use did not have > < = etc or
where = was getting worn out too frequently because of it's
infrequency on the print chain.  Our standard was to use WORDS and not
symbols - because if you used the symbol you either got a print check
or a blank space on the listing.  Not a style, not a preference - a
necessity.

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>...
> A new thread - starting with a single question:
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> style.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T00:45:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bakcij$or6$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <bfdfc3e8.0305222016.4b42e1a8@posting.google.com>`

```
As stated in the original thread, that certainly WAS an issue in the '60s,
'70s and possibly even into the '80s.  However,  no one (I think) has
reported that this is STILL an issue where they print compiler listings (if
they do that at all).  FYI, I believe (but won't swear to it) that MOST of
the printer chains that didn't support ">" or "<" also didn't support
lower-case letters, so programmers who don't use one (for historical
reasons - or current print-chain reasons) wouldn't use the other either.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

>I've worked on code on this system where this same printer with the
>same chain (it's a band actually) still exists -- within the last 30
>days.

In the Golden Age of Computers, IBM keypunch machines (models 024 and 026) had
no key for < and >. One had to hold Multi-punch and type three numbers, which
was slower than typing GREATER.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T01:37:00+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0305222016.4b42e1a8@posting.google.com...
> Bill - I didn't read the original thread - being tied up with some
> personal matters and not having time to dwell here - but I worked on
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> necessity.

I have had exactly the same experience, Thane. And the shops concerned used
words in relational conditions.

We didn't like it, but, as you pointed out, there was little choice.

It was thirty years ago.

In those days an IBM 1403 printer cost over $150,000. The cost and
maintenance to replace print chains was worth considering.

Now you would use a high speed laser printer for around $10,000.

In this day and age when source code is maintained on display screens (or
should be) it is a very weak argument.

I agree with Bill that the choice of using words or symbols is purely a
matter of style.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T12:16:05+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bap1us$fvu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ecfe892.146295370@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> The corollary is: they haven't presented ANY argumentation in support of
> their preference except 'I find it easier to read.' 

There is actually no _need_ to support our preferences.  We are not trying 
to convince you that you should follow what we do.  It is only necessary 
for it to work and to be 'easier'.  This, of course, includes it being 
easier for the next programmer, including having him being able to keep it 
working.

You may well have made several arguments, such as 'linguistics', such as 
what the Goths did, such as 'consistency' and the difficulty of 'switching 
modes'.

But all these arguments that you made were irrelevant, inconsitent, 
superficial, or simply wrong.

While I am sure that you feel that your arguments were deep and meaningful, 
relevant and cogent, otherwise you wouldn't have made them, mostly they 
were semi-uninformed nonsense attempting to parade as pseudo-intellectual.

In any case you have demonstrated many times before that you ignore, 
discount and dismiss any arguments that don't agree with you.

As here you claim 'they haven't presented ANY .. in support'.

When of course they have been presenting them, you are too ignorant (as in 
ignore) to have noticed.

Once again I say: You will never realise why people tell you to just fuck 
off.  You continually claim that your arguments are perfect and no one 
else's count (or exist) because you are so self-obsessed that you never 
notice what others say.


> One can say that about anything, even ALTER.

To someone who has been trained to work in particular ways using, say, a 
computer of the 1950s, then ALTER may be an obvious and efficient way to 
utilise the rather limited resources and instruction set available.

ALTER was, in fact, a vast improvemnt over what had to be done when 
programming using mercury delay tubes and would have seemed a major step in 
program design.

Of course we are no longer, not since the early 60s, restricted by such 
concerns and could use programming techniques which were less efficient for 
the machine and more efficient for the programmer.  The change taking place 
because of the falling cost of computing power relative to the cost of 
programmers.
```

school?

>In any case you have demonstrated many times before that you ignore,

'I like it' is not an argument, it's an emotional feeling. 'You're stupid' is
not rebuttal.

>> One can say that about anything, even ALTER.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>program design.

I worked on those machines, so I feel qualified to say speed was not why ALTER
was invented. Some people just liked the novelty of self-modifying code. It
seemed like a Good Idea at the time.

PECD bragged here about programs which 'wrote themselves' in memory. The
motivation was not speed, it was cleverness. 

>Of course we are no longer, not since the early 60s, restricted by such 
>concerns and could use programming techniques which were less efficient for 
>the machine and more efficient for the programmer.  The change taking place 
>because of the falling cost of computing power relative to the cost of

As Sherlock Holmes said to Dr. Watson: "You have a keen mind for the obvious."

- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ecfe892.146295370@news.optonline.net> <bap1us$fvu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed10d6b.221275015@news.optonline.net> <bas00e$lj9$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>If you (Mr. Wagner) actually think that you have presented any REASON that
>applies to the current COBOL language (as it exists today) that is NOT a
>matter of style, could you please restate it - as a simple statement that is
>"independent" of linguistics, history of language, what MIGHT have been
>intended in the past - but that applies to COBOL as it exists today?

Glad to. COBOL's logical notation is asymmetrical. It allows symbols for
relational operators while requiring words for logical operators. That is
equivalent to a math notation providing a symbol for subtraction (=) while
requiring words for addition (OR) and multiplication (AND).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T04:15:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<basm0i$i3a$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ecfe892.146295370@news.optonline.net> <bap1us$fvu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed10d6b.221275015@news.optonline.net> <bas00e$lj9$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed1976d.256610469@news.optonline.net>`

```
If you would like a "full set" of BOTH words and symbols for BOTH logical
operators and relational operators, that would be a "reasonable" enhancement
request to submit to J4 or WG4.  (I don't know how they would react - as
most programmers that I know of are "perfectly happy" with the current
situation).  Certainly, I know that there are programming languages with a
"symbol" for the logical "NOT"  "AND" and "OR" - so providing such to COBOL
wouldn't be totally awful.  Similarly, adding a word for the concatenation
operator "&" would also be something that might be considered.

Providing a "complete set of options" (words & symbols)  is not something
that would be "dismissed" and I don't think that many in CLC would object to
it being CONSIDERED.  All that I perceive as being "rejected" here is a
claim that either the style of using symbols or the style of using words IN
THOSE CASES WHERE BOTH ARE AVAILABLE is anything other than a "style"
choice.
```

>If you would like a "full set" of BOTH words and symbols for BOTH logical
>operators and relational operators, that would be a "reasonable" enhancement
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

The ampersand is taken but the math-like carat (^) seems to be available for
AND. The pipe symbol and bang (exclamation) appear to be available for OR and
NOT.

I think I will make that suggestion. To whom should I send it?

>Similarly, adding a word for the concatenation
>operator "&" would also be something that might be considered.

I'm not concerned about that.

>Providing a "complete set of options" (words & symbols)  is not something
>that would be "dismissed" and I don't think that many in CLC would object to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>choice.

What you are saying is don't present a argument that may be taken as criticism
-- asymmetry, flawed notational system. Just say 'I like it' or 'I find it
easier to read'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T07:43:29+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<batqob$g1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed1976d.256610469@news.optonline.net> <basm0i$i3a$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed25d72.307311122@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> What you are saying is don't present a argument that may be taken as
> criticism -- asymmetry, flawed notational system. Just say 'I like it' or
> 'I find it easier to read'.

A set of operators that is created from single character symbols, multi 
character symbols and multi charactaer alpha is only 'flawed' in the sense 
that an infinite number of symbols are not available to use.

While the '|', '^' and '!' may well be available on US English IBM PC type 
systems using the default ASCII character set, this does not mean that all 
systems running Cobol, especially foreign language ones, have those, or 
indeed any other specific character, available to them.

In many cases foreign keyboards have symbols that you do not have such as 
pound (sterling), upside down question mark, euro, and so on.

(note: I find your arguments to often be superficial because you don't 
appear to consider these issues, only seeing what your keyboard looks like, 
for example - Cobol has a wider audience).


If a combination of short words and symbols is to be considered 'flawed' 
then just about _every_ computer language and maths language is so flawed.

To most of us it seems, we prefer to have a combination of these items.  An 
extended, entirely symbolic set would cause problems with restricted-set 
printers, with foreign language printers and keyboards, and with cultural 
differeces.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>that an infinite number of symbols are not available to use.

Which is why alphabets were invented. Have I seen this movie before?

>While the '|', '^' and '!' may well be available on US English IBM PC type 
>systems using the default ASCII character set, this does not mean that all 
>systems running Cobol, especially foreign language ones, have those, or 
>indeed any other specific character, available to them.

In that case, they might prefer words over cumbersome Unicode .. just as we did
in the '60s. The more things change, the more they stay the same.

>In many cases foreign keyboards have symbols that you do not have such as 
>pound (sterling), upside down question mark, euro, and so on.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>for example - Cobol has a wider audience).

You said symbols are the norm in other programming languages such as C, Python,
etc. How do foreign programmers write those languages? I used the SAME symbols
(Basic uses carat for exponentiation).

On the one hand you argue for symbols because they're universal, then you argue
against them. Pick one or the other, you can't support both sides. 

>If a combination of short words and symbols is to be considered 'flawed' 
>then just about _every_ computer language and maths language is so flawed.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>differeces.

What restricted-set printers? Laser and inkjet printers have soft fonts. Printer
limitations were a problem in the Good Old Days, not now.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T18:31:14+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bav0mv$13j$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed25d72.307311122@news.optonline.net> <batqob$g1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed2990e.322573688@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> On the one hand you argue for symbols because they're universal, 

What I _actually_ said was that '>=' is more readily understood by a 
non-english speaker than 'IS GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO' would be.

> then you argue against them. 

What I actually said is that if you are going to extend the characters used 
as symbols then you should ensure that it is to characters that will be 
available on their screens and keyboards.

> Pick one or the other, you can't support both sides.

There are two different arguments: 

One is about recognisability of characters already in the Cobol set.

The other is about whether extending to characters _not_ in the current 
character set and whether you would check they would be usable beyond US PC 
keyboard and screen.

In order to regognise the new characters you proposed they have to be 
visible on the screen/printer. In order to be used they must be keyable.

Of course given the 2nd argument is satisfied then in the first argument 
they may be more recognisable than the english word - as long as they are 
carefully chosen to be consistent with usage in their language.

Did you know, for example, about C trigraphs and why these are required ? 
You may like to look these up and contemplate whay they exist.

Why do you mix up these two quite distinct arguments ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T08:42:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed31dd7.356571619@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed25d72.307311122@news.optonline.net> <batqob$g1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed2990e.322573688@news.optonline.net> <bav0mv$13j$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>You may like to look these up and contemplate whay they exist.

I looked them up. They are three-byte sequences -- two question marks followed
by a similar-looking symbol -- used to type symbols unavailable on foreign
keyboards but required by 'modern' programming languages such as C. 

Why does that ring a bell? In the Golden Age of Computers, keypunching >
required holding multipunch and typing three numbers -- 0, 2 and another.

After 43 years of progress, we're right back where we started. 

The solution seems simple: add 9 keycodes to foreign language keyboards,
probably with another shift key which would provide 13 spaces in the numeric
row. 

>Why do you mix up these two quite distinct arguments ?

They are not distinct. Both are about advocacy of symbols.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T21:42:53+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bavbub$5qp$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <batqob$g1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed2990e.322573688@news.optonline.net> <bav0mv$13j$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed31dd7.356571619@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>Did you know, for example, about C trigraphs and why these are required ?
>>You may like to look these up and contemplate whay they exist.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> as C.

Not quite, the trigraph exists in the source as 3 characters.  So on 
systems that don't have ^ they use ??`, on systems that don't have | they 
use ??!.  

Now the _reason_ that ^ and | is 'free' in the Cobol set is that these are 
not universally usable, as C discovered and so had to contrive alternatives.
 
> Why does that ring a bell? In the Golden Age of Computers, keypunching >
> required holding multipunch and typing three numbers -- 0, 2 and another.

Not quite the same thing, no.  The multipunch resulted in a single 
character that _was_ available as a card punching and in the character set 
of the machine, but just wasn't on the keyboard of the 026.

The trigraph is 3 characters that are equivalent to a character _not_ in 
the set of the machine.

> After 43 years of progress, we're right back where we started.

Problems are given solutions, gee, do they still do that ?

Try this on a PC: hold down ALT, hit 1 6 6 on keypad, release ALT.  What do 
you get on screen ?

> The solution seems simple: add 9 keycodes to foreign language keyboards,
> probably with another shift key which would provide 13 spaces in the
> numeric row.

No, you really don't get it yet do you.

The screen display doesn't have the character either.  See 'code page' in 
some reference manual.
 
>>Why do you mix up these two quite distinct arguments ?
> 
> They are not distinct. Both are about advocacy of symbols.

Well they may be some overlap, for example '> is more understandable that 
GREATER THAN' to a hungarian and it _is_ in their code set, but if OR was 
represented by | on US code set and this had to use ??! in C because it 
didn't exist on the hungarian code set then using OR may be preferable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T05:27:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bavejb$lcn$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <batqob$g1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed2990e.322573688@news.optonline.net> <bav0mv$13j$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed31dd7.356571619@news.optonline.net> <bavbub$5qp$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
I just HAD to add to this a section from the '85 COBOL Standard about a
"feature" that is required to be supported in that Standard - but was marked
as "OBSOLETE" and was, therefore, removed from the 2003 ISO COBOL Standard.
From page XVII-81,

"(1) Double character substitution (1 NUC). When a character set contains
fewer than 51 characters, double characters must be substituted for the
single characters. This feature has been placed in the obsolete element
category.

Justification:

These specifications are a carry-over from the time when most hardware could
not provide the complete COBOL character set. This limitation on number of
characters available in hardware no longer exists."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T19:52:10+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb0fjt$kke$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <bav0mv$13j$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed31dd7.356571619@news.optonline.net> <bavbub$5qp$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <bavejb$lcn$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> Justification:
> 
> These specifications are a carry-over from the time when most hardware
> could not provide the complete COBOL character set. This limitation on
> number of characters available in hardware no longer exists."

As has been shown here in the last few days such hardware does still exist.

I would suppose that 'the complete Cobol character set' has been carefully 
chosen to, as far as possible, avoid those characters which do not 
universally exist.  The C trigraphs are for {}[]|^!#\~ - characters which 
are not in the Cobol set for (I would suppose) exactly the same reason that 
they are trigraphs.

Which is why I suggested that RW should investigate this further before 
proposing the use of characters which would be unacceptable to an 
international audience.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 13)_

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>use ??!.  

One can't do Unix without a pipe symbol, so shells need trigraphs as well. Is
support in some low-level IO access method?

>Now the _reason_ that ^ and | is 'free' in the Cobol set is that these are 
>not universally usable, as C discovered and so had to contrive alternatives.

Maybe the solution is already there, available for Cobol to use.

>> Why does that ring a bell? In the Golden Age of Computers, keypunching >
>> required holding multipunch and typing three numbers -- 0, 2 and another.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>of the machine, but just wasn't on the keyboard of the 026.

Are you saying foreign countries have changed the low end of the ASCII code set?
I find that hard to believe. I'll bet they used the high end, in which case
carat still exists in the low end. 

>The trigraph is 3 characters that are equivalent to a character _not_ in 
>the set of the machine.

The machine doesn't care; it sees eight bits. But software and IO devices care. 

>Try this on a PC: hold down ALT, hit 1 6 6 on keypad, release ALT.  What do 
>you get on screen ?

Looks like an alpha with an underbar. What's your point? I was writing keyboard
drivers in the early 80s ..

>> The solution seems simple: add 9 keycodes to foreign language keyboards,
>> probably with another shift key which would provide 13 spaces in the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>some reference manual.

.. and I wrote a commercially successful Hercules BIOS. I was a beta tester for
Hercules. (FWIW, some of their adaptors had downloadable fonts.)

We stopped using character-mode screens *at least* by 1995 (Win95). Impact
printers died about the same time. You're describing obsolete technology. 

>>>Why do you mix up these two quite distinct arguments ?
>> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>didn't exist on the hungarian code set then using OR may be preferable.

Right. A close analogy to the US situation in 1960. 

I thought Unicode was supposed to solve these problems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T02:55:38+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb18dt$vuj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <bav0mv$13j$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed31dd7.356571619@news.optonline.net> <bavbub$5qp$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed3f77b.412296085@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>Not quite, the trigraph exists in the source as 3 characters.  So on
>>systems that don't have ^ they use ??`, on systems that don't have | they
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is support in some low-level IO access method?

Do all systems that run Cobol also run Unix ?  I don't think so.
 
>>Now the _reason_ that ^ and | is 'free' in the Cobol set is that these are
>>not universally usable, as C discovered and so had to contrive
>>alternatives.
> 
> Maybe the solution is already there, available for Cobol to use.

Why would Cobol use ??! to stand for | when OR can be used ?
 
> Are you saying foreign countries have changed the low end of the ASCII
> code set? I find that hard to believe. I'll bet they used the high end, in
> which case carat still exists in the low end.

There is no 'high end' of the ASCII character set, it is a 7 bit code.  The 
'high end' is an extended ASCII set that _some_ machines use, usually in 
many varied ways (see the code sets on the IBM PC). 

> The machine doesn't care; it sees eight bits. But software and IO devices
> care.

Not all machines see '8 bits'.
 
> Looks like an alpha with an underbar. What's your point? I was writing
> keyboard drivers in the early 80s ..

Because _that_ is the equivalent of the 026 multipunch and neither have 
anything to do with trigraphs.

> .. and I wrote a commercially successful Hercules BIOS. I was a beta
> tester for Hercules. (FWIW, some of their adaptors had downloadable
> fonts.)

Exactly, and when a foreign font is downloaded this determines what 
characters are viewable on screen, hence the need for trigraphs if the 
character set has replaced the real characters.
 
> We stopped using character-mode screens *at least* by 1995 (Win95). Impact
> printers died about the same time. You're describing obsolete technology.

That may be true in office environments, but not all computers are used by 
accountants and secretaries.

In the warehousing and distribution industries the 'green screen' and dot 
matrix are still used.  Mainly because it is just a tool to do the job and 
playing solitaire for half the day doesn't get the orders out.

These still do text-mode GUI (TUI) but are mostly barcode driven. 'Windows 
for RF terminals' ?  I don't think so.

Appropriate technology is the watch word, not pretty screens.

> I thought Unicode was supposed to solve these problems.

Are your programs unicode compliant and internationalised ?  Have you ever 
thought they should be ?
```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

>>>Now the _reason_ that ^ and | is 'free' in the Cobol set is that these are
>>>not universally usable, as C discovered and so had to contrive

Maybe it's not a Cobol issue. Trigraph support might be in IO methods, thus
available to all compilers, shells and applications. 

>> Are you saying foreign countries have changed the low end of the ASCII
>> code set? I find that hard to believe. I'll bet they used the high end, in

>> The machine doesn't care; it sees eight bits. But software and IO devices
>> care.

Can you cite a 7 bit computer? If the computer doesn't see the 8th bit, that's

>> tester for Hercules. (FWIW, some of their adaptors had downloadable
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>character set has replaced the real characters.

This makes no sense. Trigraphs are an input issue. The computer wouldn't send a
trigraph to a display. 

>> We stopped using character-mode screens *at least* by 1995 (Win95). Impact

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>playing solitaire for half the day doesn't get the orders out.

>for RF terminals' ?  I don't think so.

Symbol's flagship PTC-2000 series has a graphic screen and runs Windows CE.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T12:40:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb5glu$b3q$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <bav0mv$13j$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed31dd7.356571619@news.optonline.net> <bavbub$5qp$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed3f77b.412296085@news.optonline.net> <bb18dt$vuj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d9c4.45232037@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed5d9c4.45232037@news.optonline.net...
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
<snip>
>
> Can you cite a 7 bit computer? If the computer doesn't see the 8th bit,
that's
> because some input device isn't sending it.
>

Historically, (I am not sure how true it is) many  PC's running in Japan use
a facility called "Shift-JIS".  In this system, the high-order bit is used
to "indicated" that the current byte and the next byte are to be treated as
DBCS.  Therefore, a "single byte" *ASCII* character must be 7-bit.  For a
more detailed description, see:

 http://nihongo.kurogane.net/encodingdescs/Shift_JIS.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T13:08:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305291208.6357ad23@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <bav0mv$13j$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed31dd7.356571619@news.optonline.net> <bavbub$5qp$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed3f77b.412296085@news.optonline.net> <bb18dt$vuj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d9c4.45232037@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >Do all systems that run Cobol also run Unix ?  I don't think so.
> 
> Do all foreign computers run Unix? I don't think so either. 

So what has Unix to do with it ?
 
> How to foreign programmers using the Windows scripting language write a pipe
> symbol? 

Is that the only option: Unix or Windows - nothing else exists ?
 
> Maybe it's not a Cobol issue.

It isn't a Cobol issue.  The reason that it isn't a Cobol issue, as I
have already explained, is that the characters that C found required
trigraphs are _not_ in the Cobol charcter set.

They are _deliberately_ not in the character set so that Cobol didn't
wind up with the same problem that C did.

> Trigraph support might be in IO methods, thus
> available to all compilers, shells and applications. 

Wind unsupported speculation gets no praise here.

> All 8 bit computers have a high end, whether they use it or not. 

ASCII is defined as a 7 bit code.  The 8th bit may be used for parity.
Note what ASCII stands for: AMERICAN standard code for INTERCHANGE of
information.

When sent over cables the codes 0-31 are control characters, and the
8th bit is a parity bit.
 
> Can you cite a 7 bit computer? 

No, but I have worked on computers with 6 bit characters.

> If the computer doesn't see the 8th bit, that's
> because some input device isn't sending it.

Or is using it for parity.
 
> >Exactly, and when a foreign font is downloaded this determines what 
> >characters are viewable on screen, hence the need for trigraphs if the 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> send a trigraph to a display. 

No, you really haven't got this yet have you.

The trigraph is in the source code.  On computers that don't have,
say, {}, they are replaced by the user typing ??< and ??>.  These
remain as source code so the program looks like:

main()
??<
      if ( x )
      ??<
          do_something();
      ??>
??>

This is what is typed, this is what is displayed.  It doesn't
'convert' it somehow.  As I had said before it is _NOT_ the same as
'multi-punch' (which on an IBM PC is done using ALT+3_numeric_keys).

In fact if you keyed the above into your C compiler it should accept
it as good source code.
```

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0305291208.6357ad23@posting.google.com...
> robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
[snip]
> > Maybe it's not a Cobol issue.
>
> It isn't a Cobol issue.  The reason that it isn't a Cobol issue, as I
> have already explained, is that the characters that C found required
> trigraphs are _not_ in the Cobol charcter set.

In case anyone is interested, a working draft of the
current C standard says, "The trigraph sequences enable
the input of characters that are not defined in the Invariant
Code Set as described in ISO/IEC 646:1991, which is a
subset of the seven-bit ASCII code set."
```
