riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>So what has Unix to do with it ?

It's the corollary to your (snipped) argument. I speculated trigraph support
might already be available to Cobol via the file system. You dismissed it by
saying Cobol runs on platforms other than Unix. In that case, how do foreign
programmers write C on other platforms? 

>> How to foreign programmers using the Windows scripting language write a pipe
>> symbol? 
>
>Is that the only option: Unix or Windows - nothing else exists ?

What operating system did you have in mind? 

>> Maybe it's not a Cobol issue.
>
>It isn't a Cobol issue.  The reason that it isn't a Cobol issue, as I
>have already explained, is that the characters that C found required
>trigraphs are _not_ in the Cobol charcter set.

In the worst case, foreign programmers would have no more nor less than what
they have now. You want to deny the symbols to 80% (pick a number) because 20%
wouldn't be able to use them. 

>They are _deliberately_ not in the character set so that Cobol didn't
>wind up with the same problem that C did.

It's not the same problem. The symbols are _required_ to write C. 

>> Trigraph support might be in IO methods, thus
>> available to all compilers, shells and applications. 
>
>Wind unsupported speculation gets no praise here.

Looks like it's done by the C++ preprocessor or a standalone utility.

>ASCII is defined as a 7 bit code.  The 8th bit may be used for parity.
>Note what ASCII stands for: AMERICAN standard code for INTERCHANGE of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>8th bit is a parity bit.

That was 20 years ago. Modern communication protocols use compression and packet
framing. They don't rely on data for 'control characters' nor 'parity'. 

>> >Exactly, and when a foreign font is downloaded this determines what 
>> >characters are viewable on screen, hence the need for trigraphs if the 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>'convert' it somehow.  

That's exactly what it does. The trigraphs are converted to ASCII before the C
compiler sees the source code. A compiler listing would show ASCII. 

If you choose to leave them in source code (which is considered bad practice),
they will show as three characters on the editor screen. The display adaptor
does not convert them to anything. 

>In fact if you keyed the above into your C compiler it should accept
>it as good source code.

It does (MS VC++ 6.0).
```

robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> It's the corollary to your (snipped) argument. I speculated trigraph support
> might already be available to Cobol via the file system. You dismissed it by
> saying Cobol runs on platforms other than Unix. 

Trigraphs have nothing to do with Unix, they are not part of the file
system, you just made that up.

> In that case, how do foreign
> programmers write C on other platforms? 

Using trigraphs where necessary.

> What operating system did you have in mind? 

There are dozens, hundreds, of operating systems that are not MS nor
Unix.
 
> In the worst case, foreign programmers would have no more nor less than what
> they have now. You want to deny the symbols to 80% (pick a number) because 20%
> wouldn't be able to use them. 

For a start I don't think that symbols are necessary for Cobol's
'and', 'or' and 'not'.  These are commonly used in other languages,
the saving in key strokes in negligible, and it seems that only you
have difficulty with 'paradigm shift'.

I mena, duh, how hard is it ?  everyone seems to cope.
 
> >Wind unsupported speculation gets no praise here.
> 
> Looks like it's done by the C++ preprocessor or a standalone utility.

More wild speculation.  No it isn't.  The core compiler (mine) accepts
the trigraphs as being synonyms for the characters.
 
> That was 20 years ago. Modern communication protocols use compression and packet
> framing. They don't rely on data for 'control characters' nor 'parity'. 

Actually you will find that 'ASCII' is still defined that way so when
you refer to 'ASCII characters' that is what you are referring to. 
Now if you want to say some other set you should give, say, the
codeset.
 
> >The trigraph is in the source code.  On computers that don't have,
> >say, {}, they are replaced by the user typing ??< and ??>.  These
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> compiler sees the source code. A compiler listing would show ASCII. 

Geeez, Rob, once you get the wrong end of the stick you hang on to it
for dear life.

No. The trigraphs are _not_ converted to ASCII before the C compiler
sees the source, at least not on my compiler.  How would a C compiler
listing show 'ASCII' (remember AMERICAN ...) for '{' when the
screen/printer doesn't have this.
 
> If you choose to leave them in source code (which is considered bad practice),
> they will show as three characters on the editor screen. The display adaptor
> does not convert them to anything. 

That is exactly how they work.  They show on the screen as ??< because
there is _NO_ {.

> >In fact if you keyed the above into your C compiler it should accept
> >it as good source code.
> 
> It does (MS VC++ 6.0).

And did you take a listing from the compiler output to see if they
were still trigraphs ?

Or do you avoid doing things that may show you to be wrong, preferring
to just make it up and keep arguing until everyone gives up telling
you how it really works ?

   .. as you have done for 60 years.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T14:13:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed8a1ca.143296106@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <bav0mv$13j$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed31dd7.356571619@news.optonline.net> <bavbub$5qp$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed3f77b.412296085@news.optonline.net> <bb18dt$vuj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d9c4.45232037@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291208.6357ad23@posting.google.com> <3ed72976.46941081@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305301155.1d800fa@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>> Looks like it's done by the C++ preprocessor or a standalone utility.
>
>More wild speculation.  No it isn't.  The core compiler (mine) accepts
>the trigraphs as being synonyms for the characters.

It wasn't speculation. I looked it up here (note Caldera owns SCO)

http://osr5doc.ca.caldera.com:457/tools/clang_cpp.html

Preprocessing replaces trigraph sequences with their single-character
equivalents, partitions source files into preprocessing tokens, and executes
preprocessing directives. 

http://www.pa.msu.edu/ftp/pub/phy405/c++_std/lex.html

2.1  Phases of translation                                [lex.phases]

1 The precedence among the syntax rules of translation is  specified  by
  the following phases.1)

    1 Physical source file characters are mapped to the source character
      set  (introducing  new-line characters for end-of-line indicators)
      if necessary.  Trigraph sequences (_lex.trigraph_) are replaced by
      corresponding single-character internal representations.

Why are they using the same language? Because they are paraphrasing the ANSI C
standard (published 1990).

Borland said (in 1998): 

Translating trigraphs in the compiler would slow compilation down
considerably [yeah right], yet trigraph support is required by ANSI C. So
Turbo C++ provides a filer named TRIGRAPH.EXE to handle trigraph
sequences when you need to. The syntax for invoking this program is
  TRIGRAPH [-u]  [ ...]

http://bdn.borland.com/article/0,1410,18098,00.html

>> >The trigraph is in the source code.  On computers that don't have,
>> >say, {}, they are replaced by the user typing ??< and ??>.  These
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> That's exactly what it does. The trigraphs are converted to ASCII before the
C
>> compiler sees the source code. A compiler listing would show ASCII. 
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>screen/printer doesn't have this.

The above references and the C standard say it IS translated.

Just because they lack a key for { doesn't mean it has been deleted from the
ASCII code set. The keyboard doesn't define the code set, nor does it delete
font symbols used for screen and printer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T14:14:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305311314.64048e15@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <bav0mv$13j$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed31dd7.356571619@news.optonline.net> <bavbub$5qp$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed3f77b.412296085@news.optonline.net> <bb18dt$vuj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d9c4.45232037@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291208.6357ad23@posting.google.com> <3ed72976.46941081@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305301155.1d800fa@posting.google.com> <3ed8a1ca.143296106@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >More wild speculation.  No it isn't.  The core compiler (mine) accepts
> >the trigraphs as being synonyms for the characters.
> 
> It wasn't speculation. I looked it up here (note Caldera owns SCO)

Implementation _may_ be done by different mechanisms. Some of the
compilers that I use (eg Aztec, Mix) take the trigraphs through the
core and a listing from the compiler would show the trigraphs.

It is unwise of you to make generalisations about what things outside
your experience may do.

> Just because they lack a key for { doesn't mean it has been deleted from the
> ASCII code set. 

Geez, Rob, not all computers use ASCII. Have you heard of EBCDIC ?

> The keyboard doesn't define the code set, nor does it delete
> font symbols used for screen and printer.

The bit pattern used to represent a particular character in ASCII is
used in the foreign code set to represent some other character.  For
example hex23 on an American IBM PC is 'hash'.  On a UK machine this
is the currency symbol pound sterling (L-).

To get a hex23 on a UK machine you do a shift-3 (exactlay as for hash
on US) and this displays (L-), print it and it prints (L-).
I have a table here showing the 'ASCII National Replacement
Characters' for 18 languages.  In the case of the UK only hex23 is
affected.

For particular languages up to 11 codes in the range hex20 to hex7E
are affected.  For example what you see as '{' in Czech is 'a-acute'. 
in Danish it is  'ae', in  Spanish it is 'circle with line'.

The 11 character codes are (using US ASCII at my end): '#@{/]^`{|}~'.

These are the codes that C uses trigraphs for and which are not in the
Cobol character set (for an exact reason).

(Also the $ is a square thingie in hungarian and Swiss have [] for
<>.)

So, while the keyboard doesn't define the codeset, the codeset does
define what the keyboard, display and printer does.

(your machines may vary).
```

> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

a
> >"symbol" for the logical "NOT"  "AND" and "OR" - so providing such to
COBOL
> >wouldn't be totally awful.

> The ampersand is taken but the math-like carat (^) seems to be available
for
> AND. The pipe symbol and bang (exclamation) appear to be available for OR
and
> NOT.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >that would be "dismissed" and I don't think that many in CLC would object
to
> >it being CONSIDERED.  All that I perceive as being "rejected" here is a
> >claim that either the style of using symbols or the style of using words
IN
> >THOSE CASES WHERE BOTH ARE AVAILABLE is anything other than a "style"
> >choice.
>
> What you are saying is don't present a argument that may be taken as
criticism
> -- asymmetry, flawed notational system. Just say 'I like it' or 'I find it
> easier to read'.

Asymmetry is a problem with the "language".  You haven't shown that one of
the two is a "complete" set and the other is an "incomplete set".  However,
even if you had, I don't think that would (necessarily) mean that simply not
being a "complete" set means that it is "less good" to use one or the  other
option - where both exists.

P.S.  To send an "enhancement request/idea" to J4/WG4, send an email to the
chair of J4 / convenor of WG4 at:

Yes, Don is currently both the chair of J4 *and* the convenor of WG4. (This
occurred after Ann <Wallace> Bennett decided NOT to "re-new" for another
term as convenor of WG4 because of changes in her responsibilities within
IBM.  I don't know the exact nature of these changes.)  Don has "applied"
with no other candidates for these two jobs the last time that each "came
up" for appointment.  There is nothing to say how long ("temporary" or
"permanent") this is.  FYI, for those in the US (not Jimmy) when Don became
WG4 convenor he STOPPED being US "head-of-delegation" to WG4 - to avoid
"conflict of interest".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T15:32:44+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bas1tb$o53$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ecfe892.146295370@news.optonline.net> <bap1us$fvu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed10d6b.221275015@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>There is actually no _need_ to support our preferences.  

> So your 'cogent argument' in support of symbols 

There is _no_need_ for me to provide you with any argument in support of my 
use of symbols.  I am not attempting to convince you to change to them.

> is that I'm uninformed,
> egotistical or otherwise a Bad Person. Did you learn this style of debate
> in law school?

That was not an argument to support my use of symbols.  It was commentary 
on your semi-uninformed and irrelevant arguments that you attempted to use 
as a pseudo-intellectual backing for use of words, seemingly in an effort 
for us to switch away from our preference to your preference.

As support for use of words instead of symbols you claimed such things as:
  All languages went from symbols to phonetic alphabet (wrong!)
  Japan doen't use Kangi except in high literature (wrong!)
  The Goths invaded Rome.

All totally irrelevant and semi-uninformed.

> 'I like it' is not an argument, it's an emotional feeling. 'You're stupid'
> is not rebuttal.

You use 'greater than' because you like it better than '>'.  You may try to 
present nonsense arguments, but unless you have a restricted character set 
on the printer it is only that you 'like it'.

> I worked on those machines, so I feel qualified to say speed was not why
> ALTER was invented. Some people just liked the novelty of self-modifying
> code. It seemed like a Good Idea at the time.

ALTER was not a novelty at the time Cobol was first available, it was how 
assembler programs did it.
 
> PECD bragged here about programs which 'wrote themselves' in memory. The
> motivation was not speed, it was cleverness.

And your bragging has what as motivation ?

> As Sherlock Holmes said to Dr. Watson: "You have a keen mind for the
> obvious."

If the discussion was 'obvious' why did you bring the issue up ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T15:34:09+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed2dcf6_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ecfe892.146295370@news.optonline.net> <bap1us$fvu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed10d6b.221275015@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed10d6b.221275015@news.optonline.net...
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

OK. I asked you to quote your source and you haven't. So I went and found it
for you.

Here's what I said (Post under "Re: ALTER verb was Re: "We all agree that"
(was: Sets and subsets)"
14/05/2003):

"I once wrote a program that used ONLY "AND" "OR" "XOR" and "NEGATE"
instructions.
It read paper tape, processed the input, wrote it to
a magnetic tape, and produced a report, much to everyone's amazement.
It did this by modifying bit strings to become machine instructions.
No protection was violated. I did it for a bet and to show that at the
machine
language level the only thing that matters is binary. (The program was
written
in PLAN for an ICL 1900)."

I posted this not as a "brag" but simply in support of my position on
programs modifying themselves (which is what THAT particular discussion was
about...)

So, notwithstanding that my motivation AT THE TIME was the winning of a bet
and to demonstrate that ONLY binary and logical operations are what matters,
YOU then assigned to me the motivation of "cleverness". (Which I have no
need to prove at all).

See, what you didn't know was, that at that time I had been running a course
in Boolean Logic and this program arose as a demonstration, over a few
beers, after I had stated the position that what they had learned on the
course was the fundamental of ALL computer programming. They pushed me to a
bet and I won it. (If I had LOST it, and still reported it here, would that
be "bragging"?)

I DIDN'T do it or (report it here) to prove how clever I am.

I did it because someone expressed doubt that it could be done.

"They said it couldn't be done..."

(Red rag to a bull...<G>)

Throughout my career I have spent many thousands of hours disproving this
same assertion.

I could forgive your accusing me of bragging (even though on this occcasion
I am not guilty), I could forgive your assigning totally the wrong
motivation for what I did, but I'm buggered if I'll forgive your total lack
of understanding, demonstrated by your statement that I SHOULD have done it
to prove "speed"!

That was NEVER the issue!

I realise you have so many friends you can afford to lose one, Robert, but I
am really so pissed off over this, Satan will be wearing an anorak before
you receive my support again. (unless of course, you recant....<G>)

Pete.
```

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ecfe892.146295370@news.optonline.net...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> >If all of your posts are simply trying to explain why your style works
for
> >you, that's fine.  If you think that you have reasons that should impact
> >other peoples preferred style, then you certainly have not succeeded so
far
> >and others have explained why your reasons are not convincing to them.
>
> The corollary is: they haven't presented ANY argumentation in support of
their
> preference except 'I find it easier to read.' One can say that about
anything,
> even ALTER.
>
(Glad you mentioned ALTER, one of my all time favo[this transmission as been
suppressed because Mr. Dashwood is indulging in mischief, and not properly
addressing the issues to hand...Now then Dashwood, get serious or get
out...]

The point you may be missing Robert, is that no-one is REQUIRED to provide
arguments in support of their position.

They could just take the line:"I see no point in arguing with RW, who has
demonstrated his refusal to be persuaded. I shall continue to use what works
for me and I don't give a damn about what RW thinks."

Whether this is a "right" or "wrong" position is not up for debate. It just
is.

If you want people to actually look at your arguments and indulge in
academic debate with you, you will need to address the way you present
arguments.

Personally, I have no problem debating with you (or anyone else) but none of
us can afford to WASTE time presenting arguments that will not be evaluated
seriously, or debating issues that will NEVER change anyone's mind.

Bill's point is a fair one. Matters of style come down to personal
preference. REAL programmers can deal with ANY kind of style. We should
therefore grant people the right to use whatever style they want to (once
they have learned the language and understand what it is about.)

"One man's meat is another man's poison".

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

Robert Wagner wrote:

> When I said (paraphrasing from memory) "If you don't like my arguments,
> keep programming the way you have been", THAT drew criticism.

What you actually said was: keep programming the way you _always_ have.

The implication being 'since the 60s', that your way was 'new' and 'modern' 
and 'better' while our use of '>' was old and obsolete.

This was how you wanted to portray the two styles when you made calims 
about how natural languafes developed.
 
> If people don't want to discuss COBOL, why are they here? They should be
> holding forth in alt.flame or alt.politics.

You seldom discuss Cobol, you are too interested in bragging about 
linguistics and other nonsense.

Sometimes you do discuss what you _think_ is Cobol, but this turns out to 
just be something you misread.


>>If you want people to actually look at your arguments and indulge in
>>academic debate with you, you will need to address the way you present
>>arguments.
> 
> That is a recurring recommendation.

Well you have been taking a few unsteady steps towards rational debate. I 
should comend you for trying not to be quite so sweeping with your 
generalisations, not being quite so insulting or arrogant.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T18:34:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed25dc3.307392373@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ecfe892.146295370@news.optonline.net> <3ed02bb7$1_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3ed11413.222979459@news.optonline.net> <basgjf$u7m$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>and 'better' while our use of '>' was old and obsolete.

No. You had said earlier that your style keeps evolving. I believe it. 

I recently revisited some COBOL I wrote 30 years ago. The experience shocked and
embarrassed me. What crap code! And, at the time, I thought I was a good
programmer. No, it didn't have WS- prefixes and numbered paragraphs, but it had
GO TOs (within a Section) and it was 3,000 lines long. Today I'd write it as ten
or more called programs. 

>This was how you wanted to portray the two styles when you made calims 
>about how natural languages developed.

Jallan presented argumentation which made me reexamine the linguistic objection.
It was a refreshing change from hand waving and name calling.

>> If people don't want to discuss COBOL, why are they here? They should be
>> holding forth in alt.flame or alt.politics.
>
>You seldom discuss Cobol, you are too interested in bragging about 
>linguistics and other nonsense.

The issue was > vs. greater than. That's a legitimate Cobol topic.

>Sometimes you do discuss what you _think_ is Cobol, but this turns out to 
>just be something you misread.

More likely a feature I've been avoiding for twenty years, so didn't notice it
had changed. 

>>>If you want people to actually look at your arguments and indulge in
>>>academic debate with you, you will need to address the way you present
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>generalisations, not being quite so insulting or arrogant.

I'd read a fair amount of academic lingo, but not been exposed to the cultural
manners seen here. Programmers, especially hotshots, tend to be bombastic. My
style is mild in comparison to a twenty-something Java jockey with spiked hair.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T08:04:06+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<batruv$go2$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed11413.222979459@news.optonline.net> <basgjf$u7m$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed25dc3.307392373@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>What you actually said was: keep programming the way you _always_ have.
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> No. You had said earlier that your style keeps evolving. I believe it.

No. I _later_ said that, directly in response to your "the way you_always_ 
have", and I included your phrase as a quote.

> I recently revisited some COBOL I wrote 30 years ago. The experience
> shocked and embarrassed me. What crap code! And, at the time, I thought I
> was a good programmer. 

The question then arises is: what will you think of today's code in 10 or 
20 years time.  Hopefully you will shocked and dismayed at how awful it is 
compared to how you progressed in the intervening time.

This is why you should not take your current style as being 'best' or some 
absolute.

> No, it didn't have WS- prefixes and numbered
> paragraphs, but it had GO TOs (within a Section) and it was 3,000 lines
> long. Today I'd write it as ten or more called programs.

30 years ago was a different place.  Different reasons prevailed. Programs 
were overlayed and laid out differently because of it.  Memory restrictions 
prevented calling lots of small routines.  Programs had to do as much as 
possible in a single pass of the file.

Early PC Cobol systems (eg MF CIS Cobol and Level II) had restrictions on 
the number of entries in the CALL table so system could not be built up as 
lots of small called routines.

> The issue was > vs. greater than. That's a legitimate Cobol topic.

Which you now seem to have switched back to preferring '>', haven't you ?
 
>>Sometimes you do discuss what you _think_ is Cobol, but this turns out to
>>just be something you misread.
> 
> More likely a feature I've been avoiding for twenty years, so didn't
> notice it had changed.

The one I had in mind was abbreviated conditions in WHEN, but yes, that 
sort of thing.
 
> I'd read a fair amount of academic lingo, but not been exposed to the
> cultural manners seen here. 

I assume that you are referring to the gaining of credability based on 
what you say being accurate rather than just on _how_ you say it ?

> Programmers, especially hotshots, tend to be bombastic. 

Or is it just _wannabe_ hotshots that are the most bombastic ?

> My style is mild in comparison to a twenty-something Java
> jockey with spiked hair.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T00:28:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed29df6.323829959@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed11413.222979459@news.optonline.net> <basgjf$u7m$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed25dc3.307392373@news.optonline.net> <batruv$go2$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>have", and I included your phrase as a quote.

Then I must have inferred it. I wasn't thinking of you personally so much as the
collective readership.

>> I recently revisited some COBOL I wrote 30 years ago. The experience
>> shocked and embarrassed me. What crap code! And, at the time, I thought I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>compared to how you progressed in the intervening time.

Probably. I've seen RAD programming like Peter goes on about. Even recent
versions of MS C++ contain a little taste of it. Drag a few widgets and .. splat
.. out come 2,000 lines of _good looking_ code that works the first time. When I
see that, I think about the weeks-to-months I spent writing the equivalent. 

VB has been similar for years, but it's all under the covers. Seeing the actual
code is more impressive, to me.

>30 years ago was a different place.  Different reasons prevailed. Programs 
>were overlayed and laid out differently because of it.  Memory restrictions 
>prevented calling lots of small routines.  Programs had to do as much as 
>possible in a single pass of the file.

True, but those limitations did not apply to this particular program. 

>Early PC Cobol systems (eg MF CIS Cobol and Level II) had restrictions on 
>the number of entries in the CALL table so system could not be built up as 
>lots of small called routines.

I benchmarked CIS and found it crude. My regular compiler was Realia from 85 to
96. It had no such restrictions. 

>> The issue was > vs. greater than. That's a legitimate Cobol topic.
>
>Which you now seem to have switched back to preferring '>', haven't you ?

I'm neutral. I no longer despise '>'.

>> I'd read a fair amount of academic lingo, but not been exposed to the
>> cultural manners seen here. 
>
>I assume that you are referring to the gaining of credability based on 
>what you say being accurate rather than just on _how_ you say it ?

Exactly the opposite. The criticism, loss of credibility if you will, was over
_how_ I presented my points, not their content. You and others said so quite
explicitly. Need I find quotes?

I was thinking of the aversion to generalization and the distaste for
criticizing other peoples' programming style, but eagerness to disparage the
rhetorical style of an outsider. All are backwards from the 'shop floor'.

>> Programmers, especially hotshots, tend to be bombastic. 
>
>Or is it just _wannabe_ hotshots that are the most bombastic ?

Some legitimate hotshots think Bill Gates gave them license to be enfants
terrible. The wannabes emulate them.

Other hotshots are soft-spoken and unpretentious. You think they're mediocre
until you read their code.
```

them? By
> that I mean, required a programmer to rewrite code, not because it was
'bad
> code' but because it did not follow the letter of the law.

No, never. I have encouraged people to follow the standards (especially if
it was my job to write them <G>) but I have never forced anyone to rewrite
because they violated a standard (mine or anyone else's). I HAVE demanded a
rewrite of code that didn't work (especially after numerous attempts by the
author to "fix" it).

It comes down to priorities. For me the priorty is that the code should
work, and do so consistently. That's because it is our job to support the
Business. (We support the Business best by giving them WORKING (even if
inelegant) code). All the rest of the technical squabbles are not supporting
the Business, they are simply about technology.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T06:12:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed2fe1b.348446586@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ecfe892.146295370@news.optonline.net> <3ed02bb7$1_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3ed11413.222979459@news.optonline.net> <3ed1a053_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3ed25bf1.306926229@news.optonline.net> <3ed2e610_7@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>it?

That was an (over)simplified example. In another case I had written a small
two-program system which did an import, update, extract and report. Both
programs contained COBOL Sort. It had been running in quasi-production (due to a
Y2K freeze) for five months. The day before it was to go into production, the
team lead told me they needed a complete rewrite because she had heard somewhere
'COBOL Sorts are bad'. I pointed out the corporate style book explicitly
permitted them, and had guidelines on naming the sortwork file, which I had
followed. I also pointed out dozens of production programs in the same
department that used COBOL Sort. That wasn't good enough. I had to restructure
the whole thing in one day. Needless to say, the version which went in bypassed
nearly all testing and documentation.
```

In article <3ed25bf1.306926229@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>>No, never. I have encouraged people to follow the standards (especially if
>>it was my job to write them <G>) but I have never forced anyone to rewrite
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>          know. There are maintenance issues involved. blah blah

Mr Wagner, we seem to look upon such situations differently.  You preface
this example by mentionining 'stupid reasons'.  Consider the interchange:

Boss: 'Do it *this* way.'
Pgmr: 'For what reason should it be done that way?'
Boss: 'It should be done that way because if it is not done that way I 
will not sign your timesheet and you will not get paid.'
Pgmr: 'Hmmmm... that seem a Very Good Reason.'

DD
```

You said you hadn't worked in management. The reason is obvious. Negative,
fear-driven techniques work fine on minimum wage and blue collar employees.

Besides, they are ignoble. At the end of the day, I'd hate myself for coercing
someone like that.

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:

You gave no 'reasons', Mr Wagner, and you admitted as much.

>
>>>Negative,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>There are many many books and other documents on management. 

Then it should be easy for you to document, Mr Wagner... second request, 
then: three interesting assertions... any documentation?

>
>>>Besides, they are ignoble. At the end of the day, I'd hate myself for coercing
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>management style in general, as it applies to most people.

Now this is confusing, Mr Wagner... are you saying that you are unable to 
see what you, yourself, claim to be 'obvious'?  My own evaluation has been 
a bit more direct:

<http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=371AFEB4.75817ACA%40home.com&output=gplain>

--begin quoted text:

Pfoo... the 'good thing' is that I would be *woefully* unsuited to the
task; I do not think that I am capable of lowering the quality of my
thinking far enough to be 'management'.

--end quoted text

... while in this very forum, rather recently - in fact, in a thread in 
which you, Mr Wagner, participated - I confessed:

<http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=b8napr%2431v%241%40panix1.panix.com&output=gplain>

--begin quoted text:

I have observed more than once a tendency in organisations towards a 
sort of 'Gresham's Law of Management', or 'In an organisation in which 
there circulate two forms of Management, one Bad and one Good, the 
tendency is usually such so that Bad Management Drives Out the Good.'

--end quoted text

DD
```

words. When Bad Managers see the big dogs squabbling and backbiting, they feel
empowered to follow suit. When they see positive things happening, they're not
as bold.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T14:52:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb2if9$b51$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed11413.222979459@news.optonline.net> <3ed1a053_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3ed25bf1.306926229@news.optonline.net> <bavl90$j2u$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed40044.414545445@news.optonline.net>`

On 27-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >Boss: 'Do it *this* way.'
> >Pgmr: 'For what reason should it be done that way?'
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> someone like that.

In real life, there are no employers (or parents) that don't use such techniques
to some degree.   We never agree 100% with everything our bosses want us to do,
so we compromise and do what they want.   Maybe the effect of non-compliance
won't be so immediately apparent, but there will be an effect.

As the old joke goes, we're haggling about the price.
```

<a id="t-9a97e40f9c"></a>
## Thread: Dot Notation — 24 this month / 26 total

### Dot Notation

- **From:** ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris)
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T07:50:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cc07162.0305230650.349ebc36@posting.google.com>`

```
I've seen a few "older" posts regarding this topic, so I figured I'd
put the question out there again.

Using the example:

01  CUSTOMER.
    05  FIRST-NAME  PIC  X(10).
    05  LAST-NAME   PIC  X(10).

MOVE "TOM" TO FIRST-NAME OF CUSTOMER
MOVE "JONES" TO LAST-NAME OF CUSTOMER

I'd like something a bit more friendly on the eyes:

MOVE "TOM" TO CUSTOMER.FIRST-NAME
MOVE "JONES" TO CUSTOMER.LAST-NAME

I know that the compiler assigns special meaning to the period, so
this is most likely not going to work, at least not without some sort
of special compiler option or directive set, so does anyone know of
something oterh way to accomplish the same goal?
```

#### ↳ Re: Dot Notation

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T16:35:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ece4ad1.40357815@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0305230650.349ebc36@posting.google.com>`

```
ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris) wrote:

>I've seen a few "older" posts regarding this topic, so I figured I'd
>put the question out there again.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>something oterh way to accomplish the same goal?

Use a C compiler. COBOL qualification uses OF or IN. Of course you can define
the name with qualifier as prefix: customer-first-name. 

If you want better looking code, push CapsLock once.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dot Notation

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T09:54:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0305230854.5797b9d2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0305230650.349ebc36@posting.google.com>`

```
I suggest the use of the new "Replace" verb.  This gives you something
like a "Scope".  ie:

REPLACE ==foo== by ==OF CUSTOMER==
Move "TOM" to FIRST-NAME ==foo==
MOVE "JONES" to LAST-NAME ==foo==
REPLACE END

This is similar to a Pascal construct.

Especially hand for inline method invocation with OO contstructs of
COBOL 2002.

ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris) wrote in message news:<8cc07162.0305230650.349ebc36@posting.google.com>...
> I've seen a few "older" posts regarding this topic, so I figured I'd
> put the question out there again.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> something oterh way to accomplish the same goal?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dot Notation

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> I suggest the use of the new "Replace" verb.  This gives you something
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> REPLACE END

All this is to get around the fact that COBOL needs the dot method of
doing qualification.  The period could be used because it would not be a
full stop in context (no trailing space).  I think that I have that as a
recommendation for the next COBOL standard.  

> 
> This is similar to a Pascal construct.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> > something oterh way to accomplish the same goal?

> All this is to get around the fact that COBOL needs the dot method of
> doing qualification.  The period could be used because it would not be a

same names for fields in different records unless those fields are in copy
members.

something more like Pascal's "with" notation, including the provision of
scope terminators.   Was thinking of developing this as a proposal for the
COBOL committee, but I've been busy with other stuff.

what is there already with REPLACE accomplishs the task) to do this
with the current standard.

Something like dot notation becomes more important when you start
getting deep into OO and inline method invocation.

#### ↳ Re: Dot Notation

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T13:16:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305231216.605c352b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0305230650.349ebc36@posting.google.com>`

```
ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris) wrote

> 01  CUSTOMER.
>     05  FIRST-NAME  PIC  X(10).
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> MOVE "JONES" TO CUSTOMER.LAST-NAME

Qualification is only necessary if the same field name appear in two
or more records or group items.  There may be very good reasons why
you use the same field names in different places, without knowing
these reasons it is difficult to provide solutions.

Obviously if you must have the names the same and can't break the
program down into nested or callable sub-programs then you are stuck
with qualification.

If it is because there are two FDs being COPYed in then you could use
REPLACING First-Name BY Customer-First-Name Last-Name BY
Customer-Last-Name to give different field names to one of them.
In general what is 'friendly on the eye' is what you are used to.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dot Notation

the big picture?

01  CUSTOMER.
   02  CUSTOMER-FIRST-NAME   PIC X(10).
   02  CUSTOMER-LAST-NAME    PIC X(10).

MOVE "TOM" TO CUSTOMER-FIRST-NAME.
MOVE "JONES" TO CUSTOMER-LAST-NAME.
```

01  MYTEMP-CUSTOMER.
   02  CUSTOMER-FIRST-NAME   PIC X(10).
   02  CUSTOMER-LAST-NAME    PIC X(10).

01  CUSTOMER.
   02  CUSTOMER-FIRST-NAME   PIC X(10).
   02  CUSTOMER-LAST-NAME    PIC X(10).

understand picking "Tom"  and 'Jones" up from Screen Section fields or GUI
entryfields - but 'hard coded' ?????

Jimmy

     02  FIRST-NAME                 PIC X(10).
     02  LAST-NAME                  PIC X(30).

     02  NAME-AND-ADDRESS N-A.

#### ↳ Re: Dot Notation

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T12:22:25+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eceb87e_3@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0305230650.349ebc36@posting.google.com>`

```

"Chris" <ctaliercio@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:8cc07162.0305230650.349ebc36@posting.google.com...
> I've seen a few "older" posts regarding this topic, so I figured I'd
> put the question out there again.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> something oterh way to accomplish the same goal?

There have been some good responses, but I'll add my  2c anyway...

1. The dot notation is a shorthand form of referencing properties and
methods within an object.

The COBOL we are dealing with here is NOT Object Oriented and what you are
looking for is an alternative to "qualification". There are a number of
reasons why you would have duplicated datanames in your COBOL program. A
good one could be so that you can use MOVE...CORRESPONDING.

If you find qualification tiresome, reconsider your need for duplicated
datanames. If the names are unique, you never need to qualify them.

I have never seen a use of MOVE...CORRESPONDING that could not be coded in
an alternative way.

2. If your suggestion was picked up it would cause further confusion between
the referencing of Objects and the referencing of duplicated definitions.
(It is likely that future COBOL WILL use dot notation, but it will be for OO
reference rather than qualified datanames.)

3. Don't be confused between Object usage and qualification, just because
they look similar.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dot Notation

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T12:37:13+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bamesj$89c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0305230650.349ebc36@posting.google.com> <3eceb87e_3@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

> 1. The dot notation is a shorthand form of referencing properties and
> methods within an object.

It is, but it also has been used in C and Pascal to reference fields with 
records (structs) forever.  In fact C++ basically extended the C struct 
into the object.

With C structs you can't avoid having to use the dotted (or -> ) notation:

    struct custrec { char first_name[20], char last_name[20] } customer;

can _only be referenced as customer.first_name and customer.last_name (or 
via an alias) and cannot use first_name last_name even if there is no other 
use of those field names.

What is 'easy' is whatever one is used to.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dot Notation

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T16:45:15+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ecef610$1_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0305230650.349ebc36@posting.google.com> <3eceb87e_3@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <bamesj$89c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

news:bamesj$89c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
> Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> via an alias) and cannot use first_name last_name even if there is no
other
> use of those field names.

Thanks Richard.

I don't use C very much and my point was based on Java which is innately OO.

I take your point.

Nevertheless, I believe adding dot notation to COBOL will possibly cause
more problems than it will solve.

>
> What is 'easy' is whatever one is used to.
>
You persuaded me of this long ago... I still agree.

Pete.

#### ↳ Re: Dot Notation

- **From:** ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris)
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T06:19:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cc07162.0305270519.5603f6c3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0305230650.349ebc36@posting.google.com>`

```
Wow! This really touched off a firestorm.

Maybe a more specific reason for why I was looking for "dot notation"
would be more helpful:

  01  ACCOUNT      IS TYPEDEF.
      05  OWNER    PIC  X(50).
      05  BALANCE  PIC S9(05)V9(02).

  01  CHECKING     ACCOUNT.
  01  SAVINGS      ACCOUNT.

Now suppose I want to accompliosh the following:

  MOVE "Chris T" TO OWNER OF CHECKING OWNER OF SAVINGS
  MOVE 100 TO BALANCE OF CHECKING BALANCE OF SAVINGS

IMHO - it looks much better to have these statements as:

  MOVE "Chris T" TO CHECKING.OWNER SAVINGS.OWNER
  MOVE 100 TO CHECKING.BALANCE SAVINGS.BALANCE

I realize that the TYPEDEF clause may be specific to Micro Focus, but
I find it particularly useful. This is a very basic example that I
know can be accomplished a number of other ways, but that's really not
the point of the post/question.

I have no desire to turn COBOL into C, but can't we all agree that the
second form here is much easier to read? I don't care if its "dot
notation" or the -> construct or whatever. All I was looking for was a
way to make things like this easier on the eyes.

Thanks to all who offered constructive ideas, they are much
appreciated.

To those who railed on about this being COBOL and not C - gee thanks.
Not only were your posts particulary useless, but you also
demonstrated why many non-COBOL people think we are using an archaic
language. Your unwillingness to at least be receptive to ideas that
may perhaps enhance the language would surely doom it were you in
charge of creating the standards.

---

…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> something oterh way to accomplish the same goal?

## Thread: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant??? — 90 this month / 195 total

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T16:15:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
 J4 and WG4 posed the question "What <is> your vision for the COBOL language
5 years from now?"

Although, I haven't heard it officially yet, I believe that I have heard it
from sufficiently "reliable sources" that CobolExpo 2003 (for June in Las
Vegas) is now canceled.  I also BELIEVE that as of this moment, the "forum
on the future of COBOL" is still planned to take place that week in Las
Vegas.  Anyone already planning on attending the forum (originally in
addition to the CobolExpo) should verify exactly what the plans are for it.

HOWEVER, the major reason that I am posting this note to a few groups and
lists is that I really think that "we all" need to step up to the actual
answer to the question of where COBOL will be 5 years from now - and the
answer is close to (but not quite) irrelevant.

I *strongly* believe that there will still be COBOL compilers (even some
with new releases, new features, and new interfaces) - not only 5 years from
now but even 10 years from now.

I even believe (from some documents submitted to J4) that there MAY be one
or two compilers that conform (at least mostly) to the 2002 ISO COBOL
Standard.

I suspect that there will even be new and "upgraded" COBOL applications that
use NEW features introduced in the 2002 COBOL Standard - and some of the
TR's (Technical Reports) currently being worked on by J4 and WG4.

    ON THE OTHER HAND,

The following are some of my "crystal ball" visions (whether "fears" or
simply "forebodings")

1) I expect that MOST COBOL compilers will use the 2002 ISO Standard (and
the TR's - if approved) as a "Chinese menu" and will "pick and choose" which
of the (required - not just processor dependent) features they ever
implement.

2) COBOL will NEVER be a *major* player in the "user-side" of the web or
server application environment. (Which is NOT to say that there won't be
some new - as well as continued - applications that do this.)

3) New "businesses" doing data processing or existing businesses creating
NEW applications will *only* select COBOL as their "programming language of
choice" in infrequent and unusual circumstances.

4) Sites with existing COBOL applications will increase their
"consideration" of language conversions away from COBOL when they do "major
maintenance" or new functionality to those programs.

5) Membership in J4 and WG4 will continue to "decrease" with NO new COBOL
vendors joining J4; no new countries joining WG4; and those doing the "real
work" of the committees continuing to be limited to a VERY SMALL handful of
individuals (not vendors or user companies).

6) There never will be robust  "certification tests" for the 2002 ISO
Standard - and the US government (along with European and Asian governmental
bodies) will NEVER require "2002 conforming" compilers.  (And possibly they
will even - within the next 10 years - drop COBOL as a "authorized"
programming language for new projects - in cases where they have a list of
such authorized programming "languages" or "tools").

        ***

Bottom-Line:
  I will continue to "post" to newsgroups and others the information of what
is happening with "development" and "maintenance" of the COBOL language by
various Standards groups.  I think it is important that all existing COBOL
programmers "keep abreast" of what is or might be happening or going to
happen.

OTOH,  I can't see - unless something DRAMATICALLY changes - that COBOL will
be seen as a GROWING programming environment.  Certainly the 30-year old
question of "Is COBOL dead?" still deserves an answer of NO.  On the other
hand, the question of "Is COBOL a dynamic/growing programming environment?"
seems to warrant an equally strong "NO" as an answer.

OBVIOUSLY, this post reflects PERSONAL OPINION only.  I wouldn't mind being
proven wrong - but I won't hold my breath either.
```

#### ↳ Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T23:27:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ECEAAA3.B5AE0D4A@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

>  J4 and WG4 posed the question "What <is> your vision for the COBOL language
> 5 years from now?"
>

<snip>

Oh my gawd ! (Enter.... stage left,  Pete Dashwood arms flailing ..... <G>)

Even the J4/WG4 question is loaded. Bearing in mind, (J4 at least), being
comprised of 6 compiler vendors, out of a current total of 9 - they didn't even
really need to ask the question did they ? They must have the pulse of their
existing developer database, such as how many installations are just plodding
along with old compilers rather than playing catch-up, or how many of their
developers have pleaded for new features to compete against the language du
jour.

So perhaps we should be internalizing - my own thoughts :-

(1) Mainframers nearing retirement - do they care, or is it a case of '"I'm gong
to sweat it out with COBOL, unchanged, until I go out of the door with my
pension"..

(2) "Pooh, pooh, pooh. We've heard this one before. COBOL will still be around
even when Noah II builds his ark".

(3) I like what I've got - why all this OO COBOL nonsense ? Besides, I'll stick
with COBOL for the grunt work and use other languages and tools for GUI-ing the
Web etc., coupled with SQL and a DB engine.

(4) People keep adamantly denying it - but COBOL education shows a dramatic
decline. (I recall Thane writing, some 3 or 4 (?) Java-types had switched to
COBOL down there in Texas. That's not good enough. Had it been, 'some 500
Java-types had switched.....'.).

(5) No generalizations as answers please. The VB, C and Java crowd. Accepting
that the training 'academies' made a conscious switch to teaching new languages
and often dropping COBOL completely from the syllabus, put your finger on where
you think their developers  found COBOL to be weak. What is it that the other
languages offer that COBOL just misses out on, (at least in their perception).

(6) Signs that COBOL is in decline - Some years back, I had myself geared up
thinking of attending a Micro Focus get together. Too late. It was cancelled and
one held since. If my memory is correct the cbobolreport first Expo was
cancelled for lack of interest. So they had Expo 2002 - but no idea of
attendance. COBOL 2003 - now a goner (?) - it is not a paying proposition.

(7) (a) Comments like, we will stick with current compiler, can't cost justify
buying, say, IBM Enterprise.

(7) (b) "Yer gets what yer pays for.....". Tech support fees and upgrades to
compilers - big obstacle in the PC world.  (I haven't even mentioned the R..
T... Fees ).

(8) (a) J4's role. I don't want to belabour this one. Some (all ?) compiler
vendors introduced extensions beyond COBOL '85 to be sanctioned originally in a
targeted COBOL '97 which became COBOL 2002 (that's December 2002), so we are
talking 2003 for implementation in new compilers.

(8) (b) My particular interest - OO COBOL was kick-started in '92/93,  now
formally sanctioned in COBOL 2002 - but as at the date or writing, Fujitsu,
Hitachi and Micro Focus are still AHEAD of the COBOL 2002 Standard - with
interim extensions for destroying objects (garbage collection) and more
importantly a set of  'Standard Classes' which include the very elemental Base
Class and lists (collections/dictionaries).

(8) (c) Here's what I really don't understand. Visualize yourself as the #2 or
#3 head honcho at say, Fujitsu or Micro Focus. You get sit reps from your J4 man
as to what is going on. Considering what has happened, I could see myself in
such a position, asking, "What the F..... are you people doing at these
meetings. We've had  "xxxxx"  as a f........ extension since ...(pick a date).
What the F.... is going on ! We have an investment in this people. Money ".

I am *NOT* knocking the J4 participants and am fully aware, as Bill has
observed, that the real donkey work is done by a dedicated minority. BUT - as an
executive of a compiler vendor, I would still find myself asking the above
questions - and verbalized, there wouldn't be any dots after the "F's".

(9) In all seriousness - Is it time to call it quits on a future COBOL Standard
? With forty years behind us the syntactical pattern of COBOL is  well
established. Is it time for the compiler vendors to opt out, do their own thing,
and introduce new features (extensions), as they see fit, to compete against the
other languages ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB.
```

#### ↳ Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-23T23:37:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ecea195.62573163@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Why has this happened? Where did we fail?

Is the language inherently flawed? CS types have been saying that for 30 years
without much effect. 

Because the user community feels gouged by Y2K contractors? They have been, and
continue to be, gouged by contracting companies proffering Indian H1B visa
programmers who are paid 20% of the bill rate. Gouging didn't start nor end with
Y2K projects, nor is it specific to COBOL.  

Because standards committees took 17 years to produce the next 'release'?
That's almost the lifetime of the PC and, to my thinking, twice the lifetime of
the 'modern PC', which began when Windows 95 was released. One would reasonably
expect a vibrant, growing language to be enhanced every two to four years.
Seventeen years is out of the ballpark. It creates a Public Image problem. 

Because COBOL is not perceived as modern? If so, why doesn't ANSI C a'la 1980
suffer the same opprobium? It is still widely used. OO COBOL is modern,
especially in a .NET environment, yet nobody seems to notice. 

I've got it -- because decision-making managers' technical skills are usually
ten years old. They are assessing COBOL as it was in 1995 rather than today. If
it had been vibrant and forward-looking then, they would support it now. If it
had been 'on the edge of the horizon' pushing GUI -- a hot topic in 1995 -- they
would regard it as a vibrant language today. Reality doesn't count here, only
ten year-old perceptions. 

Well, we failed miserably. In 1995 we *could* have been supporting calls to MFC
to paint GUI screens. Instead we stared at our navels and legacy code, thinking
this too shall pass. Now we're paying the price. 


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> J4 and WG4 posed the question "What <is> your vision for the COBOL language
>5 years from now?"
…[81 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

> to paint GUI screens. Instead we stared at our navels and legacy code, thinking

Maybe because people aren't paying attention.  Self education is
important.  You don't need MFC to do GUI - in fact GUI is not the main
point of MFC.  There were at least TWO GUI tools for COBOL available
in 1995.  MF Had Dialog system and for years before that Flexus was
selling SP2 - specifically for GUI and COBOL.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-25T11:48:49+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bap0bo$f7h$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ecea195.62573163@news.optonline.net> <bfdfc3e8.0305241412.11b10991@posting.google.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:

>> Well, we failed miserably. In 1995 we *could* have been supporting calls
>> to MFC to paint GUI screens. Instead we stared at our navels and legacy
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> selling SP2 - specifically for GUI and COBOL.

I have also used Hi-Screen-Pro II for Cobol applications, it was available 
in 1992 (don't know when 'I' was) and could be used by several languages 
including MF, MS, RM and Realia Cobol, Foxpro, Pascal, C, well anything 
really.

From 1991, starting with MF versionm 2.4, I (and I do mean I working alone 
most of the time) developed a fully graphical container loading system 
doing drag and drop of containers from wharf stacks into graphical ship 
bays with windows for stability, torsion, bending curves, bonjean views of 
the hull, even a view from the helm of the containers showing the horizon 
(correctly set for the calculated vessel trim) to confirm the appropriate 
visability requirements.  The 'helm' position was variable so by changing 
this to some large numbers the view could be from half a mile behind the 
ship at a couple of hundred feet and some distance to the side.  The 
program would then redraw it and change the view point to show a 'fly by'.

All done in Cobol directly to Windows API on Windows 3.0 originally, 
updated over the years to 3.11 and Win32.

We don't need no steenking MFC.

RW may have been staring at his navel, I certainly wasn't, nor was anyone 
else who wanted to.
```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Thane Hubbell wrote:

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>really.

Typesetting programs had GUI screens since 1985. In '86 I wrote one intended to
knock PageMaker off its pedestal. In Cobol. Granted, that's not a Cobol tool but
the code could have been used to build one. 

MS demoed a GUI version of Word at Comdex '86. It looked like a copy of the
MacIntosh version. 

>From 1991, starting with MF version 2.4, I (and I do mean I working alone 
>most of the time) developed a fully graphical container loading system 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>updated over the years to 3.11 and Win32.

Very good!

I preferred direct writes to the screen _and printer_. I had an OEM laser
printer that had 8 million bits (pixels) of memory in the PC's address space.
I'd paint a page, do one IO command and .. shazam .. the image would appear on
paper in three seconds. 

We didn't need no steenking PostScript. 

>We don't need no steenking MFC.
>
>RW may have been staring at his navel, I certainly wasn't, nor was anyone 
>else who wanted to.

I wasn't either.

#### ↳ Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T12:08:20+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eceb533_3@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net...
> J4 and WG4 posed the question "What <is> your vision for the COBOL
language
> 5 years from now?"
>

Nobody asked for my opinion. (Probably because they know it wouldn't be what
they want to hear <G>)

I have some very definite views on the "future of COBOL", not just over five
years but for 10 and 15 years as well.

I also have some pretty definite views on how it "fell from Grace" and what
could have been done about it. (Not to "whip cats", just as interesting
speculation with the possibility of profiting from past mistakes.)

And it is NOT "irrelevant". The news is NOT all bad. The use of COBOL will
change and there will be some major adjustments at Corporate level. I expect
to be writing COBOL (and other languages) as long as I can see and type. (Of
course, whether I'll be making any money out of it is another point
entirely...<G>)

> Although, I haven't heard it officially yet, I believe that I have heard
it
> from sufficiently "reliable sources" that CobolExpo 2003 (for June in Las
> Vegas) is now canceled.  I also BELIEVE that as of this moment, the "forum
> on the future of COBOL" is still planned to take place that week in Las
> Vegas.  Anyone already planning on attending the forum (originally in
> addition to the CobolExpo) should verify exactly what the plans are for
it.
>

Presumably, if the response has been so weak as to cause cancellation of the
Expo, it is unlikely that many will attend the forum. Asking people to stump
up several hundred dollars (plus expenses, plus lost earnings if you are a
freelance) is a big ask in the current economic conditions. The result is
that the main interest in the Expo will be from vendors and sponsors(who can
afford to attend), but have a position to promote. You can bet that
corporate sponsored presentations on the "Future of COBOL" will be little
more than thinly veiled sales spiel based on the premise that "the future of
COBOL is rosy if you buy our product...".

> HOWEVER, the major reason that I am posting this note to a few groups and
> lists is that I really think that "we all" need to step up to the actual
> answer to the question of where COBOL will be 5 years from now - and the
> answer is close to (but not quite) irrelevant.
>

Well, that is one answer, Bill. However, it is NOT the only one.

> I *strongly* believe that there will still be COBOL compilers (even some
> with new releases, new features, and new interfaces) - not only 5 years
from
> now but even 10 years from now.
>

Possibly hand carved by devotees of the Language as a cottage industry? (I
predicted some years back that a number of the current vendors will "leave
the field" by 2015. IBM are hanging in there only because they HAVE to and
not because they WANT to. As soon as their Java and Websphere offerings have
an established market place, I predict their focus will move away from
COBOL.)


> I even believe (from some documents submitted to J4) that there MAY be one
> or two compilers that conform (at least mostly) to the 2002 ISO COBOL
> Standard.
>
> I suspect that there will even be new and "upgraded" COBOL applications
that
> use NEW features introduced in the 2002 COBOL Standard - and some of the
> TR's (Technical Reports) currently being worked on by J4 and WG4.
>

So what? Does anybody care? The organizations administering the COBOL
standard ARE irrelevant, now. They have earned this position in the same way
that everybody either earns or loses respect: by their actions and their
performance.

But that doesn't make COBOL irrelevant. The language is bigger than the
organisations that tried (successfully) to hijack it from the public domain
and put their own copyright on it.

>     ON THE OTHER HAND,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the TR's - if approved) as a "Chinese menu" and will "pick and choose"
which
> of the (required - not just processor dependent) features they ever
> implement.
>

That's no bad thing. At least some vendors will be continung to produce a
COBOL product. I'd rather see vendors (who are MUCH more in touch with
market demand) decide what should be in the Language, than a bunch of
boondoggling wannabes who have a meeting every 10 minutes in oprder to
pretend they're actually achieving something.


> 2) COBOL will NEVER be a *major* player in the "user-side" of the web or
> server application environment. (Which is NOT to say that there won't be
> some new - as well as continued - applications that do this.)
>

It is at my place...<G>.

Again, there are a number of things COBOL is extremely useful for. Some of
these things relate to the future of computing in general and the Web in
particular. There is far too much to cover here and until somebody asks me,
I won't be saying anything on the subject. (Some of it is unpalatable to
certain people and I don't want to be accused of simply stirring, as has
happened in the past...<G>. Overall though, my view on the future of COBOL
is positive. Well, at least as positive as any other language...)

> 3) New "businesses" doing data processing or existing businesses creating
> NEW applications will *only* select COBOL as their "programming language
of
> choice" in infrequent and unusual circumstances.

They won't use ANY programming language. The days of "computer programming"
for businesses are almost over. It was a phenomenon of the latter half of
the 20th Century, just like cottage lace making in the early 19th century. I
have a lot to say about this...<G>

>
> 4) Sites with existing COBOL applications will increase their
> "consideration" of language conversions away from COBOL when they do
"major
> maintenance" or new functionality to those programs.
>

Only in the short to medium term. There are events in the pipeline that will
change everything very drastically. It is very hard for people raised on
COBOL and the Waterfall to get their heads around it. Basically, the whole
way in which we implement computer applications into business (and the home,
for that matter) will undergo a sea change.


> 5) Membership in J4 and WG4 will continue to "decrease" with NO new COBOL
> vendors joining J4; no new countries joining WG4;

God, I hope so! The sooner these people get off COBOL's back, the better for
everybody.

>and those doing the "real
> work" of the committees continuing to be limited to a VERY SMALL handful
of
> individuals (not vendors or user companies).

Yes. I don't want to throw the baby out with the bath water... There are
some very dedicated and hard-working people who have contributed to these
committees (your own contribution is not insignificant, Bill).
Unfortunately, due to a number of reasons which I won't go into here, the
achievements of this group were abysmal. My criticism is not directed at
individuals; it is directed at the whole edifice and its lack of
answerability, management, and, consequently, achievement.


>
> 6) There never will be robust  "certification tests" for the 2002 ISO
> Standard - and the US government (along with European and Asian
governmental
> bodies) will NEVER require "2002 conforming" compilers.  (And possibly
they
> will even - within the next 10 years - drop COBOL as a "authorized"
> programming language for new projects - in cases where they have a list of
> such authorized programming "languages" or "tools").
>

Who cares? Why does it matter? (I reckon it doesn't...)

>         ***
>
> Bottom-Line:
>   I will continue to "post" to newsgroups and others the information of
what
> is happening with "development" and "maintenance" of the COBOL language by
> various Standards groups.  I think it is important that all existing COBOL
> programmers "keep abreast" of what is or might be happening or going to
> happen.
>

Why is it "important" Bill? The average COBOL grunt working in the Fortress
has no realistic say or control over what the standard does, and
furthermore, it isn't high on his/her list of priorities. When s(he) has
problems with syntax, it isn't the Standard manual s(he) reaches for, it is
the vendor's implementation. The people on the shop floor, for the most
part, simply don't care, and there is no reason why they should. The only
people we see jumping up and down about Standards are people who are on the
committee or have a vested interest. (As it costs a considerable amount to
join the club, you can understand the Members being sensitive about getting
value...)

> OTOH,  I can't see - unless something DRAMATICALLY changes - that COBOL
will
> be seen as a GROWING programming environment.  Certainly the 30-year old
> question of "Is COBOL dead?" still deserves an answer of NO.  On the other
> hand, the question of "Is COBOL a dynamic/growing programming
environment?"
> seems to warrant an equally strong "NO" as an answer.
>

Yes, that is probably fair. But there are other factors that need to be
considered here. As I hinted above, the whole way in which we do things is
undergoing change. It isn't just about COBOL, although, sadly,  COBOL is one
of the least equipped to deal with it. (This is no fault of the vendors...)


> OBVIOUSLY, this post reflects PERSONAL OPINION only.  I wouldn't mind
being
> proven wrong - but I won't hold my breath either.
>

Amen to that <G>!

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-24T16:25:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ECFE34C.898BF0F8@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3eceb533_3@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <ban0i1$fdh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ecf74d9_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`

```
Let me offer an analogy.

Most of you more experienced types will have heard of PRIME computers, and
perhaps the associated INFOBASIC language.  Primemnever gained more than a tiny
percentage of the market and has been out of business for years.  The machines
were very solid and the O/S bulletproof (as long as the machine wasn't actually
malfunctioning); the language, although very labour-intensive and having some
very unusual ideas, allowed for a great deal of productivity; a 32-meg 5-mips
machine with 4-5 g of disk storage could support 60 users + batch processing
with 1-2 second response time.  However, they never secured more than a niche.
I'm  working with a company that is still using one!  The O/S & compiler are
dated 1992!  There is a company in Toronto that still finds it worthwhile to
offer hardware & software support for the Prime machines.  There is a conversion
method available, to IBM-blessed UNIVERSE/UNIDATA; quite a number of companies
that I know of went this route and are running their 1992-vintage applications
in what amounts to Infobasic to this day.

The point is that while COBOL may have been pronounced old, decrepit, or even
dead - given the sheer mass of current application written in Cobol it would
suprise me if there WEREN'T many of them still going 30 years from now!

Peter the Iconoclast of Pontefract
```

Peter Lacey wrote:

> offer hardware & software support for the Prime machines.  There is a
> conversion method available, to IBM-blessed UNIVERSE/UNIDATA; quite a

As I understand it, Pr1me (spelt with a 1 wasn't it?) used a version of 
Pick and Universe is a version of Pick (or a look-alike) running on Unix.

One of my clients uses a system in Universe and runs it on RedHat Linux.


> Peter the Iconoclast of Pontefract

Are you really in Pontefract amongst the fields of licorice ?  It's just 
that my daughter will be there next week.
