```


joe kotys wrote:

> I'm not sure about the 'real world', but I just finished a 1 year sequence
> of COBOL , (intro, and file-structures)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Nothing wrong with above style, if that's what you want to use - and it is
immediately understandable. (It is a bit reminiscent of C coding-style to
identify types of variables, and possibly the source of where your instructor
picked the idea from).

I forgot to comment on Daniel's :-

> and a number.
> > W338-Unit-ID is defined in W338-Online-UCOB-WSA, and P338-Traffic is
> > defined in P338-Online-UCOB-Proc.

There's probably a good reason for that, but it didn't turn me on. It means you
are having to constantly remember it is W338-etc, P338-etc. It's reminiscent of
a style posted a while back by somebody called David ???? where he was taught to
prefix every field with a complicated numbering system - doesn't make for easy
recall. Further, if the digit numbering system was sufficiently close, from one
variable  to another, it could possibly lead to some obscure bugs.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T14:56:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED27169.2040605@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <rWsAa.990723$F1.120104@sccrnsc04> <3ED26527.CB950852@shaw.ca>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> I forgot to comment on Daniel's :-
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> variable  to another, it could possibly lead to some obscure bugs.

I'm not too crazy about how reliant our system is on copy procs.  If I 
had to redesign the system tomorrow from scratch, a lot of the copy 
procs would be callable subprograms ("components", if you will). 
Nothing's more frustrating than getting fingers pointed at you because 
YOUR proc is screwing up - then you find out that THEIR program is 
clobbering your working-storage...  Encapsulation and information hiding 
are good things, IMO.

(One of the biggest reasons our programmers are so reliant on copy procs 
is that our current configuration management system does a very good job 
of tracking which programs copy in which procs, and if a proc is 
changed, a "recompile" control number is generated for each program. 
And, we currently don't keep copies of the intermediate object modules 
from our compiles, or keep a cross-reference of Call statements, so the 
mechanism isn't in place to ensure the configuration stays up-to-date. 
It's a custom CM system, so we've just got to write that functionality 
into it.  If we have another big project after this one, I think I'm 
going to try to make a lot of the common, system-level stuff be done in 
subprograms...)

And, with your talk about numbering, it can get interesting...  308 was 
the COBOL 74 init/trans-end proc, and 338 is the COBOL 85 version.  309 
and 339 are the same, but for background run programs.  This made it 
challenging for certain procs that were checking on things in their 
environment (i.e., W338-ELC, which represents the base on which the 
program is currently running).  So, you end up coding for one, then 
using the Replacing clause on the copy statements in the other.  Not the 
most elegant solution, IMO.

And, as far as having to remember, it's not really difficult - Wxxx is 
the variable, Pxxx is the paragraph.  :)

Of course, for all its shortcomings, we do have a good style which most 
programs loosely follow.  Many of the older programs are now being 
decommissioned, not just because they are old, but because the user's 
needs have changed.  And, like many people have said, you can always do 
your part to encourage positive change - most all my programs are very 
clean, well-commented, and should be easily maintainable by anyone who 
needs to, whether they really understand what they do in the big scope 
of things or not.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T00:28:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed28f8e.320140869@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <rWsAa.990723$F1.120104@sccrnsc04> <3ED26527.CB950852@shaw.ca> <3ED27169.2040605@Knology.net>`

```
LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:


>I'm not too crazy about how reliant our system is on copy procs.  If I 
>had to redesign the system tomorrow from scratch, a lot of the copy 
>procs would be callable subprograms ("components", if you will). 

That has been the trend for at least twenty years. In an earlier discussion
about COPY, several posters said they hadn't used procedural copybooks in years.
The last time I used them was in the early '80s.

>(One of the biggest reasons our programmers are so reliant on copy procs 
>is that our current configuration management system does a very good job 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>mechanism isn't in place to ensure the configuration stays up-to-date. 

Make them dynamically called. Then there's no NEED to keep track of
dependencies. There is but one copy of the called program's executable, and
everyone gets it automatically. 

I haven't done a static link (of more than one program) in at least fifteen
years  .. on platforms ranging from huge iron to PCs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T20:21:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED2BDAE.7000305@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <rWsAa.990723$F1.120104@sccrnsc04> <3ED26527.CB950852@shaw.ca> <3ED27169.2040605@Knology.net> <3ed28f8e.320140869@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> Make them dynamically called. Then there's no NEED to keep track of
> dependencies. There is but one copy of the called program's executable, and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> years  .. on platforms ranging from huge iron to PCs. 

I would LOVE to structure them that way.  (I'd get a lot less crap from 
everyone else, because when I change something, everyone else has to 
recompile.)  However, in the TIP (transaction) environment on the Unisys 
2200, dynamic linking is not supported.  I could (and may in the near 
future) structure our background report and utility programs to use 
dynamic linking.

It's kind of interesting, the environment I'm in.  I'll read about 
something, play with it, then either implement it or pitch it in such a 
way as it seems foolish not to implement it.  Not that I'm trying to 
brag or anything, but if it weren't for my "research programming" (a 
term I made up to describe "playing" in better terms), we'd still be in 
COBOL 74 because of a 23-line assemble routine that no one knew how to 
read.  I made a contact at Unisys, sent him the routine, and he told me 
what it did (and that we were foolish for having that routine, as there 
were built-in calls that did the same thing).

All the while, I'm still responsible for the system-level application 
code and structure upon which the rest of the system runs.  Am I the 
only one that has time for this?  Am I the only one that cares enough to 
crack a book and make a phone call or two until I understand something? 
  I came into the toughest section in our shop, and 9 people have come 
and gone from it in the time I've been there.  They never took the time 
to get into it and understand it.  Now, I'm the "go-to guy" (not the 
COBOL syntax ;> ), and I just don't get it.  Yes, I've picked up a lot 
of knowledge quickly.  But I haven't done anything that anyone else in 
that office couldn't do.

Sorry...  Didn't mean to get into a rant there.  Vacations give me too 
much time to think.  :)  Hope your Memorial Day has been a good one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T04:07:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED2DEEC.5558C32F@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <rWsAa.990723$F1.120104@sccrnsc04> <3ED26527.CB950852@shaw.ca> <3ED27169.2040605@Knology.net> <3ed28f8e.320140869@news.optonline.net> <3ED2BDAE.7000305@Knology.net>`

```
Daniel,

Have I had fun trying to respond to this one. Tried to snip but at soon as I got
to your "graphics" footer, Netscape goes belly-up.

I thought your rant was interesting, plus I'd forgotten the point you made
earlier, whether you are a sergeant doing your computer thing or cooking, it's
the same pay rate.

I left training as a Junior Technician, (because of the shorthand/typing), that's
one chevron with the point northwards. They brought in Technician ranks for
corporal, sergeant and flight sergeant in the various trades; I'm awfully vague,
but I think folks had to take written exams for each level. Back to your cooks -
I really can't visualize a Sergeant Technician in the cookhouse - they were
trained to poison people not feed them. You are conversing with somebody very
intimate with communal food <G>.

Anyway, my guess, that scheme probably flew out the window long ago. And I did
mention back some 30-35 years ago, (in the RAF at least) they only had
commissioned officers in analysis/programming. I'd put money on it, that in
Britain, military programmers now, for the most part, are civilians. No problem
you can just as easily security clear a civilian as you can somebody in uniform.

I was surprised when you said you team was 'uniformed'. Doesn't really make sense
to me. Hardly likely they will want to shuffle Daniel off to Iraq along with his
laptop to do new calibrations for the guns on an Abrams tank <G>. Anyway, if you
experienced a Middle Eastern sandstorm, they do wonders for things with moving
parts, including computers.

So exactly where do your "others" get their computer training and what were their
backgrounds that made them such innovative thinkers ? Wait until you hit civvy
street - it will be identical, those really switched on, with an eye to
innovation or new techniques and the plodders - and that doesn't just apply to
computing.

I left RAF at age 30 in September '61 - started right at the BOTTOM coding
friggin invoices to go into a punch card tabulator. Then there's a girl who has a
regular month end job, "Help her out", says the boss, "She's too busy to do it on
her own".
Next time around, "She has too much on her plate, so now do the whole of that
month end job". About six months later I am switched to preparing the Profit/Loss
statements for three depots. By about January '63 I'm Deputy Accountant, not for
a piddling company, but a national distributor of dairy products.

Our financial team was about eight people, many senior in age but all making up a
pretty good conscientious group. Believe me it wasn't my accounting or
mathematical skills that got me there.

(An Aside : Pete, after having made much effort I firmly memorized that 2.5% of a
pound is sixpence. Then by using Sherlockian deduction, I've got it figured that
1.25% of a pound has got to be a three penny piece. Voila ! There's hope yet
<G>).

What did it - as in your case - was inquisitiveness and an interest in 'systems',
(although that word had no meaning to me at the time). I got the hang of doing
certain jobs, but realized the same drudge each month was tedious. In a manual
environment I was recommending/introducing system changes to take short-cuts to
get the desired results. That led to my progression and eventually a transfer to
systems analysis when the company decided to get a 'magic box'.

Stick with it - be the innovator. You are planning a civilian career, and the
same enthusiasm will carry you through - don't ride rough-shod, do it
diplomatically, but even in civvy computing you will still beat the drones who
have been at it decades longer than you.

Jimmy

LX-i wrote:

> Robert Wagner wrote:
> > Make them dynamically called. Then there's no NEED to keep track of
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> Remove NOSPAM to e-mail me...
```

Thanks.  I try to be diplomatic...  I've found that people like "Hey, 
look at this, and here's how it works" better than "Hey, look at this, I 
just found it, wonder if it works?"  Backing up hypotheses with facts 
(as anyone who posts here knows) gets you a lot further than speculation 
alone.

heh...  Sure.  Part of it is the fact that people in our office now know 
how to use Evaluate to check for a number being in a given range.  Had I 
sent out something speculative, such as

"Hey, look!  Here's how to check the range a cool way!

Evaluate MyVariable
   When MyVariable > 0 Or < 10
     Do-This
   When Other
     Do-That
End-Evaluate"

...not only would I have given poor (and non-compiling) advice, but 
folks would be a lot less willing to believe what I would say in the 
future.  However, taking a snippet of code from a compiled, 
executing-correctly program, and stating what it did (and can do), 
people not only have confidence to try it themselves, they're more 
willing to believe me when I tell them other things as well.

:)  Are case studies considered facts?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T18:24:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED54517.5050602@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <rWsAa.990723$F1.120104@sccrnsc04> <3ED26527.CB950852@shaw.ca> <3ED27169.2040605@Knology.net> <3ed28f8e.320140869@news.optonline.net> <3ED2BDAE.7000305@Knology.net> <joe_zitzelberger-4E18F7.09141628052003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> In article <3ED2BDAE.7000305@Knology.net>,
>  LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> performance implications).

No.  The problem with TIP (well, it's not really a problem, more of a 
feature) is that they have files with "absolute" elements in them that 
are assigned to TIP exclusively.  I say it's a feature because what this 
allows is the TIP subsystem to load these very fast.

(A bit about modes - "Basic mode" absolutes, for the most part, are 
actually loaded to a TIP file in "Fast-Load Format".  (Basic mode 
encompasses the compilers that preceded the Universal Compiling System 
(UCS) family of compilers.)  "Extended mode" programs are compiled to 
"object modules", which then are linked to "Zero-Overhead Object 
Modules" (ZOOMs).  The difference between "object modules" and 
"relocatables" (the intermediate set in pre-UCS) is that the 
intermediate object module is executable (although most of the time it 
doesn't work).  The Link processor resolves all the external references 
(calls) and creates the ZOOM.  You can tell it to not resolve particular 
calls (which is what we would do), and these references are resolved at 
runtime.)

Now, that leads to what the problem is - once the object module is 
loaded into the TIP file, it cannot resolve to these other libraries. 
Our background programs could do it (and we may go to that soon, so we 
only have to re-release the onlines if we have a major subprogram change).

> Huge numbers of programmers just view it as a job -- something to pass 
> by on their way to management, or something else.  It takes a real geek 
> to enjoy the puzzles presented by different languages, environments and 
> problems.  You put the effort in -- that is something nobody else in 
> your office did do.

Thank y...  hey, did you just call me a geek?  ;)

>>Sorry...  Didn't mean to get into a rant there.  Vacations give me too 
>>much time to think.  :)  Hope your Memorial Day has been a good one.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> or something...

Well, there are several about alphabets - maybe we could convert that 
thread into a digest...  :)
```

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

On 26-May-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> That has been the trend for at least twenty years. In an earlier discussion
> about COPY, several posters said they hadn't used procedural copybooks in
> years.  The last time I used them was in the early '80s.

I use them more now than ever before.    Often they are IDMS COPY's for status
checking.   We certainly had Y2K conversion routines.  We use them for
paragraphs that call canned routines.

#### ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

- **From:** Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-26T21:22:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2924314.1053984152@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net>`

```

I  think it  is still valid, I  have been using since my first program,

and i Use this

select  arqcli  assign to xxxxxx

layout record is

01  regcli.

      03  cli-mainkey    pic (06)
      03  cli-name
      03  cli-zipcode
      etc

in Linkage section

01  lk-field01
      etc

in Work

01  ws-field01

      etc

in Power cobol

Working from the Event
01 ws-field1

Working from the Forms

01 wsg-field1  global  pic  9(06) .

Working  from the Projetc

01 wsge-filed1   Global external   pic 9(06)


In all my projects

when I  move cli-field   to  wsg-field  , I know
that  I want   to move a  field from File  to
Global working storage  and vice-versa

Its very easy to find out bugs in the Program.

very easy  to manage, copy routine, etc

I have programa running for 18 years and until now
its use  to make changes.

Carlos Lages
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How much cost the runtime of Cobol that you to use now?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T14:05:56+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed419d0_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<2926024.1054034533@dbforums.com> <bfdfc3e8.0305271450.160bf19f@posting.google.com> <D6TAa.135809$rt6.42181@sccrnsc02> <Yd6dnYOUofc5j0mjXTWJiQ@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:Yd6dnYOUofc5j0mjXTWJiQ@giganews.com...
>
> "Steve" <Steven_31@aol.com> wrote in message
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I have considered using Fujitsu - could you hazard a guess how many
people
> > in the Silicon Valley and greater Bay Area use Fujitsu to develop
Windows
> > applications ?
> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Fujitsu has a "Boot Camp" in Palo Alto if you want your new hires to hit
the
> ground running.
>

Exactly! Yet time and again we see people looking for compiler-specific
experience.

Any COBOL programmer should be able to work with any compiler after a day or
so at the most. (In my experience it is often more like minutes as Jerry
said.)

But the perception remains...we need people experienced with OUR compiler.

Just another argument reinforcing the futility of a COBOL standard.

A much better solution would be to hire people who HAVEN'T got the specific
experience at a slightly lower rate, then, after say, 3 months when they've
proved themselves and the company has had the benefit of the lower cost, pay
them what they're worth... But I guess that's too simple for most
Managers...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How much cost the runtime of Cobol that you to use now?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T22:30:15-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb16vn$9bs$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<2926024.1054034533@dbforums.com> <D6TAa.135809$rt6.42181@sccrnsc02> <Yd6dnYOUofc5j0mjXTWJiQ@giganews.com> <3ed419d0_8@news.athenanews.com>`

```
In article <3ed419d0_8@news.athenanews.com>,
Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>A much better solution would be to hire people who HAVEN'T got the specific
>experience at a slightly lower rate, then, after say, 3 months when they've
>proved themselves and the company has had the benefit of the lower cost, pay
>them what they're worth... But I guess that's too simple for most
>Managers...

Mr Dashwood, this reminds me of something I posted a while ago... ahhhh, 
there it is... wonderful thing, that Dejanews... errrr... Google, bringing 
back a posting from 30 Nov 1997:

<http://groups.google.com/groups?hl=en&lr=&ie=UTF-8&selm=348196F3.7691%40erols.com>

--begin quoted text:

Heresy!  It is a Well-Known Fact that if you give someone training they
will immediately jump ship to a higher-paying company because said
company is foolish enough to think that a person, trained, is more
valuable than a person, untrained... don't they know that Gratitude Is
Enough?

[snip]

Now, repeat after me: 'The responsibility for the allocation,
co-ordination and motivation of personnel and resources towards the
accomplishment of a stated Executive goal is that of Management.' 
Repeat again.

Let's look at it another way... assume that an organisation has invested
a great deal of money in the upgrading of the physical plant.  If
sufficient investment has been made then it is likely that serious
consideration will be given to upgrading the security system (new locks,
etc.) to prevent these improvement from 'walking off'.  Also consider
the common term of 'golden handcuffs', a recognised metaphor for
increasing salary/benefits to prevent human capital from *physically*
walking away.  Now, consider the investment of money in humans to
upgrade skills in order to make them more valuable to the company. 
Consider how many times you have seen a corporate policy stating that an
increase in salary/benefits accompanies the successful completion of
such an upgrade (courses).

Years ago the Wall Street Journal did a story on one of the major NY
houses... I think it was Morgan Stanley or Morgan Guaranty or the like. 
They hired *only* the 'unhireable'... kids with BAs in Library Science,
Art History, etc... they put these kids through two years of hell, 60 -
70 hr weeks, and turned them into *crackerjack* programmers... and then
saw said kids being hired away by the competition at double or triple
the salary.  When asked why a raise did not accompany the completion of
the course the HR representative replied 'Oh, we cannot do that... all
the money has been taken up by training.' (compare this with 'Oh, we
cannot upgrade the door-locks... all the money went into oil-paintings
to hang on the walls.')

After a few years of seeing themselves serve as Wall Street's unofficial
programming school the company finally 'wised up'... and cancelled the
program entirely.

--end quoted text

As the Germans say, Mr Dashwood, 'plus ca change, plus c'est la meme 
chose'... you know, Mr Wagner says he can see 'the obvious reason' why I 
have never worked in management... can you guess what it is?

DD
```

they've

pay

> >Managers...
>
<snipped post from 1997
>
> As the Germans say, Mr Dashwood, 'plus ca change, plus c'est la meme
> chose'... you know, Mr Wagner says he can see 'the obvious reason' why I
> have never worked in management... can you guess what it is?
>
I'm not going to speculate on why you're not a Manager Doc, but I believe
you would make a better Manager than many who claim that designation.

As for the lack of investment in training, you are right that it is as true
now as it was then, and not just in the USA. I have seen at first hand
Companies in the UK who simply won't train staff for fear they'll leave.
When I've questioned this and made the obvious statement that if you pay
them what they're worth they WON'T leave, I was given the argument: "Ah yes,
but it's cheaper NOT to pay them what they're worth and simply replace them
when they leave with lower grade people who will work harder to get
ahead...".

At this point I gave up...

Pete.
> DD
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How much cost the runtime of Cobol that you to use now?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T09:40:31-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb2e8f$d3g$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<2926024.1054034533@dbforums.com> <bb16vn$9bs$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed47a1e_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <KP0Ba.25806$%_3.10398702@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
In article <KP0Ba.25806$%_3.10398702@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
>news:3ed47a1e_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com...
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>which can 'do the math' and not be squeamish little hypocrites about it...

Mr Mattias, I wonder what 'math' was done except the most elementary.  If 
a good programmer costs more than a cheaper one but gets the code past QC 
on the second go-around instead of the fifth... which is cheaper?  Stories 
about management are things I seem to have addressed a while back... 
here's one I found from 11 Oct 1999:

<http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3801D722.4D98BFFD%40home.com&output=gplain>

--begin quoted text:

Mr Hamasaki, all due respect but I have found this Respected Tradition
to be on the wane over the past decade and change; it was replaced with
a dual chorus of 'Costs too much!' and 'We can't afford to make
mistakes, we do it right the Very First Time!'.  

A story:  years ago I was working in Hartford, CT, with a Major
Insurance Company rewriting their long-term disability management system
for a new database (IMS to DB2).  At one point early on I was called to
attend a meeting... this miserable, stinkwad, two-bit, gut-crawling
idiot who was so stupid he had to have his initials monogrammed on his
cuffs in case he forgot who he was announced that there would be *no*
period of parallel testing between the two systems.  Folks got a bit...
upset and he squelched their complaints by announcing 'Nope, no way,
costs too much money, the old system costs US$500,000 per month to run
and we're just going to cut over.'

In the silence which almost always follows the Mentioning of a Large Sum
of Money (note: I believed then, as now, that this sum was completely
fictitious; very few IMS systems written fifteen years ago cost this
much to run) I said 'That's a sum of money, true... has anyone
calculated how much it will cost to fix things when six months after
cutover folks discover that they've been pumping out bad checks for the
past half-year?'

'That's a good question,' he intoned, '... I'll get back to you on
that.'

I was never asked to attend a meeting with him ever again and I retained
that particular gem for future use.

--end quoted text

So, Mr Mattias... are they 'doing the math' or are they making up numbers 
to support their own short-termist views?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How much cost the runtime of Cobol that you to use now?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T09:30:42-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb2dm2$a3m$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
In article <3ed47a1e_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com>,
Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bb16vn$9bs$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…

Shucks, I'd blush, were I able to remember how... but the adage is that 
one should be careful of what one wishes for as one might get it.

>
>As for the lack of investment in training, you are right that it is as true
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>At this point I gave up...

How interesting... that is the point where I would just start to dig in 
and ask questions like;

'So what you're saying seems to be that you feel free to ignore the
lessons in staffing management which were proven true by Henry Ford nigh a
century back and that you think it is better that we turn out, under the
Bumfco name, a second-rate product produced by admittedly 'lower grade
people'.

[pause]

'Later on I'd be very interested in hearing how you reconcile hiring
'lower grade people' with the adage that 'you surround yourself with those
most like yourself'...  but first things first; what is it that you see us 
doing that innovatively violates almost a hundred years' worth of HR 
experience?'

DD
```

```


"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:

>
> As for the lack of investment in training, you are right that it is as true
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> At this point I gave up...

While it is *not* training, the same illogical thinking prevails about salaries
in other fields. One of the major oil companies in Calgary - a 25-year old
female accountant goes job hunting, not displeased with current environment, but
for fresh pastures, new experience.

She is successful and advises her employers she is leaving. "Puhleese don't
leave us ! We will increase your salary by $TEN THOUSAND". She laughs at them.
She didn't, but could have replied, "You bastards, you've been underpaying me
for the past 'x' years !".

Jimmy
```

deemed
best for their needs.

approach.

wsimmons5@cox.net

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Newark,NJ.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T00:07:23+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed749c6_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<2926024.1054034533@dbforums.com> <bb16vn$9bs$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ed47a1e_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <GvBBa.53696$%42.8313@fed1read06> <bb7ege$kcp$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bb7ege$kcp$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <GvBBa.53696$%42.8313@fed1read06>,
> Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@cox.net> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

LOL!

Some years back I made the mistake of taking a short "working holiday" in
New York. (It was two weeks and strictly speaking, illegal, as I didn't have
the necessary Visa to do it... I haven't done it since.)

However, I enjoyed New York, met some amazing characters (one of whom tried
to persuade me to start a "programming school" with him, the idea being to
rip off minorities, give them all a certificate, and then claim the City
subsidy for educating the under-priveleged...<G> "We can charge them 100
bucks just to do the aptitood test!" He couldn't understand why I declined
politely but figured I just didn't recognise a good thing when I saw
it...<G>)

 Anyway, I had never heard of Newark, NJ, but on the way home (London at the
time) the flight was diverted from Kennedy to Newark due to a bomb scare or
a fuel strike or something, I can't remember what. I was pleased to find
that Newark was not a long distance from NY and cabbed it easily to the
airport (another experience...<G>). I bought the customary large bottle of
Jack Daniels duty free and put all my stuff into checked luggage (it was 2
bags, one of which was mainly manuals and paperwork).

Arrived at London Heathrow and found my luggage was still in Newark! They
had become "confused" because the flight (TWA) was originally from
Kennedy... So I walked through customs with only a bottle of JD. One of the
customs officers said to me: "You're travelling light, Sir, where have you
come from?" I said: "Newark, New Jersey." He looked at the forlorn
expression on my face (losing all your stuff is no joke; I wasn't
smiling...), looked at the bottle of JD and said: "Ah, Newark...I understand
Sir..." Waved me on with a sympathetic smile...

Footnote: I wouldn't want people to get the wrong idea about TWA from this.
My bags were delivered to my flat in London the next day, with an apology.
Any airline can lose your luggage; it takes a good one to find it and make
it right...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How much cost the runtime of Cobol that you to use now?

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T07:12:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0305280612.64b0f0da@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2926024.1054034533@dbforums.com> <bfdfc3e8.0305271450.160bf19f@posting.google.com> <D6TAa.135809$rt6.42181@sccrnsc02>`

```
I don't know the answer to that - but I can offer some advice.

First - consider not limiting your search to that geographic area. 
With the depressed state of affairs there, many people I know have
left for greener pastures.  Consider telecommuters and contractors. 
Since you are talking Windows development there is likely little need
to offer constant networked access to your systems.

Second - I use a tool from flexus (http://www.flexus.com) for my
COBOL/Windows development.  It is compiler independent - so it doesnt
really matter which COBOL you used - the Windows aspects are the same.
 I do tech support as well for Flexus and support persons using every
PC based COBOL compiler.  Finding an sp2 familiar programmer will be a
lot easier than finding a Fujitsu/Windows programmer.

Lastly - you might ask Fujitsu!


"Steve" <Steven_31@aol.com> wrote in message news:<D6TAa.135809$rt6.42181@sccrnsc02>...
> Thane,
> 
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > And yours?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How much cost the runtime of Cobol that you to use now?

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T11:19:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed5eb72.49758621@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2926024.1054034533@dbforums.com> <bfdfc3e8.0305271450.160bf19f@posting.google.com> <D6TAa.135809$rt6.42181@sccrnsc02>`

```
"Steve" <Steven_31@aol.com> wrote:

>I have considered using Fujitsu - could you hazard a guess how many people
>in the Silicon Valley and greater Bay Area use Fujitsu to develop Windows
>applications ?
>
>I'd like to know how large the pool of 'hirable' talent is.

COBOL is a standardized language. Fujitsu COBOL is little different from Micro
Focus, IBM or any other brand. Knowing COBOL, and in your case knowing some GUI
tool, is more important than knowing Fujitsu.
```

#### ↳ Re: How much cost the runtime of Cobol that you to use now?

- **From:** hhinman <member30421@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T07:29:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2939087.1054279778@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2926024.1054034533@dbforums.com>`

```

I really don't understand how anyone  in good conscience could recommend
using the Realia compiler today...!  Realia was a very good product in
it's day (the 1980's), but it has died a slow death over the past 10
years or so after being sold to Pansophic and then Pansophic to C.A. The
guiding force behind Realia (Marc Sokol) who is a great person, is no
longer involved with  it as I understand, althoughhe has moved on to
bigger and  better things at C.A. And I have helped serveral customers
move off of their old Realia compilers who claimed nothing has been done
to enhance the product to work with newer Windows operating systems
effectively. Please correct me if I'm misinformed, as I strongly desire
to keep abreast of all developments with all COBOL compilers. But from
the number of customers I've seen bailing off of Realia over the past
few years, I would be strongly surprised if I'm incorrect.
-Howard
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How much cost the runtime of Cobol that you to use now?

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T07:56:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb7kd301vot@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<2926024.1054034533@dbforums.com> <2939087.1054279778@dbforums.com>`

```

"hhinman" <member30421@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:2939087.1054279778@dbforums.com...
>
> I really don't understand how anyone  in good conscience
could recommend
> using the Realia compiler today...!  Realia was a very good
product in
> it's day (the 1980's), but it has died a slow death over the
past 10
> years or so after being sold to Pansophic and then Pansophic
to C.A. The
> guiding force behind Realia (Marc Sokol) who is a great
person, is no
> longer involved with  it as I understand, althoughhe has
moved on to
> bigger and  better things at C.A. And I have helped serveral
customers
> move off of their old Realia compilers who claimed nothing
has been done
> to enhance the product to work with newer Windows operating
systems
> effectively. Please correct me if I'm misinformed, as I
strongly desire
> to keep abreast of all developments with all COBOL compilers.
But from
> the number of customers I've seen bailing off of Realia over
the past
> few years, I would be strongly surprised if I'm incorrect.
> -Howard

A glancing experience that's relevant:

About a year and a half ago I was shopping for a new COBOL
compiler -- mbp/Visual Cobol was being discontinued, and my
group needed a supported replacement.  It took a couple of
weeks to find someone at CA that had even heard of Realia.
(Ultimately, someone in this group got me hooked up with one of
its developers.)

As for the sales staff, though, they pretty much treated me
like they would be doing me a favor in selling me 3 licenses.
By the time I got the trial version (weeks later,) I had no
time to evaluate it.  Given their attitude, though, the
decision was pretty much already made.

A couple of months after that, Sanjay Kumar spammed me with a
note about how CA was going to take care of me.  I computed his
real email and replied with an "are you kidding?" message that
included a brief description of my experiences.  To my
surprise, he wrote me back want to know the details, which I
provided.  I'm guessing that after discovering that I was worth
less then $10,000 in fees, the matter was dropped.
```

You're right. We HAD a lousy sales effort on the Advantage CA-Realia

CA is changing. We will shortly be introducing on-line interactive

and all components, whether you purchased them or not are

Charles Godwin

##### ↳ ↳ Realia Compier - Was How much cost the runtime....

- **From:** charles.godwin@ca.com (Charles Godwin)
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T11:09:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8035ce7.0305301009.2e141ab1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2926024.1054034533@dbforums.com> <2939087.1054279778@dbforums.com>`

```
hhinman <member30421@dbforums.com> wrote in message news:<2939087.1054279778@dbforums.com>...
> I really don't understand how anyone  in good conscience could recommend
> using the Realia compiler today...!  Realia was a very good product in
> it's day (the 1980's), but it has died a slow death over the past 10
> years or so after being sold to Pansophic and then Pansophic to C.A.
 <== text deleted ==>
Howard

You asked to be corrected. I'll do my best.

Marc Sokol has indeed moved on to new things. He left CA several years
ago.

The Realia compiler is fully compatible with all current Windows
Operating systems. The latest Generally Available (GA) version is
officially named Advantage CA-Realia II Workbench Release 3.2 (WB).
The WB has been fully tested with all available Windows versions
including Windows XP. It works fully with all Windows features. Realia
COBOL is the compiler / runtime of choice for several third party
developers who rely on COBOL for their livelihood. These developers
have written Windows GUI apps, COM+ controls and other tools using our
technology. They have paid for their software and annual support and
do not rely on CA providing complementary copies. They do this so they
can demand support and deal with CA as their clients would. The WB
must be capable or I would have heard about it a long time ago.

I do agree with you that the WB does not provide all the Windows
development tools available from other COBOL vendors. There is a
reason for this. Our market target is the mainframe shop looking for
tools to help with offloading development and to help downsize
mainframe application. We rely on the availability of excellent tools
from third party developers for the GUI Applications, Print Formatters
and other windows specific features. Computer Associates has an active
development team who are now working on the next release and we also
have an experienced and capable support group.

I would like to hear from you or anyone else in this group who can
identify failings of our current WB. I'm referring to technical
shortcomings or bugs that stop development, I'm not looking for a
feature by feature comparison of the WB to products offered by other
vendors.

Charles Godwin
Computer Associates
Development Manager
Advantage CA-Realia II Workbench
Email: Charles.Godwin@ca.com
```

Respected Gentlemen,

I have a FD with the following characteristics:

01 Registro.
05 chave-1.
10 campo-1 pic 9(8)v99 comp-3.
10 campo-2 pic x(20).
05 data-nasc pic 9(8).
05 data-nasc-r redefines data-nasc.
10 ano-mes.
15 ano pic 9(4).
15 mes pic 9(2).
10 dia pic 9(2).

If by chance I lost my coopbooks, I would have as recovering that FD, in
the way written above, starting from my file.DAT?

how?

Respectfully

Marcos Antonio de Souza
Brasil
```

               10  IDXF-DURATION                    PIC S9(9)  COMP.
               10  IDXF-USAGE-TYPE-FWD              PIC X(3).
                   88 IDXF-CBR-FWD                  VALUE "CBR".
                   88 IDXF-VBR-FWD                  VALUE "VBR".
                   88 IDXF-UBR-FWD                  VALUE "UBR".
               10  IDXF-USAGE-TYPE-BWD              PIC X(3).
                   88 IDXF-CBR-BWD                  VALUE "CBR".
                   88 IDXF-VBR-BWD                  VALUE "VBR".
                   88 IDXF-UBR-BWD                  VALUE "UBR".
               10  IDXF-TRFF-VOLUME-FWD             PIC S9(9)  COMP.
               10  IDXF-TRFF-VOLUME-BWD             PIC S9(9)  COMP.
               10  IDXF-VOLUME-USAGE-FWD            PIC S9(9)  COMP.
               10  IDXF-VOLUME-USAGE-BWD            PIC S9(9)  COMP.
               10  IDXF-CALL-REF                    PIC 9(5).
               10  IDXF-SEQ-NUM                     PIC 9(10).
               10  IDXF-USER-PLANE                  PIC X(1).
               10  IDXF-TAGGING-FWD                 PIC X(1).
               10  IDXF-TAGGING-BWD                 PIC X(1).
               10  IDXF-REJ-CODE                    PIC X(1).
               10  IDXF-STATUS                      PIC X(1).
                   88 IDXF-REJ-PENDING              VALUE "P".
                   88 IDXF-REJ-RECYCLE              VALUE "R".
                   88 IDXF-REJ-DELETE               VALUE "D".
      *
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      *
       77 ERROR-FLAG-1-W PIC X VALUE 'N'.
       01  WS-IDXF-KEY.
               10  WS-IDXF-CHRG-ID                     PIC X(16).
               10  WS-IDXF-START-DATE                  PIC S9(8) COMP-3.
               10  WS-IDXF-START-TIME                  PIC S9(6) COMP-3.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN-PARA.
           PERFORM OPEN-FILES     THRU  OPEN-FILES-EXIT.
           MOVE "N" TO ERROR-FLAG-1-W.
           PERFORM START-PARA     THRU  START-PARA-EXIT.
           IF ERROR-FLAG-1-W = "N"
           PERFORM PROCESS-PARA  THRU  PROCESS-EXIT.
           PERFORM CLOSE-PARA.
           STOP RUN.
       OPEN-FILES.
            OPEN  I-O  IDXFILE-INC.
      *
       OPEN-FILES-EXIT.
            EXIT.
       START-PARA.
            MOVE  LOW-VALUE        TO       IDXF-KEY.
            START IDXFILE-INC KEY NOT LESS THAN IDXF-KEY
                  INVALID KEY DISPLAY "** No record found: AT05 "

            MOVE "Y" TO ERROR-FLAG-1-W.
                DISPLAY "AFTER START", IDXF-CHRG-ID.
                DISPLAY IDXF-START-DATE.
                DISPLAY IDXF-START-TIME.

       START-PARA-EXIT.
           EXIT.

       PROCESS-PARA.
           MOVE "008521972590023F" TO WS-IDXF-CHRG-ID.
           MOVE 20030419 TO WS-IDXF-START-DATE.
           MOVE 185546 TO WS-IDXF-START-TIME.
                   MOVE WS-IDXF-KEY to IDXF-KEY.
      *    READ IDXFILE-INC NEXT RECORD
      *         AT END
      *         GO TO PROCESS-EXIT.
                DISPLAY  "AFTER READ", IDXF-CHRG-ID.
                DISPLAY IDXF-START-DATE.
                DISPLAY IDXF-START-TIME.
           DELETE IDXFILE-INC RECORD
            INVALID KEY
                    DISPLAY IDXF-CHRG-ID
                    DISPLAY IDXF-START-DATE
                    DISPLAY  IDXF-START-TIME
               DISPLAY "DELETE IDXFILE-REC ERROR "
                       ", KEY : " IDXF-KEY.

       PROCESS-EXIT.
           EXIT.
       CLOSE-PARA.
           CLOSE IDXFILE-INC.


Regds..Shyam

## Thread: Checking if Directori Exists in Power — 4 msgs

### Checking if Directori Exists in Power

- **From:** Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T20:58:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2928560.1054069086@dbforums.com>`

```

hi.

 01 wsg-dadosdoarquivo is global.
     03 wsg-nomeatestar    PIC X(255).
     03 wsg-name-length    PIC 9(4) BINARY.
     03 wsg-status-code     PIC S9(4) COMP-5.

     move    Pow-Text  of wpath  to wsg-filename.

     move  wsg-filename   to wsg-nomeatestar
     CALL "CBL_READ_DIR"
           USING    by reference
                    wsg-nomeatestar
                    wsg-name-length
           RETURNING wsg-status-code.

     if  wsg-status-code  not = zeros


if the Directory exist, wsg-status-code Should be ZEROS

this routine  always return wsg-status-code =  -0001

where is my mistake.

by the way  ui have already check the help and Books
and  I did not see anything suspect.

Tks

Carlos Lages
```

#### ↳ Re: Checking if Directori Exists in Power

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T20:34:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8tidnQMzwLa-j0mjXTWJig@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<2928560.1054069086@dbforums.com>`

```

"Carlos lages" <member129@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:2928560.1054069086@dbforums.com...
>
> hi.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> and  I did not see anything suspect.

---------

You've got the parameters reversed (and you don't need "by reference").

CALL "CBL_READ_DIR" USING
    wsg-name-length
   wsg-nomeatestar
   RETURNING wsg-status-code.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Checking if Directori Exists in Power

- **From:** Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T10:44:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2930547.1054118693@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2928560.1054069086@dbforums.com> <8tidnQMzwLa-j0mjXTWJig@giganews.com>`

```

I checked and doesnt work
Now all status is +0000 ,
by the way Fujistu manual instrution below

Parameter data definition
01 path-name PIC X(n).
01 path-name-length PIC 9(4) BINARY.
01 status-code PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
80 Chapter 4. File Routines
Calling format
CALL "CBL_READ_DIR"
USING path-name
path-name-length
RETURNING status-code.

Carlos Lages[


You've got the parameters reversed (and you don't need "by reference").

CALL "CBL_READ_DIR" USING
    wsg-name-length
   wsg-nomeatestar
   RETURNING wsg-status-code. 
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Checking if Directori Exists in Power

- **From:** Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T21:38:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2933379.1054157929@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2928560.1054069086@dbforums.com> <8tidnQMzwLa-j0mjXTWJig@giganews.com>`

```

I found out where my mistake was.

CBL_read_dir  , reads the current directory only

I  thought  it  reads any directory
Fujtisu manual is not clear about this

in other words , if you want to check i a Directory exists
you must use CBL_CHANGE_DIR

if was changed , OK
 if was not , Not OK

Carlos Lages
```

## Thread: Cobol Expo — 1 msgs

### Cobol Expo

- **From:** earranga@objectz.com (Ed Arranga)
- **Date:** 2003-05-27T22:59:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb17fd11.0305272159.487751f9@posting.google.com>`

```
I regret that Cobol Expo 2003! has been put on hold. Unfortunately,
this action was necessary due to a number of factors. We have been
working diligently to put together another quality Cobol Expo
conference bringing together many of the elements necessary to achieve
the same high level of substance in education, training, and
networking as we had in Chicago last year.
 
We believe the Cobol community deserves a comprehensive top-notch
conference. Given the circumstances we will not be able to provide the
type of quality we feel is necessary to build upon the foundation of
last year.
 
Thank you and please accept my apologies for the inconvenience caused.
 
Best,
Edmund Arranga
```

<a id="t-629ec24fa3"></a>
## Thread: OT - "ONE" and grammar — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - "ONE" and grammar

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T02:07:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb1n7s$i52$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Should anyone care ...  (or to put it another way)
 If one IS interested in the topic, ...

Grammatically, the pronoun "ONE" is third person singular.  Hence, it is
correct to say

   One is often surprised by ...

If it were first person plural (as suggested elsewhere), the it would be
correct to say (instead),

   One are often surprised by ...

Similarly, the correct and corresponding possessive pronoun is "one" or
possibly even "his" (or in a politically correct environment his or her).
Hence it is correct to say,

    One is often worried about his or her own concerns ... (informal
American usage)
        or
    One is often worried about one's own concerns ... (more "correct" usage)

rather than (again if it were first person plural),

   One are often worried about our own concerns ...

    ***

Having spoken about its GRAMMATICAL definition,  it is also true that it is
often used with a "first person" (either singular or plural) semantic
meaning.  (In an earlier generation of "proper" British English, the use of
the pronoun "ONE" to replace the pronoun "I" is medium-common.)  One also
finds it used with a second person "semantic" meaning.

For various "interesting" web discussions of "one", see:

 http://www.bartleby.com/64/C001/043.html

http://webster.commnet.edu/grammar/one.htm

http://www.fortunecity.com/bally/durrus/153/gramch19.html
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - "ONE" and grammar

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T20:40:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305281940.1059b594@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb1n7s$i52$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote

> Grammatically, the pronoun "ONE" is third person singular.  Hence, it is
> correct to say

Surely as an indefinite pronoun (as distinct from a personal pronoun)
it doesn't actually _have_ a 'person' at all.

> http://webster.commnet.edu/grammar/one.htm

I particularly liked:

"""As a pronoun, one can also function in an impersonal, objective
manner, standing for the writer or for all people who are like the
writer or for the average person or for all people who belong to a
class."""
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - "ONE" and grammar

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T22:44:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305282144.9168c00@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb1n7s$i52$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote 

> Should anyone care ...  (or to put it another way)
>  If one IS interested in the topic, ...

Not really, but:
 
> Grammatically, the pronoun "ONE" is third person singular.  Hence, it is
> correct to say

Grammatically it is not 'third person' at all but is an indefinite
pronoun (and thus not any 'person' which apply only to personal
pronouns):

"""indefinite pronouns:

  one    ... """

You may have taken:

"""All of the pronouns listed above take verbs in the third person
singular."""

to mean that it is third person, but it only says it uses the same
form of the verbs as third person personal pronouns do.
 
> If it were first person plural (as suggested elsewhere), the it would be
> correct to say (instead),

What _I_ actually said is:

>> 'One' is first person inclusive and singular.  It is used to
indicate
>> 'we' (inclusive) but individually one at a time.

That is to say that it is singular and 'inclusive of the first
person'.  It is indefinite (which I didn't actually say, but I thought
everyone knew that ;-) and thus (potentially) refers to a group.

      One must understand one's limitations.

May mean:

      I must understand my limitations.      
      You must understand your limitations.      
      He must understand his limitations.

or some or all of these at the same time for some group.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT - "ONE" and grammar

- **From:** "Steve" <Steven_31@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T18:32:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dnsBa.1035082$F1.123336@sccrnsc04>`
- **References:** `<bb1n7s$i52$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0305282144.9168c00@posting.google.com>`

```
but one must not forget the ramifications of a systematic reciprocal
argument when modifying a participatory response from the analogous
preponderance of the optimal transitional contingency


"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0305282144.9168c00@posting.google.com...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote
>
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> or some or all of these at the same time for some group.
```

## Thread: Is there a FAQ available? Question: Convert PC MF-Cobol .DAT files to text, knowing record length — 2 msgs

### Is there a FAQ available? Question: Convert PC MF-Cobol .DAT files to text, knowing record length

- **From:** AXM26 <axm26@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T10:46:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED491AB.2030504@hotmail.com>`

```
Hi,

I don't see a FAQs message so I apologize in advance if this is a frequently
asked question. Please send me a link to a FAQs list or a FAQs list in that
case.

Now to my question:
I have a large set (about 70) data files created by a set of MF-Cobol PC/DOC
programs. Each data file contains fixed length records and I know the record
length for each one. I do not care for the internal structure as long as I
can extract the data from the file in plain ASCII text, 1 record per line.

I know that there are issues regarding "packed" numbers, etc. and am not quite
sure how to deal with those. However, I will be fine if I can get the numeric
data even in a form such as 999999- etc.

I would prefer to use a tool if one is available but I can do the conversion
using a scripting (Perl/PHP etc.) or a programming language (C/C++/Java)
myself if someone can provide me the methodology. I cannot use Cobol for
the task, however...

I will greatly appreciate any help,


Thank You.
```

#### ↳ Re: Is there a FAQ available? Question: Convert PC MF-Cobol .DAT filesto text, knowing record length

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T07:22:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED5B561.85CEB5EE@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3ED491AB.2030504@hotmail.com>`

```
AXM26 wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> case.

If you had googled for old messages you would have found the
following:

the full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

The FAQ is pretty large.


> 
> Now to my question:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> data even in a form such as 999999- etc.

You need to know the internal structure of each record in order to
decode any packed decimal numbers that may be present.  There may be
numeric fields coded in BCD, or in some semblance of IBM
packed-decimal (similar to BCD but includes a sign nybble in the
low-order byte), or in binary (either big-endian or little-endian, in
various lengths, probably 1, 2, or 4 bytes).  If the file has multiple
record types, you may not be able to count on finding structured
numeric data at the same displacement in every record.

> 
> I would prefer to use a tool if one is available but I can do the conversion
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank You.

There's guy (actually Ed Guy) who periodically mentions a conversion
tool that might work with this kind of data:

"If you don't mind doing the conversion on a Windows machine you could
look at
ParseRat (http://www.parserat.com/)."

I don't think it is free, but it is inexpensive.

Good Luck!
```

---

## Thread: Urgent : 114 error again — 10 msgs

### Urgent : 114 error again

- **From:** benhayoune <member30388@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-28T17:32:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2932066.1054143128@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi all,

Micro Focus/MERANT Server Express 2.0.11 on AIX 4.3.3.0

I need your help with the 114 error which appears during the execution
of internal sorts (SORT statement) in cobol programs :

Execution error : file 'extsm'
error code: 114, pc=0, call=1, seg=0
114     Attempt to access item beyond bounds of memory (Signal 11)

The instruction causing the problem is as follows :

SORT  FWORK ASCENDING TYPE-SD AGCE-SD PRET-SD
INPUT PROCEDURE LEC-PRETS
OUTPUT PROCEDURE EDITION.

Did someone get this error during sorts.

Any issue will be very appreciated.

I am waiting for your replies.

Thank you in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: Urgent : 114 error again

- **From:** hhinman <member30421@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T07:34:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2939088.1054280043@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2932066.1054143128@dbforums.com>`

```

Just a long shot as  I haven't worked with Micro focus  the past several
years, but try turning on the compiler directive "NOBOUNDS" if it is
valid and not already the default in Server Express. This used to tell
the Micro Focus  runtime system not to check for bounds exceptions. It
might get you past the error. NOBOUNDS used to be the default as I
remember, but maybe they've switched it around....
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Urgent : 114 error again

- **From:** benhayoune <member30388@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T21:21:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2942015.1054329695@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2939088.1054280043@dbforums.com>`

```

Thank you very much for your replies.

The problem was in the declaration of the file FWORK used in the sort
statement :

SORT  FWORK ASCENDING TYPE-SD AGCE-SD PRET-SD
INPUT PROCEDURE LEC-PRETS
OUTPUT PROCEDURE EDITION.

It was declared like that :

FWORK      ASSIGN      FFE
ORGANIZATION SEQUENTIAL
STATUS XZ-STATUS RESERVE NO.

I suppressed the RESERVE NO option and the program executes without
any problem.

I would like to mention that this is an Oracle PRO*COBOL program. It
could be a server express bug or an Oracle one.

Thanks again.


Originally posted by hhinman 
Just a long shot as  I haven't worked with Micro focus  the past several
years, but try turning on the compiler directive "NOBOUNDS" if it is
valid and not already the default in Server Express. This used to tell
the Micro Focus  runtime system not to check for bounds exceptions. It
might get you past the error. NOBOUNDS used to be the default as I
remember, but maybe they've switched it around.... 
```

- **References:** `<2932066.1054143128@dbforums.com>`

knowledgable folks out there

I tried running a simple cobol program even after setting shlib_path
,cobdir, and ld_library_path still got the error below error.Some
cobol programs seem to run OK but in  production you never know what
you may run into
Merant cobol 2.0.1.1

Here is my program

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
DATA DIVISION.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
PARA.
DISPLAY "HELLO WORLD!!!!".

#### ↳ Re: /usr/lib/dld.sl: Can't find path for shared library: libcobccsupport.sl

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T11:25:23+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb4mtq$p6t$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<2fa13ee7.0305281208.283d3715@posting.google.com>`

```

"Vincento Harris" <wumutek@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:2fa13ee7.0305281208.283d3715@posting.google.com...
> I am pretty new to Cobol and would appreciate your the  help of the
> knowledgable folks out there
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> you may run into

Hi Vincento.

On UNIX the environment variable names -- amongst other things -- are case
sensitive.

You need to ensure COBDIR is set to the root directory of the COBOL install,
with PATH set to include $COBDIR/bin, and SHLIB_PATH (or LD_LIBRARY_PATH for
64-bit execution) to include $COBDIR/lib .

Simon.
```

export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/opt/lib/cobol/lib:${LD_LIBRARY_PATH}(I got 64 bit)
and the error did not seem to go away

I am missing something?
Thanks again

Vince

```
Hi Vince.

If you invoke 'mfsupport' from the command-line, it will create a text file,
mfpoll, in the current directory. If you could run that and post the mfpoll
back, I can take a quick look to see what may be wrong.

Simon.
```

# @(#)liblist.hp.app	3.14
# Make sure linker flags passed to CC are prefixed by '-Wl,'.
*:*:C:handoff_prfx(-Wl,)
# Specification for linking to executable

x:*:s:${LD:-ld}
x:*:C:${CCOO:-aCC}
x:*:a:-o

x:*:t:-lcobrts_t
x:*:a:-lcobcrtn

#	Add user plus options here:

#	Export symbols from executable (so that can be ref'd by shared
objs):
x:2:s:-E
x:2:s:-c
x:2:s:hidefile(-h %s)
x:2:C:-Wl,-E
x:2:C:-Wl,-c
x:2:C:hidefile(-h %s)
#       Make sure linked executables look after the SHLIB_PATH
x:2:s:+s
x:2:C:-Wl,+s
#       Make sure null addresses are trapped
x:2:s:-z
x:2:C:-Wl,-z
#	More emulation of cc (MUST be after MF/user libraries):
# 2:s:-Qy
#	OS libraries, as per cc:
x:*:t:-lpthread
x:2:s:-lcl
x:*:s:-lc
x:*:s:-ldld
x:*:sg:/opt/langtools/lib/end.o
# Specification for linking to shared object (shared library, CSO and
scCSO)

z:*:s:${LD:-ld}
z:*:C:${CCOO:-aCC}
z:*:C:+Z
z:*:a:-o
z:*:a:outfile
# Link to shared object
z:*:a:-b
#       Make sure shared libs look after the SHLIB_PATH
z:*:s:+s
z:*:C:-Wl,+s
#       Make sure null addresses are trapped
z:*:s:-z
z:*:C:-Wl,-z
#       No "-h" version numbering required for callable shared
objects.
#       Allow library version numbering:
z:*:sh:+h
z:*:sh:outfile
z:*:Ch:-Wl,+h
z:*:Ch:-Wl,outfile
#       Setup initialization/finalization routine
z:2:s:+I_mFsoinitfini
z:2:C:-Wl,+I_mFsoinitfini
#       Export all symbols _but_ hide symbols that don't want to be 
#       exported - eg. -d stubs and _mFldhandle:
z:2:s:-E
z:2:s:-c
z:2:s:hidefile(-h %s)
z:2:C:-Wl,-E
z:2:C:-Wl,-c
z:2:C:hidefile(-h %s)
#       Localize all references within the library if possible
z:2:s:-Bsymbolic
z:2:s:-Bimmediate
z:2:C:-Wl,-Bsymbolic
z:2:C:-Wl,-Bimmediate
z:2:y:-L.
#	Cobol libraries required for self contained callable shared objects:
z:*:y:-L%COBDIR/lib
z:*:y!t:-lcobrts
z:*:yt:-lcobrts_t
z:*:y:-lcobcrtn
z:*:y!t:-lcobmisc
z:*:yt:-lcobmisc_t

#       Add user plus options here:
z:*:a:plusopt

# End of file liblist.hp.app

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: /usr/lib/dld.sl: Can't find path for shared library: libcobccsupport.sl

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T13:51:53+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb7jta$3qf$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<2fa13ee7.0305281208.283d3715@posting.google.com> <bb4mtq$p6t$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <2fa13ee7.0305290752.39c3cd54@posting.google.com> <bb5b3m$r69$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <2fa13ee7.0305300353.48c56262@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Vince.

Thanks for sending on the mfpoll content -- I can now see the issue, which
appears to be a mismatch of environment variable settings and your working
mode (i.e. 32-bit or 64-bit).

In 32-bit mode, the OS dynamic loader will search SHLIB_PATH, yet in 64-bit
mode, the dynamic loader searches LD_LIBRARY_PATH. Although your setting of
LD_LIBRARY_PATH includes $COBDIR/lib, SHLIB_PATH does not.

Assuming you want to work in 32-bit mode, adding $COBDIR/lib to SHLIB_PATH
will resolve your problem.

Regards.

Simon.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: /usr/lib/dld.sl: Can't find path for shared library: libcobccsupport.sl

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** wumutek@yahoo.com (Vincento Harris)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2fa13ee7.0305301009.709ea8d0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2fa13ee7.0305281208.283d3715@posting.google.com> <bb4mtq$p6t$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <2fa13ee7.0305290752.39c3cd54@posting.google.com> <bb5b3m$r69$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <2fa13ee7.0305300353.48c56262@posting.google.com> <bb7jta$3qf$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

cob32: error(s) in compilation: hello.cob


Is there something else I need to set to run on 32 bit 

Thanks a lot for your  effort
Vince
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: /usr/lib/dld.sl: Can't find path for shared library: libcobccsupport.sl

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T19:34:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED7F88C.60506@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<2fa13ee7.0305281208.283d3715@posting.google.com> <bb4mtq$p6t$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <2fa13ee7.0305290752.39c3cd54@posting.google.com> <bb5b3m$r69$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <2fa13ee7.0305300353.48c56262@posting.google.com> <bb7jta$3qf$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <2fa13ee7.0305301009.709ea8d0@posting.google.com>`

```
Vincento Harris wrote:
> Is there something else I need to do to run on 32 bit mode?
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> parts of this thread was run  instead I got

I don't think this has anything to do with bits...  Did you start all 
your stuff in column 8?  It looks like you might have something like this...

v col 1
IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       ^ col 7

 >      1FICATION DIVISION.
 > *1001-E
 >       **
 > **    Character other than *, D, /, -, or $ found in column 7. Blank
 > assumed.

Notice column 7 is the first one displayed.  Columns 1-6 are number area 
(ignored), and column 7 is the "continuation / comment area".  Instead, 
try...

v col 1
         IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
         ^ col 8

And, once you get into the procedure division, only paragraph names need 
to start in 8 - the other statement must start in column 12 or greater.

Of course, if your compiler has a "free-form" option, you could just 
turn that option on.  :)
```

FYI - whether or not IDENTIFICATION DIVISION must start in column 8 (or
later) - i.e. the "A-margin" depends (with Micro Focus and several other
compilers) upon which "source format" option is in effect.
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T04:58:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED82F4C.703E58FA@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<2fa13ee7.0305281208.283d3715@posting.google.com> <bb4mtq$p6t$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <2fa13ee7.0305290752.39c3cd54@posting.google.com> <bb5b3m$r69$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <2fa13ee7.0305300353.48c56262@posting.google.com> <bb7jta$3qf$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <2fa13ee7.0305301009.709ea8d0@posting.google.com> <3ED7F88C.60506@Knology.net> <bb8v7g$co6$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ED829BA.7010106@Knology.net>`

```


LX-i wrote:

> William M. Klein wrote:
> > FYI - whether or not IDENTIFICATION DIVISION must start in column 8 (or
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

'Non-technical' reply. Bill can get more specific. As extensions in Micro
Focus the following,  not necessarily all - can be found in  the COBOL 2002
Standard.

- Free format can use column 1 thru whatever (251) - no Areas A and B

- I stick with Fixed format to get printing margins; I can also ignore
identifying Areas A and B. But with fixed format I finish at Column 72  :-

a) can put * or *> in column 1 as comment
b) using the new comment  *> - can place it anywhere

*> This is a comment starting at Col 1
*> This is a comment starting at Col 8 onwards.
Col 8    Display 'Hello World'  *> this statement displays the literal
Col 8    move a to b
            *> 'a' contains the value .yadda yadda
            add b to c giving d
            *> adding 'b' is the next line
Col 7    can still use * as a comment

All in all - very flexible - check COBOL 2002 for specifics.

Jimmy
```

### J4 paper on "symbols"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T17:58:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
There is a paper for review by J4 called,

  "Symbols for logical operators"

at

     http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/files/03-0145.doc

There are statements about the discussion in comp.lang.cobol that I don't
necessarily think are accurate - but others may.  If you (any CLC reader)
have your own comments on this, you might want to contact the J4 chair at:

    Don.Schricker <at> microfocus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T16:20:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb64k0$2n5e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...
> There is a paper for review by J4 called,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>     Don.Schricker <at> microfocus.com

I've responded separately to Don and the rest of J4 on this subject,
actually in response to someone else's query on the topic.

In the referenced document, Mr. Wagner asserts "After a long discussion on
comp.lang.cobol, the majority (unanimous?) opinion is that these symbols
would be welcomed by all especially younger programmers coming from other
programming cultures ...", and the set of symbols he proposes is "^" for
logical AND, "|" for logical OR, and "!" for logical NOT.

I can see the utility of using symbols >, < and = for relational operators
(I am less convinced about >= and <=, but there's not much I can do about
them -- they were added to the COBOL standard nearly twenty years ago).

But I really don't see that the ability to code relation conditions like "a
| = b ^ c <= d ! e >= f" and "a | = b ! c ^ | d | > e" represents a
significant improvement to the capabilities of the COBOL language, or an
improvement to the clarity of the programmer's intent over what the language
currently provides.

"NOT" and "^" are synonymous in ALGOL.  While I understand that perhaps
ALGOL might not be the "language du jour" for college students, there is a
significant body of folks out there who are expected to be "bilingual" in
ALGOL and COBOL.  Making "^" mean "AND" in COBOL, when it already means
"NOT" in ALGOL, is unlikely to contribute to program reliability in those
environments.

Similarly, "!" is synonymous with "OR" in ALGOL, so having it mean "NOT" in
COBOL might present some issues.

At least some dialects of PL/1 also use "^" for NOT and "!" for OR.

If this proposal were adopted, the situation would be something like this:
"^" would represent "NOT" in ALGOL and PL/1 but "AND" in COBOL; "!" would
represent "OR" in ALGOL but "NOT" in COBOL, and "|" would represent "NOT" in
PL/1 but "OR" in COBOL.

The versions of Pascal with which I am familiar require AND, OR and NOT in
Boolean expressions, though more recent dialects may well have allowed for
single-character symbols in that context.  I don't think the use of English
words for logical operators -- in ALGOL, PL/1 or Pascal -- whether or not
symbols can be used instead -- represents a major deficiency in those
languages, even though they have symbols for arithmetic operators and
comparisons.

All that being said, I also have to note that I haven't seen evidence that
his statement " ... the majority (unanimous?) opinion is that these symbols
would be welcomed by all especially younger programmers coming from other
programming cultures ..." can be demonstrated to be true, either for the
readership of comp.lang.cobol or for the programming community at large.
For the programming community represented by Unisys MCP/AS systems, the
introduction of "^" to mean "AND" in COBOL would present more than a bit of
a problem to the person who's used to the "other programming culture" that
uses that symbol to represent "NOT" (in ALGOL) for decades.  For the same
reason, the introduction of "!" as a synonym for "NOT" in COBOL would
present problems to the person who already uses the symbol as a synonym for
"OR".   Granted, these are not likely *new* programmers, but they do
represent a non-negligible portion of the real-world programmers out there.

In fact, I would go so far as to suggest a high likelihood that the
statement, as written, could be demonstrated to be *false*.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T18:57:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb66og$8l9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bb64k0$2n5e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
The following is what I sent to J4 in response to the note that Chuck sent
out (summarized elsewhere in this thread),

    ***


Unlike Chuck, I think adding a "complete set of symbols" for logical
operator would be a "NICE TO HAVE". I do NOT think it is worth a "lot of
time and resources" (much less contentious debate) - but do think it might
be useful.

I WOULD like to see a future COBOL Standard include support for

<> to mean NOT EQUAL (as originally specified in the CCC JOD)

If I were to "suggest" symbols (from the ISO 8859 "Latin-1" - which I think
is a reasonable assumption), then I would probably go with

^ (X'5E') or � (X'AC') - for NOT

| (X'7C') or � (X'A6') - for OR

I do think, however, there is a "problem" with what you use for AND. It
seems to me that the most "logical" would (of course) be "&" - but I believe
that would be impossible to use as BOTH a logical operator and (as currently
defined) the concatenation operator - because of code such as:

If A not = "B" & "C"

(is it a concatenation operator or is this a combined abbreviated
condition?)

If there were GENERAL consensus that adding a complete set of logical
operators represented as SYMBOLS was a GOOD IDEA, then we could discuss what
would be a possible symbol for "AND" (from within 8859-1). "@" (X'3E') might
be a possibility. Others might be "#" "!" "�" or "�" - none of these are
really good, but are possible. (I would be interested in knowing if any of
this "plain text" note causes problems to the non-US recipients)
```

Can you find one single posting (other than mine) saying words are better than
symbols .. either in general or in a specific case such as NOT? I couldn't.
They started appearing only after I switched sides.

Next time, maybe I'll advocate the opposite of what I believe.

These new-fangled explicit terminators are too dangerous to use. The compiler
doesn't tell you if you left one off. We should all go back to periods.

ELSE should be banned. ELSE is 'not logic' disguised to look like part of an IF.
Better to step around the negative code with a GO TO. 

How's that?

>As accusing you of using non-scientific methods might be considered an
>insult I ask for a second time, then: please be so kind as to post data

>They started appearing only after I switched sides.

Mr Wagner, I am not able to determine the readership of the group, let 
alone the opinions present in the readership, let alone the opinions 
presented by the posters ('opinion' being held distinct from 'what is 
posted'; the latter might be an exercise in logical argument which does 
not represent the author's opinion).

You, on the other hand, have made a statement of 'majority (unanimous) 
opinion' and I was interested in how you determined this... it appears you 
used the e'er-so-respected Statistical Method of 'Well... looked that way 
to me, a-yup!'

>
>Next time, maybe I'll advocate the opposite of what I believe.

Next time maybe you'll make statements for yourself and allow others to 
speak for themselves as well.

>
>These new-fangled explicit terminators are too dangerous to use. The compiler
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>How's that?

Reading this far I can say that it represents 100% of the opinions on this 
matter expressed in this posting.  Lies, damned lies and statistics, eh?

>
>>As accusing you of using non-scientific methods might be considered an
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>What additional data would you accept?

How about a few Google URLs to supporting evidence?  You know... external 
documentation, scholarly rigor, all the stuff that should be made 
available to show that one's data and methods are valid.

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T02:05:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed6b0bf.16033634@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bb64k0$2n5e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>currently provides.

I do. COBOL gives us symbols for relational operators but no symbols for logical
operators. The notational system is asymmetrical. I prefer one system over two,
which is why I prefer GREATER over >. If we could write everything in symbols,
I'd use symbolic notation. 

>"NOT" and "^" are synonymous in ALGOL.  While I understand that perhaps
>ALGOL might not be the "language du jour" for college students, there is a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>environments.

I chose ^ because it looks like the mathematical symbol for AND. & was already
taken by concatenation. The other two are the symbols used in C and Java, which
are much more well-known than Algol and PL/I. If you don't believe me, ask any
recent college grad whether he's _heard_ of Algol, much less learned its
grammar. Then ask whether he's heard of Java. 

>If this proposal were adopted, the situation would be something like this:
>"^" would represent "NOT" in ALGOL and PL/1 but "AND" in COBOL; "!" would
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>comparisons.

You are citing languages designed in the 70s and obsolete in the 80s to 90s.
Nobody programs in ALGOL or PL/I today. Pascal *may* still be used in
classrooms, but not in production code. C++ and Java, on the other hand, have
been the dominant languages for ten years. . 

>All that being said, I also have to note that I haven't seen evidence that
>his statement " ... the majority (unanimous?) opinion is that these symbols
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>represent a non-negligible portion of the real-world programmers out there.

Do you have any evidence to support current development under ALGOL?

>In fact, I would go so far as to suggest a high likelihood that the
>statement, as written, could be demonstrated to be *false*.

It is facile to 'suggest', more challenging to substantiate.
```
