###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T10:59:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb865h$156q$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bb64k0$2n5e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ed6b0bf.16033634@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed6b0bf.16033634@news.optonline.net...

> Nobody programs in ALGOL or PL/I today.

Don't know many PL/1 programmers, I admit, but how much do you know about
Unisys MCP/AS system software and the more sophisticated user application
conventions on that system?

ALGOL is virtually universal in that environment.  Combinations of ALGOL and
COBOL are very common, and a great many of our users are expected to be
familiar with both.  As it happens, WFL (a language that serves a purpose on
MCP/AS systems similar to that provided by JCL on IBM large systems) does
not use symbols for logical operators (providing AND, OR, NOT, EQV and IMP).

> Pascal *may* still be used in classrooms, but not in production code.

Much of the MCP/AS intersystem communication software is written in Pascal,
as, I believe, is some of the imaging support software for financial
applications.  Our COBOL85, C and Pascal83 compilers are written in Pascal
compiler is written in Pascal as well.  And I'm very much surprised to learn
that none of these products are used in production.

> C++ and Java, on the other hand, have been the dominant languages for ten
years. .

> >All that being said, I also have to note that I haven't seen evidence
that
> >his statement " ... the majority (unanimous?) opinion is that these
symbols
> >would be welcomed by all especially younger programmers coming from other

> Do you have any evidence to support current development under ALGOL?

Absolutely.

> It is facile to 'suggest', more challenging to substantiate.

The statement in question is this:  "the majority (unanimous?) opinion is
that these symbols would be welcomed by all."   I don't see this as having
been expressed by a majority of the active participants in this forum.  I
don't agree that the use of "^" for AND in COBOL would be welcomed by all.
You assert the truth of the statement I cited. Certainly the idea that the
opinion that the use of "^" for ALL in COBOL would be "welcomed by all" is a
*unanimous* one is rejected by a single disagreement.  Whether this is a
*majority* opinion, as you state, can only be determined through a search of
the archives or a poll of the readership in which everyone is *required* to
participate, and even at that I am as yet unconvinced that comp.lang.cobol
accurately reflects the COBOL user community as a whole.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T13:01:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED8EDE3.50507@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bb64k0$2n5e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ed6b0bf.16033634@news.optonline.net> <bb865h$156q$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ed8ad55.146250961@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> This began with me arguing that words are better than symbols. As expected, most
> took the opposite side while a few, such as Howard, remained neutral. We even
> had a visit from jallan, who posted several long, scholarly articles in support
> of symbols. NObody argued that words are better. 

You know, I'm starting to think that words aren't too bad, if not 
preferable.  :)  I especially think they're more clear for combined 
abbreviated conditions (although I see and understand your opposition to 
the pseudo "OR" in >= and <=).  IOW,

If X > 5 And < 10

vs.

If X is Less Than 5 And Greater Than 10

Maybe I just like typing...  I -do- find it easier to read, though.  I 
guess it's one of those things you don't notice until you try it.  Of 
course, I still like consistency - don't use words in one place and 
symbols in another...  :)

> Now that I'm converted (saved?), I'm STILL on the wrong side. Uhhh .. nobody
> asked for our opinion. Gimme a break. 

:)  I have a feeling that you could say "The sky is blue", and here's 
what you'd get...

DD - "Could you cite evidence on how you came to the conclusion that the 
sky is blue?"

RP - "You only took a sample of 1 sky.  Even if you took a sample of 3 
skies, that's still not enough."

HB - "It's actually 'The sky is blue.'"  ;)
                                     ^
WMK - "Robert, are you ACTUALLY saying that the sky is BLUE?  The HTML 
safety palette for blue does not look anything like the sky - perhaps 
you should say the sky is #7777FF."

PECD - "You haven't seen blue 'til you've seen these great NZ skies..."

I'm not taking sides on any of this.  From what I've seen in my couple 
of months here, you seem to rub a lot of people the wrong way.  (There's 
been lots of traffic on that too.)  I'll admit, when I read the J4 
paper, I was surprised at the use of "unanimous" as well - I didn't read 
it as "there was unanimous agreement that symbols were better than 
words," it appeared to me that you were saying "there was unanimous 
agreement that these symbols should be implemented."  You've since 
clarified, and I replied to Don Schricker via e-mail with my take on it. 
  There are also folks around here who I have yet to see proffer any 
positive information on their own, only sitting back and trying to poke 
holes in others' postulates.

It's actually kind of funny, sitting back and watching some of this. 
Yes, that above is probably a little far fetched (and I mean no 
disrespect to any of the people in whose mouths I put fictitious words). 
  But I have noticed that people will argue with you when they normally 
wouldn't, not for the sake of arguing, but for the sake of you.  The 
private conversations we've had have been great, and I'm the type of 
person that believes you can learn something from anyone, no matter how 
much you already know.  For that reason, I've really tried =not= to form 
bad opinions of anyone here - even the ones that I don't agree with.

Saturdays give me lots of time to type...  But alas, it's naptime and 
the kids are fussy.  (And my youngest just walked in here and hit his 
mother with a fly swatter, so it's definitely naptime for him...)  Have 
a great weekend!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T13:16:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305311216.5f67fb9b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bb64k0$2n5e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ed6b0bf.16033634@news.optonline.net> <bb865h$156q$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ed8ad55.146250961@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> This began with me arguing that words are better than symbols. As expected,
> most took the opposite side while a few, such as Howard, remained neutral.
> We even had a visit from jallan, who posted several long, scholarly articles
> in support of symbols. NObody argued that words are better. 

Then you failed to understand what was said.

Actually you didn't, I know that you did understand the real issue,
but you have either forgotten it or ignore it.

The argument is:

   What is 'better' for one is what one is used to.

You are used to words so they are better _for_you_.

I am used to '< > = >= <= NOT = AND OR' in Cobol, so they are better
_for_me_.

I am also used to these in other languages (with minor changes) and
with C/C++ operators and these are better in those languages _for_me_.

No one except you (RW) has expressed any problem with 'switching
paradigms' between words and symbols.

Some have argued that >= is more readily understood (_for_them_) than 
the equivalent NOT <.  This is _NOT_ altered by making it '! <'  it is
the concept of NOT that creates the need to make a reversal, not the
change between words and symbols.

Introducing _new_ symbols, especially ones that mean something
different in other languages, means that _no_one_ is used to them,
especially in that context.

Given that 'better' is what one is used to, then _no_one_ will find
them 'better'.


> Now that I'm converted (saved?), 

No one 'converted' you.  You were advocating that your way was 'best'
in some sort of absolute way.  It may have been better for you but no
one else found words 'greater than' to be better for them except when
using obsolete equipment.

Others only expressed what _they_ found better for themselves, their
preferences.  There was no intent to make you change, just to stop
your attempts at changing us.

Now you are _still_ trying to change us by coersing us with 'symbols'.

> I'm STILL on the wrong side. 

No one wants symbols for and, or, not.  No one wants _those_ symbols.

> Uhhh .. nobody asked for our opinion. 
> Gimme a break.

You never understood why they didn't use your 'better' use of words,
you never understood how they could do transformations without it
being your exact set of rules, now you don't understand why they don't
want your changes.

If you _really_ want to write code using symbols for and, or, not then
please do so and write a pre-processor.  But I doubt that you would
ever actually use them, because its not about symbols, it is all about
RW's ego.
```

#### ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-29T19:02:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305291802.784eb5f9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote

>   "Symbols for logical operators"

>      http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/files/03-0145.doc
> 
> There are statements about the discussion in comp.lang.cobol that I don't
> necessarily think are accurate - but others may.  

LOL.  I particularly liked the "majority (unanimous?) opinion",
presumably another wagnerism statistical result based on a sample of 3
or less - I suspect on just 1.

The "Several ... mentally switch gears", seems to be a single sample
named Robert Wagner (based on a google search).

Of course if a choice of symbols were to be made for these operators
then, at least, ones should be chosen which may be consistent with
other languages and not ones that mean something quite different.

Proposed COBOL Pascal Python PERL  VB   C/C++
-------- ----- ------ ------ ----  ---  -----
    ^     AND    and    and   and  and  &&
    |     OR     or     or    or   or   ||
    !     NOT    not    not   not  not  !

It seems that programmers in many other languages will be quite
familiar with what Cobol already offers, contrary to the assertions
made.  But then RW probably didn't know this.

And which languages use the proposed symbols:

Proposed       Pascal Python   PERL    C/C++
-------- ----- ------ ------   ----    ------
    ^          deref  bit xor  xor     bit xor
    |            -    bit or   bit or  bit or
    !            -     -       not     not

So, far from providing something that programmers in other languages
are used to, this is for symbols that are to mean something quite
different from their use elsewhere.  That'll make them 'switch gears'.



OK, own up, which joker faked this up to make RW look like a complete
idiot ?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T03:23:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED6C9E9.8AC0BBCC@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0305291802.784eb5f9@posting.google.com>`

```


Richard wrote:

> OK, own up, which joker faked this up to make RW look like a complete
> idiot ?

You are just plain bloody cruel Richard. I couldn't help but giggle. I don't
give a belly laugh - just giggle. If I thought it would help Robert, I would
own up as the joker.

Your fault Bill - you challenged him - and he submitted.

Robert - twice I've seen you refer to the 17 years from COBOL '85 to COBOL
2002. With a bit of Anderson type accounting you can reduce that to 5 years if
you accept COBOL '97 which gave the intrinsic functions.

Anyway reference the 'delay'. There were two factors :-

- Bill can expand if necessary. Over time as they came to introduce new
features for 2002 they found that to fit in, (backwards compatibility), they
had to consider some of the COBOL '85 wording which was somewhat ambiguous in
definition. In introducing the 'new', revamp the 'old' so the former could fit.
A pretty dull, time consuming exercise

- Sticking with our 17 year time frame they were OVERWHELMED with suggested
features - how many I don't know. Nor do I  know the date, but they had to
stop, take stock and start prioritizing, (a) Do this now (b) Kind of
interesting and (c) Put on the back burner. There's no way of knowing, but
possibly a sizeable chunk might have been in the category that I would classify
'nit picking', as opposed to new, real solid and *significant* features.

Guess what - some, just  like your current suggestion were probably 'nit
pickers'. Small ditsy features which they accommodated. But firiggin OO was put
into either (b) or (c) above, as a result !

Accepting the confusion over symbols that Chuck and Richard have pointed out, I
don't really give a damn whether they are introduced or not, just so, that
along with the symbol for in-line invocation, I don't damn well have to use
them.

! instead of say AND - 1 character instead of 3
# instead of say OR - 1 character instead of 2

BIG DEAL !

Now the only symbol I'm interested in is the <>. Been using it so long, didn't
even realize it wasn't yet in the Standard until Bill pointed that out. With a
non mathematical mind I can see the logic of <>, it isn't < and it isn't >, so
<> doesn't require too much brainwork.

And as for your assertion "unanimous" -  I got really bored reading the whole
thread(s) leading up to this, and seeing, rough guess, some half dozen folks
discussed this with you, and even assuming they agreed with you, hardly
constitutes unanimity.

Consider: at the end of the day, will introduction of your symbols truly
improve COBOL ? And to me more importantly, don't let's have another nit picker
on the list which will delay discussion on OO Standard Classes or XML  - wish
they would co-opt;  9 people, (J4),  can only stretch themselves so far.

Jimmy
```

2002.

>Anyway reference the 'delay'. There were two factors :-
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

yourself. 

No doubt this was done to some extent. Evidently not enough. 

>- Sticking with our 17 year time frame they were OVERWHELMED with suggested
>features - how many I don't know. Nor do I  know the date, but they had to

most important thing is to stay focussed on a goal. 

>Accepting the confusion over symbols that Chuck and Richard have pointed out, I
>don't really give a damn whether they are introduced or not, just so, that
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>BIG DEAL !

It's not a big deal but it's an easy decision and it's trivial to implement.
Tokenizing "!" the same as "not" takes one line of code. 

>And as for your assertion "unanimous" -  I got really bored reading the whole
>thread(s) leading up to this, and seeing, rough guess, some half dozen folks
>discussed this with you, and even assuming they agreed with you, hardly

should have qualified it 'unanimous opinion of those who bothered to write.'

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

##### ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T10:13:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed850da.122572452@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0305291802.784eb5f9@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>named Robert Wagner (based on a google search).

It was based on this quote from you:

  "I agree that it would be nice if Cobol had <> or !=.

   When I do transforms in Python or C/C++ or other I do <  to >= and > to <=
   etc. !< and !> are not available. I also do = to !=.  It would be nice to 
   have a single method so I do it that way in Cobol except the ! has to use
   not."

>Of course if a choice of symbols were to be made for these operators
>then, at least, ones should be chosen which may be consistent with
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 !     NOT    not    not   not  not  !

The proposed symbols for OR and NOT are the same as C++ and Java. I would have
chosen & for AND if it weren't already taken. Instead, I proposed the math
symbol. 

On second thought, && and || might be better choices. COBOL has no problem with
* for multiplication and ** for exponentiation. 

>It seems that programmers in many other languages will be quite
>familiar with what Cobol already offers, contrary to the assertions
>made.  But then RW probably didn't know this.

I knew enough to borrow from current languages -- C++ and Java -- rather than
languages whose standards were last updated in the '60s.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T12:34:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0305311134.7dccfc1d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0305291802.784eb5f9@posting.google.com> <3ed850da.122572452@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >The "Several ... mentally switch gears", seems to be a single sample
> >named Robert Wagner (based on a google search).
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>    not."

What has my agreement about '<>' to do with your proposal for and, or,
not ?  What has it to do with 'switch gears' ?

If you knew anything about C then you would know that '!=' is a single
not-equal operator and is not built from separate '!' and '='
operators.

A change to having '<>' or '!"' as an inequality comparator has noting
to do with having a symbol for 'not'.

Perhaps you are so unused to have anybody agree with anything you say
that you were overwealmed.
 
> >Of course if a choice of symbols were to be made for these operators
> >then, at least, ones should be chosen which may be consistent with
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> The proposed symbols for OR and NOT are the same as C++ and Java.

The symbol for not is the same as Java and C.  The symbols for and or
or are _NOT_ the same.  In Java and C they are '&&' and '||'.  You
chose '^' and '|' which means something quite different.

But this table indicates that your claims about other language
programmers was false and had no foundation as this table indicates.

> On second thought, && and || might be better choices. COBOL has no problem
> with * for multiplication and ** for exponentiation. 

Well, duh.  You finally get a clue.

You may one day realise why I say that your arguments are shallow. 
Now that you have dug a little deeper with the material supplied here
you realise that your original choices were wrong.

Far from have 'majority' support for your codes, now that you have
changed your mind that leaves _zero_ support for them.

> I knew enough to borrow from current languages -- C++ and Java --

I very much doubt that you knew what symbols Algol or PL/I used at
all. I also doubt that you knew enough about many other languages to
know what was generally used by other current programmers.  You may
recognise what C looks like, but you didn't know enough to know which
codes to borrow - you probably never heard of the bit-wise operators.
```

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed86769.128348745@news.optonline.net...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
How many of this group did you poll to
> >get a "consensus"?
>
> Everyone who chose to write on the subject -- roughly 6-10. The other
2,999,990,
> who each have a computer, didn't voice an opinion. Anyway, the suggestion
came
> from me. My comments about CLC were just supporting information.
>

It is a fair point that the suggestion came from you. That, at least, is
true.

The "supporting information" is simply untrue, and, as such, lends nothing
to your case. How can you expect them to take you seriously if the
"supporting information" is fabricated?

There are two possibilities here:

1. You honestly believe that everybody who contributed is in favour of your
proposal for Logical Operation symbols.

2. You figured it was OK to assume they all did, whether they expressed it
or not.

If the first is true you are having serious problems with handling Reality
(lay off the mushrooms...vegetarian is one thing, hallucinogenic fungi are
quite another <G>).

If the second is true, you are a very naughty boy and definitely NOT the
Messiah... (oops, sorry, I've been watching "Life of Brian" on DVD...)

Fortunately, it won't make any difference because these things never do, but
we should at least have the courtesy when dealing with J4 to make a fair
statement and not bombard them with Bullshit. One of these days, some young
eager beaver might be moved to actually DO something about a suggestion
received via this route. It would be a pity if his/her energy was wasted on
something that just wasn't accurate.

Here's a suggestion...

The next time you feel moved to make a presentation to J4 and you feel like
you are speaking for the "majority" of the people you have interacted with,
just do a quick post and ask them...something along the lines of "I'm
planning a submission to J4 based on this discussion , and proposing
<whatever>. Does anybody have any strong objections to this?"

That way you will gain the respect of your peers (..oh yes they are <G>),
and you will also ensure that you afford J4 the courtesy to which they are
entitled.

Alternatively, leave CLC out of it, make the submission entirely off your
own bat, and let the suggestion sink or swim on its own merit.

Finally, my offer stands. If this gets implemented, dinner is on me <G>.

Pete.
```

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed86769.128348745@news.optonline.net...
<snip>
>
> Everyone who chose to write on the subject -- roughly 6-10. The other
2,999,990,
> who each have a computer, didn't voice an opinion. Anyway, the suggestion
came
> from me. My comments about CLC were just supporting information.
>
>

I would STRONGLY suggest that if you now maintain that the comments about
CLC were "just supporting information", that you explicitly send a note to
the J4 chair clarifying this.  Futhermore, I would also suggest that you
indicate that you may have "misrepresented" waht the majority/unanamous
views expressed in CLC were.

    ***

Some examples that you might want to look at from various threads of what
was ACTUALLY said are:

"Certainly, I know that there are programming languages with a "symbol" for
the logical "NOT"  "AND" and "OR" - so providing such to COBOL wouldn't be
totally awful."

"COBOL actually has a reasonably complete set of arithmetic operators
represented by symbols. It has a complete set of equality/inequality
operators represented by symbols. Only the three Boolean operators are
represented by words. All their spelled-out words are very short and perhaps
therefore do not so much call out for symbols as some other operators.

But I can't understand the logic that because COBOL doesn't use symbols for
all operators, I should therefore as much as possible not use symbols for
any of them, even though I find expressions with symbols clearer. The more
logical approach is to take what I can get."

"Mr Wagner, please be so kind as to demonstrate how one's ability to readily
recognise a set of symbols has anything, whatsoever, to do with logical
capabilities... at your leisure, of course."

"Asymmetry is a problem with the "language".  You haven't shown that one of
the two is a "complete" set and the other is an "incomplete set".  However,
even if you had, I don't think that would (necessarily) mean that simply not
being a "complete" set means that it is "less good" to use one or the  other
option - where both exists."

"> You don't get it. Yes, COBOL has symbols for math operations but not for
> logical  operations.

Either way, it like every other system I can name is "incomplete", by your
standards.

So the answer is, yes, I would use such an incomplete system."

"> Not the important logical operators: AND, OR and NOT. Suppose math used
symbols
> for some operators and words for others. You wouldn't be pleased with that
> notation.

Only until I got used to it <shrug>.  In fact if that is what they did do
then I _would_ be used to it.  In fact they _do_ use sine, cosine, tan, log
etc which _are_ words and it _doesn't_ displease me."
```

#### ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T16:18:30+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed7263c_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...
> There is a paper for review by J4 called,
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I didn't follow much of this discussion as I found it tedious in the
extreme. However, looking at Robert's succinct summary I find it hard to
believe that the "unanimous" or even "majority" opinion was in favour of
symbols for Logical operators.

My first impulse was to write to Don Schricker and say that I, for one,
don't see the need to offer alternatives to AND OR and NOT.

But then I thought about it some more and decided that, as long as no one
FORCES the replacement of the words with these symbols, there is no harm
done.

It is unlikely we'll see it implemented anyway, if past experience is
anything to go on.

Pete.
```

Not at all. It never does matter to RW what anybody says.  He has

   Make something up 
   Ignore any documentation (it is usually wrong)
   Look for support by misreading
   Argue continuously that RW is right
   Claim all counter aguments support RW
   Claim no one counters with cogent arguments 
   Continue until everyone gives up or tells RW to fuck off
   Win

above knowledge by parents and the child is told to ignore the
teachers who are trying to crush dear Roberts gift for creativity by
trying to make him conform to what books say.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T17:27:38+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nukhdv88tt5q7cf63l8hdukdna3qut9v80@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed7263c_4@news.athenanews.com> <bb7p2t$s7h$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0305301240.5fd56e4b@posting.google.com> <3ed8b30f.147717754@news.optonline.net>`

```
On Sat, 31 May 2003 14:13:17 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
wrote:

>
>You say you need more disk space?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Looks to me like you have plenty of space. 

That is assuming the command "rm" works.

A good programmer knows better than just to issue an "rm -r *", as it
is bound to fail.

Instead he/she uses
find . -follow -depth -exec rm {} \;

or
find . -follow -depth -print|xargs rm

This is a free lesson. Take it while it lasts.
Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote:
>On Sat, 31 May 2003 14:13:17 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
>wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>Looks to me like you have plenty of space. 

>That is assuming the command "rm" works.

They usually disable it by aliasing it to 'rm -i' or something non-functional..
'unalias rm' removes that inconvenience. <g>

>A good programmer knows better than just to issue an "rm -r *", as it
>is bound to fail.

It fails because too many files overflow the file list. If each of Richard's
insults were stored in a separate file, five of them wouldn't come close.

>Instead he/she uses
>find . -follow -depth -exec rm {} \;
>
>or
>find . -follow -depth -print|xargs rm

ROFL. In real life I'd use a GUI tool such as Explorer talking thru SAMBA.
Click, Ctrl-A, Del, click (yes) .. two keystrokes and two clicks .. problem
solved. :)

>This is a free lesson. Take it while it lasts.

You made me laugh so hard, I almost spit wine into the keyboard.
```

#### ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols" <- Bad idea

- **From:** "Anon" <anon@anon.org>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T22:36:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb97vl$odf$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I am just a COBOL applications programmer. I've been coding
COBOL for over 20 years. I don't sit on any committees, I
don't write books or articles. I have any pie-in-sky
academic credentials. I do have a reputation for writing
stable, efficient code. I've mentored any number of younger
folks on the job and thing I stress over and over is
"Clarity not Cleverness" or another way "Don't be Clever at
the expense of Clarity". Cutesy code has no place in a
business application. The beauty of COBOL and what has
always been it's hallmark is YOU CAN READ IT! It's English
like "self documenting" style is what sets it apart and
what it makes the best language for business logic.
The best code is the clearest code.
The standard symbols + - = / * > <  are fine.
It matters not to me whether you say "is not equal to" or
whether you say "not =", both are equally clear.
Even those who don't know programming can READ it.
But IMO, "is ! =" or "^=' is NOT CLEAR.

use: AND, OR, NOT, FROM, UNTIL and so on. Spell it out, it's COBOL.

What's next then: "perform varying C ; 1 by ++ ; C > 10"? All those
hieroglyphics just cause mass confusion.

COBOL does not need a bunch c/c++ like CRYPTOGRAPHY!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols" <- Bad idea

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <wsimmons5@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T23:28:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y_XBa.56023$%42.19662@fed1read06>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bb97vl$odf$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`

```
Great, someone else said it before I did.

As I see it, a pre-processor might offer an option to help
bridge some geeks by translating symbols to words, but
generally, I don't even like to see sample code that reverts to
using something like:

        02 zilch pic x(nn).

There are times when the word I'd like to use does not quickly
come to mind. However, the guy who paid me would not tolerate
such short cuts.  In our NY Headquarters prior to the existance
of a compiler for the 1400 series, we insisted that the conversion
be to COBOL using predefined statements with the autocoder
equivalent.  They helped cross train between COBOL and assembler
also. The result was we had two source listing until we got the compiler.
Everyone involved could understand the source.

Warren Simmons
wsimmons5@cox.net
```

Thomas

<a id="t-222f627d98"></a>
## Thread: Calling a C module from a COBOL program — 6 msgs

### Calling a C module from a COBOL program

- **From:** jdhart999@aol.com (Jdhart999)
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T00:48:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030529204835.23079.00000504@mb-m13.aol.com>`

```
Hi all,

It's my first post to the list so I hope that you all might be able to help me.
I've spent a couple of days trying to resolve this but have run out of ideas.

We currently have a couple of COBOL programs that call C modules to calculate
SHA and MD5 hash values for external files. The guy who wrote the COBOL and C
programs is long gone and now we are trying to make some changes to the hash
algorithms. I'm not a C programmer but I have been able to get the C modules to
compile cleanly but I can't get them to link properly. When I link the two
object modules together the linker can't resolve the symbolic name of the
called C module.

As an example, the COBOL program is named CALC_HASHES.COB and the C routines
are CALC_SHA.C and CALC_MD5.C. 

Within the CALC_HASHES.COB I have the following call:

   CALL "CALC_SHA"
         USING FILE_NAME, 
                    HASH_VALUE
         GIVING STATUS_CODE.

When I link the two modules together the linker tells me that it can't find
CALC_SHA.

I am working on Digital/Compac/HP OpenVMS running on an Alpha Server.

As a test I wrote a stripped down COBOL program that does nothing but call a C
routine. I wrote a C routine that simply writes the standard "Hello World" to
the screen. I can't get those two to work together either. This leads me to
believe that I'm not getting them linked together properly. I guess there could
also be a C compile parameter that I'm missing too.

Any thoughts?

Thanks in advance,
    Joanne
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling a C module from a COBOL program

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T13:35:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s6JBa.289815$M81.183404@news02.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`
- **References:** `<20030529204835.23079.00000504@mb-m13.aol.com>`

```
Your COBOL compiler setting is not set correctly. Consult your COBOL and C
compiler for share library compilation.
There should be a system format file (with symbol table) you need to tell
COBOL compiler from C compilation results and this make two languages know
each other.


Thomas

#### ↳ Re: Calling a C module from a COBOL program

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T12:30:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0305301130.7ed3ff9f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20030529204835.23079.00000504@mb-m13.aol.com>`

```
I've called C programs on OpenVms fairly easily.  Check the case of
your entrypoints.  I've never seen a C program yet that used all
uppercase for the name.

Look at your C program to be sure - but try thinks like Call
"calc_sha" or "Calc_SHA" or "Calc_Sha" etc...


jdhart999@aol.com (Jdhart999) wrote in message news:<20030529204835.23079.00000504@mb-m13.aol.com>...
> Hi all,
> 
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>     Joanne
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling a C module from a COBOL program

- **From:** jdhart999@aol.com (Jdhart999)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T02:44:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030530224423.08524.00000465@mb-m24.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20030529204835.23079.00000504@mb-m13.aol.com>`

```
Thanks for the responses. 

My lack of any C training became obvious today. As I was working on the problem
I realized that I was compiling the modules with a C++ compiler. The compiler
does something called "name mangling" that wouldn't allow my COBOL program to
"see" the function names in the C object module. When I used the regular C
compiler I was able to link the modules together just fine. 

Joanne
```

The VMS linker is case sensitive.  I'd look inside the .obj to see whether
'calc_sha' is upper or lower.

<a id="t-a37abe2579"></a>
## Thread: Java API for COBOL: a shortcut for Cobol expert to become OO expert for web-based data access — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Java API for COBOL: a shortcut for Cobol expert to become OO expert for web-based data access

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T15:32:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tQKBa.90285$cK1.32419@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
Corporola Inc. is now shipping a set of Java API for COBOL programming.

This set of API includes complete COBOL data definition classes and complete
COBOL file definition classes.
With this set of API, a COBOL expert can write Java program as the same as a
Java expert and it brings all the benefits of OO, database, Web, and TCP/IP
inside Java.

Java API for COBOL provides a shortcut for COBOL programmers to become OO
experts for web-based data access while makes full use of their COBOL
expertise.


Please visit http://www.corporola.com/product/java_api_for_cobol.html for
details.


Thomas Li
Corporola Inc.
```

#### ↳ Re: Java API for COBOL: a shortcut for Cobol expert to become OO expert for web-based data access

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T14:16:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb8aoh$k15$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<tQKBa.90285$cK1.32419@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
It looks interesting.  I couldn't find (on the web page) any specific list
of WHICH COBOL "dialects" (vendor and releases) are "supported" (or have
been tested).  Do you have such a list?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Java API for COBOL: a shortcut for Cobol expert to become OO expert for web-based data access

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T02:10:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<daUBa.293638$M81.169872@news02.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`
- **References:** `<tQKBa.90285$cK1.32419@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <bb8aoh$k15$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
This set of API has no COBOL "dialects" problem because it only defines the
Data Definition and File definition for sequential, relative and indexed
files. There is no COBOL statements except statements about variable itself
(like size) and file operations. It's very small but powerful.
When used, Java statements for control structure are needed but they are
almost the same as COBOL.

Thanks for your interest.


Thomas Li

<a id="t-fee60c567d"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu and "support" — 10 this month / 14 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu and "support"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T15:14:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bb8e31$nqh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I know that there have been several (many?) past post that have explicitly
PRAISED Fujitsu for its excellent "support" (and compared this, among
others, to the cost and "responsiveness" of Micro Focus' support).  In case
you are an existing Fujitsu customer (paid up on maintenance) *OR* are
considering new/additional Fujitsu purchases, I thought that I would post to
this forum the information now available on the ServiceWise "home" page for
existing customers.

"Support Announcements

Changes in Support
We'd like to take this opportunity to let you know about a few changes the
NetCOBOL team is making to the way that we provide support.

In order to provide the best possible service to all of our customers, all
technical support incidents must be initiated through this Web support tool.
We will no longer be providing call in support. When the situation warrants,
members of our support staff will call you to help resolve issues, but our
primary method of communication will be through the Web support tool. Using
the tool ensures that we have a log of all activity related to your incident
and can bring the right resources to bear in giving you a timely response.

For incidents that fall outside the scope of our maintenance agreement,
we're introducing the option to purchase assistance for a fee of $125 per
incident. This includes How-to questions, requests for samples, and
information about interfaces to third party tools. In the past, our support
team has been quite liberal about answering such questions. Moving forward,
however, it is necessary for us to recover at least some of the cost
associated with this very costly activity.

For more detailed information about our support policies and procedures and
information on how to purchase paid incidents, please visit our Web site at
http://www.netcobol.com/support/ .

Thank you,
The Fujitsu NetCOBOL Support Team"

    ***

If you "read into" this (as I do - but possibly NOT as intended) this may
provide some "valuable" information to those who question whether or not
marketing COBOL to "individuals" (as opposed to enterprises) is profitable -
and whether or not providing "free run-times" is a good BUSINESS decision
for the vendor.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu and "support"

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T20:32:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED7BC46.C9DBA83B@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<bb8e31$nqh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

> I know that there have been several (many?) past post that have explicitly
> PRAISED Fujitsu for its excellent "support" (and compared this, among
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well to be totally fair on this one, (but didn't like what I read), we have to
compare apples with apples. Don't know if you would take to the suggestion, but
as our "FAQqer" (be careful how you pronounce that one <G>), could you use your
'reliable contacts' at least in the PC-world to get comparisons. Along with
other information in the FAQ about compilers, and treated IMPARTIALLY - it could
be a good resource.

Just as I started typing this, I recalled that some two or threes years back,
Ken Foskey offered his site to do something similar - impartial comment on each
compiler, costs etc.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu and "support"

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T12:51:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed803d4_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bb8e31$nqh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:bb8e31$nqh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...
> I know that there have been several (many?) past post that have explicitly
> PRAISED Fujitsu for its excellent "support" (and compared this, among
> others, to the cost and "responsiveness" of Micro Focus' support).  In
case
> you are an existing Fujitsu customer (paid up on maintenance) *OR* are
> considering new/additional Fujitsu purchases, I thought that I would post
to
> this forum the information now available on the ServiceWise "home" page
for
> existing customers.
>

Bill,

Fujitsu have always done their best to provide good support. It isn't easy
and it costs. Many of the requests they have to deal with are from people
who can't be bothered to read the documentation (we see a number of these
here). Over the years they have realised that support costs are a burden out
of proportion to the demands placed on it. Up until recently you could buy
support for a couple of hundred dollars a year and this also entitled you to
free (or advantageous) compiler upgrades.

There was a time when you could log a problem and a fujitsu problem solver
would deal with it. Some of these guys were outstanding. I'm not going to
mention names, but I could <G>.

more below...


> "Support Announcements
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> technical support incidents must be initiated through this Web support
tool.
> We will no longer be providing call in support. When the situation
warrants,
> members of our support staff will call you to help resolve issues, but our
> primary method of communication will be through the Web support tool.
Using
> the tool ensures that we have a log of all activity related to your
incident
> and can bring the right resources to bear in giving you a timely response.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> information about interfaces to third party tools. In the past, our
support
> team has been quite liberal about answering such questions. Moving
forward,
> however, it is necessary for us to recover at least some of the cost
> associated with this very costly activity.
>
> For more detailed information about our support policies and procedures
and
> information on how to purchase paid incidents, please visit our Web site
at
> http://www.netcobol.com/support/ .
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I stopped paying for maintenance after the Service Wise system was
introduced as I found it cumbersome and not responsive...useless.
Fortunately, I seldom need support anyway. Since Version 6 of COBOL 97 I
have found no bugs despite extensive use of most of the facilities
(including OO and component building).

As for NetCOBOL, well, I simply can't afford it, so it is academic. I build
Web Services with COBOL97/PowerCOBOL v6 and SOAP. I don't get the benefit of
the CLR but it works for me.

I think the point about interfacing to third party products is a fair one.
You would hardly expect them to maintain expertise in every third party
product that people may want to address with Fujitsu COBOL.

Putting it into perspective... the software is stable and works as specified
PROVIDED you don't try doing outlandish things with it. If you decide you
want to interface to a Bendix washing machine and drive the spin cycle with
COBOL, you can hardly expect Fujitsu to provide expertise and experience,
for free; it is your decision.


> If you "read into" this (as I do - but possibly NOT as intended) this may
> provide some "valuable" information to those who question whether or not
> marketing COBOL to "individuals" (as opposed to enterprises) is
profitable -
> and whether or not providing "free run-times" is a good BUSINESS decision
> for the vendor.
>

Providing a free runtime has enabled Fujitsu to capture a significant share
of what was once a MicroFocus market. When it comes to NetCOBOL, the runtime
is academic, because my understanding is that it generates the MS Common
Language Runtime (CLR). (I could be wrong about this; I don't claim any
expertise with NetCOBOL...)

In general, I don't think it is unfair or unreasonable for Fujitsu to
rationalise their maintenance arrangements for a new product that has cost a
fortune to develop, has a place at the cutting edge of the technology, and
is likely to require more support for that very reason. The fact that this
places it beyond the reach of "small" developers or individuals like myself,
is just a sad fact of doing business. If I could justify the cost of buying
it, I'd do so like a shot. That is not Fujitsu's problem.

For the standard Fujitsu products (COBOL 97/ PowerCOBOL) people can get help
from forums like this at no charge at all...<G>.


Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu and "support"

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T04:33:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED82993.654F5C34@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<bb8e31$nqh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed803d4_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```


"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:

> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
> news:bb8e31$nqh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm only responding because I *anticipated* you would come on a bit like St.
George v the Dragon <G>.

While the above is true, it's not dissimilar to Micro Focus. Initially of course
there was the Compuserve Forum where I "met" you and Michael M. (Completely
forgotten - how long ago was that ?). And of course there were generalized
discussions, together with specific queries. Well they certainly didn't like it
being used as a forum for knocking VISOC - so it died.

I've already forgotten the name "Answer Lab" I think - introduced with Net
Express. Pay maintenance, you had access and those automatic updates you  refer
to . Now you still need the maintenance to get upgrades, but the replacement
Answer Exchange is open to all. Monitored, but "No shouting'. Obviously most
questions are geared to specific modules per compiler - different groups for
University Edition, Server Express, Unix and the plain Jane Net Express, which
seems to have most participants.

Questions can be answered by M/F folks - they are spread all around us, our old
faithful Patrick M. doing his thing from Chicago area, Alan Wheeler down in Palo
Alto, Chris Glazier (I *think* in Ca. ), David Sands in Newbury and Jean in la
belle provence east of me. Then there's Barb S., a good resource, their in-house
'expert' on SQL in general.

Alternatively there are situations where somebody will say, "I have a problem
with......". An experienced user replies, "Same with me and this is the solution
I found....".

It's a pretty good mix, and yes it must cost M/F to support. I don't know the
details, but over and above the general support, they have graded technical
support with different dollar values.

I sympathize with beginners who jump in here with very basic questions. Sure you
can read the manuals - but if you are really new at it, it can get missed in
trying to absorb the overall mix of topics you are looking at. We used to have
printed manuals, then those dreadful Windows on-line help files. Thank God most
vendors seem to have the manuals now as on-line documents.

The other factor - without being told to, (as part of their job designation),
roughly about five of them lurking here. And genuinely, I believe, out of the
goodness of their hearts 'wanting developers to succeed', they jump in. Most
recent I recall was Simon Tobias on a Unix problem. Again they weren't asked but
'did a good one' for their company.

Correct me if I'm wrong - other than primarily Thane jumping in, and there's
Jerry also, I really can't recall any F/J personnel putting their two cents in
here. I find that strange.

Of course the above M/F personnel aren't responsible for those wretched runtime
fees, but at least the tech support is extremely good. There's a very old retail
phrase 'lost leaders' - sell it cheap (or give it away). When you've trounced
the opposition, cornered the market and got a neck hold on your consumers - now
start upping the price.

Just reflect on what happened to the British auto industry. She's dead now, but
can't help feeling my sister-in-law was right about buying 'Asian' autos - a
preference for  bumper stickers in the UK about 10-15 years back - "BLY BLITISH
!'

Freebie Tech Support Discussion Group - excellent for developers and as a plus,
a very good marketing tool for the vendor.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu and "support"

- **From:** "Lee Unterreiner" <flu@nospam.flu-ent.com>
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T13:49:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sp2Ca.1047311$3D1.611460@sccrnsc01>`
- **References:** `<bb8e31$nqh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As one of the support people fired by Fujitsu recently I guess I should
weigh in on this topic.

Please excuse the following brief personal history - there is a point to it.

For those who don't know me, I've been an COBOL advocate for 30+ years.
I've been a programmer,  trainer, manager, tech writer - I've designed
debuggers and programming tools - I've worked for three COBOL compiler
vendors.  But the job that has given me the most satisfaction over my career
have been the times when I worked the tech support hot lines.

In the early 80's I was part of the initial XPEDITER development team.  The
product was and I suppose still is a technological marvel.  It was also, in
the early years, buggy as hell.  Sales support had to cross their fingers
when demo-ing the product for fear of crashing TSO or IMS or CICS or the
whole mainframe.   The only reason we got past the early years was the
crackerjack support team back in San Jose to put out the fires.

In the late 80's I went to MicroFocus just as they started their big growth
spurt.  Again a good company with lots of tools an innovative products.  And
again the product was complicated and BUGGY. And again again it was the
great support team in Palo Alto there to back up sales.  I might also add
that the MicroFocus support team consisted a many of the same people who
worked on XPEDITER (including our own WMK).

In both of the jobs I had great satisfaction.  I was working with products,
although imperfect, that could be of tremendous benefit to my fellow COBOL
programmers.  I admit to getting a buzz upon seeing the eyes light up when a
programmer suddenly sees a better, less tedious way of doing their job.  I'm
also a compulsive problem solver - I like finding the bugs that others
overlook.  As both companies got larger, suddenly support was an expense to
be minimized.

I left in both cases.  I think it was to both companies detriment.  No, not
my leaving, but going that extra mile with personal one on one tech support
(when its needed).  Wasn't that what made IBM great when we were young?

In the late 90's I ended at Fujitsu.  Here was the ultimate company and job
I thought.  The product was virtually bug free and leading edge technology
(for Windows anyway).  COBOL at last had arrived and had a vendor to
champion it.  The documentation is very poor, and yet the technology so up
to date, and with virtually no other resources for COBOL programmers, I
thought support was essential to keeping the product line afloat.  Support
at Fujitsu was primarily a two person affair.  A guy named Alex handled most
of the routine stuff and I concentrated on the bleeding edge stuff and
legacy stuff.

Then on tax day in April we were rather uncerimoniously told our services
were no longer required.  We were told at the time it was temporary and for
budget reasons but we subsequently learned that to be horse sh*t.   We were
not let go because of money - it turns out we just weren't needed anymore.

I've done much soul searching in the last month.  I feel lost, betrayed.
Its the first time in my career I'm not needed.  I have no COBOL any more.
Is it time to switch languages for good?

I have just been reading Chris Richardson's new COBOL book.  It is
excellent.  I find a lot of ideas parallel my own right now.

OK, so I'm a little bitter.  I no longer see a reason to keep COBOL on my
resume.  Unfortunately, given my work history, I don't know how to hide it.

Lee Unterreiner    www.flu-ent.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu and "support"

- **From:** dbryant@iols.net (David K. Bryant)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T11:38:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c4884b5a.0305311038.9cee517@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb8e31$nqh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<bb8e31$nqh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>...
> "Support Announcements
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> technical support incidents must be initiated through this Web support tool.

When I read this one thing jumps out at me:  this will not 
improve support.

Anyone who has ever run or worked in a call center knows
that is quite easy to scale up to handle 600 calls per
minute but no one has figured out how to handle 600 e-mails
PER DAY.

A Web or e-mail based mechanism for customer communication
has always failed to provide a satisfactory result.  How 
many times have you sent a message to 

   webmaster@yoyodyne.com
   sales@yoyodyne.com
   support@yoyodyne.com
   info@yoyodyne.com

and gotten a response, let alone a satisfatory solution?

It just provides an easier mechanism for ignoring the
customer's needs.  A ringing telephone is seldom ignored.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu and "support"

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T22:22:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0305312122.4f21bff9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb8e31$nqh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <c4884b5a.0305311038.9cee517@posting.google.com>`

```
For the record - I don't really like it, but I really do UNDERSTAND
IT.

How it improves support:  Single point of entry and exit for support
requests.  Drives accountability.  It's not like what you describe
with the beloved Yoyodyne John Bryant, but it's a single interface -
meaning that support incidents are logged and support reps are
responsible and will be accountable.

I do tech support for a company - via email only.  I won't call (Well
in extreme cases I have been known to do so) , I won't accept a phone
call as a matter of course.  I can handle 20 electronic requests in
the time it takes to complete a single phone call.  It's too expensive
to do the phone center.  I think they did RIGHT by their customers
with this -- the alternative would be to raise support prices or start
charging for version upgrades.  I don't want to foot the bill for the
bozo who can't take a little iniative and figure things out for
himself.  It is a change - change irritates people, but it's not like
they are making you cross the 8th dimension to get support.

Yours,

John Thane Hubbell
(Black 'Lectroid)


dbryant@iols.net (David K. Bryant) wrote in message news:<c4884b5a.0305311038.9cee517@posting.google.com>...

<a id="t-bda2a757cb"></a>
## Thread: MF NE3.1 RTS 9/199 Network Error — 1 this month / 2 total

### MF NE3.1 RTS 9/199 Network Error

- **From:** pepe@gmx.com (Pepe)
- **Date:** 2003-05-30T15:39:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cb2e24f.0305301439.47f3eec8@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

we recently have some problems with MF COBOL file access.
A new installation uses both workstation and server based on W2K SP3. 
The COBOL part of the program is compiled with NetExpress 3.1 no SP.
The files access is done with "normal extfh" .
It sometimes happens now, that the complete file access (read, write)
for all opened files stops. It is also not possible, to open files
again. Even restarting the program often does not help if there are
more users accessing the files on the share.
COBOL reports an 9/199 error. Calling GetLastError directly after the
file access only leads to "an unexpected network error occured". There
is nothing written to any event logs of Windows.
I thought of problems with OpLocks, but disabling them did not solve
the problem.
Needless to say that similar installations with the same program are
working since years.

Perhaps some of you already had similar problems and could give me a
clue on where to lay stress on my investigations.

Regards,
Pepe
```

---

<a id="t-e9a2046849"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu 3.0 & Exec SQL — 3 this month / 41 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Fujitsu 3.0 & Exec SQL

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T16:24:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ED91D90.6090505@Knology.net>`

```
I've got a question.  I'm trying to play with making a DLL to access a 
little database I've got here.  The "Exec SQL Begin Declare Section 
End-Exec" works fine (in working-storage), but any Exec SQL in the 
procedure division, and I get a compile error popup dialog....

"C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\F3BHOPAS.DLL is not found. RC=126"

Is that DLL supposed to be there?  (I've checked, and I don't have any 
file by that name on my PC at all...)

Thanks...  :)
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu 3.0 & Exec SQL

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-05-31T22:03:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0305312103.5651f678@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ED91D90.6090505@Knology.net>`

```
There were several "versions" of capability with v3 - depending on the
serial number of the install.  Some serial numbers offered limited (90
day) evaluations of full capability - others offered limited
functionality.  ODBC, embedded SQL and "optimization" are disabled in
the "educational" serial numbered installations.

LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message news:<3ED91D90.6090505@Knology.net>...

> little database I've got here.  The "Exec SQL Begin Declare Section 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
